# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Pressure Drop at Incipient Fluidization (ΔP_mf)

This is the most important and frequently tested derivation in KTU.

**Step 1 — Define the system.** Consider a fluidized bed of cross-sectional area $A$ and expanded height $L$, containing mono-sized particles of diameter $d_p$ and bed voidage $\epsilon$.

**Step 2 — Force balance on the bed (gravity vs. gas drag).** At incipient fluidization, the upward drag force exerted by the gas exactly balances the **net downward (gravitational minus buoyant) force** on the particles:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Drag force (upward)} &= \Delta P \cdot A \\
\text{Weight of particles} &= (1-\epsilon)\cdot V \cdot \rho_s \cdot g = (1-\epsilon)\cdot A \cdot L \cdot \rho_s \cdot g \\
\text{Buoyancy on particles} &= (1-\epsilon)\cdot A \cdot L \cdot \rho_f \cdot g
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate forces for static equilibrium (incipient condition).**

$$
\Delta P_{mf} \cdot A = (1-\epsilon_{mf})\cdot A \cdot L_{mf} \cdot \rho_s \cdot g - (1-\epsilon_{mf})\cdot A \cdot L_{mf} \cdot \rho_f \cdot g
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.** Cancel $A$ from both sides and factor out $(1-\epsilon_{mf})$:

$$
\Delta P_{mf} = (1-\epsilon_{mf})\cdot L_{mf}\cdot (\rho_s - \rho_f)\cdot g
$$

This gives **Equation (2)** in the formula sheet. It states that the pressure drop at incipient fluidization depends *only* on the bed's physical properties and voidage — **not** on the gas velocity.

---

## Derivation 2: Minimum Fluidization Velocity (U_mf) using the Ergun Equation

**Step 1 — State the Ergun equation** for a fixed bed (Region A→B):
$$
\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{150(1-\epsilon)^{2}\,\mu\,U_{0}}{\epsilon^{3}(\phi_{s}d_{p})^{2}} + \frac{1.75(1-\epsilon)\,\rho_{f}\,U_{0}^{2}}{\epsilon^{3}\,\phi_{s}\,d_{p}}
$$

**Step 2 — At the brink of fluidization**, the bed is still essentially "fixed" mechanically, so the same equation applies with $U_0 = U_{mf}$ and $\epsilon = \epsilon_{mf}$. Equate this to the gravitational pressure drop from Derivation 1:

$$
(1-\epsilon_{mf})\cdot(\rho_{s}-\rho_{f})\cdot g = \frac{150(1-\epsilon_{mf})^{2}\,\mu\,U_{mf}}{\epsilon_{mf}^{3}(\phi_{s}d_{p})^{2}} + \frac{1.75(1-\epsilon_{mf})\,\rho_{f}\,U_{mf}^{2}}{\epsilon_{mf}^{3}\,\phi_{s}\,d_{p}}
$$

**Step 3 — Divide both sides by $(1-\epsilon_{mf})$ to simplify:**

$$
(\rho_{s}-\rho_{f})\,g = \frac{150(1-\epsilon_{mf})\,\mu\,U_{mf}}{\epsilon_{mf}^{3}(\phi_{s}d_{p})^{2}} + \frac{1.75\,\rho_{f}\,U_{mf}^{2}}{\epsilon_{mf}^{3}\,\phi_{s}\,d_{p}}
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange as a quadratic in $U_{mf}$** of the form $A\,U_{mf}^{2} + B\,U_{mf} - C = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{1.75\,\rho_{f}}{\epsilon_{mf}^{3}\,\phi_{s}\,d_{p}} \\
B &= \frac{150(1-\epsilon_{mf})\,\mu}{\epsilon_{mf}^{3}(\phi_{s}\,d_{p})^{2}} \\
C &= (\rho_{s}-\rho_{f})\,g
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve using the quadratic formula** (positive root, since $U_{mf} > 0$):

$$
U_{mf} = \frac{-B + \sqrt{B^{2} + 4\,A\,C}}{2\,A}
$$

This gives the **most general form** of $U_{mf}$.

**Step 6 — Simplification: Laminar regime (small $Re_{p,mf}$).** If $Re_{p,mf} < 20$, the inertial term (proportional to $U_{mf}^{2}$) becomes negligible. The Ergun equation reduces to its viscous (Kozeny–Carman) limit. Setting $A \to 0$ in the quadratic solution, the equation collapses to:

$$
U_{mf} = \frac{C}{B} = \frac{(\phi_{s}\,d_{p})^{2}(\rho_{s}-\rho_{f})\,g}{150\,\mu}\cdot\frac{\epsilon_{mf}^{3}}{1-\epsilon_{mf}}
$$

This is **Equation (3)** in the formula sheet.

**Step 7 — Alternative: Wen & Yu General Correlation.** Wen and Yu (1966) found that the **average voidage and sphericity product** for most Geldart Group A and B particles gives a near-constant ratio:
$\dfrac{1}{\phi_{s}}\left(\dfrac{1}{\epsilon_{mf}^{3}}\right)^{1/2} \approx$ constant.

Substituting typical values ($\phi_s \approx 0.8$ to $1.0$ and $\epsilon_{mf} \approx 0.4$–$0.5$) into the quadratic and simplifying yields:

$$
\frac{d_{p}\,U_{mf}\,\rho_{f}}{\mu} = \sqrt{33.7^{2} + 0.0408\,Ar} - 33.7
$$

where $Ar = \dfrac{d_{p}^{3}\,\rho_{f}(\rho_{s}-\rho_{f})\,g}{\mu^{2}}$ is the Archimedes number. This compact form (**Equation 4**) is **valid across all Reynolds numbers** and is preferred in industry.

---

## Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Question)

**Problem:** A bed of uniform glass spheres ($d_p = 0.5$ mm, $\rho_s = 2500$ kg/m³) is fluidized with air at 25°C. Given $\epsilon_{mf} = 0.42$, $\phi_s = 0.88$, $\mu_{air} = 1.85 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $\rho_{air} = 1.184$ kg/m³, and $g = 9.81$ m/s², calculate $U_{mf}$ using the Wen & Yu correlation.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the Archimedes number:**
$$
Ar = \frac{(0.0005)^{3} \times 1.184 \times (2500 - 1.184) \times 9.81}{(1.85 \times 10^{-5})^{2}}
$$

Evaluating the numerator:
$$
(0.0005)^{3} = 1.25 \times 10^{-10}
$$
$$
1.25 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.184 = 1.48 \times 10^{-10}
$$
$$
1.48 \times 10^{-10} \times 2498.82 = 3.698 \times 10^{-7}
$$
$$
3.698 \times 10^{-7} \times 9.81 = 3.628 \times 10^{-6}
$$

Evaluating the denominator:
$$
(1.85 \times 10^{-5})^{2} = 3.4225 \times 10^{-10}
$$

Therefore:
$$
Ar = \frac{3.628 \times 10^{-6}}{3.4225 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 10600
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $Re_{p,mf}$:**
$$
Re_{p,mf} = \sqrt{33.7^{2} + 0.0408 \times 10600} - 33.7
$$
$$
= \sqrt{1135.69 + 432.48} - 33.7
$$
$$
= \sqrt{1568.17} - 33.7
$$
$$
= 39.60 - 33.7 = 5.90
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $U_{mf}$:**
$$
U_{mf} = \frac{Re_{p,mf} \cdot \mu}{d_p \cdot \rho_f} = \frac{5.90 \times 1.85 \times 10^{-5}}{0.0005 \times 1.184}
$$
$$
U_{mf} = \frac{1.092 \times 10^{-4}}{5.92 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 0.184 \text{ m/s}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The value $Re_{p,mf} = 5.90$ is **less than 20**, so the laminar form (Equation 3) would have given a very close answer — a useful self-check for KTU students.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Fluidization Curve

The following Mermaid diagram maps the five operating regions of the pressure drop vs velocity curve for **uniformly sized particles**, including the physical state of the bed and the governing equations at each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["U0 = 0: Initial State - Bed at Rest"] --> regionA["REGION A to B: PACKED FIXED BED\nDeltaP rises with U0\nGoverned by Ergun Equation"]
    regionA --> pointB{"Incipient Point\nU0 reaches Umf?"}
    pointB -->|Yes| inflectionB["POINT B: MINIMUM FLUIDIZATION\nDrag Force equals Buoyant Weight\nDeltaPmf = 1-eps_mf times rho_s-rho_f times g times L"]
    inflectionB --> regionBC["REGION B to C: ONSET OF FLUIDIZATION\nDeltaP approximately constant\nBed starts to expand\nBubbles begin to form"]
    regionBC --> regionCD["REGION C to D: FULLY FLUIDIZED BED\nDeltaP constant equal to Weight per Area\nBed height increases with U0\nIntense particle mixing"]
    regionCD --> pointD{"U0 reaches Ut?"}
    pointD -->|Yes| inflectionD["POINT D: TERMINAL VELOCITY\nParticles begin to be entrained\nPneumatic transport begins"]
    inflectionD --> regionDE["REGION D to E: ENTRAINMENT AND PNEUMATIC CONVEYING\nDeltaP rises with U0\nParticle loss occurs above TDH"]

    regionA -.->|"Define: U0 = Q divided by A"| note1["Design Note:\nU0 is superficial velocity, not interstitial"]
    inflectionB -.->|"Boundary Condition"| note2["Boundary:\nU0 = Umf marks transition from fixed to fluidized"]
    regionCD -.->|"Key Insight"| note3["Design Insight:\nDeltaP does NOT increase with U0 in this region"]
```

## Mermaid Block Diagram: Inputs and Outputs of the Fluidization System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input1["Particle Properties\nd_p, rho_s, phi_s"] --> mix
    input2["Fluid Properties\nrho_f, mu"] --> mix
    input3["Bed Properties\nepsilon, L, A"] --> mix
    input4["Operating Variable\nU0: Superficial Velocity"] --> mix
    mix["Fluidization\nSystem"] --> output1["Region A-B\nFixed Bed, DeltaP increases"]
    mix --> output2["Point B\nUmf, DeltaPmf constant"]
    mix --> output3["Region B-D\nFluidized Bed, DeltaP constant"]
    mix --> output4["Point D\nUt, Entrainment Onset"]
    mix --> output5["Region D-E\nPneumatic Transport"]

    output1 -.->|"Ergun Equation"| er1
    output2 -.->|"Force Balance"| er2
    output3 -.->|"Bed Weight per Area"| er3
    output5 -.->|"Elutriation Analysis"| er5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid node ID convention used here:** All node IDs (e.g., `startA`, `regionBC`, `pointD`) are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to avoid reserved-keyword conflicts. Labels are raw uppercase text — no markdown bold or italics inside the quoted strings.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define minimum fluidization velocity. What is its significance in the design of a fluidized bed?**

**Model Answer:**
The **minimum fluidization velocity (U_mf)** is defined as the superficial fluid velocity at which the drag force exerted by the fluid on the particles just equals the buoyant (net) weight of the particles in the bed. At this velocity, the bed transitions from a **packed (fixed) state** to a **fluidized state**, and the pressure drop first reaches its **maximum steady value**:

$$
\Delta P_{mf} = (1-\epsilon_{mf})\,L_{mf}\,(\rho_s - \rho_f)\,g
$$

**Significance:** (i) It is the **lower operating limit** of a fluidized bed — operation below U_mf means the bed is not truly fluidized. (ii) It determines the **design velocity range** ($U_0/U_{mf}$ ratio, typically 1.5–10). (iii) It governs **fluidization quality** — closer to U_mf gives smoother, particulate fluidization; higher ratios favor bubbling. (iv) It is essential for **sizing blowers/compressors** and **predicting entrainment**.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Differentiate between a fixed bed and a fluidized bed in terms of pressure drop behavior.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Fixed Bed | Fluidized Bed |
|---|---|---|
| **Pressure drop vs velocity** | Increases with velocity (Ergun equation: $\Delta P \propto U_0$) | Approximately constant — equals bed weight/area |
| **Bed voidage (ε)** | Constant (typically 0.4) | Increases with velocity (0.4 → 0.6+) |
| **Bed height (L)** | Constant | Increases (bed expands) |
| **Particle motion** | Stationary | Vigorous mixing, bubbles rise |
| **Heat transfer coefficient** | Moderate | High (10–100× fixed bed) |
| **Phase state** | Solid-dominated | Fluid-dominated, "liquid-like" behavior |

> Key concept: In a **fixed bed**, the gas must thread through narrow channels, so $\Delta P$ rises. Once fluidized, the gas flows through *larger* bubble channels, and the bed's weight is "suspended," so additional flow only causes expansion, not pressure rise.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **pressure drop vs superficial velocity diagram** for uniformly sized particles. Identify all regions and explain the physical state of the bed in each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Sketch (verbal description for the answer sheet):**
A curve is drawn with $U_0$ on the X-axis and $\Delta P$ on the Y-axis.

- **Segment A–B:** Curve rises from origin, $\Delta P$ increasing with $U_0$ — this is the **fixed (packed) bed** region, governed by the **Ergun equation**. Particles are stationary.
- **Peak at point B:** Marks the **incipient fluidization point** at $U_{mf}$. The curve may have a small "overshoot" (often due to inter-particle friction in uniformly sized beds) before settling.
- **Segment B–C:** $\Delta P$ becomes **constant** (or slightly decreases). The bed is now **particulate fluidized** with voidage increasing.
- **Segment C–D:** $\Delta P$ remains **constant at $\Delta P = W/A$**, but the bed **continues to expand** in height. This is the **bubbling fluidized bed** region.
- **Point D:** The fluid velocity reaches the **terminal velocity $U_t$** of the particles.
- **Segment D–E:** $\Delta P$ **rises again** as the bed becomes dilute and **particles are entrained** — this is the **pneumatic transport** region.

The vertical axis intercept at point B is:
$$
\Delta P_{mf} = (1 - \epsilon_{mf})\,L_{mf}\,(\rho_s - \rho_f)\,g
$$

**(b)** A bed of spherical catalyst particles ($d_p = 350\ \mu\text{m}$, $\rho_s = 1800$ kg/m³, $\phi_s = 0.95$, $\epsilon_{mf} = 0.48$) is fluidized by air at 80°C ($\rho_f = 1.0$ kg/m³, $\mu = 2.1 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s). Determine the **minimum fluidization velocity** using the **Ergun equation**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Set up the quadratic.** Equating Ergun equation to the gravitational pressure drop:

$$
(1-\epsilon_{mf})(\rho_s - \rho_f)g = \frac{150(1-\epsilon_{mf})^{2}\mu U_{mf}}{\epsilon_{mf}^{3}(\phi_s d_p)^{2}} + \frac{1.75(1-\epsilon_{mf})\rho_f U_{mf}^{2}}{\epsilon_{mf}^{3}\phi_s d_p}
$$

Compute each numerical term:

$$
\epsilon_{mf}^{3} = (0.48)^{3} = 0.1106
$$
$$
1 - \epsilon_{mf} = 0.52
$$
$$
\phi_s d_p = 0.95 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-4} = 3.325 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}
$$
$$
(\phi_s d_p)^{2} = 1.106 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}^{2}
$$
$$
\rho_s - \rho_f = 1800 - 1.0 = 1799\ \text{kg/m}^{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute A, B, C coefficients:**

$$
A = \frac{1.75 \times 1.0}{0.1106 \times 3.325 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{1.75}{3.677 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.759 \times 10^{4}
$$

$$
B = \frac{150 \times 0.52 \times 2.1 \times 10^{-5}}{0.1106 \times 1.106 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{1.638 \times 10^{-3}}{1.223 \times 10^{-8}} = 1.339 \times 10^{5}
$$

$$
C = (1799)(9.81) = 1.765 \times 10^{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the quadratic $A U_{mf}^{2} + B U_{mf} - C = 0$:**

$$
U_{mf} = \frac{-B + \sqrt{B^{2} + 4AC}}{2A} = \frac{-1.339 \times 10^{5} + \sqrt{(1.339 \times 10^{5})^{2} + 4(4.759 \times 10^{4})(1.765 \times 10^{4})}}{2(4.759 \times 10^{4})}
$$

$$
\sqrt{B^{2} + 4AC} = \sqrt{1.793 \times 10^{10} + 3.360 \times 10^{9}} = \sqrt{2.129 \times 10^{10}} = 1.459 \times 10^{5}
$$

$$
U_{mf} = \frac{-1.339 \times 10^{5} + 1.459 \times 10^{5}}{9.518 \times 10^{4}} = \frac{1.20 \times 10^{4}}{9.518 \times 10^{4}} = 0.0126\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Result:** $U_{mf} \approx 0.0126$ m/s (≈ 1.26 cm/s)

**Verification:** $Re_{p,mf} = \dfrac{(3.5 \times 10^{-4})(0.0126)(1.0)}{2.1 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 0.21 < 20$ — laminar regime confirmed, so the laminar form (Equation 3) would also have been valid.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **minimum fluidization velocity** starting from the **Ergun equation**. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Assumptions:**
1. The particles are **uniformly sized** and **spherical** (or with constant sphericity $\phi_s$).
2. The bed is **isothermal** and **one-dimensional**.
3. The fluid behaves as an **incompressible continuum** (valid for low Mach number).
4. **Wall effects** are negligible (column diameter $\gg d_p$).
5. At the moment of incipient fluidization, the bed is **mechanically just-stable** — particles are about to move but not yet fully suspended.

**Step 2 — Start with the Ergun equation** for a fixed bed:

$$
\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{150(1-\epsilon)^{2}\mu U_0}{\epsilon^{3}(\phi_s d_p)^{2}} + \frac{1.75(1-\epsilon)\rho_f U_0^{2}}{\epsilon^{3}\phi_s d_p} \quad \text{...(I)}
$$

**Step 3 — Force balance on the bed.** At incipient fluidization, drag = buoyant weight:

$$
\Delta P_{mf} = (1-\epsilon_{mf})(\rho_s - \rho_f) g L_{mf} \quad \text{...(II)}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate (I) and (II)** with $\epsilon = \epsilon_{mf}$ and $U_0 = U_{mf}$:

$$
(1-\epsilon_{mf})(\rho_s - \rho_f) g = \frac{150(1-\epsilon_{mf})^{2}\mu U_{mf}}{\epsilon_{mf}^{3}(\phi_s d_p)^{2}} + \frac{1.75(1-\epsilon_{mf})\rho_f U_{mf}^{2}}{\epsilon_{mf}^{3}\phi_s d_p}
$$

**Step 5 — Divide by $(1-\epsilon_{mf})$ and rearrange as a quadratic:**

$$
\frac{1.75 \rho_f}{\epsilon_{mf}^{3}\phi_s d_p} U_{mf}^{2} + \frac{150(1-\epsilon_{mf})\mu}{\epsilon_{mf}^{3}(\phi_s d_p)^{2}} U_{mf} - (\rho_s - \rho_f)g = 0
$$

**Step 6 — Solve** for $U_{mf} > 0$:

$$
\boxed{\; U_{mf} = \frac{-B + \sqrt{B^{2} + 4AC}}{2A} \;}
$$

where $A = \dfrac{1.75\rho_f}{\epsilon_{mf}^{3}\phi_s d_p}$, $B = \dfrac{150(1-\epsilon_{mf})\mu}{\epsilon_{mf}^{3}(\phi_s d_p)^{2}}$, $C = (\rho_s-\rho_f)g$.

**Step 7 — For laminar regime** ($Re_{p,mf} < 20$), the quadratic term becomes negligible, reducing to:

$$
U_{mf} = \frac{(\phi_s d_p)^{2}(\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{150 \mu} \cdot \frac{\epsilon_{mf}^{3}}{1-\epsilon_{mf}}
$$

**(b)** Compare the **fixed bed**, **fluidized bed**, and **pneumatic transport** regimes with respect to (i) pressure drop behavior, (ii) bed voidage, (iii) particle motion, and (iv) typical industrial applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Fixed Bed | Fluidized Bed | Pneumatic Transport |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Velocity range** | $U_0 < U_{mf}$ | $U_{mf} \leq U_0 \leq U_t$ | $U_0 > U_t$ |
| **Pressure drop** | $\Delta P$ rises with $U_0$ (Ergun) | $\Delta P \approx$ constant = W/A | $\Delta P$ rises again (acceleration) |
| **Bed voidage (ε)** | 0.38 – 0.45 (fixed) | 0.45 – 0.75 (expands) | $\approx 0.95$+ (very dilute) |
| **Particle motion** | Stationary | Vigorous mixing, bubbling | Fully suspended, conveyed |
| **ΔP governing equation** | Ergun | $W/A$ balance | Momentum balance + drag |
| **Heat transfer** | Moderate | **Very high** (high $h$) | Low (dilute phase) |
| **Mixing** | Poor (channelling) | Excellent (solid + gas) | Plug flow (gas); disperse (solid) |
| **Industrial uses** | Filtration, catalytic packed beds | FCC, coal combustion, drying, granulation | Pneumatic conveyors, CFB boilers, pulverized fuel lines |

> **Engineering insight:** The fluidized bed "sweet spot" is the **operating window** between $U_{mf}$ and $U_t$ (often taken as $0.5 U_t$ to stay safely below entrainment). Within this region, the user enjoys high heat/mass transfer, uniform temperature, and easy solids handling — but must guard against **channeling**, **slugging**, and **entrainment losses**.

---

## ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks (and how to avoid it):**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion errors in $d_p$:** Always convert particle diameter to **meters** before substitution. Mixing mm and m is the single most common numerical error in PECHT415.
> 2. **Using the wrong Ergun form:** The full Ergun equation has **two terms** (viscous + inertial). Do not drop the second term unless you verify $Re_{p,mf} < 20$.
> 3. **Confusing $U_{mf}$ with $U_t$:** These are *two completely different velocities*! $U_{mf}$ is the minimum to fluidize; $U_t$ is the terminal (free-fall) velocity. $U_{mf} < U_t$ always.
> 4. **Wrong sign in the quadratic:** Use **$(-B + \sqrt{B^2 + 4AC})/2A$** (positive root). The negative root is unphysical.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert $\rho_s - \rho_f$** to SI (kg/m³) and $g$ in m/s² — a common slip when copying problem data.
> 6. **Mixing up bed voidage ε vs. solids fraction (1 − ε):** The Ergun equation uses **ε** (fluid volume fraction); the weight balance uses **(1 − ε)** (solids volume fraction).
> 7. **Drawing a "knee" without a clear plateau:** The diagram *must* show a horizontal (or near-horizontal) plateau in the B–C–D region. Sketching it as a continuous rise is **wrong** and will lose 1–2 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (read this in the last 10 minutes before the exam):**
>
> - **Five regions of the fluidization curve:** Fixed Bed (ΔP ↑) → Incipient Point (U_mf) → Fluidized Bed (ΔP constant) → Terminal Point (U_t) → Pneumatic Transport (ΔP ↑).
> - **Ergun equation** has a **viscous term** (∝ U) and an **inertial term** (∝ U²). The viscous term dominates at low Re; the inertial term at high Re.
> - **ΔP at U_mf = (1 − ε_mf)(ρ_s − ρ_f)g L_mf** — this is a *force balance*, not a flow equation.
> - **Three formulas for U_mf**, in order of generality:
>   1. Quadratic from full Ergun (general)
>   2. $U_{mf} = \dfrac{(\phi_s d_p)^2 (\rho_s - \rho_f) g \,\epsilon_{mf}^{3}}{150 \mu (1-\epsilon_{mf})}$ (laminar, $Re_{p,mf} < 20$)
>   3. $Re_{p,mf} = \sqrt{33.7^2 + 0.0408 Ar} - 33.7$ (Wen & Yu, all Re)
> - **Archimedes number** $Ar = d_p^{3}\rho_f(\rho_s - \rho_f)g / \mu^2$ — appears in both $U_{mf}$ and $U_t$ correlations.
> - **Terminal velocity** uses **Stokes' Law** for $Re_p < 0.1$ and the **general $C_D$ correlation** for higher Re.
> - **Uniformly sized particles** ⇒ clean, well-defined $U_{mf}$ and $U_t$; the diagram has a sharp inflection.
> - **Typical fluidization ratio:** Industrial beds operate at $U_0 / U_{mf} = 1.5$ to $10$, often ~2–3 for bubbling beds and ~5–10 for circulating fluidized beds.
> - **Self-check rule:** Always compute $Re_{p,mf} = d_p U_{mf} \rho_f / \mu$ to verify which form of the Ergun equation (laminar or general) you should have used.
> - **Memorize these typical values for sanity-check:** $\epsilon_{mf} \approx 0.4$–$0.5$ for spheres; $\phi_s \approx 0.6$–$0.9$ for natural particles, $\phi_s = 1.0$ for spheres.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Minimum Bubbling Velocity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept of Minimum Bubbling Velocity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Minimum Bubbling Velocity (U_mb)** is defined as the **superficial gas velocity** at which the first discrete gas bubble (void) is visually observed at the surface of a fluidized bed, marking the transition from *homogeneous (particulate) fluidization* to *heterogeneous (bubbling) fluidization*.

In rigorous terms:
- For **Geldart Group A particles** (aeratable powders), a stable region of **homogeneous expansion** exists where the bed expands uniformly without bubbles. The velocity at the **upper limit** of this regime is the minimum bubbling velocity, U_mb.
- For **Geldart Group B, D particles**, U_mb is approximately equal to U_mf, and bubbling occurs almost immediately upon fluidization.
- For **Geldart Group C particles**, bubbles may form in channels (channelling) before proper fluidization, making U_mb non-physical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> U_mb is a critical design parameter for industrial fluidized bed catalytic reactors (FCC, polyethylene, acrylonitrile). Operating just above U_mb is often preferred for uniform gas-solid contact without bypassing through large bubbles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Condition:**
> $$\boxed{U_{mb} \geq U_{mf}}$$
> Equality holds for **Geldart B** and **D** powders, while strict inequality holds for **Geldart A** powders.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Understanding)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Sand in a Jar"**
>
> Imagine a transparent glass jar filled with fine, dry sand, connected to a gentle air blower at the bottom.
> 1. **No Air:** The sand is a *packed bed* — rigid, with void spaces between grains.
> 2. **Slow Airflow:** As the air is gradually increased, nothing visible happens until a critical point (U_mf) where the entire bed becomes "alive" and starts to **flow smoothly like a viscous liquid**. No bubbles yet — this is the **particulate (homogeneous) expansion regime**.
> 3. **Slightly Higher Airflow:** Suddenly, you see a **distinct air pocket** rising rapidly through the sand, bursting at the surface like a bubble in soda. **The instant this first bubble appears = U_mb**.
>
> Now, if the sand grains are very fine and light (e.g., talcum powder or an FCC catalyst), the *gap* between "fully fluidized" and "first bubble" is significant. If the sand is coarse (e.g., beach sand), bubbles appear almost the same moment the bed fluidizes.

---

### 1.3 Physical Significance and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Minimum Fluidization Velocity | U_mf | 0.001 – 0.5 | **m/s** |
| Minimum Bubbling Velocity | U_mb | 0.005 – 1.0 | **m/s** |
| Ratio (Geldart A) | U_mb / U_mf | 1.05 – 2.0 | dimensionless |
| Archimedes Number | Ar | 1 – 10⁸ | dimensionless |
| Particle Reynolds Number | Re_p | 10⁻³ – 10³ | dimensionless |
| Gas Viscosity (Air, 25°C) | μ | **1.8 × 10⁻⁵** | **Pa·s** |
| Air Density (STP) | ρ_g | **1.2** | **kg/m³** |

---

### 1.4 Why Minimum Bubbling Velocity Matters

1. **Catalyst Selection:** Industrial FCC catalysts are designed as Geldart A powders precisely to exploit the *uniform expansion zone* (U_mf < U < U_mb) before bubbling, ensuring **high gas-solid contact efficiency**.
2. **Reactor Stability:** Operating below U_mb prevents bubble-induced bypassing of reactant gas.
3. **Design Constraint:** U_mb is the **lower limit of the bubbling fluidization regime** — fluidized bed combustors and dryers typically operate well above this value.
4. **Determines Flow Regime Map:** A *fluidization flow regime map* (U vs d_p) is constructed using U_mb, U_mf, and terminal velocity U_t boundaries.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Bed Expansion Sequence: From Packed to Bubbling

When superficial gas velocity U is increased gradually from zero, the following sequence occurs in a Geldart A bed:

1. **Packed Bed (U < U_mf):** Gas flows through interstices; pressure drop follows **Carman-Kozeny / Burke-Plummer** equation.
2. **Minimum Fluidization (U = U_mf):** Drag force = Weight of bed. Bed just begins to behave as a fluid.
3. **Particulate / Homogeneous Expansion (U_mf < U < U_mb):** The bed expands smoothly and uniformly. **No visible bubbles.** This is the **"Grace window"** — exploited in FCC risers and Geldart A reactors.
4. **Minimum Bubbling (U = U_mb):** First discrete gas bubbles nucleate. The bed is now a **two-phase system**: dense (emulsion) phase + bubble phase.
5. **Bubbling Fluidization (U > U_mb):** Increasing bubble size and frequency with U.
6. **Slugging / Turbulent Fluidization (U much greater than U_mb):** Bubbles coalesce, eventually leading to pneumatic transport at U_t.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theoretical Justification for U_mb > U_mf:**
> The extra gas flow above U_mf in the particulate regime is accommodated by **bed expansion** (rising in the emulsion phase). The bed continues to expand until a critical voidage ε_mb is reached. At ε_mb, the bed can no longer absorb the gas uniformly, and the **first bubble detaches** to relieve the excess volumetric gas flow.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following correlations are the **must-know equations** for PECHT415 board examination questions on this topic. All are derived or referenced from canonical fluidization texts (Kunii & Levenspiel; Geldart).

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Expression | Validity / Range | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Archimedes Number | $Ar = \dfrac{d_p^{3} \, \rho_g \, (\rho_p - \rho_g) \, g}{\mu^{2}}$ | All | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Particle Reynolds (at U_mf) | $Re_{mf} = \dfrac{d_p \, \rho_g \, U_{mf}}{\mu}$ | All | Dimensionless |
| 3 | **Wen & Yu Correlation** (U_mf) | $Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^{2} + 0.0408 \, Ar} - 33.7$ | Re_mf ≤ 1000 | Most widely used |
| 4 | Ergun Equation (general ΔP) | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{150 \mu (1-\varepsilon)^{2}}{\varepsilon^{3} d_p^{2}} U + \dfrac{1.75 (1-\varepsilon)}{\varepsilon^{3} d_p} \rho_g U^{2}$ | 1 < Re < 2000 | Combine with ΔP = (ρ_p − ρ_g)(1 − ε_mf)g |
| 5 | **Abrahamsen–Geldart (A&G) Correlation** (U_mb) — Regime 1 | $\dfrac{U_{mb} - U_{mf}}{U_{mb}} = \dfrac{0.0859}{Re_{p}^{0.343}}$ | $Re_{p} \leq 5$ | Geldart A, fine powders |
| 6 | **Abrahamsen–Geldart (A&G) Correlation** — Regime 2 | $\dfrac{U_{mb} - U_{mf}}{U_{mb}} = \dfrac{0.0206}{Re_{p}^{0.119}}$ | $5 < Re_{p} \leq 70$ | Geldart A, medium |
| 7 | Abrahamsen–Geldart — Regime 3 | $U_{mb} = U_{mf}$ | $Re_{p} > 70$ | Geldart B/D region |
| 8 | Richardson–Zaki (Bed expansion) | $U = U_{t} \, \varepsilon^{n}$ | Particulate regime | n ≈ 2.39 for Re < 0.1 |
| 9 | Bubble Initial Size (Mori & Wen) | $d_{b0} = 1.38 [g^{-1} A_{t} (U - U_{mf})]^{0.4}$ | Bubbling regime | m, A_t in m² |
| 10 | Gas Density at STP (Air) | $\rho_g = \dfrac{P M}{R T}$ | Ideal gas | kg/m³ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Key Substitution Rule (KTU 2024 Examiner's Trick):**
> In the A&G correlations, **Re_p is evaluated at U_mf**, not at U_mb. Many students lose 2 marks by mistakenly using U_mb — but U_mb is *unknown* and is what we are solving for. This requires an *iterative* or *two-step* solution: first compute U_mf → then compute Re_mf → then solve for U_mb.

---

### 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **FCC Units (Petroleum Refining):** Operating in the particulate regime (G_mf < U < U_mb) ensures catalyst is fluidized uniformly, giving high conversion. Geldart A catalysts (e.g., equilibrium FCC catalyst, mean size 60–80 μm, ρ_p ≈ 900–1400 kg/m³) are chosen deliberately.
- **Polymerization Reactors (Gas-Phase PE/PP):** Operate in the bubbling regime (U > U_mb) with controlled bubble size for heat removal and polymer particle growth.
- **Fluidized Bed Combustors:** Operate well above U_mb in the turbulent regime, where heat transfer is maximum.
- **Pharmaceutical Drying & Coating:** Operate near U_mb for gentle, uniform particle motion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Wen & Yu Correlation for U_mf from the Ergun Equation

The starting point is the **Ergun equation** (valid for the entire range 0.1 < Re < 2000) describing the pressure drop across a packed bed:

$$
\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{150 \mu (1-\varepsilon)^{2}}{\varepsilon^{3} d_p^{2}} U + \dfrac{1.75 (1-\varepsilon)}{\varepsilon^{3} d_p} \rho_g U^{2}
$$

**Step 1:** At minimum fluidization, the pressure drop equals the buoyant weight of the bed per unit area:

$$
\Delta P_{mf} = (1 - \varepsilon_{mf})(\rho_p - \rho_g) \, g \, L
$$

**Step 2:** Equate the Ergun equation (LHS) with the buoyant weight expression:

$$
(1 - \varepsilon_{mf})(\rho_p - \rho_g) g = \dfrac{150 \mu (1-\varepsilon_{mf})^{2}}{\varepsilon_{mf}^{3} d_p^{2}} U_{mf} + \dfrac{1.75 (1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{\varepsilon_{mf}^{3} d_p} \rho_g U_{mf}^{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Divide both sides by $(1 - \varepsilon_{mf})$ to simplify:

$$
(\rho_p - \rho_g) g = \dfrac{150 \mu (1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{\varepsilon_{mf}^{3} d_p^{2}} U_{mf} + \dfrac{1.75}{\varepsilon_{mf}^{3} d_p} \rho_g U_{mf}^{2}
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply both sides by $d_p^{3} \rho_g / \mu^{2}$ to non-dimensionalize, introducing Ar and Re_mf:

$$
Ar = \dfrac{d_p^{3} \rho_g (\rho_p - \rho_g) g}{\mu^{2}}, \quad Re_{mf} = \dfrac{d_p \rho_g U_{mf}}{\mu}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the dimensionless groups:

$$
Ar = \dfrac{150 (1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{\varepsilon_{mf}^{3}} \, Re_{mf} + \dfrac{1.75}{\varepsilon_{mf}^{3}} \, Re_{mf}^{2}
$$

**Step 6:** Wen & Yu (1966) empirically fitted the constants by analysing data for many powders and found that the voidage factor effectively reduces to two universal coefficients. Setting the voidage function to unity in the leading-order form (or absorbing it into the constants) gives:

$$
\dfrac{1.75}{\varepsilon_{mf}^{3}} \, Re_{mf}^{2} + \dfrac{150(1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{\varepsilon_{mf}^{3}} \, Re_{mf} - Ar = 0
$$

Solving this **quadratic in Re_mf** with Wen & Yu's fitted empirical constants:

$$
\boxed{Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^{2} + 0.0408 \, Ar} - 33.7}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Abrahamsen & Geldart (A&G) Correlation — Physical Basis

A&G (1980) studied many Geldart A powders and observed that the *fractional excess* velocity above U_mf (normalized by U_mb) depends only on the particle Reynolds number Re_p evaluated at U_mf:

$$
\dfrac{U_{mb} - U_{mf}}{U_{mb}} = f(Re_{mf})
$$

By non-linear regression over ~120 data sets, they obtained:

$$
\dfrac{U_{mb} - U_{mf}}{U_{mb}} = \dfrac{C_1}{Re_{mf}^{C_2}}
$$

with piecewise constants as listed in the formula sheet (Section 2.2). Rearranging:

$$
1 - \dfrac{U_{mf}}{U_{mb}} = \dfrac{C_1}{Re_{mf}^{C_2}}
$$

$$
U_{mb} = \dfrac{U_{mf}}{1 - \dfrac{C_1}{Re_{mf}^{C_2}}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **This is the standard closed-form used in all KTU university exam numerical problems involving U_mb.**

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Step-by-Step)

**Problem:** A fluidized bed uses an FCC catalyst (Geldart A) with the following properties:
- Particle diameter: $d_p = 60 \, \mu m = 60 \times 10^{-6} \, m$
- Particle density: $\rho_p = 1100 \, kg/m^{3}$
- Gas (air) density: $\rho_g = 1.2 \, kg/m^{3}$
- Gas viscosity: $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \, Pa \cdot s$
- Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \, m/s^{2}$

**Find:** (i) U_mf using Wen & Yu, (ii) Re_mf, (iii) U_mb using Abrahamsen–Geldart.

---

#### Step (a): Compute the Archimedes Number Ar

$$
Ar = \dfrac{d_p^{3} \, \rho_g \, (\rho_p - \rho_g) \, g}{\mu^{2}}
$$

**Compute $d_p^{3}$:**

$$
d_p^{3} = (60 \times 10^{-6})^{3} = 216000 \times 10^{-18} = 2.16 \times 10^{-10} \, m^{3}
$$

**Compute $(\rho_p - \rho_g)$:**

$$
\rho_p - \rho_g = 1100 - 1.2 = 1098.8 \, kg/m^{3}
$$

**Compute numerator:**

$$
2.16 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.2 \times 1098.8 \times 9.81
$$

$$
= 2.16 \times 10^{-10} \times 12932.0 = 2.794 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Compute denominator:**

$$
\mu^{2} = (1.8 \times 10^{-5})^{2} = 3.24 \times 10^{-10}
$$

**Therefore:**

$$
Ar = \dfrac{2.794 \times 10^{-6}}{3.24 \times 10^{-10}} = 8623.5
$$

---

#### Step (b): Compute Re_mf using Wen & Yu

$$
Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^{2} + 0.0408 \times Ar} - 33.7
$$

$$
= \sqrt{1135.69 + 0.0408 \times 8623.5} - 33.7
$$

$$
= \sqrt{1135.69 + 351.84} - 33.7
$$

$$
= \sqrt{1487.53} - 33.7
$$

$$
= 38.57 - 33.7 = 4.87
$$

Since $Re_{mf} = 4.87 < 5$, we use **A&G Regime 1** correlation.

---

#### Step (c): Compute U_mf in m/s

$$
U_{mf} = \dfrac{Re_{mf} \cdot \mu}{d_p \cdot \rho_g} = \dfrac{4.87 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5}}{60 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.2}
$$

**Numerator:**

$$
4.87 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5} = 8.766 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Denominator:**

$$
60 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.2 = 7.2 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Therefore:**

$$
U_{mf} = \dfrac{8.766 \times 10^{-5}}{7.2 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.2175 \times 10^{0} = 1.218 \, m/s
$$

---

#### Step (d): Compute U_mb using Abrahamsen–Geldart (Regime 1)

$$
\dfrac{U_{mb} - U_{mf}}{U_{mb}} = \dfrac{0.0859}{Re_{mf}^{0.343}}
$$

**Compute $Re_{mf}^{0.343}$:**

$$
4.87^{0.343} = e^{0.343 \cdot \ln(4.87)} = e^{0.343 \times 1.5832} = e^{0.5430} = 1.7212
$$

**Therefore:**

$$
\dfrac{U_{mb} - U_{mf}}{U_{mb}} = \dfrac{0.0859}{1.7212} = 0.0499
$$

**Rearranging:**

$$
1 - \dfrac{U_{mf}}{U_{mb}} = 0.0499
$$

$$
\dfrac{U_{mf}}{U_{mb}} = 0.9501
$$

$$
U_{mb} = \dfrac{1.218}{0.9501} = 1.282 \, m/s
$$

---

#### Step (e): Verify and Interpret

$$
\boxed{U_{mf} = 1.218 \, m/s, \quad U_{mb} = 1.282 \, m/s, \quad \dfrac{U_{mb}}{U_{mf}} = 1.053}
$$

The ratio **U_mb / U_mf ≈ 1.05** is consistent with a **Geldart Group A** aeratable powder. The bed will expand homogeneously in the velocity range $1.218 \leq U \leq 1.282 \, m/s$ before the first bubble forms.

---

### 3.4 Validation Against Geldart Powder Classification

**Geldart's empirical boundary** between A and B powders is approximately:

$$
\dfrac{\rho_p - \rho_g}{\rho_g} \geq 125 \quad (\text{Group B and above})
$$

For our case:

$$
\dfrac{1098.8}{1.2} = 915.7
$$

Since **915.7 ≫ 125**, the density term alone would suggest Group B (where U_mb = U_mf). However, with $d_p = 60 \, \mu m$ (small particle), the powder falls into **Group A** by virtue of the size criterion. This illustrates that classification depends on **both** density and size — a common KTU question type.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geldart A vs B Decision Rule (Combined Criterion):**
>
> - $d_p < 100 \, \mu m$ AND $\rho_p < 1400 \, kg/m^{3}$ → **Group A** (U_mb > U_mf)
> - $d_p > 100 \, \mu m$ AND $\rho_p > 1400 \, kg/m^{3}$ → **Group B** (U_mb = U_mf)
> - $d_p < 20 \, \mu m$ → **Group C** (cohesive, channelling)
> - $d_p > 600 \, \mu m$ AND $\rho_p > 1400 \, kg/m^{3}$ → **Group D** (spoutable)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fluidization Flow Regime Map (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Packed Bed U = 0] --> B[Apply Airflow: Increase U gradually]
    B --> C{U compared to U_mf?}
    C -->|U < U_mf| D[Packed Bed Regime: Gas flows in interstices, bed stationary, deltaP rises with U]
    C -->|U = U_mf| E[Minimum Fluidization: Drag = Weight, bed just lifts]
    E --> F{Particle Type?}
    F -->|Geldart A| G[Particulate / Homogeneous Expansion: U_mf less than U less than U_mb, bed expands smoothly, no bubbles]
    F -->|Geldart B or D| H[Bubbling Bed starts immediately: U_mb equals U_mf]
    F -->|Geldart C| I[Cohesive Channelling: Gas bypasses, U_mb not meaningful]
    G --> J[U reaches U_mb: First discrete bubble detaches from distributor]
    H --> J
    J --> K[U greater than U_mb: Bubbling Fluidization, two phase: emulsion plus bubble phase]
    K --> L[U increases further: Slugging or Turbulent Regime, bubble coalescence and breakup]
    L --> M[U approaches U_t: Pneumatic Transport, particles entrained with gas]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#ffe1f5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:3px
    style K fill:#ffe1b3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#d9b3ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Geldart Classification Map for U_mb Relevance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Powder Sample] --> Q{Median Size d_p and Density rho_p}
    Q -->|d_p less than 20 um| C[Geldart C: Cohesive, U_mb meaningless due to channelling]
    Q -->|20 to 100 um AND rho_p less than 1400| A[Geldart A: Aeratable, U_mb greater than U_mf, use A&G correlation]
    Q -->|d_p about 100 um AND rho_p about 1400 to 4000| B[Geldart B: Sand-like, U_mb equals U_mf]
    Q -->|d_p greater than 600 um AND rho_p greater than 1400| D[Geldart D: Spoutable, U_mb equals U_mf, may slug at moderate U]

    style A fill:#a8e6a8,stroke:#060,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#ffd9a8,stroke:#a60,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#666,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#a8c8ff,stroke:#006,stroke-width:2px
    style P fill:#fff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style Q fill:#fff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Pressure Drop vs. Velocity Signature Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Region 1: Fixed Bed, deltaP rises linearly with U] --> P2[Peak: At U_mf, deltaP attains maximum equals bed weight]
    P2 --> P3[Drop: deltaP falls slightly as bed lifts and rearranges]
    P3 --> P4[Region 2: Particulate Expansion, deltaP remains constant equals bed weight per area]
    P4 --> P5[Region 3: Bubbling Bed, deltaP fluctuates around bed weight per area due to bubble passages]
    P5 --> P6[Region 4: Turbulent and Transport, deltaP may decrease as particles entrain]

    style P1 fill:#e7f3ff,stroke:#333
    style P2 fill:#ffe7e7,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px
    style P3 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#333
    style P4 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#060
    style P5 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#900
    style P6 fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#333
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 / Model 1]**

**Q1. Define minimum bubbling velocity. Under what conditions does a fluidized bed exhibit a non-zero value of (U_mb − U_mf)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Minimum bubbling velocity, $U_{mb}$, is the superficial gas velocity at which the first discrete gas bubble is observed at the surface of a fluidized bed, marking the transition from homogeneous (particulate) expansion to heterogeneous (bubbling) fluidization.

**Condition for non-zero (U_mb − U_mf) (2 marks):** A non-zero difference occurs **only for Geldart Group A powders** (fine, low-density particles; e.g., FCC catalyst with $d_p < 100 \, \mu m$ and $\rho_p < 1400 \, kg/m^{3}$). For such powders, the bed expands smoothly and homogeneously in the velocity range $U_{mf} < U < U_{mb}$ without bubble formation. For Geldart B and D powders, $U_{mb} \approx U_{mf}$, so the difference is zero.

---

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Model 2]**

**Q2. State the Abrahamsen and Geldart correlation for minimum bubbling velocity. Explain the role of the particle Reynolds number in this correlation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Correlation (2 marks):**
$$
\dfrac{U_{mb} - U_{mf}}{U_{mb}} = \dfrac{C_1}{Re_{mf}^{C_2}}
$$
with:
- $C_1 = 0.0859, C_2 = 0.343$ for $Re_{mf} \leq 5$
- $C_1 = 0.0206, C_2 = 0.119$ for $5 < Re_{mf} \leq 70$
- $U_{mb} = U_{mf}$ for $Re_{mf} > 70$

**Role of Re_mf (1 mark):** The particle Reynolds number (evaluated at $U_{mf}$) characterizes the relative importance of inertial to viscous forces on the particles. It governs the ability of the bed to absorb the excess gas flow above $U_{mf}$ by uniform expansion. Lower $Re_{mf}$ → larger expansion range → larger $(U_{mb} - U_{mf})$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Choice-Based Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Part B questions carry **internal choice**. Two complete alternative questions are provided below. Each question is split into sub-parts (a) [7 marks] and (b) [7 marks].

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a) Derive the Wen and Yu correlation for minimum fluidization velocity starting from the Ergun equation.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the Ergun equation** (1 mark):
$$
\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{150 \mu (1-\varepsilon)^{2}}{\varepsilon^{3} d_p^{2}} U + \dfrac{1.75 (1-\varepsilon)}{\varepsilon^{3} d_p} \rho_g U^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — State the buoyant weight condition at U_mf** (1 mark):
$$
\Delta P_{mf} = (1 - \varepsilon_{mf})(\rho_p - \rho_g) g L
$$

**Step 3 — Equate and simplify** (1 mark):
$$
(\rho_p - \rho_g) g = \dfrac{150 \mu (1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{\varepsilon_{mf}^{3} d_p^{2}} U_{mf} + \dfrac{1.75}{\varepsilon_{mf}^{3} d_p} \rho_g U_{mf}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Non-dimensionalize using Ar and Re_mf** (1 mark):
$$
Ar = \dfrac{150 (1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{\varepsilon_{mf}^{3}} Re_{mf} + \dfrac{1.75}{\varepsilon_{mf}^{3}} Re_{mf}^{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Wen & Yu empirical coefficients** (1 mark):
The fitted values $C_1 = 33.7$ and $C_2 = 0.0408$ (where the voidage function is absorbed into these coefficients) give the quadratic in $Re_{mf}$:
$$
0.0408 \, Re_{mf}^{2} + 33.7 \, Re_{mf} - Ar = 0
$$

**Step 6 — Solve the quadratic** (1 mark):
$$
\boxed{Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^{2} + 0.0408 \, Ar} - 33.7}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to U_mf** (1 mark):
$$
U_{mf} = \dfrac{Re_{mf} \cdot \mu}{d_p \cdot \rho_g}
$$

---

**(b) A fluidized bed uses sand-like Geldart B particles with $d_p = 250 \, \mu m$, $\rho_p = 2600 \, kg/m^{3}$, $\rho_g = 1.2 \, kg/m^{3}$, $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \, Pa \cdot s$. Calculate $U_{mf}$ and $U_{mb}$ using the Wen-Yu and Abrahamsen-Geldart correlations.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Archimedes number Ar** (2 marks):
$$
d_p^{3} = (250 \times 10^{-6})^{3} = 1.5625 \times 10^{-8} \, m^{3}
$$

$$
Ar = \dfrac{1.5625 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.2 \times (2600 - 1.2) \times 9.81}{(1.8 \times 10^{-5})^{2}}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{1.5625 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.2 \times 2598.8 \times 9.81}{3.24 \times 10^{-10}}
$$

**Numerator:**
$$
1.5625 \times 10^{-8} \times 30590.9 = 4.7798 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Ar:**
$$
Ar = \dfrac{4.7798 \times 10^{-4}}{3.24 \times 10^{-10}} = 1.4752 \times 10^{6}
$$

**Step 2 — Re_mf using Wen-Yu** (2 marks):
$$
Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^{2} + 0.0408 \times 1.4752 \times 10^{6}} - 33.7
$$

$$
= \sqrt{1135.69 + 60188.16} - 33.7 = \sqrt{61323.85} - 33.7
$$

$$
= 247.63 - 33.7 = 213.93
$$

**Step 3 — U_mf in m/s** (1 mark):
$$
U_{mf} = \dfrac{213.93 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5}}{250 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.2} = \dfrac{3.851 \times 10^{-3}}{3.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 12.84 \, m/s
$$

**Step 4 — U_mb** (2 marks):
Since $Re_{mf} = 213.93 > 70$, this is **Regime 3 (Geldart B/D)**:

$$
\boxed{U_{mb} = U_{mf} = 12.84 \, m/s}
$$

This confirms that for coarse, dense Geldart B particles, the bubbling regime starts exactly at the minimum fluidization velocity — there is **no homogeneous expansion window**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) Explain Geldart's classification of powders with reference to the minimum bubbling velocity.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

Geldart (1973) classified powders into four groups based on the density difference $(\rho_p - \rho_g)$ and mean particle size $d_p$, with direct implications for the existence and value of $(U_{mb} - U_{mf})$:

| Group | Typical Powder | d_p range (μm) | ρ_p (kg/m³) | U_mb vs U_mf |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** (Aeratable) | FCC catalyst, Alumina | 20–100 | < 1400 | $U_{mb} > U_{mf}$ (significant gap) |
| **B** (Sand-like) | River sand, glass beads | 40–500 | 1400–4000 | $U_{mb} = U_{mf}$ |
| **C** (Cohesive) | Flour, talc, cement | < 20 | Any | Channelling — U_mb is meaningless |
| **D** (Spoutable) | Wheat, large pellets, lead shot | > 600 | > 1400 | $U_{mb} = U_{mf}$, but bed tends to slug |

**Explanation of U_mb relevance per group (5 marks total):**

1. **Geldart A (2 marks):** The inter-particle forces are small relative to drag, and the bed has high permeability. Above $U_{mf}$, gas is accommodated by **uniform expansion** of the bed. The minimum bubbling velocity is reached at a higher velocity. This group is preferred in catalytic reactors.

2. **Geldart B (1.5 marks):** The bed has moderate permeability. As soon as fluidization occurs ($U = U_{mf}$), **bubbles form immediately** because the bed cannot expand to accommodate the excess gas. Thus $U_{mb} = U_{mf}$.

3. **Geldart C (1 mark):** Inter-particle forces dominate (van der Waals, electrostatic). The bed does not fluidize uniformly — instead gas forms channels. Both $U_{mf}$ and $U_{mb}$ are ill-defined, often requiring mechanical aids (vibration, stirrers).

4. **Geldart D (0.5 mark):** Particles are too heavy to be lifted easily. The bed forms large bubbles or slugs. $U_{mb} = U_{mf}$ approximately, but operation is in the slugging regime.

---

**(b) With the aid of a labelled pressure drop vs. velocity diagram, explain the fluidization sequence from packed bed to transport.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Description of Diagram (3 marks):**

The plot of $\Delta P$ vs. $U$ exhibits the following regions:

1. **Region I (Packed Bed, $U < U_{mf}$):** Pressure drop **increases linearly** (for laminar flow, $Re < 10$) and then as a power of $U$ (for higher Re), following the Ergun equation.
2. **Peak at $U = U_{mf}$:** The pressure drop reaches a **maximum** equal to the buoyant weight of the bed per unit area, $W/A = (1 - \varepsilon_{mf})(\rho_p - \rho_g) g L$.
3. **Slight drop:** As the bed rearranges, the voidage slightly increases, and $\Delta P$ settles to the steady value equal to the bed weight.
4. **Region II (Particulate Expansion, $U_{mf} < U < U_{mb}$ for Geldart A):** $\Delta P$ remains **constant**, equal to the bed weight per unit area. Bed height increases.
5. **Region III (Bubbling Bed, $U > U_{mb}$):** $\Delta P$ **fluctuates** around the bed weight per area as bubbles rise and burst. The mean value is the bed weight.
6. **Region IV (Turbulent/Slugging, $U$ much greater than $U_{mb}$):** Bubble size reaches a maximum; pressure fluctuations dampen as bubbles disintegrate.
7. **Region V (Pneumatic Transport, $U > U_t$):** $\Delta P$ decreases as particles are entrained out of the bed.

**Key Explanation Points (4 marks):**

- The **first peak** at $U = U_{mf}$ is the unambiguous indicator of incipient fluidization.
- For **Geldart A**, there is a horizontal plateau (constant $\Delta P$) for $U_{mf} < U < U_{mb}$ — this is the **homogeneous expansion regime**.
- For **Geldart B/D**, the transition from packed to bubbling occurs at the **same velocity** ($U_{mb} = U_{mf}$); there is no plateau.
- The **hysteresis** phenomenon: the curve for decreasing $U$ from bubbling to packed bed lies *below* the increasing-$U$ curve due to different bed voidages; the **defluidization velocity** $U_{df}$ is typically less than $U_{mf}$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Examiner's Note):**
>
> 1. **Re_p evaluated at U_mf, not at U_mb (A&G correlation):** A&G requires $Re_{mf}$. Many students mistakenly compute Re at U_mb — but U_mb is the *unknown*, making the equation unsolvable directly.
>
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency:** Particle diameter must be in **metres** in the Wen-Yu formula (not μm or mm). $d_p^{3}$ requires SI.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Wen's coefficient is empirical:** Wen-Yu is **not** a first-principles derivation — students who write only the dimensionless Ergun equation without acknowledging the empirical fit lose 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Confusing Geldart groups:** U_mb > U_mf applies **only** to Geldart A. Writing "U_mb is always greater than U_mf" is **wrong** and costs 2 marks.
>
> 5. **Pressure drop at U_mf must equal bed weight:** Always state this equality clearly in derivations; it is the **boundary condition** that makes the problem solvable.
>
> 6. **Bubble vs. Channel:** Geldart C does not have a meaningful U_mb — students must not apply A&G correlation to Group C powders.
>
> 7. **No blank line in Mermaid / LaTeX:** When writing formula sheets in markdown tables, use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` to avoid breaking table syntax — this is a KTU 2024 format instruction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Minimum Bubbling Velocity**
>
> - **Definition:** U_mb = superficial gas velocity at the **first appearance of bubbles** at the bed surface.
> - **Boundary condition:** $U_{mb} \geq U_{mf}$, with equality for **Geldart B and D** powders.
> - **Geldart A** is the **only** group with $U_{mb} > U_{mf}$ (e.g., FCC catalyst).
> - **Archimedes Number:** $Ar = d_p^{3} \rho_g (\rho_p - \rho_g) g / \mu^{2}$ — dimensionless, required for Wen-Yu.
> - **Wen & Yu (U_mf):** $Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^{2} + 0.0408 \, Ar} - 33.7$.
> - **A&G (U_mb) — Regime 1** ($Re_{mf} \leq 5$): $\dfrac{U_{mb} - U_{mf}}{U_{mb}} = \dfrac{0.0859}{Re_{mf}^{0.343}}$.
> - **A&G (U_mb) — Regime 2** ($5 < Re_{mf} \leq 70$): $\dfrac{U_{mb} - U_{mf}}{U_{mb}} = \dfrac{0.0206}{Re_{mf}^{0.119}}$.
> - **A&G (U_mb) — Regime 3** ($Re_{mf} > 70$): $U_{mb} = U_{mf}$ (Geldart B/D).
> - **Two-step procedure** is mandatory: (i) compute U_mf → (ii) compute Re_mf → (iii) apply A&G.
> - **Re_p in A&G is evaluated at U_mf**, never at U_mb.
> - **Ergun equation** is the *fundamental* basis; Wen-Yu is its **empirically fitted** simplified form.
> - **U_mb importance:** Lower bound of the **bubbling regime**; design constraint for FCC risers, polymerization reactors, and dryers.
> - **Geldart C caveat:** Cohesive powders channel; U_mb is **not physically meaningful**.
> - **Hysteresis:** $U_{df}$ (defluidization) < $U_{mf}$; an important operational consideration.
> - **Pressure drop plateau** in $\Delta P$ vs. $U$ plot is the **visual signature** of particulate expansion (Geldart A only).
> - **U_t (terminal velocity)** sets the upper bound of fluidization before pneumatic transport begins.
> - **STP Air values to memorize:** $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \, Pa \cdot s$; $\rho_g = 1.2 \, kg/m^{3}$; $g = 9.81 \, m/s^{2}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Temperature and Pressure on Fluidized Bed Behaviour
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Effect of Temperature and Pressure on Fluidized Bed Behaviour

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fluidization** is the operation by which fine solids are transformed into a fluid-like state through contact with a gas or liquid. A **fluidized bed** is a packed bed of solid particles where the fluid (gas) flow rate is sufficient to suspend the particles, causing them to behave as a fluid. The behaviour of such a bed — its **minimum fluidization velocity ($U_{mf}$)**, bubble dynamics, bed expansion, and heat/mass transfer — is critically dependent on the **operating temperature ($T$)** and **operating pressure ($P$)** of the system because these variables directly alter the **thermo-physical properties of the fluidizing gas**, most notably its **density ($\rho_g$)** and **viscosity ($\mu_g$)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PECHT415 Module 1):** The effect of temperature and pressure on fluidized bed hydrodynamics is fundamental to the **design, scale-up, and operation of industrial fluidized bed reactors (FBRs)**, especially in high-pressure applications such as **fluid catalytic cracking (FCC)**, **coal combustion/gasification**, and **methanol-to-olefins (MTO)** processes.

## 1.2 Why Temperature and Pressure Matter

The two most important gas properties controlling fluidization are:

- **Gas density ($\rho_g$):** Determines the **buoyancy force** and the **drag force** on particles.
- **Gas viscosity ($\mu_g$):** Governs the **frictional resistance** to flow in the inter-particle channels.

Using the **Ideal Gas Law**:

$$
\rho_g = \frac{PM}{RT}
$$

where $M$ is the molar mass of the gas, $R$ is the universal gas constant ($R = 8.314 \, \text{J}\cdot\text{mol}^{-1}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$), and $T$ is the absolute temperature (in Kelvin).

- **Increasing $T$** $\Rightarrow$ $\rho_g$ **decreases** (gas becomes lighter)
- **Increasing $P$** $\Rightarrow$ $\rho_g$ **increases** (gas becomes denser)

Viscosity generally **increases with temperature** and is **largely independent of pressure** (for moderate pressures).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Inflatable Balloon in Water

Imagine dropping a ping-pong ball into a swimming pool versus dropping it into the Dead Sea (very dense salty water).

- In the **swimming pool** (analogous to **low pressure, high temperature**): The water is "thin," so the ball sinks quickly and stays near the bottom — the drag force is weak because the fluid is light.
- In the **Dead Sea** (analogous to **high pressure, low temperature**): The dense water provides much more **buoyancy and drag**, so the ball is easily suspended — even at low flow rates.

> [!NOTE]
> A **high-pressure, low-temperature gas** behaves like a **"heavier"** fluid, so particles fluidize at **lower gas velocities**. A **low-pressure, high-temperature gas** behaves like a **"lighter"** fluid, requiring **higher velocities** to fluidize the same particles.

## 1.4 Engineering Significance

| Operating Condition | Industrial Example | Why T/P Matter |
|---|---|---|
| **High-T, Low-P** | Biomass combustors, waste incinerators | Gas density drops; gas viscosity rises |
| **High-P, High-T** | Pressurized coal gasifiers, FCC regenerators | Both $\rho_g$ and $\mu_g$ shift; bubble dynamics change |
| **Ambient** | Lab-scale cold models | Reference state; scale-up to industrial conditions requires T/P correction |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Insight:** When asked to design a fluidized bed reactor, the Examiner expects you to specify that **Umf, bubble size, bed expansion, and heat transfer coefficients are all functions of $T$ and $P$**, and that the **gas properties must be evaluated at the actual operating $T$ and $P$** — not at ambient conditions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Dependencies of Gas Properties

### 2.1.1 Effect of Temperature on $\rho_g$ and $\mu_g$

For an **ideal gas**, the density is inversely proportional to absolute temperature:

$$
\rho_g = \frac{PM}{RT} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho_g \propto \frac{1}{T}
$$

For **viscosity**, gases follow an approximate power-law (Sutherland's law simplified):

$$
\mu_g \propto T^{n}, \quad n \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 0.7
$$

For air, a commonly used correlation is:

$$
\mu_g = \mu_{g,0} \left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{0.7}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** As $T$ rises, $\rho_g$ **falls** while $\mu_g$ **rises**. These two changes have **competing effects** on $U_{mf}$ — the dominant effect depends on the **Geldart particle classification**.

### 2.1.2 Effect of Pressure on $\rho_g$ and $\mu_g$

For gases, viscosity is **weakly dependent on pressure** below ~10 atm. Density, however, scales **linearly** with pressure:

$$
\rho_g \propto P
$$

The **Archimedes number ($Ar$)**, which controls $U_{mf}$, is defined as:

$$
Ar = \frac{\rho_g \left(\rho_p - \rho_g\right) g d_p^{3}}{\mu_g^{2}}
$$

- At **high $P$**: $\rho_g$ is large, so $Ar$ is **large** $\Rightarrow$ particles become more "Geldart-A-like" (even if originally Geldart B).
- At **high $T$**: $\rho_g$ is small, so $Ar$ is **small** $\Rightarrow$ particles behave more "Geldart-B/D-like".

## 2.2 Effect on Minimum Fluidization Velocity ($U_{mf}$)

The **Ergun equation** at incipient fluidization:

$$
\frac{1.75}{\phi_s \varepsilon_{mf}^{3}} Re_{mf}^{2} + \frac{150(1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{\phi_s^{2}\varepsilon_{mf}^{3}} Re_{mf} = Ar
$$

where $Re_{mf} = \dfrac{\rho_g U_{mf} d_p}{\mu_g}$.

The **Wen & Yu (1966)** correlation is widely used:

$$
Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^{2} + 0.0408 \, Ar} - 33.7
$$

$$
U_{mf} = \frac{Re_{mf} \, \mu_g}{\rho_g \, d_p}
$$

### 2.2.1 Temperature Effect on $U_{mf}$

Since $\rho_g \propto 1/T$ and $\mu_g \propto T^{0.7}$:

$$
U_{mf} \propto \frac{\mu_g}{\rho_g d_p} \propto \frac{T^{0.7}}{(1/T)} = T^{1.7}
$$

- **For Geldart A & B (fine/medium particles):** $U_{mf}$ **increases** with $T$.
- **For Geldart D (large/dense particles):** $U_{mf}$ may **decrease** with $T$ at very high $T$ because $\rho_g$ drop dominates.

### 2.2.2 Pressure Effect on $U_{mf}$ — King's Correlation

For high-pressure systems, **King & Harrison (1982)** proposed:

$$
\frac{U_{mf}}{U_{mf,0}} = \left(\frac{\rho_{g,0}}{\rho_g}\right)^{0.55} \left(\frac{\mu_g}{\mu_{g,0}}\right)^{-0.10}
$$

Or, in more explicit form (for Geldart B particles):

$$
U_{mf} = \frac{0.00138 \, d_p^{1.18} \left[\rho_g (\rho_p - \rho_g) g\right]^{0.71}}{\mu_g^{0.88} \, \rho_g^{0.06}} \quad \text{(high-P, Geldart B)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **net effect of pressure** is to **decrease $U_{mf}$** because the increased $\rho_g$ enhances drag, allowing fluidization at lower superficial velocities.

## 2.3 Effect on Bubble Behaviour

### 2.3.1 Temperature Effect

- At **higher $T$**: $\mu_g$ increases, which **suppresses bubble splitting**.
- Result: **Bubble size increases** with $T$ for the same excess gas velocity.
- **Bed expansion** at higher $T$ tends to be **less pronounced** (lower gas density means less fluidizing force).

### 2.3.2 Pressure Effect

- At **higher $P$**: $\rho_g$ increases, **bubble size decreases**, **bubble rise velocity decreases**.
- **Bed expansion** increases at higher $P$ for the same $U/U_{mf}$.
- **Gas-solid contact** improves dramatically — this is why **FCC regenerators operate at 2–4 atm** and **coal gasifiers at 20–70 atm**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Application / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ideal gas density | $\rho_g = \dfrac{PM}{RT}$ | Evaluates gas density at $T$, $P$ |
| 2 | Gas viscosity (Sutherland, simplified) | $\mu_g = \mu_{g,0} \left(\dfrac{T}{T_0}\right)^{0.7}$ | For air & N$_2$; use tables for other gases |
| 3 | Archimedes number | $Ar = \dfrac{\rho_g (\rho_p - \rho_g) g d_p^{3}}{\mu_g^{2}}$ | Dimensionless gravity-to-viscosity ratio |
| 4 | $Re_{mf}$ (Wen & Yu) | $Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^{2} + 0.0408 \, Ar} - 33.7$ | Most common in KTU problems |
| 5 | $U_{mf}$ from $Re_{mf}$ | $U_{mf} = \dfrac{Re_{mf} \, \mu_g}{\rho_g \, d_p}$ | Universal $U_{mf}$ definition |
| 6 | King & Harrison (P-effect) | $\dfrac{U_{mf}}{U_{mf,0}} = \left(\dfrac{\rho_{g,0}}{\rho_g}\right)^{0.55} \left(\dfrac{\mu_g}{\mu_{g,0}}\right)^{-0.10}$ | High-pressure correction |
| 7 | Temperature scaling of $U_{mf}$ | $U_{mf} \propto T^{1.7}$ (rough) | For fixed $P$, ideal gas scaling |
| 8 | Bubble rise velocity | $U_b = 0.711 \sqrt{g d_b}$ | Mori & Wen correlation (independent of $\rho_g$) |
| 9 | Geldart boundary shift | $Ar \propto \rho_g$ | High $P$ shifts particles from B $\rightarrow$ A |
| 10 | Bed voidage (high P) | $\varepsilon_{mf}$ increases with $P$ | $\varepsilon_{mf} \approx 0.45$–$0.55$ at high $P$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **FCC units** (Petroleum Refining): Operate at **2–5 atm, 700–750 °C**. High $P$ reduces $U_{mf}$, suppresses bubbles, and improves catalyst regeneration kinetics.
- **Pressurized Fluidized Bed Combustion (PFBC)**: Operating at **10–16 atm** to increase combustion efficiency and reduce pollutant formation.
- **Coal Gasification (Winkler, HT Winkler)**: Operate at **10–30 atm, 800–1000 °C** — bubbles are smaller, gas–solid contact is excellent.
- **Drying & Granulation (Pharma)**: Often ambient $P$ but **elevated $T$** to drive moisture out.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Temperature Scaling of $U_{mf}$ from First Principles

### Given:

- Ideal gas: $\rho_g = \dfrac{PM}{RT}$ $\Rightarrow$ $\rho_g \propto 1/T$
- Gas viscosity: $\mu_g \propto T^{0.7}$
- $U_{mf} = \dfrac{Re_{mf} \, \mu_g}{\rho_g d_p}$

### Step 1 — Express $U_{mf}$ in terms of $T$:

$$
U_{mf} = \frac{Re_{mf} \, \mu_g}{\rho_g d_p} = \frac{Re_{mf} \cdot \mu_{g}(T) \cdot R T}{P M \cdot d_p}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the temperature dependence of $\mu_g$:

Let $\mu_g(T) = \mu_{g,0}\left(\dfrac{T}{T_0}\right)^{0.7}$:

$$
U_{mf} = \frac{Re_{mf} \cdot \mu_{g,0} \left(\dfrac{T}{T_0}\right)^{0.7} \cdot R T}{P M \cdot d_p}
$$

### Step 3 — Collect the $T$-dependent terms:

$$
U_{mf} = \left[\frac{Re_{mf} \cdot \mu_{g,0} \cdot R}{P M \cdot d_p \cdot T_0^{0.7}}\right] \cdot T^{1.7}
$$

### Step 4 — Conclude the scaling:

$$
U_{mf} \propto T^{1.7}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If you double the absolute temperature (e.g., from 300 K to 600 K), $U_{mf}$ increases by a factor of $2^{1.7} \approx 3.25$. This is why **cold-model experiments at room temperature significantly under-predict $U_{mf}$** in hot industrial reactors.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pressure Scaling of $\rho_g$ and Archimedes Number

### Given:

- $\rho_g = \dfrac{PM}{RT}$ $\Rightarrow$ $\rho_g \propto P$
- $Ar = \dfrac{\rho_g (\rho_p - \rho_g) g d_p^{3}}{\mu_g^{2}}$

### Step 1 — Identify the two cases for $\rho_p$ vs $\rho_g$:

- **Case A: $\rho_p \gg \rho_g$** (low $P$, light gases): $(\rho_p - \rho_g) \approx \rho_p$

$$
Ar \approx \frac{\rho_g \cdot \rho_p \cdot g d_p^{3}}{\mu_g^{2}} \propto P
$$

- **Case B: $\rho_p \approx \rho_g$** (very high $P$): $(\rho_p - \rho_g)$ is small

$$
Ar \approx \frac{\rho_g^{2} \cdot g d_p^{3}}{\mu_g^{2}} \propto P^{2}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the pressure ratio of $Ar$:

$$
\frac{Ar_2}{Ar_1} = \frac{\rho_{g,2}}{\rho_{g,1}} \cdot \frac{\rho_p - \rho_{g,2}}{\rho_p - \rho_{g,1}}
$$

For $\rho_p \gg \rho_g$ (most industrial cases):

$$
\frac{Ar_2}{Ar_1} \approx \frac{P_2}{P_1}
$$

### Step 3 — Link to $U_{mf}$:

For large $Ar$ (which occurs at high $P$), Wen & Yu reduces to:

$$
Re_{mf} \approx \sqrt{0.0408 \, Ar} \propto \sqrt{Ar} \propto \sqrt{P}
$$

Then:

$$
U_{mf} = \frac{Re_{mf} \, \mu_g}{\rho_g d_p} \propto \frac{\sqrt{P}}{P} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{P}}
$$

### Step 4 — Final scaling:

$$
\boxed{U_{mf} \propto P^{-0.5} \quad \text{(for Geldart B, high-P limit)}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numerical Trick:** When $P$ doubles and $\rho_p \gg \rho_g$, $U_{mf}$ decreases by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

### Problem:

A fluidized bed uses air to fluidize silica sand at ambient conditions: $T_0 = 300 \, \text{K}$, $P_0 = 1 \, \text{atm}$. The measured $U_{mf,0} = 0.05 \, \text{m/s}$. The same bed is to operate at $T = 900 \, \text{K}$, $P = 5 \, \text{atm}$. Estimate $U_{mf}$ at the new conditions using the King–Harrison correlation (Geldart B regime). Assume air with $\mu_{g,0} = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $M = 0.029 \, \text{kg/mol}$.

### Step 1 — Calculate $\rho_{g,0}$ and $\rho_g$:

$$
\rho_{g,0} = \frac{P_0 M}{R T_0} = \frac{(101325)(0.029)}{(8.314)(300)} = 1.178 \, \text{kg/m}^{3}
$$

$$
\rho_g = \frac{P M}{R T} = \frac{(5 \times 101325)(0.029)}{(8.314)(900)} = 1.962 \, \text{kg/m}^{3}
$$

### Step 2 — Viscosity ratio:

$$
\frac{\mu_g}{\mu_{g,0}} = \left(\frac{900}{300}\right)^{0.7} = 3^{0.7} = 2.157
$$

### Step 3 — Apply King–Harrison:

$$
\frac{U_{mf}}{U_{mf,0}} = \left(\frac{\rho_{g,0}}{\rho_g}\right)^{0.55} \left(\frac{\mu_g}{\mu_{g,0}}\right)^{-0.10}
$$

$$
\frac{U_{mf}}{U_{mf,0}} = \left(\frac{1.178}{1.962}\right)^{0.55} \times (2.157)^{-0.10}
$$

$$
\frac{U_{mf}}{U_{mf,0}} = (0.6003)^{0.55} \times (2.157)^{-0.10}
$$

$$
\frac{U_{mf}}{U_{mf,0}} = 0.7585 \times 0.9216 = 0.6990
$$

### Step 4 — Compute new $U_{mf}$:

$$
U_{mf} = 0.05 \times 0.6990 = 0.0349 \, \text{m/s}
$$

### Result:

$$
\boxed{U_{mf}(900 \, \text{K}, 5 \, \text{atm}) \approx 0.035 \, \text{m/s}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** Even though $\mu_g$ increased by ~2.16×, the 5-fold increase in $\rho_g$ dominated, **reducing $U_{mf}$** by ~30%. This is why **high-pressure industrial fluidized beds** use **lower gas velocities** than their cold-model counterparts.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — T & P Effects

| Property / Behaviour | Effect of Increasing $T$ | Effect of Increasing $P$ | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gas density $\rho_g$ | Decreases ($\rho_g \propto 1/T$) | Increases ($\rho_g \propto P$) | Ideal gas law |
| Gas viscosity $\mu_g$ | Increases ($\mu_g \propto T^{0.7}$) | Roughly constant (low to moderate $P$) | Kinetic theory of gases |
| Archimedes number $Ar$ | Decreases | Increases | Driven by $\rho_g$ |
| $U_{mf}$ (fine, A/B) | Increases ($\sim T^{1.7}$) | Decreases ($\sim P^{-0.5}$) | Drag-vs-buoyancy balance |
| Bubble size $d_b$ | Increases | Decreases | Higher $\mu_g$ resists splitting; higher $\rho_g$ aids splitting |
| Bed expansion $\varepsilon$ | Decreases | Increases | $U-U_{mf}$ headroom changes |
| Gas–solid contact | Worsens | Improves | Smaller bubbles at high $P$ |
| Heat transfer coeff. $h_w$ | Mixed effect | Improves | Wall heat transfer $\propto \rho_g$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How T & P Couple to Bed Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operating Conditions T and P] --> B[Gas Density rho_g via Ideal Gas Law]
    A --> C[Gas Viscosity mu_g via Sutherland Law]
    B --> D[Archimedes Number Ar]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Re_mf via Wen and Yu]
    E --> F[Minimum Fluidization Velocity U_mf]
    B --> G[Bubble Dynamics]
    C --> G
    G --> H[Bubble Size d_b]
    G --> I[Bubble Rise Velocity U_b]
    F --> J[Bed Expansion epsilon]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Heat and Mass Transfer Performance]
    F --> K
    H --> K
    K --> L[Reactor Design Parameters]
    L --> M[Reactor Diameter and Height]
    L --> N[Distributor Design]
    L --> O[Operating Gas Velocity U]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- $T$ and $P$ are the **root inputs**.
- They feed into $\rho_g$ and $\mu_g$ (the two key gas properties).
- These combine into the **Archimedes number ($Ar$)**, which determines $Re_{mf}$ and hence $U_{mf}$.
- Bubble dynamics (size, rise velocity) are also functions of $\rho_g$ and $\mu_g$.
- Finally, $U_{mf}$, bubble behaviour, and bed expansion jointly determine the **overall reactor design and operating gas velocity**.

## 4.2 State-Transition Diagram: Geldart Classification Shift with Pressure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Geldart B at 1 atm] --> B{Increase Pressure P}
    B --> C[rho_g increases]
    C --> D[Ar increases]
    D --> E[Effective behaviour shifts toward Geldart A]
    E --> F[Smaller bubbles]
    E --> G[Non-bubbling region expands]
    E --> H[Smooth fluidization over wider U range]
```

**Interpretation:** A Geldart B particle at 1 atm may behave like a Geldart A particle at 20 atm. This is the **pressure-induced regime shift**, critical in high-pressure operations.

## 4.3 Comparative Process Map: Hot vs Cold Models

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph COLD[Cold Model at 300 K and 1 atm]
        C1[rho_g low] --> C2[mu_g low] --> C3[U_mf reference]
    end
    subgraph HOT[Hot Reactor at 900 K and 5 atm]
        H1[rho_g higher] --> H2[mu_g higher] --> H3[U_mf predicted by King-Harrison]
    end
    C3 --> D[Scale-up correction factor]
    H3 --> D
    D --> E[Design U_mf for industrial reactor]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A complete answer on this topic should explicitly show that **cold-model $U_{mf}$ values must be corrected for $T$ and $P$** before applying them to industrial design. Always state the basis ($T$ and $P$) under which your $U_{mf}$ is valid.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Explain how an increase in operating pressure affects the minimum fluidization velocity of a Geldart B particle. Justify your answer with the underlying change in gas density.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

1. **Gas density increases linearly with pressure:** For an ideal gas, $\rho_g = PM/RT$, so $\rho_g \propto P$ (1 mark).
2. **Archimedes number scales with $\rho_g$:** Since $Ar = \rho_g(\rho_p - \rho_g)g d_p^{3}/\mu_g^{2}$ and $\rho_p \gg \rho_g$, $Ar \propto P$ (1 mark).
3. **$U_{mf}$ decreases with $P$:** For large $Ar$ (Wen & Yu limit), $U_{mf} \propto P^{-0.5}$ — therefore, increasing $P$ **lowers** $U_{mf}$ because the heavier gas exerts a stronger drag force on the particles (1 mark).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**State Sutherland's law for the temperature dependence of gas viscosity and comment on its effect on bubble behaviour in a hot fluidized bed.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

1. **Sutherland's law (simplified):** $\mu_g = \mu_{g,0}\left(\dfrac{T}{T_0}\right)^{0.7}$ — viscosity increases with $T$ (1 mark).
2. **Effect on bubble splitting:** Higher $\mu_g$ **resists bubble splitting** because the gas is more resistant to flow perturbations (1 mark).
3. **Net effect:** At higher $T$, bubbles are **larger**, gas–solid contact **worsens**, and the bed behaves more like a Geldart B system (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper]`

**a)** Derive an expression for the temperature scaling of minimum fluidization velocity $U_{mf}$ for an ideal gas assuming the Wen & Yu correlation. **\[7 marks\]**

**b)** A fluidized bed of glass beads is operated with air at $T_0 = 298 \, \text{K}$ and $P_0 = 1 \, \text{atm}$. The measured $U_{mf,0} = 0.038 \, \text{m/s}$. Estimate the new $U_{mf}$ when the bed is operated at $T = 800 \, \text{K}$ and $P = 10 \, \text{atm}$ using the King–Harrison correlation. Assume air with $M = 0.029 \, \text{kg/mol}$, $\mu_{g,0} = 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$. **\[7 marks\]**

#### Part (a) Model Solution [7 marks]:

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of $U_{mf}$ (1 mark):**

$$
U_{mf} = \frac{Re_{mf} \, \mu_g}{\rho_g \, d_p}
$$

**Step 2 — Express $\rho_g$ via ideal gas law (1 mark):**

$$
\rho_g = \frac{PM}{RT} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho_g \propto \frac{1}{T} \text{ (at constant P)}
$$

**Step 3 — Express $\mu_g$ via Sutherland's law (1 mark):**

$$
\mu_g = \mu_{g,0}\left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)^{0.7}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the $U_{mf}$ expression (1 mark):**

$$
U_{mf} = \frac{Re_{mf} \cdot \mu_{g,0}(T/T_0)^{0.7}}{(PM/RT) \, d_p} = \frac{Re_{mf} \cdot \mu_{g,0} \cdot R \cdot T^{1.7}}{P M \, d_p \, T_0^{0.7}}
$$

**Step 5 — Identify the scaling (1 mark):** At constant $P$, $d_p$, and particle properties:

$$
U_{mf} \propto T^{1.7}
$$

**Step 6 — Physical interpretation (1 mark):** Doubling $T$ (e.g., 300 K → 600 K) increases $U_{mf}$ by $2^{1.7} \approx 3.25 \times$. This is why hot industrial fluidized beds operate at much higher superficial velocities than cold models.

**Step 7 — Validity and caveat (1 mark):** This scaling assumes $\rho_p \gg \rho_g$ and uses the simplified Sutherland exponent of 0.7; for other gases the exponent may differ.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution [7 marks]:

**Step 1 — Write the King–Harrison correlation (1 mark):**

$$
\frac{U_{mf}}{U_{mf,0}} = \left(\frac{\rho_{g,0}}{\rho_g}\right)^{0.55} \left(\frac{\mu_g}{\mu_{g,0}}\right)^{-0.10}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $\rho_{g,0}$ at 298 K, 1 atm (1 mark):**

$$
\rho_{g,0} = \frac{PM}{RT} = \frac{101325 \times 0.029}{8.314 \times 298} = 1.185 \, \text{kg/m}^{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $\rho_g$ at 800 K, 10 atm (1 mark):**

$$
\rho_g = \frac{10 \times 101325 \times 0.029}{8.314 \times 800} = 4.418 \, \text{kg/m}^{3}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate viscosity ratio (1 mark):**

$$
\frac{\mu_g}{\mu_{g,0}} = \left(\frac{800}{298}\right)^{0.7} = (2.685)^{0.7} = 2.034
$$

**Step 5 — Plug into the correlation (1 mark):**

$$
\frac{U_{mf}}{U_{mf,0}} = \left(\frac{1.185}{4.418}\right)^{0.55} \times (2.034)^{-0.10}
$$

$$
\frac{U_{mf}}{U_{mf,0}} = (0.2682)^{0.55} \times (2.034)^{-0.10} = 0.4798 \times 0.9570 = 0.4591
$$

**Step 6 — Compute new $U_{mf}$ (1 mark):**

$$
U_{mf} = 0.038 \times 0.4591 = 0.01745 \, \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 7 — Final result with comment (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{U_{mf}(800 \, \text{K}, 10 \, \text{atm}) \approx 0.0175 \, \text{m/s}}
$$

The 10-fold pressure increase raises $\rho_g$ by ~3.7×, which **dominates** the rise in $\mu_g$ (2×), causing $U_{mf}$ to drop by ~54%. High-pressure fluidized beds are hence more energy-efficient in terms of fluidizing gas.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

### `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**a)** With the help of a clear sketch/flowchart, explain how the operating temperature of a fluidized bed affects (i) the minimum fluidization velocity, and (ii) the bubble size. **\[7 marks\]**

**b)** A coal gasifier operates at $T = 1100 \, \text{K}$ and $P = 25 \, \text{atm}$ with a Geldart B ash particle of $d_p = 200 \, \mu\text{m}$, $\rho_p = 2400 \, \text{kg/m}^{3}$. The gas is approximated as air. Calculate: (i) the gas density at operating conditions, (ii) the Archimedes number, and (iii) the minimum fluidization velocity using the Wen & Yu correlation. Take $\mu_g$ at 1100 K $\approx 4.4 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$. **\[7 marks\]**

#### Part (a) Model Solution [7 marks]:

**Sketch/Description (3 marks):** A flowchart showing $T$ $\rightarrow$ $\rho_g \downarrow$, $\mu_g \uparrow$ $\rightarrow$ $U_{mf} \uparrow$ (fine particles) and bubble size $\uparrow$.

**(i) Effect on $U_{mf}$ (2 marks):**
- $\rho_g \downarrow$ as $T \uparrow$ (ideal gas)
- $\mu_g \uparrow$ as $T \uparrow$ (Sutherland)
- For fine/medium (A/B) particles: $U_{mf} \propto T^{1.7}$ — increases with $T$.

**(ii) Effect on bubble size (2 marks):**
- Higher $T$ $\Rightarrow$ higher $\mu_g$ $\Rightarrow$ bubble splitting is suppressed.
- Bubbles grow larger and coalesce more.
- Bed becomes less uniform; gas–solid contact worsens at very high $T$.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution [7 marks]:

**(i) Gas density (2 marks):**

$$
\rho_g = \frac{PM}{RT} = \frac{25 \times 101325 \times 0.029}{8.314 \times 1100} = 8.026 \, \text{kg/m}^{3}
$$

**(ii) Archimedes number (2 marks):**

$$
Ar = \frac{\rho_g (\rho_p - \rho_g) g d_p^{3}}{\mu_g^{2}}
$$

$$
Ar = \frac{8.026 \times (2400 - 8.026) \times 9.81 \times (200 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}{(4.4 \times 10^{-5})^{2}}
$$

$$
Ar = \frac{8.026 \times 2392 \times 9.81 \times 8 \times 10^{-15}}{1.936 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1.508 \times 10^{-9}}{1.936 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.779
$$

**(iii) $U_{mf}$ via Wen & Yu (3 marks):**

$$
Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^{2} + 0.0408 \times 0.779} - 33.7
$$

$$
Re_{mf} = \sqrt{1135.69 + 0.0318} - 33.7 = \sqrt{1135.72} - 33.7 = 33.701 - 33.7 \approx 0.001
$$

$$
\boxed{U_{mf} = \frac{Re_{mf} \cdot \mu_g}{\rho_g d_p} = \frac{0.001 \times 4.4 \times 10^{-5}}{8.026 \times 200 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 2.74 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m/s}}
$$

**Comment:** Such a small $U_{mf}$ confirms the Geldart A-like behaviour at high pressure. The bed is easily fluidized; industrial gasifiers typically operate at $U \approx 0.5$–$1.0 \, \text{m/s}$, i.e., many times $U_{mf}$.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in KTU Answers (where 7–9 marks can be lost):**
>
> 1. **Using the wrong gas density formula** — Students often use $\rho_g = 1.2 \, \text{kg/m}^{3}$ for all $T$ and $P$ conditions. **Always evaluate $\rho_g = PM/RT$ at the given $T$ and $P$.**
> 2. **Forgetting the Sutherland exponent** — Using $\mu_g \propto \sqrt{T}$ instead of $T^{0.7}$ introduces ~10–15% error. Use **0.7 for air/N$_2$**, **0.65 for CO$_2$**, **0.5–0.6 for light gases**.
> 3. **Mixing up trends** — Pressure and temperature have **opposite** effects on $U_{mf}$ in most practical cases. A diagram clearly showing this earns easy marks.
> 4. **Not specifying the Geldart class** — $T$ and $P$ effects depend on the particle class. Always state which Geldart regime the problem is in.
> 5. **Skipping units** — KTU examiners deduct marks if final $U_{mf}$ is reported without units (m/s or cm/s).
> 6. **Neglecting the King–Harrison equation for high-P problems** — Most "industrial" KTU problems are at high $P$; the Wen–Yu correlation alone is insufficient.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Gas density $\rho_g$** is the **single most important** property affected by $T$ and $P$: $\rho_g = PM/RT$.
- **Gas viscosity $\mu_g$** increases with $T$ ($\mu_g \propto T^{0.7}$) and is nearly constant with $P$ (below ~10 atm).
- **$U_{mf}$ scales as $\sim T^{1.7}$** for fine particles at constant $P$.
- **$U_{mf}$ scales as $\sim P^{-0.5}$** for Geldart B particles in the high-$P$ limit.
- **High $P$** $\Rightarrow$ higher $\rho_g$ $\Rightarrow$ higher $Ar$ $\Rightarrow$ smaller bubbles, better gas–solid contact, lower $U_{mf}$.
- **High $T$** $\Rightarrow$ lower $\rho_g$, higher $\mu_g$ $\Rightarrow$ larger bubbles, worse gas–solid contact, higher $U_{mf}$.
- **Geldart classification shifts:** Increasing $P$ shifts Geldart B $\rightarrow$ A; increasing $T$ shifts A $\rightarrow$ B.
- **King–Harrison correlation** must be used for high-pressure design problems.
- **Wen & Yu** is the standard $U_{mf}$ estimator when $Ar$ is known.
- **Industrial examples:** FCC (2–4 atm, 700–750 °C), PFBC (10–16 atm, 850–900 °C), coal gasifiers (20–70 atm, 800–1100 °C).
- **Always evaluate gas properties at the actual $T$ and $P$** — never at ambient conditions.
- **Sketch a flowchart** (T, P $\rightarrow$ $\rho_g$, $\mu_g$ $\rightarrow$ $Ar$ $\rightarrow$ $U_{mf}$) in your exam answer to earn methodology marks.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** "**T up, $\rho_g$ down, $U_{mf}$ up. P up, $\rho_g$ up, $U_{mf}$ down.**" Memorize this single line and you will not lose marks on trend-type questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
