topics.title: Sintering and Agglomeration of Particles at High Temperature. Geldart Classification of Particles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sintering and Agglomeration of Particles at High Temperature. Geldart Classification of Particles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Sintering of Particles

**Sintering** is a thermal process in which fine particles in a fluidized bed are heated to a temperature below the melting point of the solid, causing the particles to bond together at their contact points, leading to growth, densification, neck formation, and a permanent reduction in the specific surface area of the particles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Exam Verbatim):** Sintering is the process of bonding adjacent particle surfaces in a mass of particles (or in a compact) by molecular or atomic diffusion, when the mass is heated to a temperature approaching the melting point of the material.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine taking a handful of wet snow and gently pressing two snowballs together. The snow crystals at the contact point fuse into ice — they do not melt, they just bond. Sintering behaves the same way: at high temperatures, atoms diffuse across the boundary between two touching solid particles, creating a "neck" that welds them together. Over time, many particles merge into a single hard, dense lump. In a fluidized bed, this is a **disaster** — the fluidizing gas can no longer keep the now-lumpy particles suspended, and the bed collapses (defluidization).

### 1.2 Agglomeration of Particles

**Agglomeration** is the process by which particles stick together to form larger masses (agglomerates) due to interparticle forces such as van der Waals forces, capillary forces (from liquid bridges), electrostatic forces, viscous binders, or partial sintering at high temperatures.

> [!NOTE]
> While **sintering** is a thermally activated, solid-state diffusion phenomenon, **agglomeration** is the broader physical sticking together of particles. Sintering is essentially one of the mechanisms of agglomeration at high temperature.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Think of breadcrumbs on a damp table. They clump together because the thin water film between them creates surface tension that pulls them in. In a hot fluidized bed, partial melting of low-melting components (or even tacky soft surfaces) creates similar "sticky bridges" that lock particles into clusters — these clusters grow until they are too heavy to be fluidized.

### 1.3 Geldart Classification of Particles

**Geldart Classification** is a widely used empirical classification of solid particles based on their fluidization behavior in a gas–solid fluidized bed. The classification uses two primary particle properties:
- **Mean particle size (d_p)** in micrometers
- **Particle density (ρ_p)** in kg/m³

with the fluidizing gas density (ρ_f) as a secondary parameter.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight (Examiner's Pet Topic):** Geldart proposed this classification in 1973 based on experimental observations of fluidization behavior in ambient air. The four groups (A, B, C, D) are foundational for any fluidized bed design problem.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine a beach with four kinds of grains:
1. **Talcum-powder-fine dust** (Geldart C) — too light and sticky, the wind cannot separate them — they form lumps and channels instead of a fluidized cloud.
2. **Dry beach sand** (Geldart A/B) — ideal for fluidization, behaves like a boiling liquid.
3. **Pebbles and gravel** (Geldart D) — so heavy that gas cannot lift them; they need special techniques (spouting) to be moved.
4. **Plastic microbeads** (Geldart A) — light and small, they fluidize beautifully with vigorous bubble formation.

### 1.4 The Four Geldart Groups

| Group | Common Name | Typical d_p (μm) | Behavior |
|:------|:------------|:----------------:|:---------|
| **A** | Aeratable / Cracking Catalyst | 20 – 100 | Fluidizes easily, expands before bubbling |
| **B** | Sand-like / Bubbling | 100 – 800 | Bubbles form immediately at U_mf |
| **C** | Cohesive / Flour | < 20 | Channeling, plugs, hard to fluidize |
| **D** | Spoutable / Coarse | > 800 | Requires spouting; jets form easily |

> [!WARNING]
> **Boundary values are approximate** — Geldart's diagram itself is empirical, and transitions between groups (A↔B, C↔A, B↔D) depend on both density and gas viscosity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sintering — Stages and Mechanisms

Sintering proceeds through **three distinct stages**, and KTU examiners routinely test this sequence.

**Stage 1: Initial Stage (Neck Formation)**
- Point contacts between particles develop into necks.
- Neck radius grows, but particles are still distinguishable.
- No significant shrinkage occurs.
- Rate controlled by surface diffusion, volume diffusion, or viscous flow.

**Stage 2: Intermediate Stage**
- Pores between particles become cylindrical/rounded.
- Grain boundaries form and grow.
- Significant densification (10–20 % shrinkage).
- Rate controlled by grain-boundary diffusion and lattice diffusion.

**Stage 3: Final Stage**
- Pores become isolated and spherical.
- Pore elimination; almost full densification.
- Microstructure coarsens; grain growth dominates.
- Rate controlled by lattice diffusion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mechanisms of Sintering (Six paths):**
> 1. Surface diffusion
> 2. Volume (lattice) diffusion
> 3. Grain-boundary diffusion
> 4. Viscous flow
> 5. Evaporation–condensation (vapor transport)
> 6. Plastic flow (under stress)

### 2.2 Effects of Sintering and Agglomeration on Fluidization

- **Minimum fluidization velocity (U_mf) increases** because particle size and density grow.
- **Bed defluidizes** when sintered/agglomerated lumps become too heavy.
- **Channeling and gas bypassing** dominate; conversion efficiency drops.
- **Heat and mass transfer coefficients** drop sharply once agglomerates form.
- In catalytic processes, **catalyst activity is lost** due to loss of surface area.

### 2.3 Geldart's Classification — Boundaries on the Diagram

The Geldart diagram plots **(ρ_p − ρ_f)** on the y-axis (kg/m³) versus **d_p** (μm) on the x-axis (log–log scale). Boundaries are drawn as empirical curves.

- The **A/B boundary** corresponds approximately to:
  
$$d_p \approx 100 \ \mu m \quad \text{(for } \rho_p \approx 2500 \text{ kg/m}^3\text{)}$$

- The **A/C boundary** is set by interparticle cohesive forces overcoming fluid drag.
- The **B/D boundary** depends on the size at which bubbles become larger than particles and jets dominate.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam-Critical Equations for Sintering and Geldart Classification**

| # | Formula | Description / Meaning | Units |
|:--|:--------|:----------------------|:------|
| 1 | $U_{mf} = \dfrac{d_p^{\,2} \left(\rho_p - \rho_f\right) g}{1650 \mu}$ | Minimum fluidization velocity (laminar, d_p < 100 μm) | m/s |
| 2 | $U_{mf} = \dfrac{d_p^{1.82} \left(\rho_p - \rho_f\right)^{0.94} g^{0.94}}{1650 \, \mu^{0.88} \, \rho_f^{0.06}}$ | Minimum fluidization velocity (Ergun, general) | m/s |
| 3 | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \left(1 - \varepsilon_{mf}\right) \left(\rho_p - \rho_f\right) g$ | Bed pressure drop at fluidization | Pa/m |
| 4 | $\dfrac{x}{a} = \left(\dfrac{kt}{a^2}\right)^{n}$ | Sintering neck-radius growth (Kingery–Bhat–Coble) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\left(\dfrac{x}{a}\right)^{2} = \dfrac{k\,t}{a^2}$ | Initial-stage sintering, surface diffusion, n = 1/2 | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\dfrac{x}{a} = \dfrac{k\,t}{a^2}$ | Initial-stage sintering, viscous flow, n = 1 | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = k\,t^{n}$ | Shrinkage kinetics (isothermal sintering) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{Ar} = \dfrac{d_p^{\,3}\,\rho_f \left(\rho_p - \rho_f\right) g}{\mu^2}$ | Archimedes number (used in Geldart diagrams for viscous correction) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{Re}_{mf} = \dfrac{d_p\, U_{mf}\,\rho_f}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number at minimum fluidization | dimensionless |
| 10 | $U_t = \dfrac{d_p^{\,2}\left(\rho_p - \rho_f\right) g}{18 \mu}$ | Terminal settling velocity (Stokes' law) | m/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **Sintering equations (4–7)** are typically asked as **5–7 mark theory** questions. The KTU Formula Sheet above is the **minimum** you must memorize for a 14-mark problem.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Catalytic cracking (FCC units):** Catalyst regeneration occurs at ~700 °C; sintering must be controlled to retain surface area.
- **Coal combustion:** Coal ash contains low-melting potassium/vanadium compounds that cause bed agglomeration and defluidization.
- **Pharma and food drying:** Geldart A powders are chosen to ensure uniform fluidized-bed drying.
- **Iron ore reduction:** Geldart D particles dominate; spouted beds are mandatory.
- **Polymer coating:** Geldart B powders are preferred for even coating.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Sintering Neck-Growth Equation (Initial Stage)

**Starting Point (Two-Sphere Model):**
Consider two identical spheres of radius $a$ in point contact. A neck of radius $x$ forms between them after time $t$ at temperature $T$. The geometry is shown below conceptually:

- Neck radius: $x$
- Sphere radius: $a$
- Radius of curvature of the neck: $\rho$
- Surface area: $A_s$
- Volume: $V$
- Surface energy: $\gamma_s$

The driving force for sintering is the **Laplace pressure** (curvature-induced stress) given by:

$$\Delta P = \gamma_s \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho} - \dfrac{1}{x}\right) \approx -\dfrac{\gamma_s}{\rho}$$

> The negative sign indicates a **compressive stress** that drives atomic diffusion toward the neck region.

**Step 1 — Geometric relationship between $x$, $a$, and $\rho$:**

From the geometry of two intersecting spheres:

$$\rho \approx \dfrac{x^2}{2a}$$

**Step 2 — Material transport (vacancy diffusion mechanism):**

The flux of atoms into the neck is driven by the gradient in chemical potential. The volume of material transported per unit time into the neck is given by:

$$\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A_s \, D \, \gamma_s \, \Omega}{k_B T \, \rho \, L}$$

where
- $D$ = diffusion coefficient (m²/s)
- $\Omega$ = atomic volume (m³)
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant (J/K)
- $L$ = diffusion path length (m)
- $A_s$ = cross-sectional area for diffusion (m²)

**Step 3 — Volume of the neck:**

The neck is approximated as a toroid. Its volume is:

$$V \approx \dfrac{\pi^2 x^4}{2a}$$

**Step 4 — Combine geometry and transport:**

Differentiating $V$ with respect to $t$:

$$\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{2\pi^2 x^3}{a} \cdot \dfrac{dx}{dt}$$

Setting this equal to the material transport rate and simplifying:

$$\dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{A_s \, D \, \gamma_s \, \Omega \, a}{2\pi^2 \, k_B T \, L \, x^3 \, \rho}$$

Substituting $\rho = x^2 / 2a$:

$$\dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{A_s \, D \, \gamma_s \, \Omega \, a^2}{\pi^2 \, k_B T \, L \, x^5}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate (assuming $A_s$, $D$, $L$ are constants):**

$$\int_0^x x^5 \, dx = \dfrac{A_s \, D \, \gamma_s \, \Omega \, a^2}{\pi^2 \, k_B T \, L} \int_0^t dt$$

$$\dfrac{x^6}{6} = \dfrac{A_s \, D \, \gamma_s \, \Omega \, a^2}{\pi^2 \, k_B T \, L} \, t$$

**Step 6 — Final form (neck-growth law):**

$$\boxed{\dfrac{x^6}{a^6} = \dfrac{6 A_s \, D \, \gamma_s \, \Omega}{\pi^2 \, k_B T \, L} \cdot \dfrac{t}{a^4} = k_s \cdot \dfrac{t}{a^4}}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\dfrac{x}{a} = \left(k_s \cdot \dfrac{t}{a^4}\right)^{1/6}$$

This is the classical **Kingery–Bhat–Coble** sintering equation for initial-stage, surface-diffusion-controlled sintering.

**General Power-Law Form:**

$$\dfrac{x}{a} = \left(\dfrac{k \, t}{a^2}\right)^{n}$$

where the exponent $n$ depends on the dominant mechanism:

| Mechanism | n |
|:----------|:-:|
| Viscous flow | 1 |
| Volume diffusion | 1/3 |
| Grain-boundary diffusion | 1/4 |
| Surface diffusion | 1/5 |
| Evaporation–condensation | 1/3 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** If a problem states "the dominant mechanism is viscous flow," you must write $(x/a) = (kt/a^2)^1$, **not** $1/3$ or $1/5$. KTU examiners **always** test this distinction.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Archimedes Number and Geldart Boundary

The Archimedes number represents the ratio of gravitational to viscous forces on a particle:

$$\text{Ar} = \dfrac{\text{buoyancy-corrected gravity}}{\text{viscous drag}} = \dfrac{d_p^{\,3}\,\rho_f \left(\rho_p - \rho_f\right) g}{\mu^2}$$

In fluidized bed correlations, the **Reynolds number at minimum fluidization** is often written as:

$$\text{Re}_{mf} = \dfrac{d_p\, U_{mf}\,\rho_f}{\mu}$$

For laminar flow at minimum fluidization, $\text{Re}_{mf} < 20$. Equating the Ergun equation's pressure drop at incipient fluidization to the bed weight per unit area yields:

$$\dfrac{1.75}{\phi_s \varepsilon_{mf}^{\,3}} \text{Re}_{mf}^{\,2} + \dfrac{150 \left(1 - \varepsilon_{mf}\right)}{\phi_s^{\,2} \varepsilon_{mf}^{\,3}} \text{Re}_{mf} = \text{Ar}$

For spherical particles ($\phi_s = 1$) with $\varepsilon_{mf} = 0.4$, this reduces to:

$$\text{Re}_{mf} = \sqrt{27.2^2 + 0.0408 \,\text{Ar}} - 27.2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the Wen & Yu (1966) correlation** — the most widely used general equation for $U_{mf}$ and a KTU examiner's favorite. It correctly handles Geldart groups A, B, and D.

**Geldart Boundary Derivation (Molerus Approximation):**

For the **A/B boundary**, Molerus (1982) showed that the critical condition corresponds to the transition from non-bubbling to bubbling fluidization. The boundary satisfies:

$$d_p^{*} = d_p \, \rho_f^{\,1/3} \left(\dfrac{g \left(\rho_p - \rho_f\right)}{\mu^2}\right)^{1/3} \approx 0.1 \ \text{to} \ 0.2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reduced particle diameter $d_p^{*}$** is the canonical parameter on the modern Geldart diagram. The boundaries between groups are:
> - **A/C boundary:** $d_p^{*} \approx 0.1$
> - **A/B boundary:** $d_p^{*} \approx 0.2$
> - **B/D boundary:** $\text{Re}_{mf} \approx 20$ (i.e., $d_p^{*} \approx 0.6$)

### 3.3 Worked Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A bed of glass beads is fluidized with air at 25 °C. The particle density is $\rho_p = 2500$ kg/m³, the mean particle size is $d_p = 60$ μm, and the bed voidage at minimum fluidization is $\varepsilon_{mf} = 0.42$. Determine the Geldart group and the minimum fluidization velocity. Given: $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $\rho_f = 1.2$ kg/m³.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the Archimedes number:**

$$\text{Ar} = \dfrac{d_p^{\,3}\,\rho_f \left(\rho_p - \rho_f\right) g}{\mu^2} = \dfrac{(60 \times 10^{-6})^3 \cdot 1.2 \cdot (2500 - 1.2) \cdot 9.81}{(1.8 \times 10^{-5})^2}$$

Numerator:

$$(60 \times 10^{-6})^3 = 2.16 \times 10^{-13} \text{ m}^3$$

$$2.16 \times 10^{-13} \cdot 1.2 \cdot 2498.8 \cdot 9.81 = 6.35 \times 10^{-9}$$

Denominator:

$$(1.8 \times 10^{-5})^2 = 3.24 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$\text{Ar} = \dfrac{6.35 \times 10^{-9}}{3.24 \times 10^{-10}} = 19.6$$

**Step 2 — Compute Re_mf using Wen & Yu:**

$$\text{Re}_{mf} = \sqrt{27.2^2 + 0.0408 \cdot 19.6} - 27.2$$

$$= \sqrt{739.84 + 0.80} - 27.2 = \sqrt{740.64} - 27.2 = 27.21 - 27.2 = 0.01$$

**Step 3 — Compute U_mf:**

$$U_{mf} = \dfrac{\text{Re}_{mf} \cdot \mu}{d_p \cdot \rho_f} = \dfrac{0.01 \cdot 1.8 \times 10^{-5}}{60 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1.2} = \dfrac{1.8 \times 10^{-7}}{7.2 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Determine the Geldart group:**

For $d_p = 60$ μm and $\rho_p = 2500$ kg/m³, the point lies in the **Group A (Aeratable)** region of the Geldart diagram. This is consistent with the very low Re_mf of 0.01 (laminar bubbling expansion will occur before bubbles form).

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** Geldart Group A, $U_{mf} \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s (2.5 mm/s).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Sintering Process Flow and Consequences in a Fluidized Bed

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Particulate Feed d_p and rho_p"] --> node2["Fluidized Bed at High T"]
    node2 --> node3["Stage 1 Neck Formation"]
    node3 --> node4["Stage 2 Pore Rounding"]
    node4 --> node5["Stage 3 Pore Elimination"]
    node5 --> node6["Sintered Agglomerate"]
    node6 --> node7["U_mf Increases"]
    node6 --> node8["Channeling and Bypassing"]
    node6 --> node9["Loss of Surface Area"]
    node6 --> node10["Bed Defluidization"]
    node7 --> node11["Process Shutdown"]
    node8 --> node11
    node9 --> node11
    node10 --> node11
    node2 --> node12["Heat Transfer Drops"]
    node12 --> node11
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The sintering process is a one-way path that ultimately leads to defluidization. The only practical remedies are (a) lowering operating temperature, (b) using additives to suppress sintering (e.g., SO₂ to reduce ash stickiness), or (c) switching to alternative process technology.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Geldart Classification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Solid Particle Properties d_p and rho_p"] --> nodeB{"d_p less than 20 micrometers"}
    nodeB -- Yes --> nodeC{"Cohesive Forces Dominate"}
    nodeB -- No --> nodeD{"d_p between 20 and 100 micrometers"}
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeE["Geldart Group C Cohesive"]
    nodeC -- No --> nodeF["Geldart Group A Aeratable"]
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeG["Geldart Group A Aeratable"]
    nodeD -- No --> nodeH{"d_p between 100 and 800 micrometers"}
    nodeH -- Yes --> nodeI["Geldart Group B Sand Like"]
    nodeH -- No --> nodeJ["Geldart Group D Spoutable"]
    nodeE --> nodeK["Channeling and Plugging"]
    nodeF --> nodeL["Smooth Fluidization with Expansion"]
    nodeI --> nodeM["Bubbling at U_mf"]
    nodeJ --> nodeN["Spouting or Jetting Required"]
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Fluidized Bed Showing Sintering Hot Spots

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["Gas Distributor"] --> nodeQ["Bottom Plenum"]
    nodeQ --> nodeR["Main Fluidized Bed"]
    nodeR --> nodeS["Freeboard"]
    nodeS --> nodeT["Cyclone and Dust Collection"]
    nodeR --> nodeU["Internal Heat Exchanger Tubes"]
    nodeU --> nodeV["Hot Spot Near Tubes"]
    nodeV --> nodeW["Local Sintering Initiation"]
    nodeW --> nodeX["Agglomerate Growth Zone"]
    nodeX --> nodeY["Defluidization Front"]
    nodeY --> nodeZ["Bed Collapse"]
    nodeR --> nodeAA["Continued Feed and Gas"]
    nodeAA --> nodeZ
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering interpretation:** Hot spots near immersed heat-exchanger tubes (especially in boilers and FCC regenerators) are the **most common nucleation sites** for sintering. Tube surface temperature must be kept below the sintering onset temperature (T_sinter) of the bed material.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Define sintering. How does it differ from agglomeration in a fluidized bed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Sintering (1 mark):** Sintering is a thermally activated process in which contacting solid particles bond together at temperatures below their melting point due to atomic/molecular diffusion, forming necks and growing into a dense mass.
- **Agglomeration (1 mark):** Agglomeration is the broader phenomenon of particles sticking together to form clusters due to interparticle forces (van der Waals, capillary, electrostatic, or partial sintering).
- **Key difference (1 mark):** Sintering is specifically a **high-temperature, solid-state diffusion** phenomenon, whereas agglomeration can occur at any temperature from **physical forces** (e.g., moist particles, electrostatics). Sintering produces a permanent, irreversible bond; some forms of agglomeration are reversible.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **Classify the four Geldart groups of particles. Give one industrial example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Group A (Aeratable):** Small particles, 20–100 μm, low density difference. **Example:** FCC catalyst in catalytic cracking.
- **Group B (Sand-like):** Medium particles, 100–800 μm, moderate density. **Example:** Ordinary sand in a fluidized-bed dryer.
- **Group C (Cohesive):** Very fine particles, < 20 μm. **Example:** Cement, flour, talcum powder.
- **Group D (Spoutable):** Large/heavy particles, > 800 μm. **Example:** Wheat grains in spouted-bed dryers, iron ore pellets.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Sintering Focus

**Q3 (a).** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

> **Describe the three stages of sintering with suitable diagrams. Discuss the consequences of sintering on fluidized-bed operation.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Stage 1: Initial Stage (Neck Formation) — 2 marks**
- Adjacent particles are in point contact at the start.
- Atomic diffusion (surface, volume, or grain-boundary) drives mass transport to the contact point.
- A neck forms; neck radius $x$ grows with time according to $(x/a)^m = kt/a^2$.
- No appreciable shrinkage of the body.

**Stage 2: Intermediate Stage — 2 marks**
- Necks grow; pores between particles become cylindrical and rounded.
- Grain boundaries form and migrate; significant densification begins (10–20 %).
- Rate controlled by grain-boundary and lattice diffusion.

**Stage 3: Final Stage — 1 mark**
- Pores become isolated, closed, and spherical.
- Slow pore elimination, dense polycrystalline structure, grain growth.
- Rate controlled by lattice diffusion.

**Consequences on Fluidization — 2 marks**
- Particle size grows → $U_{mf}$ increases.
- Surface area is lost → catalytic activity drops.
- Bed may defluidize, channels form, gas bypassing occurs.
- Heat- and mass-transfer coefficients fall.

---

**Q3 (b).** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

> **Starting from first principles, derive the initial-stage sintering equation for a surface-diffusion-controlled mechanism. State the assumptions clearly.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Two identical spheres of radius $a$ in initial point contact.
- Isothermal sintering, constant temperature $T$.
- Surface diffusion is the dominant mass-transport mechanism.
- Small neck approximation: $x \ll a$.

**Derivation (6 marks):**

**Step 1:** Geometric relation from two-sphere geometry:

$$\rho \approx \dfrac{x^2}{2a}$$

**Step 2:** Volume of the neck (toroid approximation):

$$V \approx \dfrac{\pi^2 x^4}{2a}$$

**Step 3:** Driving force — Laplace pressure:

$$\Delta \mu \approx \dfrac{-\gamma_s \Omega}{\rho}$$

**Step 4:** Surface-diffusion flux (atoms per unit area per second):

$$J_s = -\dfrac{D_s}{k_B T} \nabla \mu \approx \dfrac{D_s \gamma_s \Omega}{k_B T \rho L_s}$$

where $L_s$ is the surface diffusion length (≈ $a$).

**Step 5:** Rate of volume deposition into the neck:

$$\dfrac{dV}{dt} = A_s J_s \cdot \Omega \approx \dfrac{D_s \gamma_s \Omega^{\,2}}{k_B T \, a}$$

with $A_s \approx 2\pi x a$ (diffusion area on the sphere).

**Step 6:** Differentiating $V$ with respect to $t$:

$$\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{2\pi^2 x^3}{a} \cdot \dfrac{dx}{dt}$$

**Step 7:** Equate the two rates and integrate:

$$\dfrac{2\pi^2 x^3}{a} \cdot \dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{2\pi x a \cdot D_s \gamma_s \Omega^{\,2}}{k_B T a}$$

$$\pi^2 x^2 \cdot \dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{D_s \gamma_s \Omega^{\,2}}{k_B T}$$

$$\int_0^x x^2 \, dx = \dfrac{D_s \gamma_s \Omega^{\,2}}{\pi^2 k_B T} \int_0^t dt$$

$$\dfrac{x^3}{3} = \dfrac{D_s \gamma_s \Omega^{\,2}}{\pi^2 k_B T} \cdot t$$

**Step 8:** Final form (surface diffusion, n = 1/3):

$$\boxed{\dfrac{x^3}{a^3} = \dfrac{3 D_s \gamma_s \Omega^{\,2}}{\pi^2 k_B T \, a^2} \cdot t = k_s \cdot \dfrac{t}{a^2}}$$

> **Note:** The exponent 3 in $x^3$ corresponds to $n = 1/3$ in the general power-law form, distinguishing surface diffusion from viscous flow (n = 1) and grain-boundary diffusion (n = 1/4).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Geldart Classification Focus

**Q4 (a).** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

> **Explain Geldart's classification of particles in detail. Draw the Geldart diagram and describe the fluidization characteristics of each group.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Geldart (1973) classified particles into four groups — A, B, C, D — based on mean particle size $d_p$ and the density difference $(\rho_p - \rho_f)$. The classification is empirical and is plotted on a log–log diagram of $(\rho_p - \rho_f)$ versus $d_p$.

**Group A — Aeratable (1.5 marks):**
- $d_p \approx 20$–$100$ μm, low $\rho_p$ (e.g., 1400 kg/m³).
- Smooth fluidization with notable bed expansion **before** bubbling starts.
- Bubbles form only at $U_{mb} > U_{mf}$.
- Examples: FCC catalyst, fine alumina.

**Group B — Sand-like (1.5 marks):**
- $d_p \approx 100$–$800$ μm, $\rho_p \approx 1400$–$4500$ kg/m³.
- Bubbles appear **immediately** at $U = U_{mf}$.
- No particulate phase expansion.
- Examples: Sand, glass beads.

**Group C — Cohesive (1.5 marks):**
- $d_p < 20$ μm.
- Interparticle forces exceed fluid drag.
- Channels, plugs, rat-holes; very poor or impossible fluidization in conventional beds.
- Requires mechanical agitation, vibration, or addition of larger "starter" particles.
- Examples: Cement, talc, flour.

**Group D — Spoutable (1.5 marks):**
- $d_p > 800$ μm or very dense.
- Bed requires high gas velocity; jets and spouts form.
- Conventional fluidization is sluggish.
- Examples: Wheat, coal, roasting ores, lead shot.

> **Diagram:** A schematic Geldart diagram is rendered in Mermaid in Section 4.2 above.

---

**Q4 (b).** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

> **A fluidized bed operates with air at 25 °C. The particles have $d_p = 250$ μm and $\rho_p = 1800$ kg/m³. Determine (i) the Archimedes number, (ii) Re_mf using the Wen & Yu correlation, and (iii) the Geldart group of the particles. Given: $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $\rho_f = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\varepsilon_{mf} = 0.42$, $g = 9.81$ m/s².** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Archimedes number (2 marks):**

$$\text{Ar} = \dfrac{d_p^{\,3}\,\rho_f \left(\rho_p - \rho_f\right) g}{\mu^2}$$

$$d_p^{\,3} = (250 \times 10^{-6})^3 = 1.5625 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3$$

$$\text{Ar} = \dfrac{1.5625 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 1.2 \cdot 1798.8 \cdot 9.81}{(1.8 \times 10^{-5})^2} = \dfrac{3.305 \times 10^{-4}}{3.24 \times 10^{-10}} = 1020$$

**Step 2 — Re_mf via Wen & Yu (3 marks):**

$$\text{Re}_{mf} = \sqrt{27.2^2 + 0.0408 \cdot \text{Ar}} - 27.2$$

$$= \sqrt{739.84 + 41.62} - 27.2 = \sqrt{781.46} - 27.2 = 27.95 - 27.2 = 0.75$$

**Step 3 — Geldart group (2 marks):**

For $d_p = 250$ μm and $\rho_p = 1800$ kg/m³, the point lies in the **Group B (Sand-like)** region of the Geldart diagram. Confirm: Re_mf = 0.75 > 0.001 (Group A threshold) and bubbles form immediately at U_mf, consistent with Group B.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students **forget to compute Re_mf first** and jump to the Geldart group. The full mark is only awarded when Re_mf is calculated **and** used to confirm the classification. **Always show both.**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Sintering + Geldart):**
> 1. **Confusing $U_{mf}$ and $U_t$:** $U_{mf}$ is the velocity at which the bed just fluidizes; $U_t$ is the terminal velocity of a single particle. They are **different** by a factor of ~50–80 for typical powders.
> 2. **Wrong exponent $n$ in sintering:** If the question says "grain-boundary diffusion is rate-controlling," you must use $n = 1/4$, not $n = 1/3$ or $n = 1/5$. This is the **#1 cause of lost marks** in sintering problems.
> 3. **Confusing the A/B boundary:** Some students say "Group A is for $d_p < 100$ μm." The boundary is **not** a straight line — it depends on $\rho_p$. Always use the diagram.
> 4. **Using Stokes' drag in gas systems:** Stokes' law is **wrong** for gas fluidization at moderate Reynolds numbers. Always use the Ergun or Wen & Yu correlation.
> 5. **Saying "sintering = melting":** Sintering occurs **below the melting point**. Writing "particles melt and fuse" loses **at least 1 mark**.
> 6. **Skipping units in the final answer:** KTU examiners **strictly** deduct 0.5 marks for missing units in $U_{mf}$ calculations.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Sintering** is high-temperature atomic bonding **without melting**; it proceeds in **three stages** (neck formation → pore rounding → pore elimination).
- [ ] The general sintering law is $(x/a) = (kt/a^2)^n$, with $n$ depending on mechanism:
  - Viscous flow: $n = 1$
  - Volume diffusion: $n = 1/3$
  - Surface diffusion: $n = 1/3$
  - Grain-boundary diffusion: $n = 1/4$
- [ ] **Agglomeration** is a broader phenomenon than sintering; causes include capillary, electrostatic, and viscous forces in addition to partial sintering.
- [ ] Sintering and agglomeration cause **defluidization**, **higher $U_{mf}$**, **loss of surface area**, **channeling**, and **reduced heat transfer**.
- [ ] **Geldart's four groups**:
  - **A** (20–100 μm): aeratable, smooth fluidization with expansion.
  - **B** (100–800 μm): sand-like, immediate bubbling.
  - **C** (< 20 μm): cohesive, channeling.
  - **D** (> 800 μm): spoutable, requires spouting.
- [ ] The **Geldart diagram** is plotted as $(\rho_p - \rho_f)$ vs $d_p$ on log–log axes; the modern version uses the **reduced diameter $d_p^{*}$**.
- [ ] **Wen & Yu correlation**: $\text{Re}_{mf} = \sqrt{27.2^2 + 0.0408 \,\text{Ar}} - 27.2$ is the **canonical** equation for $U_{mf}$ in KTU problems.
- [ ] The **Archimedes number** $\text{Ar} = d_p^{\,3}\,\rho_f (\rho_p - \rho_f) g / \mu^2$ is the **dimensionless group** that captures all material properties for fluidization.
- [ ] **Hot spots** near immersed heat-exchanger tubes are the **most common** sites for sintering initiation in industrial fluidized beds.
- [ ] **Mitigation strategies**: lower operating temperature, add anti-sintering agents (e.g., SO₂ for coal ash), use mechanical agitation for Group C powders, switch to spouted beds for Group D.
- [ ] For **2-mark definition questions**, always state (a) the phenomenon, (b) the driving force, and (c) the consequence in a fluidized bed.
- [ ] For **7-mark problems**, always derive equations with **clear assumptions** and **labeled diagrams**; KTU examiners reward stepwise working.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Mapping of Fluidization Regimes (Grace Diagram)

### Formal Academic Definition
A **fluidization regime map** (popularly called the **Grace Diagram**, first proposed by John R. Grace in 1986) is a two-dimensional, dimensionless phase plot that demarcates the various hydrodynamic flow regimes encountered when a fluid (gas or liquid) is passed upward through a bed of solid particles. It is the most important universal engineering tool used to predict, scale up, and design commercial fluidized bed reactors, combustors, dryers, and coating units. The plot correlates the **dimensionless particle diameter (d\*)** on the abscissa with the **dimensionless superficial gas velocity (u\*)** on the ordinate, while superimposing the boundaries of the **Geldart particle classification (Groups A, B, C, D)**.

The dimensionless groups are mathematically defined as:

$$d^* = d_p \left[ \frac{\rho_g(\rho_p - \rho_g)g}{\mu^2} \right]^{1/3}$$

$$u^* = u \left[ \frac{\rho_g^2}{\mu(\rho_p - \rho_g)g} \right]^{1/3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Grace diagram is a **mandatory 14-mark question topic** in Part B of Module 1. Every KTU student must be able to draw, label, and interpret the regime map, including the location of Geldart Group boundaries and the transition velocities (Minimum Fluidization, Bubbling-to-Turbulent Choking, Turbulent-to-Fast, and Transport Disengaging).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a **kitchen aquarium filled with sand**, sitting on top of an air pump. As you slowly increase the air flow:
- At first, the air simply sneaks through the **gaps between stationary sand grains** — this is the *packed bed*.
- Increase further, and the bed **suddenly "boils"** like thick lava — this is *minimum fluidization* and then *bubbling fluidization* (large bubbles rising like in a hot boiling pot of dal).
- Crank the air even more, and the bubbles lose identity, the surface vanishes into a chaotic cloud — this is *turbulent fluidization*.
- Turn the pump to maximum, and the sand grains are **flung out the top like volcanic ash** — this is *pneumatic conveying*.
- Now, if you had used a *different* sand (heavier, coarser, or finer), the *exact same sequence* would happen at *totally different air flow rates*. The Grace diagram is the **master chart** that tells you, for any sand and any air, exactly which regime you are operating in. It removes the guesswork by using dimensionless numbers that lump together all the physical properties (size, density, viscosity) into a single universal map.

### Identification of Flow Regimes
The Grace diagram identifies the following seven principal gas–solid fluidization regimes, ordered from low to high gas velocity:

| Sl. No. | Regime | Characteristic Feature |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Packed Bed (Fixed Bed)** | Particles stationary; fluid percolates through interstices |
| 2 | **Distributor Zone** | Local jetting near the gas distributor plate |
| 3 | |**Minimum Fluidization (U_mf)** | Incipient condition where bed pressure drop equals buoyant weight |
| 4 | **Bubbling Fluidization** | Discrete gas bubbles rising through a dense emulsion phase |
| 5 | **Slugging Fluidization** | Bubbles coalesce to fill entire column cross-section |
| 6 | **Turbulent Fluidization** | Bubbles disintegrate; surface highly indistinct |
| 7 | **Fast Fluidization** | Entrainment significant; requires cyclone recycle |
| 8 | **Pneumatic Conveying** | Particles transported in dilute phase suspension |

## Particle Size Distribution (PSD)

### Formal Definition
The **Particle Size Distribution (PSD)** is a statistical, mathematical, and graphical representation of the relative amounts (typically by mass, volume, or number) of particles of different sizes present in a granular powder sample. It is the single most important property characterizing a fluidization feedstock, since it directly determines the **Geldart Group**, the **minimum fluidization velocity (U_mf)**, the **terminal velocity (U_t)**, and the **bubble size distribution** in a fluidized bed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants & Metrics Used in PSD Analysis:**
> - **Standard Sieve Series (Tyler/ISO):** 45, 63, 90, 125, 180, 250, 355, 500, 710, 1000, 1400, 2000 µm
> - **Reference Diameter d_p** for Geldart classification
> - **Density of air (ρ_g) at STP ≈ 1.225 kg/m³** (standard reference)
> - **Density of water (ρ_f) at 25°C ≈ 997 kg/m³**

### Conceptual Analogy — The Pebble Beach Analogy
Imagine you go to a beach and scoop up a bucket of mixed sand and pebbles. If you pour this bucket through a set of stacked sieves (largest holes on top, smallest at the bottom), each sieve will retain the particles that are too big to fall through. By weighing what is retained on each sieve, you can describe the *complete* size makeup of the bucket — how much is "gravel", how much is "coarse sand", how much is "fine sand", and how much is "silt". This recipe of sizes is the **Particle Size Distribution**. A well-designed fluidized bed reactor demands a *controlled* PSD, because too much fines causes channeling, while too many coarse particles cause dead zones and defluidization.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## A. Construction Logic of the Grace Diagram

The Grace diagram is constructed by plotting the logarithms of dimensionless velocity and diameter:

$$\log(u^*) = f[\log(d^*)]$$

The primary axes combine all four key physical properties: **particle diameter (d_p)**, **particle density (ρ_p)**, **gas density (ρ_g)**, and **gas viscosity (μ)**, plus gravity (**g = 9.81 m/s²**). This dimensional reduction means a *single* universal map applies to air-fluidized sand, steam-fluidized catalysts, or nitrogen-fluidized polymer particles.

### Geldart Group Boundaries on the Grace Diagram

- **Geldart Group A (Aeratable):** Small, low-density particles (catalysts). d\* range roughly **0.04 to 1.0**. Exhibit a non-bubbling region of stable expansion between U_mf and U_mb (minimum bubbling velocity).
- **Geldart Group B (Sand-like / Bubbling):** Most common industrial powders. d\* range roughly **1.0 to 10**. Bubbles form immediately at U_mf.
- **Geldart Group C (Cohesive):** Ultra-fine powders (d_p < 30 µm). Channel and crack instead of fluidizing.
- **Geldart Group D (Spoutable):** Large, dense particles. d\* > 10. Require spouting or high velocities.

### Why is the Grace Map Important? (Real-World Engineering Utility)
- **Scale-up:** Predicts whether a 0.1 m lab reactor will behave like a 5 m commercial unit.
- **Reactor Sizing:** Engineers locate their operating point (d\*, u\*) to choose a safe regime.
- **Troubleshooting:** A bed that suddenly channels is identified as having moved into the Group C region.
- **FCC Units:** Fluid Catalytic Cracking regenerators operate deliberately in the *turbulent* regime for optimum heat transfer.
- **Coal Combustors:** Operate in the *bubbling* regime for Geldart B coal particles.

## B. Particle Size Distribution: Theoretical Framework

### Cumulative and Differential Distributions

A powder is divided into narrow size intervals. For sieve i with aperture $d_i$:

$$x_i = \frac{m_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} m_j}$$

where $x_i$ is the **mass fraction retained** on sieve $i$, and $\sum x_i = 1.0$.

**Cumulative fraction oversize** (mass fraction larger than $d_i$):

$$F_{\text{oversize}}(d_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{i} x_j$$

**Cumulative fraction undersize** (mass fraction smaller than $d_i$):

$$F_{\text{undersize}}(d_i) = \sum_{j=i+1}^{N} x_j = 1 - F_{\text{oversize}}$$

> [!TIP]
> For plotting, $F_{\text{undersize}}$ is plotted on the **y-axis (probability scale)** versus particle diameter on the **x-axis (log scale)**. The intersection of the curve with the 50% line gives the **mass-median diameter d₅₀**.

## KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Meaning / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dimensionless Particle Diameter | $d^* = d_p \left[ \dfrac{\rho_g(\rho_p - \rho_g)g}{\mu^2} \right]^{1/3}$ | x-axis of Grace diagram; combines all system properties |
| 2 | Dimensionless Gas Velocity | $u^* = u \left[ \dfrac{\rho_g^2}{\mu(\rho_p - \rho_g)g} \right]^{1/3}$ | y-axis of Grace diagram |
| 3 | Archimedes Number | $Ar = \dfrac{d_p^3 \rho_g(\rho_p - \rho_g)g}{\mu^2} = (d^*)^3$ | Index of gravity-to-viscous ratio |
| 4 | Reynolds Number at U_mf | $Re_{mf} = \dfrac{d_p u_{mf} \rho_g}{\mu}$ | Used in Ergun equation |
| 5 | Mass Fraction Retained | $x_i = \dfrac{m_i}{\sum m_j}$ | Discrete PSD point; dimensionless |
| 6 | Cumulative Undersize | $F_{\text{undersize}} = \sum_{j=i+1}^{N} x_j$ | Used to find d₅₀; dimensionless |
| 7 | Arithmetic Mean Diameter | $\bar{d}_a = \sum x_i d_i$ | First moment; µm or m |
| 8 | Sauter Mean Diameter (SMD) | $d_{sv} = \dfrac{1}{\sum \left(\dfrac{x_i}{d_i}\right)}$ | Volume-to-surface mean; critical for mass transfer |
| 9 | Mass-Median Diameter (MMD) | $F_{\text{undersize}}(d_{50}) = 0.50$ | Median by mass; µm |
| 10 | Geometric Standard Deviation | $\sigma_g = \dfrac{d_{84}}{d_{50}} = \dfrac{d_{50}}{d_{16}}$ | Width/spread of distribution |
| 11 | Rosin-Rammler Distribution | $F_{\text{oversize}} = \exp\left[-\left(\dfrac{d}{d'}\right)^n\right]$ | Two-parameter model; d' = size constant, n = distribution modulus |
| 12 | Geldart Boundary (A/B) | $d^* \approx 1.0$ | Practical demarcation between aeratable and bubbling powders |
| 13 | Geldart Boundary (B/D) | $d^* \approx 10$ | Demarcation between bubbling and spoutable powders |
| 14 | Minimum Fluidization (Ergun) | $1.75 \dfrac{Re_{mf}^2}{\epsilon_{mf}^3} \psi + \dfrac{150(1-\epsilon_{mf})Re_{mf}}{\epsilon_{mf}^3}\psi^2 = Ar$ | Combined Ergun-Kunii-Levenspiel for U_mf |
| 15 | Choking Velocity (Yang) | $\dfrac{u_{ch}}{\sqrt{gd_p}} = \left[\left(\dfrac{32}{Re_t}\right) + \left(\dfrac{0.0671 Re_t}{1 + 3.08 \times 10^{-4} Re_t^2}\right)\right]^{1/2}$ | Bubbling-to-Turbulent transition |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The dimensionless groups $d^*$ and $u^*$ are **NOT** independent dimensionless numbers (like Reynolds, Froude, etc.). They are *combined* dimensionless numbers specifically designed for the Grace diagram. Many students confuse them with Reynolds number — do not!

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Example

## 3.1 Derivation: Linking the Grace Diagram to the Archimedes–Reynolds Plane

The Archimedes number ($Ar$) and Reynolds number ($Re$) are defined as:

$$Ar = \frac{d_p^3 \rho_g(\rho_p - \rho_g)g}{\mu^2} \quad \text{and} \quad Re = \frac{d_p u \rho_g}{\mu}$$

**Step 1:** Cube the dimensionless diameter $d^*$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(d^*)^3 &= \left( d_p \left[\frac{\rho_g(\rho_p - \rho_g)g}{\mu^2}\right]^{1/3} \right)^3 \\
&= d_p^3 \cdot \frac{\rho_g(\rho_p - \rho_g)g}{\mu^2} \\
&= Ar
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** The cube of $d^*$ equals the Archimedes number directly. This is a critical relationship: $d^* = Ar^{1/3}$.

**Step 2:** Form the ratio $u^*/d^*$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{u^*}{d^*} &= \frac{u \left[\dfrac{\rho_g^2}{\mu(\rho_p - \rho_g)g}\right]^{1/3}}{d_p \left[\dfrac{\rho_g(\rho_p - \rho_g)g}{\mu^2}\right]^{1/3}} \\
&= \frac{u}{d_p} \cdot \left[ \frac{\rho_g^2}{\mu(\rho_p - \rho_g)g} \cdot \frac{\mu^2}{\rho_g(\rho_p - \rho_g)g} \right]^{1/3} \\
&= \frac{u}{d_p} \cdot \left[ \frac{\rho_g \mu}{(\rho_p - \rho_g)^2 g^2} \right]^{1/3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $\rho_g$ inside the cube root to extract Reynolds number:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{u^*}{d^*} &= \frac{u \rho_g}{d_p \rho_g} \cdot \left[ \frac{\mu \rho_g^2}{(\rho_p - \rho_g)^2 g^2 \rho_g} \right]^{1/3} \\
&= \frac{Re}{d_p \rho_g} \cdot \left[ \frac{\mu \rho_g}{(\rho_p - \rho_g)^2 g^2} \right]^{1/3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Through a similar rearrangement (substituting $Ar$ for $d^{*3}$ and simplifying), one arrives at the clean result:

$$
u^* = \frac{Re}{Ar^{1/3}}
$$

**Textual Conclusion:** Therefore, **any Grace diagram point is a 1-to-1 mapping from a (d\*, u\*) coordinate to an (Ar, Re) coordinate** via the transformation $d^* = Ar^{1/3}$ and $u^* = Re / Ar^{1/3}$. This is why the Grace diagram is universal — it is simply a *renormalized* Ar–Re plot.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Calculation of Particle Size Distribution

**Problem Statement [KTU University Exam Pattern]:**
A sand sample is subjected to sieve analysis. The mass retained on successive sieves is given below. Calculate:
(a) Mass fraction $x_i$ retained on each sieve
(b) Cumulative fraction *oversize* $F_{\text{oversize}}$
(c) Cumulative fraction *undersize* $F_{\text{undersize}}$
(d) Mass-median diameter $d_{50}$
(e) Arithmetic mean diameter $\bar{d}_a$
(f) Sauter mean diameter $d_{sv}$
(g) Geometric standard deviation $\sigma_g$
(h) Comment on the Geldart classification if $\rho_p = 2500$ kg/m³ and fluidizing gas is air at STP.

| Sieve No. | Aperture $d_i$ (µm) | Mass Retained $m_i$ (g) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1000 | 5 |
| 2 | 710 | 18 |
| 3 | 500 | 42 |
| 4 | 355 | 75 |
| 5 | 250 | 88 |
| 6 | 180 | 60 |
| 7 | 125 | 40 |
| 8 | 90 | 22 |
| Pan | < 90 | 10 |
| **Total** | — | **360** |

### Solution Step-by-Step

**Part (a) — Mass Fraction Retained $x_i$:**

Using $x_i = m_i / m_{\text{total}}$, where $m_{\text{total}} = 360$ g:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= 5/360 = 0.0139 \\
x_2 &= 18/360 = 0.0500 \\
x_3 &= 42/360 = 0.1167 \\
x_4 &= 75/360 = 0.2083 \\
x_5 &= 88/360 = 0.2444 \\
x_6 &= 60/360 = 0.1667 \\
x_7 &= 40/360 = 0.1111 \\
x_8 &= 22/360 = 0.0611 \\
x_{\text{pan}} &= 10/360 = 0.0278
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $\sum x_i = 0.0139 + 0.0500 + 0.1167 + 0.2083 + 0.2444 + 0.1667 + 0.1111 + 0.0611 + 0.0278 = 1.0000$ ✓

**Part (b) — Cumulative Oversize $F_{\text{oversize}}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{ov}}(d_1=1000) &= x_1 = 0.0139 \\
F_{\text{ov}}(d_2=710)  &= x_1 + x_2 = 0.0639 \\
F_{\text{ov}}(d_3=500)  &= 0.0639 + 0.1167 = 0.1806 \\
F_{\text{ov}}(d_4=355)  &= 0.1806 + 0.2083 = 0.3889 \\
F_{\text{ov}}(d_5=250)  &= 0.3889 + 0.2444 = 0.6333 \\
F_{\text{ov}}(d_6=180)  &= 0.6333 + 0.1667 = 0.8000 \\
F_{\text{ov}}(d_7=125)  &= 0.8000 + 0.1111 = 0.9111 \\
F_{\text{ov}}(d_8=90)   &= 0.9111 + 0.0611 = 0.9722 \\
F_{\text{ov}}(\text{pan}) &= 0.9722 + 0.0278 = 1.0000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (c) — Cumulative Undersize $F_{\text{undersize}}$:**

$$
F_{\text{und}}(d_i) = 1 - F_{\text{ov}}(d_i)
$$

| Sieve | $d_i$ (µm) | $F_{\text{undersize}}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1000 | 0.9861 |
| 2 | 710 | 0.9361 |
| 3 | 500 | 0.8194 |
| 4 | 355 | 0.6111 |
| 5 | 250 | 0.3667 |
| 6 | 180 | 0.2000 |
| 7 | 125 | 0.0889 |
| 8 | 90 | 0.0278 |
| Pan | — | 0.0000 |

**Part (d) — Mass-Median Diameter $d_{50}$:**

Locate the sieve where $F_{\text{undersize}}$ crosses 0.50. From the table, $F_{\text{undersize}} = 0.6111$ at 355 µm and $F_{\text{undersize}} = 0.3667$ at 250 µm. Interpolate using log-linear interpolation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log d_{50} &= \log(355) - \frac{0.6111 - 0.50}{0.6111 - 0.3667} \cdot [\log(355) - \log(250)] \\
\log(355) &= 2.5502 \\
\log(250) &= 2.3979 \\
\Delta \log d &= 0.1523 \\
\text{Fraction} &= \frac{0.1111}{0.2444} = 0.4547 \\
\log d_{50} &= 2.5502 - 0.4547 \times 0.1523 = 2.5502 - 0.0693 = 2.4809 \\
d_{50} &= 10^{2.4809} \approx 302.7 \text{ µm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (e) — Arithmetic Mean Diameter $\bar{d}_a$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{d}_a &= \sum x_i d_i \\
&= (0.0139 \times 1000) + (0.0500 \times 710) + (0.1167 \times 500) \\
&\quad + (0.2083 \times 355) + (0.2444 \times 250) + (0.1667 \times 180) \\
&\quad + (0.1111 \times 125) + (0.0611 \times 90) + (0.0278 \times 45^*) \\
&= 13.90 + 35.50 + 58.35 + 73.95 + 61.10 + 30.01 + 13.89 + 5.50 + 1.25 \\
&\approx 293.4 \text{ µm}
\end{aligned}
$$

(*Assumed pan average diameter = midpoint between 0 and 90 µm = 45 µm.)

**Part (f) — Sauter Mean Diameter $d_{sv}$:**

$$
d_{sv} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i} \left(\dfrac{x_i}{d_i}\right)}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum \frac{x_i}{d_i} &= \frac{0.0139}{1000} + \frac{0.0500}{710} + \frac{0.1167}{500} + \frac{0.2083}{355} + \frac{0.2444}{250} \\
&\quad + \frac{0.1667}{180} + \frac{0.1111}{125} + \frac{0.0611}{90} + \frac{0.0278}{45} \\
&= 1.39 \times 10^{-5} + 7.04 \times 10^{-5} + 2.334 \times 10^{-4} + 5.867 \times 10^{-4} + 9.776 \times 10^{-4} \\
&\quad + 9.261 \times 10^{-4} + 8.888 \times 10^{-4} + 6.789 \times 10^{-4} + 6.178 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 5.564 \times 10^{-3} \text{ µm}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
d_{sv} = \frac{1}{5.564 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 179.7 \text{ µm}
$$

**Part (g) — Geometric Standard Deviation $\sigma_g$:**

We need $d_{16}$ and $d_{84}$. From the table:
- $F_{\text{undersize}} = 0.20$ at 180 µm and $F_{\text{undersize}} = 0.0889$ at 125 µm. To get $F = 0.16$, interpolate between 180 and 125:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log d_{16} &= \log(180) + \frac{0.20 - 0.16}{0.20 - 0.0889} \times [\log(125) - \log(180)] \\
\log(180) &= 2.2553, \quad \log(125) = 2.0969 \\
\Delta \log &= -0.1584 \\
\text{Fraction} &= \frac{0.04}{0.1111} = 0.3600 \\
\log d_{16} &= 2.2553 + 0.3600 \times (-0.1584) = 2.2553 - 0.0570 = 2.1983 \\
d_{16} &\approx 158.0 \text{ µm}
\end{aligned}
$$

- $F_{\text{undersize}} = 0.8194$ at 500 µm and $0.6111$ at 355 µm. To get $F = 0.84$, extrapolate to a larger size:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log d_{84} &= \log(500) + \frac{0.84 - 0.8194}{1.0 - 0.8194} \times [\log(710) - \log(500)] \\
\log(500) &= 2.6990, \quad \log(710) = 2.8513 \\
\text{Fraction} &= \frac{0.0206}{0.1806} = 0.1140 \\
\log d_{84} &= 2.6990 + 0.1140 \times 0.1523 = 2.6990 + 0.0174 = 2.7164 \\
d_{84} &\approx 520.5 \text{ µm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\sigma_g = \sqrt{\frac{d_{84}}{d_{16}}} = \sqrt{\frac{520.5}{158.0}} = \sqrt{3.294} \approx 1.815
$$

Equivalently, $\sigma_g = d_{84}/d_{50} = 520.5/302.7 = 1.72$, and $d_{50}/d_{16} = 302.7/158.0 = 1.92$. The average gives $\sigma_g \approx 1.82$, consistent with above.

> [!NOTE]
> Since $\sigma_g = 1.82$ is between 1.4 and 2.0, this represents a **moderately broad** size distribution — typical of naturally graded industrial powders.

**Part (h) — Geldart Classification:**

We need $d^*$ using $\rho_p = 2500$ kg/m³, $\rho_g = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $g = 9.81$ m/s², and $d_p \approx d_{sv} = 179.7 \text{ µm} = 1.797 \times 10^{-4}$ m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
d^* &= d_p \left[\frac{\rho_g(\rho_p - \rho_g)g}{\mu^2}\right]^{1/3} \\
\left[\frac{\rho_g(\rho_p - \rho_g)g}{\mu^2}\right]^{1/3} &= \left[\frac{1.225 \times (2500-1.225) \times 9.81}{(1.81 \times 10^{-5})^2}\right]^{1/3} \\
&= \left[\frac{1.225 \times 2498.775 \times 9.81}{3.2761 \times 10^{-10}}\right]^{1/3} \\
&= \left[\frac{30020}{3.2761 \times 10^{-10}}\right]^{1/3} = (9.164 \times 10^{13})^{1/3} \\
&\approx 4505 \text{ m}^{-1} \\
d^* &= 1.797 \times 10^{-4} \times 4505 \approx 0.810
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $d^* \approx 0.81$ lies between **0.04 and 1.0**, this is **Geldart Group A (aeratable powder)** — typical of fine catalyst material. To confirm with the $d_p$–$\rho_p$ chart: at $\rho_p = 2500$ kg/m³ and $d_p \approx 180$ µm, the point is well inside Group A.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Implication:** A Geldart A powder has a *non-bubbling* region between $U_{mf}$ and $U_{mb}$ where the bed expands smoothly. This is excellent for uniform gas–solid contact in catalytic reactors (e.g., FCC, polyolefin production).
