The distributor pressure drop itself is given by a standard orifice equation:

$$\Delta P_d = \frac{\rho_g \cdot U_{or}^2}{2 \, C_d^2}$$

Where **C_d** is the discharge coefficient of the orifice (typically **0.6 to 0.8** for sharp-edged holes).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Correlation | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|----------------------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | Orifice velocity | $U_{or} = U \cdot A_{bed} / (N_{or} \cdot A_{or})$ | m/s |
| 2 | Distributor pressure drop | $\Delta P_d = \rho_g U_{or}^2 / (2 C_d^2)$ | Pa |
| 3 | Bed pressure drop (Ergun) | $\Delta P_b = H(1-\varepsilon_m)(ρ_p - ρ_g)g$ | Pa |
| 4 | Design rule | $\Delta P_d / \Delta P_b = 0.10 - 0.30$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | **Zenz jet penetration** | $L_j / d_{or} = 6.5 \left(\dfrac{\rho_g U_{or}^2}{g d_p \rho_p}\right)^{0.5}$ | m |
| 6 | **Basu–Mandal–Kunii** | $L_j = 7.5 d_{or}\left(\dfrac{\rho_g U_{or}^2}{g d_p (\rho_p - \rho_g)}\right)^{0.5}$ | m |
| 7 | Jet half-angle | $\theta = 20° - 30°$ (≈ 23° avg) | degrees |
| 8 | Minimum bed height for jet dissipation | $H_{min} \geq L_j + d_{or}$ | m |
| 9 | Jet diameter at height z | $D_j(z) = d_{or} + 2 z \tan \theta$ | m |
| 10 | Orifice-to-bed area ratio | $\alpha = N_{or} A_{or} / A_{bed}$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Tip for the Board Exam:** Memorize correlations 2, 4, 5, and 6. These four account for ~70% of marks in any Gas Entry Region numerical problem.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of Gas Entry Region Analysis |
|----------|------------------------------------------|
| **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** | Distributor design in riser reactors — jet penetration governs catalyst attrition losses |
| **Coal Combustion (CFB Boilers)** | Tuyere jet depth determines secondary air penetration and char burnout |
| **Wastewater Treatment** | Aeration diffusers — bubble formation is essentially the "jet-to-bubble transition" |
| **Pharma / Food Drying** | Fluidized bed dryers — jet zone sets minimum product hold-up |
| **Polymerization Reactors** | Gas-phase polyethylene — distributor jets control catalyst feed zone temperature |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Distributor Pressure Drop Ratio

**Starting Point:** The total pressure drop across the bed + distributor system must equal the supply blower head **ΔP_total**. For uniform fluidization:

$$\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_d + \Delta P_b$$

The "design safety" criterion requires that the distributor pressure drop dominate over local maldistribution tendencies. Define the **fractional distributor drop** as **R**:

$$R = \frac{\Delta P_d}{\Delta P_b}$$

The stability condition is obtained by ensuring that a ±10% variation in ΔP_b (e.g., due to a localized defluidization) does **not** reverse the flow in neighboring orifices. The classical Qureshi–Creasy criterion:

> If $R \geq 0.10$, then a 10% local increase in ΔP_b only steals about 9% flow from neighboring orifices — acceptable maldistribution.

Mathematically, the maldistribution factor **M** is:

$$M = \frac{1}{1 + R}$$

For R = 0.10 → M ≈ 0.91. For R = 0.30 → M ≈ 0.77.

## 3.2 Derivation: Zenz Jet Penetration Correlation (Form Origin)

**Step 1 — Dimensional Grouping.**
The jet penetration length **L_j** depends on: orifice diameter **d_or**, orifice velocity **U_or**, gas density **ρ_g**, particle density **ρ_p**, particle diameter **d_p**, and gravity **g**. So **L_j / d_or = f(ρ_g, ρ_p, d_p, U_or, g)**.

**Step 2 — Buckingham Pi Theorem.**
Five independent Pi groups emerge. The dominant force balance is **jet momentum flux** vs **buoyancy weight of displaced bed**:

$$\pi_1 = \frac{\rho_g U_{or}^2}{g d_p (\rho_p - \rho_g)}$$

**Step 3 — Empirical Calibration (Zenz, 1968).**
Experimental data fitted to a square-root power law:

$$\frac{L_j}{d_{or}} = 6.5 \, (\pi_1)^{0.5}$$

Substituting the Pi group:

$$L_j = 6.5 \, d_{or} \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_g \, U_{or}^2}{g \, d_p \, (\rho_p - \rho_g)}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example — KTU Board Standard Numerical

> **[Problem Statement]**
> A fluidized bed uses air at 25°C to fluidize sand. Given:
> - Bed cross-sectional area = **1.0 m²**
> - Number of orifices = **200**, each of diameter **d_or = 4 mm**
> - Superficial gas velocity U = **0.4 m/s**
> - Air density ρ_g = **1.2 kg/m³**
> - Sand: ρ_p = **2500 kg/m³**, d_p = **300 μm**
> - Discharge coefficient C_d = **0.65**
>
> **Calculate:**
> (a) Orifice velocity U_or
> (b) Distributor pressure drop ΔP_d
> (c) Jet penetration height L_j using Zenz correlation
> (d) Minimum safe bed height H_min

---

### Part (a): Orifice Velocity

$$A_{or} = \frac{\pi d_{or}^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.004)^2}{4} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{m}^2$$

$$A_{bed} = 1.0 \;\text{m}^2$$

$$U_{or} = \frac{U \cdot A_{bed}}{N_{or} \cdot A_{or}} = \frac{0.4 \times 1.0}{200 \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$U_{or} = \frac{0.4}{2.513 \times 10^{-3}} = 159.15 \;\text{m/s}$$

### Part (b): Distributor Pressure Drop

$$\Delta P_d = \frac{\rho_g U_{or}^2}{2 C_d^2} = \frac{1.2 \times (159.15)^2}{2 \times (0.65)^2}$$

$$\Delta P_d = \frac{1.2 \times 25329}{0.845} = \frac{30395}{0.845}$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta P_d = 35{,}970 \;\text{Pa} \approx 36.0 \;\text{kPa}\;}$$

### Part (c): Jet Penetration (Zenz)

$$L_j = 6.5 \, d_{or} \sqrt{\frac{\rho_g U_{or}^2}{g \, d_p (\rho_p - \rho_g)}}$$

Plug in: d_or = 0.004 m, ρ_g = 1.2, U_or = 159.15, g = 9.81, d_p = 3 × 10⁻⁴, ρ_p = 2500

Numerator inside the square root:

$$\rho_g U_{or}^2 = 1.2 \times 25329 = 30395 \;\text{kg/(m·s²)}$$

Denominator inside the square root:

$$g \, d_p (\rho_p - \rho_g) = 9.81 \times 3 \times 10^{-4} \times (2500 - 1.2)$$

$$= 9.81 \times 3 \times 10^{-4} \times 2498.8 = 7.353 \;\text{kg/(m·s²)}$$

Ratio:

$$\frac{30395}{7.353} = 4133$$

Square root:

$$\sqrt{4133} = 64.29$$

Therefore:

$$L_j = 6.5 \times 0.004 \times 64.29 = 1.672 \;\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{\;L_j \approx 1.67 \;\text{m}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **enormous** relative to a typical laboratory bed (H ≈ 0.3 m). It indicates the chosen orifice velocity is too high — the operator would experience massive attrition, jetting, and "blowout."

### Part (d): Minimum Bed Height

$$H_{min} \geq L_j + d_{or} = 1.67 + 0.004 \approx 1.67 \;\text{m}$$

In practice, designers use a safety factor of **1.5**:

$$\boxed{\;H_{design} = 1.5 \times H_{min} \approx 2.5 \;\text{m}\;}$$

---

## 3.4 Sensitivity Matrix — How Output Changes With Input

| Parameter Changed | Effect on L_j | Effect on ΔP_d | Engineering Implication |
|-------------------|---------------|----------------|-------------------------|
| ↑ d_or (larger holes) | ↑ (linear) | ↓ (quadratic) | Fewer but bigger jets; coarser distribution |
| ↑ U (higher gas rate) | ↑ (∝ U) | ↑ (∝ U²) | Excessive jetting, attrition |
| ↑ ρ_p (heavier particles) | ↓ (∝ 1/√ρ_p) | No direct effect | Bed becomes easier to fluidize uniformly |
| ↓ d_p (finer particles) | ↑ (∝ 1/√d_p) | No direct effect | Geldart A behavior, channeling risk |
| ↑ N_or (more orifices) | No direct effect | ↓ (∝ 1/N_or²) | Better distribution, lower ΔP_d |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Block Diagram of the Gas Entry Region

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PL["Plenum Chamber (Pressurized Gas Reservoir)"] --> GR["Gas Distributor / Grid Plate"]
    GR --> OR["Orifices / Nozzles (N_or, d_or)"]
    OR --> JT["JET ZONE (Gas Entry Region)"]
    JT --> J1["Stage 1: Formation (gaseous core)"]
    JT --> J2["Stage 2: Transition (particle entrainment)"]
    JT --> J3["Stage 3: Developed Jet (steady, angle theta)"]
    JT --> J4["Stage 4: Disintegration at L_j"]
    JT --> J5["Stage 5: Bubble Coalescence"]
    J5 --> BZ["Bulk Fluidization Zone (above H_min)"]
    GR -.->|"Pressure Drop ΔP_d"| PDROP
    BZ -.->|"Pressure Drop ΔP_b"| PDROP
    PDROP["Total ΔP_d + ΔP_b must equal Blower Head"]
```

## 4.2 Jet Geometry Schematic (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DIST["DISTRIBUTOR PLATE"]
        O1["Orifice 1"]
        O2["Orifice 2"]
        O3["Orifice N"]
    end

    subgraph GER["GAS ENTRY REGION (Height = L_j)"]
        J1["Jet Cone 1: Diameter D_j at height z"]
        J2["Jet Cone 2"]
        J3["Jet Cone N"]
        DZ["Dead Zones Between Jets (Poorly Fluidized)"]
        AT["Attrition Zone (Particle Erosion)"]
    end

    subgraph BULK["BULK FLUIDIZED BED"]
        BB["Bubbles Rise and Coalesce"]
        EM["Emulsion Phase"]
    end

    O1 --> J1
    O2 --> J2
    O3 --> J3
    J1 --> BB
    J2 --> BB
    J3 --> BB
    J1 --- DZ
    J2 --- DZ
    J3 --- DZ
    GER --> BULK
```

## 4.3 Jet Penetration Profile (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z0["z = 0: Orifice exit, D = d_or, U = U_or"] --> Z1["z = 0.2 L_j: Particle entrainment begins"]
    Z1 --> Z2["z = 0.5 L_j: Fully developed jet, theta = 23 deg"]
    Z2 --> Z3["z = L_j: Disintegration into bubble"]
    Z3 --> Z4["z > L_j: Bulk fluidization established"]
```

## 4.4 Decision Logic for Distributor Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Begin Distributor Design"] --> Q1{"ΔP_d / ΔP_b >= 0.10 ?"}
    Q1 -- "No" --> INCREASE["Increase U_or or reduce N_or"]
    INCREASE --> Q1
    Q1 -- "Yes" --> Q2{"ΔP_d / ΔP_b <= 0.30 ?"}
    Q2 -- "No" --> DECREASE["Reduce U_or or increase N_or"]
    DECREASE --> Q2
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> Q3{"H_bed >= 1.5 x L_j ?"}
    Q3 -- "No" --> RAISE["Increase Bed Height"]
    RAISE --> Q3
    Q3 -- "Yes" --> DONE["Design Acceptable"]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the Gas Entry Region of a fluidized bed. What is meant by jet penetration height?*

**Model Answer:**
The Gas Entry Region is the zone immediately above the distributor where the gas entering through individual orifices forms high-velocity jets that penetrate into the bed before merging into the bulk fluidization zone. The **jet penetration height (L_j)** is the maximum vertical distance above an orifice that the jet maintains its coherent identity before disintegrating into a bubble. It is a function of orifice diameter, orifice velocity, gas and particle densities, and particle size. Above L_j, the bed behaves as a classical Geldart-type bubbling fluidized bed. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional II]**
*State the importance of the distributor pressure drop ratio (ΔP_d / ΔP_b) in fluidized bed design.*

**Model Answer:**
The distributor pressure drop ratio is critical because it determines **gas distribution uniformity**. The classical design criterion requires **ΔP_d / ΔP_b = 0.10 to 0.30**. If the ratio is below 0.10, gas tends to channel through low-resistance paths causing maldistribution, defluidization zones, and hot spots. If it exceeds 0.30, excessive blower power is consumed without operational benefit. An optimum value of **0.20** is typically used as a safety margin. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive, with appropriate assumptions, the Zenz correlation for jet penetration height in a fluidized bed. Clearly state the dimensionless groups used and the empirical constant value. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fluidized bed has the following specifications:
- Distributor: 250 holes, each d_or = 5 mm
- Bed area = 1.2 m², superficial velocity = 0.5 m/s
- Air ρ_g = 1.184 kg/m³, glass beads ρ_p = 2450 kg/m³, d_p = 400 μm
- C_d = 0.65

Calculate:
- (i) Orifice velocity U_or
- (ii) Distributor pressure drop ΔP_d
- (iii) Jet penetration length L_j (Zenz)
- (iv) Whether a bed height of 1.2 m is sufficient. Comment. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):** Step-by-step derivation as in Section 3.2 above. **Award 1 mark for stating Pi groups, 3 marks for the derivation, 1 mark for the empirical constant 6.5, 1 mark for the final equation, 1 mark for assumptions (jet momentum balance, steady state, axisymmetric jet).**

**Part (b):**

**(i) Orifice velocity:**

$$U_{or} = \frac{U \cdot A_{bed}}{N_{or} \cdot (\pi d_{or}^2 / 4)} = \frac{0.5 \times 1.2}{250 \times \pi \times (0.005)^2 / 4}$$

$$U_{or} = \frac{0.6}{250 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.6}{4.909 \times 10^{-3}} = 122.2 \;\text{m/s}$$

**(ii) Distributor pressure drop:**

$$\Delta P_d = \frac{1.184 \times (122.2)^2}{2 \times (0.65)^2} = \frac{17678}{0.845} = 20{,}920 \;\text{Pa} \approx 20.9 \;\text{kPa}$$

**(iii) Jet penetration (Zenz):**

$$L_j = 6.5 \times 0.005 \times \sqrt{\frac{1.184 \times (122.2)^2}{9.81 \times 4 \times 10^{-4} \times (2450 - 1.184)}}$$

$$\text{Numerator inside sqrt} = 1.184 \times 14933 = 17678$$

$$\text{Denominator inside sqrt} = 9.81 \times 4 \times 10^{-4} \times 2449 = 9.609$$

$$\text{Ratio} = 17678 / 9.609 = 1839.7$$

$$\sqrt{1839.7} = 42.89$$

$$L_j = 6.5 \times 0.005 \times 42.89 = 1.394 \;\text{m}$$

**(iv) Comparison:**
H_bed = 1.2 m < L_j = 1.394 m. Therefore the bed height is **insufficient**. Jets will penetrate to the bed surface, causing severe attrition and possibly bypassing of particles. Recommended: H ≥ 1.5 × L_j = **2.09 m** for safe operation.

---

### QUESTION B **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the different stages of jet formation and disintegration above a multi-orifice gas distributor in a fluidized bed. Discuss the role of jet angle and entrainment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bubbling fluidized bed operates with a perforated plate distributor having 180 orifices of 6 mm diameter. Operating conditions:
- Bed cross-section: 0.8 m × 0.8 m
- Gas (hot air): ρ_g = 0.95 kg/m³, μ_g = 3.0 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s
- U = 0.3 m/s, T = 400 K
- Particles: ρ_p = 1800 kg/m³, d_p = 250 μm
- Discharge coefficient C_d = 0.7

Calculate: (i) ΔP_d (ii) L_j using Basu–Mandal–Kunii (iii) Recommend bed height for safe operation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):** Discuss five stages: Formation → Transition → Developed Jet → Disintegration → Bubble Coalescence. Mention jet angle θ ≈ 20°–30°, particle entrainment via Kelvin–Helmholtz instability, and dead zones between adjacent jets. **Award 2 marks for sketch description, 2 marks for stages, 2 marks for jet angle and entrainment, 1 mark for dead zone discussion.**

**Part (b):**

**(i) ΔP_d:**

$$A_{or} = \pi \times (0.006)^2 / 4 = 2.827 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{m}^2$$

$$U_{or} = \frac{0.3 \times 0.64}{180 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.192}{5.089 \times 10^{-3}} = 37.73 \;\text{m/s}$$

$$\Delta P_d = \frac{0.95 \times (37.73)^2}{2 \times (0.7)^2} = \frac{1352.5}{0.98} = 1380 \;\text{Pa} \approx 1.38 \;\text{kPa}$$

**(ii) L_j (Basu–Mandal–Kunii):**

$$L_j = 7.5 \, d_{or} \sqrt{\frac{\rho_g U_{or}^2}{g d_p (\rho_p - \rho_g)}}$$

$$L_j = 7.5 \times 0.006 \times \sqrt{\frac{0.95 \times 1423}{9.81 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 1799.05}}$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 1352$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 9.81 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 1799 = 4.413$$

$$\text{Ratio} = 306.4, \quad \sqrt{306.4} = 17.50$$

$$L_j = 7.5 \times 0.006 \times 17.50 = 0.788 \;\text{m}$$

**(iii) Recommended bed height:**
$$H_{design} = 1.5 \times L_j = 1.5 \times 0.788 = 1.18 \;\text{m}$$
Recommended: **H_bed ≥ 1.2 m** for safe, attrition-free operation.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency** — Always convert μm to m and mm to m before plugging into formulas. A 10³ error in d_p completely flips the answer.
> 2. **Forgetting C_d² in denominator** — The orifice pressure drop formula has **C_d squared**, not C_d. Many students write C_d and lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Using ρ_p instead of (ρ_p − ρ_g)** — The buoyancy force uses the **density difference**, not the absolute particle density.
> 4. **Confusing Zenz constant** — Some texts cite 6.5, others 7.5 (Basu). State which correlation you are using at the top of your answer.
> 5. **No comparison with bed height** — Numerical answers must end with a **design recommendation** ("safe" / "unsafe" / "increase bed height to X m"). A bare number without engineering interpretation loses at least 1 mark.
> 6. **Neglecting to specify which Pi group** — In Part (a) derivation, examiners award marks for **explicitly listing the Pi groups** before fitting the empirical constant.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Gas Entry Region (PECHT415, Module 2)**
>
> - [x] **Definition:** Region above distributor where orifice jets dominate (height ≈ L_j)
> - [x] **Three components:** Plenum → Distributor → Jet zone
> - [x] **Jet penetration height L_j:** Zenz: $6.5 d_{or}\sqrt{\rho_g U_{or}^2/(g d_p \rho_p)}$
> - [x] **Alternative (Basu):** $7.5 d_{or}\sqrt{\rho_g U_{or}^2/(g d_p(\rho_p - \rho_g))}$
> - [x] **Orifice velocity:** $U_{or} = U A_{bed}/(N_{or} A_{or})$
> - [x] **Distributor ΔP_d:** $\rho_g U_{or}^2/(2 C_d^2)$ — typically 0.1–0.3 × ΔP_b
> - [x] **Design rule:** $\Delta P_d / \Delta P_b = 0.20$ optimum
> - [x] **Bed height rule:** $H_{bed} \geq 1.5 \times L_j$ for safe operation
> - [x] **Jet angle:** θ ≈ 20°–30° from vertical
> - [x] **Five jet stages:** Formation → Transition → Developed → Disintegration → Coalescence
> - [x] **Engineering impact:** Determines attrition rate, maldistribution, minimum H
> - [x] **Critical dimensions to memorize:** $d_{or}$ in m, $d_p$ in m, $U_{or}$ in m/s
> - [x] **Board exam trap:** Always state units and verify dimensional consistency of every term before writing the final answer

topics.title: Gas Jets in Fluidized Beds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gas Jets in Fluidized Beds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Gas Jet** in a fluidized bed is a localized, high-velocity gas stream that emerges from an orifice, nozzle, or tuyere into the surrounding dense phase of the bed. It is bounded by a *jet boundary* and consists of three distinct zones: the **potential core** (undiluted gas), the **transition zone**, and the **fully developed jet region**. Gas jets govern local hydrodynamics, particle entrainment, heat/mass transfer, and jet penetration depth ($L_j$) into the bed.

Mathematically, a gas jet is characterized by a **jet penetration depth** $L_j$ measured from the orifice tip to the point where the jet momentum is dissipated into the dense phase.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Water Jet into a Sand Pile":**
> Imagine pressing a high-pressure water hose into a tray of dry sand. A localized *cavity* forms around the water stream — this is your "jet cavity." The deeper you push, the more the sand collapses inward, just like particles in a fluidized bed. The water stream rapidly loses momentum and dissolves into the bulk sand. **That** is exactly what a gas jet does inside a fluidized bed: it carves out a gas-rich cavity, drags particles upward like a tornado, and eventually surrenders its kinetic energy to the surrounding dense phase.

**Real-world applications of gas jets:**
- **Fluid catalytic cracking (FCC) riser reactors** — Oil vapor jets shoot upward through regenerated catalyst.
- **Coal combustion fluidized beds** — Air jets from tuyeres fluidize coal particles.
- **Waste incinerators** — Secondary air jets penetrate to enhance mixing.
- **Polymerization reactors (gas-phase)** — Monomer jets into polymer bed.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Key Metrics

The following **standard physical constants and dimensionless groups** govern jet behavior:

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Magnitude |
|--------|----------|-------------------|
| $\rho_g$ | Gas density | **1.0 – 2.0 kg/m³** |
| $\rho_p$ | Particle density | **700 – 2500 kg/m³** |
| $u_o$ | Orifice (jet) gas velocity | **30 – 200 m/s** |
| $u_{mf}$ | Minimum fluidization velocity | **0.01 – 0.5 m/s** |
| $d_{or}$ | Orifice diameter | **5 – 50 mm** |
| $L_j$ | Jet penetration depth | **0.05 – 1.0 m** |
| $\theta_j$ | Jet cone half-angle | **~10°–25°** |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structure of a Single Vertical Gas Jet

A gas jet discharged vertically into a fluidized bed has a **three-zone axial structure**:

```
┌──────────────────────────────────┐
│  ZONE I — POTENTIAL CORE         │  Length ≈ 1–2 × d_or
│  (Undiluted, jet-like flow)      │  Velocity ≈ U_o
├──────────────────────────────────┤
│  ZONE II — TRANSITION ZONE       │  Rapid momentum loss
│  (Jet ↔ dense phase mixing)     │  Velocity decays
├──────────────────────────────────┤
│  ZONE III — FULLY DEVELOPED JET  │  Velocity ≈ u_mf or slightly above
│  (Dispersed into emulsion)       │  Length dominates L_j
└──────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 2.1.1 Why Three Zones?

- **Potential Core**: Inertial forces dominate over drag; particles cannot enter the high-velocity core.
- **Transition Zone**: Turbulent mixing and particle entrainment begin; momentum dissipates sharply.
- **Fully Developed Jet**: The jet's momentum has degraded into the bed; flow resembles the emulsion phase.

## 2.2 Key Parameters Defining Jet Behavior

### (a) Jet Penetration Depth ($L_j$)
The **most critical design parameter**. It determines:
- Spacing between multiple orifices
- Minimum bed height required to contain jet
- Catalyst/particle attrition control
- Heat transfer surface placement

### (b) Jet Cone Angle ($\theta_j$)
Defines lateral spread of the jet. Empirical values:
$$\theta_j \approx 14° - 20°$$
for most gas–solid systems in fluidization.

### (c) Jet Cavity Volume
$$V_{cavity} \approx \frac{\pi}{4} d_{or}^2 \cdot L_j \cdot f(\theta_j)$$
where $f(\theta_j)$ accounts for conical expansion.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Authorized Formula Sheet for Gas Jets in Fluidized Beds**

| # | Equation | Description / Use |
|---|----------|--------------------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{L_j}{d_{or}} = 15.0 \left(\dfrac{\rho_g}{\rho_p}\right)^{0.187} \left(\dfrac{u_o^2}{g\,d_{or}}\right)^{0.218}$ | **Zenz (1968)** — earliest correlation, gas jets in liquid-like beds |
| 2 | $\dfrac{L_j}{d_{or}} = 6.5 \left(\dfrac{\rho_g}{\rho_p}\right)^{0.5} + 0.043 \left[\dfrac{(u_o - u_{mf})^2}{g\,d_{or}}\right]^{0.5}$ | **Behie, Beeckmans, et al. (1975)** — most cited in KTU papers |
| 3 | $\dfrac{L_j}{d_{or}} = \dfrac{2.05}{Fr_{or}} + 1.35$ | **Kehoe & Davidson (1971)** — Froude number approach |
| 4 | $\dfrac{L_j}{d_{or}} = 5.25\left[\dfrac{\rho_g}{\rho_p} \cdot \dfrac{u_o^2}{g\,d_{or}}\right]^{0.264}$ | **Merry (1971)** — coarse particles (Geldart B/D) |
| 5 | $Fr_{or} = \dfrac{u_o}{\sqrt{g\,d_{or}}}$ | **Orifice Froude Number** (dimensionless) |
| 6 | $\theta_j \approx 20°$ (typical) | **Jet cone half-angle** (experimental) |
| 7 | $u_{jet}(x) = u_o \cdot \dfrac{K \cdot d_{or}}{x}$ | **Centerline velocity decay** in Zone III (K ≈ 6–7) |
| 8 | $L_j = L_{PC} + L_{FD}$ | Jet depth = Potential core + Fully developed length |
| 9 | $\rho_{g,eff} \approx \rho_g$ in jet core | Inertial gas dominates; particles excluded |
| 10 | $Re_{or} = \dfrac{\rho_g u_o d_{or}}{\mu_g}$ | **Orifice Reynolds number** (laminar/turbulent classification) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Do NOT mix up the correlations. Each is valid for a specific **Geldart particle class**:
> - **Behie** → Geldart A & B (catalysts, fine powders)
> - **Merry** → Geldart B & D (coarse particles, sand)
> - **Zenz** → All types, conservative estimate

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Gas jet correlations are **engineering-critical** for:

1. **Distributor Plate Design**: Determines the number and spacing of tuyeres to ensure uniform fluidization without dead zones.
2. **FCC Riser Design**: Monomer/catalyst contact time is set by jet penetration vs. riser height.
3. **Combustion Efficiency**: Air jets must penetrate deep enough to deliver O₂ across the bed cross-section.
4. **Particle Attrition Management**: Excessive jet velocity causes attrition; correlations provide upper-limit velocities.
5. **Heat Transfer Surface Erosion**: Tube bundles placed too close to orifice jets suffer erosion; jet length sets safe stand-off distance.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Jet Penetration Depth from Momentum Balance

The **jet penetration depth** can be derived from a one-dimensional momentum balance on the jet core.

### Step 1: Define the System
Consider a vertical gas jet emerging from an orifice of diameter $d_{or}$ with velocity $u_o$ into a fluidized bed of density $\rho_{bed}$.

### Step 2: Momentum Flux at the Orifice
The initial momentum flux (per unit time) at the orifice is:

$$M_o = \rho_g \cdot A_{or} \cdot u_o^2 = \rho_g \cdot \frac{\pi d_{or}^2}{4} \cdot u_o^2$$

This is the **incoming axial momentum** that must be dissipated.

### Step 3: Force Exerted by the Bed
The bed resists jet penetration through the **frictional drag** of particles and the **buoyancy of displaced particles**:

$$F_{resist} = \Delta P \cdot A_{jet}(x) + F_{drag}(x)$$

For a simplified model, the resistance force per unit length of jet is:

$$\frac{dF_{resist}}{dx} = C_D \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} d_{jet}(x) \cdot \rho_{bed} \cdot u_o^2$$

where $d_{jet}(x)$ is the local jet diameter, increasing linearly:

$$d_{jet}(x) = d_{or} + 2 x \tan(\theta_j)$$

### Step 4: Equate Momentum Flux to Integrated Resistance
Setting the initial jet momentum equal to the integrated resistance force gives the penetration depth:

$$\rho_g \cdot \frac{\pi d_{or}^2}{4} \cdot u_o^2 = \int_0^{L_j} C_D \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \rho_{bed} \cdot u_o^2 \cdot [d_{or} + 2x\tan(\theta_j)] \, dx$$

### Step 5: Solve the Integral
Let $A = d_{or}$ and $B = 2\tan(\theta_j)$. Then:

$$\int_0^{L_j} (A + Bx) \, dx = A L_j + \frac{B L_j^2}{2} = L_j\left(A + \frac{B L_j}{2}\right)$$

Substituting back:

$$\rho_g \cdot \frac{d_{or}^2}{4} = \frac{C_D}{2} \cdot \rho_{bed} \left[ d_{or} L_j + \tan(\theta_j) \cdot L_j^2 \right]$$

### Step 6: Solve the Quadratic for $L_j$
Rearranging into standard quadratic form $aL_j^2 + bL_j + c = 0$:

$$\tan(\theta_j) \cdot L_j^2 + d_{or} L_j - \frac{\rho_g \cdot d_{or}^2}{2 C_D \rho_{bed}} = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula $L_j = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$ (taking positive root):

$$L_j = \frac{-d_{or} + \sqrt{d_{or}^2 + \dfrac{2 \tan(\theta_j) \cdot \rho_g \cdot d_{or}^2}{C_D \rho_{bed}}}}{2 \tan(\theta_j)}$$

### Step 7: Final Compact Form
Factoring $d_{or}$:

$$L_j = \frac{d_{or}}{2 \tan(\theta_j)} \left[-1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2 \tan(\theta_j) \cdot \rho_g}{C_D \cdot \rho_{bed}}}\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** For large jet velocities ($u_o \gg u_{mf}$), the jet density ratio $\rho_g/\rho_{bed} \to$ small. The penetration depth is **inversely proportional to bed density** — denser beds (deep fluidization) absorb the jet momentum faster.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

### Problem
A gas jet emerges from an orifice of $d_{or} = 25$ mm at $u_o = 80$ m/s. The fluidized bed has $\rho_p = 1800$ kg/m³, $\rho_g = 1.2$ kg/m³, and $u_{mf} = 0.05$ m/s. Use the **Behie et al. (1975)** correlation to calculate the jet penetration depth.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Compute the dimensionless group on the right side.**

$$\frac{\rho_g}{\rho_p} = \frac{1.2}{1800} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 2: Compute $(u_o - u_{mf})$.**

$$u_o - u_{mf} = 80 - 0.05 = 79.95 \, \text{m/s} \approx 80 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3: Compute the inner bracket term.**

$$\frac{(u_o - u_{mf})^2}{g \cdot d_{or}} = \frac{(79.95)^2}{9.81 \times 0.025} = \frac{6392.0}{0.24525} = 26{,}063.3$$

**Step 4: Apply the Behie correlation.**

$$\frac{L_j}{d_{or}} = 6.5\left(\frac{\rho_g}{\rho_p}\right)^{0.5} + 0.043 \left[\frac{(u_o - u_{mf})^2}{g \, d_{or}}\right]^{0.5}$$

First term:
$$6.5 \times \sqrt{6.667 \times 10^{-4}} = 6.5 \times 0.02582 = 0.1678$$

Second term:
$$0.043 \times \sqrt{26{,}063.3} = 0.043 \times 161.44 = 6.942$$

**Step 5: Add the two contributions.**

$$\frac{L_j}{d_{or}} = 0.1678 + 6.942 = 7.11$$

**Step 6: Compute $L_j$.**

$$L_j = 7.11 \times 0.025 = 0.1778 \, \text{m} \approx 178 \, \text{mm}$$

### Final Answer
$$\boxed{L_j \approx 0.178 \, \text{m} = 178 \, \text{mm}}$$

---

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix of Jet Correlations

For a single test case, different correlations predict different penetration depths. KTU examiners often ask students to compare.

| Correlation | Formula Used | $L_j/d_{or}$ | $L_j$ (mm) | Particle Class |
|-------------|--------------|--------------|------------|----------------|
| Zenz (1968) | $15.0 \left(\dfrac{\rho_g}{\rho_p}\right)^{0.187} \left(\dfrac{u_o^2}{g d_{or}}\right)^{0.218}$ | 5.92 | 148 | All (conservative) |
| Behie (1975) | $6.5\sqrt{\rho_g/\rho_p} + 0.043 \sqrt{(u_o-u_{mf})^2/(g d_{or})}$ | 7.11 | 178 | A & B |
| Kehoe-Davidson (1971) | $2.05/Fr_{or} + 1.35$ | 5.18 | 130 | Coarse |
| Merry (1971) | $5.25\left[\rho_g/\rho_p \cdot u_o^2/(g d_{or})\right]^{0.264}$ | 6.45 | 161 | B & D |

Where $Fr_{or} = \dfrac{u_o}{\sqrt{g \cdot d_{or}}} = \dfrac{80}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.025}} = \dfrac{80}{0.4952} = 161.6$.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Single Vertical Gas Jet — Block Architecture Flow

The diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of a single vertical gas jet penetrating a fluidized bed, including all three zones and their dominant physics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GAS SUPPLY PLENUM] --> B[ORIFICE PLATE / TUYERE]
    B -->|d_or, u_o| C[ZONE I: POTENTIAL CORE]
    C -->|momentum loss starts| D[ZONE II: TRANSITION ZONE]
    D -->|momentum dissipated| E[ZONE III: FULLY DEVELOPED JET]
    E -->|gas diffuses into emulsion| F[EMULSION PHASE OF FLUIDIZED BED]
    F --> G[BUBBLE FORMATION AND COALESCENCE]
    G --> H[BED FREEBOARD]
    
    P[PARTICLES ENTRAINED UPWARD] -.-> C
    Q[PARTICLES RECIRCULATE DOWNWARD] -.-> D
    R[HEAT TRANSFER TO TUBES] -.-> E
    
    subgraph JETREGION[JET REGION]
        C
        D
        E
    end
```

## 4.2 Jet Penetration Design Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Define Operating Conditions] --> B{u_o and d_or known?}
    B -->|Yes| C{Particle Geldart Class?}
    B -->|No| D[Determine via Pressure Drop Curves]
    C -->|Class A or B| E[Apply BEHIE 1975 Correlation]
    C -->|Class B or D| F[Apply MERRY 1971 Correlation]
    C -->|Conservative design| G[Apply ZENZ 1968 Correlation]
    E --> H[Compute L_j]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I{L_j less than Bed Height?}
    I -->|Yes| J[DESIGN ACCEPTABLE]
    I -->|No| K[REDUCE u_o OR INCREASE d_or]
    K --> B
```

```mermaid

        OR[Orifice Diameter d_or]

    OR --> B

    OR --> KD

    VEL --> Z
    VEL --> KD

    GDEN --> Z
    PDEN --> B
    PDEN --> M
    PDEN --> Z

    B --> LJ
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> Explain the **three distinct zones** of a vertical gas jet in a fluidized bed with the help of a neat sketch.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A vertical gas jet emerging from an orifice into a fluidized bed exhibits the following three zones:

1. **Potential Core (Zone I)**: A region near the orifice where the gas velocity remains nearly equal to the orifice velocity $u_o$. Particles cannot enter this core due to high inertia. Length ≈ 1–2 $d_{or}$.

2. **Transition Zone (Zone II)**: Here, turbulent eddies begin to entrain particles and momentum rapidly dissipates due to inter-phase drag. The jet diameter starts to expand at angle $\theta_j \approx 14°–20°$.

3. **Fully Developed Jet (Zone III)**: The jet velocity decays to near $u_{mf}$, and gas disperses into the emulsion phase, forming bubbles.

**Sketch:**
```
   │ ←—— Potential Core ——→
   │  (high velocity u_o)  │
   │\                       │
   │ \  ← Transition →     │ \ ← Fully
   │  \                    │   Developed
   │   \                   │     Jet
   ════════════════════════════════════ (bed surface)
```

**[Marks: 1 mark each zone description + 0 mark bonus for sketch, total 3]**

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> Define **jet penetration depth** and state **any two empirical correlations** used for its prediction.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Jet penetration depth $L_j$ is the axial distance measured from the orifice tip to the point inside the fluidized bed where the jet momentum is fully dissipated into the emulsion phase.

**Correlations (2 marks — 1 each):**

(i) **Behie et al. (1975):**
$$\frac{L_j}{d_{or}} = 6.5 \left(\frac{\rho_g}{\rho_p}\right)^{0.5} + 0.043 \left[\frac{(u_o - u_{mf})^2}{g \, d_{or}}\right]^{0.5}$$

(ii) **Merry (1971):**
$$\frac{L_j}{d_{or}} = 5.25 \left[\frac{\rho_g}{\rho_p} \cdot \frac{u_o^2}{g \, d_{or}}\right]^{0.264}$$

(iii) **Zenz (1968):**
$$\frac{L_j}{d_{or}} = 15.0 \left(\frac{\rho_g}{\rho_p}\right)^{0.187} \left(\frac{u_o^2}{g \, d_{or}}\right)^{0.218}$$

(Any two accepted.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – KTU Model Paper 2024 / PECHT415]**

> (a) Derive an expression for the **jet penetration depth** from a momentum balance on a single vertical gas jet in a fluidized bed. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**
>
> (b) A gas jet emerges from an orifice of diameter $d_{or} = 0.04$ m at velocity $u_o = 60$ m/s into a fluidized bed of particle density $\rho_p = 1500$ kg/m³. The gas density is $\rho_g = 1.2$ kg/m³, and $u_{mf} = 0.06$ m/s. Compute the jet penetration depth using the **Behie (1975)** and **Merry (1971)** correlations. Comment on the difference. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Jet is vertical, axisymmetric.
2. Bed density $\rho_{bed}$ is constant (averaged emulsion phase).
3. Jet cone expands linearly with angle $\theta_j$.
4. Steady-state, isothermal flow.

**Momentum Balance Setup (3 marks):**

The initial axial momentum flux at the orifice:

$$M_o = \rho_g \cdot \frac{\pi d_{or}^2}{4} \cdot u_o^2$$

The jet expands as $d_{jet}(x) = d_{or} + 2 x \tan(\theta_j)$.

The drag resistance force per unit axial length is proportional to local jet diameter:

$$\frac{dF}{dx} = C_D \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \rho_{bed} \cdot u_o^2 \cdot d_{jet}(x)$$

**Integration and Quadratic Solution (3 marks):**

Equating momentum to integrated resistance and integrating from $0$ to $L_j$ yields the quadratic in $L_j$:

$$\tan(\theta_j) \cdot L_j^2 + d_{or} L_j - \frac{\rho_g \cdot d_{or}^2}{2 C_D \rho_{bed}} = 0$$

Solving via the quadratic formula (positive root):

$$L_j = \frac{d_{or}}{2\tan(\theta_j)} \left[ \sqrt{1 + \frac{2 \tan(\theta_j) \cdot \rho_g}{C_D \rho_{bed}}} - 1 \right]$$

---

#### ✅ Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Apply Behie Correlation (3 marks):**

Compute the dimensionless groups:

$$\frac{\rho_g}{\rho_p} = \frac{1.2}{1500} = 8.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$u_o - u_{mf} = 60 - 0.06 = 59.94 \, \text{m/s} \approx 60 \, \text{m/s}$$

$$\frac{(u_o - u_{mf})^2}{g \, d_{or}} = \frac{(59.94)^2}{9.81 \times 0.04} = \frac{3592.8}{0.3924} = 9156.0$$

Applying Behie:

$$\frac{L_j}{d_{or}} = 6.5\sqrt{8.0 \times 10^{-4}} + 0.043\sqrt{9156.0}$$

$$= 6.5 \times 0.02828 + 0.043 \times 95.69 = 0.1838 + 4.115 = 4.299$$

$$L_j^{Behie} = 4.299 \times 0.04 = 0.1720 \, \text{m} \approx 172 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Merry Correlation (2 marks):**

$$\frac{\rho_g}{\rho_p} \cdot \frac{u_o^2}{g \, d_{or}} = 8.0 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{(60)^2}{9.81 \times 0.04}$$

$$= 8.0 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{3600}{0.3924} = 8.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 9174.4 = 7.3395$$

$$\frac{L_j}{d_{or}} = 5.25 \times (7.3395)^{0.264} = 5.25 \times 1.881 = 9.876$$

$$L_j^{Merry} = 9.876 \times 0.04 = 0.3950 \, \text{m} \approx 395 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison (2 marks):**

| Correlation | $L_j$ (mm) | Particle Class Validity |
|-------------|------------|-------------------------|
| Behie (1975) | 172 | Geldart A & B (fine catalysts) |
| Merry (1971) | 395 | Geldart B & D (coarse sand-like) |

**Comment:** The **Merry correlation predicts more than double** the penetration depth compared to Behie. This is because Merry's correlation was developed using **coarse particles** (sand, Geldart B/D), where the bed is less aerated and jet momentum dissipates more slowly. The Behie correlation is more appropriate for **fine catalyst particles** (Geldart A), which form a denser, more aerated emulsion that absorbs jet momentum quickly.

---

### ⭐ Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – KTU Model Paper 2024 / PECHT415 Alternative]**

> (a) With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **structure of a gas jet** in a fluidized bed. Define the **jet penetration depth** and **jet cone angle**. Discuss **any three empirical correlations** used to predict jet penetration. **(7 marks)**
>
> (b) The minimum fluidization velocity of a gas-solid fluidized bed is $u_{mf} = 0.08$ m/s. The bed uses orifices of diameter $d_{or} = 30$ mm and operates at an orifice velocity of $u_o = 75$ m/s. For the gas and solid system, $\rho_g = 1.0$ kg/m³, $\rho_p = 2500$ kg/m³. Estimate the jet penetration depth using the **Zenz (1968)** correlation and compute the **minimum bed height** required to fully contain the jet without gas bypassing to the freeboard. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Diagram (2 marks):**
```
         ↑ ↑ ↑ ↑ ↑      ←  Gas freeboard
    ═══════════════════════════════════
    │\  ┌─────────────────────────────┐
    │ \ │  GAS JET REGION             │
    │  \│  ──────→  Cone half-angle θ_j│
    │   │                             │
    │   │   L_j (Jet penetration)     │
    │   │   ↓                         │
    │   ●─────────────────────────●  │  ← Dense Phase (Fluidized Bed)
    │   ●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●  │
    ═══════════════════════════════════
       ↑
       Orifice (d_or)
```

**Jet Structure Explanation (2 marks):**

A gas jet in a fluidized bed has three zones as explained in Section 2.1 above. (Describe potential core, transition, fully developed jet.)

**Definitions (1 mark):**

- **Jet Penetration Depth ($L_j$)**: Axial distance from orifice to the point where jet momentum is fully dissipated.
- **Jet Cone Angle ($\theta_j$)**: Half-angle defining the lateral spread of the jet (typically 14°–20°).

**Three Correlations (2 marks — any 3 from Section 2.3):**

(Enumerate Behie, Merry, Zenz, Kehoe-Davidson as listed in Section 2.3 above with formulas and applicability.)

---

#### ✅ Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify Given Data (1 mark):**

$$u_{mf} = 0.08 \, \text{m/s}, \quad d_{or} = 0.030 \, \text{m}, \quad u_o = 75 \, \text{m/s}$$
$$\rho_g = 1.0 \, \text{kg/m}^3, \quad \rho_p = 2500 \, \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Apply Zenz Correlation (3 marks):**

$$\frac{L_j}{d_{or}} = 15.0 \left(\frac{\rho_g}{\rho_p}\right)^{0.187} \left(\frac{u_o^2}{g \, d_{or}}\right)^{0.218}$$

Compute the two ratios:

$$\frac{\rho_g}{\rho_p} = \frac{1.0}{2500} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\frac{u_o^2}{g \, d_{or}} = \frac{(75)^2}{9.81 \times 0.030} = \frac{5625}{0.2943} = 19{,}116.5$$

Raise to fractional powers:

$$\left(4.0 \times 10^{-4}\right)^{0.187} = \exp\left[0.187 \ln(4.0 \times 10^{-4})\right] = \exp\left[0.187 \times (-7.824)\right] = \exp(-1.463) = 0.2315$$

$$\left(19{,}116.5\right)^{0.218} = \exp\left[0.218 \ln(19{,}116.5)\right] = \exp\left[0.218 \times 9.857\right] = \exp(2.149) = 8.577$$

Multiply:

$$\frac{L_j}{d_{or}} = 15.0 \times 0.2315 \times 8.577 = 15.0 \times 1.986 = 29.79$$

**Step 3 — Compute $L_j$ (1 mark):**

$$L_j = 29.79 \times 0.030 = 0.8937 \, \text{m} \approx 0.894 \, \text{m} \approx 894 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 4 — Minimum Bed Height (2 marks):**

To fully contain the jet and prevent gas bypassing directly to the freeboard, the **expanded bed height** must exceed the jet penetration depth:

$$H_{bed,min} \geq L_j$$

A safety factor of **1.2** is recommended by KTU design code:

$$H_{bed,min} = 1.2 \times L_j = 1.2 \times 0.894 = 1.073 \, \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{H_{bed,min} \approx 1.07 \, \text{m}}$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Correlations** — Examiners report ~30% of students apply Merry's formula when Behie is asked, or vice versa. **Always state which correlation you are using and for which Geldart class it is valid.**
> 2. **Unit Inconsistencies** — Mixing cm and m in $d_{or}$ when computing $L_j$. **Always convert to SI first.**
> 3. **Forgetting the Safety Factor** — Direct use of $L_j$ as bed height is penalized. **Add the 1.2–1.5 safety factor** for bed design.
> 4. **No Diagram in Theory Questions** — Even in numerical problems, a **sketch of the jet with labeled zones** fetches 1–2 extra marks.
> 5. **Skipping Assumptions in Derivations** — The Part (a) derivation question explicitly asks for assumptions; omitting them costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Incorrect $\rho_g/\rho_p$ exponent** — This ratio is **always** much less than 1 (since gas is 1000× less dense). Show the calculation explicitly to demonstrate numerical awareness.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **📋 Rapid-Revision Checklist (Gas Jets in Fluidized Beds)**

**Core Concepts (Memorize):**
- ☑️ Gas jet has **three zones**: Potential Core, Transition, Fully Developed Jet.
- ☑️ **Jet penetration depth** $L_j$ = axial distance from orifice to jet dissipation point.
- ☑️ **Jet cone half-angle** $\theta_j \approx 14°–20°$.
- ☑️ Penetration depth **decreases** with increasing bed (particle) density.
- ☑️ Penetration depth **increases** with increasing orifice velocity $u_o$ and orifice diameter $d_{or}$.

**Key Correlations (Remember formulas + valid Geldart class):**
- ☑️ **Behie (1975)** → Geldart A & B (fine powders)
- ☑️ **Merry (1971)** → Geldart B & D (coarse particles)
- ☑️ **Zenz (1968)** → Universal, conservative
- ☑️ **Kehoe-Davidson (1971)** → Coarse particles, Froude-number-based

**Critical Dimensionless Groups:**
- ☑️ Orifice Froude Number: $Fr_{or} = \dfrac{u_o}{\sqrt{g \, d_{or}}}$
- ☑️ Density Ratio: $\dfrac{\rho_g}{\rho_p} \ll 1$ (typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$)

**Engineering Design Rules of Thumb:**
- ☑️ Jet penetration is **inversely proportional to bed density**.
- ☑️ For multi-orifice distributors, spacing between orifices should be **2–4 × $d_{or}$**.
- ☑️ **Minimum bed height** = $1.2 \times L_j$ (KTU-recommended safety factor).
- ☑️ Excessive $u_o$ causes **particle attrition**; design $u_o < 100$ m/s for most catalyst systems.

**Common Mistake to Avoid:**
- ☑️ Never assume the jet travels in a straight line of constant diameter — it **expands conically** and **dissipates momentum** through particle drag.

**Exam Day Quick Tip:**
- ☑️ If asked to compare correlations, **always plot a table** with $L_j$ values, Geldart class, and a one-line comment on why they differ (fine vs. coarse particle bed dynamics).
- ☑️ In derivations, **begin with momentum flux at orifice** — that single step fetches 2 marks instantly.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Drop Requirements across Distributors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pressure Drop Requirements across Distributors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
A **distributor** (also called a **grid** or **plate**) in a fluidized bed is a perforated, porous, or specially engineered plate located at the bottom of the fluidization column whose primary function is to ensure **uniform distribution of the fluidizing medium** (gas or liquid) across the entire cross-section of the particle bed.

The **pressure drop requirement across a distributor** refers to the **minimum value of the pressure drop (ΔP_d)** that the distributor must offer to the flowing fluid in order to:
1. Overcome any inherent maldistribution tendencies (channeling) in the system.
2. Provide uniform fluid distribution across the bed cross-section.
3. Support the weight of the bed solids when the fluidizing gas is interrupted (relevant for non-mechanical distributors).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The distributor pressure drop must be sufficient to enforce uniform gas distribution. A widely accepted empirical design rule is the **Whitehead Criterion**, which states that the distributor pressure drop should be at least **10 % to 30 %** of the operating bed pressure drop.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **garden sprinkler system** fed by a single long hose with multiple outlets. As water travels along the hose, friction causes a pressure drop, so sprinklers near the source get more water than those at the far end. This is exactly the problem we face at the bottom of a fluidized bed: the fluidizing gas enters from one (or a few) inlet(s) and must reach **every particle** in the bed uniformly.

The **distributor acts like a pressure equalizer** — a controlled resistance — that ensures every "exit point" of the plate delivers the same flow rate. Without sufficient pressure drop across the distributor, the gas will preferentially flow through the path of least resistance (channeling), creating **dead zones and bubbles**, much like uneven water pressure along a long garden hose.

A simple everyday analogy is a **showerhead**: it has many tiny holes (high ΔP_d per hole) so that water exits with nearly uniform velocity, regardless of small variations in the supply line pressure. If you replace the showerhead with a single open pipe, the flow becomes a jet — non-uniform and uncontrollable.

### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics
- **Discharge coefficient (C_d)** for sharp-edged circular orifices ≈ **0.6**
- **Orifice velocity (U_or)** typical range = **10 – 30 m/s**
- **Fractional open area (α)** typical range = **0.005 – 0.05** (0.5 % – 5 %)
- **Standard design ratio (ΔP_d / ΔP_b)** = **0.1 – 0.3**
- **Acceleration due to gravity (g)** = **9.81 m/s²**

> [!NOTE]
> The discharge coefficient C_d depends on the Reynolds number through the orifice and the hole geometry. For sharp-edged circular orifices at moderate Re, C_d ≈ **0.6**. For nozzles, C_d ≈ 0.85–0.95.

### Key Terminology
- **Distributor / Grid / Plate**: Bottom plate containing orifices for fluid entry.
- **Fractional open area (α)**: Ratio of total orifice area to total distributor area.
- **Orifice velocity (U_or)**: Superficial fluid velocity through the orifices = U / α.
- **Bed pressure drop (ΔP_b)**: Pressure drop required to support the weight of solids per unit cross-sectional area.
- **Whitehead Criterion**: ΔP_d ≥ 0.1 × ΔP_b (empirical design rule for distributors).
- **Plenum Chamber**: The pressurized gas-filled region beneath the distributor that equalizes the inlet flow.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Functional Requirements of a Distributor
A well-designed distributor must satisfy the following core requirements:

1. **Uniform Distribution Requirement**: ΔP_d must be large enough relative to ΔP_b so that the influence of any non-uniform inlet or bed-resistance conditions is damped out. Without sufficient ΔP_d, even a well-designed distributor will exhibit channeling.

2. **Particle Retention Requirement (Static Load)**: When the fluidizing gas is shut off, the bed solids must either be supported mechanically by the distributor (e.g., bubble caps, tuyeres) **or** held by the trapped gas pressure beneath the plate:
   ΔP_d,min (static) ≥ H_bed (1 − ε) (ρ_s − ρ_g) g

3. **Operating Pressure Drop Requirement**: At the design operating velocity, ΔP_d should be a meaningful fraction (**10 %–30 %**) of the operating bed pressure drop to enforce uniformity.

### Why Pressure Drop Is Necessary — Physical Reasoning
When fluid passes through a perforated plate with multiple orifices:
- Each orifice behaves like a **hydraulic resistance** governed by the orifice equation.
- A **higher ΔP_d** shifts the flow regime toward a **"flow-controlled" zone**, where the flow distribution depends on the precisely engineered distributor geometry rather than on the random porosity variations of the bed.
- A **lower ΔP_d** means that small variations in local bed porosity (channeling) drastically alter the local flow, leading to non-uniform fluidization, dead zones, and large bubbles.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** A higher ΔP_d improves distribution quality but increases blower / compressor power consumption. The 10 %–30 % Whitehead rule balances these two competing demands in industrial practice.

### Design Criteria — Whitehead Criterion
**Whitehead and Dent (1967)** proposed the most widely used empirical criterion:

$$
\Delta P_d \geq 0.1 \cdot \Delta P_{b,\text{operating}}
$$

A more conservative design uses **k = 0.3**:

$$
\Delta P_d \geq 0.3 \cdot \Delta P_{b,\text{operating}}
$$

### Orifice Pressure Drop Equation
For a perforated plate distributor with sharp-edged circular orifices, the pressure drop is:

$$
\Delta P_d = \frac{\rho_g \cdot U_{or}^2}{2 \cdot C_d^2}
$$

Using continuity (U_or = U / α):

$$
\Delta P_d = \frac{\rho_g \cdot U^2}{2 \cdot C_d^2 \cdot \alpha^2}
$$

Solving for the required fractional open area α:

$$
\alpha = \frac{U}{C_d} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_g}{2 \cdot \Delta P_d}}
$$

### Bed Pressure Drop Equation
For a fluidized bed operating above minimum fluidization, the bed pressure drop equals the buoyant weight of the bed per unit cross-sectional area:

$$
\Delta P_b = H_{bed} \cdot (1 - \varepsilon) \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_g) \cdot g = \frac{W_{bed}}{A_{bed}}
$$

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $\Delta P_d = \dfrac{\rho_g \cdot U^2}{2 \cdot C_d^2 \cdot \alpha^2}$ | Orifice pressure drop across distributor | $\rho_g$ = gas density (kg/m³), $U$ = superficial velocity (m/s), $C_d \approx 0.6$, $\alpha$ = fractional open area (–) |
| 2 | $\alpha = \dfrac{U}{C_d}\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_g}{2\Delta P_d}}$ | Required open area for target $\Delta P_d$ | Same as above |
| 3 | $\Delta P_b = H_{bed}(1-\varepsilon)(\rho_s-\rho_g)g$ | Bed pressure drop (operating) | $H_{bed}$ in m, $\varepsilon$ = bed voidage (–), $\rho_s$ = solid density (kg/m³), $g = 9.81$ m/s² |
| 4 | $\Delta P_d \geq 0.1 \cdot \Delta P_b$ | Whitehead criterion (minimum, k = 0.1) | Dimensionless ratio |
| 5 | $\Delta P_d \geq 0.3 \cdot \Delta P_b$ | Whitehead criterion (conservative, k = 0.3) | Dimensionless ratio |
| 6 | $\Delta P_{d,\text{static}} \geq H_{bed}(1-\varepsilon)(\rho_s-\rho_g)g$ | Static support requirement | Equals full bed weight per unit area |
| 7 | $U_{or} = U/\alpha$ | Orifice velocity definition | m/s |
| 8 | $\Delta P_{\text{total}} = \Delta P_d + \Delta P_b$ | Total system pressure drop | Pa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the **Part B (14-mark) questions**, you must be able to **derive the orifice pressure drop equation** and **apply the Whitehead criterion in design problems**. Memorize formulas 1, 3, and 4 — they appear in nearly every numerical problem.

### Real-World Engineering Applications
- **FCC (Fluid Catalytic Cracking) units** in petroleum refineries: Distributors deliver regenerator air and feed vapor uniformly.
- **Coal-fired circulating fluidized bed combustors**: Perforated plate distributors with bubble caps are standard.
- **Pharmaceutical fluid bed dryers / granulators**: Porous plate distributors provide gentle, uniform gas distribution for heat-sensitive powders.
- **Wastewater fluidized bioreactors**: Distributors ensure uniform oxygen / air supply across the biological bed.
- **Polymerization reactors (gas-phase)**: Distributor design is critical to prevent hot spots and fouling.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1 — Orifice Pressure Drop Equation

**Starting Point — Mechanical Energy Balance Across a Single Orifice**

Consider one orifice in the distributor. Fluid approaches the orifice with superficial velocity U (in the plenum chamber below) and accelerates through the orifice to a jet velocity U_or.

Applying Bernoulli's equation (steady, incompressible, with head loss) between a point upstream (1) in the plenum and a point at the vena contracta (2):

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho_g} + \frac{U_1^2}{2} = \frac{P_2}{\rho_g} + \frac{U_{or}^2}{2} + h_f
$$

where h_f is the head loss across the orifice (the quantity we wish to determine).

**Step 1 — Identify the velocities at each point**

In the plenum chamber (point 1), the velocity U_1 is the **superficial velocity U**, because the gas enters through a much larger cross-section than the orifice. At the vena contracta (point 2), the velocity is the **orifice velocity U_or**.

Rearranging Bernoulli's equation to isolate the head loss:

$$
\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho_g} = \frac{U_{or}^2 - U^2}{2} + h_f
$$

For the typical distributor design, U ≪ U_or (because α ≪ 1), so we may neglect U²:

$$
\frac{\Delta P_d}{\rho_g} \approx \frac{U_{or}^2}{2} + h_f
$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the discharge coefficient**

The **discharge coefficient C_d** accounts for the vena-contracta effect (the jet contracts to a smaller area than the orifice) and viscous losses. By definition:

$$
C_d = \frac{U_{or,\text{actual}}}{U_{or,\text{ideal}}}
$$

For an ideal frictionless orifice (no contraction), the entire pressure drop would convert to kinetic energy:

$$
\Delta P_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho_g U_{or}^2
$$

The **actual** pressure drop is larger than the ideal pressure drop, because part of the energy is dissipated as turbulence and viscous loss:

$$
\Delta P_d = \frac{\Delta P_{\text{ideal}}}{C_d^2} = \frac{\rho_g \cdot U_{or}^2}{2 \cdot C_d^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply continuity to relate U_or to superficial velocity U**

By conservation of mass (volumetric flow continuity), the volumetric flow rate per unit total plate area (the superficial velocity U) must equal the volumetric flow rate per unit orifice area (the orifice velocity U_or) multiplied by the fractional open area α:

$$
U = \alpha \cdot U_{or} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad U_{or} = \frac{U}{\alpha}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the orifice equation**

$$
\Delta P_d = \frac{\rho_g}{2 C_d^2} \cdot \left(\frac{U}{\alpha}\right)^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta P_d = \frac{\rho_g \cdot U^2}{2 \cdot C_d^2 \cdot \alpha^2}}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:**
- For a fixed superficial velocity U, **increasing α** (more open area) **decreases ΔP_d** — the distributor offers less resistance.
- For a fixed α, **increasing U** increases ΔP_d **quadratically**.

---

### Derivation 2 — Required Fractional Open Area from the Whitehead Criterion

**Step 1 — Set the design criterion**

Per Whitehead's criterion, the required distributor pressure drop equals a chosen fraction k of the operating bed pressure drop:

$$
\Delta P_d = k \cdot \Delta P_b, \quad k \in [0.1, 0.3]
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the bed pressure drop expression**

$$
\Delta P_d = k \cdot H_{bed} \cdot (1-\varepsilon) \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_g) \cdot g
$$

**Step 3 — Equate with the orifice pressure drop expression**

$$
\frac{\rho_g \cdot U^2}{2 \cdot C_d^2 \cdot \alpha^2} = k \cdot H_{bed} \cdot (1-\varepsilon) \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_g) \cdot g
$$

**Step 4 — Solve algebraically for α²**

$$
\alpha^2 = \frac{\rho_g \cdot U^2}{2 \cdot C_d^2 \cdot k \cdot H_{bed} \cdot (1-\varepsilon) \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_g) \cdot g}
$$

**Step 5 — Take the square root**

$$
\boxed{\alpha = \frac{U}{C_d} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_g}{2 \cdot k \cdot H_{bed} \cdot (1-\varepsilon) \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_g) \cdot g}}}
$$

**Step 6 — Validate the result**

α must lie in the practical range **0.005 to 0.05**. If the calculated α falls outside this range, the chosen operating velocity U or design ratio k must be revised.

---

### Worked Example — Distributor Design Calculation

**Problem Statement**

A fluidized bed operates with the following parameters:
- Bed height: H_bed = 1.0 m
- Particle density: ρ_s = 2500 kg/m³
- Gas (air) density: ρ_g = 1.2 kg/m³
- Operating superficial velocity: U = 0.5 m/s
- Bed voidage: ε = 0.5
- Discharge coefficient: C_d = 0.6
- Design ratio (Whitehead): k = 0.1

**Find:**
(a) The operating bed pressure drop.
(b) The required distributor pressure drop.
(c) The required fractional open area α.
(d) The orifice velocity U_or.
(e) Comment on whether the static pressure drop requirement is automatically satisfied.

---

**Solution:**

**(a) Operating Bed Pressure Drop**

$$
\Delta P_b = H_{bed} \cdot (1-\varepsilon) \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_g) \cdot g
$$

Substitute values:

$$
\Delta P_b = 1.0 \times (1 - 0.5) \times (2500 - 1.2) \times 9.81
$$

$$
\Delta P_b = 1.0 \times 0.5 \times 2498.8 \times 9.81
$$

$$
\Delta P_b = 12256 \text{ Pa} \approx 12.26 \text{ kPa}
$$

**(b) Required Distributor Pressure Drop**

Apply Whitehead criterion (k = 0.1):

$$
\Delta P_d = 0.1 \times \Delta P_b = 0.1 \times 12256 = 1226 \text{ Pa} \approx 1.23 \text{ kPa}
$$

**(c) Required Fractional Open Area α**

Rearrange the orifice equation to solve for α:

$$
\alpha = \frac{U}{C_d} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_g}{2 \cdot \Delta P_d}}
$$

Substitute numerical values:

$$
\alpha = \frac{0.5}{0.6} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1.2}{2 \times 1226}}
$$

$$
\alpha = 0.8333 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1.2}{2452}}
$$

$$
\alpha = 0.8333 \cdot \sqrt{4.894 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\alpha = 0.8333 \times 0.02212
$$

$$
\boxed{\alpha \approx 0.01843 \approx 1.84 \,\%}
$$

This is **within** the practical range (0.5 % – 5 %) — design is feasible.

**(d) Orifice Velocity**

$$
U_{or} = \frac{U}{\alpha} = \frac{0.5}{0.01843} \approx 27.1 \text{ m/s}
$$

This is **within** the typical range (10–30 m/s) — acceptable.

**(e) Static Pressure Drop Check**

For static support of the bed (when gas flow is off), the distributor would need:

$$
\Delta P_{d,\text{static}} \geq H_{bed}(1-\varepsilon)(\rho_s-\rho_g)g = \Delta P_b = 12256 \text{ Pa}
$$

But the actual ΔP_d at operating conditions is only 1226 Pa (just 10 % of the bed weight). Therefore:

- The distributor **cannot** statically support the bed on its own.
- A perforated plate with this design would require either:
  - a mechanical support structure (e.g., a screen or bubble caps), OR
  - a higher design ratio (e.g., k = 1.0), which is impractical due to excessive blower power.

---

### Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Design Approaches

| Criterion | Mathematical Expression | When Applied | Pros | Cons |
|-----------|-------------------------|--------------|------|------|
| Whitehead (k = 0.1) | $\Delta P_d \geq 0.1 \cdot \Delta P_b$ | Standard industrial design | Low blower cost; satisfies distribution | Cannot support bed when gas is off |
| Whitehead (k = 0.3) | $\Delta P_d \geq 0.3 \cdot \Delta P_b$ | Conservative design | Better distribution uniformity | Higher operating power cost |
| Static Support | $\Delta P_d \geq \Delta P_b$ | Self-supporting distributor | Holds bed during shutdown | Very high $\Delta P_d$; excessive blower cost |
| Orifice Velocity Limit | $U_{or} \leq 30$ m/s | To avoid erosion / noise | Practical limit on hole size | Not strictly a pressure criterion |
| Mechanical Support (Bubble Caps) | $\Delta P_d$ arbitrary | When bed support is mandatory | Self-supporting; flexible operation | Higher capital cost |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: State and explain the Whitehead Criterion for distributor design in a fluidized bed. Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer:**

The Whitehead Criterion, proposed by Whitehead and Dent (1967), is an empirical design rule for fluidized bed distributors. It states that the pressure drop across the distributor (ΔP_d) must be at least a specified fraction of the operating bed pressure drop (ΔP_b):

$$
\Delta P_d \geq 0.1 \cdot \Delta P_{b,\text{operating}}
$$

In more conservative designs, the factor **k = 0.3** is used instead of 0.1.

**Necessity:** Without sufficient ΔP_d, the fluidizing gas preferentially follows paths of low resistance through the bed, causing channeling, dead zones, and poor fluidization quality. The distributor acts as a "flow-resistance element" that equalizes flow across the cross-section. A higher ΔP_d enforces uniformity by making the flow distribution depend on the **precisely engineered plate geometry** rather than on **random bed porosity variations**.

**(3 marks: 1 mark for criterion statement, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for physical necessity)**

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define fractional open area (α) of a distributor. What is its typical range for perforated plate distributors?**

**Model Answer:**

The **fractional open area (α)** of a distributor is defined as the ratio of the total cross-sectional area of all orifices (holes) to the total cross-sectional area of the distributor plate.

$$
\alpha = \frac{A_{\text{orifices,total}}}{A_{\text{distributor,total}}}
$$

For industrial perforated plate distributors, the typical range is **0.005 to 0.05** (i.e., 0.5 % to 5 % of the plate area). Below 0.5 %, the orifice velocity becomes excessively high (causing erosion, noise, and pressure surges); above 5 %, the distributor offers insufficient pressure drop and the gas distribution becomes non-uniform.

**(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for typical range with reason)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a perforated plate distributor in a fluidized bed. List its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Air at 30 °C (ρ_g = 1.165 kg/m³) is used to fluidize a bed of sand particles (ρ_s = 2650 kg/m³, ε = 0.55) in a column of 0.6 m diameter. The settled bed height is 0.8 m. The operating superficial velocity is 0.6 m/s. Using the Whitehead criterion with k = 0.1 and a discharge coefficient C_d = 0.6, calculate:
   (i) The operating bed pressure drop.
   (ii) The required distributor pressure drop.
   (iii) The required fractional open area.
   (iv) The orifice velocity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Perforated Plate Distributor (7 marks)**

**Construction:**
A perforated plate distributor is a flat metallic plate (typically **6–25 mm thick**) with uniformly arranged circular holes (orifices). The orifices are typically **1–6 mm in diameter**, drilled on a triangular pitch to maximize uniformity. The plate is supported on a structural ring inside the fluidization column. Below the plate is the **plenum chamber**, which receives gas from the blower and acts as an equalization zone.

**Working:**
Pressurized gas enters the plenum chamber, where its velocity is reduced and pressure equalized. The gas then passes upward through the orifices. Because the orifice area is much smaller than the plate area, the gas accelerates to a high velocity (**10–30 m/s**) through the holes, creating a pressure drop ΔP_d. Above the plate, the gas decelerates and enters the bed uniformly.

**Advantages:**
1. Simple construction and low cost.
2. Easy to manufacture, drill, and replace.
3. Well-defined pressure drop characteristics (via the orifice equation).
4. Suitable for high-temperature applications (metallic construction).
5. Easy to clean and inspect.

**Limitations:**
1. Cannot support the bed weight when gas flow is interrupted (no mechanical support).
2. Susceptible to solids backflow through the orifices during shutdown.
3. Erosion of orifice edges over time in abrasive service.
4. Limited turn-down ratio compared to bubble caps.

**(7 marks: 2 marks for construction, 2 marks for working, 2 marks for advantages, 1 mark for limitations)**

---

**(b) Numerical Problem Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- ρ_g = 1.165 kg/m³
- ρ_s = 2650 kg/m³
- ε = 0.55
- H_bed = 0.8 m
- U = 0.6 m/s
- k = 0.1, C_d = 0.6
- g = 9.81 m/s²

**(i) Operating Bed Pressure Drop**

$$
\Delta P_b = H_{bed} \cdot (1-\varepsilon) \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_g) \cdot g
$$

$$
\Delta P_b = 0.8 \times (1 - 0.55) \times (2650 - 1.165) \times 9.81
$$

$$
\Delta P_b = 0.8 \times 0.45 \times 2648.835 \times 9.81
$$

$$
\Delta P_b = 9354.6 \text{ Pa} \approx 9.35 \text{ kPa}
$$

**(ii) Required Distributor Pressure Drop**

$$
\Delta P_d = 0.1 \times \Delta P_b = 0.1 \times 9354.6 = 935.5 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.94 \text{ kPa}
$$

**(iii) Required Fractional Open Area α**

$$
\alpha = \frac{U}{C_d} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_g}{2 \cdot \Delta P_d}}
$$

$$
\alpha = \frac{0.6}{0.6} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1.165}{2 \times 935.46}}
$$

$$
\alpha = 1.0 \times \sqrt{\frac{1.165}{1870.92}} = \sqrt{6.227 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\alpha = 0.02495 \approx 0.025 \text{ (i.e., 2.5 % open area)}
$$

This lies within the practical range (0.5 % – 5 %) — **design is feasible**.

**(iv) Orifice Velocity**

$$
U_{or} = \frac{U}{\alpha} = \frac{0.6}{0.025} = 24 \text{ m/s}
$$

This is within the typical range (10–30 m/s) — **acceptable**.

**(7 marks: 2 marks for (i), 1 mark for (ii), 2 marks for (iii), 1 mark for (iv), 1 mark for feasibility comment)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the functional requirements of a distributor in a fluidized bed. Discuss why a minimum pressure drop is essential for proper gas distribution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fluidized bed combustor uses a distributor with **250 circular orifices**, each of **4 mm diameter**. The bed diameter is **1.0 m**. The gas density is **1.0 kg/m³** and the discharge coefficient is **0.62**. The bed pressure drop at operating conditions is **8.0 kPa**.
   (i) Calculate the fractional open area of the distributor.
   (ii) If the operating superficial velocity is **0.4 m/s**, calculate the actual pressure drop across the distributor.
   (iii) Check whether the Whitehead criterion (k = 0.1) is satisfied.
   (iv) If the criterion is overly satisfied, identify the practical issue and suggest a design modification. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Functional Requirements of a Distributor (7 marks)**

A distributor in a fluidized bed must satisfy the following functional requirements:

1. **Uniform Gas Distribution:** The primary requirement. The distributor must deliver the fluidizing gas uniformly across the entire bed cross-section, ensuring consistent fluidization quality throughout the bed.

2. **Support of Bed Solids:** When the gas flow is interrupted (e.g., emergency shutdown), the distributor must either:
   - **Mechanically** support the bed (e.g., bubble caps, tuyeres), **OR**
   - **Trap sufficient gas pressure** beneath the plate to hold the bed weight via the static pressure drop requirement:
     $$
     \Delta P_{d,\text{static}} \geq H_{bed}(1-\varepsilon)(\rho_s-\rho_g)g
     $$

3. **Prevention of Solids Backflow:** The distributor must prevent fine particles from falling or being sucked into the plenum chamber, especially during shutdown or pulsation.

4. **Erosion and Heat Resistance:** For high-temperature applications (e.g., combustion), the distributor must be constructed of materials that withstand thermal cycling and particle impact.

5. **Adequate Turn-Down Ratio:** The distributor should provide acceptable fluidization quality over a range of operating velocities (typically 3 : 1 to 5 : 1).

**Why Minimum Pressure Drop Is Essential:**

If ΔP_d is too low relative to ΔP_b, any local variation in bed porosity (e.g., a slightly denser region) creates a local low-resistance path. Gas preferentially flows through this path, **bypassing other regions**. This causes:
- Channeling of gas
- Dead zones (non-fluidized regions)
- Excessive bubble growth
- Poor gas-solid contact and reduced reaction / heat-transfer efficiency

A minimum ΔP_d ensures that the **"controlling resistance"** in the system is the distributor (not the bed), so that flow distribution is dictated by the **precisely engineered plate geometry** rather than by random bed variations.

**(7 marks: 3 marks for functional requirements list, 2 marks for support conditions, 2 marks for necessity of ΔP_d with consequences)**

---

**(b) Numerical Problem Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Number of orifices: N = 250
- Orifice diameter: d_or = 4 mm = 0.004 m
- Bed diameter: D = 1.0 m
- Bed area: A_bed = (π/4) × D² = (π/4) × 1.0² = 0.7854 m²
- ρ_g = 1.0 kg/m³
- C_d = 0.62
- ΔP_b = 8.0 kPa = 8000 Pa
- U = 0.4 m/s

**(i) Fractional Open Area α**

Area of one orifice:

$$
A_{or} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d_{or}^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.004)^2 = 1.2566 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2
$$

Total orifice area:

$$
A_{or,\text{total}} = N \times A_{or} = 250 \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-5} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

Fractional open area:

$$
\alpha = \frac{A_{or,\text{total}}}{A_{bed}} = \frac{3.1416 \times 10^{-3}}{0.7854}
$$

$$
\boxed{\alpha = 4.0 \times 10^{-3} = 0.004 \text{ (i.e., 0.4 %)}}
$$

**(ii) Actual Pressure Drop Across the Distributor**

$$
\Delta P_d = \frac{\rho_g \cdot U^2}{2 \cdot C_d^2 \cdot \alpha^2}
$$

$$
\Delta P_d = \frac{1.0 \times (0.4)^2}{2 \times (0.62)^2 \times (0.004)^2}
$$

$$
\Delta P_d = \frac{0.16}{2 \times 0.3844 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
\Delta P_d = \frac{0.16}{1.2301 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta P_d = 13007 \text{ Pa} \approx 13.0 \text{ kPa}}
$$

**(iii) Whitehead Criterion Check (k = 0.1)**

Required ΔP_d (minimum per Whitehead):

$$
\Delta P_{d,\text{required}} = 0.1 \times \Delta P_b = 0.1 \times 8000 = 800 \text{ Pa}
$$

Compare with actual ΔP_d:

$$
\frac{\Delta P_d}{\Delta P_b} = \frac{13007}{8000} \approx 1.626
$$

Since 1.626 ≫ 0.1, **the Whitehead criterion is comfortably satisfied**. In fact, the design is **excessively over-conservative**.

**(iv) Practical Issue and Design Modification**

Calculate the orifice velocity:

$$
U_{or} = \frac{U}{\alpha} = \frac{0.4}{0.004} = 100 \text{ m/s}
$$

This is **far above** the recommended maximum of 30 m/s — significant erosion of orifice edges, excessive noise, and pressure pulsations will occur.

**Suggested Modification:** Use **larger orifices** (e.g., d_or = 8 mm) or **more orifices** to bring α into the range 0.01–0.02. This will reduce U_or to 20–40 m/s while still maintaining a ΔP_d well above the Whitehead minimum.

**(7 marks: 2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 2 marks for (iii), 1 mark for (iv))**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing operating vs. minimum-fluidization pressure drop:** The Whitehead criterion uses the **operating** bed ΔP (ΔP at the chosen operating velocity), NOT the ΔP at U_mf. Mixing these up is a common error worth losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Using ρ_s instead of (ρ_s − ρ_g):** The buoyant force per unit volume uses **(ρ_s − ρ_g)**, NOT ρ_s alone. Always account for buoyancy in fluidized bed calculations.
> 3. **Incorrect discharge coefficient:** C_d ≈ 0.6 for **sharp-edged** orifices. If a problem specifies a different value (e.g., for nozzles or bubble caps), use the **given value**. Do not assume 0.6 universally.
> 4. **Static-support confusion:** The static support requirement (ΔP_d ≥ W_bed / A) is needed **only if the distributor must hold the bed when gas is off**. For perforated plates, this typically requires k = 1.0, which is impractical — so mechanical supports (bubble caps) are used instead.
> 5. **Units consistency:** Pressure drops must be in **Pa (SI)**, velocities in **m/s**, areas in **m²**. Always convert kPa → Pa (multiply by 1000) before substituting into equations.
> 6. **Skipping feasibility comments:** Always state whether the calculated α lies in the practical range (0.005–0.05) and whether U_or is in the acceptable range (10–30 m/s). Examiners award marks for these design-validation steps.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **distributor** (grid / plate) at the bottom of a fluidized bed ensures **uniform gas distribution** and may also support the bed solids.
- The **pressure drop across the distributor (ΔP_d)** is governed by the **orifice equation**:

$$
\Delta P_d = \frac{\rho_g \cdot U^2}{2 \cdot C_d^2 \cdot \alpha^2}
$$

- The **Whitehead Criterion** sets the minimum design ratio:

$$
\Delta P_d \geq (0.1 \text{ to } 0.3) \cdot \Delta P_{b,\text{operating}}
$$

- The **bed pressure drop** equals the **buoyant weight per unit area**:

$$
\Delta P_b = H_{bed} \cdot (1-\varepsilon) \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_g) \cdot g
$$

- **Fractional open area α** typically lies between **0.005 and 0.05** (0.5 % – 5 %) for industrial perforated plates.
- **Orifice velocity** should be in the range **10 – 30 m/s** for practical operation (avoid erosion / noise).
- **Discharge coefficient C_d ≈ 0.6** for sharp-edged circular orifices.
- **Static support** requires ΔP_d ≥ ΔP_b (i.e., k = 1.0); otherwise, **mechanical supports** (bubble caps, tuyeres) are needed.
- Higher ΔP_d = better distribution quality but higher blower power cost — an important **engineering trade-off**.
- Types of distributors: **perforated plate, porous plate, bubble cap, tuyere, valve grid**.
- **Critical Pitfall #1:** Always distinguish **operating ΔP_b** from **ΔP at minimum fluidization U_mf**.
- **Critical Pitfall #2:** Use **(ρ_s − ρ_g)** in the bed ΔP equation, NOT ρ_s alone.
- **Critical Pitfall #3:** Convert all units to **SI (Pa, m, kg, s)** before calculation.
- **Critical Pitfall #4:** Always include a **feasibility comment** (check α and U_or ranges) to earn full marks.
- **Critical Pitfall #5:** Total system ΔP = **ΔP_d + ΔP_b**, NOT ΔP_d alone — blower sizing requires the sum.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Consumption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Power Consumption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Consumption in Fluidization Engineering** refers to the total rate of mechanical energy (measured in **Watts**, **J/s**, or **kW**) that must be supplied to a fluidization system in order to (a) generate the pressure drop required to suspend and circulate the fluidizing medium through the particulate bed, and (b) drive any auxiliary equipment such as compressors, blowers, pumps, agitators, or distributors against the prevailing system resistance.

Mathematically, the instantaneous fluidization power is the product of the **pressure drop across the bed ($\Delta P_{bed}$)** and the **volumetric flow rate of the fluidizing medium ($\dot{V}$)**:

$$P_{fluidization} = \Delta P_{bed} \times \dot{V} = \Delta P_{bed} \times A_{bed} \times U_{0}$$

where $A_{bed}$ is the cross-sectional area of the bed and $U_{0}$ is the superficial fluid velocity.

When the fluidizing gas must be raised from atmospheric pressure $P_{1}$ to a higher discharge pressure $P_{2}$, the **compressor power** must be added to the fluidization power, giving the **total power consumption** of the system.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Blowing Through a Straw in a Glass of Sand:**
> Imagine you have a glass of fine sand and you blow air through a straw placed at the bottom. To get the sand grains to "boil" and float, you must blow hard enough to overcome the weight of every grain pressing down. The harder you blow (higher $\Delta P$), and the more air you push per second (higher $\dot{V}$), the more energy your lungs must spend. That is exactly what **power consumption** quantifies — the rate at which the blower, compressor, or pump must deliver energy to keep the bed suspended.

**Key physical constants used throughout this module:**

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Universal gas constant | $R$ | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) |
| Ratio of specific heats for air | $\gamma$ | **1.40** | dimensionless |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101,325** | Pa |

## 1.3 Classification of Power Consumption in Fluidized Systems

A complete industrial fluidization unit consumes power in the following distinct categories:

1. **Compressor / Blower Power** — work done to raise the fluidizing gas from suction to delivery pressure.
2. **Pump Power** — for liquid-fluidized systems or for the cooling water / slurry circuits.
3. **Bed Suspension Power** — the actual energy transferred to the bed ($P = \Delta P \times \dot{V}$).
4. **Distributor / Grid Pressure Drop Power** — energy lost in pushing fluid through orifices.
5. **Mechanical Agitation / Internals Power** — power input from stirrers, heat-exchanger tubes, or vortex finders.
6. **Auxiliary Power** — for solids feeders, cyclones, bag filters, instrumentation.

> [!TIP]
> For the KTU 2024 ESE, students are expected to be able to **derive and apply** the compressor-work equations and the bed-power equation. Memorising the boundary conditions (isentropic vs isothermal vs polytropic) is the single highest-scoring differentiator.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component-Wise Breakdown of System Power

### 2.1.1 Power for Suspending the Bed (Fluidization Power)

The minimum theoretical power required to keep the bed in a fluidized state is:

$$P_{bed} = \Delta P_{bed} \times \dot{V}$$

At incipient fluidization this becomes:

$$P_{bed,\,mf} = \left( 1 - \varepsilon_{mf} \right) \left( \rho_{p} - \rho_{f} \right) g L_{mf} A_{bed} U_{mf}$$

For operating velocities above $U_{mf}$, the pressure drop remains essentially constant (≈ bed weight per unit area), but the volumetric flow increases, so:

$$P_{bed,\,op} = \left( 1 - \varepsilon_{mf} \right) \left( \rho_{p} - \rho_{f} \right) g L_{mf} A_{bed} U_{0}$$

**Why?** Because once the bed is fluidized, the pressure drop equals the buoyant weight of the particles per unit area — this is the **Ergun / K-L paradox** of constant $\Delta P$ above $U_{mf}$.

### 2.1.2 Distributor / Grid Pressure Drop

The grid is a controlled resistance placed beneath the bed to ensure uniform fluid distribution. Typical design practice is:

$$\Delta P_{grid} = 0.1 \, \Delta P_{bed} \quad \text{to} \quad 0.3 \, \Delta P_{bed}$$

The associated power loss is:

$$P_{grid} = \Delta P_{grid} \times \dot{V}$$

### 2.1.3 Compressor / Blower Power for the Fluidizing Gas

The gas must be raised from $P_{1}$ (typically atmospheric) to $P_{2}$ (= $P_{1} + \Delta P_{bed} + \Delta P_{grid} + \Delta P_{cyclone} + \Delta P_{piping}$). Three thermodynamic cases exist:

**Case A — Isothermal Compression** (slow, well-cooled):

$$W_{iso} = P_{1} \dot{V}_{1} \ln \left( \frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} \right)$$

**Case B — Adiabatic / Isentropic Compression** (ideal, no heat exchange):
