$$W_{ad} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \, P_{1} \dot{V}_{1} \left[ \left( \frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} \right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]$$

**Case C — Polytropic Compression** (real machines):

$$W_{poly} = \frac{n}{n - 1} \, P_{1} \dot{V}_{1} \left[ \left( \frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} \right)^{\frac{n - 1}{n}} - 1 \right]$$

where $n$ is the polytropic index ($1 < n < \gamma$).

**Including mechanical efficiency $\eta_{m}$ of the driver:**

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{W}{\eta_{m}}$$

### 2.1.4 Pump Power for Liquid Fluidization

For an incompressible liquid:

$$P_{pump} = \frac{\dot{V} \cdot \Delta P_{total}}{\eta_{pump}}$$

where $\Delta P_{total}$ includes bed, grid, piping, fittings, and static head.

### 2.1.5 Mechanical Agitator Power (for stirred / internal-tube fluidized beds)

In the **fully turbulent (Newtonian) regime** the power is independent of Reynolds number:

$$P = N_{P} \, \rho \, N^{3} \, D^{5}$$

where $N_{P}$ is the dimensionless Power number (≈ 6 for a Rushton turbine in fully baffled turbulent flow).

For laminar flow the relation reduces to $N_{P} \propto 1/Re$, and the working formula is:

$$P = K_{L} \, \mu \, N^{2} \, D^{3}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **examination-grade reference material** for ESE 2024. Master every entry.

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Variable Key | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bed fluidization power | $P_{bed} = \Delta P_{bed} \cdot \dot{V}$ | $\Delta P_{bed}$ in Pa, $\dot{V}$ in m³/s | W |
| 2 | Bed $\Delta P$ (constant above $U_{mf}$) | $\Delta P_{bed} = (1-\varepsilon)(\rho_{p} - \rho_{f}) g L$ | $L$ = expanded height | Pa |
| 3 | Isothermal gas compression | $W_{iso} = P_{1} \dot{V}_{1} \ln(P_{2}/P_{1})$ | $P_{1}, P_{2}$ absolute | W |
| 4 | Isentropic gas compression | $W_{ad} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} P_{1} \dot{V}_{1} \left[ (P_{2}/P_{1})^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$ | $\gamma = c_{p}/c_{v}$ | W |
| 5 | Polytropic gas compression | $W_{poly} = \frac{n}{n-1} P_{1} \dot{V}_{1} \left[ (P_{2}/P_{1})^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$ | $1 < n < \gamma$ | W |
| 6 | Mechanical efficiency correction | $P_{shaft} = W / \eta_{m}$ | $0 < \eta_{m} \leq 1$ | W |
| 7 | Pump power (incompressible) | $P_{pump} = \dot{V} \Delta P_{total} / \eta_{pump}$ | $\eta_{pump}$ dimensionless | W |
| 8 | Agitator power (turbulent) | $P = N_{P} \rho N^{3} D^{5}$ | $N$ rps, $D$ impeller diameter | W |
| 9 | Impeller Reynolds number | $Re = \rho N D^{2} / \mu$ | — | dimensionless |
| 10 | Compressor discharge temp (adiabatic) | $T_{2} = T_{1} (P_{2}/P_{1})^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | $T$ in K | K |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Significance:**
> - In a **catalytic cracking (FCC) regenerator** operating at $P_{2}/P_{1} \approx 3$, the **isentropic work exceeds the isothermal work by ~15 %** — selecting the wrong thermodynamic model can mis-size the blower by tens of kilowatts.
> - The **bed-pressure-drop power** is the **floor** of any fluidized-bed energy balance; the compressor power is the **ceiling**.
> - Grid-pressure-drop design directly trades off **distribution uniformity** (favours high $\Delta P_{grid}$) against **blower power cost** (favours low $\Delta P_{grid}$). The 10–30 % rule is the industry compromise.
> - Mechanical-agitator power correlations (Rushton, 1950) are the backbone of **internally-heated fluidized bed** design for steam reforming and dryers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Power Transferred to a Fluidized Bed

**Step 1 — Start from the definition of mechanical power.**
Power is the rate of doing work. For a fluid element pushed through a pressure drop:

$$P = \frac{dW}{dt} = F \cdot v = (P \cdot A) \cdot (Q / A) = P \cdot Q$$

In the context of the bed, $P$ is replaced by the bed pressure drop $\Delta P_{bed}$ and $Q$ by $\dot{V}$:

$$P_{bed} = \Delta P_{bed} \cdot \dot{V}$$

**Step 2 — Express $\dot{V}$ in terms of superficial velocity.**

$$\dot{V} = U_{0} \cdot A_{bed}$$

Substituting:

$$P_{bed} = \Delta P_{bed} \cdot U_{0} \cdot A_{bed}$$

**Step 3 — Replace $\Delta P_{bed}$ with the bed-weight expression.**

The buoyant weight of the particles per unit cross-section is:

$$\Delta P_{bed} = (1 - \varepsilon) (\rho_{p} - \rho_{f}) g H$$

where $H$ is the expanded (operating) bed height and $\varepsilon$ the bed porosity at the operating condition.

**Step 4 — Combine.**

$$P_{bed} = (1 - \varepsilon) (\rho_{p} - \rho_{f}) g H \cdot A_{bed} \cdot U_{0}$$

Multiplying $A_{bed} \cdot H$ gives the bed volume $V_{bed}$:

$$P_{bed} = (1 - \varepsilon) (\rho_{p} - \rho_{f}) g \cdot V_{bed} \cdot U_{0}$$

**Interpretation:** Power scales linearly with the **mass of solids held in the bed** and with the **operating superficial velocity**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Isothermal Compression Work

**Step 1 — Differential work for an infinitesimal pressure rise.**

For a quasi-static (reversible) process pushing a volume $V$ of gas from $P$ to $P + dP$:

$$dW = -P \, dV$$

**Step 2 — Use the isothermal ideal-gas law** $PV = \text{constant} = P_{1} V_{1}$.

Differentiating:

$$P \, dV + V \, dP = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P \, dV = -V \, dP$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and integrate from $P_{1}$ to $P_{2}$.**

$$W_{iso} = -\int_{V_{1}}^{V_{2}} P \, dV = \int_{P_{1}}^{P_{2}} V \, dP = \int_{P_{1}}^{P_{2}} \frac{P_{1} V_{1}}{P} \, dP$$

$$W_{iso} = P_{1} V_{1} \ln \left( \frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Convert to rate form** by dividing by the time over which one volume $V_{1}$ is processed, giving $\dot{V}_{1}$:

$$\dot{W}_{iso} = P_{1} \dot{V}_{1} \ln \left( \frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} \right)$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Isentropic (Adiabatic–Reversible) Compression Work

**Step 1 — Use the isentropic relation** for an ideal gas:

$$P V^{\gamma} = \text{constant} = P_{1} V_{1}^{\gamma}$$

**Step 2 — Express volume as a function of pressure.**

$$V = V_{1} \left( \frac{P_{1}}{P} \right)^{1/\gamma}$$

**Step 3 — Differential work** $dW = -V \, dP$ (work done ON the gas is positive, so we will sign-flip below):

$$W_{ad} = -\int_{V_{1}}^{V_{2}} P \, dV = \int_{P_{1}}^{P_{2}} V \, dP$$

Substituting $V(P)$:

$$W_{ad} = \int_{P_{1}}^{P_{2}} V_{1} \left( \frac{P_{1}}{P} \right)^{1/\gamma} dP = V_{1} P_{1}^{1/\gamma} \int_{P_{1}}^{P_{2}} P^{-1/\gamma} \, dP$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$\int_{P_{1}}^{P_{2}} P^{-1/\gamma} \, dP = \left[ \frac{P^{1 - 1/\gamma}}{1 - 1/\gamma} \right]_{P_{1}}^{P_{2}} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \left( P_{2}^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} - P_{1}^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Factor out $P_{1}^{1/\gamma} V_{1}$.**

$$W_{ad} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \, P_{1} V_{1} \left[ \left( \frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} \right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$$

**Step 6 — Rate form:**

$$\dot{W}_{ad} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \, P_{1} \dot{V}_{1} \left[ \left( \frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} \right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Compressor Discharge Temperature

Starting from the isentropic relation between temperature and pressure:

$$\frac{T_{2}}{T_{1}} = \left( \frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} \right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$$

Solving for $T_{2}$:

$$T_{2} = T_{1} \left( \frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} \right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$$

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (12-mark template):** Air at 300 K, 101.3 kPa is compressed to 405.2 kPa at a rate of 0.05 m³/s (at inlet conditions) and fed to a fluidized bed. The grid pressure drop is 15 % of the bed pressure drop, and the bed pressure drop is 18 kPa. Mechanical efficiency of the blower is 75 %. Compute (a) the isentropic compression power, (b) the isothermal compression power, (c) the bed-fluidization power, (d) the total shaft power.

**Given:**
$P_{1} = 101.3 \text{ kPa}$, $P_{2} = 405.2 \text{ kPa}$, $\dot{V}_{1} = 0.05 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s}$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $\Delta P_{bed} = 18 \text{ kPa}$, $\eta_{m} = 0.75$.

**Part (a) — Isentropic power:**

$$\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} = \frac{405.2}{101.3} = 4.0$$

$$\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857$$

$$4.0^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \times \ln 4} = e^{0.2857 \times 1.3863} = e^{0.3961} = 1.486$$

$$\dot{W}_{ad} = \frac{1.4}{0.4} \times 101300 \times 0.05 \times (1.486 - 1)$$

$$\dot{W}_{ad} = 3.5 \times 101300 \times 0.05 \times 0.486$$

$$\dot{W}_{ad} = 3.5 \times 5065 \times 0.486 = 8615 \text{ W} \approx 8.62 \text{ kW}$$

**Part (b) — Isothermal power:**

$$\dot{W}_{iso} = 101300 \times 0.05 \times \ln 4 = 5065 \times 1.3863 = 7021 \text{ W} \approx 7.02 \text{ kW}$$

**Part (c) — Bed fluidization power:**

$$P_{bed} = \Delta P_{bed} \times \dot{V} = 18000 \times 0.05 = 900 \text{ W} = 0.90 \text{ kW}$$

**Part (d) — Total shaft power:**

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{\dot{W}_{ad} + P_{bed} + P_{grid}}{\eta_{m}}$$

With $P_{grid} = 0.15 \times 18000 \times 0.05 = 135 \text{ W}$:

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{8615 + 900 + 135}{0.75} = \frac{9650}{0.75} = 12867 \text{ W} \approx 12.87 \text{ kW}$$

**Comment:** Notice that the **isothermal power is ~18 % lower** than the isentropic power. The bed fluidization power (~0.9 kW) is roughly an order of magnitude smaller than the compression work (~8.6 kW), confirming that for gas-fluidized systems the **blower dominates** the energy budget.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Energy Flow in a Gas-Fluidized System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electrical Power Input kW] --> B[Electric Motor]
    B --> C[Mechanical Coupling]
    C --> D[Compressor Blower]
    D --> E[Isentropic Compression Work]
    D --> F[Heat Loss to Cooling]
    D --> G[Pressure Rise P1 to P2]
    G --> H[Pipe and Valve Losses]
    H --> I[Grid Distributor dP]
    I --> J[Fluidized Bed dP bed]
    J --> K[Cyclone dP]
    K --> L[Bag Filter dP]
    L --> M[Exhaust Stack]
    J --> N[Solid Feed System]
    J --> O[Heat Exchanger Internals]
    O --> P[Auxiliary Agitator Power]
    P --> Q[Total Useful Fluidization Power]
    E --> Q
    F --> R[Wasted Energy]
    H --> R
    I --> R
    J --> R
    K --> R
    L --> R
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Power Audit Tree for an Industrial FCC Regenerator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Input Power]
        S1A[Motor Shaft Power] --> S1B[Compressor Isentropic]
        S1A --> S1C[Compressor Isothermal]
    end
    subgraph S2[Delivery Power]
        S2A[High Pressure Air] --> S2B[Grid dP Power]
        S2B --> S2C[Bed dP Power]
        S2C --> S2D[Particle Suspension Work]
        S2C --> S2E[Heat Removal Work]
    end
    subgraph S3[Losses]
        S3A[Piping Friction] --> S3B[Cyclone dP]
        S3B --> S3C[Stack Loss]
    end
    S1B --> S2A
    S1C --> S2A
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic — Selecting the Right Compression Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Compressor Selection] --> Q1{Is process adiabatic?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is compression reversible?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is temperature constant?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R1[Use Isentropic eqn gamma]
    Q2 -- No --> R2[Use Polytropic eqn n]
    Q3 -- Yes --> R3[Use Isothermal eqn ln]
    Q3 -- No --> R4[Use Polytropic eqn n]
    R1 --> End[Shaft Power = W / eta]
    R2 --> End
    R3 --> End
    R4 --> End
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

### Q1. Define the term "power consumption" as applied to a fluidized bed and state the basic governing equation.

**Model Answer:**
Power consumption in a fluidized bed is the rate at which mechanical energy must be supplied to sustain the suspension and circulation of particles against the resistance offered by the bed, grid, and connected piping. It is given by:

$$P = \Delta P_{bed} \times \dot{V}$$

where $\Delta P_{bed}$ is the pressure drop across the bed (Pa) and $\dot{V}$ is the volumetric flow rate of the fluidizing medium (m³/s). The unit is the watt (W = J/s). When the fluidizing gas must be compressed, the **compressor work** term is added to obtain the total power.

> **[3 Marks: 1 for definition, 2 for equation and units]**

---

### Q2. Compare isothermal and isentropic compression of a fluidizing gas. Which gives the lower work requirement and why?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Isothermal | Isentropic |
|---|---|---|
| Process | Slow, perfectly cooled | Adiabatic, reversible |
| Governing equation | $W = P_1 \dot{V}_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$ | $W = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} P_1 \dot{V}_1 [ (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 ]$ |
| Heat transfer | Yes — gas rejected heat to surroundings | No — fully insulated |
| Temperature rise | $T_2 = T_1$ | $T_2 = T_1 (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} > T_1$ |
| Magnitude of work | **Lower** | **Higher** (because it stores thermal energy) |

> **[3 Marks: 1 each for difference and 1 for the lower-work conclusion]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – December 2023 / 2024 Pattern, Module 2]**

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**a)** Starting from first principles, derive an expression for the **isentropic work of compression** of an ideal gas from pressure $P_1$ to $P_2$ at a volumetric flow rate $\dot{V}_1$ measured at inlet conditions. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A fluidized bed dryer handles 800 kg of sand ($d_p = 250 \, \mu m$, $\rho_p = 2500 \, kg/m^3$, $\varepsilon_{mf} = 0.45$) on a circular distributor of diameter 1.0 m. Hot air at 400 K, 110 kPa is used; the minimum fluidization velocity is 0.05 m/s, and the operating superficial velocity is $1.4 \, U_{mf}$. The grid pressure drop is 20 % of the bed pressure drop. The blower has a mechanical efficiency of 70 % and compresses air from 101.3 kPa, 300 K. Calculate:
   (i) Bed pressure drop in kPa
   (ii) Total volumetric flow of air at operating conditions
   (iii) Shaft power of the blower using the isentropic formula with $\gamma = 1.4$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Step 1: For reversible work, the infinitesimal work done ON the gas is:

$$dW = -P \, dV$$

Step 2: For an isentropic (adiabatic, reversible) process with an ideal gas, $P V^{\gamma} = \text{const}$. Let this constant equal $P_1 V_1^{\gamma}$. Then:

$$V = V_1 \left( \frac{P_1}{P} \right)^{1/\gamma}$$

Step 3: The total work is:

$$W = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV = \int_{P_1}^{P_2} V(P) \, dP$$

Step 4: Substitute $V(P)$:

$$W = V_1 P_1^{1/\gamma} \int_{P_1}^{P_2} P^{-1/\gamma} \, dP$$

Step 5: Evaluate:

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} P^{-1/\gamma} dP = \left[ \frac{P^{1 - 1/\gamma}}{1 - 1/\gamma} \right]_{P_1}^{P_2} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \left( P_2^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - P_1^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} \right)$$

Step 6: Therefore:

$$W = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \, P_1 V_1 \left[ \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$$

Step 7: Rate form using $\dot{V}_1$:

$$\dot{W} = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \, P_1 \dot{V}_1 \left[ \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$$

Step 8: With mechanical efficiency:

$$P_{shaft} = \dot{W} / \eta_m$$

> **[7 Marks: 2 marks for Step 1–2, 3 marks for integration, 2 marks for final form with units]**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Bed pressure drop:**

The mass of sand is 800 kg. First compute its bulk volume at minimum fluidization:

$$V_{bed,mf} = \frac{m_p}{\rho_p (1 - \varepsilon_{mf})} = \frac{800}{2500 \times 0.55} = 0.5818 \text{ m}^3$$

Bed cross-section area:

$$A_{bed} = \frac{\pi}{4} (1.0)^2 = 0.7854 \text{ m}^2$$

Bed height at $U_{mf}$:

$$L_{mf} = \frac{V_{bed,mf}}{A_{bed}} = \frac{0.5818}{0.7854} = 0.741 \text{ m}$$

Now compute the bed pressure drop (assume air density $\rho_f \approx 0.88 \, kg/m^3$ at 400 K, 110 kPa; this is small relative to $\rho_p$):

$$\Delta P_{bed} = (1 - \varepsilon_{mf})(\rho_p - \rho_f) g L_{mf}$$

$$\Delta P_{bed} = 0.55 \times (2500 - 0.88) \times 9.81 \times 0.741$$

$$\Delta P_{bed} = 0.55 \times 2499.12 \times 9.81 \times 0.741 \approx 9.99 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa} \approx 10.0 \text{ kPa}$$

**(ii) Volumetric flow of air at operating conditions:**

$$U_0 = 1.4 \, U_{mf} = 1.4 \times 0.05 = 0.07 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\dot{V}_{op} = U_0 \times A_{bed} = 0.07 \times 0.7854 = 0.0550 \text{ m}^3/\text{s (at 400 K, 110 kPa)}$$

Convert to inlet conditions (101.3 kPa, 300 K) using the ideal-gas law $\dot{V} \propto T/P$:

$$\dot{V}_1 = \dot{V}_{op} \times \frac{T_1}{T_{op}} \times \frac{P_{op}}{P_1} = 0.0550 \times \frac{300}{400} \times \frac{110}{101.3} = 0.0550 \times 0.75 \times 1.0858 = 0.0448 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**(iii) Shaft power:**

Total pressure drop the blower must overcome:

$$\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_{bed} + \Delta P_{grid} = 10.0 + 0.2 \times 10.0 = 12.0 \text{ kPa}$$

$$P_2 = P_1 + \Delta P_{total} = 101.3 + 12.0 = 113.3 \text{ kPa}$$

Pressure ratio:

$$r = \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{113.3}{101.3} = 1.1184$$

Isentropic work:

$$\dot{W} = \frac{1.4}{0.4} \times 101300 \times 0.0448 \times \left[ 1.1184^{0.2857} - 1 \right]$$

Compute $1.1184^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \ln(1.1184)} = e^{0.2857 \times 0.1119} = e^{0.03198} = 1.0325$

$$\dot{W} = 3.5 \times 101300 \times 0.0448 \times 0.0325 = 3.5 \times 4538.24 \times 0.0325 \approx 516.4 \text{ W}$$

Shaft power with efficiency 70 %:

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{516.4}{0.70} \approx 738 \text{ W} \approx 0.74 \text{ kW}$$

> **[7 Marks: (i) 2 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 3 marks including unit conversions]**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**a)** Distinguish clearly between **isothermal, isentropic, and polytropic compression** of a gas. For which type of industrial compressor is each assumption most appropriate? **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A mechanically agitated fluidized-bed crystalliser uses a 6-blade Rushton turbine of diameter $D = 0.6$ m, rotating at $N = 2$ rps in a slurry of density $\rho = 1400 \, kg/m^3$ and viscosity $\mu = 0.005 \, Pa \cdot s$. The system is fully baffled.
   (i) Calculate the impeller Reynolds number.
   (ii) The Power number $N_P$ in the fully turbulent regime is 6.0. Calculate the agitator power.
   (iii) If a second, geometrically similar impeller is installed, but with diameter reduced to 0.3 m at the same $N$, what is the new power consumption? Comment on the scaling law. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Conceptual Comparison:**

| Parameter | Isothermal | Isentropic (Adiabatic–Rev) | Polytropic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat transfer | Continuous — $T$ held constant | None | Some, parameterised by $n$ |
| Path on P-V diagram | Isotherm | Adiabat | Intermediate |
| $T$ at exit | $T_2 = T_1$ | $T_2 = T_1 (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | $T_2 = T_1 (P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n}$ |
| Work magnitude | **Lowest** | **Highest** | Intermediate |
| Best suited for | Slow large blowers, very well-cooled reciprocating machines; idealised analysis | Turbo-compressors; ideal reference work | **Real reciprocating and centrifugal compressors** (most industrial cases) |

Step 1: For **isothermal** the gas remains at constant temperature, so the work integral reduces to $P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$.

Step 2: For **isentropic** no heat crosses the boundary, and the polytropic index equals $\gamma$ (1.4 for air, 1.33 for diatomic gases like CO₂).

Step 3: For **polytropic** the actual index $n$ lies between 1 (isothermal) and $\gamma$ (isentropic), determined experimentally.

> **[7 Marks: 2 for tabulation, 2 for equations, 2 for industrial examples, 1 for conclusion]**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Impeller Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho N D^2}{\mu} = \frac{1400 \times 2 \times (0.6)^2}{0.005} = \frac{1400 \times 2 \times 0.36}{0.005} = \frac{1008}{0.005} = 201600$$

Since $Re \gg 10^4$, the flow is **fully turbulent**.

**(ii) Agitator power:**

$$P = N_P \, \rho \, N^3 \, D^5 = 6.0 \times 1400 \times (2)^3 \times (0.6)^5$$

$$P = 6.0 \times 1400 \times 8 \times 0.07776 = 6.0 \times 1400 \times 0.6221 = 5225 \text{ W} \approx 5.23 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Power with $D = 0.3$ m at same $N$:**

$$P_{new} = 6.0 \times 1400 \times 8 \times (0.3)^5 = 6.0 \times 1400 \times 8 \times 0.00243 = 6.0 \times 1400 \times 0.01944 = 163.3 \text{ W}$$

**Comment on scaling law:** Halving the impeller diameter (at constant $N$) reduces the power by a factor of $2^5 = 32$. This is the **power number scaling law**: $P \propto D^5$ in the fully turbulent regime. Designers use this relation for scale-up — but they must keep the **tip speed** $N \cdot D$ (and hence Reynolds number) constant to preserve geometric and dynamic similarity.

> **[7 Marks: (i) 2 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 3 marks with the $D^5$ conclusion]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Pitfalls — Where Marks are Lost:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in Compression Power:** Using $P_1$ in bar instead of Pa makes the answer off by a factor of $10^5$. Always state units explicitly and convert.
> 2. **Inlet vs. Operating Flow Rate:** Students frequently plug the **operating** volumetric flow (at bed temperature and pressure) into the isentropic formula. The correct input is $\dot{V}_1$ at **compressor inlet** conditions.
> 3. **Forgetting the Mechanical Efficiency:** The isentropic work is the **minimum**; the actual shaft power is **higher** by $1/\eta_m$. Examiners specifically look for this distinction.
> 4. **Sign of $\ln(P_2/P_1)$:** For compression $P_2 > P_1$, the logarithm is positive — students sometimes write $\ln(P_1/P_2)$ and obtain a negative work, losing 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Power-Number Mis-application:** $N_P = 6$ is valid only for **fully baffled, fully turbulent, standard Rushton turbines**. In laminar flow ($Re < 10$), the $N_P \propto 1/Re$ correlation is required.
> 6. **Mixing up $\gamma$ and $n$:** Polytropic index $n$ is not equal to $\gamma$ for real machines. Read the question carefully.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Power Consumption):**
> - **Bed Power** is $P_{bed} = \Delta P_{bed} \cdot \dot{V}$. Above $U_{mf}$, $\Delta P_{bed}$ is **constant** (equal to bed weight/area), so power scales with $U_0$.
> - **Three compression models** — Isothermal (lowest work, $\ln$ form), Isentropic (highest work, $\gamma$ form), Polytropic (real machines, $n$ form with $1 < n < \gamma$).
> - **Total blower shaft power** = (compression work + bed work + grid work) / $\eta_m$.
> - **Grid design** trade-off: $\Delta P_{grid} = 10\text{–}30\%$ of $\Delta P_{bed}$. Higher value = better distribution but higher power cost.
> - **Agitator power** in the turbulent regime follows $P = N_P \rho N^3 D^5$, with $N_P \approx 6$ for standard Rushton turbines.
> - **Laminar agitator** uses $P = K_L \mu N^2 D^3$ where $K_L$ is a geometry constant.
> - **Compressor discharge temperature** in adiabatic case: $T_2 = T_1 (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$.
> - **Scaling law** for power: $P \propto D^5$ in the turbulent regime — even modest diameter changes produce dramatic power changes.
> - **Inlet conditions** of the compressor are the reference for $\dot{V}_1$; convert from operating conditions using the ideal-gas law.
> - **Units check**: 1 W = 1 Pa·m³/s = 1 J/s; 1 kW ≈ 1.341 hp.
> - **Industrial rule of thumb**: For gas-fluidized systems, **compressor power dominates** (>85 % of total); for liquid-fluidized systems, **pump power is comparable** to bed work.
> - Always report answers to **3 significant figures** unless the problem specifies otherwise.
> - **KTU 2024 emphasis**: Derivation questions (first-principles isentropic work) carry 7 of the 14 marks — practice the integral steps until they are automatic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Entrainment and Elutriation - Concept of FBH
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Entrainment and Elutriation - Concept of FBH

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Entrainment — Formal Definition

**Entrainment** is the physical phenomenon in which solid particles from a fluidized bed are continuously carried upward and out of the vessel by the fluidizing gas stream. It occurs because the superficial gas velocity ($U$) exceeds the terminal settling velocity ($U_t$) of certain particles, and these particles are essentially "dragged" out with the gas into the freeboard region and beyond the exit of the column.

The entrainment flux is defined as the mass of solids carried over per unit cross-sectional area of the bed per unit time:

$$E = \frac{\dot{m}_{s,out}}{A_{c}}$$

where $\dot{m}_{s,out}$ is the mass flow rate of entrained solids (kg/s) and $A_{c}$ is the cross-sectional area of the column (m²).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Entrainment is fundamentally a **velocity-driven** phenomenon. Any particle whose terminal velocity is below the operating gas velocity is a candidate for entrainment.

### 1.2 Elutriation — Formal Definition

**Elutriation** is the *preferential* and *selective* removal of finer (smaller) particles from a fluidized bed. Because fines have lower terminal velocities, they are entrained faster than coarse particles. Over time, the bed becomes depleted of fines and enriched in coarse particles — a self-classification mechanism.

Mathematically, the **elutriation rate constant** ($K_i^{\ast}$) is defined for a given particle size fraction $i$:

$$K_i^{\ast} = \frac{R_{i,\infty}}{x_{i,\infty}}$$

where $R_{i,\infty}$ is the elutriation rate of size $i$ at infinite freeboard height, and $x_{i,\infty}$ is the weight fraction of size $i$ in the bed at steady state.

### 1.3 Freeboard Height (FBH) — Concept

The **Freeboard Height (FBH)**, often synonymous with the **Transport Disengaging Height (TDH)**, is the vertical distance between the top of the dense fluidized bed (the bed surface or interface) and the gas exit (or cyclone inlet) of the fluidized bed column.

Mathematically:

$$H_{fb} = H_{vessel} - H_{bed}$$

The FBH is the "parking zone" where entrained particles either:
- Fall back into the bed (if $H_{fb} < TDH$), or
- Continue to be carried out of the system (when $H_{fb} \geq TDH$).

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — The "bubble fountain" picture:**
> Imagine a boiling pot of water on a stove (the fluidized bed). The steam bubbles burst at the surface and splash tiny droplets upward. Most droplets fall back into the pot, but the smallest, lightest droplets escape into the air above. The space between the water surface and the kitchen exhaust hood is analogous to the **freeboard**. A taller freeboard (higher hood) gives more droplets a chance to fall back, but beyond a certain height, every remaining droplet escapes — this critical height is the **TDH**. The smallest droplets that escape are analogous to the **fines being elutriated**.

### 1.5 Distinction — Entrainment vs. Elutriation

| Aspect | Entrainment | Elutriation |
|---|---|---|
| **What is carried?** | All particles above their $U_t$ | Selectively the *fines* |
| **Mechanism** | Bulk gas–solid drag | Selective classification |
| **Time-scale** | Instantaneous (steady-state) | Long-term (cumulative depletion) |
| **Design concern** | Cyclone loading, TDH selection | Particle size distribution in bed, makeup policy |

Both phenomena are governed by the same physics, but entrainment is a *rate* and elutriation is a *history*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept of FBH in Fluidization

A fluidized bed column can be divided into three distinct vertical regions:

1. **Dense Phase (Bed):** Below the bed surface, $H_{bed}$. High solids concentration (~40–60 % voids).
2. **Freeboard Region:** From bed surface to gas exit, height $H_{fb}$. Solids concentration decays exponentially.
3. **Exit/Disengaging Zone:** Near the gas exit, where particles may be captured by cyclones or internal baffles.

The solids concentration in the freeboard follows an approximate exponential decay:

$$\rho_{s}(h) = \rho_{s,\infty} + (\rho_{s,0} - \rho_{s,\infty}) \cdot e^{-a \cdot h}$$

where $h$ is the height above the bed surface, $\rho_{s,0}$ is the concentration at the bed surface, and $a$ is a decay constant (m⁻¹).

### 2.2 The "Why" — Why FBH Matters

- **If FBH < TDH:** Some entrained particles still have upward momentum and exit. Entrainment losses are high → product lost, cyclone overloaded, environmental concern.
- **If FBH = TDH:** Disengagement is complete above this height. Entrainment is at its asymptotic minimum value $F_{\infty}$.
- **If FBH > TDH:** No further benefit; capital cost wasted. The entrainment rate is **constant** and equal to $F_{\infty}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (KTU 2024):** The freeboard height is typically designed to be **at least equal to TDH** to minimize carryover. TDH is usually **1.5 to 2.5 times the expanded bed height** for fine powders (Geldart A).

### 2.3 The "How" — Mechanism of Elutriation

1. Gas bubbles burst at the bed surface → throw particles into the freeboard ("splash zone").
2. Particles with low $U_t$ are carried upward by the gas.
3. Larger particles decelerate and fall back due to gravity ($U > U_t$ condition reverses).
4. Fines (with $U_t \ll U$) remain in suspension and are elutriated.
5. Over time, weight fraction $x_i$ of fines in the bed decreases, and elutriation rate $R_i$ decreases too.
6. **Steady state:** Makeup rate of fines = Elutriation rate of fines.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All vertical pipe symbols in equations use `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbol / Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{s,out}}{A_{c}}$ | $E$ = kg/m²·s | Entrainment flux |
| 2 | $U_{t,i} = \dfrac{g \, d_{i}^{2} \, (\rho_{p} - \rho_{g})}{18 \mu}$ | m/s | Terminal velocity (Stokes regime, $\mathrm{Re}_{t} < 0.1$) |
| 3 | $\mathrm{Re}_{t,i} = \dfrac{d_{i} \, U_{t,i} \, \rho_{g}}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Terminal Reynolds number |
| 4 | $K_{i}^{\ast} = \dfrac{R_{i,\infty}}{x_{i,\infty}}$ | kg/m²·s | Elutriation rate constant for size $i$ |
| 5 | $R_{i,\infty} = K_{i}^{\ast} \cdot x_{i,\infty}$ | kg/m²·s | Elutriation rate at infinite FBH |
| 6 | $H_{fb} = H_{vessel} - H_{bed}$ | m | Freeboard height |
| 7 | $TDH = 1.5 \, H_{bed}$ to $2.5 \, H_{bed}$ (Geldart A) | m | Transport Disengaging Height |
| 8 | $\dfrac{F}{F_{\infty}} = 1 + \dfrac{1}{K_{i}^{\ast} \cdot H_{fb}}$ | dimensionless | Effect of FBH on entrainment (Wen & Hashinger) |
| 9 | $E \propto (U - U_{t})^{n}$ | $n \approx 1$ to $2$ | Velocity dependence of entrainment |
| 10 | $\Delta P = \rho_{p} \, g \, (1-\varepsilon) \, H_{bed}$ | Pa | Bed pressure drop |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Catalytic Cracking (FCC):** Freeboard + cyclones recover expensive catalyst; elutriation determines catalyst makeup rate.
- **Coal Combustion (CFB Boilers):** FBH controls fly-ash carryover; TDH sizing prevents stack pollution.
- **Drying & Granulation:** Elutriation sets the steady-state size distribution of product.
- **Pharmaceutical Fluid Bed Dryers:** FBH design prevents loss of active ingredient fines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Terminal Velocity (Stokes' Law)

The terminal velocity $U_t$ is the gas velocity at which the **net force** on a particle is zero: drag force = gravitational minus buoyancy force.

**Step 1 — Force balance on a single spherical particle:**

$$F_{drag} = F_{gravity} - F_{buoyancy}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the expressions:**

$$C_{D} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho_{g} U_{t}^{2} \cdot \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi d^{3}}{6} (\rho_{p} - \rho_{g}) g$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the geometric terms ($\pi/4$ vs $\pi/6$):**

$$C_{D} \, \rho_{g} \, U_{t}^{2} = \frac{4}{3} d \, (\rho_{p} - \rho_{g}) g$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to isolate $U_t$:**

$$U_{t}^{2} = \frac{4 \, d \, (\rho_{p} - \rho_{g}) g}{3 \, C_{D} \, \rho_{g}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Stokes regime** ($C_D = 24 / \mathrm{Re}_t$ for $\mathrm{Re}_t < 0.1$):

$$U_{t}^{2} = \frac{4 \, d \, (\rho_{p} - \rho_{g}) g}{3 \cdot (24 / \mathrm{Re}_t) \cdot \rho_{g}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\mathrm{Re}_t = d U_t \rho_g / \mu$:**

$$U_{t}^{2} = \frac{4 \, d^{2} \, (\rho_{p} - \rho_{g}) g \, \rho_{g}}{72 \, \mu \, \rho_{g}} = \frac{d^{2} \, (\rho_{p} - \rho_{g}) g}{18 \mu}$$

**Step 7 — Final Stokes terminal velocity:**

$$U_{t} = \frac{g \, d^{2} \, (\rho_{p} - \rho_{g})}{18 \mu}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity:** This form is valid only for $\mathrm{Re}_t < 0.1$ (very fine particles in gas). For larger particles, the full drag curve must be used iteratively.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Elutriation Rate from Mass Balance

Consider a well-mixed bed of total weight $W$ with weight fraction $x_i$ of size $i$. Fines of size $i$ are elutriated at rate $R_i$ (kg/m²·s) and added at makeup rate $F_i$ (kg/m²·s).

**Step 1 — Component mass balance on fines of size $i$:**

$$\frac{d(W x_i)}{dt} = F_i \cdot A_c - R_i \cdot A_c$$

**Step 2 — Assume $W$ is approximately constant** (large bed, small fines content), so $W \, dx_i/dt \approx -R_i A_c$.

**Step 3 — Elutriation rate is proportional to the amount present:**

$$R_i = K_i^{\ast} \cdot x_i$$

This is the **definition** of the elutriation rate constant $K_i^{\ast}$.

**Step 4 — Substitute into the ODE:**

$$W \frac{dx_i}{dt} = F_i A_c - K_i^{\ast} x_i A_c$$

**Step 5 — Solve this first-order linear ODE with initial condition** $x_i(0) = x_{i,0}$:

$$x_i(t) = \frac{F_i}{K_i^{\ast}} + \left(x_{i,0} - \frac{F_i}{K_i^{\ast}}\right) e^{-(K_i^{\ast} A_c / W) t}$$

**Step 6 — Steady-state condition** ($t \to \infty$): $dx_i/dt = 0$, so $F_i = K_i^{\ast} x_{i,\infty}$:

$$x_{i,\infty} = \frac{F_i}{K_i^{\ast}}$$

**Step 7 — Elutriation rate at steady state:**

$$R_{i,\infty} = K_i^{\ast} \, x_{i,\infty} = F_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering interpretation:** At steady state, **the makeup rate of fines equals the elutriation rate of fines**. This is the operating principle for catalyst/fines inventory control.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Effect of FBH on Entrainment (Wen & Hashinger Form)

The Wen & Hashinger correlation (1960) is one of the most widely used KTU-board-tested correlations for entrainment as a function of freeboard height.

**Step 1 — Definition of the elutriation rate constant:**

$$K_i^{\ast} = A \left( \frac{U - U_{t,i}}{U_{t,i}} \right)^{m} \frac{\rho_{g}^{n-1} \mu^{2-n}}{g \rho_{p} d_{i}^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Empirical constants** (Wen & Hashinger, fitted to air–solid systems):

$$A = 0.00110, \quad m = 1.30, \quad n = 0.50$$

These are dimensionless fitting constants.

**Step 3 — Entrainment rate at finite FBH:**

$$F_i = F_{i,\infty} \left(1 + \frac{1}{K_i^{\ast} H_{fb}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Equivalently:**

$$\frac{F_i}{F_{i,\infty}} = 1 + \frac{1}{K_i^{\ast} H_{fb}}$$

**Step 5 — Limiting cases:**

- $H_{fb} \to \infty$: $F_i \to F_{i,\infty}$ (asymptotic minimum, classical TDH condition).
- $H_{fb} \to 0$: $F_i \to \infty$ (bed surface entrainment is unbounded — physical singularity of the model).

**Step 6 — TDH definition:** TDH is the freeboard height above which $F_i$ becomes practically constant (within 1–2 % of $F_{i,\infty}$):

$$TDH \approx \frac{10}{K_i^{\ast}}$$

This is the **"10-rule"** — above 10/(K*) the entrainment has essentially reached the asymptotic value.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Elutriation Correlations

| Correlation | Regime | $K_i^{\ast}$ Expression | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wen & Hashinger (1960)** | Broad | $0.00110 \cdot \left(\dfrac{U-U_t}{U_t}\right)^{1.30} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_{g}^{1.50} \mu^{-0.50}}{g \rho_p d^{2}}$ | Air–solid, Geldart A/B |
| **Yagi & Aochi (1955)** | Geldart A | $0.0015 \cdot \mathrm{Re}_t^{-0.6} \cdot \mathrm{Re}^{0.8} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\rho_g}{\rho_p}\right)^{1.4} \cdot \left(\dfrac{U_t}{U}\right)^{2.4}$ | FCC catalyst |
| **Geldart et al. (1979)** | Geldart A | Empirical power-law in $(U - U_t)$ | Laboratory-scale beds |
| **Zenz & Weil (1958)** | Geldart B | $F \propto (U - U_t)^2$ | Coarse particles |

where $\mathrm{Re} = d U \rho_g / \mu$ is the operating Reynolds number based on superficial gas velocity.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Freeboard Region

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the physical regions and mass-flow interactions within a fluidized bed column, including the FBH and entrainment pathways.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Gas Inlet Plenum] --> B[Distributor Plate]
    B --> C[Dense Fluidized Bed]
    C -- Bed surface --> D[Splash Zone]
    D -- Particles thrown up --> E[Freeboard Region FBH]
    E -- Fines escape --> F[Gas Exit to Cyclone]
    E -- Coarse particles fall back --> C
    F --> G[Cyclone Separator]
    G -- Clean gas --> H[Stack]
    G -- Recovered fines --> I[Fines Recycle Hopper]
    I -- Makeup stream --> C
    C -- Elutriation of fines --> E
    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#385723,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#bf8f00,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#0070c0,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#a6a6a6,color:#000000
    style I fill:#385723,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **blue path** (A → B → C) is the gas flow upward.
- The **red block (E)** is the freeboard region — the focus of this topic.
- The **green loop** (C ↔ E ↔ I ↔ C) represents the elutriation–makeup cycle.
- The **purple block (F → G)** is the gas-exit / cyclone-train branch.

### 4.2 Solids Flux vs. Freeboard Height — Conceptual Plot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low FBH - small H_fb] --> B[High entrainment F]
    B --> C[Increasing H_fb]
    C --> D[F decreases exponentially]
    D --> E[FBH equals TDH]
    E --> F[F approaches F_infinity]
    F --> G[FBH greater than TDH]
    G --> H[F remains constant at F_infinity]
    style A fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#bf8f00,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#bf8f00,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#385723,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#385723,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#0070c0,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#0070c0,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the plot:**

- **Red zone:** Sub-TDH freeboard; high, FBH-sensitive entrainment.
- **Yellow zone:** Transition region; exponential decay of flux.
- **Green zone:** TDH; entrainment reaches asymptotic minimum.
- **Blue zone:** Above TDH; no further benefit, wasted column height.

### 4.3 Particle Classification by Terminal Velocity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polydisperse Bed] --> B{U_t compared to U}
    B -- U_t much greater than U --> C[Stays in bed - Coarse]
    B -- U_t slightly greater than U --> D[Splashed but returns - Intermediate]
    B -- U_t less than U --> E[Entrained and elutriated - Fines]
    C --> F[Steady bed composition]
    D --> F
    E --> G[Recovered in cyclone]
    G --> H[Makeup or loss]
    style A fill:#385723,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#bf8f00,color:#000000
    style C fill:#0070c0,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#a6a6a6,color:#000000
    style G fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1.** Define **entrainment** and **elutriation** in a fluidized bed. How are they different from each other? *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

- **Entrainment** is the phenomenon in which solid particles from a fluidized bed are carried out of the column by the gas stream when the superficial gas velocity exceeds the terminal velocity of the particles. *(1.5 Marks)*
- **Elutriation** is the *selective* removal of finer particles from a bed of polydisperse solids, due to the lower terminal velocities of fines. *(1 Mark)*
- **Key difference:** Entrainment applies to *all* particles above their $U_t$; elutriation is *selective* — it preferentially removes fines and is a long-term, cumulative process. *(0.5 Marks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** What is the significance of the **Transport Disengaging Height (TDH)** in the design of a fluidized bed? *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

- TDH is the freeboard height above the dense bed surface above which the entrainment flux of solids becomes essentially constant (asymptotic minimum $F_{\infty}$). *(1.5 Marks)*
- If the gas exit is below TDH, entrained particles are carried out, causing product/catalyst loss and cyclone overload. *(1 Mark)*
- Hence, the freeboard must be designed $\geq$ TDH to minimize carryover — typically 1.5 to 2.5 times the expanded bed height for Geldart A powders. *(0.5 Marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**

#### **Question A**

**(a)** A fluidized bed operates with air ($\rho_g = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s) and catalyst particles of diameter $d_p = 75 \, \mu$m and density $\rho_p = 1500$ kg/m³. Calculate the **terminal velocity** using Stokes' law. Comment on its validity. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Define the **Freeboard Height (FBH)**. Explain with a labeled sketch how entrainment flux varies with freeboard height in a fluidized bed, and identify the **TDH** point on the curve. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify the Stokes regime criterion** $\mathrm{Re}_t < 0.1$. Assume it holds; verify later.

**Step 2 — Apply Stokes' terminal velocity formula:**

$$U_{t} = \frac{g \, d^{2} \, (\rho_{p} - \rho_{g})}{18 \mu}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values** ($g = 9.81$ m/s², $d = 75 \times 10^{-6}$ m):

$$U_{t} = \frac{9.81 \times (75 \times 10^{-6})^{2} \times (1500 - 1.2)}{18 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator:**

$$9.81 \times 5.625 \times 10^{-9} \times 1498.8 = 8.272 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N/m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute denominator:**

$$18 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5} = 3.24 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Step 6 — Divide:**

$$U_{t} = \frac{8.272 \times 10^{-5}}{3.24 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.2553 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 7 — Verify the Stokes regime** by computing $\mathrm{Re}_t$:

$$\mathrm{Re}_{t} = \frac{d \, U_{t} \, \rho_{g}}{\mu} = \frac{75 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.2553 \times 1.2}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.276$$

**Step 8 — Comment:** $\mathrm{Re}_t \approx 1.28 > 0.1$, so Stokes' law is **not strictly valid**. Use the intermediate regime correction factor or iterative drag-curve solution. The actual $U_t$ is somewhat higher than 0.255 m/s. *(2 Marks for the discussion)*

**Final numerical answer (Stokes estimate):** $U_t \approx 0.255$ m/s *(5 Marks for calculation)*

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Step 1 — Definition of FBH:**

$$H_{fb} = H_{vessel} - H_{bed}$$

It is the vertical distance between the top of the dense bed and the gas exit. *(2 Marks)*

**Step 2 — Sketch description** *(3 Marks):*

```
Gas exit ↑
│
│   Entrainment flux F (kg/m²·s)
│   ↑                                 ___________  F_∞
│   │                              __/
│   │                           __/
│   │                        __/
│   │                     __/
│   │                  __/
│   │               __/
│   │            __/
│   │         __/
│   │      __/
│   │   __/
│   │  /
│   └_/___________________________________________→ H_fb (m)
│   0          TDH
```

**Step 3 — Identify TDH on the curve:** TDH is the freeboard height at which the entrainment flux **levels off** at the asymptotic minimum $F_\infty$. Beyond TDH, increasing $H_{fb}$ does **not** reduce entrainment. *(1 Mark)*

**Step 4 — Engineering significance:** Freeboard design must be $\geq$ TDH to prevent unnecessary loss of catalyst/product. TDH is typically 1.5–2.5 times the expanded bed height for Geldart A powders. *(1 Mark)*

---

#### **Question B (Alternative choice)**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **elutriation rate constant** $K_i^{\ast}$ based on a steady-state mass balance on a polydisperse fluidized bed. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Using the **Wen & Hashinger correlation**, calculate the entrainment rate of a size fraction $i$ given: $U = 0.5$ m/s, $U_{t,i} = 0.1$ m/s, $\rho_g = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $\rho_p = 1200$ kg/m³, $d_i = 100 \, \mu$m, and $H_{fb} = 1.5$ m. Assume $F_{i,\infty} = 0.05$ kg/m²·s. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**Step 1 — Setup:** Consider a polydisperse bed with total weight $W$ and weight fraction $x_i$ of size $i$. Fines are elutriated at rate $R_i$ (kg/m²·s) and added via makeup at $F_i$ (kg/m²·s).

**Step 2 — Mass balance on size $i$:**

$$W \frac{dx_i}{dt} = F_i A_c - R_i A_c$$

**Step 3 — Define the elutriation rate constant** such that:

$$R_i = K_i^{\ast} x_i$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$W \frac{dx_i}{dt} = (F_i - K_i^{\ast} x_i) A_c$$

**Step 5 — Solve the linear ODE with $x_i(0) = x_{i,0}$:**

$$x_i(t) = \frac{F_i}{K_i^{\ast}} + \left(x_{i,0} - \frac{F_i}{K_i^{\ast}}\right) \exp\!\left(-\frac{K_i^{\ast} A_c}{W} t\right)$$

**Step 6 — At steady state** ($t \to \infty$): $dx_i/dt = 0$, giving $F_i = K_i^{\ast} x_{i,\infty}$.

**Step 7 — The elutriation rate constant** is therefore:

$$K_i^{\ast} = \frac{R_{i,\infty}}{x_{i,\infty}} = \frac{F_i}{x_{i,\infty}}$$

> This is the fundamental definition of $K_i^{\ast}$ used in all elutriation correlations. *(7 Marks distributed as 2+2+1+1+1 for steps 1–7)*

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $K_i^{\ast}$ using Wen & Hashinger:**

$$K_{i}^{\ast} = 0.00110 \cdot \left( \frac{U - U_{t,i}}{U_{t,i}} \right)^{1.30} \cdot \frac{\rho_{g}^{1.50} \, \mu^{-0.50}}{g \, \rho_{p} \, d_{i}^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the velocity ratio term:**

$$\frac{U - U_{t,i}}{U_{t,i}} = \frac{0.5 - 0.1}{0.1} = 4.0$$

$$4.0^{1.30} = e^{1.30 \ln 4} = e^{1.30 \times 1.3863} = e^{1.8022} = 6.063$$

**Step 3 — Compute the density–viscosity term:**

$$\rho_g^{1.5} = (1.2)^{1.5} = 1.3145 \text{ kg}^{1.5}/\text{m}^{4.5}$$

$$\mu^{-0.5} = (1.8 \times 10^{-5})^{-0.5} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.8 \times 10^{-5}}} = \frac{1}{4.243 \times 10^{-3}} = 235.7$$

**Step 4 — Combine numerator:**

$$1.3145 \times 235.7 = 309.8$$

**Step 5 — Compute denominator:**

$$g \, \rho_p \, d_i^{2} = 9.81 \times 1200 \times (100 \times 10^{-6})^{2} = 9.81 \times 1200 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$= 1.1772 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 6 — Ratio:**

$$\frac{309.8}{1.1772 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.632 \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 7 — Multiply all factors:**

$$K_{i}^{\ast} = 0.00110 \times 6.063 \times 2.632 \times 10^{6} = 17{,}550 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 8 — Apply the FBH correction:**

$$F_{i} = F_{i,\infty} \left(1 + \frac{1}{K_{i}^{\ast} H_{fb}}\right) = 0.05 \left(1 + \frac{1}{17{,}550 \times 1.5}\right)$$

$$= 0.05 \left(1 + \frac{1}{26{,}325}\right) = 0.05 \times 1.000038 \approx 0.0500 \text{ kg/m}^{2}\text{·s}$$

**Step 9 — Interpretation:** Since $K_i^{\ast} H_{fb} \gg 1$ (freeboard far exceeds $10/K_i^{\ast}$), the bed operates **well above TDH**, and entrainment has reached its asymptotic minimum. *(7 Marks distributed as 2+2+1+1+1 for steps 1–9)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Scheme Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to verify Stokes' regime** when applying $U_t = gd^2(\rho_p - \rho_g)/18\mu$. Always check $\mathrm{Re}_t < 0.1$ and comment if invalid. *(Loses 1–2 marks)*
> 2. **Confusing TDH with FBH** — they are *not* the same; FBH is the actual height installed, TDH is the minimum required. *(Loses 1 mark)*
> 3. **Wrong units in $K_i^{\ast}$** — the elutriation rate constant has units of m/s (or m⁻¹ when used in the FBH correction). Watch dimensional consistency.
> 4. **Missing the steady-state condition** in the elutriation derivation — without $t \to \infty$, the relation $F_i = K_i^{\ast} x_{i,\infty}$ is not justified. *(Loses 2 marks)*
> 5. **In Wen & Hashinger:** the constants $A = 0.00110$, $m = 1.30$, $n = 0.50$ are *empirical* and must be cited from the correlation; do not invent other values.
> 6. **Sketch quality:** Always draw the F vs. $H_{fb}$ curve with axes labeled, and clearly mark TDH on the curve. *(Loses 1–2 marks if missing)*

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Entrainment** = bulk carryover; **Elutriation** = selective fine removal. Both are FBH-dependent.
- **FBH** = $H_{vessel} - H_{bed}$; **TDH** = minimum FBH at which entrainment reaches $F_\infty$.
- **Stokes' terminal velocity** is $U_t = g d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_g) / (18\mu)$; valid for $\mathrm{Re}_t < 0.1$.
- **Elutriation rate constant** $K_i^{\ast} = R_{i,\infty} / x_{i,\infty}$ — a particle-size-specific property.
- **Steady state:** $F_i^{makeup} = R_{i,\infty} = K_i^{\ast} x_{i,\infty}$.
- **FBH effect:** $F_i = F_{i,\infty} (1 + 1/(K_i^{\ast} H_{fb}))$ — Wen & Hashinger form.
- **"10-rule":** TDH $\approx 10 / K_i^{\ast}$ (when $\mathrm{Re}_t < 0.1$ and Geldart A).
- **Design guideline:** For Geldart A powders, TDH $\approx 1.5$ to $2.5 \times H_{bed}$.
- **Key correlations to memorize:** Wen & Hashinger, Yagi & Aochi, Geldart et al., Zenz & Weil.
- **Bed pressure drop:** $\Delta P = \rho_p g (1 - \varepsilon) H_{bed}$ — independent of $U$ beyond minimum fluidization.
- **Geldart classification** (recap from Module 1): A = aeratable, B = sand-like, C = cohesive, D = spoutable.
- **Engineering applications:** FCC units, CFB boilers, dryers, granulators, roasters.
- **Practical tip:** Install **internal cyclones** or **baffles** if column height is constrained below TDH.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TDH
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** TDH ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition **Transport Disengaging Height (TDH)** is the vertical height above the surface of a fluidized bed at which the net entrainment rate of solid particles becomes essentially constant (saturates) and does not decrease further with increasing height. Above this height, only the **saturated carrying capacity** (*G*ₛₐₜ) of the gas exists, and the concentration of solids in the gas phase remains constant. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Implication:** TDH defines the *minimum height* at which internal cyclones (or other primary separation devices) must be installed in the freeboard of a fluidized bed reactor to ensure efficient recovery of catalyst/fines. Placing cyclones **below** TDH results in excessive catalyst loss. ### 1.2 Physical Intuition — The "Bubbling Kettle Analogy" Imagine boiling milk in a deep saucepan on a stove: - In the **bubbling zone** (dense bed), large bubbles rise and burst violently at the surface, throwing droplets of milk **upwards** in all directions. - Just above the surface, droplets **fall back** into the pan due to gravity — this is the **splash zone**. - As you go higher up the walls of the saucepan, fewer droplets make it back; only the **very smallest mist particles** escape. - The height where **droplets can no longer return** to the pan is analogous to **TDH**. Above this height, only a constant fine mist escapes — the saturated entrainment. Similarly, in a fluidized bed: - Gas bubbles **burst at the bed surface**, ejecting solid particles into the freeboard. - Larger particles **fall back** due to gravity within a finite height (the splash zone). - Above TDH, only particles too small to settle back are carried out — this flux is constant and represents **elutriation**. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Value:** For industrial FCC (Fluid Catalytic Cracking) units, TDH typically ranges from **6 m to 12 m** depending on gas velocity, particle size, and column diameter. Design guidelines (e.g., from Grace, 1986) suggest **TDH/D_t ≈ 2 to 4** for many practical units. ### 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance | Term | Symbol | Meaning | |------|--------|---------| | Transport Disengaging Height | TDH | Height above bed where entrainment becomes constant | | Freeboard | — | Total empty height above the dense bed | | Splash Zone | — | Region between bed surface and TDH where particles fall back | | Saturated Carrying Capacity | *G*ₛₐₜ | Maximum solid flux above TDH (kg/m²·s) | | Elutriation | — | Selective removal of fines from the bed | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation The concept of TDH arises from the **two-zone model** of the freeboard region: 1. **Splash Zone (0 → TDH):** Just above the dense bed surface, particles ejected by bursting bubbles have a downward velocity component (due to gravity) and a horizontal component (from bubble eruption). The net result is that coarser particles decelerate and fall back, while finer particles continue upward. The solids flux **decreases exponentially** with height in this zone. 2. **Dilute Phase (above TDH):** The remaining particles are too fine to settle back against the upward gas drag. Their flux becomes constant and equals the **saturated carrying capacity** (*G*ₛₐₜ) of the gas. This constant flux region is what defines the upper boundary (TDH). Mathematically, the solids flux in the freeboard follows an exponential decay: $$ G_s(h) = G_{sat} + (G_{bed\ surface} - G_{sat}) \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{h}{H_0}\right) $$ where *H*₀ is a characteristic decay length related to particle terminal velocity and gas velocity. ### 2.2 Why is TDH Important? - **Catalyst Recovery & Economics:** TDH determines where cyclones must be placed. Below TDH, the cyclone would face a much higher dust load (because particles haven't yet disengaged), leading to higher erosion and pressure drop. - **Reactor Total Height:** Reactor freeboard = TDH + (some safety margin, typically 1–2 m). - **Elutriation Control:** Above TDH, fines loss is governed by *G*ₛₐₜ alone — predictable and manageable. - **Fluidization Quality:** Helps prevent defluidization by ensuring the dense bed receives sufficient recycle of coarse particles. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Critical Correlations) > [!NOTE] > All the following correlations are commonly tested in KTU examinations. Master the **Zenz-Othmer** and **Colakyan** correlations as primary choices. | # | Correlation Name | Formula | Range / Applicability | |---|------------------|---------|----------------------| | 1 | **Zenz & Othmer (1959)** | $\dfrac{TDH}{D_t} = \left(18 \cdot Re_t\right)^{0.5}$ | Coarse particles, Geldart B/D, freeboard design | | 2 | **Fournol et al. (1973)** | $\dfrac{TDH}{D_t} = \dfrac{18 \cdot Re_t^{0.5}}{0.05 + x_f}$ | Includes fines correction (x_f = mass fraction < 40 μm) | | 3 | **Colakyan et al. (1986)** | $TDH = 1.2 \cdot \left(H_{mf} \cdot D_b \cdot G_s\right)^{0.5} \cdot \left(\dfrac{G}{G_{mf}}\right)^{0.6}$ | Industrial FCC units; uses operating gas flux | | 4 | **Smolders & Baeyens (1997)** | $TDH = 0.5 \cdot D_t \cdot Ga^{-0.16} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\rho_g}{\rho_p}\right)^{-0.12} \cdot \left(\dfrac{G}{G_{mf}}\right)^{0.25}$ | Geldart B/D powders, comprehensive form | | 5 | **Baron et al. (1988)** | $TDH = 7.4 \cdot D_t \cdot \left(G - G_e\right)^{0.7} \cdot \left[g \cdot d_p \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_g)\right]^{-0.7}$ | General fluidization design | Where the supporting variables are defined as: | Variable | Definition | Units | |----------|------------|-------| | *Re*ₜ | Particle Reynolds number = $d_p \cdot u_t \cdot \rho_g / \mu$ | dimensionless | | *D*ₜ | Column diameter | m | | *d*ₚ | Particle (Sauter mean) diameter | m | | *u*ₜ | Particle terminal velocity | m/s | | *G*ₛ | Solid mass flux above TDH | kg/m²·s | | *G*, *G*ₘf | Operating and minimum fluidization gas mass fluxes | kg/m²·s | | *H*ₘf | Bed height at minimum fluidization | m | | *Ga* | Galileo number = $d_p^3 \cdot \rho_g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_g) \cdot g / \mu^2$ | dimensionless | | *x*ₓ | Mass fraction of fines smaller than 40 μm | dimensionless | ### 2.4 Engineering Utility The TDH concept is central to: - **Petroleum refining:** FCC regenerator and reactor design (where catalyst recovery is critical to economics) - **Coal combustion:** Circulating fluidized bed (CFB) boiler freeboard sizing - **Polymerization reactors:** Polyolefin production with metallocene catalysts - **Pharmaceutical drying:** Fluidized bed dryers for granular products - **Mineral processing:** Roasting and calcination operations --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Example ### 3.1 Conceptual Derivation of the Zenz-Othmer Form The empirical correlation $\dfrac{TDH}{D_t} = (18 \cdot Re_t)^{0.5}$ can be understood from first principles by considering a particle ejected at the bed surface with initial upward velocity equal to the superficial gas velocity *u*₀. **Force balance on the ejecting particle:** $$ m_p \frac{du}{dt} = F_{drag} - F_{gravity} - F_{buoyancy} $$ For spherical particles in the Stokes regime: $$ F_{drag} = 3\pi \mu d_p u $$ $$ F_{gravity} - F_{buoyancy} = \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho_g) g $$ At terminal velocity, these forces balance, giving the well-known Stokes terminal velocity: $$ u_t = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_g) g}{18 \mu} $$ **Distance traveled to disengage:** The distance a particle travels upward before decelerating to terminal velocity (or zero) scales with the column diameter and the square root of the particle Reynolds number. Empirical fits to entrainment data yield: $$ \boxed{\frac{TDH}{D_t} = (18 \cdot Re_t)^{0.5}} $$ This is the **Zenz-Othmer** form and is the most cited in undergraduate texts. ### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Zenz-Othmer Method **Problem Statement:** Calculate the Transport Disengaging Height (TDH) for an air-fluidized bed of FCC catalyst using the Zenz-Othmer correlation. **Given Data:** - Particle diameter: *d*ₚ = 60 μm = 60 × 10⁻⁶ m - Particle density: *ρ*ₚ = 1500 kg/m³ - Air density: *ρ*ₘ = 1.2 kg/m³ - Air viscosity: *μ* = 1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s - Column diameter: *D*ₜ = 1.5 m - Gravitational acceleration: *g* = 9.81 m/s² **Required:** TDH (in meters) --- #### Step 1 — Initial Guess of Terminal Velocity (Stokes Regime Assumption) Stokes law is valid for *Re* < 0.1. As a starting estimate: $$ u_{t,\text{Stokes}} = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_g) g}{18 \mu} $$ Substituting values: $$ u_{t,\text{Stokes}} = \frac{(60 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times (1500 - 1.2) \times 9.81}{18 \times 1.81 \times 10^{-5}} $$ Numerator: $$ (60 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 3.6 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2 $$ $$ 3.6 \times 10^{-9} \times 1498.8 \times 9.81 = 5.293 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg·m/s}^2 $$ Denominator: $$ 18 \times 1.81 \times 10^{-5} = 3.258 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/(m·s)} $$ Therefore: $$ u_{t,\text{Stokes}} = \frac{5.293 \times 10^{-5}}{3.258 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.1624 \text{ m/s} $$ --- #### Step 2 — Verify Reynolds Number $$ Re_1 = \frac{\rho_g \cdot d_p \cdot u_t}{\mu} = \frac{1.2 \times 60 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.1624}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}} $$ $$ Re_1 = \frac{1.169 \times 10^{-5}}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.646 $$ Since *Re*₁ = 0.646 > 0.1, **Stokes law is NOT valid**. We must iterate using the intermediate (Allen) regime. --- #### Step 3 — Iteration Using Intermediate Regime For 0.1 < *Re* < 1000, use: $$ C_D = \frac{18.5}{Re^{0.6}} $$ And the terminal velocity expression: $$ u_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot d_p \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_g) \cdot g}{3 \cdot C_D \cdot \rho_g}} $$ **Iteration 2** — Start with *Re* = 0.646: $$ C_D = \frac{18.5}{0.646^{0.6}} = \frac{18.5}{0.789} = 23.45 $$ $$ u_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 60 \times 10^{-6} \times 1498.8 \times 9.81}{3 \times 23.45 \times 1.2}} $$ Numerator: $$ 4 \times 60 \times 10^{-6} \times 1498.8 \times 9.81 = 3.529 \text{ (kg·m/s²)} $$ Denominator: $$ 3 \times 23.45 \times 1.2 = 84.42 \text{ kg/(m³·s²)} $$ $$ u_t = \sqrt{\frac{3.529}{84.42}} = \sqrt{0.04181} = 0.2045 \text{ m/s} $$ Updated Reynolds: $$ Re_2 = \frac{1.2 \times 60 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.2045}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.814 $$ --- **Iteration 3** — With *Re* = 0.814: $$ C_D = \frac{18.5}{0.814^{0.6}} = \frac{18.5}{0.888} = 20.83 $$ $$ u_t = \sqrt{\frac{3.529}{3 \times 20.83 \times 1.2}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.529}{75.00}} = \sqrt{0.04705} = 0.2169 \text{ m/s} $$ $$ Re_3 = \frac{1.2 \times 60 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.2169}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.863 $$ --- **Iteration 4** — With *Re* = 0.863: $$ C_D = \frac{18.5}{0.863^{0.6}} = \frac{18.5}{0.913} = 20.26 $$ $$ u_t = \sqrt{\frac{3.529}{3 \times 20.26 \times 1.2}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.529}{72.94}} = \sqrt{0.04838} = 0.2199 \text{ m/s} $$ $$ Re_4 = \frac{1.2 \times 60 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.2199}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.875 $$ --- **Iteration 5** — With *Re* = 0.875: $$ C_D = \frac{18.5}{0.875^{0.6}} = \frac{18.5}{0.921} = 20.09 $$ $$ u_t = \sqrt{\frac{3.529}{3 \times 20.09 \times 1.2}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.529}{72.32}} = \sqrt{0.04880} = 0.2209 \text{ m/s} $$ $$ Re_5 = \frac{1.2 \times 60 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.2209}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.879 $$ --- **Convergence Check:** |0.879 − 0.875| / 0.875 < 1% ✓ **Converged Values:** $$ \boxed{u_t = 0.221 \text{ m/s}, \quad Re_t = 0.88} $$ --- #### Step 4 — Apply Zenz-Othmer Correlation $$ \frac{TDH}{D_t} = (18 \cdot Re_t)^{0.5} = (18 \times 0.88)^{0.5} = (15.84)^{0.5} = 3.98 $$ $$ TDH = 3.98 \times D_t = 3.98 \times 1.5 = 5.97 \text{ m} $$ --- #### Step 5 — Final Answer with Industrial Sanity Check $$ \boxed{TDH \approx 6.0 \text{ m}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Validation:** Industrial FCC units typically operate with TDH ≈ 4–10 m for columns of diameter 1–3 m. Our calculated value of 6 m for a 1.5 m column gives TDH/D_t ≈ 4, which falls within the accepted design range of 2–4 (Grace, 1986). The answer is **dimensionally consistent and physically reasonable**. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Fluidized Bed Reactor — Freeboard Architecture (TDH Zones) The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the functional architecture of a fluidized bed reactor, identifying each region relevant to TDH calculation and cyclone placement. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["INLET GAS
Superficial Velocity U0"] --> B["DENSE FLUIDIZED BED
Height = Hmf"] B --> C["BED SURFACE
Bubble Eruption Zone"] C --> D["SPLASH ZONE
0 to TDH Region
Particles Fall Back Here"] D --> E["TDH MARKER
Transport Disengaging Height
Entrainment Becomes Constant"] E --> F["DILUTE PHASE
Saturated Carrying Capacity Gsat"] F --> G["PRIMARY CYCLONE
Placed AT or ABOVE TDH
Recovers Coarse Fines"] G --> H["SECONDARY CYCLONE
Captures Fine Particles"] H --> I["CLEAN GAS OUTLET
Dust Free"] G --> J["DIPLEG RETURN
Solids Recycled to Bed"] J --> B style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#01579b,color:#000 style B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style C fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style D fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style E fill:#ff5252,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#fff style F fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 style G fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style H fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style I fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 style J fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Solids Flux Profile in the Freeboard ```mermaid flowchart LR K["HEIGHT H ABOVE BED
X Axis: Vertical Position"] --> L["ZONE 1: SPLASH REGION
Exponential Decay
Gs decreases rapidly"] L --> M["ZONE 2: TDH BOUNDARY
Decay Approaches Gsat"] M --> N["ZONE 3: DILUTE PHASE
Constant Flux Equal to Gsat
Only Fines Escape"] style K fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000 style L fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style M fill:#ff5252,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#fff style N fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Design Decision Logic for Cyclone Placement ```mermaid flowchart TD Q{"IS FREEBOARD HEIGHT
>= TDH?"} -->|"YES"| R["SAFE INSTALL
Place Cyclone at Freeboard Top
Low Solid Loading"] Q -->|"NO"| S["RISKY INSTALL
Cyclone Below TDH
High Erosion Risk"] style Q fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style R fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style S fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- #### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Define Transport Disengaging Height (TDH). Why is it important in fluidized bed reactor design?** **Model Answer:** > **Definition:** Transport Disengaging Height (TDH) is the height above the fluidized bed surface at which the net entrainment rate of solid particles becomes essentially constant and equal to the saturated carrying capacity (*G*ₛₐₜ) of the gas. Above TDH, solids flux no longer decreases with increasing height. > **Importance:** > (i) TDH determines the **minimum freeboard height** required in a fluidized bed reactor. > (ii) Internal cyclones must be installed at or **above TDH** to ensure efficient catalyst recovery and minimize erosion. > (iii) Below TDH, entrainment flux is exponentially higher, leading to excessive catalyst loss, increased cyclone loading, and possible defluidization. > (iv) TDH directly impacts **capital cost** of the reactor (taller vessel = more steel) and operating economics. --- #### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Explain the role of particle terminal velocity in determining TDH using the Zenz-Othmer correlation.** **Model Answer:** The Zenz-Othmer correlation expresses TDH as: $$ \frac{TDH}{D_t} = (18 \cdot Re_t)^{0.5} $$ where *Re*ₜ = *d*ₚ *u*ₜ *ρ*ₘ / *μ* is the **particle Reynolds number based on terminal velocity**. **Role of terminal velocity:** - Higher *u*ₜ indicates **larger/denser particles** which require greater distance to disengage from the upward gas stream. - *Re*ₜ scales directly with *u*ₜ, so TDH ∝ *u*ₜ⁰·⁵ — a **square-root dependence**. - For fine particles (low *u*ₜ), TDH is small; for coarse particles, TDH is much larger. - The correlation indicates that **higher gas velocities** (drag the particles more vigorously) and **larger columns** both increase TDH proportionally. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- #### Question B (Choice A) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** Derive the Zenz-Othmer correlation for Transport Disengaging Height. State its limitations clearly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A fluidized bed operates with the following data. Calculate TDH using Zenz-Othmer correlation: - Particle diameter: *d*ₚ = 80 μm - Particle density: *ρ*ₚ = 1800 kg/m³ - Air density: *ρ*ₘ = 1.2 kg/m³ - Air viscosity: *μ* = 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s - Column diameter: *D*ₜ = 2.0 m **(7 marks)** --- ##### Model Solution — Part (a) **Derivation Concept:** The Zenz-Othmer correlation arises from empirical fit of freeboard entrainment data. Theoretically, when a particle of diameter *d*ₚ is ejected at the bed surface with vertical velocity equal to *u*₀ (superficial gas velocity), it decelerates due to gravity and drag forces. The **distance required for the particle to fall back to zero upward velocity** scales with the column diameter *D*ₜ. For particles in the intermediate drag regime, integrating the momentum equation: $$ \int_0^{TDH} \frac{u \, du}{g - \frac{F_{drag}}{m_p}} = D_t $$ Empirical data fit yields: $$ \boxed{\frac{TDH}{D_t} = (18 \cdot Re_t)^{0.5}} $$ **Limitations:** 1. Valid only for **Geldart B and D** particles (coarse). 2. Assumes **uniform particle size** — not accurate for wide distributions with significant fines (< 40 μm). 3. Does **not account for operating gas velocity** beyond *Re*ₜ. 4. Ignores column geometry effects (e.g., internal baffles, heat transfer tubes). 5. For Geldart A (fine) powders, Fournol correction with fines fraction must be used. --- ##### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1: Initial terminal velocity (Stokes):** $$ u_t = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_g) g}{18\mu} = \frac{(80 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times 1798.8 \times 9.81}{18 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5}} $$ Numerator: 6.4 × 10⁻⁹ × 1798.8 × 9.81 = 1.129 × 10⁻⁴ Denominator: 3.24 × 10⁻⁴ $$ u_{t,\text{Stokes}} = 0.3485 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 2: Check Reynolds:** $$ Re = \frac{1.2 \times 80 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.3485}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.86 $$ Since *Re* = 1.86 > 0.1, Stokes not valid. Iterate. **Step 3: Iterate (Allen regime):** | Iteration | *Re* | *C*_D = 18.5/*Re*⁰·⁶ | *u*ₜ (m/s) | |-----------|------|------------------------|-------------| | 1 | 1.86 | 18.5/1.86⁰·⁶ = 12.78 | 0.486 | | 2 | 2.59 | 18.5/2.59⁰·⁶ = 10.85 | 0.529 | | 3 | 2.82 | 18.5/2.82⁰·⁶ = 10.36 | 0.541 | | 4 | 2.89 | 18.5/2.89⁰·⁶ = 10.24 | 0.544 | | 5 | 2.91 | 18.5/2.91⁰·⁶ = 10.21 | 0.545 | **Converged:** *u*ₜ = 0.545 m/s, *Re*ₜ = 2.91 **Step 4: Apply Zenz-Othmer:** $$ \frac{TDH}{D_t} = (18 \times 2.91)^{0.5} = (52.38)^{0.5} = 7.24 $$ $$ \boxed{TDH = 7.24 \times 2.0 = 14.48 \text{ m}} $$ --- #### Question B (Choice B) [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **(a)** Compare the Zenz-Othmer, Fournol, and Colakyan correlations for TDH. Under what conditions is each preferred? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A fluidized catalytic cracking unit has the following operating data. Estimate TDH using the Colakyan correlation: - Bed diameter: *D*_b = 3.0 m - Bed height at minimum fluidization: *H*ₘf = 0.6 m - Solid flux above TDH: *G*ₛ = 0.8 kg/m²·s - Operating gas mass flux: *G* = 1.4 kg/m²·s - Gas mass flux at minimum fluidization: *G*ₘf = 0.5 kg/m²·s **(7 marks)** --- ##### Model Solution — Part (a) **Comparison Table:** | Aspect | Zenz-Othmer | Fournol et al. | Colakyan et al. | |--------|-------------|----------------|-----------------| | **Year** | 1959 | 1973 | 1986 | | **Basis** | Particle Re & column diameter | Particle Re + fines correction | Operating gas flux + bed geometry | | **Key Parameters** | *Re*ₜ, *D*ₜ | *Re*ₜ, *x*ₓ (fines fraction) | *H*ₘf, *D*_b, *G*ₛ, *G*/*G*ₘf | | **Fines Correction** | None | Yes (x_f = fines < 40 μm) | Implicit via *G*ₛ | | **Operating Velocity** | Indirect (via *Re*ₜ) | Indirect | Explicit | | **Best Suited For** | Quick estimates, Geldart B/D | Powders with significant fines | Industrial FCC scale-up | | **Limitations** | No fines effect | Empirical constants | Limited to FCC-type systems | **Preferred Conditions:** - **Zenz-Othmer:** Coarse particles (Geldart B/D), preliminary design, narrow size distributions. - **Fournol:** When fines (< 40 μm) constitute > 5% of bed mass — typical for Geldart A powders. - **Colakyan:** Industrial design of FCC and CFB units where operating gas velocity differs significantly from *u*ₘf. --- ##### Model Solution — Part (b) **Colakyan Correlation:** $$ TDH = 1.2 \times (H_{mf} \cdot D_b \cdot G_s)^{0.5} \times \left(\frac{G}{G_{mf}}\right)^{0.6} $$ **Step 1: Compute velocity ratio:** $$ \frac{G}{G_{mf}} = \frac{1.4}{0.5} = 2.8 $$ $$ \left(\frac{G}{G_{mf}}\right)^{0.6} = 2.8^{0.6} = 1.943 $$ **Step 2: Compute geometric term:** $$ H_{mf} \cdot D_b \cdot G_s = 0.6 \times 3.0 \times 0.8 = 1.44 \text{ m²·kg/(m²·s)} $$ $$ (1.44)^{0.5} = 1.200 $$ **Step 3: Final calculation:** $$ TDH = 1.2 \times 1.200 \times 1.943 = 2.80 \text{ m} $$ $$ \boxed{TDH \approx 2.8 \text{ m}} $$ **Industrial sanity check:** For a 3 m diameter FCC unit, TDH/D ≈ 0.93 — this is on the **lower end** of typical values, suggesting either a low operating velocity ratio or a high *G*ₛ value. In practice, designers add a **safety factor of 1.5–2** to ensure adequate disengagement. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to verify Reynolds regime:** Students apply Stokes law blindly even when *Re* > 0.1. This gives incorrect *u*ₜ and cascading errors in TDH. **(Lose 2–3 marks)** > 2. **Unit inconsistencies:** Mixing SI and CGS units in correlation formulas. Always ensure *d*ₚ in meters, *μ* in Pa·s, *ρ* in kg/m³. > 3. **Ignoring iteration:** Writing "Re = 0.646 > 0.1, therefore Stokes is invalid" without performing the iteration is incomplete. **(Lose 1–2 marks)** > 4. **Confusing TDH with freeboard height:** Freeboard = TDH + safety margin (1–2 m). Students often forget the safety margin. > 5. **Using wrong correlation:** Applying Zenz-Othmer for Geldart A (fine) powders without Fournol's fines correction. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist - [x] **TDH Definition:** Height above bed where entrainment rate becomes constant (= saturated carrying capacity *G*ₛₐₜ). - [x] **Two-Zone Model:** Splash zone (exponential decay) above bed → TDH boundary → Dilute phase (constant flux). - [x] **Zenz-Othmer Formula:** TDH/Dₜ = (18·Reₜ)^0.5 — most cited in KTU textbooks. - [x] **Fournol Modification:** Adds fines correction (x_f < 40 μm). - [x] **Colakyan Formula:** TDH = 1.2·(H_mf·D_b·G_s)^0.5·(G/G_mf)^0.6 — industrial FCC. - [x] **Smolders-Baeyens:** Includes Galileo number; comprehensive form. - [x] **Terminal Velocity Calculation:** Always verify Stokes regime; iterate for Allen regime (*Re* 0.1–1000) or Newton's regime (*Re* > 1000). - [x] **Drag Coefficient Equations:** - Stokes (*Re* < 0.1): *C*_D = 24/*Re* - Allen (0.1 < *Re* < 1000): *C*_D = 18.5/*Re*⁰·⁶ - Newton (*Re* > 1000): *C*_D = 0.44 - [x] **Industrial Design Value:** TDH/Dₜ typically ranges from **2 to 4** for most fluidized beds. - [x] **Cyclone Placement Rule:** Cyclones MUST be installed at or above TDH to minimize erosion and maximize recovery. - [x] **Key Constants to Memorize:** *g* = 9.81 m/s², *ρ*_air ≈ 1.2 kg/m³, *μ*_air ≈ 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s. - [x] **Engineering Applications:** FCC units, CFB boilers, polymerization reactors, mineral roasters, pharmaceutical dryers. --- > **End of Module 2 — TDH Notes**

topics.title: Solid Movement - Vertical and Horizontal Movement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solid Movement - Vertical and Horizontal Movement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Solid Movement in Fluidized Beds — Formal Definition

In a gas–solid fluidized bed, **solid movement** refers to the macroscopic and microscopic motion of solid particles induced by the rising gas bubbles and interstitial gas flow. The KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 2) treats solid movement as a **two-component phenomenon**:

1. **Vertical Movement (Axial Solid Motion)** — Bulk upward/downward transport of solids driven by bubbles (emulsion carries solids downward in the wake, freeboard carries particles upward by entrainment).
2. **Horizontal Movement (Lateral Solid Motion)** — Radial and lateral displacement of particles driven by bubble eruption at the surface and bubble-induced shear at the bed walls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The topic "Solid Movement" focuses on how **bubbles act as the engine of solid mixing**. Without bubble motion, no gross solid circulation can occur — this is the unifying concept.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boiling Popcorn" Picture

Imagine a large popcorn maker filled with unpopped kernels at the bottom:
- The **hot air bubbles** rise like "balloons" through the kernels.
- As each bubble rises, it carries a **trailing wake of kernels** upward.
- Near the surface, the bubble bursts (erupts) and throws kernels sideways — this is the **horizontal throw**.
- The displaced kernels then fall back down along the walls and through the dense phase — this is the **gross circulation loop**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Think of a bubble as a "particle taxi" — it picks up solids at the bottom, drops them at the top, and the eruption spreads them horizontally. The bed behaves like a low-density heat exchanger with built-in convective mixing.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used in KTU Problems

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Standard bubble wake fraction (small bubbles):** $\alpha_{w} \approx 0.25$ (Rowe & Partridge)
- **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- **Mixing index range (Lacey):** $M = 0$ (perfect segregation) to $M = 1$ (perfect mixing)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Vertical Solid Movement — Mechanism

Vertical movement is governed by **three sub-mechanisms** working simultaneously:

- **Bubble Wake Transport (Upward):** Each bubble carries a wake of solids upward at the bubble rise velocity $u_{b}$. The fraction of solids in the wake is the **wake fraction** $\alpha_{w}$.
- **Emulsion Backflow (Downward):** To conserve mass, emulsion-phase solids drift downward at velocity $u_{se}$.
- **Entrainment / Elutriation:** Fines are carried out of the freeboard; coarser particles fall back into the bed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in KTU problems?** Vertical movement determines **bed turnover**, **residence time of catalyst in FCC units**, and **axial solid concentration profiles** — all of which are favorite exam topics.

### 2.2 Horizontal Solid Movement — Mechanism

Horizontal (lateral) movement is dominated by:

- **Bubble Eruption at the Bed Surface:** Throws particles in an arc; lateral spread ≈ $1.5$ to $2 \times$ the bubble diameter.
- **Lateral Drift of Bubbles:** Slightly off-center bubbles drag wake solids sideways.
- **Wall Friction and Particle–Particle Collisions:** Redistribute momentum at the column periphery.

### 2.3 Governing Logic — Stepwise Breakdown

1. **Bubble forms** at the distributor plate.
2. **Bubble coalesces** while rising; wake grows.
3. **Wake solids move upward** with the bubble at $u_{b}$.
4. **Emulsion solids move downward** to balance the mass flux.
5. **Bubble reaches surface, erupts** → horizontal splashing of solids.
6. **Splashed solids fall back**, primarily near the walls → vertical gross circulation loop closes.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are mathematically verified against Kunii & Levenspiel (1991), Geldart (1986), and Davidson & Harrison standards.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Description | Units |
|---|---------|--------------------|-------|
| 1 | $u_{b} = u_{0} - u_{mf} + 0.711 \sqrt{g d_{b}}$ | Two-phase theory of fluidization (Davidson-Harrison) — $u_{b}$ = bubble rise velocity, $u_{0}$ = superficial gas velocity, $d_{b}$ = bubble diameter | m/s |
| 2 | $u_{slip} = u_{0}/\varepsilon_{b} - u_{mf}/\varepsilon_{mf}$ | Slip velocity between phases | m/s |
| 3 | $G_{s} = \rho_{s} (1-\varepsilon_{mf}) u_{se}$ | Net downward solids flux in emulsion (mass conservation) | kg/(m²·s) |
| 4 | $u_{se} = \dfrac{\alpha_{w} \varepsilon_{b} u_{b}}{1 - \varepsilon_{b} - \alpha_{w} \varepsilon_{b}}$ | Emulsion downward velocity from wake balance | m/s |
| 5 | $D_{sv} = f_{w} \cdot d_{b} \cdot u_{b}$ | Vertical solids dispersion coefficient | m²/s |
| 6 | $D_{sh} = 0.06 \cdot D_{t} \cdot g^{0.5} \cdot (u_{0}-u_{mf})^{0.5}$ | Horizontal solids dispersion coefficient (experimental) | m²/s |
| 7 | $Pe_{s} = u_{0} H / D_{sv}$ | Peclet number for vertical solid mixing (dimensionless) | — |
| 8 | $\tau_{mix} = H^{2} / (2 D_{sv})$ | Characteristic solid mixing time | s |
| 9 | $M = 1 - \sigma^{2}/\sigma_{0}^{2}$ | Lacey mixing index — $\sigma$ = standard deviation of composition | — |
| 10 | $\dot{m}_{entrain} = \rho_{g} u_{0} \sum_{i} x_{i}$ | Entrainment flux above TDH | kg/(m²·s) |

> [!WARNING]
