> **Table Symbol Pitfall:** Notice that absolute values use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ notation only — **never** use the pipe symbol `|` directly inside markdown tables, as it breaks column rendering.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of Solid Movement |
|----------|-------------------------------|
| Petroleum Refining (FCC) | Catalyst circulation between riser and regenerator |
| Mineral Processing | Roasting of sulfide ores in fluidized beds |
| Pharmaceutical | Fluid bed drying / granulation |
| Coal Combustion | CFB boiler solid inventory management |
| Polymer Industry | Solid-state polymerization |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Emulsion Downward Velocity $u_{se}$ (Wake-Balance Approach)

### Step 1 — Define the System
Consider a fluidized bed of cross-section $A$ at steady state. Mass conservation on the solid phase gives:

$$
\text{Upward solids flux} = \text{Downward solids flux}
$$

### Step 2 — Identify Upward Flux Carried by Bubble Wake
Each bubble carries a wake fraction $\alpha_{w}$ of solids. The volumetric flow of wake solids per unit area is:

$$
j_{w} = \alpha_{w} \cdot \varepsilon_{b} \cdot u_{b}
$$

where $\varepsilon_{b}$ is the bubble hold-up (fraction of bed volume occupied by bubbles).

### Step 3 — Identify Downward Flux in the Emulsion Phase
The emulsion phase occupies a fraction $(1 - \varepsilon_{b} - \alpha_{w} \varepsilon_{b})$ of the bed. Solids in the emulsion move downward at velocity $u_{se}$:

$$
j_{se} = (1 - \varepsilon_{b})(1-\varepsilon_{mf}) \cdot u_{se}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Mass Balance
Setting upward wake flux equal to downward emulsion flux:

$$
\alpha_{w} \cdot \varepsilon_{b} \cdot u_{b} = (1 - \varepsilon_{b} - \alpha_{w} \varepsilon_{b})(1-\varepsilon_{mf}) u_{se}
$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $u_{se}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{se} &= \frac{\alpha_{w} \cdot \varepsilon_{b} \cdot u_{b}}{(1 - \varepsilon_{b} - \alpha_{w} \varepsilon_{b})(1-\varepsilon_{mf})}
\end{aligned}
$$

For dilute bubbling beds where $\varepsilon_{b}$ is small and $\varepsilon_{mf} \approx 0.5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{se} &\approx \frac{\alpha_{w} \cdot \varepsilon_{b} \cdot u_{b}}{1 - \varepsilon_{b}(1 + \alpha_{w})}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation
This equation shows that **emulsion downward velocity scales linearly with bubble velocity and wake fraction**. Hence, doubling the gas velocity (and thus $u_{b}$) approximately doubles the gross solid circulation rate.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Mixing Time $\tau_{mix}$ from Fick's Law

### Step 1 — Start with the 1-D Diffusion Equation
For solid dispersion in the vertical direction:

$$
\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D_{sv} \frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial z^{2}}
$$

### Step 2 — Assume an Initial Spike of Tracer at $z=0$
Initial condition: $C(z,0) = M_{0} \delta(z)$ (Dirac delta function of total mass $M_{0}$).

### Step 3 — Apply Standard Solution (Error Function Form)
The solution for diffusion from a point source in a 1-D semi-infinite medium is:

$$
C(z,t) = \frac{M_{0}}{2 \sqrt{\pi D_{sv} t}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{z^{2}}{4 D_{sv} t}\right)
$$

### Step 4 — Define Mixing Time as the Time for the Tracer to Spread Over Bed Height $H$
We require the characteristic diffusion length $\sqrt{2 D_{sv} t}$ to equal the bed height $H$:

$$
H = \sqrt{2 D_{sv} \tau_{mix}}
$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $\tau_{mix}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{mix} &= \frac{H^{2}}{2 D_{sv}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Substitute the Vertical Dispersion Formula
Using $D_{sv} = f_{w} d_{b} u_{b}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{mix} &= \frac{H^{2}}{2 f_{w} d_{b} u_{b}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Typical KTU Numerical

**Problem:** A fluidized bed operates at $u_{0} = 0.45 \text{ m/s}$, $u_{mf} = 0.10 \text{ m/s}$, $d_{b} = 0.04 \text{ m}$. Compute (a) bubble velocity $u_{b}$, (b) emulsion downflow $u_{se}$ if $\varepsilon_{b}=0.30$ and $\alpha_{w}=0.25$.

### Part (a) — Bubble Rise Velocity
$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{b} &= u_{0} - u_{mf} + 0.711 \sqrt{g d_{b}} \\
&= 0.45 - 0.10 + 0.711 \sqrt{9.81 \times 0.04} \\
&= 0.35 + 0.711 \times \sqrt{0.3924} \\
&= 0.35 + 0.711 \times 0.6264 \\
&= 0.35 + 0.4454 \\
&= 0.7954 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Part (b) — Emulsion Downflow Velocity
$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{se} &= \frac{\alpha_{w} \varepsilon_{b} u_{b}}{1 - \varepsilon_{b} - \alpha_{w} \varepsilon_{b}} \\
&= \frac{0.25 \times 0.30 \times 0.7954}{1 - 0.30 - 0.25 \times 0.30} \\
&= \frac{0.05966}{1 - 0.30 - 0.075} \\
&= \frac{0.05966}{0.625} \\
&= 0.0955 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Solids drift downward in the emulsion at approximately **$0.0955$ m/s**, confirming the gross circulation loop.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Solid Movement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Gas Inlet via Distributor] --> B[Bubble Formation Zone]
    B --> C{Bubble Phase Engine}
    C -->|Wake Captures Solids| D[Upward Wake Transport ub]
    C -->|Erupts at Surface| E[Lateral Splash Zone]
    D --> F[Splashed Solids Thrown Horizontally]
    F --> G[Solids Fall Back Along Walls]
    G --> H[Emulsion Phase Downflow use]
    H --> I[Mass Balance Closure]
    I --> C
    E --> J[Horizontal Dispersion Dsh]
    J --> K[Radial Mixing Zone]
    K --> L[Uniform Bed Composition]

    style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style H fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style L fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Yellow block (A):** Gas entry.
- **Blue block (C):** Bubble phase acts as the **mixing engine**.
- **Green block (D):** Vertical wake-driven transport.
- **Orange block (E):** Eruption induces **horizontal movement**.
- **Purple block (H):** Emulsion downflow closes the **vertical gross circulation loop**.
- **Gold block (L):** End state — uniform bed mixing achieved.

## 4.2 Vertical vs Horizontal Movement — Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[Bubble Wake] -->|Upward Transport| V2[Freeboard Throw]
    V2 -->|Particles Fall Back| V3[Emulsion Downflow]
    V3 -->|Mass Conservation| V1

    H1[Bubble Eruption] -->|Lateral Spread| H2[Wall Recirculation]
    H2 -->|Radial Drift| H3[Particle Redistribution]
    H3 -->|Mixing Index M rises| H1

    style V1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style H1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style V3 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style H3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Engineering Insight:**
- **Left loop (green/purple):** Describes the **vertical gross circulation** — closed by mass balance.
- **Right loop (orange/blue):** Describes the **horizontal recirculation** — closed by particle–particle and wall collisions.
- The two loops share the **bubble eruption event** as their common driver.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q:** Define the term **wake fraction** in fluidization engineering and state its typical value for fine powders.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **wake fraction** $\alpha_{w}$ is defined as the ratio of the volume of solids carried in the wake of a rising bubble to the volume of the bubble itself.

$$
\alpha_{w} = \frac{\text{Volume of wake solids}}{\text{Volume of bubble}}
$$

For typical Geldart Group A powders (fine, easily fluidizable), $\alpha_{w} \approx 0.25$ (Rowe & Partridge). For larger Geldart Group B particles, the wake fraction can rise to **0.4–0.6** because larger bubbles entrain more wake material.

**[1 mark: definition | 1 mark: equation | 1 mark: typical value]**

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q:** Distinguish between **gross solid circulation** and **local particle mixing** in a fluidized bed.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Gross Solid Circulation | Local Particle Mixing |
|---------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Scale | Bed-wide (meters) | Particle-level (mm–cm) |
| Driver | Bubble-induced bulk transport | Diffusion + collisions |
| Time scale | Seconds to minutes | Fraction of a second |
| Visualization | Tracer displacement curves | High-speed video tracking |
| Mechanism | Wake + downflow loop | Random walk + drift |

**[3 marks — complete table receives full credit]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### ⭐ QUESTION A — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**Q:** A fluidized bed catalytic cracker (FCC) unit operates with the following data:

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Bed diameter $D_{t}$ | 1.5 m |
| Bed height $H$ | 4.0 m |
| Superficial gas velocity $u_{0}$ | 0.50 m/s |
| Minimum fluidization velocity $u_{mf}$ | 0.08 m/s |
| Mean bubble diameter $d_{b}$ | 0.06 m |
| Wake fraction $\alpha_{w}$ | 0.30 |
| Bubble hold-up $\varepsilon_{b}$ | 0.25 |

**Solve the following:**
**(a)** Calculate the bubble rise velocity using the Davidson–Harrison two-phase theory. **[7 marks]**
**(b)** Compute the emulsion downward velocity and the gross solid circulation rate, then comment on whether the bed will achieve uniform mixing within 60 seconds. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Solution A:

**Part (a) — Bubble Rise Velocity (7 marks)**

Applying the Davidson–Harrison equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{b} &= u_{0} - u_{mf} + 0.711 \sqrt{g \, d_{b}} \\
&= 0.50 - 0.08 + 0.711 \sqrt{9.81 \times 0.06} \\
&= 0.42 + 0.711 \times \sqrt{0.5886} \\
&= 0.42 + 0.711 \times 0.7672 \\
&= 0.42 + 0.5455 \\
&= 0.9655 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**[Full 7 marks: 1 mark substitution, 3 marks arithmetic, 1 mark final answer, 2 marks correct interpretation]**

**Part (b) — Emulsion Velocity and Mixing Time (7 marks)**

Step 1 — Emulsion Downflow Velocity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{se} &= \frac{\alpha_{w} \varepsilon_{b} u_{b}}{1 - \varepsilon_{b} - \alpha_{w} \varepsilon_{b}} \\
&= \frac{0.30 \times 0.25 \times 0.9655}{1 - 0.25 - (0.30 \times 0.25)} \\
&= \frac{0.07241}{1 - 0.25 - 0.075} \\
&= \frac{0.07241}{0.675} \\
&= 0.1073 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Vertical Solids Dispersion Coefficient (using $f_{w} \approx 0.5$ empirical):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{sv} &= f_{w} \cdot d_{b} \cdot u_{b} \\
&= 0.5 \times 0.06 \times 0.9655 \\
&= 0.02897 \text{ m}^{2}/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Mixing Time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{mix} &= \frac{H^{2}}{2 D_{sv}} \\
&= \frac{(4.0)^{2}}{2 \times 0.02897} \\
&= \frac{16.0}{0.05794} \\
&= 276.1 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Comparison with 60-second criterion:

Since $\tau_{mix} = 276$ s $\gg$ 60 s, the bed will **NOT** achieve uniform mixing within 60 seconds. The gross solid circulation will only redistribute solids partially.

**[Final marks: 2 marks for emulsion velocity, 3 marks for dispersion + mixing time, 2 marks for engineering comment]**

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Q:** With a neat sketch and derivation, explain the **bubble-induced horizontal solid movement** in a fluidized bed of diameter $D_{t} = 1.2$ m. Use the experimentally observed correlation:

$$
D_{sh} = 0.06 \, D_{t} \, g^{0.5} \, (u_{0} - u_{mf})^{0.5}
$$

**(a)** Derive the expression for horizontal solid dispersion $D_{sh}$ from first principles of **bubble eruption theory**. **[7 marks]**
**(b)** A bed operates at $u_{0} = 0.40$ m/s, $u_{mf} = 0.10$ m/s. Calculate $D_{sh}$, the horizontal Peclet number $Pe_{h} = u_{0} D_{t} / D_{sh}$, and interpret the result. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Solution B:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** At bed surface, a bubble of diameter $d_{b}$ erupts and throws particles in a parabolic arc. The lateral throw distance $L_{h}$ is approximately:

$$
L_{h} \approx 1.5 \, d_{b}
$$

**Step 2:** The horizontal velocity imparted to a particle is on the order of the bubble's vertical velocity at the moment of eruption:

$$
v_{h} \approx 0.5 \, u_{b}
$$

**Step 3:** The horizontal dispersion coefficient scales as (length × velocity):

$$
D_{sh} \propto d_{b} \cdot u_{b}
$$

**Step 4:** Using the Davidson–Harrison result $u_{b} \propto \sqrt{g d_{b}}$ and $d_{b} \propto (u_{0} - u_{mf})^{0.5} D_{t}^{0.5}$ (Mori–Wen correlation), substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{sh} &\propto \sqrt{g} \cdot (u_{0}-u_{mf})^{0.5} \cdot D_{t}^{0.5} \cdot (u_{0}-u_{mf})^{0.5} \cdot D_{t}^{0.5} \\
&= g^{0.5} \cdot (u_{0}-u_{mf}) \cdot D_{t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Empirical fit to experimental data gives the proportionality constant as **0.06**, yielding:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{sh} = 0.06 \, D_{t} \, g^{0.5} \, (u_{0}-u_{mf})^{0.5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

Step 1 — Calculate $(u_{0} - u_{mf})$:

$$
u_{0} - u_{mf} = 0.40 - 0.10 = 0.30 \text{ m/s}
$$

Step 2 — Substitute into $D_{sh}$ formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{sh} &= 0.06 \times 1.2 \times (9.81)^{0.5} \times (0.30)^{0.5} \\
&= 0.06 \times 1.2 \times 3.132 \times 0.5477 \\
&= 0.1235 \text{ m}^{2}/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Horizontal Peclet number:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Pe_{h} &= \frac{u_{0} \cdot D_{t}}{D_{sh}} \\
&= \frac{0.40 \times 1.2}{0.1235} \\
&= \frac{0.48}{0.1235} \\
&= 3.89
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Interpretation:

Since $Pe_{h} \approx 3.89$ (of order unity), the bed is in the **intermediate mixing regime** — neither plug-flow nor perfectly mixed. The horizontal mixing is significant but not dominant. This is typical of industrial-scale FCC regenerators where additional internals (baffles, cyclones) are needed for finer radial uniformity.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** — Every numerical answer MUST carry units. A "0.9655" without m/s loses **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Sign error on $u_{b}$ formula** — Students often write $u_{mf} - u_{0}$ instead of $u_{0} - u_{mf}$ — loses **2 marks**.
> 3. **Wake fraction missing in $u_{se}$ numerator** — A very common mistake; the formula is **NOT** just $\varepsilon_{b} u_{b} / (1 - \varepsilon_{b})$.
> 4. **Mixing time definition confusion** — Mixing time ≠ residence time. Mixing time is for **bed uniformity**, residence time is for **particle throughput**.
> 5. **Confusing $D_{sv}$ and $D_{sh}$** — Vertical dispersion $D_{sv}$ uses $d_{b}$ and $u_{b}$; horizontal dispersion $D_{sh}$ uses $D_{t}$ and $g$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print & Memorize Before Exam)**

- **Wake fraction $\alpha_{w}$:** Typical value **0.25** for Geldart A; can rise to **0.6** for Geldart B.
- **Davidson–Harrison bubble velocity:** $u_{b} = u_{0} - u_{mf} + 0.711 \sqrt{g d_{b}}$ — **must include all 3 terms**.
- **Emulsion downflow velocity:** $u_{se} = \dfrac{\alpha_{w} \varepsilon_{b} u_{b}}{1 - \varepsilon_{b} - \alpha_{w} \varepsilon_{b}}$ — **wake fraction in both numerator AND denominator correction term**.
- **Vertical dispersion:** $D_{sv} = f_{w} d_{b} u_{b}$ — order of magnitude $10^{-2}$ m²/s.
- **Horizontal dispersion:** $D_{sh} = 0.06 D_{t} g^{0.5} (u_{0} - u_{mf})^{0.5}$ — depends on **bed diameter**, not bubble size.
- **Mixing time:** $\tau_{mix} = H^{2} / (2 D_{sv})$ — for industrial beds typically **100–500 seconds**.
- **Gross circulation loop:** Wake UP → Eruption → Splash DOWN at walls → Emulsion DOWN → back to distributor.
- **Horizontal splash distance:** $L_{h} \approx 1.5 d_{b}$.
- **Mixing index (Lacey):** $M = 0$ (segregated) to $M = 1$ (perfectly mixed) — used in **batch mixing experiments**.
- **Bubble is the engine:** Without bubbles → no gross circulation → only diffusion (very slow).
- **FCC relevance:** Catalyst spends ~30 s in the bed before being carried out; gross circulation rate determines **catalyst activity profile**.
- **Industrial units:** Always check if bed is Geldart A or B; wake fraction differs significantly.
- **Freeboard & TDH:** Above TDH (Transport Disengaging Height), entrainment flux decays exponentially with height.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Segregation of Solid Particles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Segregation of Solid Particles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Segregation of Solid Particles** in the context of fluidization engineering is defined as the **non-uniform spatial distribution** of particles of differing physical properties (size, density, or shape) within a fluidized bed. When a polydisperse particle mixture is fluidized, particles spontaneously stratify into distinct layers along the vertical axis of the bed due to hydrodynamic and gravitational forces.

The phenomenon of segregation arises whenever the particle population in a fluidized bed is **not perfectly monodisperse**. The bed deviates from ideal homogeneous fluidization, and two distinct classifications of particles emerge based on their preferential vertical position:

> [!NOTE]
> **Flotsam Particles (Floating Fraction):**
> These are particles that tend to **migrate upward** and accumulate in the upper regions (freeboard / splash zone) of the fluidized bed. Flotsam typically consists of **smaller, lighter, or lower-density** particles. They have a lower minimum fluidization velocity ($U_{mf}$) than jetsam.

> [!NOTE]
> **Jetsam Particles (Sinking Fraction):**
> These are particles that tend to **sink downward** and accumulate in the lower regions (distributor zone) of the fluidized bed. Jetsam typically consists of **larger, heavier, or higher-density** particles. They have a higher minimum fluidization velocity ($U_{mf}$) than flotsam.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you have a large plastic jar half-filled with a mixture of **sand (fine, dense)** and **unpopped popcorn kernels (large, light)**. If you shake the jar vigorously, you will observe a clear **stratification**:

- The **heavy sand particles** percolate through the gaps between the popcorn kernels and settle at the **bottom**.
- The **light popcorn kernels** are pushed upward by the sand and float to the **top**.

This is exactly analogous to what happens inside a fluidized bed: the gas (acting like the shaking motion) provides the kinetic energy, and the particles segregate based on their density and size. The dense/large particles (jetsam) sink to the distributor region, while the fine/light particles (flotsam) rise to the freeboard region — and may even be **elutriated** (blown out) from the top of the bed if the gas velocity is high enough.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Nomenclature Reminder:**
> - $U_{mf}$ = Minimum fluidization velocity (m/s)
> - $U_t$ = Terminal velocity of a single particle (m/s)
> - $d_p$ = Particle diameter (m)
> - $\rho_p$ = Particle density (kg/m³)
> - $\rho_f$ = Fluid density (kg/m³)
> - $H$, $H_{eq}$ = Bed height, Equilibrium bed height (m)
> - $x$, $x_A$ = Local mass fraction, Mass fraction of species A
> - $w$, $w^*$ = Segregation-mixing flux (kg/m²·s), Equilibrium flux

### 1.3 Physical Significance in Industrial Processes

| Industrial Context | Consequence of Segregation |
|---|---|
| **Coal Combustion** | Larger coal pieces (jetsam) sink to the grate, fines (flotsam) blow out as unburnt carbon loss |
| **Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** | Catalyst segregation affects conversion uniformity in the riser/regenerator |
| **Drying of Polydisperse Solids** | Wet coarse particles stay wet at bottom, dry fine particles at top → non-uniform drying |
| **Mineral Processing** | Ore beneficiation depends on controlled segregation of valuable/heavy minerals |
| **Granulation / Coating** | Segregation leads to poor product homogeneity and off-spec material |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Mechanisms of Segregation

Segregation in a fluidized bed does not occur by a single mechanism. KTU examiners frequently test the **three primary mechanisms**, and you must know each one:

1. **Percolation Mechanism (Interstitial Sifting):**
   - When gas flows upward through the bed, it creates voidage fluctuations and rearranges particles.
   - **Smaller particles physically slip through the interstitial gaps** between larger particles under the influence of gravity.
   - This is the dominant mechanism when the size ratio $d_{p,\text{large}} / d_{p,\text{small}} > 1.5$.

2. **Elutriation Mechanism (Entrainment):**
   - When the superficial gas velocity $U$ exceeds the **terminal velocity** $U_t$ of a particle species, those particles are carried out of the bed top by the gas stream.
   - This mechanism preferentially removes **fine, light flotsam particles** and is the major cause of particle loss and dust emissions.

3. **Rolling / Bubbling Mechanism:**
   - As bubbles rise through the bed, they drag particles upward in their wake.
   - When bubbles burst at the bed surface, particles are thrown laterally and then **roll back down** the bubble-free regions (the emulsion phase).
   - The size/density of particles being carried by bubbles vs. those that roll down determines the segregation pattern.

### 2.2 Factors Affecting Segregation — A Structured Hierarchy

- **Particle Size Ratio** ($\bar{d}_p = d_{p,\text{large}} / d_{p,\text{small}}$):
  - For $\bar{d}_p > \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$, particles are geometrically capable of segregation.
  - For $\bar{d}_p < \sqrt{2}$, particles are "geometrically locked" and segregation is minimal.

- **Density Ratio** ($\bar{\rho}_p = \rho_{p,\text{heavy}} / \rho_{p,\text{light}}$):
  - Higher density ratio intensifies segregation, even if particle sizes are similar.

- **Gas Velocity** ($U / U_{mf}$):
  - **Low velocities ($U \approx U_{mf}$):** Bubbling is weak, mixing is poor, segregation is **maximized**.
  - **High velocities ($U \gg U_{mf}$):** Vigorous bubbling promotes mixing, segregation is reduced (but elutriation increases).

- **Bed Geometry:** High L/D ratios (tall beds) have more vertical distance for stratification. Shallow beds approach well-mixed behavior.

- **Distributor Design:** Poor distributors create dead zones and preferential flow, worsening segregation.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **mandatory memorize-and-reproduce** set of formulas for the KTU ESE on this topic. Every symbol and its unit are explicitly listed.

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Variable Definitions & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Perfect Mixing Index (Lacey Index) | $M = \dfrac{S_0^2 - S^2}{S_0^2 - S_R^2}$ | $S$ = actual variance, $S_0^2$ = fully segregated variance, $S_R^2$ = random (perfectly mixed) variance. $M = 1$ → perfect mixing; $M = 0$ → perfect segregation. |
| 2 | Local Segregation Intensity | $\Psi(x) = \dfrac{x_A - x_{A,\text{mean}}}{x_{A,\text{mean}}(1 - x_{A,\text{mean}})}$ | $x_A$ = local mass fraction, $x_{A,\text{mean}}$ = bulk mass fraction |
| 3 | Mixing Flux (Fickian) | $J_{\text{mix}} = - D_s \dfrac{\partial \rho_s}{\partial z}$ | $D_s$ = solid dispersion coefficient (m²/s), $\rho_s$ = solid concentration (kg/m³) |
| 4 | Segregation Flux | $J_{\text{seg}} = K \cdot \rho_s \cdot x_A (1 - x_A)$ | $K$ = segregation rate constant (m/s); typically $K = K_0 \exp(-U/U_{mf})$ |
| 5 | Net Downward Solid Flux | $w = w_{\text{seg}} - w_{\text{mix}}$ | $w$ = net segregation flux (kg/m²·s) |
| 6 | Equilibrium Bed Height (Bin Model) | $H_{eq} = \dfrac{H_0 (1 - x_B^{\text{jetsam}})}{(1 - f_j^{\text{top}})}$ | $H_0$ = initial bed height, $f_j^{\text{top}}$ = jetsam fraction at top |
| 7 | Particle Size Ratio Criterion | $\bar{d}_p = \dfrac{d_{p,L}}{d_{p,S}} > \sqrt{2}$ | Geometric sifting threshold (Rowe & Nienow criterion) |
| 8 | Archimedes Number (size segregation) | $Ar = \dfrac{g d_p^3 \rho_f (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{\mu^2}$ | Dimensionless group; $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $\mu$ = fluid viscosity (Pa·s) |
| 9 | Reynolds Number at Minimum Fluidization | $Re_{mf} = \dfrac{d_p U_{mf} \rho_f}{\mu}$ | Used in Ergun equation for $U_{mf}$ |
| 10 | Standard Deviation (Segregation Quality) | $S = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | Variance-based measure of composition uniformity |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The **mixing index $M$** (also called the Lacey index) is the **single most-used diagnostic tool** in industrial fluidized bed design. Process engineers compute $M$ at multiple axial positions to:
- Validate Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations of fluidized beds.
- Optimize distributor plate hole patterns.
- Decide between mechanical mixing aids (internals, stirrers) and process changes (lower $U$, narrower PSD).
- Quantify product quality in continuous operations like drying and granulation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Two-Phase Model for Equilibrium Segregation

The classical two-phase model treats the fluidized bed as two interpenetrating phases: the **bubble phase** (gas-rich, particle-lean) and the **emulsion phase** (gas + dense particulates). In the limit of a fully segregated steady state, the downward flux of jetsam particles equals the upward mixing flux at every axial position.

**Starting point — Solid species balance for jetsam species A in the emulsion phase:**

Consider a horizontal slice of bed of thickness $dz$ at height $z$ above the distributor. The mass of jetsam A entering from above (mixing flux) and leaving below (segregation flux) must balance at steady state:

$$
\frac{d w}{dz} = 0
$$

**Step 1 — Express the mixing flux (Fickian diffusion analogy):**

$$
J_{\text{mix}} = -D_s \frac{d(\rho_s x_A)}{dz}
$$

*Explanation:* The mixing flux is driven by concentration gradients; $D_s$ is the solid dispersion coefficient. The negative sign indicates flux flows from high to low concentration.

**Step 2 — Express the segregation flux (driven by gravity, proportional to local composition):**

$$
J_{\text{seg}} = K \, \rho_s \, x_A \, (1 - x_A)
$$

*Explanation:* The segregation flux depends on the product $x_A (1 - x_A)$. This is the **Gaudin–Kapur–Fuerstenau form**, which captures the fact that segregation is zero when the bed is pure ($x_A = 0$ or $x_A = 1$) and maximal at $x_A = 0.5$.

**Step 3 — Write the net flux balance:**

$$
w = J_{\text{seg}} - J_{\text{mix}} = K \rho_s x_A (1 - x_A) + D_s \frac{d(\rho_s x_A)}{dz}
$$

**Step 4 — At equilibrium (steady state), the net flux $w = 0$ at every height:**

$$
0 = K \rho_s x_A (1 - x_A) + D_s \rho_s \frac{d x_A}{dz}
$$

*Explanation:* Assuming $\rho_s$ (bulk solid concentration) is approximately uniform across the bed height in the dense phase, we can divide both sides by $\rho_s$.

**Step 5 — Separate variables and integrate:**

$$
\frac{d x_A}{x_A (1 - x_A)} = - \frac{K}{D_s} \, dz
$$

*Explanation:* This is a separable ODE. The LHS uses the standard partial fraction identity.

**Step 6 — Apply partial fraction decomposition to LHS:**

$$
\frac{1}{x_A (1 - x_A)} = \frac{1}{x_A} + \frac{1}{1 - x_A}
$$

So the integral becomes:

$$
\int \left( \frac{1}{x_A} + \frac{1}{1 - x_A} \right) dx_A = - \frac{K}{D_s} \int dz
$$

**Step 7 — Perform the integration:**

$$
\ln(x_A) - \ln(1 - x_A) = - \frac{K}{D_s} z + C
$$

*Explanation:* The integration constant $C$ is determined by boundary conditions.

**Step 8 — Combine logarithms and solve for $x_A(z)$:**

$$
\ln \left( \frac{x_A}{1 - x_A} \right) = - \frac{K}{D_s} z + C
$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$
\frac{x_A}{1 - x_A} = A_0 \exp\left(- \frac{K}{D_s} z\right)
$$

where $A_0 = e^C$ is determined from the boundary condition at $z = 0$ (jetsam mass fraction $x_{A,0}$).

**Step 9 — Final equilibrium composition profile (KTU standard result):**

$$
x_A(z) = \frac{x_{A,0} \exp(-K z / D_s)}{1 + x_{A,0} \left[ \exp(-K z / D_s) - 1 \right]}
$$

*Explanation:* This equation gives the **vertical concentration profile** of jetsam particles in a fluidized bed at steady state. As $z$ increases, $x_A(z)$ decreases exponentially, confirming that jetsam concentrates at the bottom and flotsam at the top — a quantitative match to the qualitative picture.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Conditions and Resulting Segregation State

| Condition | Gas Velocity Regime | Dominant Mechanism | Segregation State |
|---|---|---|---|
| $U < U_{mf,\text{jetsam}}$ | Below jetsam fluidization | Static bed, no mixing | Complete segregation (static layers) |
| $U_{mf,\text{jetsam}} < U < U_{mf,\text{flotsam}}$ | Jetsam fluidized, flotsam static | Bubbling + percolation | Partial segregation, jetsam mixed, flotsam frozen |
| $U_{mf,\text{flotsam}} < U < U_{t,\text{flotsam}}$ | Both fluidized | All three mechanisms active | Equilibrium exponential profile (Eq. 9 above) |
| $U > U_{t,\text{flotsam}}$ | Flotsam elutriated | Elutriation dominant | Steady loss of flotsam, jetsam saturates bed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Insight:** The ratio $K/D_s$ is a **dimensionless segregation length** — its reciprocal tells you the characteristic height over which the jetsam concentration drops by a factor of $e$. This is the most important parameter extracted from experimental mixing data in KTU laboratory sessions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Segregation Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polydisperse Particle Bed]
    B[Gas Inlet at Distributor]
    C[Fluidization Onset]
    D[Bubble Formation in Emulsion]
    E[Three Parallel Mechanisms]
    F[Percolation Sifting]
    G[Elutriation by Gas Drag]
    H[Rolling via Bubble Wakes]
    I[Steady State Equilibrium]
    J[Vertical Concentration Profile]
    K[Jetsam Enriched Bottom Layer]
    L[Flotsam Enriched Top Layer]
    M[Fine Particles Lost to Freeboard]
    N[Mixing Index M Computed]

    A --> C
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    E --> G
    E --> H
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J
    J --> K
    J --> L
    G --> M
    J --> N
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Decision Logic for Segregation Severity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Input: Particle System]
    Q[Compute Size Ratio dpRatio]
    R{dpRatio greater than sqrt 2?}
    S[Compute Density Ratio rhoRatio]
    T{rhoRatio greater than 1.5?}
    U[Compute Gas Velocity U over Umf]
    V{Is U between Umfjetsam and Umfflotsam?}
    W[SEVERE SEGREGATION]
    X[MODERATE SEGREGATION]
    Y[MILD SEGREGATION]
    Z[Measure Lacey Index M]
    AA{Is M less than 0.8?}

    P --> Q
    Q --> R
    R -- Yes --> S
    R -- No --> X
    S --> T
    T -- Yes --> U
    T -- No --> Y
    U --> V
    V -- Yes --> W
    V -- No --> Y
    W --> Z
    X --> Z
    Y --> Z
    Z --> AA
    AA -- Yes --> W
    AA -- No --> Y
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Feedback Loop of Segregation Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sensor: Axial Sampling Probes] --> S2[Compute Local Mass Fraction xA at height z]
    S2 --> S3[Calculate Lacey Mixing Index M]
    S3 --> S4{Is M within target band 0.85 to 1.0?}
    S4 -- No --> S5[Adjust Gas Velocity U]
    S4 -- No --> S6[Adjust Distributor Design]
    S4 -- No --> S7[Add Internal Baffles or Stirrers]
    S5 --> S8[Return to Steady State]
    S6 --> S8
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S1
    S4 -- Yes --> S9[Maintain Operating Conditions]
    S9 --> S1
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Schematic of the Fluidized Bed Segregation Zones

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Z1[Freeboard Zone - Elutriation and Dust Loss]
    Z2[Splash Zone - Bubbles Burst Particles Here]
    Z3[Main Fluidized Bed - Segregation Profile Active]
    Z4[Distributor Zone - Jetsam Enriched]
    Z5[Windbox and Plenum - Gas Distribution]

    Z5 --> Z4
    Z4 --> Z3
    Z3 --> Z2
    Z2 --> Z1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic:** Zone Z3 is where the exponential equilibrium segregation profile (derived in Section 3, Step 9) applies. Zone Z4 has jetsam mass fraction approaching unity ($x_A \to 1$). Zone Z1 is where elutriated flotsam particles leave the system and are recovered in cyclones (in industrial practice).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Differentiate between **flotsam** and **jetsam** particles in a fluidized bed with a binary solid mixture. State the typical range of gas velocity in which flotsam is preferentially elutriated from a fluidized bed.

**Model Answer:**

Flotsam and jetsam are classifications based on the spatial location of particles in a segregating fluidized bed:

| Property | Flotsam | Jetsam |
|---|---|---|
| Position | Top of bed (freeboard) | Bottom of bed (distributor) |
| Size | Smaller | Larger |
| Density | Lighter | Heavier |
| Minimum fluidization velocity $U_{mf}$ | Lower | Higher |
| Terminal velocity $U_t$ | Lower | Higher |

Flotsam is preferentially elutriated when $U > U_{t,\text{flotsam}}$, i.e., when the superficial gas velocity exceeds the terminal velocity of the flotsam species. This is the regime of operation in circulating fluidized beds (CFBs) used in FCC and coal combustion applications.

#### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
State the criterion (in terms of particle size ratio) for geometric sifting and segregation to occur in a fluidized bed.

**Model Answer:**

According to the **Rowe–Nienow criterion**, geometric sifting and segregation become significant when the ratio of large to small particle diameters exceeds the square root of two:

$$
\bar{d}_p = \frac{d_{p,\text{large}}}{d_{p,\text{small}}} > \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414
$$

If $\bar{d}_p < \sqrt{2}$, the particles are **geometrically locked** and cannot segregate effectively because the smaller particles are too large to pass through the interstices of the larger particles.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A** (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **three mechanisms** by which segregation of solid particles takes place in a gas–solid fluidized bed. Highlight the role of each mechanism in determining the steady-state vertical composition profile. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A binary mixture of spherical glass beads (density $\rho_p = 2500\ \text{kg/m}^3$) is fluidized by air at ambient conditions. The coarse fraction (jetsam) has $d_{p,J} = 1.0\ \text{mm}$ and the fines (flotsam) have $d_{p,F} = 0.4\ \text{mm}$. Determine whether **geometric segregation** is expected. Given: $\rho_f = 1.2\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu_f = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$. Also compute the **terminal velocity** of the flotsam particles assuming Stokes flow and compare with a typical fluidizing velocity of $U = 0.5\ \text{m/s}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The three mechanisms of segregation are:

1. **Percolation (Interstitial Sifting):** Small particles fall under gravity through the interstices between larger particles. It is the dominant mechanism when $\bar{d}_p > \sqrt{2}$. It results in jetsam enrichment at the bottom.

2. **Elutriation (Entrainment):** When gas velocity exceeds the terminal velocity of fine particles, they are carried out of the bed top. This causes a continuous loss of flotsam from the system.

3. **Rolling / Bubble Wake Transport:** Bubbles carry particles upward in their wakes; these particles are released at the bed surface and roll down the inclined bubble-free regions. The asymmetry in the size/density of carried vs. rolling particles creates segregation.

At steady state, the three mechanisms interact to produce an exponential composition profile given by the equation derived in Section 3, Step 9.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the size ratio:**

$$
\bar{d}_p = \frac{d_{p,J}}{d_{p,F}} = \frac{1.0\ \text{mm}}{0.4\ \text{mm}} = 2.5
$$

Since $\bar{d}_p = 2.5 > \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$, **geometric segregation IS expected**.

**Step 2 — Verify flotsam is in Stokes regime:**

$$
Re = \frac{d_{p,F} \rho_f U}{\mu_f} \approx \frac{(4 \times 10^{-4})(1.2)(0.5)}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} = 13.33
$$

Since $Re \approx 13 > 1$, Stokes flow is **not strictly valid**, but we proceed as an order-of-magnitude estimate.

**Step 3 — Terminal velocity under Stokes flow:**

$$
U_{t,F} = \frac{g \, d_{p,F}^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \mu_f}
$$

$$
U_{t,F} = \frac{(9.81)(4 \times 10^{-4})^2 (2500 - 1.2)}{18 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
U_{t,F} = \frac{(9.81)(1.6 \times 10^{-7})(2498.8)}{3.24 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{3.921 \times 10^{-3}}{3.24 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 12.1\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Stokes correction (or use Schiller–Naumann drag):**

For $Re \approx 13$, the Schiller–Naumann correction factor $C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re}(1 + 0.15 Re^{0.687})$ gives a more accurate terminal velocity. The corrected value is significantly lower than the Stokes estimate. Iterating:

Assuming $Re_t \approx 2$, $C_D \approx 12.1 \times (1 + 0.15 \times 2^{0.687}) \approx 14.2$

$$
U_{t,F} = \sqrt{\frac{4 g d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{3 C_D \rho_f}} = \sqrt{\frac{4(9.81)(4\times10^{-4})(2498.8)}{3(14.2)(1.2)}} \approx 3.12\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with operating velocity $U = 0.5\ \text{m/s}$:**

Since $U = 0.5\ \text{m/s} < U_{t,F} \approx 3.12\ \text{m/s}$, the operating velocity is **below the terminal velocity of flotsam**. Therefore, flotsam particles will not be elutriated but will accumulate in the upper bed region by percolation. The system will exhibit clear vertical segregation.

---

#### **Question B** (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Define the **Lacey Mixing Index** $M$. Show that $M = 0$ corresponds to a **completely segregated** state and $M = 1$ corresponds to a **perfectly mixed** state. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fluidized bed of height $H = 0.6\ \text{m}$ is operated with a binary mixture. The equilibrium jetsam concentration profile follows:

$$
x_A(z) = \frac{0.7 \, e^{-3z}}{1 + 0.7 (e^{-3z} - 1)}
$$

Determine (i) the **jetsam concentration at the distributor** ($z = 0$), (ii) the **jetsam concentration at the bed surface** ($z = 0.6\ \text{m}$), and (iii) comment on the **segregation intensity** using the local segregation intensity function $\Psi(x) = \dfrac{x_A - \bar{x}_A}{\bar{x}_A (1 - \bar{x}_A)}$ where $\bar{x}_A = 0.5$ is the mean mass fraction. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The **Lacey Mixing Index** is defined as:

$$
M = \frac{S_0^2 - S^2}{S_0^2 - S_R^2}
$$

where:
- $S^2$ = variance of the actual composition in the sampled slices
- $S_0^2$ = variance of the fully segregated system (composition = 0 or 1 in each slice)
- $S_R^2$ = variance of the random (perfectly mixed) system

**Proof of $M = 0$ for perfect segregation:**

In a perfectly segregated bed, each slice contains only one species. Hence the variance equals the maximum possible variance $S_0^2$. Substituting:

$$
M = \frac{S_0^2 - S_0^2}{S_0^2 - S_R^2} = \frac{0}{S_0^2 - S_R^2} = 0 \quad \blacksquare
$$

**Proof of $M = 1$ for perfect mixing:**

In a perfectly mixed bed, all slices have identical composition equal to the mean. The variance $S^2 = S_R^2$. Substituting:

$$
M = \frac{S_0^2 - S_R^2}{S_0^2 - S_R^2} = 1 \quad \blacksquare
$$

Thus $0 \le M \le 1$, with $M \to 0$ indicating complete segregation and $M \to 1$ indicating perfect mixing.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Part (i) — At $z = 0$:**

$$
x_A(0) = \frac{0.7 \, e^{0}}{1 + 0.7 (e^{0} - 1)} = \frac{0.7}{1 + 0.7(0)} = \frac{0.7}{1.0} = 0.70
$$

So jetsam concentration at the distributor is **70 %**.

**Part (ii) — At $z = 0.6\ \text{m}$:**

First compute $e^{-3 \times 0.6} = e^{-1.8} \approx 0.1653$

$$
x_A(0.6) = \frac{0.7 \times 0.1653}{1 + 0.7 (0.1653 - 1)} = \frac{0.1157}{1 + 0.7(-0.8347)} = \frac{0.1157}{1 - 0.5843} = \frac{0.1157}{0.4157} \approx 0.278
$$

So jetsam concentration at the surface is approximately **27.8 %**.

**Part (iii) — Segregation intensity:**

At $z = 0$:
$$
\Psi(0) = \frac{0.70 - 0.50}{0.50 \times 0.50} = \frac{0.20}{0.25} = +0.80
$$

At $z = 0.6$:
$$
\Psi(0.6) = \frac{0.278 - 0.50}{0.25} = \frac{-0.222}{0.25} = -0.888
$$

The **antisymmetric sign** of $\Psi$ about zero confirms a strongly segregated bed — positive $\Psi$ at the bottom (jetsam enriched) and negative $\Psi$ at the top (flotsam enriched). The magnitudes $|\Psi| > 0.8$ at both extremes indicate **severe segregation** requiring process intervention (e.g., internal baffles, increased gas velocity, or narrower particle size distribution).

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU ESE on Segregation:**
>
> 1. **Confusing flotsam and jetsam definitions:** Students often swap these definitions. Memorize: **Flotsam FL**oats (top), **Jetsam** sinks to bottom (think of "jet" as in deep-sea jetsam cargo).
>
> 2. **Using the wrong fluidization velocity regime:** Segregation analysis is only valid when $U_{mf,\text{jetsam}} < U < U_{t,\text{flotsam}}$. Outside this regime, the model breaks down. Examiners deduct 2 marks if you do not state this explicitly.
>
> 3. **Forgetting units in Lacey Index calculations:** Always quote $M$ as a **dimensionless** quantity.
>
> 4. **Stokes vs. Schiller–Naumann drag:** For $Re > 1$, Stokes law overestimates terminal velocity. Examiners want to see you mention the correction.
>
> 5. **Boundary conditions in exponential profile:** In Part B(b), many students forget to verify $x_A(0) = 0.70$ — examiners use this as a quick sanity check.
>
> 6. **Sign of $\Psi$:** Positive means jetsam-rich zone, negative means flotsam-rich zone. Students often swap these signs.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Segregation of Solid Particles (Module 2):**
>
> ☐ **Flotsam** = small, light particles, rise to top.
>
> ☐ **Jetsam** = large, dense particles, sink to bottom.
>
> ☐ Three mechanisms: **Percolation, Elutriation, Rolling/Wake transport.**
>
> ☐ Geometric sifting threshold: $\bar{d}_p > \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.
>
> ☐ **Lacey Index** $M \in [0, 1]$: $M = 0$ segregated, $M = 1$ mixed.
>
> ☐ Equilibrium composition profile: $x_A(z) = \dfrac{x_{A,0} e^{-Kz/D_s}}{1 + x_{A,0}(e^{-Kz/D_s} - 1)}$.
>
> ☐ $K/D_s$ is the dimensionless segregation length scale.
>
> ☐ Gaudin–Kapur–Fuerstenau segregation flux: $J_{\text{seg}} = K \rho_s x_A (1 - x_A)$.
>
> ☐ Fickian mixing flux: $J_{\text{mix}} = - D_s \dfrac{d(\rho_s x_A)}{dz}$.
>
> ☐ Local segregation intensity: $\Psi(x) = \dfrac{x_A - \bar{x}_A}{\bar{x}_A (1 - \bar{x}_A)}$.
>
> ☐ Operating velocity must satisfy $U_{mf,\text{jetsam}} < U < U_{t,\text{flotsam}}$ for clean segregation analysis.
>
> ☐ For $Re > 1$ terminal velocity calculations, use Schiller–Naumann correction, not Stokes law.
>
> ☐ Industrial relevance: Coal combustion, FCC, drying, granulation, mineral processing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Large Solids in Beds of Smaller Particles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Large Solids in Beds of Smaller Particles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Large Solids in Beds of Smaller Particles** refers to a specialized fluidization configuration in which one or more objects whose characteristic size and/or density is significantly greater than that of the surrounding bed particles are immersed in, or continuously processed through, a fluidized bed of fine solids. The large solid may be a single discrete object (e.g., a coated tablet, a heat-exchanger tube, a catalyst support) or a stream of coarse particles (e.g., seeds, granules, coated pellets) being conveyed through a bed of much finer fluidizing solids.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this topic falls under the broad classification of **non-conventional or auxiliary fluidization operations**, and is treated as a critical industrial application because it directly links classical fluidization theory (minimum fluidization velocity, bed expansion, bubble dynamics) with **heat transfer, mass transfer, and surface modification processes**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (The River-Boulder Picture):** Imagine a shallow river flowing over a bed of fine sand. When the water level rises (analogous to increasing gas velocity $u$ above $u_{mf}$), the sand starts to "boil" and behaves like a liquid. Now drop a large, smooth boulder into this fluidized sand. The boulder either (a) sinks to the bottom if it is much denser than the sand (this is called a **Jetsam** situation), or (b) floats on top of the sand if it is much lighter (this is a **Flotsam** situation), or (c) remains suspended within the bed if its density closely matches the bulk density of the fluidized sand (this is the **Equilibrium** condition).
>
> The boulder continuously interacts with the bubbling, fluidized sand – it gets scrubbed, heated, cooled, coated, or dried by the surrounding "pseudo-liquid" of fine particles. This is exactly what happens industrially when large objects (tablets, food pieces, fertilizer prills) are treated inside a fluidized bed of fine particles (sand, glass beads, catalyst fines).

### 1.3 Classification of Large Solids

Large solids in a fluidized bed of smaller particles are classified by two physical parameters: **density difference** and **size ratio**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Classification Metrics:**
> - **Density ratio, $\rho_{L}/\rho_{S}$** = density of large solid / density of bed particles
> - **Size ratio, $d_{L}/d_{S}$** = diameter of large solid / mean diameter of bed particles
> - **Threshold values** (Kunii & Levenspiel, 1991, universally referenced): The system is considered "large solids in fine bed" when $d_{L}/d_{S} \geq 3$ **or** $\rho_{L}/\rho_{S} \geq 5$.

| Class | Density Condition | Size Condition | Behaviour in Bed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Jetsam** | $\rho_{L} > \rho_{S}$ (large solid denser) | $d_{L} > d_{S}$ | Sinks to the distributor / bottom |
| **Flotsam** | $\rho_{L} < \rho_{S}$ (large solid lighter) | $d_{L} > d_{S}$ | Floats on top of the bed surface |
| **Equilibrium** | $\rho_{L} \approx \rho_{bed,bulk}$ | $d_{L} \gg d_{S}$ | Suspended at intermediate height |
| **Equal-density** | $\rho_{L} \approx \rho_{S}$ | $d_{L} > d_{S}$ | Mixes uniformly with bed |

### 1.4 Industrial Relevance and Examples

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does industry care about this configuration?** Because it offers **simultaneous heat/mass transfer and mechanical surface treatment** in a single vessel. Major industrial applications include:
> 1. **Pharmaceutical tablet coating** – tablets (large, $d_{L} \approx 8$–$15$ mm) are tumbled in a fluidized bed of coating powder or in a bed where warm air fluidizes inert fines.
> 2. **Fertilizer prill coating** – urea prills are coated with sulphur or polymer in a fluidized bed of fine coating powder.
> 3. **Food processing** – drying of large food pieces (cubes, slices) in a fluidized bed of fine salt or sand that transfers heat rapidly.
> 4. **Heat transfer surfaces** – immersed heat-exchanger tubes act as "large solids" inside a fluidized bed of catalyst or sand.
> 5. **Roasting and calcination** – large ore lumps in a fluidized bed of fine inert heat carrier.
> 6. **Catalyst regeneration** – spent catalyst pellets are fluidized in a regenerator where they contact a bed of fine combustion-supporting solids.

The unifying engineering objective is to **maximize the contact efficiency between the large solid surface and the fluidized fine particles/gas**, while ensuring the large object is well-mixed in the bed (to prevent over-heating or non-uniform coating on one side).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physics of Interaction

When a large solid of size $d_{L}$ is placed in a fluidized bed of mean particle size $d_{S}$ at a superficial gas velocity $u$ (where $u_{mf,S} < u < u_{t,S}$, with the subscript $S$ denoting the small/fine bed particles), three simultaneous transport phenomena govern the system's behaviour:

1. **Gas–Particle Convective Transfer to the Large Solid Surface** – analogous to single-object heat/mass transfer in a fluidized suspension. The fluidized fines act as a *pseudo-fluid* whose effective thermal conductivity is far higher than that of the gas alone.
2. **Particle–Large-Solid Impact Transfer** – bubbles bursting at the surface of the large solid throw packets of hot/cold fine particles onto its surface (this is the **packet renewal** or **packet theory** mechanism, originally proposed by Mickley & Fairbanks for immersed surfaces).
3. **Mixing and Segregation of the Large Solid within the Bed** – dictated by the **density ratio** $\rho_{L}/\rho_{S}$ and the **size ratio** $d_{L}/d_{S}$.

### 2.2 The Two Operating Regimes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regime I – Dense-Phase Immersion (Bubbling Bed):** The large solid is fully immersed in the dense phase (emulsion phase) of the fluidized fines. The gas velocity is moderate: $1.1 \, u_{mf,S} < u < 3 \, u_{mf,S}$. Heat transfer coefficients are high because the large solid is continuously bathed in the emulsion of fine particles.
>
> **Regime II – Surface-Floating / Intermittent Contact (Slugging or Fast Bed):** As $u$ increases towards the terminal velocity of the fines $u_{t,S}$, the fines are entrained and the large solid is intermittently contacted by bursts of falling particles. Heat transfer in this regime is lower because the residence time of particles on the large-solid surface is shorter.

### 2.3 Segregation vs Mixing Criterion (Foscolo–Gelperin–Nack Criterion)

The most important academic question in this topic is: **Will the large solid mix with the bed, or segregate to a particular region?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Mixing Index (qualitative):** A large solid of density $\rho_{L}$ and size $d_{L}$ will **mix uniformly** with smaller particles of density $\rho_{S}$ and size $d_{S}$ if its **effective fluidization velocity equals the operating gas velocity**, i.e., if
> $$u_{mf,L} \approx u_{mf,S} \approx u$$
> If $u_{mf,L} \gg u$, the large solid acts as **jetsam** and segregates to the bottom. If $u_{mf,L} \ll u$, the large solid acts as **flotsam** and segregates to the top.

A more quantitative criterion (Rowe, Nienow, and Agbim, 1972, universally referenced in textbooks) is given by the **segregation-mixing transition velocity**:

$$u_{trans} = u_{mf,S} \cdot \left[ 1 + \left( \frac{\rho_{L} - \rho_{S}}{\rho_{S}} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{d_{L}}{d_{S}} \right)^{0.44} \right]$$

- If $u < u_{trans}$: large solid **segregates** to the bottom (jetsam behaviour).
- If $u > u_{trans}$: large solid is **mixed** in the bed.

### 2.4 Heat Transfer to a Large Immersed Solid

For a single large object immersed in a bubbling fluidized bed of fines, the **overall surface-to-bed heat transfer coefficient** $h$ is the sum of three contributions:

$$h = h_{gp,c} + h_{p,c} + h_{rad}$$

where each term has a specific physical meaning:

| Term | Physical Meaning | Dominant Regime |
|---|---|---|
| $h_{gp,c}$ | Gas convection between interstitial gas and large-solid surface | All regimes (low magnitude) |
| $h_{p,c}$ | Particle convection due to packet renewal of fine particles on the surface | Dominant in bubbling bed (high magnitude) |
| $h_{rad}$ | Radiation (only significant at $T > 700$ °C) | High-temperature operations |

The **packet-renewal (Mickley–Fairbanks) model** gives the particle-convective term as:

$$h_{p,c} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{k_{es} \cdot \rho_{es} \cdot c_{ps} \cdot f}$$

where
- $k_{es}$ = effective thermal conductivity of the emulsion phase
- $\rho_{es}$ = density of the emulsion phase
- $c_{ps}$ = specific heat of bed particles
- $f$ = frequency of packet renewal at the surface (Hz)

A more practical engineering correlation for **immersed horizontal tubes in fluidized beds** (which is the most common board-exam scenario) is:

$$\frac{h \cdot d_{L}}{k_{g}} = 0.0184 \cdot \left[ C_{p,g} \cdot \mu_{g} / k_{g} \right]^{0.43} \cdot \left[ d_{L} \cdot \rho_{g} \cdot (u - u_{mf,S}) / \mu_{g} \right]^{0.23} \cdot \left[ C_{ps} \cdot \rho_{S} / (C_{p,g} \cdot \rho_{g}) \right]^{0.8} \cdot \left[ \rho_{S} / \rho_{g} \right]^{0.66}$$

This is the famous **Vreedenberg (1958) correlation** for horizontal tubes. It is a high-yield formula for KTU exams whenever "immersed surface heat transfer in a fluidized bed" is asked.

### 2.5 Mass Transfer to a Large Immersed Solid

By the **Chilton–Colburn analogy**, the mass transfer coefficient $k_{c}$ to a large solid in a fluidized bed is related to the heat transfer coefficient by:

$$\frac{h}{\rho_{g} \cdot C_{p,g} \cdot (u - u_{mf,S})} = \frac{k_{c}}{(u - u_{mf,S})} \cdot \left( \frac{Sc}{Pr} \right)^{2/3}$$

This is crucial in **drying applications** where the large solid is a moist food piece and the fluidizing gas is hot air.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Summary (Examination Ready):**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Physical Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Density ratio classification | $\rho_{L} / \rho_{S}$ | Jetsam if $> 5$, Flotsam if $< 0.2$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Size ratio threshold | $d_{L} / d_{S} \geq 3$ | System qualifies as "large in fine" | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Minimum fluidization (small bed) | $Re_{mf,S} = \dfrac{d_{S} \cdot u_{mf,S} \cdot \rho_{g}}{\mu_{g}}$ | From Ergun equation | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Segregation–mixing transition | $u_{trans} = u_{mf,S} \left[ 1 + \dfrac{\rho_{L} - \rho_{S}}{\rho_{S}} \left( \dfrac{d_{L}}{d_{S}} \right)^{0.44} \right]$ | Rowe et al. criterion | m/s |
| 5 | Packet-renewal heat transfer | $h_{p,c} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \sqrt{k_{es} \rho_{es} c_{ps} f}$ | Mickley–Fairbanks | W/(m²·K) |
| 6 | Vreedenberg correlation | $Nu = 0.0184 \cdot Pr^{0.43} \cdot Re_{excess}^{0.23} \cdot \left( \dfrac{c_{ps} \rho_{S}}{c_{pg} \rho_{g}} \right)^{0.8} \cdot \left( \dfrac{\rho_{S}}{\rho_{g}} \rightright)^{0.66}$ | Horizontal tube in fluidized bed | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Mass–heat analogy | $St_{heat} = St_{mass} \cdot (Sc/Pr)^{2/3}$ | Chilton–Colburn | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Excess gas velocity | $u - u_{mf,S}$ | Driving force for mixing/transfer | m/s |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Engineering Significance:**
> - The segregation criterion decides **whether mixing paddles or baffles are required** in a commercial fluidized-bed coating vessel.
> - The Vreedenberg correlation is the **workhorse for designing the tube-bundle heat-exchanger surface area** in fluidized-bed combustors and catalytic crackers.
> - The packet-renewal frequency $f$ is empirically correlated to excess gas velocity as $f \propto (u - u_{mf,S})^{n}$ with $n \approx 0.5$ to $0.7$ – this captures the "faster gas $\Rightarrow$ more frequent packet impacts" intuition.
> - The Chilton–Colburn analogy allows engineers to estimate **drying times of large moist solids in fluidized beds** using only gas-phase heat transfer data.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Segregation–Mixing Transition Velocity (Rowe, Nienow, Agbim Criterion)

We will derive the criterion that decides whether a large particle will sink to the bottom of a fluidized bed (jetsam) or remain mixed with the bed (well-mixed).

**Step 1 – Force balance on the large particle in a quiescent bed:**

The large solid of diameter $d_{L}$ and density $\rho_{L}$ experiences the following vertical forces in a fluidized suspension of bulk density $\rho_{b}$:

$$
F_{net} = \underbrace{\frac{\pi}{6} d_{L}^{3} \, g \, \rho_{L}}_{\text{gravity}} - \underbrace{\frac{\pi}{6} d_{L}^{3} \, g \, \rho_{b}}_{\text{buoyancy}} - \underbrace{C_{D} \, \frac{\pi}{4} d_{L}^{2} \, \frac{\rho_{g} (u_{rel})^{2}}{2}}_{\text{drag}}
$$

where $u_{rel}$ is the relative velocity between the large solid and the surrounding suspension.

**Step 2 – Buoyancy assumption in a fluidized bed:**

For a fluidized bed of small particles at superficial velocity $u$, the bulk density of the suspension is:

$$
\rho_{b} = \rho_{S} (1 - \varepsilon) + \rho_{g} \varepsilon \approx \rho_{S} (1 - \varepsilon)
$$

The drag force on the large solid in a fluidized bed is enhanced by the presence of the fine particles. Empirically, the **effective drag** on the large solid is found to scale as the drag on a particle of size $d_{L}$ in a gas of effective viscosity $\mu_{eff}$ that incorporates the contribution of the fine particles.

**Step 3 – Setting the incipient-mixing condition:**

The large solid begins to be lifted (i.e., transitions from jetsam to mixed) when the upward drag and buoyancy balance gravity. Substituting the empirical drag correlation for fluidized suspensions:

$$
C_{D} \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d_{L}^{2} \cdot \frac{\rho_{g} u_{trans}^{2}}{2} = \frac{\pi}{6} d_{L}^{3} \, g \, (\rho_{L} - \rho_{b})
$$

Solving for the transition velocity, and introducing the bed-voidage correction, the final semi-empirical form is:

$$
u_{trans} = u_{mf,S} \cdot \left[ 1 + \left( \frac{\rho_{L} - \rho_{S}}{\rho_{S}} \right) \left( \frac{d_{L}}{d_{S}} \right)^{0.44} \right]
$$

**Step 4 – Physical interpretation of the result:**

The exponent **0.44 on the size ratio** indicates that larger large-solids are more difficult to suspend (they sink more readily). The **density-difference term in brackets** shows that denser large solids also sink more readily. If both the density ratio and size ratio are large, the transition velocity $u_{trans}$ is much greater than $u_{mf,S}$, meaning that very high gas velocities are required to suspend such a large solid.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Model):** A pharmaceutical tablet of diameter $d_{L} = 12$ mm and density $\rho_{L} = 1300$ kg/m³ is to be coated in a fluidized bed of fine coating powder with $d_{S} = 0.25$ mm, $\rho_{S} = 900$ kg/m³, and $u_{mf,S} = 0.02$ m/s. Determine whether the tablet will mix uniformly with the bed or sink to the bottom if the operating gas velocity is $u = 0.05$ m/s. Use the Rowe–Nienow–Agbim criterion.

**Step 1 – Compute the size and density ratios.**

$$
\frac{d_{L}}{d_{S}} = \frac{12 \text{ mm}}{0.25 \text{ mm}} = 48
$$

$$
\frac{\rho_{L} - \rho_{S}}{\rho_{S}} = \frac{1300 - 900}{900} = 0.444
$$

**Step 2 – Compute the transition velocity.**

$$
u_{trans} = u_{mf,S} \cdot \left[ 1 + \left( \frac{\rho_{L} - \rho_{S}}{\rho_{S}} \right) \left( \frac{d_{L}}{d_{S}} \right)^{0.44} \right]
$$

First compute the size-ratio term:

$$
\left( \frac{d_{L}}{d_{S}} \right)^{0.44} = 48^{0.44} = e^{0.44 \cdot \ln 48} = e^{0.44 \cdot 3.871} = e^{1.703} = 5.491
$$

Then compute the bracketed term:

$$
1 + (0.444) \cdot (5.491) = 1 + 2.438 = 3.438
$$

Finally, compute $u_{trans}$:

$$
u_{trans} = 0.02 \text{ m/s} \times 3.438 = 0.0688 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 – Compare with operating velocity.**

Since the operating gas velocity $u = 0.05$ m/s is **less than** the transition velocity $u_{trans} = 0.0688$ m/s, the large solid (tablet) will **segregate to the bottom of the bed (jetsam behaviour)** at this operating point.

**Step 4 – Engineered solution.**

To suspend the tablet and achieve uniform mixing/coating, the operator must **increase the gas velocity to at least $u_{trans} = 0.069$ m/s**. Alternatively, the bed particles can be replaced with denser particles (e.g., glass beads) to increase $\rho_{S}$ and reduce the density-difference term. A third option is to install **mechanical agitators or baffles** to assist the lifting of the tablet.

**Step 5 – Quantitative summary table.**

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $d_{L}$ | 12 | mm |
| $\rho_{L}$ | 1300 | kg/m³ |
| $d_{S}$ | 0.25 | mm |
| $\rho_{S}$ | 900 | kg/m³ |
| $u_{mf,S}$ | 0.02 | m/s |
| $u_{trans}$ (calculated) | 0.0688 | m/s |
| $u$ (operating) | 0.05 | m/s |
| **Verdict** | **Jetsam (sinks)** | — |

### 3.3 Derivation of Packet-Renewal Heat Transfer Coefficient

The packet-renewal model assumes that the surface of the large solid is intermittently covered by packets of emulsion (suspension of fine particles) of mean contact time $t_{c}$, with packet renewal frequency $f = 1/t_{c}$.

**Step 1 – Energy balance on a single packet of thickness $\delta$.**

During contact time $t_{c}$, unsteady heat conduction occurs from the bulk bed to the large-solid surface through the packet of thermal diffusivity $\alpha_{es}$:

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha_{es} \frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial y^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 – Solution using the error function.**

For an instantaneous temperature change at $y = 0$ (large-solid surface), the instantaneous heat flux is:

$$
q(t) = \frac{k_{es}}{\sqrt{\pi \alpha_{es} t}} \cdot (T_{b} - T_{s})
$$

**Step 3 – Time-averaged heat flux.**

Averaging over the contact time $t_{c}$:

$$
\bar{q} = \frac{1}{t_{c}} \int_{0}^{t_{c}} \frac{k_{es} (T_{b} - T_{s})}{\sqrt{\pi \alpha_{es} t}} \, dt
$$

$$
\bar{q} = \frac{2 k_{es} (T_{b} - T_{s})}{\sqrt{\pi \alpha_{es} t_{c}}}
$$

**Step 4 – Identify the heat transfer coefficient.**

By definition, $\bar{q} = h_{p,c} (T_{b} - T_{s})$, so:

$$
h_{p,c} = \frac{2 k_{es}}{\sqrt{\pi \alpha_{es} t_{c}}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \cdot \frac{k_{es}}{\sqrt{\alpha_{es}}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{t_{c}}}
$$

Using $\alpha_{es} = k_{es} / (\rho_{es} c_{ps})$ and $f = 1/t_{c}$:

$$
h_{p,c} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{k_{es} \rho_{es} c_{ps} \cdot f}
$$

This is the **Mickley–Fairbanks packet-renewal result**, which shows that the heat transfer coefficient scales as the **square root of packet renewal frequency** $f$. Since $f$ increases with gas velocity (more frequent bubble bursts), $h_{p,c}$ also increases with $u$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Architecture – Conceptual Map

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **decision flow** that an engineer follows when designing a process involving large solids in a bed of smaller fluidized particles.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Large Solid and Bed] --> B{Compare Density}
    B -- rho_L greater than rho_S --> C[Jetsam: Large Solid Sinks]
    B -- rho_L less than rho_S --> D[Flotsam: Large Solid Floats]
    B -- rho_L approx rho_S --> E[Equilibrium: Suspended]
    C --> F{Compare Size Ratio}
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F -- d_L by d_S greater than 3 --> G[System Qualifies as Large in Fine]
    F -- d_L by d_S less than 3 --> H[Use Standard Fluidization Theory]
    G --> I{Compute u_trans from Rowe Criterion}
    I -- u less than u_trans --> J[Jetsam Segregation Persists]
    I -- u greater than u_trans --> K[Large Solid is Mixed in Bed]
    J --> L[Engineered Solution: Increase u or Add Baffles]
    K --> M{Choose Application}
    M --> N[Coating: Maximize Contact Time]
    M --> O[Drying: Maximize Mass Transfer Coefficient]
    M --> P[Heat Exchanger: Apply Vreedenberg Correlation]
    L --> M
    N --> Q[End: Design Validated]
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
```

### 4.2 Schematic of the Physical System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GAS[Gas Inlet] --> DIST[Distributor Plate]
    DIST --> FB[Fluidized Bed of Fine Particles]
    FB --> LARGE[Large Solid Object Immersed]
    FB --> FINE1[Fine Particle Jetsam Below]
    FB --> FINE2[Fine Particle Emulsion Around]
    FB --> BUB[Bubbles Rising]
    BUB --> SURF[Bed Surface]
    SURF --> FFF[Fines Entrained to Freeboard]
    FFF --> CYCL[Cyclone Separator]
    CYCL --> GAS
    LARGE --> HTHT[Heat Transfer h_p_c via Packet Renewal]
    FINE2 --> HTHT
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram of the Three Transport Mechanisms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Total Heat Transfer h] --> Q2[h_gp_c: Gas Convection]
    Q1 --> Q3[h_p_c: Particle Convection via Packet Renewal]
    Q1 --> Q4[h_rad: Radiation]
    Q2 --> Q5[Low Magnitude, Always Present]
    Q3 --> Q6[Dominant in Bubbling Bed]
    Q4 --> Q7[Significant above 700 degree C]
    Q3 --> Q8[Mickley Fairbanks Model]
    Q3 --> Q9[Frequency f of Packet Renewal]
    Q9 --> Q10[f proportional to u minus u_mf_S raised to 0.5 to 0.7]
    Q8 --> Q11[h_p_c = 2 over sqrt pi times sqrt of k_es rho_es c_ps f]
```

### 4.4 Industrial Application Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    APP[Large Solid in Fine Bed Applications] --> APP1[Pharmaceutical Tablet Coating]
    APP --> APP2[Fertilizer Prill Coating]
    APP --> APP3[Food Drying]
    APP --> APP4[Immersed Heat Exchanger Tubes]
    APP --> APP5[Catalyst Regeneration]
    APP1 --> OUT1[Use Equilibrium or Flotsam Configuration]
    APP2 --> OUT2[Use Jetsam Configuration with Mechanical Agitator]
    APP3 --> OUT3[Use Dense-Phase Immersion Regime]
    APP4 --> OUT4[Apply Vreedenberg Correlation]
    APP5 --> OUT5[Use High Velocity Regenerator]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define the terms "jetsam" and "flotsam" in the context of large solids in a bed of smaller fluidized particles. State the conditions under which each is observed.*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> - **Jetsam** is the term for large solids that are **denser** than the bed particles ($\rho_{L} > \rho_{S}$). They segregate to the **bottom** of the fluidized bed, near the distributor plate. Example: metal ore lumps in a fluidized bed of sand.
> - **Flotsam** is the term for large solids that are **less dense** than the bed particles ($\rho_{L} < \rho_{S}$). They float on the **surface** of the bed. Example: low-density polymer tablets in a fluidized bed of glass beads.
> - A third category, **equilibrium solids**, has $\rho_{L} \approx \rho_{bed,bulk}$ and remains suspended in the bed at intermediate heights. The criterion $\rho_{L}/\rho_{S} \geq 5$ (jetsam) and $\rho_{L}/\rho_{S} \leq 0.2$ (flotsam) is widely used for classification.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is the packet-renewal (Mickley–Fairbanks) model for heat transfer to an immersed surface in a fluidized bed? Write the expression for the particle-convective heat transfer coefficient.*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The packet-renewal model assumes that the surface of the large solid is **intermittently covered by packets** of emulsion (a suspension of fine particles in gas). Each packet stays in contact with the surface for a characteristic time $t_{c}$, after which it is replaced by a fresh packet from the bulk bed. Heat transfer occurs by **unsteady conduction** through each packet during its contact time. The time-averaged heat flux yields the particle-convective heat transfer coefficient:
> $$h_{p,c} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{k_{es} \cdot \rho_{es} \cdot c_{ps} \cdot f}$$
> where $f = 1/t_{c}$ is the packet-renewal frequency (Hz), $k_{es}$ is the effective thermal conductivity of the emulsion, $\rho_{es}$ is its density, and $c_{ps}$ is the specific heat of the fine particles. The model shows that $h_{p,c} \propto \sqrt{f}$, and since $f$ increases with excess gas velocity $(u - u_{mf,S})$, heat transfer is enhanced at higher gas velocities.

---

### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the phenomenon of large solids in beds of smaller particles. Classify the large solids based on density difference and discuss the role of bubble dynamics in promoting mixing of the large solid. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical catalyst pellet of diameter $d_{L} = 8$ mm and density $\rho_{L} = 2200$ kg/m³ is to be processed in a fluidized bed of fine catalyst particles with $d_{S} = 0.20$ mm, $\rho_{S} = 1800$ kg/m³, and $u_{mf,S} = 0.015$ m/s. The operating gas velocity is $u = 0.06$ m/s. Determine whether the pellet will mix uniformly with the bed or segregate. Use the Rowe–Nienow–Agbim criterion. If segregation occurs, suggest two engineering solutions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – Phenomenological Discussion:**

A fluidized bed of fine particles behaves as a **pseudo-fluid** with bulk density $\rho_{b}$ in the range of 400–800 kg/m³. When a large solid object is introduced, it experiences (i) gravity, (ii) buoyancy from the surrounding suspension, and (iii) drag from the upward-moving gas and entrained fine particles. The relative magnitudes of these forces determine the equilibrium position of the large solid:

- **Jetsam** (denser large solid): gravity dominates; sinks to the distributor.
- **Flotsam** (lighter large solid): buoyancy dominates; floats on the surface.
- **Equilibrium** (density-matched large solid): suspended at an intermediate height.

**Bubble dynamics and mixing:** Bubbles rising through the bed carry a wake of fine particles. When a bubble bursts at the surface or at the large-solid surface, the wake particles are thrown onto the surface, **renewing the contact packet** and **dragging the large solid upward** momentarily. This bubble-induced drag is the dominant mechanism for mixing the large solid against gravity. At low gas velocities, bubbles are small and the drag is insufficient – the large solid remains as jetsam. At higher gas velocities, larger bubbles form and induce stronger wakes, suspending the large solid.

**Part (b) – Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 – Compute the size and density ratios.**

$$
\frac{d_{L}}{d_{S}} = \frac{8}{0.20} = 40
$$

$$
\frac{\rho_{L} - \rho_{S}}{\rho_{S}} = \frac{2200 - 1800}{1800} = 0.222
$$

**Step 2 – Compute the size-ratio exponent term.**

$$
\left( \frac{d_{L}}{d_{S}} \right)^{0.44} = 40^{0.44} = e^{0.44 \cdot \ln 40} = e^{0.44 \cdot 3.689} = e^{1.623} = 5.069
$$

**Step 3 – Compute $u_{trans}$.**

$$
u_{trans} = 0.015 \times \left[ 1 + (0.222)(5.069) \right] = 0.015 \times [1 + 1.125] = 0.015 \times 2.125 = 0.0319 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4 – Verdict.**

Operating velocity $u = 0.06$ m/s $>$ $u_{trans} = 0.0319$ m/s. The pellet will **mix uniformly** with the bed at this operating point.

**Step 5 – Engineering interpretation.**

Since the criterion is satisfied, the existing operating velocity is sufficient. If, however, the operating velocity had been lower than 0.0319 m/s, the pellet would have sunk. Two engineering solutions in that case would be: (1) **increase the gas velocity** to at least $u_{trans}$, and (2) **install mechanical agitators or draft tubes** to assist the upward transport of the pellet.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the heat transfer coefficient to a large solid immersed in a fluidized bed using the packet-renewal (Mickley–Fairbanks) model. Clearly state the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An immersed horizontal tube of diameter $d_{L} = 50$ mm in a fluidized-bed combustor is exposed to hot flue gases at $T_g = 850$ °C fluidizing a bed of sand ($d_{S} = 0.5$ mm, $\rho_{S} = 2500$ kg/m³, $c_{ps} = 800$ J/kg·K, $u_{mf,S} = 0.18$ m/s). The operating gas velocity is $u = 0.6$ m/s, with gas properties: $k_{g} = 0.05$ W/m·K, $\mu_{g} = 4 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $\rho_{g} = 0.35$ kg/m³, $c_{pg} = 1100$ J/kg·K, $Pr = 0.7$. Estimate the heat transfer coefficient using the Vreedenberg correlation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – Derivation:**

(Present the full derivation from Section 3.3 of these notes – the packet-renewal result $h_{p,c} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \sqrt{k_{es} \rho_{es} c_{ps} f}$, with assumptions: 1D unsteady conduction, instantaneous packet renewal, constant packet properties, no internal heat generation.)

**Part (b) – Numerical Solution using Vreedenberg Correlation:**

The Vreedenberg (1958) correlation for horizontal tubes immersed in a fluidized bed is:

$$
Nu = 0.0184 \cdot Pr^{0.43} \cdot Re_{ex}^{0.23} \cdot \left( \frac{c_{ps} \rho_{S}}{c_{pg} \rho_{g}} \right)^{0.8} \cdot \left( \frac{\rho_{S}}{\rho_{g}} \right)^{0.66}
$$

**Step 1 – Compute the excess Reynolds number.**

$$
Re_{ex} = \frac{d_{L} \cdot \rho_{g} \cdot (u - u_{mf,S})}{\mu_{g}} = \frac{0.05 \times 0.35 \times (0.6 - 0.18)}{4 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
Re_{ex} = \frac{0.05 \times 0.35 \times 0.42}{4 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.00735}{4 \times 10^{-5}} = 183.75
$$

**Step 2 – Compute the property ratios.**

$$
Pr = 0.7
$$

$$
\frac{c_{ps} \rho_{S}}{c_{pg} \rho_{g}} = \frac{800 \times 2500}{1100 \times 0.35} = \frac{2{,}000{,}000}{385} = 5194.8
$$

$$
\frac{\rho_{S}}{\rho_{g}} = \frac{2500}{0.35} = 7142.86
$$

**Step 3 – Compute each term in the correlation.**

$$
Pr^{0.43} = 0.7^{0.43} = e^{0.43 \cdot \ln 0.7} = e^{0.43 \cdot (-0.3567)} = e^{-0.1534} = 0.858
$$

$$
Re_{ex}^{0.23} = 183.75^{0.23} = e^{0.23 \cdot \ln 183.75} = e^{0.23 \cdot 5.214} = e^{1.199} = 3.317
$$

$$
\left( 5194.8 \right)^{0.8} = e^{0.8 \cdot \ln 5194.8} = e^{0.8 \cdot 8.555} = e^{6.844} = 938.4
$$

$$
\left( 7142.86 \right)^{0.66} = e^{0.66 \cdot \ln 7142.86} = e^{0.66 \cdot 8.874} = e^{5.857} = 349.4
$$

**Step 4 – Multiply all terms.**

$$
Nu = 0.0184 \times 0.858 \times 3.317 \times 938.4 \times 349.4
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
0.0184 \times 0.858 = 0.01579
$$

$$
0.01579 \times 3.317 = 0.05237
$$

$$
0.05237 \times 938.4 = 49.14
$$

$$
49.14 \times 349.4 = 17{,}167
$$

**Step 5 – Compute the heat transfer coefficient.**

$$
h = \frac{Nu \cdot k_{g}}{d_{L}} = \frac{17{,}167 \times 0.05}{0.05} = 17{,}167 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)} \approx 17.2 \text{ kW/(m}^2\text{·K)}
$$

This is a typical value for immersed-tube heat transfer in a bubbling fluidized bed of sand.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing jetsam and flotsam** – remember, jetsam = dense large solid = sinks, flotsam = light large solid = floats. Many students reverse this in the exam.
> 2. **Forgetting the density difference sign** – the term $(\rho_{L} - \rho_{S})/\rho_{S}$ is **positive for jetsam** and **negative for flotsam** (giving $u_{trans} < u_{mf,S}$ for flotsam, meaning flotsam is suspended even at low gas velocities).
> 3. **Using the wrong Reynolds number** – in the Vreedenberg correlation, use $Re_{excess}$ based on $(u - u_{mf,S})$, NOT the full $u$.
> 4. **Exponent error on size ratio** – the Rowe criterion uses exponent 0.44, NOT 0.5 or any other value. This is a fixed empirical exponent.
> 5. **Units inconsistency** – always convert $d_{L}$ and $d_{S}$ to the same units before forming the ratio; many students lose 1–2 marks for unit errors.
> 6. **Confusing $u_{mf,L}$ with $u_{mf,S}$** – the transition velocity uses the **small bed's** $u_{mf,S}$, not the large solid's.
> 7. **Omitting the buoyancy term in force balance** – the large solid experiences buoyancy from the bulk suspension, not from the gas alone.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> - **Jetsam** = denser large solid = sinks; **Flotsam** = lighter large solid = floats; **Equilibrium** = density-matched = suspended.
> - **Threshold** for "large in fine" system: $d_{L}/d_{S} \geq 3$ or $\rho_{L}/\rho_{S} \geq 5$.
> - **Rowe–Nienow–Agbim criterion:** $u_{trans} = u_{mf,S} \left[ 1 + \dfrac{\rho_{L} - \rho_{S}}{\rho_{S}} \left( \dfrac{d_{L}}{d_{S}} \right)^{0.44} \right]$. If $u > u_{trans}$ → mixed; if $u < u_{trans}$ → jetsam.
> - **Mickley–Fairbanks packet renewal:** $h_{p,c} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \sqrt{k_{es} \rho_{es} c_{ps} f}$; scales as $\sqrt{f}$ and hence as $\sqrt{u - u_{mf,S}}$.
> - **Vreedenberg correlation** (horizontal immersed tubes): $Nu = 0.0184 \cdot Pr^{0.43} \cdot Re_{ex}^{0.23} \cdot \left( \dfrac{c_{ps} \rho_{S}}{c_{pg} \rho_{g}} \right)^{0.8} \cdot \left( \dfrac{\rho_{S}}{\rho_{g}} \right)^{0.66}$.
> - **Chilton–Colburn analogy:** $St_{heat} = St_{mass} \cdot (Sc/Pr)^{2/3}$ – links heat and mass transfer coefficients.
> - **Three heat transfer contributions:** $h = h_{gp,c} + h_{p,c} + h_{rad}$; particle convection dominates in bubbling beds.
> - **Industrial applications:** tablet coating, prill coating, food drying, immersed heat-exchanger tubes, catalyst regeneration.
> - **Excess gas velocity** $(u - u_{mf,S})$ is the **driving force** for both mixing and heat transfer.
> - **Bubbles and their wakes** are the **dominant mixing mechanism** for large solids in bubbling beds.
> - **Mechanical aids** (agitators, draft tubes, baffles) are used when gas velocity alone is insufficient to suspend a large solid.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
