Use the **Bubble–Cloud–Wake (BCW) model** of Kunii–Levenspiel instead. - ✅ Three regions of a bubble: **Bubble core** (gas + fines) | **Cloud** (Geldart A only) | **Wake** (universal torus of solids). - ✅ **Wake fraction $\alpha_w$** = $V_w / V_b$ ; typically **0.2 – 0.5**, increases with $d_b / d_p$ and $\rho_p$. - ✅ Davidson–Harrison bubble rise velocity: $u_b = (U_0 - U_{mf}) + 0.711 \sqrt{g d_b}$. - ✅ Bubble fraction: $\delta_b = (U_0 - U_{mf}) / u_b$. - ✅ **Wake solids mass flux (the master equation):** $G_s = \alpha_w \cdot \rho_p \cdot (U_0 - U_{mf})$. - ✅ Bernoulli suction + boundary layer separation = physical cause of wake formation. - ✅ Wake solids are the **dominant heat carrier** in FCC, MTO, polyethylene, and combustion fluidized-bed reactors. - ✅ For KTU numerical: always show **5 steps** — (1) $U_{ex}$, (2) $u_b$, (3) $\delta_b$, (4) $G_s$, (5) physical interpretation. - ✅ Examiner's pet pitfall: do **NOT** confuse wake volume with bubble volume; do **NOT** use $U_0$ in place of $U_0 - U_{mf}$. **End of Topic — Solids within Bubbles (Overview Only), Module 3, PECHT415 Fluidization Engineering (KTU 2024 Scheme).**

topics.title: Coalescence and Splitting of Bubbles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Coalescence and Splitting of Bubbles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bubble Coalescence** is the dynamic process in a gas-solid fluidized bed whereby two or more bubbles, rising through the dense phase, approach each other due to differences in their rise velocities and/or wake entrainment effects, and merge to form a single, larger bubble with a reduced total interfacial area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bubble Splitting (Breakage)** is the converse dynamic phenomenon in which a single bubble, under the influence of hydrodynamic instabilities, turbulent eddies of the dense phase, or interactions with solid particles and internals, ruptures into two or more smaller daughter bubbles with a higher total interfacial area.

In a freely bubbling fluidized bed operating in the **bubbling regime (Geldart Group B and D powders)**, these two phenomena occur **simultaneously and continuously**, making the bubble size distribution in the bed inherently **non-uniform, height-dependent, and stochastic**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **net bubble growth rate** at any horizontal cross-section of the bed is given by:
> $$\left(\frac{d\,d_b}{dz}\right)_{\text{net}} = \left(\frac{d\,d_b}{dz}\right)_{\text{coalescence}} - \left(\frac{d\,d_b}{dz}\right)_{\text{splitting}}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Boiling-Pot Intuition

Imagine a **pot of boiling water** on a stove. As bubbles of water vapour rise from the heated base:

- **Near the bottom**: Tiny bubbles form at the nucleation sites. As they rise, they **coalesce** (merge) — much like two soap bubbles touching to form one larger bubble. The trailing bubble is "sucked" into the leading one.
- **Near the top**: The bubbles become large and unstable. The surface tension and turbulence cause them to **split** into smaller droplets that pop at the surface.
- **In the middle**: Coalescence and splitting compete. The largest, most stable bubbles survive.

> A **fluidized bed** behaves exactly like this boiling pot, except the "fluid" is gas and the "particles" act as a quasi-liquid dense phase. The wake of each rising bubble carries solid particles upward (just like a boat leaves a wake), and trailing bubbles are entrained into this wake, leading to **coalescence**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Magnitude | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Bubble rise velocity (isolated) | $u_b$ | **0.5 – 3.0** | m/s |
| Wake fraction | $f_w$ | **0.1 – 0.6** | dimensionless |
| Coalescence frequency | $\omega_c$ | **1 – 50** | s⁻¹ |
| Splitting frequency | $\omega_s$ | **0.1 – 10** | s⁻¹ |
| Maximum stable bubble size | $d_{b,\text{max}}$ | **0.05 – 1.0** | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance in KTU Examinations**: Coalescence and splitting jointly determine the **gas-solid contacting efficiency** in catalytic reactors (FCC risers, acrylonitrile synthesis, Fischer-Tropsch reactors). Enhanced coalescence → larger bubbles → bypassing of reactant gas → poor conversion. Hence, understanding these phenomena is critical for **reactor scale-up and design**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Wake Concept — The Engine of Coalescence

The phenomenon of bubble coalescence in fluidized beds is **fundamentally driven by wake entrainment**:

- A rising bubble **entrains solid particles** in its wake (a low-pressure, low-velocity region immediately above the bubble).
- The wake behaves like a **"particulate shadow"** that travels with the bubble.
- Trailing bubbles, moving along streamlined paths, are drawn into this wake and merge with the leading bubble.

### 2.1.1 Wake Fraction ($f_w$)

The **wake fraction** is defined as the volume of wake per unit volume of bubble:

$$f_w = \frac{V_{\text{wake}}}{V_{\text{bubble}}}$$

For a spherical bubble, the wake volume can be expressed as:

$$V_{\text{wake}} = f_w \cdot \frac{\pi\, d_b^3}{6}$$

**Kato-Wen Correlation for Wake Fraction:**

$$f_w = 0.0735 \, \text{Ar}^{0.425} \left(\frac{U}{U_{mf}}\right)^{-0.35}$$

where $\text{Ar}$ is the **Archimedes number** and $U / U_{mf}$ is the **fluidization number**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $f_w$ matters**: A larger wake fraction means more trailing bubbles are captured → faster coalescence → larger bubbles → lower gas-solid contact.

## 2.2 Bubble Coalescence — Mechanisms

Bubble coalescence in fluidized beds proceeds via **three sequential stages**:

1. **Approach Stage**: Two bubbles approach each other due to differential rise velocities. The faster-rising bubble "catches up" with the slower one.
2. **Drainage Stage**: The thin liquid-like dense-phase film between the two bubbles drains until critical thickness is reached.
3. **Rupture Stage**: The inter-bubble film ruptures, and the two bubbles merge into one.

The **rate of approach** depends on the relative rise velocities. By the Wallis correlation for an isolated bubble:

$$u_b = 0.711 \sqrt{g\,d_b}$$

The relative velocity of a trailing bubble (radius $r_1$) with respect to a leading bubble (radius $r_2$):

$$u_{\text{rel}} = 0.711 \sqrt{g}\left(\sqrt{d_{b,1}} - \sqrt{d_{b,2}}\right)$$

## 2.3 Bubble Splitting — Mechanisms

Bubble splitting is induced by:

| Mechanism | Description |
|---|---|
| **Turbulent eddy shedding** | Eddies in the dense phase shear the bubble surface |
| **Particle impact** | Solid particles striking the bubble roof penetrate and rupture it |
| **Surface instability** | Bubble surface tension cannot sustain deformation at high $d_b$ |
| **Wall/internals collision** | Mechanical rupture against baffles, tubes, or distributor |

The **critical (maximum stable) bubble size** beyond which splitting dominates:

$$d_{b,\text{max}} = \frac{2.78}{g}\left(U - U_{mf}\right)^2 \quad \text{(Mori-Wen, 1975)}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM-READY FORMULA TABLE** — Memorize this for KTU 2024 ESE preparation. Pay attention to units and the column separators — all absolute values use `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown.

| # | Equation / Correlation | Domain / Applicability | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_w = 0.0735\,\text{Ar}^{0.425}\left(U/U_{mf}\right)^{-0.35}$ | Wake fraction, Kato-Wen | dimensionless |
| 2 | $u_b = 0.711\,\sqrt{g\,d_b}$ | Isolated bubble, Wallis | m/s |
| 3 | $u_b = U - U_{mf} + 0.711\,\sqrt{g\,d_b}$ | Bubble in dense phase, Davidson-Yates | m/s |
| 4 | $\dfrac{d(d_b)}{dz} = \dfrac{(U - U_{mf})\,f_w}{u_b\,(1 - f_w)}$ | Bubble growth, Kato-Wen | m⁻¹ |
| 5 | $d_{b,\text{max}} = \dfrac{2.78}{g}\left(U - U_{mf}\right)^2$ | Maximum stable size, Mori-Wen | m |
| 6 | $N_b(z) = \dfrac{N_0}{(1 + \alpha\,z)^2}$ | Bubble number density, Darton-type | m⁻³ |
| 7 | $\omega_c = C_c\,N_b^2$ | Coalescence frequency, second-order kinetic | s⁻¹ |
| 8 | $\omega_s = C_s\,N_b\,d_b^n$ | Splitting frequency | s⁻¹ |
| 9 | $d_b^* = d_{bm}\left[1 + \dfrac{6.84}{D_t^0.6}\left(U - U_{mf}\right)^{0.4}\,z^{0.8}\right]$ | Bubble size profile, Darton | m |
| 10 | $\text{Ar} = \dfrac{\rho_g\left(\rho_p - \rho_g\right)g\,d_p^3}{\mu_g^2}$ | Archimedes number | dimensionless |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

| Industrial Application | Relevance of Coalescence/Splitting |
|---|---|
| **FCC Risers** (Fluid Catalytic Cracking) | Excessive coalescence → bypassing → poor gasoline yield |
| **Acrylonitrile synthesis** (Sohio process) | Coalescence reduces gas-solid contact → lower conversion |
| **Coal combustion fluidized beds** | Splitting enhances gas distribution → uniform temperature |
| **Pharmaceutical coating** | Bubble size uniformity critical for product quality |
| **Polymerization reactors** (gas-phase) | Determines molecular weight distribution |

> **Industrial rule of thumb**: To **suppress coalescence**, use **fine particles, high $U/U_{mf}$, and vertical internals (baffles)**. To **promote splitting**, install **horizontal tubes or screens** that mechanically rupture large bubbles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Kato-Wen Bubble Growth Model (1969)

**Starting Physical Assumption**: A bubble grows as it rises by engulfing smaller bubbles that are entrained in its wake. The bubble shed a wake of volume $V_w$ per unit rise height, and any bubble in this wake is captured.

**Step 1 — Wake volume captured per unit rise height:**

The volume of wake swept per unit axial rise is:

$$\frac{dV_w}{dz} = f_w \cdot \frac{\pi\,d_b^2}{4}$$

> **Why?** Cross-sectional area of the bubble projected in the horizontal plane is $\pi d_b^2/4$. The wake volume per unit height equals the projected area times the wake fraction.

**Step 2 — Gas holdup in bubble (single bubble):**

$$V_b = \frac{\pi\,d_b^3}{6}$$

**Step 3 — Increase in bubble volume per unit height (mass balance on the wake):**

As the bubble rises by $dz$, the wake volume $dV_w$ is added. Considering the wake is filled with dense phase containing dispersed small bubbles of mean diameter $d_{bm}$:

$$\frac{dV_b}{dz} = \frac{dV_w}{dz} \cdot \frac{d_{bm}^3}{d_b^3 - d_{bm}^3} \cdot \frac{1}{f_w}$$

> **Why the ratio $d_{bm}^3/(d_b^3 - d_{bm}^3)$?** The wake volume $dV_w$ contains dense phase + small bubbles. Only the small bubble volume (size $d_{bm}$) is incorporated into the leading bubble. The denominator excludes the original bubble volume.

**Step 4 — Differentiate bubble volume with respect to $d_b$:**

$$\frac{dV_b}{dd_b} = \frac{\pi\,d_b^2}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Combine the relations:**

$$\frac{\pi\,d_b^2}{2}\,\frac{d(d_b)}{dz} = \frac{f_w\,\pi\,d_b^2}{4} \cdot \frac{d_{bm}^3}{d_b^3 - d_{bm}^3} \cdot \frac{1}{f_w}$$

After algebraic simplification (cancelling $\pi$, $d_b^2$, and $f_w$):

$$\frac{d(d_b)}{dz} = \frac{d_{bm}^3}{2\left(d_b^3 - d_{bm}^3\right)}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $d_{bm}$ using the Davidson-Harrison relation for initial bubble size at the distributor:**

The initial bubble size at the distributor plate is:

$$d_{bm} = \frac{1.38}{g^{0.2}} \left[\frac{A\left(U - U_{mf}\right)}{N_0}\right]^{0.4}$$

**Step 7 — Final form of the Kato-Wen growth equation (height-time conversion):**

The bed height is related to bubble velocity via $dz = u_b\,dt$, so:

$$\frac{d(d_b)}{dz} = \frac{(U - U_{mf})\,f_w}{u_b\,(1 - f_w)}$$

This is the **Kato-Wen bubble growth equation** — extremely high-yield for KTU exams.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Mori-Wen Maximum Stable Bubble Size (1975)

**Starting Physical Assumption**: A bubble grows by coalescence until it reaches a size where **splitting forces equal coalescence forces**. The maximum stable size is set by the balance between bubble rise kinetic energy and the disruptive turbulent kinetic energy of the dense phase.

**Step 1 — Coalescence-induced growth rate (per unit rise):**

Using the Davidson-Harrison bubble interaction frequency:

$$\frac{d(d_b)}{dz}\bigg|_{\text{coal}} = \frac{(U - U_{mf})\cdot f_w}{u_b \cdot (1 - f_w)}$$

**Step 2 — Splitting-induced shrinkage rate (per unit rise):**

From hydrodynamic stability analysis, the splitting rate scales as:

$$\frac{d(d_b)}{dz}\bigg|_{\text{split}} = k_s \cdot d_b^{-n}$$

where $k_s$ is a splitting constant and $n \approx 1$ experimentally.

**Step 3 — Equate the two rates at equilibrium (maximum size):**

$$\frac{(U - U_{mf})\,f_w}{u_b\,(1 - f_w)} = \frac{k_s}{d_{b,\text{max}}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $u_b = 0.711\,\sqrt{g\,d_{b,\text{max}}}$ and solve for $d_{b,\text{max}}$:**

$$\frac{(U - U_{mf})\,f_w}{0.711\,\sqrt{g\,d_{b,\text{max}}}\,(1 - f_w)} = \frac{k_s}{d_{b,\text{max}}}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$(U - U_{mf})\,f_w \cdot d_{b,\text{max}} = 0.711\,k_s\,\sqrt{g\,d_{b,\text{max}}}\,(1 - f_w)$$

Squaring both sides to eliminate the square root:

$$(U - U_{mf})^2 f_w^2 \cdot d_{b,\text{max}}^2 = 0.505\,k_s^2\,g\,d_{b,\text{max}}\,(1 - f_w)^2$$

Solving for $d_{b,\text{max}}$:

$$d_{b,\text{max}} = \frac{0.505\,k_s^2\,g\,(1 - f_w)^2}{(U - U_{mf})^2 f_w^2}$$

**Step 5 — Insert empirical proportionality constant** (Mori-Wen fitted $k_s$ from experiments):

After data regression on multiple fluidized beds, Mori and Wen obtained:

$$\boxed{\,d_{b,\text{max}} = \frac{2.78}{g}\left(U - U_{mf}\right)^2\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation**: The maximum stable bubble size scales with the **square of the excess gas velocity**. Doubling $U - U_{mf}$ quadruples $d_{b,\text{max}}$. This is why tall fluidized beds with high gas throughput experience dramatic bubble growth and consequent gas bypassing.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Wake Fraction — Energetic Approach

**Starting Assumption**: The wake volume is determined by the balance between the **buoyancy force** on the bubble and the **drag from displaced particles** entering the wake.

**Step 1 — Buoyancy force on the bubble:**

$$F_B = \left(\rho_p - \rho_g\right)(1 - \varepsilon_{mf}}) g \cdot V_b$$

where $\varepsilon_{mf}$ is the voidage at minimum fluidization.

**Step 2 — Drag force on particles in the wake (Stokes regime):**

$$F_D = C_{D,\text{wake}} \cdot \frac{\rho_g u_b^2}{2} \cdot A_{\text{wake}}$$

**Step 3 — Force balance sets wake geometry:**

The wake fraction follows from the balance $F_B = F_D$ and from dimensional analysis on the Archimedes number:

$$f_w = f(\text{Ar}, U/U_{mf})$$

**Step 4 — Empirical fit (Kato-Wen form):**

$$f_w = 0.0735\,\text{Ar}^{0.425}\left(\frac{U}{U_{mf}}\right)^{-0.35}$$

This correlation has been validated for **Geldart Group B particles** in the range $\text{Ar} \in [10, 10^5]$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When Coalescence vs Splitting Dominates

| Operating Regime | Dominant Phenomenon | $d_b$ trend with height | Gas-solid contact quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| $U/U_{mf} < 1.5$, fine particles | **Splitting dominant** | Slightly decreases | Excellent |
| $1.5 < U/U_{mf} < 4$, moderate $d_p$ | **Coalescence dominant** | Increases monotonically | Moderate to poor |
| $U/U_{mf} > 4$, large $d_p$ | **Coalescence–splitting balance** | Approaches $d_{b,\text{max}}$ | Variable |
| With vertical internals | **Splitting enhanced** | Stable, smaller | Excellent |
| With horizontal screens | **Forced splitting** | Decreases | Excellent |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Phenomenological Architecture of Coalescence and Splitting

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GAS INLET U greater than Umf] --> B[DISTRIBUTOR PLATE]
    B --> C[INITIAL BUBBLE FORMATION dbm]
    C --> D[BUBBLE PHASE REGION]

    D --> E[BUBBLE COALESCENCE ENGINE]
    D --> F[BUBBLE SPLITTING ENGINE]

    E --> E1[Wake Entrainment]
    E1 --> E2[Trailing Bubble Capture]
    E2 --> E3[Film Drainage]
    E3 --> E4[Membrane Rupture]
    E4 --> E5[Larger Daughter Bubble db increases]

    F --> F1[Turbulent Eddy Shear]
    F1 --> F2[Particle Impact Piercing]
    F2 --> F3[Surface Instability]
    F3 --> F4[Daughter Bubble Formation]
    F4 --> F5[Smaller Bubbles db decreases]

    E5 --> G{BUBBLE EQUILIBRIUM CHECK db vs dbmax}
    F5 --> G

    G -->|db less than dbmax| H[COALESCENCE CONTINUES]
    G -->|db greater than dbmax| I[SPLITTING TRIGGERED]
    G -->|db equals dbmax| J[STABLE EQUILIBRIUM STATE]

    H --> K[BUBBLE SIZE PROFILE PREDICTION]
    I --> K
    J --> K

    K --> L[OUTPUT: REACTOR PERFORMANCE METRICS]
    L --> L1[Conversion Efficiency]
    L --> L2[Gas Solid Contact Time]
    L --> L3[Heat and Mass Transfer Coefficients]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence — Coalescence of Two Bubbles (Stepwise)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1([STAGE 1: APPROACH]):::stage --> S2([STAGE 2: WAKE CAPTURE]):::stage
    S2 --> S3([STAGE 3: FILM THINNING]):::stage
    S3 --> S4([STAGE 4: FILM RUPTURE]):::stage
    S4 --> S5([STAGE 5: MERGER COMPLETE]):::stage

    S1 -.- S1A[Trailing bubble catches up to leading bubble due to higher rise velocity]
    S2 -.- S2A[Trailing bubble enters low pressure wake of leading bubble]
    S3 -.- S3A[Dense phase film between bubbles thins under capillary forces]
    S4 -.- S4A[Critical film thickness breached, surface tension fails]
    S5 -.- S5A[Two bubbles merge into single larger bubble with conserved gas volume]

    classDef stage fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Splitting Mechanisms Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[BUBBLE SPLITTING IN FLUIDIZED BEDS] --> M1[HYDRODYNAMIC SPLITTING]
    ROOT --> M2[MECHANICAL SPLITTING]
    ROOT --> M3[THERMAL SPLITTING]

    M1 --> M1A[Turbulent Eddy Bombardment]
    M1 --> M1B[Rayleigh Taylor Instability]
    M1 --> M1C[Kelvin Helmholtz Shear]

    M2 --> M2A[Particle Impact Piercing]
    M2 --> M2B[Internals and Baffle Collision]
    M2 --> M2C[Wall Shear Induced Rupture]

    M3 --> M3A[Local Hot Spot Gas Expansion]
    M3 --> M3B[Phase Change Induced Shock]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Bubble Size Profile Along Bed Height

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H0([z = 0: Distributor]) --> H1([z = 0.2H: Coalescence zone])
    H1 --> H2([z = 0.5H: Equilibrium zone])
    H2 --> H3([z = 0.8H: Saturation zone])
    H3 --> H4([z = H: Bed surface])

    H0 -.- NOTE1[db equals dbm, all bubbles small]
    H1 -.- NOTE2[db grows rapidly with z, coalescence dominates]
    H2 -.- NOTE3[db approaches dbmax, balanced regime]
    H3 -.- NOTE4[db stabilizes near dbmax]
    H4 -.- NOTE5[Bubbles erupt at surface, db no longer relevant]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual interpretation**: The bubble diameter increases monotonically with bed height in the lower half (coalescence zone), then asymptotically approaches $d_{b,\text{max}}$ in the upper half (saturation zone). This profile is the basis for designing **tall vs shallow fluidized bed reactors**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**Q: Define bubble coalescence. With a neat sketch, explain how wake entrainment promotes bubble coalescence in a gas-solid fluidized bed.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Bubble coalescence is the process by which two or more bubbles rising through the dense phase of a fluidized bed merge to form a single, larger bubble due to differences in rise velocities and wake entrainment effects.

> **Wake Mechanism (2 marks):** A rising bubble creates a low-pressure **wake region** immediately above it that entrains solid particles. Trailing bubbles, which rise along streamlined paths, are drawn into this wake. Once captured, the thin dense-phase film separating the two bubbles drains under capillary forces until it ruptures, completing the merger. The wake fraction $f_w$ (volume of wake per unit volume of bubble) governs the rate of capture: higher $f_w$ → faster coalescence → larger equilibrium bubble size.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: What is the maximum stable bubble size in a fluidized bed? State the Mori-Wen correlation and explain the significance of the $(U - U_{mf})^2$ dependence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** The maximum stable bubble size $d_{b,\text{max}}$ is the bubble diameter beyond which splitting forces (turbulent eddies, particle impact) overwhelm coalescence forces, and the bubble can no longer grow.

> **Mori-Wen Correlation (1 mark):** 
> $$d_{b,\text{max}} = \frac{2.78}{g}\left(U - U_{mf}\right)^2$$

> **Significance of the squared dependence (1 mark):** Because the dependence is quadratic, doubling the excess gas velocity $(U - U_{mf})$ quadruples the maximum stable bubble size. This explains why **high-velocity fluidized beds** (e.g., circulating fluidized bed risers) experience severe gas bypassing and poor gas-solid contact unless internals or baffles are used to mechanically split the bubbles.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - 2024 Model]

**Q: (a)** Derive the **Kato-Wen bubble growth model** starting from the wake volume balance. Clearly state all assumptions and explain each derivation step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fluidized bed operates with $U = 0.45$ m/s, $U_{mf} = 0.05$ m/s, particle diameter $d_p = 350\,\mu m$, $\rho_p = 2500$ kg/m³, $\rho_g = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\mu_g = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, and column diameter $D_t = 0.5$ m. Calculate: **(i)** the wake fraction, **(ii)** the maximum stable bubble size, and **(iii)** the bubble diameter at a height of $z = 1.0$ m above the distributor. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the full derivation. Key steps:

1. **Assumption**: Bubbles grow by engulfing smaller bubbles in their wake.
2. **Wake volume per unit rise**: $dV_w/dz = f_w \cdot \pi d_b^2/4$ ........... (1 mark)
3. **Bubble volume derivative**: $dV_b/dd_b = \pi d_b^2/2$ ........... (1 mark)
4. **Mass balance on wake-entrained bubbles**: ratio of small bubble to leading bubble volume ........... (2 marks)
5. **Final equation derivation** (algebraic simplification) ........... (2 marks)
6. **Final boxed result**: $\dfrac{d(d_b)}{dz} = \dfrac{(U - U_{mf})\,f_w}{u_b\,(1 - f_w)}$ ........... (1 mark)

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute the Archimedes number (1 mark):**

$$\text{Ar} = \frac{\rho_g(\rho_p - \rho_g)g\,d_p^3}{\mu_g^2}$$

$$\text{Ar} = \frac{(1.2)(2500 - 1.2)(9.81)(350 \times 10^{-6})^3}{(1.8 \times 10^{-5})^2}$$

$$\text{Ar} = \frac{(1.2)(2498.8)(9.81)(4.2875 \times 10^{-14})}{3.24 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$\text{Ar} = \frac{1.2606 \times 10^{-7}}{3.24 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 389$$

**Step 2 — Compute wake fraction (1 mark):**

$$f_w = 0.0735 \cdot (389)^{0.425} \cdot (0.45/0.05)^{-0.35}$$

$$f_w = 0.0735 \cdot (389)^{0.425} \cdot (9)^{-0.35}$$

Calculate $(389)^{0.425}$:

$$\ln(389) = 5.964, \quad 0.425 \times 5.964 = 2.535, \quad e^{2.535} \approx 12.62$$

Calculate $(9)^{-0.35}$:

$$\ln(9) = 2.197, \quad -0.35 \times 2.197 = -0.769, \quad e^{-0.769} \approx 0.463$$

Therefore:

$$f_w = 0.0735 \times 12.62 \times 0.463 \approx 0.430$$

**Step 3 — Compute maximum stable bubble size (1 mark):**

$$d_{b,\text{max}} = \frac{2.78}{9.81}(0.45 - 0.05)^2 = \frac{2.78}{9.81}(0.16) \approx 0.0453 \text{ m}$$

$$d_{b,\text{max}} \approx 45.3 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute bubble rise velocity at this maximum (1 mark):**

$$u_b = 0.711 \sqrt{9.81 \times 0.0453} = 0.711 \sqrt{0.4444} = 0.711 \times 0.6667 \approx 0.474 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate Kato-Wen equation (3 marks):**

Starting from:

$$\frac{d(d_b)}{dz} = \frac{(U - U_{mf})\,f_w}{u_b\,(1 - f_w)}$$

Using $u_b = 0.711\sqrt{g\,d_b}$, we have:

$$\frac{d(d_b)}{dz} = \frac{0.40 \times 0.430}{0.711\sqrt{9.81 \cdot d_b} \times (1 - 0.430)} = \frac{0.172}{0.711 \times 3.132 \times \sqrt{d_b}}$$

$$\frac{d(d_b)}{dz} = \frac{0.0773}{\sqrt{d_b}}$$

Separating variables and integrating from $d_{bm}$ to $d_b(z)$ and $0$ to $z$:

$$\int_{d_{bm}}^{d_b} \sqrt{d_b}\, d(d_b) = 0.0773 \int_0^z dz$$

$$\frac{2}{3}\left[d_b^{3/2} - d_{bm}^{3/2}\right] = 0.0773 \cdot z$$

**Step 6 — Compute $d_{bm}$ using Davidson-Harrison correlation (0.5 mark):**

For $D_t = 0.5$ m, $N_0 \approx 100$ orifices/m²:

$$d_{bm} = 0.00376 \left(U - U_{mf}\right)^{0.4} = 0.00376 \times (0.40)^{0.4} \approx 0.0027 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $d_b$ at $z = 1.0$ m (0.5 mark):**

$$d_b^{3/2} = d_{bm}^{3/2} + 1.5 \times 0.0773 \times 1.0$$

$$d_b^{3/2} = (0.0027)^{1.5} + 0.116$$

$$(0.0027)^{1.5} = 4.43 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$d_b^{3/2} \approx 0.116$$

$$d_b = (0.116)^{2/3} \approx 0.237 \text{ m} \approx 237 \text{ mm}$$

> **Note**: This value is much larger than $d_{b,\text{max}} \approx 45$ mm, indicating that the simple Kato-Wen model **overpredicts** at high $z$. In practice, the bubble size saturates at $d_{b,\text{max}}$ due to splitting. The actual $d_b \approx d_{b,\text{max}} = 45$ mm. This is a critical insight for reactor design.

**Final Answer Summary (Question A):** $f_w \approx 0.43$, $d_{b,\text{max}} \approx 45.3$ mm, $d_b$ at $z = 1$ m is bounded by $d_{b,\text{max}} \approx 45$ mm.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Internal Choice]

**Q: (a)** Explain the **mechanisms of bubble splitting** in gas-solid fluidized beds. Discuss the role of **turbulent eddy shedding, particle impact, and surface instability** in determining the maximum stable bubble size. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **variation of bubble size with height** in a bubbling fluidized bed. Identify the **coalescence zone, equilibrium zone, and saturation zone**, and explain the engineering significance of each. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Definition of bubble splitting (1 mark):** Bubble splitting is the rupture of a single bubble into two or more smaller daughter bubbles due to disruptive forces exceeding the surface tension restoring force.

2. **Mechanism 1 — Turbulent eddy shedding (2 marks):** In the dense phase of a fluidized bed, the gas-solid suspension behaves as a turbulent fluid. Eddies of characteristic size $l_e$ shed from the dense phase and bombard the rising bubble. When the eddy kinetic energy exceeds the bubble surface energy $(4\pi r_b^2 \sigma)$, the bubble ruptures. This mechanism dominates for bubbles with $d_b > d_{b,\text{max}}$.

3. **Mechanism 2 — Particle impact (2 marks):** Solid particles accelerated by the local gas flow can pierce the bubble roof like a needle. When the kinetic energy of an impacting particle exceeds the work of adhesion, the bubble splits. This mechanism is significant for Geldart Group D particles (large, heavy).

4. **Mechanism 3 — Surface instability (1 mark):** At high local voidage gradients, the bubble interface becomes unstable (Rayleigh-Taylor or Kelvin-Helmholtz type). The interface undulations grow until pinch-off occurs, splitting the bubble.

5. **Maximum stable bubble size synthesis (1 mark):** The Mori-Wen correlation $d_{b,\text{max}} = 2.78(U - U_{mf})^2 / g$ represents the diameter at which splitting and coalescence rates are equal. Below this, coalescence wins; above, splitting dominates.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **Sketch description (2 marks):** Draw a tall vertical column with distributor at bottom. Plot $d_b$ (y-axis) vs height $z$ (x-axis). The curve starts at $d_{bm}$ (small), rises steeply in the lower zone, then asymptotically approaches $d_{b,\text{max}}$ in the upper zone.

2. **Zone 1 — Coalescence zone ($0 < z < 0.3H$) (2 marks):** Near the distributor, small initial bubbles grow rapidly by wake-entrainment coalescence. This zone is short but intense. The Kato-Wen equation governs bubble growth here.

3. **Zone 2 — Equilibrium zone ($0.3H < z < 0.7H$) (1.5 marks):** Bubble size continues to grow but at a decreasing rate. Coalescence is partially balanced by incipient splitting. The bubble size distribution broadens.

4. **Zone 3 — Saturation zone ($z > 0.7H$) (1 mark):** Bubbles reach $d_{b,\text{max}}$ and remain roughly constant. Splitting equals coalescence. This is the most predictable region for design calculations.

5. **Engineering significance (0.5 mark):** Designers prefer **tall beds in the saturation zone** to ensure predictable performance. **Shallow beds** operate in the coalescence zone, leading to height-dependent conversion and scale-up issues.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Unit mismatch in Archimedes number**: Always convert $d_p$ from $\mu m$ to **metres** before cubing. A student who uses $d_p$ in $\mu m$ in the cube term gets $\text{Ar}$ wrong by 18 orders of magnitude.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the wake fraction correction**: The Kato-Wen equation has the term $(1 - f_w)$ in the denominator. Students frequently drop it, leading to 30–50% errors in $d_b$ predictions.
> 
> 3. **Misapplying the maximum bubble size**: The Mori-Wen $d_{b,\text{max}}$ is an **asymptote**, not the bubble size at a given height. Students who set $d_b = d_{b,\text{max}}$ for any $z$ lose 2–3 marks.
> 
> 4. **Confusing $u_b$ correlations**: The Wallis equation ($0.711\sqrt{g\,d_b}$) applies to **isolated** bubbles. In a dense swarm, use the Davidson-Yates form $u_b = (U - U_{mf}) + 0.711\sqrt{g\,d_b}$.
> 
> 5. **Ignoring the saturation effect**: At $z > 0.7H$, bubble size does **not** keep growing. The simple Kato-Wen integral overpredicts. Mention this explicitly to earn full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Coalescence and Splitting of Bubbles**

- ☐ **Coalescence** = merger of 2+ bubbles → larger bubble → reduced interfacial area
- ☐ **Splitting** = rupture of 1 bubble → smaller bubbles → increased interfacial area
- ☐ **Wake fraction** $f_w$ is the **engine of coalescence** (Kato-Wen: $f_w = 0.0735\,\text{Ar}^{0.425}(U/U_{mf})^{-0.35}$)
- ☐ **Kato-Wen growth equation**: $d(d_b)/dz = (U - U_{mf})f_w / [u_b(1 - f_w)]$ — **must know for 14-mark derivations**
- ☐ **Mori-Wen max size**: $d_{b,\text{max}} = 2.78(U - U_{mf})^2/g$ — **quadratic in excess velocity**
- ☐ **Wallis isolated bubble velocity**: $u_b = 0.711\sqrt{g\,d_b}$
- ☐ **Davidson-Yates in-swarm velocity**: $u_b = (U - U_{mf}) + 0.711\sqrt{g\,d_b}$
- ☐ **Archimedes number**: $\text{Ar} = \rho_g(\rho_p - \rho_g)g\,d_p^3/\mu_g^2$ — always SI units
- ☐ **Bubble profile has 3 zones**: coalescence (bottom), equilibrium (middle), saturation (top, $d_b \to d_{b,\text{max}}$)
- ☐ **Industrial control**: Vertical internals & fine particles → suppress coalescence; horizontal screens → promote splitting
- ☐ **Coalescence splits** occur in **Geldart B and D** powders; negligible in Geldart A (no visible bubbles) and impossible in Geldart C (channelling)
- ☐ **Frequent mistake to avoid**: The Kato-Wen equation is a **differential form**; integrate it correctly with proper initial condition $d_b(0) = d_{bm}$
- ☐ **Examiner favourite**: "Compare Kato-Wen and Mori-Wen approaches" — know that Kato-Wen is **deterministic/differential**, while Mori-Wen is **stochastic/population balance**
- ☐ **Conversion impact**: Excessive coalescence → gas bypassing → lower reactant conversion in FCC and acrylonitrile reactors
- ☐ **Memory tip**: "**C**oalescence makes bubbles **C**hunky (big); **S**plitting keeps them **S**mall"
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bubble Formation above Distributor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bubble Formation above Distributor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bubble Formation above the Distributor** is the fundamental phenomenon in gas–solid fluidization wherein, when a gas is introduced into a bed of solid particles through a perforated or porous distributor plate at velocities exceeding the **minimum fluidization velocity ($U_{mf}$)** and the **minimum bubbling velocity ($U_{mb}$)**, the gas issues as discrete **jets** from the orifices, penetrates into the bed to a characteristic **jet penetration height ($L_j$)**, and then collapses to form discrete **bubbles** that rise through the particulate phase. The dynamics of this region are critical because the distributor region governs the initial bubble size, bubble frequency, gas distribution uniformity, and consequently the overall performance of the fluidized bed reactor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The distributor region (typically the bottom 10–30% of the bed height) is treated as a separate, distinct hydrodynamic zone where the two-phase theory of fluidization originates. The bubble properties in this zone strongly influence the entire reactor.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **kitchen faucet dripping water into a thick milkshake**. When you slowly open the tap (low flow rate), the water seeps quietly through the dense liquid without disturbance — analogous to gas flowing in the interstices of particles in a *particulate fluidization* regime. As you turn the tap further, the water emerges as a small stream (jet) that pushes into the milkshake, then breaks off into droplets (bubbles) that rise to the surface. The point at which droplets start pinching off from the stream is exactly what we call **bubble formation above the distributor**!

**Another Analogy – Boiling Water:** Think of a pot of water on a stove. The heating element (distributor) supplies energy (gas). Below a certain temperature (sub-cooled regime), no bubbles form. Once the temperature crosses the saturation point (minimum bubbling velocity), bubbles nucleate at specific sites (orifices) and rise. In the same way, gas bubbles form at the distributor orifices of a fluidized bed.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Key Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Values used in KTU 2024 Numerical Problems:**
> - Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$
> - Atmospheric pressure: $P_{atm} = 101.325 \, kPa$
> - Air density at STP: $\rho_g = 1.2 \, kg/m^3$
> - Typical particle density: $\rho_p = 1000$ to $3000 \, kg/m^3$
> - Bed voidage at minimum fluidization: $\epsilon_{mf} = 0.4$ to $0.6$ (Geldart Group B and D)
> - Wake fraction of a bubble: $\alpha_w = 0.2$ to $0.4$ (typically taken as **0.3**)

## 1.4 Why is Bubble Formation Above Distributor Important?

| Aspect | Significance |
|---|---|
| **Gas Distribution Uniformity** | Determines whether the bed operates isothermally |
| **Initial Bubble Size** | Sets the lower bound for bubble size in the bed |
| **Bubble Frequency** | Controls gas–solid contacting efficiency |
| **Entrainment of Fines** | Influences solid loss and dust formation |
| **Erosion & Attrition** | High-velocity jets cause particle attrition and tube erosion |
| **Reactor Scale-Up** | Bubble dynamics dictate design of industrial fluidized beds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The distributor acts as the **birth-place of all bubbles** in a freely-bubbling fluidized bed. The size of bubbles born here largely controls the bed's hydrodynamic regime, mass transfer coefficients, and heat transfer rates.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Bubble Formation — Stepwise Logic

The formation of bubbles above a distributor occurs through a well-defined sequence of events. The process can be broken down into the following structured logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Gas Entry into Plenum Chamber:** The fluidizing gas is delivered to a plenum chamber beneath the distributor, where its pressure and velocity are stabilized. The pressure drop across the distributor $\Delta P_d$ must be sufficiently large to ensure uniform distribution.

- **Step 2 — Sub-Ohmic Flow (Pre-Bubble Regime):** When $U_0 < U_{mb}$, the gas passes through the interstices between particles, and the bed behaves as a *particulate* (homogeneous) fluidization. No discrete bubbles exist.

- **Step 3 — Jet Initiation at Orifice:** At $U_0 \geq U_{mb}$, the gas emerges from each orifice as a discrete **jet**, also called an *in-line cavity* or *permanent cavity*. The jet has a velocity approximately equal to the orifice velocity $u_{or}$.

- **Step 4 — Jet Penetration into Bed:** The jet penetrates into the bed to a depth $L_j$, which is determined by the balance between the jet's **momentum flux** and the **buoyant/dissipative forces** of the surrounding particulate phase.

- **Step 5 — Jet Collapse and Bubble Detachment:** At the tip of the jet, the jet loses coherence. Two scenarios are observed experimentally:
  - **Case A (Internal Bubble Formation):** Bubbles form *within* the jet itself, due to gas–solid instabilities.
  - **Case B (External Bubble Formation):** The jet pinches off at its base, releasing a single bubble that detaches and rises.

- **Step 6 — Bubble Rise, Coalescence, and Eruption:** The newly formed bubbles rise, coalesce with neighbouring bubbles, and eventually erupt at the bed surface, causing the familiar bubbling action.

## 2.2 Two-Phase Theory of Fluidization at the Distributor

According to the **Two-Phase Theory** (originally proposed by Toomey and Johnstone, 1952), the total superficial gas velocity is split between two phases:

$$U_0 = U_{emulsion} + U_{bubble}$$

- The **emulsion phase** carries gas at the minimum fluidization velocity, $U_{emulsion} = U_{mf}$.
- The **bubble phase** carries the *excess* gas:

$$U_{bubble} = U_0 - U_{mf}$$

This excess gas concentrates into discrete bubbles, which form first at the distributor orifices and rise through the bed.

## 2.3 The Concept of Jet Penetration Depth ($L_j$)

The **jet penetration depth (or jet height)** is the axial distance from the distributor plate to the point at which the jet collapses into bubbles. It is a critical design parameter because:

- If $L_j$ is too **small**, the gas does not distribute well horizontally; large pockets of stagnant gas form.
- If $L_j$ is too **large**, the high-velocity jet impinges directly on the bed, causing severe erosion, particle attrition, and back-mixing.

## 2.4 Wake Concept

When a bubble rises through a fluidized bed, it drags a wake of particles (and some gas) beneath it. This **wake** typically occupies **20–40 % of the bubble volume**. At the distributor, the bubble first forms, and its wake contains particles that have been carried down from the jet region.

- **Wake fraction** $f_w = V_{wake}/V_{bubble} \approx 0.2$ to $0.4$

The wake concept is essential because particles circulate through the bed via bubble wakes, providing excellent gas–solid contact.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates the **high-yield formulas** required for KTU 2024 university exam questions on Bubble Formation above the Distributor.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Validity / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Orifice Velocity** | $u_{or} = U_0 \cdot \dfrac{A_{bed}}{N_b \cdot A_{or}}$ | $u_{or}$ in $m/s$, $A_{bed}$ = bed cross-section in $m^2$, $N_b$ = no. of orifices, $A_{or}$ = orifice area in $m^2$ | Used to find gas velocity through each hole |
| 2 | **Two-Phase Theory** | $U_0 = U_{emulsion} + U_{bubble}$ | All velocities in $m/s$ | $U_{emulsion} \approx U_{mf}$ |
| 3 | **Basu Correlation (1975)** for Jet Penetration | $\dfrac{L_j}{d_{or}} = 7.45 \left[\dfrac{\rho_g u_{or}^2}{g \, d_{or} \, \rho_p (1 - \epsilon_{mf})}\right]^{0.5} - 38.5$ | $L_j, d_{or}$ in $m$; $\rho_g, \rho_p$ in $kg/m^3$; $u_{or}$ in $m/s$ | Most commonly tested in KTU; valid for $d_{or} \geq 0.002$ m |
| 4 | **Zenz Correlation (1968)** | $\dfrac{L_j}{d_{or}} = 1.05 \left[\dfrac{\rho_g u_{or}^2}{g \, d_{or} (\rho_p - \rho_g)}\right]^{0.5} - 0.5$ | Same as above | Earlier correlation; less accurate for small orifices |
| 5 | **Merry Correlation (1971)** | $\dfrac{L_j}{d_{or}} = 5.2 \left[\dfrac{\rho_g u_{or}^2}{g \, d_{or} (\rho_p - \rho_g)}\right]^{0.5} - 4$ | Same as above | Accounts for jet stability effects |
| 6 | **Davidson–Harrison Bubble Velocity** | $u_b = (U_0 - U_{mf}) + 0.711 \sqrt{g \, d_b}$ | $u_b, U_0, U_{mf}$ in $m/s$; $d_b$ = bubble diameter in $m$ | For a single isolated bubble |
| 7 | **Bubble Volume at Orifice** | $V_b = Q \cdot t$ | $V_b$ in $m^3$, $Q$ = volumetric flow in $m^3/s$, $t$ = time in $s$ | During the growth phase |
| 8 | **Bubble Radius (Spherical)** | $r_b = \left(\dfrac{3 V_b}{4 \pi}\right)^{1/3}$ | $r_b$ in $m$ | Assumes spherical geometry |
| 9 | **Distributor Pressure Drop Criterion** | $\Delta P_{dist} = (0.1 \text{ to } 0.3) \cdot \Delta P_{bed}$ | Both in $Pa$ | Design rule of thumb for uniform distribution |
| 10 | **Wake Fraction** | $f_w = V_{wake}/V_b \approx 0.2 \text{ to } 0.4$ | Dimensionless | Used in bubble-wake models |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024, when an absolute value is required, use the form $\lvert x \rvert$ (written as `\lvert` in LaTeX) or the descriptive phrase "magnitude of $x$". Never use the bare vertical bar `|` inside markdown tables, as it breaks the table syntax.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The bubble formation phenomenon above the distributor governs the performance of critical industrial processes, including:

- **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC) Units:** Bubble size at the distributor determines catalyst circulation rates and product yield.
- **Coal Combustion in CFB Boilers:** Bubble dynamics influence heat transfer to in-bed tubes.
- **Drying of Granular Materials:** Uniform bubble formation ensures even moisture removal.
- **Polymerization Reactors (Gas-Phase):** Bubble size controls monomer conversion and polymer morphology.
- **Waste-to-Energy Gasifiers:** Distributor jet penetration affects char conversion efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Basu Correlation for Jet Penetration Depth

The Basu correlation (1975) is the most widely used correlation in KTU textbooks. The derivation is based on **dimensional analysis** combined with experimental data fitting.

### Step 1: Identify the Governing Variables

The jet penetration depth $L_j$ depends on:

$$L_j = f(d_{or}, u_{or}, \rho_g, \rho_p, g, \epsilon_{mf})$$

- $d_{or}$ — orifice diameter
- $u_{or}$ — orifice (jet) velocity
- $\rho_g$ — gas density
- $\rho_p$ — particle density
- $g$ — gravitational acceleration
- $\epsilon_{mf}$ — bed voidage at minimum fluidization

### Step 2: Apply Buckingham Pi Theorem

There are 6 variables and 3 fundamental dimensions (M, L, T), so we expect **3 dimensionless groups**. The chosen groups are:

$$\Pi_1 = \frac{L_j}{d_{or}}, \quad \Pi_2 = \frac{\rho_g u_{or}^2}{g \, d_{or} \, \rho_p (1 - \epsilon_{mf})}, \quad \Pi_3 = \text{others absorbed}$$

### Step 3: Form the Functional Relationship

$$\frac{L_j}{d_{or}} = f\left[\frac{\rho_g u_{or}^2}{g \, d_{or} \, \rho_p (1 - \epsilon_{mf})}\right]$$

### Step 4: Assume a Power-Law Form

$$\frac{L_j}{d_{or}} = A \left[\frac{\rho_g u_{or}^2}{g \, d_{or} \, \rho_p (1 - \epsilon_{mf})}\right]^{B} + C$$

### Step 5: Fit Experimental Data (Basu, 1975)

From experiments with a wide range of particle sizes and gas velocities, Basu obtained:

$$A = 7.45, \quad B = 0.5, \quad C = -38.5$$

This gives the final form:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{L_j}{d_{or}} = 7.45 \left[\dfrac{\rho_g u_{or}^2}{g \, d_{or} \, \rho_p (1 - \epsilon_{mf})}\right]^{0.5} - 38.5}$$

### Step 6: Logical Interpretation

- The term under the bracket is the **dimensionless jet momentum number**, also called $F$ or jet Froude number.
- The **7.45** coefficient represents the proportionality between jet height and momentum flux.
- The **−38.5** offset accounts for the minimum jet height below which the correlation becomes physically meaningless (a jet of negative height is impossible).
- The condition $L_j > 0$ requires the bracketed term to be greater than $(38.5/7.45)^2 \approx 26.7$.

## 3.2 Worked Example — KTU Numerical Pattern

**Problem (Typical KTU 2024 Model):**

A fluidized bed combustor has the following specifications:

- Particle diameter: $d_p = 300 \, \mu m$
- Particle density: $\rho_p = 2400 \, kg/m^3$
- Gas density: $\rho_g = 1.2 \, kg/m^3$
- Orifice diameter: $d_{or} = 6 \, mm = 0.006 \, m$
- Orifice velocity: $u_{or} = 45 \, m/s$
- Bed voidage at minimum fluidization: $\epsilon_{mf} = 0.5$
- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$

**Calculate the jet penetration depth using the Basu correlation.**

### Solution Steps

**Step A — Compute the dimensionless group inside the bracket:**

$$\Phi = \frac{\rho_g u_{or}^2}{g \, d_{or} \, \rho_p (1 - \epsilon_{mf})}$$

Numerator:

$$\rho_g u_{or}^2 = 1.2 \times (45)^2 = 1.2 \times 2025 = 2430 \, kg/(m \cdot s^2)$$

Denominator:

$$g \, d_{or} \, \rho_p (1 - \epsilon_{mf}) = 9.81 \times 0.006 \times 2400 \times (1 - 0.5)$$

$$= 9.81 \times 0.006 \times 2400 \times 0.5 = 70.632 \, kg/(m \cdot s^2)$$

Therefore:

$$\Phi = \frac{2430}{70.632} = 34.41$$

**Step B — Take the square root:**

$$\Phi^{0.5} = \sqrt{34.41} = 5.866$$

**Step C — Apply the Basu correlation:**

$$\frac{L_j}{d_{or}} = 7.45 \times 5.866 - 38.5$$

$$= 43.70 - 38.5 = 5.20$$

**Step D — Solve for $L_j$:**

$$L_j = 5.20 \times 0.006 = 0.0312 \, m = 31.2 \, mm$$

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{L_j = 31.2 \, mm}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Correlations

The following matrix summarizes the **conditions of applicability** of various jet penetration correlations frequently tested in KTU exams.

| Correlation | Form | Best Suited For | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Zenz (1968)** | $\frac{L_j}{d_{or}} = 1.05 \left[\frac{\rho_g u_{or}^2}{g d_{or}(\rho_p - \rho_g)}\right]^{0.5} - 0.5$ | Large orifices, Geldart Group D particles | Unreliable for $d_{or} < 5$ mm |
| **Basu (1975)** | $\frac{L_j}{d_{or}} = 7.45 \left[\frac{\rho_g u_{or}^2}{g d_{or} \rho_p (1-\epsilon_{mf})}\right]^{0.5} - 38.5$ | Wide range, Geldart B particles | Requires $L_j > 0$ |
| **Merry (1971)** | $\frac{L_j}{d_{or}} = 5.2 \left[\frac{\rho_g u_{or}^2}{g d_{or}(\rho_p - \rho_g)}\right]^{0.5} - 4$ | Intermediate range, jet stability | Limited data on fines |
| **Yang & Keairns (1978)** | $\frac{L_j}{d_p} = 0.04 \left(\frac{u_{or}^2}{g d_p}\right)^{0.42} \left(\frac{\rho_g}{\rho_p}\right)^{0.34} \left(\frac{d_{or}}{d_p}\right)^{0.59}$ | FCC catalysts, Geldart A particles | Particle-diameter based |

## 3.4 Derivation of the Davidson–Harrison Bubble Velocity

For a single isolated bubble rising in an incipiently fluidized bed, **Davidson and Harrison (1963)** proposed the following model. The pressure inside the bubble equals the hydrostatic pressure of the surrounding emulsion phase at the same elevation.

### Step 1: Hydrodynamic Balance

The bubble behaves as a void with no internal solids. The emulsion phase behaves as an inviscid fluid of density $\rho_{emulsion} \approx \rho_p(1 - \epsilon_{mf})$.

### Step 2: Stream Function Approach

Davidson used potential flow theory to derive the gas velocity field around the bubble. The bubble rises with velocity $u_b$ relative to the wall.

### Step 3: Final Result

The Davidson–Harrison model gives the **bubble rise velocity** as:

$$u_b = (U_0 - U_{mf}) + 0.711 \sqrt{g \, d_b}$$

Where:
- $(U_0 - U_{mf})$ is the superficial gas velocity in the bubble phase
- $0.711 \sqrt{g d_b}$ is the rise velocity of a single bubble in a stagnant fluid

This equation is the **starting point** for most KTU derivations involving bubble dynamics in fluidized beds.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Bubble Formation above Distributor

The following Mermaid diagram presents the **process flow architecture** of bubble formation, illustrating the sequential interaction between the gas feed system, distributor, jet dynamics, and bubble formation in the dense phase of the fluidized bed.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GAS INLET PLENUM] --> B[PRESSURE STABILIZATION CHAMBER]
    B --> C[PERFORATED DISTRIBUTOR PLATE]
    C --> D[GAS JET EMISSION AT ORIFICE]
    D --> E[JET PENETRATION ZONE L J]
    E --> F[JET COLLAPSE MECHANISM]
    F --> G[BUBBLE NUCLEATION]
    G --> H[BUBBLE RISE IN DENSE PHASE]
    H --> I[WAKE FORMATION BENEATH BUBBLE]
    I --> J[BUBBLE COALESCENCE]
    J --> K[BUBBLE BURST AT FREEBOARD]
    K --> L[GAS EXIT TO CYCLONE OR STACK]
    
    M[PARTICLE BED PHASE] --> N[EMULSION PHASE AT U MF]
    N --> H
    F --> N
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style C fill:#fff4e1
    style E fill:#ffe1e1
    style G fill:#f4e1ff
    style H fill:#e1ffe1
    style K fill:#fffae1
    style M fill:#f0f0f0
```

## 4.2 Schematic Annotation — Key Physical Zones

The above architecture reveals three critical physical zones in a fluidized bed near the distributor:

| Zone | Physical Description | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Jet Zone (0 to $L_j$)** | High-velocity gas cavity surrounded by emulsion | Erosion zone, attrition zone |
| **Bubbling Zone ($L_j$ to $H_{bed}$)** | Discrete bubbles rising through emulsion | Reaction zone, mass transfer zone |
| **Freeboard (above $H_{bed}$)** | Bubbles erupt and disengage | Entrainment zone, fines loss zone |

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Jet Stability and Bubble Formation Mode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INITIAL STATE: GAS ENTERS ORIFICE] --> B{ORIFICE VELOCITY CHECK}
    B --> ULOW[U OR LESS THAN U MB]
    B --> UHIGH[U OR GREATER THAN U MB]
    
    ULOW --> C[PARTICULATE FLUIDIZATION]
    UHIGH --> D[CALCULATE L J USING BASU]
    D --> E{L J GREATER THAN ZERO}
    E --> YES[F1[JET FORMS AND PENETRATES BED]]
    E --> NO[F2[NO JET, HOMOGENEOUS EXPANSION]]
    F1 --> G{JET STABILITY CHECK}
    G --> STABLE[HIGH STABLE JET, BUBBLES FORM AT TIP]
    G --> UNSTABLE[OSCILLATING JET, INTERNAL BUBBLES]
    STABLE --> I[BUBBLE DETACHMENT]
    UNSTABLE --> I
    I --> J[BUBBLE ENTERS DENSE PHASE]
    J --> K[BUBBLE RISE COALESCENCE ERUPTION]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style D fill:#fff4e1
    style F1 fill:#ffe1e1
    style I fill:#f4e1ff
    style K fill:#e1ffe1
```

## 4.4 Functional Annotation of the Distributor Region

> [!NOTE]
> The **distributor region** is the first 10–30% of the bed height measured from the bottom. It is the only region where:
> 1. Bubbles are *created* (nucleation zone)
> 2. The jet dynamics dominate (high gas velocities)
> 3. Particle entrainment into bubbles is most intense
> 4. The two-phase theory is most directly applicable

The jet region must be carefully designed to:
- Provide uniform gas distribution
- Minimize particle attrition
- Avoid dead zones
- Ensure smooth transition from jet to bubble phase

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "jet penetration depth" in a fluidized bed. What is its significance?**

**Model Answer:**
Jet penetration depth ($L_j$) is defined as the axial distance from the distributor plate to the point where the high-velocity gas jet, emerging from an orifice, loses its coherence and collapses to form discrete bubbles.

**Significance:**
- Determines the height of the erosion/attrition zone above the distributor.
- Influences initial bubble size and bubble frequency.
- Affects gas distribution uniformity and overall reactor performance.
- Critical for distributor design and selection of distributor type.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for significance)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State the Basu correlation for jet penetration depth. List all variables with units.**

**Model Answer:**

$$\frac{L_j}{d_{or}} = 7.45 \left[\frac{\rho_g u_{or}^2}{g \, d_{or} \, \rho_p (1 - \epsilon_{mf})}\right]^{0.5} - 38.5$$

**Variables:**
- $L_j$ = jet penetration depth, $m$
- $d_{or}$ = orifice diameter, $m$
- $\rho_g$ = gas density, $kg/m^3$
- $\rho_p$ = particle density, $kg/m^3$
- $u_{or}$ = orifice velocity, $m/s$
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity, $9.81 \, m/s^2$
- $\epsilon_{mf}$ = bed voidage at minimum fluidization (dimensionless)

*(3 marks: 2 marks for correlation, 1 mark for variables/units)*

---

### Question 3 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**What is the "wake" of a bubble? State its typical volumetric fraction.**

**Model Answer:**
The wake of a bubble is the region of particles (and entrained gas) that is dragged along with a rising bubble, located immediately beneath the bubble. The wake is formed because of the Bernoulli effect — low pressure is created behind the rising bubble, sucking particles into the wake.

**Volumetric fraction:** $f_w = V_{wake}/V_{bubble} \approx 0.2$ to $0.4$ (typically taken as **0.3**).

*(3 marks: 2 marks for definition, 1 mark for volumetric fraction)*

---

### Question 4 `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Differentiate between particulate and aggregative fluidization.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Particulate Fluidization | Aggregative Fluidization |
|---|---|---|
| Gas–solid contact | Uniform, homogeneous | Non-uniform, with bubbles |
| Expansion | Smooth, continuous | Stepwise, with bubbling |
| Typical particles | Geldart Group A (small, light) | Geldart Group B and D (large, dense) |
| Example | Liquid-like fluidization | Gas–solid fluidization |
| Bubble formation | Absent | Prominent |

*(3 marks: 1 mark per row × 3 rows)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Module 3, 14 Marks]`

**a) [7 Marks]** Explain the mechanism of bubble formation above a perforated plate distributor with the help of a neat sketch. Discuss the two-phase theory of fluidization as applicable to the distributor region.

**b) [7 Marks]** A fluidized bed has the following data:
- $\rho_p = 2200 \, kg/m^3$, $\rho_g = 1.2 \, kg/m^3$
- $d_{or} = 5 \, mm$, $u_{or} = 40 \, m/s$
- $\epsilon_{mf} = 0.45$, $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$

Calculate the jet penetration depth using (i) Basu correlation and (ii) Zenz correlation. Compare the results.

---

### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Mechanism of Bubble Formation:**

The mechanism of bubble formation above a distributor involves the following steps:

1. **Gas Entry:** Fluidizing gas enters the plenum chamber below the distributor, where it is pressurized and stabilized.

2. **Jet Formation:** At velocities above the minimum bubbling velocity $U_{mb}$, gas emerges from each orifice as a discrete jet. The jet has a velocity $u_{or} = U_0 \cdot A_{bed}/(N_b \cdot A_{or})$.

3. **Jet Penetration:** The jet penetrates into the bed to a depth $L_j$. This depth is determined by the balance between jet momentum and resisting forces from the particulate phase.

4. **Jet Collapse:** At the tip of the jet, gas–solid instabilities cause the jet to break up. Two modes are observed:
   - **Mode I:** Bubbles form *within* the jet (internal bubbling).
   - **Mode II:** The jet pinches off at its base, releasing a discrete bubble (external bubbling).

5. **Bubble Rise:** The detached bubble rises through the emulsion phase, dragging a wake of particles.

**Two-Phase Theory at the Distributor:**

According to Toomey and Johnstone (1952), the total gas flow splits into two phases:
- **Emulsion phase:** Carries gas at velocity $U_{mf}$.
- **Bubble phase:** Carries excess gas at velocity $(U_0 - U_{mf})$.

$$U_0 = U_{emulsion} + U_{bubble} = U_{mf} + (U_0 - U_{mf})$$

At the distributor, the bubble phase is born as jets, which then collapse into rising bubbles. This two-phase concept forms the foundation of all KTU derivations on bubble dynamics.

*(7 marks: 3 marks for mechanism, 2 marks for sketch description, 2 marks for two-phase theory)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\rho_p = 2200 \, kg/m^3$, $\rho_g = 1.2 \, kg/m^3$
- $d_{or} = 5 \, mm = 0.005 \, m$, $u_{or} = 40 \, m/s$
- $\epsilon_{mf} = 0.45$, $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$

**(i) Basu Correlation:**

Compute the dimensionless group:

$$\Phi = \frac{\rho_g u_{or}^2}{g \, d_{or} \, \rho_p (1 - \epsilon_{mf})}$$

Numerator:

$$\rho_g u_{or}^2 = 1.2 \times (40)^2 = 1.2 \times 1600 = 1920 \, kg/(m \cdot s^2)$$

Denominator:

$$g \, d_{or} \, \rho_p (1 - \epsilon_{mf}) = 9.81 \times 0.005 \times 2200 \times (1 - 0.45)$$

$$= 9.81 \times 0.005 \times 2200 \times 0.55 = 59.37 \, kg/(m \cdot s^2)$$

Therefore:

$$\Phi = \frac{1920}{59.37} = 32.34$$

$$\Phi^{0.5} = \sqrt{32.34} = 5.687$$

Apply Basu:

$$\frac{L_j}{d_{or}} = 7.45 \times 5.687 - 38.5 = 42.37 - 38.5 = 3.87$$

$$L_j^{Basu} = 3.87 \times 0.005 = 0.01935 \, m = 19.35 \, mm$$

**(ii) Zenz Correlation:**

$$\Psi = \frac{\rho_g u_{or}^2}{g \, d_{or} (\rho_p - \rho_g)} = \frac{1920}{9.81 \times 0.005 \times (2200 - 1.2)} = \frac{1920}{107.85} = 17.80$$

$$\Psi^{0.5} = \sqrt{17.80} = 4.219$$

Apply Zenz:

$$\frac{L_j}{d_{or}} = 1.05 \times 4.219 - 0.5 = 4.43 - 0.5 = 3.93$$

$$L_j^{Zenz} = 3.93 \times 0.005 = 0.01965 \, m = 19.65 \, mm$$

**Comparison:**
- Basu: $L_j = 19.35 \, mm$
- Zenz: $L_j = 19.65 \, mm$
- Difference: ~1.5 % (excellent agreement)

The two correlations give very close results, confirming the reliability of both for these conditions.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for Basu calculation, 2 marks for Zenz calculation, 2 marks for comparison, 1 mark for final answer)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 3, 14 Marks – Alternative Choice]`

**a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the various types of distributors used in fluidized beds. State the design criteria for the distributor pressure drop.

**b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the Davidson–Harrison model of bubble formation at an orifice. Derive the expression for bubble rise velocity in an incipiently fluidized bed.

---

### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Types of Distributors:**

The distributor (also called *grid* or *plate*) is the device that introduces the fluidizing gas into the bed uniformly. Common types are:

1. **Perforated Plate Distributor:** A flat plate with drilled holes (1–10 mm diameter). Simple and cheap, but susceptible to weeping at low velocities and erosion at high velocities.

2. **Porous Plate Distributor:** A sintered metal or ceramic plate with fine pores. Provides excellent gas distribution but has high pressure drop and is expensive. Used for laboratory-scale units.

3. **Nozzle-Type Distributor:** Each orifice is fitted with a cap or nozzle (e.g., bubble caps, tuyere nozzles). Prevents solids from falling through the distributor and back-flow of gas.

4. **Pipe Grid Distributor:** A network of parallel pipes with holes drilled on top. Used in large industrial fluidized beds (e.g., FCC regenerators).

5. **Cone-Shaped Distributor:** Conical or pyramidal distributor used when the gas inlet is from the side.

**Design Criteria for Distributor Pressure Drop:**

The pressure drop across the distributor must be:

$$\Delta P_{dist} = (0.1 \text{ to } 0.3) \cdot \Delta P_{bed}$$

- **Lower limit (0.1):** Ensures gas distributes uniformly; below this, channelling and maldistribution occur.
- **Upper limit (0.3):** Above this, the blower must supply more energy, increasing operating costs.

This is known as the **"10 % to 30 % rule"** and is universally used in KTU-level distributor design.

*(7 marks: 3 marks for distributor types, 2 marks for sketch description, 2 marks for pressure drop criteria)*

---

**Part (b) — Davidson–Harrison Model:**

The Davidson–Harrison (1963) model describes the flow field around an isolated bubble rising in a fluidized bed. The key assumptions are:

1. The emulsion phase behaves as an **inviscid fluid** of density $\rho_e = \rho_p (1 - \epsilon_{mf})$.
2. The gas in the bubble is in **potential flow** with negligible viscosity.
3. The pressure inside the bubble equals the hydrostatic pressure of the surrounding emulsion at the same elevation.

**Derivation of Bubble Rise Velocity:**

Consider a single bubble of diameter $d_b$ rising in a stagnant fluidized bed (no net gas flow in the emulsion). Applying Bernoulli's equation and the potential flow solution for a sphere:

**Step 1 — Stream function for flow around a sphere:**

For potential flow around a sphere, the stream function is:

$$\psi = -\frac{u_b r^2 \sin^2\theta}{2} \left(1 - \frac{r_b^3}{r^3}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Apply boundary condition at infinity:**

At $r \to \infty$, the velocity approaches $-u_b$ (far-field), which is consistent with the stream function.

**Step 3 — Match velocity at bubble surface:**

At the bubble surface $r = r_b$, the radial velocity is zero (gas cannot cross the bubble boundary into the emulsion).

**Step 4 — Derive the rise velocity:**

For a sphere rising in an inviscid fluid under gravity, the balance of buoyancy and drag forces yields:

$$u_b = 0.711 \sqrt{g \, d_b}$$

This is the **single-bubble rise velocity**.

**Step 5 — Add the effect of net gas flow:**

When the emulsion phase has a net upward velocity $(U_0 - U_{mf})$, the bubble rise velocity is augmented:

$$\boxed{u_b = (U_0 - U_{mf}) + 0.711 \sqrt{g \, d_b}}$$

**Significance:**
- For small bubbles: $u_b \approx 0.711 \sqrt{g d_b}$ (capillary-bubble regime).
- For large bubbles: $u_b \approx (U_0 - U_{mf})$ (slug-flow regime).
- This is the foundational equation for all KTU bubble dynamics problems.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for assumptions, 3 marks for derivation steps, 2 marks for final equation and significance)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Examinations (Distributor/Bubble Formation Questions):**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Converting orifice diameter from $mm$ to $m$ is the #1 reason students lose 1–2 marks. Always state units explicitly in the final answer.
>
> 2. **Forgetting $(1 - \epsilon_{mf})$:** Many students write the Basu correlation as $\frac{\rho_g u_{or}^2}{g d_{or} \rho_p}$ instead of $\frac{\rho_g u_{or}^2}{g d_{or} \rho_p (1 - \epsilon_{mf})}$. This is a 2-mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Sign Error in $(ρ_p − ρ_g)$ vs. $ρ_p$:** The Zenz correlation uses $(\rho_p - \rho_g)$, while the Basu correlation uses $\rho_p (1 - \epsilon_{mf})$. Mixing them up leads to a 1-mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Sketch Quality:** A poorly labelled sketch (no clear arrow for jet, no $L_j$ marked) can cost 1–2 marks even if the written explanation is correct. Always include: (i) distributor, (ii) jet, (iii) $L_j$ marking, (iv) bubble, (v) emulsion phase.
>
> 5. **Bubble Wake Fraction:** Students often write $f_w = 0.5$ or $f_w = 1$ — the correct range is **0.2 to 0.4** (often taken as 0.3).
>
> 6. **Two-Phase Theory:** Writing $U_0 = U_{bubble}$ alone is incomplete. Always write $U_0 = U_{emulsion} + U_{bubble}$ and state $U_{emulsion} = U_{mf}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Bubble Formation above Distributor (Module 3)**

- **Distributor Functions:** Uniform gas distribution, support of bed weight, prevention of solids back-flow, pressure equalization.

- **Major Distributor Types:** Perforated plate, porous plate, nozzle-type, pipe grid, cone-shaped.

- **Distributor Pressure Drop Rule:** $\Delta P_{dist} = 0.1$ to $0.3 \cdot \Delta P_{bed}$ (the 10–30 % rule).

- **Orifice Velocity Formula:** $u_{or} = \dfrac{U_0 \cdot A_{bed}}{N_b \cdot A_{or}}$ — always in $m/s$.

- **Two-Phase Theory:** $U_0 = U_{mf} + (U_0 - U_{mf})$ — emulsion + bubble phases.

- **Basu Correlation (Most Tested):** $\dfrac{L_j}{d_{or}} = 7.45 \left[\dfrac{\rho_g u_{or}^2}{g \, d_{or} \, \rho_p (1 - \epsilon_{mf})}\right]^{0.5} - 38.5$

- **Zenz Correlation (Alternate):** $\dfrac{L_j}{d_{or}} = 1.05 \left[\dfrac{\rho_g u_{or}^2}{g \, d_{or} (\rho_p - \rho_g)}\right]^{0.5} - 0.5$

- **Merry Correlation (Alternate):** $\dfrac{L_j}{d_{or}} = 5.2 \left[\dfrac{\rho_g u_{or}^2}{g \, d_{or} (\rho_p - \rho_g)}\right]^{0.5} - 4$

- **Davidson–Harrison Bubble Velocity:** $u_b = (U_0 - U_{mf}) + 0.711 \sqrt{g \, d_b}$

- **Wake Fraction:** $f_w \approx 0.2$ to $0.4$ (typical **0.3**).

- **Physical Insight:** The jet zone (0 to $L_j$) is the bubble *birth region*; the bubbling zone ($L_j$ to $H_{bed}$) is the bubble *growth and coalescence* region; the freeboard is the *disengagement* region.

- **Bubble Size Hierarchy in Bed:** $d_b^{initial} < d_b^{rising} < d_b^{coalesced} < d_b^{erupting}$ (bubbles grow as they rise due to coalescence and decreasing hydrostatic pressure).

- **Critical Distributor Design Rule:** Always verify that the calculated $L_j$ is **physically realistic** ($L_j > 0$). If the Basu correlation gives $L_j \leq 0$, it means the orifice velocity is too low to form a stable jet — redesign the distributor.

- **Engineering Application Tip:** For industrial FCC units, the distributor jet penetration depth is typically **50 to 200 mm**. For CFB boilers, $L_j$ is usually **100 to 500 mm**.

- **Geldart Particle Classification Relevance:** Bubble formation is most vigorous in **Geldart Group B** (sand-like) and **Group D** (large/dense) particles. Group A (fine, aeratable) particles exhibit delayed bubble formation due to inter-particle forces.

- **Reactor Scale-Up Tip:** When scaling up, the orifice diameter $d_{or}$ is usually kept the same, but the **number of orifices** is increased to maintain the same superficial velocity — this ensures similar jet dynamics across scales.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Slug Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Slug Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Slug Flow** (also called **Slugging**) is a regime of heterogeneous gas–solid fluidization in which gas bubbles, having grown by coalescence, become comparable in size to the diameter of the fluidization column. When a single gas bubble (or a coalesced train of bubbles) occupies nearly the entire cross-section of the column, it behaves as a "slug" — a void region of gas that pushes the surrounding emulsion of particles upward as a coherent piston-like plug.

According to KTU (2024 Scheme) terminology (aligned with Kunii & Levenspiel, and Yates & Lettieri), slug flow is the extreme case of **bubbling fluidization** in which:

> **"A bubble becomes a slug when its diameter approaches the column diameter ($D_t$), transforming the bed into a sequence of alternating gas-rich (slug) and dense-phase (emulsion) regions."**

Two geometrically distinct sub-types are recognized:
1. **Axisymmetric (Hertzberg) Slug** – Round-nosed, axisymmetric gas pocket that rises along the centerline; dominant in small-diameter columns.
2. **Asymmetric (Wall / Flat) Slug** – A flat gas region hugging one wall while particles rain down the other; dominant in large-diameter columns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PECHT415, Module 3):**
> Students are expected to *identify* the onset of slugging from geometric criteria ($D_t/d_p$), *distinguish* between the two slug types, *predict* slug rise velocity, and *estimate* bed expansion under slugging conditions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are blowing air through a tall, transparent cylinder filled with fine sand.

- At **low flow**, the sand just loosens — this is **particulate (homogeneous) fluidization**.
- At **higher flow**, small air bubbles rise through the sand — this is **bubbling fluidization**.
- Now push more air. The bubbles merge and merge… until ONE giant air pocket fills almost the entire tube. It looks like a single bullet-shaped air bubble traveling upward, pushing a plug of sand ahead of it like a fist pushing through mud. The sand around it cascades downward in a thin film along the walls.

That giant air pocket is the **slug**. The whole column now behaves rhythmically — *push, push, push* — and the surface of the bed jerks up and down. This is **slug flow**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Significance:**
> - In **industrial fluidized-bed reactors (FCC, coal combustors)**, slugging is *unwanted* — it causes severe pressure fluctuations, poor gas–solid contact, and mechanical vibration.
> - However, in **slugging-mode airlift reactors and biotechnology contactors**, controlled slugging is *desired* because it gives excellent mixing and predictable plug-flow hydrodynamics.
> - The classical bench-scale experiment to **measure particle size** uses slugging frequency: count slugs per second, and you get $d_p$ from Stewart–Davidson correlation.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Industrial Value |
|----------|--------|--------------------------|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Column diameter for slug formation (rule) | $D_t$ | **≥ 20 × $d_p$** |
| Void fraction at minimum fluidization | $\varepsilon_{mf}$ | **0.4 – 0.6** |
| Slug rise velocity (cylindrical case) | $U_{sl}$ | **≈ 0.35 $\sqrt{g D_t}$** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework of Slug Formation

**Step 1 — Bubble Coalescence Begins**
- In a bubbling bed, bubbles rise at velocity $u_b = (0.711 \sqrt{g D_b})$.
- Bubbles of different sizes overtake one another → **coalescence**.
- The maximum stable bubble diameter $D_{b,\max}$ is set by the bed geometry: $D_{b,\max} \approx 0.65\,(A_t (u - u_{mf}))^{0.4}$ (Mori–Wen).

**Step 2 — Bubble Size Approaches Column Diameter**
- As $D_b$ approaches $D_t$, the bubble is no longer a "bubble" — the wall constrains it.
- The transition criterion is empirically:
$$
D_b \geq 0.6 \, D_t \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{Slug Flow Onset}
$$

**Step 3 — Slug Types Branch Out**
- If $D_t < \mathbf{0.10 \text{ m}}$: **axisymmetric (Hertzberg) slugs** dominate. Slug is a bullet shape with rounded nose.
- If $D_t > \mathbf{0.10 \text{ m}}$: **asymmetric (flat / wall) slugs** dominate. Slug is dome-shaped; particles rain down a chute on the other side.

**Step 4 — Slug Dynamics Become Piston-Like**
- A slug pushes the entire emulsion above it upward, then the particles above it collapse down as the slug bursts at the surface.
- This produces **rhythmic, large-amplitude pressure fluctuations** ($\Delta P$ swings) and **bed-surface heaving**.

**Step 5 — Bed Expansion in Slugging Beds**
- The total bed height $H$ is greater than that predicted by homogeneous fluidization.
- Using the two-region (slug + emulsion) model:
$$
H = H_{slug} + H_{emulsion}
$$

### 2.2 Why Each Step Happens — Physical Insight

- **Why do bubbles coalesce?** Because of hydrodynamic instability of bubbles larger than the critical Mori–Wen size.
- **Why does $D_t$ matter?** Because the wall breaks the spherical symmetry of bubbles above a critical size — the gas region is squeezed flat.
- **Why are slugs piston-like?** Because the high-velocity gas inside the slug has no escape except through the bottom (entering) and top (leaving), so it acts as a coherent void.
- **Why does this matter industrially?** Predicting slug velocity and frequency is essential for designing **gas distributors, cyclones (entrainment), and pressure-vessel mechanical supports**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute values to preserve Markdown table rendering.**

| # | Equation | Description | Validity / Domain |
|---|----------|-------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $D_b \geq 0.6 D_t$ | **Slug onset criterion** | Bubbling → Slugging transition |
| 2 | $U_{sl} = 0.35\,\sqrt{g D_t}\,\left(1 + \dfrac{D_b}{D_t}\right)^{3/2}$ | **Stewart–Davidson slug rise velocity** (most commonly tested) | Axisymmetric slugs, $D_b \le D_t$ |
| 3 | $U_{sl} = 0.35\,\sqrt{g D_t}$ | **Limiting case** for full-column-sized slug ($D_b = D_t$) | Reference value for design |
| 4 | $U_{sl} = 0.35\,\sqrt{2 g D_t}$ | **Matsen et al. correlation** (wall slugs, large $D_t$) | Asymmetric / wall slugs |
| 5 | $f_{sl} = \dfrac{u - u_{mf}}{H_{sl} - \alpha_s L_{sl}}$ | **Slug frequency** (slugging rate, Hz) | Steady slugging bed |
| 6 | $H = H_{sl} + H_e$ | **Total bed height** (two-region model) | Slugging regime |
| 7 | $L_{sl} \approx 2 D_t$ | **Typical slug length** | Empirical, axisymmetric slugs |
| 8 | $\varepsilon_{sl} = \dfrac{U_{sl}}{U_{sl} + U_{b\infty}}$ | **Voidage inside slug** | Approximate |
| 9 | $\Delta P_{bed} = (1 - \varepsilon_{mf})(\rho_s - \rho_g) g H$ | **Pressure drop across bed** | Even during slugging (carried by emulsion) |
| 10 | $H_{mf} = \dfrac{H(1 - \varepsilon_f)}{1 - \varepsilon_{mf}}$ | **Bed expansion relation** | Bubble-containing bed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility of these equations:**
> - Equations (2)–(4) → design of **cyclone / entrainment separators** above the bed.
> - Equation (5) → diagnostic tool: counting slug pulses = estimating $(u - u_{mf})$.
> - Equation (9) → sizing of **blowers and compressors**; it is essentially constant (independent of $u$) once fluidized.

### 2.4 Engineering Applications in Industry

| Application | Use of Slug Flow Theory |
|-------------|--------------------------|
| **FCC riser/regenerator** | Avoid slugging → design $D_t$ to keep $D_b < 0.6 D_t$ |
| **Coal/biomass combustors** | Slugging causes thermal hotspots and CO spikes — must be suppressed |
| **Slug-flow bioreactors** | Designed to operate *in* slugging mode for uniform gas–liquid–solid contact |
| **Drying of granular solids** | Slug mode gives pulsating contact — used for heat-sensitive materials |
| **Pneumatic conveying** | Transition from fluidized → slugging → plug → dilute transport |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Limiting Slug Velocity $U_{sl} = 0.35\sqrt{g D_t}$ (Stewart & Davidson Limit)

The full Stewart–Davidson (1967) slug velocity for an axisymmetric slug rising in a fluidized column of diameter $D_t$, with a bubble of diameter $D_b$ (capped at $D_t$ for a true slug), is:

$$
U_{sl} = 0.35\,\sqrt{g D_t}\,\left(1 + \dfrac{D_b}{D_t}\right)^{3/2}
$$

**Step 1 — Assume the gas pocket has grown to the full column size.**
For a true *slug*, $D_b \rightarrow D_t$. Substitute $D_b = D_t$:

$$
U_{sl} = 0.35\,\sqrt{g D_t}\,\left(1 + \dfrac{D_t}{D_t}\right)^{3/2}
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the bracketed term.**

$$
\left(1 + \dfrac{D_t}{D_t}\right)^{3/2} = (1 + 1)^{3/2} = 2^{3/2} = 2\sqrt{2}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* By definition of the exponent, $2^{3/2} = 2^{1} \cdot 2^{1/2} = 2\sqrt{2}$.

**Step 3 — Multiply the prefactor.**

$$
U_{sl} = 0.35 \cdot 2\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{g D_t} = 0.99\,\sqrt{g D_t} \approx \sqrt{g D_t}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* $0.35 \times 2 = 0.70$, and $0.70 \times \sqrt{2} = 0.70 \times 1.4142 = 0.9899 \approx 1.0$.

**Result (Matsen et al. form, asymmetric slug):**

$$
\boxed{U_{sl} \approx \sqrt{g D_t} \approx 0.35 \sqrt{2 g D_t}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** When the bubble fully spans the column, the slug rise velocity depends *only* on the column diameter and gravity — independent of $u$ and $d_p$! This is the **design-limiting** velocity used to size the freeboard.

### 3.2 Derivation of Slug Frequency

**Slug frequency** $f_{sl}$ (Hz) is the rate at which slugs pass a fixed elevation. By a simple volumetric balance at steady state:

$$
\text{Volumetric flow of gas in excess of } u_{mf} = \text{Volumetric flow carried by slugs}
$$

**Step 1 — Excess gas flow rate above minimum fluidization.**

$$
Q_{excess} = A_t (u - u_{mf})
$$

**Step 2 — Gas volume carried per slug.**
Each slug of length $L_{sl}$ and cross-section $A_t$ carries:

$$
V_{sl} = A_t \cdot L_{sl} \cdot \varepsilon_{sl}
$$

**Step 3 — Volume of dense-phase displaced per slug passage.**
As the slug passes, the emulsion of length $\alpha_s L_{sl}$ is displaced:

$$
V_{displaced} = A_t \cdot \alpha_s L_{sl} \cdot \varepsilon_{mf}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate volumetric flows.**

$$
A_t (u - u_{mf}) = f_{sl} \left(A_t L_{sl} \varepsilon_{sl} - A_t \alpha_s L_{sl} \varepsilon_{mf}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $A_t$ and solve for $f_{sl}$.**

$$
\boxed{f_{sl} = \dfrac{u - u_{mf}}{L_{sl}\left(\varepsilon_{sl} - \alpha_s \varepsilon_{mf}\right)} \approx \dfrac{u - u_{mf}}{H_{sl} - \alpha_s L_{sl}}}
$$

where $H_{sl}$ is the average slug-to-slug distance. For a *fully developed* slugging bed, $H_{sl} \approx L_{sl}$, simplifying to:

$$
f_{sl} \approx \dfrac{u - u_{mf}}{L_{sl}(1 - \alpha_s \varepsilon_{mf})}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* The product $\alpha_s L_{sl} \varepsilon_{mf}$ is the volume of solids per slug length; the difference $H_{sl} - \alpha_s L_{sl}$ is the "gas-rich" length per slug period.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Model Answer Style)

**Problem (KTU-style):** A fluidized bed of diameter $D_t = 0.15$ m operates at $u = 0.40$ m/s. The minimum fluidization velocity is $u_{mf} = 0.05$ m/s. Estimate (a) the slug rise velocity, (b) the slug frequency, assuming an average slug length of $L_{sl} = 0.30$ m, solid fraction in slug $\alpha_s = 0.01$, and $\varepsilon_{mf} = 0.5$.

**Part (a) — Slug velocity (using Matsen form, slug fully spans the column):**

$$
U_{sl} = 0.35\,\sqrt{2 g D_t} = 0.35\,\sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.15}
$$

$$
U_{sl} = 0.35\,\sqrt{2.943} = 0.35 \times 1.7154 = 0.600 \text{ m/s}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* $2 \times 9.81 = 19.62$; $19.62 \times 0.15 = 2.943$; $\sqrt{2.943} = 1.7154$; $0.35 \times 1.7154 = 0.6004$.

**Part (b) — Slug frequency:**

$$
f_{sl} = \dfrac{u - u_{mf}}{L_{sl}\,(1 - \alpha_s \varepsilon_{mf})}
$$

$$
f_{sl} = \dfrac{0.40 - 0.05}{0.30\,(1 - 0.01 \times 0.5)} = \dfrac{0.35}{0.30 \times 0.995}
$$

$$
f_{sl} = \dfrac{0.35}{0.2985} = 1.172 \text{ Hz}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* $u - u_{mf} = 0.35$ m/s; $\alpha_s \varepsilon_{mf} = 0.005$; $1 - 0.005 = 0.995$; denominator $= 0.2985$ m; $0.35/0.2985 = 1.172$ s⁻¹ = 1.172 Hz.

**Slug period:**

$$
T_{sl} = \dfrac{1}{f_{sl}} = 0.853 \text{ s/slug}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture of Slug Flow Dynamics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Gas Inlet at Distributor] --> B[Initial Bubble Formation Region]
    B --> C{Bubble Diameter vs Column Diameter}
    C -- D_b less than 0.6 D_t --> D[Bubbling Fluidization Regime]
    C -- D_b greater than or equal 0.6 D_t --> E[Slug Flow Regime Onset]
    E --> F{Slug Geometry Branch}
    F -- D_t less than 0.10 m --> G[Axisymmetric Hertzberg Slug]
    F -- D_t greater than 0.10 m --> H[Asymmetric Wall Flat Slug]
    G --> I[Slug Rise Velocity by Stewart Davidson]
    H --> J[Slug Rise Velocity by Matsen]
    I --> K[Slug Frequency and Bed Pressure Fluctuation]
    J --> K
    K --> L{Surface Rupture and Particle Ejection}
    L --> M[Freeboard Entrainment into Cyclone]
    K --> N[Mechanical Stress on Vessel and Distributor]
    L --> O[Gas Solid Contact Time Analysis]
    M --> P[Engineering Design Output]
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Reactor Scale and Distributor Sizing]
```
