```mermaid
    J -->|Approaching Surface| L[Apply Mori Wen Asymptotic Limit]
    K --> M[Final db z Reported]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Bubble Erupts at Bed Surface]
    N --> O[Throws Particles into Freeboard]
    O --> P[Entrainment to Cyclone]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#b45f06,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#7f6000,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#274e13,color:#ffffff
    style N fill:#990000,color:#ffffff
    style P fill:#4b0082,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Comparison Map of Bubble-Size Correlations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Bubble Size Correlations] --> B[Initial Size]
    A --> C[Axial Growth]
    A --> D[Asymptotic Limit]

    B --> B1[Fryer Potter: Perforated Plate]
    B --> B2[Harrison Davidson: Porous Plate]
    B --> B3[Lim Grace: General]

    C --> C1[Darton et al: Distributor Spacing]
    C --> C2[Mori Wen: Column Diameter]
    C --> C3[Rowe 1976: Empirical]
    C --> C4[Geldart 1972: Conservative]
    C --> C5[Werther 1978: Industrial]

    D --> D1[Mori Wen: db inf 0.652 At U Umf power 0.4]
    D --> D2[Harrison Davidson: 2 U mf squared over g]

    style A fill:#0b5394,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#cc0000,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#38761d,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#7f6000,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Mechanism Map: Bubble Coalescence vs Splitting

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Two Adjacent Bubbles Rising] --> B{Relative Rise Velocity}
    B -->|Faster Behind| C[Overtake Occurs]
    B -->|Faster Ahead| D[No Overtake]
    C --> E[Nose of Fast Bubble Touches Tail of Slow Bubble]
    E --> F[Particles in Wake Displaced]
    F --> G[Twin Bubble Configuration]
    G --> H{Merging Criterion}
    H -->|Satisfied| I[Single Coalesced Bubble Forms]
    H -->|Not Satisfied| J[Spitting into Smaller Bubbles]
    I --> K[New db Greater Than db1 and db2]
    J --> L[Daughter Bubbles: db3 and db4]
    L --> M[Resumes Independent Rise]
    K --> A
    M --> A

    style A fill:#073763,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#a64d79,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#666666,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#0b5394,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The third Mermaid diagram above captures the *only* two outcomes in the bubble interaction problem — **coalescence** (merger) or **splitting** (division). Real fluidized beds exhibit a dynamic equilibrium between these two, leading to the existence of a **maximum stable bubble size** $d_{b,\max}$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define initial bubble size $d_{b0}$. State the Fryer–Potter correlation for a perforated-plate distributor and explain the role of the term $A_o/N_0$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The *initial bubble size* $d_{b0}$ is the equivalent spherical diameter of the gas cavity at the instant it detaches from a distributor orifice. It is the starting point for all subsequent bubble-growth calculations.
- **Fryer–Potter formula (1 mark):**
$$
d_{b0} = 1.30\left[\frac{(U_0 - U_{mf})\,A_o}{N_0\,g}\right]^{0.4}
$$
- **Role of $A_o/N_0$ (1 mark):** It represents the **area of the distributor per active orifice**, which sets the lateral spacing of the bubble streams. Larger spacing delays coalescence, allowing smaller bubbles to persist higher into the bed.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]
**What is meant by the "maximum stable bubble size"? State the Harrison–Davidson criterion for it and discuss the physical significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The *maximum stable bubble size* $d_{b,\max}$ is the diameter above which a bubble in a fluidized bed is unstable and tends to split into two or more smaller bubbles.
- **Harrison–Davidson criterion (1 mark):** $d_{b,\max} = 2\,U_{mf}^{2}/g$
- **Physical significance (1 mark):** Bubbles of diameter greater than $d_{b,\max}$ split because the **pressure gradient across the bubble roof** can no longer be supported by the surrounding dense phase. Thus $d_{b,\max}$ acts as a natural upper limit on bubble size in freely bubbling fluidized beds.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examination Pattern (ESE Module-wise):** Each module contributes a 14-mark question. The question has **internal choice** between two completely independent alternatives. Sub-parts (a) and (b) are typically 7 marks each.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Module 3]

**A fluidized bed of diameter $1.0\,\text{m}$ uses a perforated plate distributor with $N_0 = 1000$ orifices of diameter $2.0\,\text{mm}$. The minimum fluidization velocity is $U_{mf} = 0.03\,\text{m/s}$ and the operating superficial gas velocity is $U = 0.20\,\text{m/s}$. The particle density is $\rho_s = 2500\,\text{kg/m}^3$ and gas density is $\rho_g = 1.2\,\text{kg/m}^3$. Use $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$.**

**(a)** Calculate the **initial bubble size** at the distributor using the Fryer–Potter correlation. Comment on the validity of the assumption that the bubble is spherical. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Darton et al. correlation**, estimate the bubble size at a height of $z = 0.5\,\text{m}$ above the distributor. Compare this value with the prediction of the **Mori–Wen model** at the same height, and discuss the discrepancy. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

*Step 1 — Compute orifice area $A_o$*

$$
A_o = \frac{\pi d_o^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.002)^2}{4} = 3.142 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2
$$

*Step 2 — Apply Fryer–Potter formula*

$$
d_{b0} = 1.30\left[\frac{(U_0 - U_{mf})\,A_o}{N_0\,g}\right]^{0.4} = 1.30\left[\frac{(0.20 - 0.03)(3.142 \times 10^{-6})}{1000 \times 9.81}\right]^{0.4}
$$

Compute the inner term:

$$
\frac{(0.17)(3.142 \times 10^{-6})}{9810} = \frac{5.341 \times 10^{-7}}{9810} = 5.445 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}^2
$$

Raise to power 0.4:

$$
(5.445 \times 10^{-11})^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln(5.445 \times 10^{-11})} = e^{0.4 \times (-23.63)} = e^{-9.452} = 7.86 \times 10^{-5}
$$

Multiply by 1.30:

$$
\boxed{\,d_{b0} = 1.30 \times 7.86 \times 10^{-5} = 1.02 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m} \approx 0.10\,\text{cm}\,}
$$

*Step 3 — Comment on sphericity (1 mark)*

The bubble at the instant of detachment is approximately **hemispherical or cap-shaped**, not strictly spherical. The Fryer–Potter formula gives the **equivalent spherical diameter**, meaning the diameter of a sphere having the same volume as the actual cavity. Therefore, the result $d_{b0} \approx 1\,\text{mm}$ is consistent with the **detachment volume** of gas from a $2\,\text{mm}$ orifice under typical bubbling conditions.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

*Step 1 — Apply Darton correlation at $z = 0.5\,\text{m}$*

Effective origin shift:

$$
4\sqrt{A_o/N_0} = 4\sqrt{3.142 \times 10^{-6}} = 4 \times 1.773 \times 10^{-3} = 7.09 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}
$$

$$
z_{\text{eff}} = 0.5 + 0.00709 = 0.5071\,\text{m}
$$

Compute each factor:

- $(U - U_{mf})^{0.5} = (0.17)^{0.5} = 0.4123$
- $(z_{\text{eff}})^{0.75} = (0.5071)^{0.75} = e^{0.75 \times \ln 0.5071} = e^{0.75 \times (-0.6788)} = e^{-0.5091} = 0.6008$
- $g^{-0.25} = (9.81)^{-0.25} = 0.5656$ (i.e., $1/1.768$)

$$
d_b^{\text{Darton}} = 0.54 \times 0.4123 \times 0.6008 \times 0.5656 = 0.0755\,\text{m} \approx 7.55\,\text{cm}
$$

*Step 2 — Apply Mori–Wen*

Maximum bubble size:

$$
d_{b,\infty} = 0.652\,[A_t\,(U - U_{mf})]^{0.4} = 0.652\,[0.785 \times 0.17]^{0.4} = 0.652 \times (0.1335)^{0.4}
$$

$(0.1335)^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \times (-2.013)} = e^{-0.805} = 0.447$

$$
d_{b,\infty} = 0.652 \times 0.447 = 0.291\,\text{m}
$$

Exponential term:

$$
e^{-0.3 \times 0.5 / 1.0} = e^{-0.15} = 0.861
$$

$$
d_b^{\text{MW}} = 0.291 - (0.291 - 0.000102)(0.861) = 0.291 - 0.2504 = 0.0406\,\text{m} \approx 4.06\,\text{cm}
$$

*Step 3 — Compare and discuss (2 marks)*

| Model | $d_b$ at $z = 0.5\,\text{m}$ |
|---|---|
| Darton | 7.55 cm |
| Mori–Wen | 4.06 cm |

**Discrepancy discussion:**
- Darton is **distributor-controlled** and predicts faster growth because it assumes immediate coalescence between neighbouring bubble streams.
- Mori–Wen is **column-diameter-controlled** and exhibits a slow approach to $d_{b,\infty}$ for large $D_t$ (here $D_t = 1.0\,\text{m}$). At $z = 0.5\,\text{m}$ the bubble is still in the early growth regime.
- In practice, the true bubble size lies **between the two estimates**, with Darton being more accurate for **shallow beds with small bubble spacings** and Mori–Wen being more accurate for **deep, large-diameter industrial beds**.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Module 3, Alternative]

**(a)** Starting from the assumption that bubble growth follows a first-order relaxation, **derive the Mori–Wen correlation** for $d_b(z)$. Clearly state the boundary conditions and the physical interpretation of the constant $0.3$ in the model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gas–solid fluidized bed operates with $U - U_{mf} = 0.15\,\text{m/s}$ in a column of cross-section $A_t = 0.50\,\text{m}^2$. Using **Rowe's correlation**, compute the bubble diameter at heights $z = 0.1, 0.3$ and $0.5\,\text{m}$. Comment on the rate of bubble growth. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

*Step 1 — Postulate the ODE (2 marks)*

The Mori–Wen model assumes the rate of bubble growth with respect to the dimensionless height $z/D_t$ is proportional to the **remaining headroom** between the current bubble size and the asymptotic maximum:

$$
\frac{d(d_b)}{d(z/D_t)} = k\,[d_{b,\infty} - d_b]
$$

where $k = 0.3$ is the empirical proportionality constant determined from air–FCC fluidization data.

*Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate (2 marks)*

$$
\int_{d_{b0}}^{d_b} \frac{d(d_b)}{d_{b,\infty} - d_b} = k \int_0^z \frac{dz}{D_t}
$$

$$
-\ln\!\left(\frac{d_{b,\infty} - d_b}{d_{b,\infty} - d_{b0}}\right) = k \frac{z}{D_t}
$$

*Step 3 — Apply boundary conditions (1 mark)*

- $z = 0 \Rightarrow d_b = d_{b0}$
- $z \to \infty \Rightarrow d_b \to d_{b,\infty}$

*Step 4 — Solve and obtain final form (2 marks)*

$$
\boxed{\,d_b = d_{b,\infty} - (d_{b,\infty} - d_{b0})\,e^{-0.3 z / D_t}\,}
$$

*Physical interpretation of 0.3:* The constant represents the **inverse of the dimensionless relaxation length** for bubble growth. A larger $D_t$ makes the exponent smaller, slowing the approach to $d_{b,\infty}$. This explains why **laboratory beds (small $D_t$) reach equilibrium quickly** while **industrial beds require much taller columns** to achieve bubble-equilibrium.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

*Step 1 — Apply Rowe's correlation at three heights*

Rowe (1976) correlation:

$$
d_b = 0.4\,(U - U_{mf})^{0.4}\, z^{0.8}
$$

With $U - U_{mf} = 0.15\,\text{m/s}$:

$$
(U - U_{mf})^{0.4} = (0.15)^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 0.15} = e^{0.4 \times (-1.897)} = e^{-0.7588} = 0.4683
$$

Hence:

$$
d_b = 0.4 \times 0.4683 \times z^{0.8} = 0.1873\,z^{0.8}
$$

*Step 2 — Evaluate at each height*

- $z = 0.1\,\text{m}$: $(0.1)^{0.8} = e^{0.8 \times \ln 0.1} = e^{0.8 \times (-2.303)} = e^{-1.842} = 0.1585$

$$
d_b = 0.1873 \times 0.1585 = 0.0297\,\text{m} = 2.97\,\text{cm}
$$

- $z = 0.3\,\text{m}$: $(0.3)^{0.8} = e^{0.8 \times (-1.204)} = e^{-0.963} = 0.3819$

$$
d_b = 0.1873 \times 0.3819 = 0.0715\,\text{m} = 7.15\,\text{cm}
$$

- $z = 0.5\,\text{m}$: $(0.5)^{0.8} = e^{0.8 \times (-0.6931)} = e^{-0.5545} = 0.5743$

$$
d_b = 0.1873 \times 0.5743 = 0.1076\,\text{m} = 10.76\,\text{cm}
$$

*Step 3 — Tabulate and comment (2 marks)*

| Height $z$ (m) | Bubble Size $d_b$ (cm) | % Increase |
|---|---|---|
| 0.1 | 2.97 | — |
| 0.3 | 7.15 | +141% |
| 0.5 | 10.76 | +50% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment:** The bubble grows approximately as $z^{0.8}$, i.e., **sub-linearly**. The growth rate is fast near the distributor and slows as $z$ increases, consistent with the asymptotic approach to a maximum bubble size imposed by the bed hydrodynamics. In a 0.5 m bed, the bubble has **already grown by a factor of 3.6** compared to its size at 0.1 m, demonstrating that **coalescence dominates the lower third of the bed**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls where students lose marks (BEFORE you submit your answer script):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $U - U_{mf}$ to m/s** when the question gives data in cm/s. This single error can cascade and yield bubble sizes off by a factor of 3.
> 2. **Using $A_o$ instead of $A_o/N_0$** in the Darton correlation. The term inside the square root is the **area per orifice**, not the total orifice area.
> 3. **Plugging $z = 0$ into Darton and getting $d_b = 0$**. The shifted origin $4\sqrt{A_o/N_0}$ exists precisely to prevent this; always include it.
> 4. **Mixing up $U$ with $U - U_{mf}$**. All bubble correlations use the **excess gas velocity**, not the superficial velocity itself.
> 5. **Using $g = 9.8$ instead of $9.81$** (or vice versa) when the problem specifies a particular value. Match the problem.
> 6. **Reporting $d_b$ in mm when the formula is in m**. Always carry units throughout the calculation and present the final answer in the requested unit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Bubble Size and Bubble Growth**

- **Initial bubble size $d_{b0}$** depends on **distributor type**: porous → small (≈ 2–6 mm), perforated → larger, function of $A_o/N_0$.
- **Fryer–Potter formula** for perforated plates: $d_{b0} = 1.30[(U_0 - U_{mf})A_o / (N_0 g)]^{0.4}$.
- **Harrison–Davidson** for porous plates: $d_{b0} \approx 0.0036(U - U_{mf})^{0.9}$.
- **Bubble growth is primarily by coalescence** — larger bubbles rise faster and overtake smaller ones.
- **Darton's correlation** is **distributor-controlled**: $d_b \propto (z + 4\sqrt{A_o/N_0})^{0.75}$.
- **Mori–Wen's correlation** is **column-diameter-controlled** and uses a first-order relaxation with $z/D_t$ as the independent variable.
- **Rowe's empirical correlation** is the simplest: $d_b = 0.4(U - U_{mf})^{0.4} z^{0.8}$ — good for **quick estimates**.
- **Geldart's correlation** gives the most conservative estimate: $d_b = 0.044(U - U_{mf})^{0.5} z^{0.75}$.
- **Maximum stable bubble size** (Harrison–Davidson): $d_{b,\max} = 2 U_{mf}^2 / g$ — physical upper bound beyond which bubbles split.
- **Mori–Wen asymptotic limit:** $d_{b,\infty} = 0.652 [A_t (U - U_{mf})]^{0.4}$.
- **Bubble rise velocity (single):** $u_b = 0.711 (g d_b)^{0.5}$.
- **Visible bubble flow fraction:** $\delta = (U - U_{mf}) / u_b$.
- **Always** use **SI units** in all formulas; convert cm/s, mm, etc. to m/s, m before substituting.
- **Industrial application:** Smaller $d_b$ ⇒ higher gas–solid contact ⇒ better catalytic conversion. Designers try to **suppress coalescence** by using **internals** (tube banks, baffles) or by operating **closer to $U_{mf}$**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bubble Rise Velocity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Bubble Rise Velocity ## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bubble Rise Velocity ($U_{br}$)** is defined as the absolute upward velocity of a single gas bubble (or a swarm of bubbles) relative to the surrounding emulsion phase in a gas–solid fluidized bed. It quantifies how fast a discrete bubble phase traverses the dense particulate suspension under the influence of buoyancy, pressure gradients, and gas throughflow. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this parameter belongs to the **two-phase theory of fluidization**, where the bed is conceptually split into: - A **bubble phase** (lean in solids, gas-rich), and - An **emulsion phase** (at minimum fluidization, dense in solids). The bubble rise velocity is therefore **NOT** the gas superficial velocity ($U$), nor the interstitial gas velocity ($U/U_mf$), but a characteristic velocity associated with the bubble as a discrete entity. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Rising Balloon in Sticky Mud:** > Imagine blowing an air bubble into a thick, sticky mud puddle (the *emulsion phase*). The bubble does not stay still — it rushes upward because the surrounding mud is heavier (denser). Now imagine blowing another air bubble into flowing honey. The upward motion of the bubble is assisted by the honey itself flowing upward — so the bubble moves even faster. > > In a fluidized bed: > - The **buoyancy effect** (mud pushing the balloon up) is captured by the term $0.711\sqrt{gD_b}$. > - The **gas throughflow assistance** (honey flowing upward) is captured by the term $(U - U_{mf})$. > - Adding both gives the actual **bubble rise velocity**. ### 1.3 Why Bubble Rise Velocity Matters - It governs **gas–solid contact efficiency** in fluidized bed reactors. - It determines the **residence time** of bubbles in the bed. - It directly influences **bubble coalescence**, bed expansion, and **elutriation** of fines. - Critical for design of **FCC regenerators**, **combustors**, and **polymerization reactors**. --- ## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 Two-Phase Theory Foundation (Bed Assumption) According to the classical **Toomey–Johnstone two-phase theory**: > All gas in excess of that required for minimum fluidization passes through the bed as bubbles. $$ U = U_{mf} + U_{b,\text{volumetric contribution}} $$ This implies that **only the excess gas** $(U - U_{mf})$ forms bubbles, which is the conceptual basis for the Davidson–Harrison bubble rise velocity model. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Bubble Motion 1. **A gas bubble forms at the distributor** with an initial diameter $D_{b0}$. 2. **Buoyancy** drives the bubble upward through the denser emulsion (treated as an inviscid fluid of density $\rho_s(1-\varepsilon_{mf})$). 3. **Coalescence** with neighboring bubbles increases bubble size as it rises. 4. **Gas throughflow** of excess fluidizing gas adds to the bubble's translational velocity. 5. The **resultant bubble rise velocity** $U_{br}$ is a vector sum of the buoyancy-driven rise and the gas convective rise. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description / Use | Typical Range / Condition | |---|---------|-------------------|---------------------------| | 1 | $U_{br,\text{single}} = 0.711\sqrt{g D_b}$ | Davies–Taylor equation for a **single bubble in inviscid fluid** (no gas flow) | Isolated bubble, $Re_b > 20$ | | 2 | $U_{br} = (U - U_{mf}) + 0.711\sqrt{g D_b}$ | **Davidson–Harrison model** — bubble rise velocity in a fluidized bed | Most widely used; assumes emulsion at $U_{mf}$ | | 3 | $U_{br} = 0.711\sqrt{g D_b} \left[1 + \dfrac{U - U_{mf}}{0.711\sqrt{g D_b}}\right]$ | Reform of Davidson–Harrison showing convective + buoyancy terms | For comparison with single-bubble limit | | 4 | $U_{br,\text{Werther}} = \psi \sqrt{g D_b} + (U - U_{mf})$ | Werther empirical correlation; $\psi = 0.64$–$1.0$ | For Geldart B/D powders | | 5 | $U_{br} = 1.55 \left[\dfrac{(U - U_{mf})\sigma_{br} D_e}{D_t^{1.6}}\right]^{0.6}$ | Geldart correlation for small bubbles | Geldart A powders, $D_b < D_{bt}$ | | 6 | $U_b = (U - U_{mf}) + 0.711\sqrt{g D_b}$ (same as 2) | KTU board exam most-tested equation | Standard 14-mark question | | 7 | $D_{bt} = \left(\dfrac{U - U_{mf}}{0.711}\right)^{2}\dfrac{1}{g}$ | **Transition bubble diameter** where rise velocity = $2(U-U_{mf})$ | Boundary between slow & fast bubbles | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Equation (2) — the Davidson–Harrison equation — appears in **nearly every KTU question** on bubble rise velocity. Memorize its form, derivation, and physical meaning. ### 2.4 Engineering Utility | Application | Relevance of $U_{br}$ | |-------------|------------------------| | **FCC Catalytic Cracking** | Determines catalyst residence time in regenerator | | **Fluidized Bed Combustors** | Controls bubble-induced mixing and heat transfer | | **Wastewater Aeration** | Governs oxygen transfer via bubble contact time | | **Polymerization Reactors** | Influences monomer conversion per pass | | **Drying & Coating** | Sets particle exposure time to hot gases | ### 2.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ - Constant $0.711$ arises from the **Davies–Taylor potential flow solution** for a spherical-cap bubble. - Typical $U_{br}$ values in industrial fluidized beds: **0.1 – 2.0 m/s**. --- ## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation of the Davies–Taylor Single Bubble Rise Velocity **Starting point:** Consider a single gas bubble rising in an infinite inviscid liquid of density $\rho_L$ under potential flow conditions. **Step 1:** Apply Bernoulli's equation between the leading nose (point 1) and the equator (point 2) of the bubble. $$ P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_L u_1^2 = P_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_L u_2^2 $$ **Step 2:** Recognize that at the leading stagnation point, $u_1 = 0$ (potential flow). $$ P_1 - P_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_L u_2^2 $$ **Step 3:** At the equator, applying the kinematic free-surface condition for a spherical cap of radius $R$, the tangential velocity equals the rise velocity. $$ u_2 = \tfrac{3}{2} U_{br} \quad \text{(for a potential-flow sphere)} $$ **Step 4:** The pressure difference $(P_1 - P_2)$ provides the buoyancy force on the upper cap. Integrating over the cap area gives the upward thrust $\approx \rho_L g R^2$. **Step 5:** Equating drag force from pressure and buoyancy-derived thrust: $$ \rho_L g R^2 \propto \rho_L U_{br}^2 R^2 $$ **Step 6:** Solving for $U_{br}$ yields the **Davies–Taylor constant**: $$ U_{br} = 0.711 \sqrt{g D_b} $$ where $D_b = 2R$ is the equivalent bubble diameter. > [!NOTE] > The constant **0.711** is empirically verified for spherical-cap bubbles of equivalent diameter $D_b$. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the Davidson–Harrison Bubble Rise Velocity **Starting point:** In a fluidized bed, the bubble rises relative to the emulsion, but the emulsion itself has interstitial gas flow at $U_{mf}/\varepsilon_{mf}$. Excess gas $(U - U_{mf})$ passes through as bubbles. **Step 1:** Decompose bubble motion into two additive contributions: $$ U_{br} = U_{br,\text{buoyancy}} + U_{br,\text{convective}} $$ **Step 2:** The buoyancy component follows Davies–Taylor: $$ U_{br,\text{buoyancy}} = 0.711\sqrt{g D_b} $$ **Step 3:** The convective component equals the **excess gas velocity** passing through the bubble phase (gas in excess of minimum fluidization): $$ U_{br,\text{convective}} = U - U_{mf} $$ **Step 4:** Superpose both contributions (assumed independent and additive): $$ U_{br} = (U - U_{mf}) + 0.711\sqrt{g D_b} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **canonical KTU board equation** for bubble rise velocity. KTU 2024 Scheme examiners expect both the derivation steps and physical justification of each term. --- ### 3.3 Worked Analytical Problem (KTU Board Style) **Problem:** A fluidized bed operates at superficial gas velocity $U = 0.45 \text{ m/s}$ with $U_{mf} = 0.12 \text{ m/s}$. A bubble of diameter $D_b = 0.08 \text{ m}$ is observed. Compute: (a) Bubble rise velocity using Davidson–Harrison. (b) Percentage contribution of buoyancy vs. convective terms. (c) Transition bubble diameter. **Solution:** **Part (a):** Apply Davidson–Harrison equation. $$ U_{br} = (U - U_{mf}) + 0.711\sqrt{g D_b} $$ $$ U - U_{mf} = 0.45 - 0.12 = 0.33 \text{ m/s} $$ $$ 0.711 \sqrt{9.81 \times 0.08} = 0.711 \times \sqrt{0.7848} = 0.711 \times 0.8859 $$ $$ = 0.630 \text{ m/s} $$ $$ U_{br} = 0.33 + 0.630 = 0.960 \text{ m/s} $$ **Part (b):** Percentage contributions. $$ \%_{\text{buoyancy}} = \frac{0.630}{0.960} \times 100 = 65.6\% $$ $$ \%_{\text{convective}} = \frac{0.33}{0.960} \times 100 = 34.4\% $$ **Part (c):** Transition bubble diameter. $$ D_{bt} = \frac{(U - U_{mf})^2}{(0.711)^2 \, g} = \frac{(0.33)^2}{0.505 \times 9.81} $$ $$ = \frac{0.1089}{4.954} = 0.02198 \text{ m} \approx 22 \text{ mm} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Verification Rule (Davidson–Harrison):** If $D_b > D_{bt}$, the bubble is **fast-rising** (buoyancy-dominated). If $D_b < D_{bt}$, it is **slow-rising** (convective-dominated). Here $D_b = 0.08 > D_{bt} = 0.022$, confirming buoyancy dominance — consistent with the 65.6% figure. --- ### 3.4 Bubble Growth Correlation — Two-Phase Model As bubbles rise through the bed, they coalesce and grow. The **Mori–Wen correlation** is often used in KTU: $$ \frac{D_b}{D_{bm}} = 1 + \frac{0.32}{D_{bm}}(h - h_0)\sqrt{\frac{U - U_{mf}}{D_t}} $$ where: - $D_{bm}$ = maximum stable bubble diameter - $h$ = height above distributor - $h_0$ = initial height of bubble formation - $D_t$ = bed diameter For quick estimation, a simpler form often used: $$ D_b(h) = D_{b0} \, \exp\!\left(\frac{0.25 \, h}{D_t}\right) $$ Substituting $D_b(h)$ into Davidson–Harrison gives $U_{br}(h)$ as a function of position, enabling calculation of **bubble residence time** and **contact efficiency**. --- ## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 Bubble Rise Velocity — Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Gas Distributor Plate] --> B[Gas enters bed at superficial velocity U] B --> C{U > Umf ?} C -- No --> D[Fixed Bed - No Bubbles] C -- Yes --> E[Excess Gas forms Bubbles] E --> F[Initial Bubble D_b0 at distributor] F --> G[Bubble Rises Through Emulsion Phase] G --> H[Coalescence with Neighboring Bubbles] H --> I[Bubble Diameter D_b grows with height h] I --> J[Davies-Taylor Buoyancy Term: 0.711 sqrt g D_b] I --> K[Convective Throughflow Term: U minus Umf] J --> L[Superposition: Davidson-Harrison Model] K --> L L --> M[Bubble Rise Velocity U_br] M --> N{Is D_b greater than D_bt ?} N -- Yes --> O[Fast Bubble - Buoyancy Dominated] N -- No --> P[Slow Bubble - Convective Dominated] O --> Q[Bubble Erupts at Bed Surface] P --> Q Q --> R[Gas Exchange with Freeboard] R --> S[Elutriation of Fines] style A fill:#e1f5ff style L fill:#fff4b8 style M fill:#ffd9b3 style O fill:#c8f7c5 style P fill:#c8e0f7 ``` ### 4.2 Davidson–Harrison Superposition Schematic (Block View) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph INPUTS A1[U: Superficial Gas Velocity] A2[Umf: Minimum Fluidization Velocity] A3[Db: Bubble Equivalent Diameter] end subgraph TERMS B1[Convective Term: U - Umf] B2[Buoyancy Term: 0.711 × sqrt g × Db] end subgraph EQUATION C1[Davidson-Harrison Model] end subgraph OUTPUT D1[Ubr: Bubble Rise Velocity] end A1 --> B1 A2 --> B1 A3 --> B2 B1 --> C1 B2 --> C1 C1 --> D1 style C1 fill:#fff4b8 style D1 fill:#ffd9b3 style B1 fill:#e1f5ff style B2 fill:#e1f5ff ``` ### 4.3 Bubble Classification Matrix (Slow vs Fast) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Compute Dbt = U - Umf squared divided by 0.711 squared times g] --> B{Compare Db with Dbt} B -- Db less than Dbt --> C1[Slow Bubble] B -- Db greater than Dbt --> C2[Fast Bubble] C1 --> D1[Ubr ≈ U - Umf
Convective Dominated
Gas Throughflow Drives Bubble] C2 --> D2[Ubr ≈ 0.711 × sqrt g × Db
Buoyancy Dominated
Like Single Bubble in Fluid] style C1 fill:#c8e0f7 style C2 fill:#c8f7c5 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** Define the term **bubble rise velocity** in a gas–solid fluidized bed. State the Davidson–Harrison equation. **Model Answer:** > **Bubble rise velocity ($U_{br}$)** is the absolute upward velocity of a discrete gas bubble relative to the surrounding emulsion phase in a fluidized bed. According to the Davidson–Harrison model, it is the superposition of a buoyancy-driven contribution (Davies–Taylor term) and a convective contribution (excess gas throughflow): > > $$U_{br} = (U - U_{mf}) + 0.711\sqrt{g D_b}$$ --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** What is the **transition bubble diameter** ($D_{bt}$) in a fluidized bed? Write its expression. **Model Answer:** > The **transition bubble diameter** $D_{bt}$ is the bubble size at which the bubble rise velocity equals **twice** the excess gas velocity, i.e., $U_{br} = 2(U - U_{mf})$. It marks the boundary between slow-rising (gas-threshold) and fast-rising (buoyancy-dominated) bubbles: > > $$D_{bt} = \frac{(U - U_{mf})^2}{(0.711)^2 \, g} = \frac{(U - U_{mf})^2}{0.505 \, g}$$ --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]** **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the Davidson–Harrison equation for bubble rise velocity in a gas–solid fluidized bed. Clearly state all assumptions. **(b)** [7 Marks] A fluidized bed operates with the following data: - Superficial gas velocity $U = 0.40 \text{ m/s}$ - Minimum fluidization velocity $U_{mf} = 0.10 \text{ m/s}$ - Bubble equivalent diameter $D_b = 0.06 \text{ m}$ Compute: 1. The bubble rise velocity using Davidson–Harrison model. 2. The percentage contributions of buoyancy and convective terms. 3. The transition bubble diameter $D_{bt}$ and classify the bubble as slow or fast. --- **SOLUTION TO QUESTION A** **(a) Derivation [7 Marks]:** The Davidson–Harrison bubble rise velocity model is derived by superposition of two physically distinct contributions: **Step 1 — Buoyancy Component:** A single gas bubble rising in an inviscid emulsion (treated as a liquid-like continuum) follows the Davies–Taylor potential-flow solution: $$ U_{br,\text{buoyancy}} = 0.711\sqrt{g D_b} $$ **Step 2 — Convective Component:** By the Toomey–Johnstone two-phase theory, gas in excess of minimum fluidization passes through the bed as bubbles. The volumetric flux of excess gas per unit cross-section gives the convective contribution: $$ U_{br,\text{convective}} = U - U_{mf} $$ **Step 3 — Superposition:** Assuming independence and additivity (a key assumption — Davidson validated this for Geldart B and D powders): $$ U_{br} = (U - U_{mf}) + 0.711\sqrt{g D_b} $$ **Assumptions:** 1. Emulsion phase behaves as an inviscid fluid at $U_{mf}$. 2. Spherical-cap bubble geometry. 3. Bubbles rise independently (no wake interactions). 4. Bubbles are sufficiently large ($Re_b > 20$). 5. Two-phase theory of fluidization holds. --- **(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:** **Step 1:** Compute excess gas velocity. $$ U - U_{mf} = 0.40 - 0.10 = 0.30 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 2:** Compute buoyancy term. $$ 0.711 \sqrt{9.81 \times 0.06} = 0.711 \times \sqrt{0.5886} = 0.711 \times 0.7672 = 0.5455 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 3:** Total bubble rise velocity. $$ U_{br} = 0.30 + 0.5455 = 0.8455 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 4:** Percentage contributions. $$ \%_{\text{buoyancy}} = \frac{0.5455}{0.8455} \times 100 = 64.5\% $$ $$ \%_{\text{convective}} = \frac{0.30}{0.8455} \times 100 = 35.5\% $$ **Step 5:** Transition bubble diameter. $$ D_{bt} = \frac{(0.30)^2}{(0.711)^2 \times 9.81} = \frac{0.09}{0.5054 \times 9.81} = \frac{0.09}{4.958} = 0.01815 \text{ m} $$ Since $D_b = 0.06 \text{ m} > D_{bt} = 0.018 \text{ m}$, the bubble is **fast-rising (buoyancy-dominated)**. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]** **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain with a neat diagram the **two-phase theory of fluidization**. Discuss how it forms the basis of the Davidson–Harrison bubble rise velocity model. **(b)** [7 Marks] A gas–solid fluidized bed of diameter $D_t = 0.5 \text{ m}$ is operated at $U = 0.30 \text{ m/s}$ and $U_{mf} = 0.08 \text{ m/s}$. The initial bubble diameter at the distributor is $D_{b0} = 0.012 \text{ m}$. Using the exponential bubble growth correlation $D_b = D_{b0} \exp(0.25 h / D_t)$, determine the bubble rise velocity at heights $h = 0.1 \text{ m}$, $h = 0.3 \text{ m}$, and $h = 0.5 \text{ m}$. --- **SOLUTION TO QUESTION B** **(a) Two-Phase Theory [7 Marks]:** The **two-phase theory of fluidization** (Toomey & Johnstone, 1952) postulates that: - The fluidized bed consists of two interpenetrating phases: 1. **Emulsion phase** — a dense particulate suspension maintained at minimum fluidization ($U_{mf}$). 2. **Bubble phase** — gas-rich voids that transport excess gas upward. - All gas in excess of $U_{mf}$ flows as bubbles: $$ U - U_{mf} = \text{gas flow through bubble phase (per unit area)} $$ **Connection to Davidson–Harrison:** The bubble rise velocity model uses this two-phase decomposition as its foundation: - The emulsion is treated as a pseudo-fluid in which the bubble rises due to buoyancy (Davies–Taylor term). - The excess gas $(U - U_{mf})$ adds a convective component to bubble motion. Without the two-phase theory, the additive form of the Davidson–Harrison equation would lack physical justification. --- **(b) Numerical Computation [7 Marks]:** **Given:** $U = 0.30$ m/s, $U_{mf} = 0.08$ m/s, $D_{b0} = 0.012$ m, $D_t = 0.5$ m. **Step 1:** Compute excess gas velocity (constant). $$ U - U_{mf} = 0.30 - 0.08 = 0.22 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 2:** Compute bubble diameter at each height. At $h = 0.1$ m: $$ D_b(0.1) = 0.012 \exp\!\left(\frac{0.25 \times 0.1}{0.5}\right) = 0.012 \exp(0.05) = 0.012 \times 1.0513 = 0.01262 \text{ m} $$ At $h = 0.3$ m: $$ D_b(0.3) = 0.012 \exp\!\left(\frac{0.25 \times 0.3}{0.5}\right) = 0.012 \exp(0.15) = 0.012 \times 1.1618 = 0.01394 \text{ m} $$ At $h = 0.5$ m: $$ D_b(0.5) = 0.012 \exp\!\left(\frac{0.25 \times 0.5}{0.5}\right) = 0.012 \exp(0.25) = 0.012 \times 1.2840 = 0.01541 \text{ m} $$ **Step 3:** Apply Davidson–Harrison at each height. At $h = 0.1$ m: $$ U_{br} = 0.22 + 0.711 \sqrt{9.81 \times 0.01262} = 0.22 + 0.711 \times 0.3519 = 0.22 + 0.2502 = 0.470 \text{ m/s} $$ At $h = 0.3$ m: $$ U_{br} = 0.22 + 0.711 \sqrt{9.81 \times 0.01394} = 0.22 + 0.711 \times 0.3698 = 0.22 + 0.2629 = 0.483 \text{ m/s} $$ At $h = 0.5$ m: $$ U_{br} = 0.22 + 0.711 \sqrt{9.81 \times 0.01541} = 0.22 + 0.711 \times 0.3888 = 0.22 + 0.2765 = 0.496 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 4: Tabulated Output:** | Height $h$ (m) | $D_b$ (m) | $U_{br}$ (m/s) | |-----------------|-----------|-----------------| | 0.1 | 0.01262 | 0.470 | | 0.3 | 0.01394 | 0.483 | | 0.5 | 0.01541 | 0.496 | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:** > - **Missing units** in final answer → **−1 mark** > - **Forgetting to convert $D_b$ in mm to m** before substitution → **−2 marks** > - **Using $U$ instead of $(U - U_{mf})$** in the convective term → **−3 marks** > - **Not stating assumptions** of Davidson–Harrison (inviscid emulsion, spherical-cap, no wake) → **−1 to −2 marks** > - **Confusing Davies–Taylor with Davidson–Harrison** — remember D–T is for a *single bubble in a quiescent fluid*; D–H is for *bubbles in a fluidized bed*. --- ### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **📌 Important Things to Remember — Bubble Rise Velocity** - **Core Equation (MUST MEMORIZE):** $$U_{br} = (U - U_{mf}) + 0.711\sqrt{g D_b}$$ - **Davies–Taylor Term:** $0.711\sqrt{gD_b}$ — buoyancy-driven rise, applies to a single bubble in inviscid fluid. - **Convective Term:** $(U - U_{mf})$ — comes from Toomey–Johnstone two-phase theory; equals excess gas flux. - **Transition Bubble Diameter:** Bubble size at which buoyancy = convective term. $$D_{bt} = \frac{(U - U_{mf})^2}{0.505 \, g}$$ - **Slow vs Fast Bubbles:** - $D_b < D_{bt}$ → slow bubble (gas throughflow drives it) - $D_b > D_{bt}$ → fast bubble (buoyancy dominates) - **Bubble Growth with Height:** Use exponential or Mori–Wen correlation. $$D_b(h) = D_{b0} \exp\!\left(\frac{0.25 \, h}{D_t}\right)$$ - **Validity Range:** Best for Geldart B and D powders; for Geldart A powders, use Geldart or Werther correlations. - **Engineering Impact:** Higher $U_{br}$ → shorter bubble residence time → lower gas–solid contact → reduced conversion efficiency. - **Common Mistake:** Bubble rise velocity is **not** equal to superficial velocity $U$ nor to $U/U_{mf}$ — it is a separate kinematic quantity of the bubble phase. - **Exam Triggers:** Whenever the question mentions "bubble rise," "two-phase theory," or "Davidson–Harrison," immediately write the master equation and proceed. - **Units Check:** Always verify $D_b$ is in **meters**, $U$ in **m/s**, $g$ in **m/s²** to get $U_{br}$ in m/s. ---

topics.title: K-L Flow Model for Bubbling Fluidized Bed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** K-L Flow Model for Bubbling Fluidized Bed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kunii-Levenspiel (K-L) Flow Model**: A rigorous two-phase hydrodynamic model for bubbling fluidized beds that explicitly accounts for gas exchange between three distinct regions — **(1) the bubble phase** (solid-free gas), **(2) the cloud-wake phase** (gas + entrained fines), and **(3) the emulsion phase** (gas + bulk solids at minimum fluidization). It is used to predict gas–solid contact and reactor conversion for catalytic and non-catalytic fluidized bed reactors.

In the K-L framework, any gas flow in **excess** of that required for minimum fluidization is assumed to rise through the bed in the form of **bubbles** that behave as a separate phase, while the rest of the gas percolates through the dense emulsion. Interchange of gas occurs between bubble ↔ cloud ↔ emulsion, and chemical reaction is presumed to take place **only in the emulsion phase** (or in the cloud if the solids are very fine and active).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boiling Tea Pot"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world intuition**: Imagine a kettle of vigorously boiling water. The **steam bubbles** rising through the water are the *bubble phase*; they carry almost no water (no "solid" content). A thin layer of hot, turbulent water is dragged *with* each bubble — that is the *cloud-wake*. The bulk of the water surrounding the bubbles, still containing the tea leaves in suspension, is the *emulsion phase*. Now, if you dissolve sugar at the bottom, it gets transferred from the bulk water → cloud → bubble, and the bubbles "vent" the steam-sugar mixture out the top. The K-L model mathematically describes this three-region mass transfer with chemical reaction occurring only in the bulk phase.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following quantities are central to the K-L model:

- **$U_{mf}$** — minimum fluidization velocity (m/s), typically **0.001 – 0.1 m/s**
- **$U$** — superficial gas velocity (m/s)
- **$\varepsilon_{mf}$** — bed voidage at minimum fluidization, typically **0.4 – 0.55**
- **$D_t$** — bed (column) diameter (m)
- **$g$** — acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- **$d_p$** — particle diameter (m)
- **$D_{AB}$** — molecular gas diffusivity (m²/s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The K-L model is one of the *highest-weightage* topics in PECHT415. Students must be able to (i) state assumptions, (ii) compute bubble size and velocity, (iii) evaluate interchange coefficients $K_{bc}$ and $K_{ce}$, and (iv) predict fractional conversion for a first-order reaction.

## 1.4 Salient Features at a Glance

| Feature | Description |
|---|---|
| Number of phases | 3 (Bubble + Cloud-Wake + Emulsion) |
| Where reaction occurs | Emulsion (and cloud if solids are very fine) |
| Gas in bubble phase | Plug flow |
| Gas in cloud-wake | Plug flow |
| Gas in emulsion | Plug flow or mixed flow (model selectable) |
| Solids condition | At minimum fluidization in emulsion |
| Excess gas | Passes as bubbles ($U_b = U - U_{mf}$ for Geldart B) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Foundational Assumptions of the K-L Model

> [!NOTE]
> These assumptions must be **memorized verbatim** — they are routinely asked in KTU Part A questions for 3 marks.

1. **Excess-gas assumption**: All gas flow in excess of $U_{mf}$ rises as bubbles: $U_b = U - U_{mf}$ (valid for Geldart **B** powders).
2. **Solid-free bubbles**: The bubble phase contains **no catalyst particles**, hence no reaction occurs there.
3. **Emulsion at $U_{mf}$**: The emulsion phase remains at minimum fluidization conditions with voidage $\varepsilon_{mf}$.
4. **Cloud-wake region**: Each bubble is enveloped by a cloud of gas + wake solids, of finite and predictable volume.
5. **Three interchange streams**: Gas exchanges as Bubble → Cloud → Emulsion, with interchange coefficients $K_{bc}$ and $K_{ce}$ (units: m³ gas / m³ bubble · s).
6. **Plug flow of gas** in both the bubble and the cloud-wake regions.

## 2.2 Why Bubbles Matter — The Engineering "Why"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A bubbling fluidized bed looks perfectly mixed externally, but **internally** the gas is segregated. Fast-rising bubbles short-circuit the bed, leaving unreacted gas in their wake. Without a quantitative model, designers cannot predict conversion. The K-L model converts this *qualitative intuition* into a *quantitative reactor design equation*.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid using `|` inside tables** — I have used `\vert` / `\mid` for absolute value to preserve Markdown table integrity.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bubble volume fraction | $\delta = (U - U_{mf}) / U_b$ | – | Ratio of bubble to total gas velocity |
| 2 | Bubble rise velocity (single bubble) | $u_{br} = 0.711\,(g\,d_b)^{0.5}$ | m/s | Davies-Taylor equation |
| 3 | Bubble velocity in bed | $u_b = U - U_{mf} + 0.711\,(g\,d_b)^{0.5}$ | m/s | Account for gas drift |
| 4 | Bubble size (Mori-Wen) | $d_b = d_{bm} - (d_{bm} - d_{b0})\,e^{-0.3\,z/D_t}$ | m | $z$ = height above distributor |
| 5 | Maximum bubble size | $d_{bm} = 0.652\,[A_t\,(U - U_{mf})]^{0.4}$ | m | $A_t = \pi D_t^2 / 4$ |
| 6 | Initial bubble size | $d_{b0}$ | m | From distributor design (often 0 → small) |
| 7 | Effective interchange, bubble↔cloud | $K_{bc} = 4.5\,(U_{mf}/d_b) + 5.85\,(D^{0.5}\,g^{0.25}/d_b^{1.25})$ | s⁻¹ | Higbie-type penetration model |
| 8 | Effective interchange, cloud↔emulsion | $K_{ce} = 6.77\,(D_{AB}\,\varepsilon_{mf}\,U_b / d_b^{3})^{0.5}$ | s⁻¹ | Diffusive interchange |
| 9 | Cloud thickness ratio | $\alpha = V_c / V_b = (3\,U_{mf})/(\,U_{mf} - \varepsilon_{mf}\,U_b\,)$ | – | Approximation; for fine particles |
| 10 | Cross-flow factor (per bubble) | $X = \dfrac{K_{bc}\,d_b}{U_b}\cdot\dfrac{1}{\delta}$ | – | Dimensionless; needed in conversion eq. |
| 11 | First-order rate (emulsion) | $-r_A = k\,C_A$ | mol/m³·s | $k$ is rate constant |
| 12 | Damköhler-like group | $\gamma = k\,\delta\,(1-\varepsilon_{mf})/U_b$ | – | Reaction modulus |
| 13 | Conversion (PF gas in emulsion) | $X = 1 - \exp[-K\,(1-\varepsilon_{mf})\,H\,/\,U_b]$ | – | After eliminating terms |
| 14 | Bed height | $H = L_{mf}\,(1 - \delta)$ | m | Often used to relate $z$ to total height |

> [!NOTE]
> **Variable legend for the Cheat Sheet**:
> - $D_t$ = column diameter, $A_t$ = column cross-section
> - $D$ = gas-phase diffusion coefficient (use $D_{AB}$ interchangeably)
> - $V_c, V_b$ = cloud and bubble volume per bubble
> - $L_{mf}$ = bed height at $U_{mf}$

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of the K-L Model

| Engineering Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **FCC (Fluid Catalytic Cracking)** | Predicting gasoline yield in refinery risers/regenerators |
| **Coal Combustion** | Designing CFB boilers (circulating fluidized bed) |
| **Wastewater Treatment** | Biological fluidized bed bioreactor scale-up |
| **Pharma Drying** | Granulator and coater design |
| **Mineral Processing** | Roasting of sulfide ores, alumina calcination |
| **Polymerization** | Gas-phase polyethylene/polypropylene reactors |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bubble Rise Velocity from Davies-Taylor Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Davies-Taylor equation is **derived by balancing buoyancy and drag** on a single rising bubble in a stagnant liquid. The terminal velocity scales with the square root of bubble diameter, giving the famous **$g^{0.5} d_b^{0.5}$** dependence.

For a single gas bubble in a liquid of negligible viscosity, the buoyancy force equals the inertial drag:

$$F_{\text{buoyancy}} = F_{\text{inertia}}$$

$$\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) d_b^{3}\,(\rho_L - \rho_G)\,g = C_D\,\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) d_b^{2}\,\left(\frac{\rho_L\,u_{br}^{2}}{2}\right)$$

Since $\rho_L \gg \rho_G$, we replace $(\rho_L - \rho_G)$ with $\rho_L$:

$$\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) d_b^{3}\,\rho_L\,g = C_D\,\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) d_b^{2}\,\left(\frac{\rho_L\,u_{br}^{2}}{2}\right)$$

Cancelling $\rho_L$ and one $d_b$ from both sides:

$$\left(\frac{1}{6}\right) d_b\,g = C_D\,\left(\frac{1}{8}\right) u_{br}^{2}$$

Solving for $u_{br}$:

$$u_{br}^{2} = \frac{4\,g\,d_b}{3\,C_D}$$

The experimentally determined drag coefficient for a single bubble is $C_D \approx 0.376$ (from Davies & Taylor, 1950). Substituting:

$$u_{br} = \left[\frac{4\,g\,d_b}{3 \times 0.376}\right]^{0.5} = \left[3.55\,g\,d_b\right]^{0.5} = 0.711\,(g\,d_b)^{0.5}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final result**: $\boxed{u_{br} = 0.711\,(g\,d_b)^{0.5}}$ — this is the **Davies-Taylor** equation and is a **must-know** for KTU Module 3.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bubble Size Distribution (Mori-Wen Correlation)

> [!NOTE]
> Mori & Wen (1975) proposed that bubbles grow by coalescence as they rise. The growth is exponential in nature, limited by the column diameter.

The phenomenological rate of bubble growth with height $z$ is:

$$\frac{d(d_b)}{dz} = \lambda - \mu\,d_b$$

where $\lambda$ is the bubble birth rate (frequency of formation at distributor) and $\mu$ is a coalescence decay constant. Experimental fit gives:

$$\frac{d(d_b)}{dz} = 0.3 - \frac{0.3}{D_t}\,d_b$$

> **Why $0.3$?** — It is a fitted empirical constant; the factor $0.3 / D_t$ represents the inverse-column length scale beyond which bubbles stop growing.

Separating variables and integrating from $z = 0$ (where $d_b = d_{b0}$) to a general height $z$ (where $d_b$ is the local size):

$$\int_{d_{b0}}^{d_b}\frac{d(d_b)}{1 - d_b/D_t} = 0.3 \int_{0}^{z} dz$$

The left integral is $-\,D_t \ln(1 - d_b/D_t) + D_t \ln(1 - d_{b0}/D_t)$, giving:

$$-\,D_t \ln\left(\frac{1 - d_b/D_t}{1 - d_{b0}/D_t}\right) = 0.3\,z$$

Exponentiating and isolating $d_b$:

$$1 - \frac{d_b}{D_t} = \left(1 - \frac{d_{b0}}{D_t}\right) e^{-0.3\,z/D_t}$$

Rearranging (note that $d_{bm} = D_t$, the maximum stable bubble size, used as the asymptotic limit):

$$d_b = D_t - (D_t - d_{b0})\,e^{-0.3\,z/D_t}$$

Replacing $D_t$ with the empirical maximum $d_{bm}$:

$$\boxed{d_b = d_{bm} - (d_{bm} - d_{b0})\,e^{-0.3\,z/D_t}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Mori-Wen bubble size correlation**, another must-memorize formula for KTU Module 3.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effective Interchange Coefficient $K_{bc}$ (Bubble ↔ Cloud)

The interchange between bubble and cloud is controlled by two parallel mechanisms:

**(a) Convective through-flow** (gas percolating through the bubble, evaluated per unit bubble volume):

$$K_{bc,\text{conv}} = 4.5\,\frac{U_{mf}}{d_b}$$

> **Physical meaning**: Gas leaves the bottom of the bubble into the cloud and enters the top from the cloud. The factor 4.5 comes from the geometry of a spherical cap bubble.

**(b) Diffusive penetration** (Higbie-style surface renewal):

$$K_{bc,\text{diff}} = 5.85\,\frac{D^{0.5}\,g^{0.25}}{d_b^{1.25}}$$

> **Physical meaning**: $g^{0.25} / d_b^{1.25}$ arises from the characteristic time for a gas element to slide around the bubble ($t \sim d_b / u_{br} \sim d_b^{0.5} / g^{0.25}$). Multiplying by $D^{0.5}$ gives a penetration depth, and the constant 5.85 fits experimental data.

Since the two mechanisms act **in parallel**, total interchange is the **sum**:

$$\boxed{K_{bc} = 4.5\,\frac{U_{mf}}{d_b} + 5.85\,\frac{D^{0.5}\,g^{0.25}}{d_b^{1.25}}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Effective Interchange Coefficient $K_{ce}$ (Cloud ↔ Emulsion)

Diffusion across the cloud boundary follows Higbie's penetration theory. The diffusion length per exchange is $(D_{AB}\,t_c)^{0.5}$, and the exchange time is $t_c = d_b / U_b$. Thus the volumetric gas exchange rate per unit bubble volume is:

$$K_{ce} \sim \frac{(D_{AB})^{0.5}}{d_b^{1.5}}\,U_b^{0.5}\,\varepsilon_{mf}^{0.5}$$

Fitting the constant against bubbling bed data gives:

$$\boxed{K_{ce} = 6.77\left(\frac{D_{AB}\,\varepsilon_{mf}\,U_b}{d_b^{3}}\right)^{0.5}}$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Conversion in a Bubbling Fluidized Bed (First-Order Reaction in Emulsion, Plug Flow of Gas)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the **culmination** of the K-L model and is the most commonly asked 14-mark derivation in KTU Module 3.

#### Step 1: Set up the gas balance on the rising bubble

For a steady-state bubble rising through a height $dz$, the change in moles of reactant $A$ carried in the bubble is:

$$-U_b\,\frac{dC_{Ab}}{dz} = K_{bc}\,(C_{Ab} - C_{Ac}) - \text{(loss to cloud due to reaction)}$$

For the **cloud-wake**, assuming plug flow and accounting for interchange with both bubble and emulsion:

$$\delta_b\,\frac{dC_{Ac}}{dz} = K_{bc}\,(C_{Ab} - C_{Ac}) - K_{ce}\,(C_{Ac} - C_{Ae})$$

For the **emulsion phase**, where reaction $-r_A = k\,C_{Ae}$ occurs (and treating the emulsion as well-mixed in the cross-section but plug flow in the vertical direction):

$$U_{mf}\,\frac{dC_{Ae}}{dz} + K_{ce}\,(C_{Ac} - C_{Ae}) + k\,(1 - \varepsilon_{mf})\,C_{Ae} = 0$$

#### Step 2: Dimensionless transformation

Let $\zeta = z/H$ (reduced height), and define:

$$\eta = \frac{C_A}{C_{A0}}, \quad \gamma = \frac{k\,H\,(1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{U_b}, \quad \beta = \frac{K_{bc}\,H}{U_b}, \quad \phi = \frac{K_{ce}\,H}{U_{mf}}$$

#### Step 3: Solve the coupled ODEs

The three equations become a linear 3×3 ODE system. In the limit where the bubble and cloud are well-mixed vertically compared to the emulsion (a common KTU simplification), the bubble and cloud concentrations are slaved to the emulsion:

$$C_{Ab} - C_{Ac} \approx \frac{K_{ce}}{\beta+\phi}\,(C_{Ac} - C_{Ae})$$

Substituting back and integrating, with boundary conditions $C_{Ab}(0) = C_{A0}$, $C_{Ac}(0) = C_{A0}$, $C_{Ae}(0) = C_{A0}$:

$$C_{Ab} = C_{A0}\,\exp(-\zeta\,\Gamma)$$

where

$$\Gamma = \beta\left[1 + \frac{\phi}{\beta + \phi + \gamma}\right]$$

#### Step 4: Exit concentration and conversion

At the top of the bed ($\zeta = 1$):

$$C_{Ab,\text{exit}} = C_{A0}\,\exp(-\Gamma)$$

The total molar flow out of the bed, $F_{\text{out}} = U\,A_t\,C_{Ab,\text{exit}}$ (since all gas eventually leaves as bubble-phase gas at the top, or equivalently, the cup-mixed exit concentration equals the bubble exit concentration). Therefore:

$$\boxed{X = 1 - \exp\left\{-\frac{K_{bc}\,H}{U_b}\left[1 + \frac{K_{ce}}{K_{bc} + K_{ce} + k(1-\varepsilon_{mf})\,U_{mf}/U_b}\right]\right\}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Compact form often used in KTU problems**:
> $$X = 1 - \exp(-K)$$
> where $K$ is the combined "effective reaction–interchange" modulus.

#### Step 5: Limiting cases (very important for KTU problem-solving)

| Limit | Physical meaning | Result |
|---|---|---|
| $K_{bc} \to 0$ | Bubble is isolated, no interchange | $X \to 0$ (no conversion) |
| $K_{bc} \to \infty$ | Perfect interchange (well-mixed) | $X = 1 - \exp(-k\,\tau_{emulsion})$ |
| $k \to \infty$ | Diffusion-limited fast reaction | $X = 1 - \exp(-K_{bc}H/U_b)$ |
| $K_{ce} \to 0$ | Cloud isolated from emulsion | Reaction suppressed |

## 3.6 Worked Example — A Typical KTU Numerical

> [!NOTE]
> **Given**: $U = 0.40$ m/s, $U_{mf} = 0.04$ m/s, $D_t = 1.0$ m, $d_p = 200\,\mu m$, $\varepsilon_{mf} = 0.5$, $D_{AB} = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $H = 1.5$ m, $k = 1$ s⁻¹, first-order.
> **Find**: $X$ at the top of the bed.

**Step 1 — Bubble size at $z = H$**:
$d_{bm} = 0.652\,[A_t\,(U - U_{mf})]^{0.4} = 0.652\,[0.785 \times 0.36]^{0.4} = 0.652\,[0.2826]^{0.4} = 0.652 \times 0.6014 = 0.392$ m
Taking $d_{b0} = 0.01$ m:
$d_b = 0.392 - (0.392 - 0.01)\exp(-0.3 \times 1.5 / 1.0) = 0.392 - 0.382 \times e^{-0.45} = 0.392 - 0.382 \times 0.6376 = 0.392 - 0.2436 = 0.148$ m

**Step 2 — Bubble rise velocity**:
$u_b = U - U_{mf} + 0.711\,(g\,d_b)^{0.5} = 0.36 + 0.711\,(9.81 \times 0.148)^{0.5} = 0.36 + 0.711 \times 1.205 = 0.36 + 0.857 = 1.217$ m/s

**Step 3 — Interchange coefficients**:
$K_{bc} = 4.5 \times (0.04 / 0.148) + 5.85 \times [(2\times 10^{-5})^{0.5} \times 9.81^{0.25} / 0.148^{1.25}]$
$= 1.216 + 5.85 \times [0.00447 \times 1.773 / 0.1037]$
$= 1.216 + 5.85 \times 0.0764 = 1.216 + 0.447 = 1.663$ s⁻¹

$K_{ce} = 6.77 \times [(2 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.5 \times 1.217) / 0.148^{3}]^{0.5}$
$= 6.77 \times [0.01217 / 0.00324]^{0.5} = 6.77 \times [3.756]^{0.5} = 6.77 \times 1.938 = 13.12$ s⁻¹

**Step 4 — Effective modulus**:
Denominator $= K_{bc} + K_{ce} + k(1-\varepsilon_{mf})\,U_{mf}/U_b = 1.663 + 13.12 + 1 \times 0.5 \times 0.04/1.217$
$= 14.783 + 0.0164 = 14.80$ s⁻¹
Numerator $= K_{bc} \times K_{ce} = 1.663 \times 13.12 = 21.82$ s⁻²

$\Gamma = (K_{bc}\,H/U_b)\,[1 + K_{ce}/(K_{bc} + K_{ce} + k(1-\varepsilon_{mf})U_{mf}/U_b)]$
$= (1.663 \times 1.5 / 1.217) \times [1 + 13.12/14.80]$
$= 2.049 \times [1 + 0.8865] = 2.049 \times 1.8865 = 3.866$

**Step 5 — Conversion**:
$X = 1 - e^{-3.866} = 1 - 0.0209 = 0.979 \approx 97.9\,\%$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: ~98% conversion. Notice that most of the gas exchange is cloud↔emulsion ($K_{ce} \gg K_{bc}$), meaning the cloud acts as the active transport intermediary. This is a typical result for fine Geldart A/B catalysts.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three-Phase K-L Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The K-L model is best visualized as three vertically-stacked gas streams with two cross-currents. The diagram below shows a single bubble rising through the bed, with explicit mass-exchange pathways.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inlet[Superficial Gas Inlet U]:::inlet
    distributor[Gas Distributor]:::inlet
    split{Split of Gas Flow}:::decision
    bubbleflow[Bubble Phase U - Umf, plug flow]:::bubble
    emulflow[Emulsion Phase Umf, plug flow]:::emul
    bubble1[Bubble Region solid free, no reaction]:::bubble
    cloud[Cloud Wake Region gas + entrained fines]:::cloud
    emul[Emulsion Region gas + bulk solids at Umf]:::emul
    kbc[Interchange Kbc bubble to cloud]:::exchange
    kce[Interchange Kce cloud to emulsion]:::exchange
    reaction[First Order Reaction kCA occurs here]:::reaction
    outlet[Exiting Gas Cup Mixed]:::outlet

    inlet --> distributor
    distributor --> split
    split --> bubbleflow
    split --> emulflow
    bubbleflow --> bubble1
    emulflow --> emul
    bubble1 --> cloud
    cloud --> emul
    bubble1 <-->|Kbc| cloud
    cloud <-->|Kce| emul
    emul --> reaction
    reaction --> outlet
    cloud --> outlet

    classDef inlet fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px,color:#0d47a1
    classDef decision fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#f57f17
    classDef bubble fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#b71c1c
    classDef cloud fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px,color:#4a148c

    classDef exchange fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#bf360c
    classDef reaction fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#ad1457,stroke-width:2px,color:#880e4f
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECHT415]**

> **Q: State the assumptions of the Kunii-Levenspiel (K-L) model for a bubbling fluidized bed.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The K-L model is based on the following six assumptions:

1. The gas velocity in excess of the minimum fluidization velocity, $U - U_{mf}$, passes through the bed as bubbles, so that $U_b = U - U_{mf}$.
2. The bubbles are spherical (or spherical-cap) and contain no solid particles; therefore, no reaction takes place in the bubble phase.
3. The emulsion phase remains at the incipient fluidization condition, with voidage equal to $\varepsilon_{mf}$.
4. The gas in both the bubble phase and the cloud-wake region flows in plug flow.
5. The cloud-wake region around each bubble has a well-defined, finite volume that is a function of $U_{mf}$, $U_b$ and $\varepsilon_{mf}$.
6. Gas exchange between bubble-cloud and cloud-emulsion is described by effective interchange coefficients $K_{bc}$ and $K_{ce}$.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECHT415]**

> **Q: Differentiate between the bubble-cloud interchange coefficient $K_{bc}$ and the cloud-emulsion interchange coefficient $K_{ce}$ in the K-L model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | $K_{bc}$ (Bubble ↔ Cloud) | $K_{ce}$ (Cloud ↔ Emulsion) |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Convective through-flow + diffusive penetration across bubble surface | Diffusive penetration across cloud boundary |
| Magnitude | Lower (≈ 0.5 – 5 s⁻¹) | Higher (≈ 5 – 50 s⁻¹) for fine particles |
| Driving force | Bulk flow of gas through the bubble | Concentration gradient in the surrounding emulsion |
| Formula | $K_{bc} = 4.5\,U_{mf}/d_b + 5.85\,D^{0.5}\,g^{0.25}/d_b^{1.25}$ | $K_{ce} = 6.77\,(D_{AB}\,\varepsilon_{mf}\,U_b/d_b^{3})^{0.5}$ |
| Role | Rate-limiting step in many industrial reactors | Often the dominant resistance |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **MODULE 3 INTERNAL CHOICE — Students must answer ONE of the two options.**

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, PECHT415]**

> **(a)** [7 marks] Derive the Davies-Taylor equation for the rise velocity of a single bubble in a fluidized bed. State the assumptions clearly.
> **(b)** [7 marks] For a gas of $D_{AB} = 2.2 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s fluidizing a bed of $d_p = 180\,\mu m$ particles ($\varepsilon_{mf} = 0.5$, $U_{mf} = 0.025$ m/s) at a superficial velocity of $U = 0.30$ m/s, the bubble diameter is measured as $d_b = 0.10$ m. Compute the interchange coefficients $K_{bc}$ and $K_{ce}$.

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Step 1 — State the assumptions:**
- The bubble is a single, isolated gas cavity rising in an effectively infinite, stagnant liquid-like continuum.
- The surrounding "emulsion" has density $\rho_L$ much greater than the gas density $\rho_G$ inside the bubble, so we can neglect gas inertia.
- The flow is steady, and viscous effects at the bubble surface are negligible (i.e., the Reynolds number is high).
- The bubble takes a spherical-cap shape, for which Davies & Taylor empirically found a drag coefficient of $C_D = 0.376$.

**Step 2 — Force balance on the bubble:**

Buoyancy = Inertial Drag

$$\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) d_b^{3} \rho_L g = C_D \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) d_b^{2} \left(\frac{\rho_L u_{br}^{2}}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Simplify and solve:**

$$u_{br}^{2} = \frac{4\,g\,d_b}{3\,C_D}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $C_D = 0.376$:**

$$u_{br} = \left(\frac{4\,g\,d_b}{3 \times 0.376}\right)^{0.5} = 0.711\,(g\,d_b)^{0.5}$$

$\boxed{u_{br} = 0.711\,(g\,d_b)^{0.5}}$

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 1 — Compute $U_b$:**

$$U_b = U - U_{mf} = 0.30 - 0.025 = 0.275 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $K_{bc}$:**

Convective term:

$$4.5 \times \frac{U_{mf}}{d_b} = 4.5 \times \frac{0.025}{0.10} = 1.125 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

Diffusive term:

$$5.85 \times \frac{D^{0.5}\,g^{0.25}}{d_b^{1.25}} = 5.85 \times \frac{(2.2 \times 10^{-5})^{0.5} \times (9.81)^{0.25}}{(0.10)^{1.25}}$$

- $(2.2 \times 10^{-5})^{0.5} = 4.69 \times 10^{-3}$
- $(9.81)^{0.25} = 1.773$
- $(0.10)^{1.25} = 0.0562$

$$= 5.85 \times \frac{4.69 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.773}{0.0562} = 5.85 \times 0.1480 = 0.866 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$K_{bc} = 1.125 + 0.866 = 1.991 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $K_{ce}$:**

$$K_{ce} = 6.77 \times \left(\frac{D_{AB}\,\varepsilon_{mf}\,U_b}{d_b^{3}}\right)^{0.5}$$

$$\frac{D_{AB}\,\varepsilon_{mf}\,U_b}{d_b^{3}} = \frac{2.2 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.5 \times 0.275}{(0.10)^{3}} = \frac{3.025 \times 10^{-6}}{0.001} = 3.025 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$K_{ce} = 6.77 \times (3.025 \times 10^{-3})^{0.5} = 6.77 \times 0.0550 = 0.372 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Final results:**
- $\boxed{K_{bc} = 1.99 \text{ s}^{-1}}$
- $\boxed{K_{ce} = 0.37 \text{ s}^{-1}}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: In this coarse-particle case, $K_{bc} \gg K_{ce}$, meaning the bubble–cloud interface is the more active exchange surface. Contrast this with the fine-particle worked example in Section 3.6 where $K_{ce} \gg K_{bc}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, PECHT415]**

> **(a)** [7 marks] With the help of a neat sketch, describe the three-phase K-L model for a bubbling fluidized bed and the role of interchange coefficients.
> **(b)** [7 marks] A first-order catalytic reaction ($k = 2$ s⁻¹) is carried out in a bubbling fluidized bed of height 2 m. Given $U = 0.50$ m/s, $U_{mf} = 0.05$ m/s, $\varepsilon_{mf} = 0.50$, $K_{bc} = 4$ s⁻¹, $K_{ce} = 8$ s⁻¹. Compute the fractional conversion at the top of the bed. Assume Geldart B powder and plug flow in the emulsion.

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Sketch description (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block diagram):**

The K-L model postulates a fluidized bed consisting of three vertical, parallel gas streams:

1. **Bubble phase** (volume fraction $\delta$): solid-free gas rising in plug flow; velocity $U_b = U - U_{mf}$.
2. **Cloud-wake phase** (volume fraction $\alpha \cdot \delta$): gas plus entrained catalyst fines surrounding each bubble; plug flow.
3. **Emulsion phase** (volume fraction $1 - \delta$): gas + bulk solids at $U_{mf}$ and $\varepsilon_{mf}$; reaction $-r_A = k\,C_A$ occurs here.

**Interchange:**
- **$K_{bc}$** quantifies gas exchange between bubble and cloud (units: s⁻¹). Includes convective through-flow and diffusive penetration.
- **$K_{ce}$** quantifies gas exchange between cloud and emulsion. Predominantly diffusional.

The **role of $K_{bc}$ and $K_{ce}$** is to quantify the rate at which reactant A in the bubble finds its way to the catalyst in the emulsion. The combined effect determines how much the bed "looks like" a plug-flow reactor versus a well-mixed reactor.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 1 — Compute $U_b$:**

$$U_b = U - U_{mf} = 0.50 - 0.05 = 0.45 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the effective modulus:**

$$X = 1 - \exp\left\{-\frac{K_{bc}\,H}{U_b}\left[1 + \frac{K_{ce}}{K_{bc} + K_{ce} + k(1-\varepsilon_{mf})\,U_{mf}/U_b}\right]\right\}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

- $K_{bc}\,H/U_b = 4 \times 2 / 0.45 = 17.78$
- $k(1-\varepsilon_{mf})\,U_{mf}/U_b = 2 \times 0.5 \times 0.05/0.45 = 0.111$ s⁻¹
- $K_{bc} + K_{ce} + k(1-\varepsilon_{mf})\,U_{mf}/U_b = 4 + 8 + 0.111 = 12.111$ s⁻¹
- $K_{ce} / (K_{bc} + K_{ce} + \ldots) = 8/12.111 = 0.6605$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the bracket:**

$$1 + 0.6605 = 1.6605$$

**Step 5 — Compute the exponent:**

$$-17.78 \times 1.6605 = -29.53$$

**Step 6 — Compute $X$:**

$$X = 1 - e^{-29.53} \approx 1 - 1.43 \times 10^{-13} \approx 1.0$$

$$\boxed{X \approx 100\,\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: With such a tall bed (2 m) and high interchange rates, the bed behaves as a near-ideal reactor and the reactant is fully consumed. The K-L model correctly captures this designer's intuition quantitatively.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks on K-L Model problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units of $K_{bc}$ and $K_{ce}$** — they are in **s⁻¹**, not dimensionless. Writing $K_{bc} = 4.5$ without units costs 0.5 marks.
> 2. **Using $U$ instead of $U_b$** in the conversion exponent. The bubble-phase gas velocity is $U_b = U - U_{mf}$, **not** $U$. This single error can shift the conversion by 30–40%.
> 3. **Mixing up $D_t$ and $d_t$** — capital $D_t$ is column diameter, lowercase $d_b$ is bubble diameter. They differ by orders of magnitude.
> 4. **Confusing $D$ and $D_{AB}$** — both denote diffusivity in this model. If a problem uses the symbol $D$, assume it means $D_{AB}$.
> 5. **Omitting the cloud-wake term** — students often write $X = 1 - \exp(-K_{bc}H/U_b)$ and forget the cloud-emulsion coupling. The cloud term can contribute 20–60% of the conversion.
> 6. **Plug-flow vs. mixed-flow in the emulsion** — the problem statement must specify. By default, KTU expects **plug flow** in the emulsion, but be prepared for the alternative.
> 7. **Not stating the $C_D = 0.376$ empirical constant** in the Davies-Taylor derivation. The 0.711 is *not* a universal constant — it depends on the fitted $C_D$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bookmark this section for last-day KTU exam revision.**

- [ ] **Six assumptions** of the K-L model — must list all 6 for full marks.
- [ ] **Three phases**: Bubble (no solid), Cloud-wake, Emulsion (with reaction).
- [ ] **Bubble velocity** = $U - U_{mf} + 0.711\,(g\,d_b)^{0.5}$.
- [ ] **Davies-Taylor** coefficient: **0.711** (from $C_D = 0.376$).
- [ ] **Mori-Wen** bubble size: $d_b = d_{bm} - (d_{bm} - d_{b0})\exp(-0.3\,z/D_t)$.
- [ ] **$d_{bm}$** = $0.652\,[A_t\,(U - U_{mf})]^{0.4}$.
- [ ] **$K_{bc}$** = convective (4.5$U_{mf}/d_b$) + diffusive (5.85$D^{0.5}g^{0.25}/d_b^{1.25}$).
- [ ] **$K_{ce}$** = $6.77\,(D_{AB}\varepsilon_{mf}U_b/d_b^{3})^{0.5}$.
- [ ] **Conversion** follows $X = 1 - \exp(-\Gamma)$ with $\Gamma$ including the cloud coupling.
- [ ] **Reaction occurs only in the emulsion** for first-order catalytic cases.
- [ ] **Plug flow in the emulsion** is the KTU default unless otherwise stated.
- [ ] **Units check**: $K_{bc}, K_{ce}$ in **s⁻¹**; $d_b, D_t$ in **m**; velocities in **m/s**.
- [ ] **Limiting cases** of conversion: $K_{bc} \to \infty$ → ideal mixed; $K_{ce} \to 0$ → no reaction.
- [ ] **Sign convention**: $U_b > 0$ requires $U > U_{mf}$ (i.e., the bed must be bubbling, not just at incipient fluidization).
- [ ] **Typical magnitude**: $K_{bc} \sim 1$–$5$ s⁻¹; $K_{ce} \sim 5$–$50$ s⁻¹ for fine catalysts; reverse for coarse particles.

---

*End of Notes — PECHT415 Fluidization Engineering, Module 3, Topic: K-L Flow Model for Bubbling Fluidized Bed.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas Dispersion and Gas Interchange in Bubbling Beds (Concept Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gas Dispersion and Gas Interchange in Bubbling Beds (Concept Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Gas Dispersion in Bubbling Beds** refers to the macroscopic distribution and transport of the fluidizing gas through the dense phase (emulsion) and the dilute phase (bubbles) of a gas–solid fluidized bed operating above the minimum fluidization velocity ($U > U_{mf}$). In accordance with the **Two-Phase Theory of Fluidization** (Toomey–Johnstone, 1952), any gas supplied in excess of that required for minimum fluidization passes through the bed preferentially as **voids (bubbles)** rather than through the emulsion phase.

**Gas Interchange** is the process of mass transfer (and to a lesser extent, heat transfer) of gas-phase species between the bubble phase and the surrounding dense (emulsion/cloud/wake) phases. It quantifies how rapidly reactive or tracer gases are exchanged across the bubble interface, which directly controls reactor conversion, selectivity, and design (Keyfitz–Kunii–Levenspiel models).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The two-phase theory is the foundational assumption on which the bubbling bed model is built. Any analysis of gas interchange **must** begin with the statement: *"All gas in excess of $U_{mf} A$ flows as bubbles,"* where $A$ is the cross-sectional area of the bed.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **boiling kettle of milk on a gas stove**:
- The milk represents the **emulsion phase** (dense suspension of solid particles + interstitial gas).
- The rising **steam bubbles** represent the **bubble phase** (nearly solids-free voids).
- The thin layer of milk dragged along behind each bubble is the **wake**.
- The pocket of milk vapor that the bubble "carries" with it is the **cloud**.
- The continuous bubbling action mixes the milk — this is **gas dispersion**.
- The dissolving of a flavor (e.g., cardamom) into the milk from the steam represents **gas interchange**.

Just as cardamom aroma spreads through boiling milk even though the steam bubbles only occupy a small fraction of the kettle volume, the **interchange of gas between bubble and emulsion controls the entire reactor performance**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used in this Module:**
> - Gravitational acceleration: **$g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$**
> - Typical gas-phase diffusivity in fluidized beds: **$D_g \approx 2 \times 10^{-5}$ to $2 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$**
> - Bed voidage at minimum fluidization: **$\epsilon_{mf} \approx 0.4 - 0.55$** (Geldart B and D powders)

---

### 1.3 The Two-Phase Structure of a Bubbling Bed

A bubbling fluidized bed is conceptually divided into **three sub-phases**, each with distinct gas–solid flow characteristics:

| Sub-Phase | Solids Fraction | Gas Velocity | Role in Mass Transfer |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bubble phase** (b) | Negligible ($\sim 0$) | High (~$u_b$) | Carries gas through bed as voids |
| **Cloud + Wake** (c) | Same as $\epsilon_{mf}$ | Moves with bubble | Intermediate exchange medium |
| **Emulsion** (e) | At $\epsilon_{mf}$ | $U_{mf}/\epsilon_{mf}$ | The "sink/source" of reactants |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exam answers, always state the **two-phase theory assumption first**, then describe the bubble–cloud–wake structure. Examiners award the first 2 marks for correctly framing the assumption.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Logic — The "Why" and "How"

The mechanistic chain of events in a bubbling bed is as follows:

1. **Why bubbles form:** Once $U > U_{mf}$, the bed cannot accommodate additional gas by merely expanding; the gas consolidates into voids (bubbles) that rise through the suspension.
2. **How bubbles rise:** Bubbles rise at velocity $u_b$ (typically 0.5–5 m/s), which exceeds the interstitial gas velocity of the emulsion.
3. **Why a cloud forms:** Because the bubble rises faster than the gas can leak through the interface, a recirculating gas envelope (cloud) surrounds the bubble — this is the **Davidson–Harrison circulation criterion**.
4. **How interchange happens:** Two parallel mechanisms operate:
   - **Bulk throughflow**: gas flows *through* the bubble (cross-flow model).
   - **Diffusion**: molecular diffusion of species across the bubble boundary.
5. **Why the wake matters:** The wake transports solids (and entrained emulsion gas) upward with the bubble; it eventually sheds and re-mixes with the dense phase, causing **bulk gas exchange**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities use **SI units**. Symbols marked $(\ast)$ appear in KTU university exam problems with high frequency.

| # | Quantity | Equation | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Excess gas velocity $(\ast)$ | $U - U_{mf}$ | m/s | Gas bypassing the emulsion as bubbles |
| 2 | Bubble volumetric flow $(\ast)$ | $Q_b = (U - U_{mf})A$ | m³/s | Total bubble gas flow rate |
| 3 | Visible bubble flow rate | $Q_{b,vis} = Y(U - U_{mf})A$ | m³/s | $Y \approx 0.8$ for Geldart B powders |
| 4 | Cloud-to-bubble volume ratio $(\ast)$ | $f_c = \dfrac{3}{u_{br}/u_{mf} - 1}$ | — | Increases as bubble velocity drops |
| 5 | Wake-to-bubble volume ratio | $f_w \approx f_c$ (small bubbles) | — | Wake is shed periodically |
| 6 | Cloud radius $(ast)$ | $r_c = r_b \left(\dfrac{u_{br} + 2u_{mf}}{u_{br} - u_{mf}}\right)^{1/3}$ | m | Cloud envelope radius |
| 7 | Bubble-cloud interchange $(ast)$ | $K_{bc} = 4.5\dfrac{u_{mf}}{d_b} + 5.85\dfrac{D_g^{0.5} g^{0.25}}{d_b^{1.25}}$ | s⁻¹ | DH bulk + diffusion interchange |
| 8 | Cloud–emulsion interchange | $K_{ce} \approx 6.78 \left(\dfrac{\epsilon_{mf} D_g u_{br}}{d_b^{3}}\right)^{0.5}$ | s⁻¹ | Kunii–Levenspiel penetration model |
| 9 | Bubble rise velocity (large bubble) | $u_b = 0.711 \sqrt{g d_b}$ | m/s | Davies–Taylor correlation |
| 10 | Bubble rise velocity (small bubble) | $u_b = u_{br} = (U - U_{mf}) + 0.711 \sqrt{g d_b}$ | m/s | Two-region model |
| 11 | Effective interchange | $\dfrac{1}{K_{be}} = \dfrac{1}{K_{bc}} + \dfrac{1}{K_{ce}}$ | s⁻¹ | Resistances in series |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance for Engineers:** The value of $K_{be}$ dictates whether a bubbling bed behaves as a **well-mixed reactor** (large $K_{be}$, typical of small $d_b$) or as a **plug-flow reactor with bypassing** (small $K_{be}$, typical of large bubbles at high $U$). This is the central design lever in FCC regenerators, coal combustors, and dryers.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **FCC Regenerators** (petroleum refining): bubble size is engineered to maximize $K_{bc}$ and ensure uniform coke combustion.
- **Fluidized-bed dryers**: gas interchange determines drying uniformity; large bubbles cause channeling.
- **Ozone-driven wastewater treatment**: ozone in the bubble must interchange into the liquid-coated emulsion to be effective.
- **Drug-coating in fluidized bed granulators**: controlled interchange of coating vapor ensures uniform film deposition.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Cloud Volume Fraction ($f_c$) — Davidson–Harrison

We begin with the **continuity equation for gas flow around a single rising bubble**, considering the relative velocity of the bubble with respect to the dense phase.

**Step 1 — Define bubble relative velocity:**
The bubble rises faster than the surrounding gas, so we define a *relative* velocity:
$$u_{br} = u_b - \frac{U_{mf}}{\epsilon_{mf}}$$

**Step 2 — Define a stream function in spherical coordinates (potential flow analogy):**
For a sphere moving through a fluid, the stream function is
$$\psi = \frac{u_{br} r^2 \sin^2\theta}{2}$$

The radial and tangential components of velocity are
$$v_r = -\frac{1}{r^2 \sin\theta}\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta}$$
$$v_\theta = \frac{1}{r \sin\theta}\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the radial velocity at the bubble surface $r = r_b$:**
At $\theta = 0$ (top of bubble), the radial component is
$$v_r \vert_{r=r_b, \theta=0} = -u_{br}$$

**Step 4 — Determine the cloud boundary where the radial velocity reverses sign:**
The cloud is the region in which gas *circulates* with the bubble. The dividing surface is where $v_r = 0$ for all $\theta$ — the stream surface that encloses a constant volumetric flow rate equal to the throughflow.

Setting $\psi = \text{const}$ at the cloud surface and equating to the value at the equator of the bubble:
$$\frac{u_{br} r_c^2}{2} = \frac{u_{br} r_b^2}{2} \cdot f(u_{br}/u_{mf})$$

A detailed analysis (Davidson & Harrison, 1963) shows that the cloud is *smaller* than the bubble when $u_{br} > u_{mf}$ and that the throughflow rate through the bubble is
$$q = \pi r_b^2 u_{mf}$$

**Step 5 — Derive the volume ratio:**
The cloud is the volume occupied by the gas circulating with the bubble. The volume of the sphere of radius $r_c$ minus the bubble volume equals the cloud volume:
$$V_c = \frac{4}{3}\pi r_c^{3} - \frac{4}{3}\pi r_b^{3}$$

Dividing by the bubble volume $V_b = (4/3)\pi r_b^3$:
$$f_c = \frac{V_c}{V_b} = \left(\frac{r_c}{r_b}\right)^3 - 1$$

Substituting the Davidson–Harrison result
$$\frac{r_c}{r_b} = \left(\frac{u_{br} + 2 u_{mf}}{u_{br} - u_{mf}}\right)^{1/3}$$

gives the famous result:
$$f_c = \left(\frac{u_{br} + 2u_{mf}}{u_{br} - u_{mf}}\right) - 1 = \frac{3 u_{mf}}{u_{br} - u_{mf}}$$

Defining $u_{br}/u_{mf} = \alpha$ for compactness:
$$f_c = \frac{3}{\alpha - 1}$$

This is the result tabulated in the formula sheet. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limit check:** As $u_{br} \to u_{mf}$ (very slow bubble), $f_c \to \infty$ — physically, the cloud engulfs the entire emulsion. As $u_{br} \to \infty$, $f_c \to 0$ — fast bubbles carry no cloud.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Bubble–Cloud Interchange Coefficient ($K_{bc}$)

The Davidson–Harrison model splits $K_{bc}$ into two additive contributions.

**Step 1 — Bulk throughflow contribution:**
Gas enters the bottom of the bubble and exits the top at a rate $q = \pi r_b^2 u_{mf}$. The total gas flow rate through the bubble per unit volume of bubble is
$$K_{b,\text{bulk}} = \frac{\pi r_b^2 u_{mf}}{(4/3)\pi r_b^3} = \frac{3 u_{mf}}{4 r_b}$$

For a bubble in a cloud (i.e., accounting for the partial recirculation), the effective bulk-flow interchange is approximately
$$K_{b,\text{bulk}}^{\text{eff}} \approx 4.5 \frac{u_{mf}}{d_b}$$

(The factor $4.5$ comes from integrating over the spherical cap geometry — KTU students may quote this as a *semi-empirical constant*.)

**Step 2 — Molecular diffusion contribution:**
For diffusion through a stagnant boundary layer of effective thickness $\delta \sim (D_g / u_{br})^{0.5}$, the Sherwood-type expression is
$$K_{b,\text{diff}} \propto \frac{D_g^{0.5} u_{br}^{0.5}}{r_b}$$

Substituting $u_{br} \propto \sqrt{g d_b}$ (Davies–Taylor) and adjusting the numerical coefficient for spherical geometry gives
$$K_{b,\text{diff}} \approx 5.85 \frac{D_g^{0.5} g^{0.25}}{d_b^{1.25}}$$

**Step 3 — Add the two mechanisms (parallel transport):**
$$K_{bc} = K_{b,\text{bulk}}^{\text{eff}} + K_{b,\text{diff}}$$

$$\boxed{K_{bc} = 4.5 \frac{u_{mf}}{d_b} + 5.85 \frac{D_g^{0.5} g^{0.25}}{d_b^{1.25}}}$$

This is the **Davidson–Harrison interchange coefficient**. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Effective Interchange Coefficient ($K_{be}$)

The total resistance between bubble gas and emulsion gas is the sum of the bubble–cloud resistance and the cloud–emulsion resistance.

**Step 1 — Resistance analogy:**
Mass transfer resistance is analogous to electrical resistance:
$$R = \frac{1}{K}$$

**Step 2 — Two resistances in series:**
$$\frac{1}{K_{be}} = \frac{1}{K_{bc}} + \frac{1}{K_{ce}}$$

Solving for $K_{be}$:
$$K_{be} = \frac{K_{bc} \cdot K_{ce}}{K_{bc} + K_{ce}}$$

**Step 3 — Typical magnitude check:**
- $K_{bc} \sim 0.1 - 5 \ \text{s}^{-1}$
- $K_{ce} \sim 0.05 - 2 \ \text{s}^{-1}$
- Hence $K_{be} \sim 0.04 - 1.4 \ \text{s}^{-1}$

This $K_{be}$ is the input to the **Kunii–Levenspiel bubbling bed model** for reactor design (covered in detail in Module 4). $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Given:** A fluidized bed operates with $U = 0.40$ m/s, $U_{mf} = 0.05$ m/s, particle diameter $d_p = 500 \ \mu\text{m}$, gas diffusivity $D_g = 2 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$, $\epsilon_{mf} = 0.50$, and an estimated bubble diameter $d_b = 0.10$ m.

**Find:** $f_c$, $K_{bc}$, and the effective interchange $K_{be}$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Bubble rise velocity (Davies–Taylor):
$$u_b = 0.711 \sqrt{g d_b} = 0.711 \sqrt{9.81 \times 0.10} = 0.704 \ \text{m/s}$$

Step 2 — Relative velocity:
$$u_{br} = (U - U_{mf}) + u_b = (0.40 - 0.05) + 0.704 = 1.054 \ \text{m/s}$$

Step 3 — Cloud volume fraction:
$$f_c = \frac{3}{u_{br}/u_{mf} - 1} = \frac{3}{1.054/0.05 - 1} = \frac{3}{20.08} = 0.149$$

Step 4 — Bubble–cloud interchange:
$$K_{bc} = 4.5 \frac{u_{mf}}{d_b} + 5.85 \frac{D_g^{0.5} g^{0.25}}{d_b^{1.25}}$$
$$K_{bc} = 4.5 \frac{0.05}{0.10} + 5.85 \frac{(2\times10^{-5})^{0.5} (9.81)^{0.25}}{(0.10)^{1.25}}$$
$$K_{bc} = 2.25 + 5.85 \frac{4.472\times 10^{-3} \times 1.771}{0.0562}$$
$$K_{bc} = 2.25 + 5.85 \frac{7.92\times 10^{-3}}{0.0562} = 2.25 + 0.824 = 3.07 \ \text{s}^{-1}$$

Step 5 — Cloud–emulsion interchange:
$$K_{ce} = 6.78 \left(\frac{\epsilon_{mf} D_g u_{br}}{d_b^{3}}\right)^{0.5}$$
$$K_{ce} = 6.78 \left(\frac{0.50 \times 2\times 10^{-5} \times 1.054}{(0.10)^3}\right)^{0.5}$$
