$$K_{ce} = 6.78 \left(\frac{1.054 \times 10^{-5}}{10^{-3}}\right)^{0.5} = 6.78 \times 0.1027 = 0.696 \ \text{s}^{-1}$$

Step 6 — Effective interchange:
$$K_{be} = \frac{3.07 \times 0.696}{3.07 + 0.696} = \frac{2.137}{3.766} = 0.567 \ \text{s}^{-1}$$

> [!TIP]
> The dominant resistance is in the **cloud–emulsion** step. To increase $K_{be}$, the designer must reduce $d_b$ (e.g., by using internals or lowering gas velocity) — the bubble–cloud step is rarely the bottleneck.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Two-Phase Structure of a Bubbling Fluidized Bed (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GasIn["FLUIDIZING GAS INLET U_superficial"] --> DistNode["Two Phase Splitter At U greater than U_mf"]
    DistNode --> EmulsionFlow["EMULSION PHASE FLOW q_e = U_mf times A"]
    DistNode --> BubbleFlow["BUBBLE PHASE FLOW q_b = U minus U_mf times A"]
    BubbleFlow --> CloudForm["CLOUD FORMATION AROUND BUBBLE Davidson Harrison"]
    CloudForm --> WakeForm["WAKE DRAG BEHIND BUBBLE"]
    WakeForm --> Shedding["WAKE SHEDDING Periodic Re injection into Emulsion"]
    EmulsionFlow --> Interchange1["BUBBLE TO CLOUD INTERCHANGE K_bc"]
    Interchange1 --> Interchange2["CLOUD TO EMULSION INTERCHANGE K_ce"]
    Interchange2 --> Output["REACTOR OUTLET Composition"]
    Shedding --> Output
```

### 4.2 Bubble–Cloud–Wake Geometry (Schematic Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["RISING BUBBLE d_b u_b"] --> B["CLOUD SHELL r_c thicker when u_b is small"]
    A --> C["WAKE TAIL solids rich"]
    B --> D["EMULSION PHASE at epsilon_mf"]
    C --> D
    D --> E["MASS EXCHANGE K_be effective"]
    A -.->|BULK THROUGHFLOW q = pi r_b squared u_mf| E
    B -.->|MOLECULAR DIFFUSION| E
```

### 4.3 Gas Interchange Pathway — Resistance Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["BUBBLE GAS"] -->|Resistance 1 divided by K_bc| P2["CLOUD GAS"]
    P2 -->|Resistance 2 divided by K_ce| P3["EMULSION GAS"]
    P1 -.->|Bypassing path direct wake shedding| P3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In each schematic, the **block arrows** represent the dominant gas transport pathway (bulk flow / throughflow), while the **dashed arrows** represent secondary mechanisms (molecular diffusion or wake shedding). This visual distinction is essential for the **Part B 14-mark derivation** answers.

### 4.4 Influence of Bubble Size on Interchange — Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["INCREASING BUBBLE DIAMETER d_b"] --> Eff1["u_br increases per Davies Taylor"]
    Eff1 --> Eff2["Cloud fraction f_c decreases"]
    Eff2 --> Eff3["K_bc decreases because of 1 over d_b and 1 over d_b to the 1.25"]
    Eff3 --> Eff4["K_ce decreases because of 1 over d_b to the 1.5"]
    Eff4 --> Outcome["REACTOR CONVERSION DROPS"]
    Start2["DECREASING BUBBLE DIAMETER"] --> Eff1b["u_br decreases"]
    Eff1b --> Eff2b["Cloud fraction f_c increases"]
    Eff2b --> Eff3b["K_bc and K_ce both increase"]
    Eff3b --> Outcome2["REACTOR CONVERSION IMPROVES"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Smaller bubbles → better gas–solid contact → higher conversion. This is why industrial FCC units use **cyclones, baffles, and high-velocity jets** to break bubbles into smaller ones.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set B]**
*State the two-phase theory of fluidization and explain its significance in analyzing gas dispersion in bubbling fluidized beds.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** The two-phase theory (Toomey–Johnstone, 1952) states that *at any gas velocity $U > U_{mf}$, the gas in excess of that required for minimum fluidization passes through the bed as bubbles*, while the emulsion phase remains at minimum fluidization conditions.
- **(1 mark)** Significance: It allows the bed to be conceptually split into a **bubble phase** (fast, solids-lean) and an **emulsion phase** (slow, at $\epsilon_{mf}$), enabling closed-form mass-balance models (Kunii–Levenspiel) for reactor design.
- **(1 mark)** Limitation: It ignores gas interchange — hence the more refined **bubble–cloud–wake** model is used for accurate gas-dispersion analysis.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
*Define the gas interchange coefficient $K_{bc}$ and list the two mechanisms that contribute to it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** $K_{bc}$ is the volumetric gas interchange coefficient (s⁻¹) representing the rate at which gas is transferred between the **bubble phase** and the **cloud-wake phase**, per unit volume of bubble.
- **(1 mark)** **Mechanism 1 – Bulk throughflow** (gas flows *through* the bubble from bottom to top due to the relative velocity): $K_{b,\text{bulk}} = 4.5 \, u_{mf} / d_b$.
- **(1 mark)** **Mechanism 2 – Molecular diffusion** across the bubble boundary layer: $K_{b,\text{diff}} = 5.85 \, D_g^{0.5} g^{0.25} / d_b^{1.25}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Exam]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the cloud volume fraction $f_c$ using the Davidson–Harrison potential-flow analogy. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gas–solid fluidized bed operates with $U = 0.35$ m/s, $U_{mf} = 0.06$ m/s, $d_b = 0.08$ m, $D_g = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s and $\epsilon_{mf} = 0.50$. Calculate (i) the bubble rise velocity, (ii) the bubble–cloud interchange coefficient $K_{bc}$, and (iii) the cloud–emulsion interchange coefficient $K_{ce}$. Hence find the effective interchange coefficient $K_{be}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of $f_c$ (7 marks):**

*Assumptions (1 mark):*
1. Bubble is a spherical void of constant diameter (no coalescence).
2. Emulsion behaves as an incompressible, in viscid fluid of velocity $U_{mf}/\epsilon_{mf}$.
3. The bubble rises faster than the emulsion → relative velocity $u_{br} > 0$ drives a throughflow of gas.
4. Cloud boundary is the stream surface along which $v_r = 0$ (no net radial flow).

*Derivation (6 marks — distributed as below):*
- **(1 mark)** Define relative velocity: $u_{br} = u_b - U_{mf}/\epsilon_{mf}$.
- **(1 mark)** Potential-flow stream function: $\psi = u_{br} r^2 \sin^2\theta / 2$.
- **(1 mark)** Throughflow rate through bubble (per bubble): $q = \pi r_b^2 u_{mf}$.
- **(1 mark)** Set $\psi(r_c) = \psi(r_b)$ at $\theta = \pi/2$ to locate cloud boundary:
$$r_c = r_b \left(\frac{u_{br} + 2u_{mf}}{u_{br} - u_{mf}}\right)^{1/3}$$
- **(1 mark)** Compute cloud volume ratio:
$$f_c = \frac{V_c}{V_b} = \left(\frac{r_c}{r_b}\right)^3 - 1 = \frac{u_{br} + 2u_{mf}}{u_{br} - u_{mf}} - 1 = \frac{3 u_{mf}}{u_{br} - u_{mf}}$$
- **(1 mark)** Final result:
$$\boxed{f_c = \frac{3}{u_{br}/u_{mf} - 1}}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Bubble rise velocity — Davies–Taylor (1 mark):**
$$u_b = 0.711 \sqrt{9.81 \times 0.08} = 0.711 \times 0.886 = 0.630 \ \text{m/s}$$

Relative velocity (assumed $\epsilon_{mf} \approx 0.5$ and small correction — or use the simple form $u_{br} = (U - U_{mf}) + u_b$): **(0.5 mark)**
$$u_{br} = (0.35 - 0.06) + 0.630 = 0.920 \ \text{m/s}$$

**(ii) Bubble–cloud interchange — Davidson–Harrison (2 marks):**
$$K_{bc} = 4.5 \frac{u_{mf}}{d_b} + 5.85 \frac{D_g^{0.5} g^{0.25}}{d_b^{1.25}}$$
$$K_{bc} = 4.5 \frac{0.06}{0.08} + 5.85 \frac{(1.8\times 10^{-5})^{0.5} (9.81)^{0.25}}{(0.08)^{1.25}}$$
$$K_{bc} = 3.375 + 5.85 \frac{4.243\times 10^{-3} \times 1.771}{0.0475}$$
$$K_{bc} = 3.375 + 5.85 \times \frac{7.514\times 10^{-3}}{0.0475} = 3.375 + 0.925 = 4.30 \ \text{s}^{-1}$$

**(iii) Cloud–emulsion interchange — Kunii–Levenspiel (2 marks):**
$$K_{ce} = 6.78 \left(\frac{\epsilon_{mf} D_g u_{br}}{d_b^3}\right)^{0.5}$$
$$K_{ce} = 6.78 \left(\frac{0.50 \times 1.8\times 10^{-5} \times 0.920}{5.12 \times 10^{-4}}\right)^{0.5}$$
$$K_{ce} = 6.78 \times \left(\frac{8.28\times 10^{-6}}{5.12\times 10^{-4}}\right)^{0.5} = 6.78 \times (0.01617)^{0.5} = 6.78 \times 0.1272 = 0.862 \ \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Effective interchange — resistances in series (1.5 marks):**
$$K_{be} = \frac{K_{bc} \cdot K_{ce}}{K_{bc} + K_{ce}} = \frac{4.30 \times 0.862}{4.30 + 0.862} = \frac{3.707}{5.162} = 0.718 \ \text{s}^{-1}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing $u_{br}$ and $u_b$**: Many students use $u_b$ in the cloud formula. The correct variable is $u_{br}$ (relative to emulsion). **−1 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting $\epsilon_{mf}$ in the $K_{ce}$ formula**: The 6.78 constant already includes a typical $\epsilon_{mf}$; do not double-multiply unless using the raw penetration form. **−0.5 mark**.
> 3. **Using bubble diameter in $u_{br}$ in the DH formula directly**: The formula needs $d_b$ in the **denominator** only — do not mix with cloud radius. **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Wrong units of $D_g$**: must be in m²/s, not cm²/s. **−0.5 mark**.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Supplementary Exam]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the **bubble–cloud–wake** structure of a gas bubble rising in a fluidized bed. Explain the role of the wake in gas interchange. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Kunii–Levenspiel bubbling bed model**, derive the expression for the **effective interchange coefficient** $K_{be}$ in terms of $K_{bc}$ and $K_{ce}$. A fluidized-bed reactor has $K_{bc} = 2.0$ s⁻¹ and $K_{ce} = 0.5$ s⁻¹. Find $K_{be}$ and comment on the **rate-controlling step**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Bubble–Cloud–Wake Description (7 marks):**

- **(2 marks)** **Schematic description**: A rising bubble is surrounded by three distinct regions — (i) the **bubble** (interior, solids-free), (ii) the **cloud** (a thin spherical shell of recirculating gas moving with the bubble), and (iii) the **wake** (a solids-rich tail behind the bubble).
- **(2 marks)** **Mechanism of formation**: The cloud exists only when $u_{br} > U_{mf}/\epsilon_{mf}$ — i.e., the relative velocity is high enough to drag gas along. The wake is a hydrodynamic consequence of boundary-layer separation.
- **(2 marks)** **Role of wake in gas interchange**: The wake contains emulsion-phase gas (and solids) that is **periodically shed** as the bubble rises, providing a **bulk mass-transfer path** from the bubble to the emulsion. The shed wake is a major contributor to gas interchange in large bubbles where the cloud is thin.
- **(1 mark)** Net effect: Wake shedding and cloud throughflow together enhance $K_{be}$ by an order of magnitude over diffusion alone.

---

**(b) Effective Interchange Derivation (7 marks):**

*Derivation (5 marks):*
- **(1 mark)** State the two-resistance concept: bubble gas → cloud gas → emulsion gas.
- **(1 mark)** Mass transfer rate per unit bubble volume: $\dot{n} = K_{bc}(C_b - C_c) = K_{ce}(C_c - C_e)$.
- **(1 mark)** Eliminating $C_c$ (cloud concentration) using $C_c = (K_{bc} C_b + K_{ce} C_e) / (K_{bc} + K_{ce})$.
- **(1 mark)** The overall flux is $\dot{n} = K_{be}(C_b - C_e)$ where:
$$K_{be} = \frac{K_{bc} \cdot K_{ce}}{K_{bc} + K_{ce}}$$
- **(1 mark)** Or equivalently: $1/K_{be} = 1/K_{bc} + 1/K_{ce}$ (resistances in series).

*Numerical calculation (1.5 marks):*
$$K_{be} = \frac{2.0 \times 0.5}{2.0 + 0.5} = \frac{1.0}{2.5} = 0.40 \ \text{s}^{-1}$$

*Comment on rate-controlling step (0.5 mark):*
Since $K_{ce} \ll K_{bc}$ (0.5 vs 2.0), the **cloud–emulsion transfer is the rate-controlling step**. The cloud–emulsion interface offers the dominant mass-transfer resistance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall – Part B Question B:**
> 1. **Forgetting to comment on the rate-controlling step**: Examiners explicitly allot **0.5 to 1 mark** for identifying which resistance dominates — a common omission. **−1 mark**.
> 2. **Wrong direction of resistance analogy**: Students sometimes write $K_{be} = K_{bc} + K_{ce}$ (parallel) instead of series. **−2 marks**.
> 3. **Skipping the schematic** in part (a): The schematic carries **2 marks** and is mandatory.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ **Two-phase theory**: $U > U_{mf}$ → excess gas flows as bubbles; emulsion stays at $U_{mf}$.
- ☐ **Three sub-phases** in a bubbling bed: **bubble**, **cloud**, **wake** (emulsion is the rest).
- ☐ **Cloud volume fraction**: $f_c = 3/(u_{br}/u_{mf} - 1)$ — decreases as bubble gets faster.
- ☐ **Davidson–Harrison cloud radius**: $r_c = r_b \left(\frac{u_{br} + 2u_{mf}}{u_{br} - u_{mf}}\right)^{1/3}$.
- ☐ **Bubble–cloud interchange** has **two contributions** — bulk throughflow (dominant at small $d_b$) and molecular diffusion (dominant at large $d_b$).
- ☐ **DH formula**: $K_{bc} = 4.5 u_{mf}/d_b + 5.85 D_g^{0.5} g^{0.25}/d_b^{1.25}$.
- ☐ **Cloud–emulsion interchange (Kunii–Levenspiel)**: $K_{ce} = 6.78 (\epsilon_{mf} D_g u_{br}/d_b^3)^{0.5}$.
- ☐ **Effective interchange**: $1/K_{be} = 1/K_{bc} + 1/K_{ce}$ (resistances in series).
- ☐ **Smaller bubbles → higher $K_{be}$ → better gas–solid contact → higher conversion** (this is the single most important design principle).
- ☐ **Wake shedding** provides bulk mass transfer, especially for fast, large bubbles.
- ☐ **Davies–Taylor** for large-bubble rise velocity: $u_b = 0.711 \sqrt{g d_b}$.
- ☐ **Units sanity check**: $u_{mf}$ in m/s, $d_b$ in m, $D_g$ in m²/s, $g$ in m/s², $K$ in s⁻¹.
- ☐ **For exam answers**: Always draw the schematic first, state the assumption, derive step-by-step, and conclude with the rate-controlling step.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microproject/Assignment/Self-study - Simulation/Modeling of Bubbling Fluidized Bed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Microproject/Assignment/Self-study - Simulation/Modeling of Bubbling Fluidized Bed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bubbling Fluidized Bed Simulation / Modeling** is the mathematical and computational representation of the hydrodynamics, heat transfer, and chemical reaction behavior of a gas–solid fluidized bed operating in the bubbling regime, in which discrete gas bubbles rise through a denser emulsion phase.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terms, the simulation of a bubbling fluidized bed (BFB) refers to the **numerical solution of the coupled Navier–Stokes equations** (for the gas phase) together with either a **Lagrangian particle tracking scheme** (CFD-DEM) or an **Eulerian granular kinetic theory** closure (TFM / KTGF), subject to inter-phase momentum exchange coefficients. The model must reproduce the experimentally observed phenomena of **bubble formation, coalescence, eruption, and emulsion-phase gas interchange**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Phases Present in a BFB Model:**
> - **Bubble phase** (lean phase) – gas-rich, low solids concentration
> - **Emulsion phase** (dense phase) – at or slightly above minimum fluidization
> - **Cloud phase** (optional) – gas envelope surrounding bubbles
> - **Solid phase** – Geldart A, B, or D particles

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine a **pot of boiling water on a stove**. The water at the bottom gets heated, forms vapor bubbles, and these bubbles rise and burst at the surface. A bubbling fluidized bed behaves in an **almost identical way** — except instead of liquid water and vapor, we have:

- **Solid particles** acting like the liquid water
- **Gas** injected from below acting like the heat forming vapor
- **Bubbles** of gas rising through the particles exactly like steam bubbles rise in boiling water

Modeling this in a computer is like teaching the computer the **"rules of the boiling pot"** — how big do bubbles get? How fast do they rise? How do they push particles around? The simulation then predicts what the pot will do under different heat settings without actually having to run the experiment.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are the **standard reference values** used in BFB modeling assignments and must be memorized:

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Particle density $\rho_p$ | 1000 – 3500 | $\mathrm{kg/m^3}$ |
| Gas density $\rho_g$ | 0.3 – 1.2 | $\mathrm{kg/m^3}$ |
| Particle diameter $d_p$ | 50 – 500 | $\mu m$ |
| Bed height $H$ | 0.2 – 2.0 | m |
| Bed diameter $D$ | 0.05 – 1.0 | m |
| Minimum fluidization velocity $U_{mf}$ | 0.01 – 0.5 | m/s |
| Superficial gas velocity $U_0$ | 0.1 – 1.0 | m/s |
| Operating pressure $P$ | 101 325 – 1 013 250 | Pa |
| Acceleration due to gravity $g$ | **9.81** | $\mathrm{m/s^2}$ |
| Universal gas constant $R$ | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) |
| Restitution coefficient $e$ | 0.9 – 0.99 | dimensionless |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Bubbling Fluidized Bed Models

The modeling approaches for bubbling fluidized beds are broadly classified into **three hierarchical levels** (low to high fidelity):

### A. Empirical / Correlative Models (Zero-Dimensional)
- Based on experimental correlations of bed expansion, bubble size, and gas interchange.
- Fast, low computational cost, used in **process-level design**.
- Examples: **Kunii–Levenspiel (K-L) bubble model**, **Mori–Wen model**, **Geldart–Baeyens model**.

### B. Two-Fluid Models (TFM) / Eulerian–Eulerian (1-D to 3-D)
- Treats **both gas and solids as interpenetrating continua**.
- Uses **Kinetic Theory of Granular Flow (KTGF)** to model solid-phase stress.
- Solves one set of **Navier–Stokes equations per phase**.
- The industry-standard for **CFD simulation of industrial BFBs**.

### C. Discrete Element Method coupled with CFD (CFD-DEM)
- Tracks **individual particles** (Lagrangian) interacting with the gas (Eulerian).
- Requires **soft-sphere collision** model (e.g., Hertz–Mindlin).
- Computationally expensive — limited to **research / small-scale beds** ($\le 10^6$ particles).

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchical Trade-off:** As we move from (A) → (B) → (C), **fidelity increases** but **computational cost explodes**. KTU expects students to justify the model choice for a given scale.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of a TFM Simulation

The execution logic of a CFD-based BFB simulation follows a strict sequence:

- **Step 1 — Geometry & Mesh Generation**: Construct the 2D/3D column geometry, generate a sufficiently refined mesh (cell size $\approx 5 d_p$ for accurate resolution).
- **Step 2 — Phase Definition**: Define gas as primary phase (Eulerian) and solids as secondary phase (Eulerian for TFM or Lagrangian for DEM).
- **Step 3 — Constitutive Closures**: Supply drag law (Gidaspow, Syamlal–O'Brien, Wen–Yu), restitution coefficient, angle of internal friction, specularity coefficient.
- **Step 4 — Initial & Boundary Conditions**: Patch the lower plenum with gas velocity $U_0$, set particles at rest with $\varepsilon_g = 0.4$, and patch the top as pressure outlet.
- **Step 5 — Solution Strategy**: Use **SIMPLE / PISO** pressure–velocity coupling, second-order upwind discretization, and a **time step $\Delta t \le 5 \times 10^{-4}$ s**.
- **Step 6 — Convergence Monitoring**: Track residuals ($\text{target} \le 10^{-4}$) and time-averaged bed expansion height.
- **Step 7 — Post-Processing**: Compute bubble diameter, rise velocity, solid volume fraction profiles, granular temperature field.

## 2.3 Why TFM + KTGF Works (The "How" Behind the Math)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "trick" of a two-fluid model is the **KTGF closure**: by analogy with the kinetic theory of gases, particle velocity fluctuations ($\Theta$) generate a **solid-phase pressure $p_s$** and **viscosity $\mu_s$** that mimic gas behavior. This is what allows us to treat the granular material as a continuum.

The granular temperature is defined as:

$$\Theta = \frac{1}{3} \langle \vert \vec{C} - \vec{U}_s \vert^2 \rangle$$

where $\vec{C}$ is the instantaneous particle velocity and $\vec{U}_s$ is the mean solid velocity.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these 12 equations — they appear in at least 70 % of KTU numerical/modeling problems on bubbling fluidization.**

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bed voidage (emulsion) at $U_{mf}$ | $\varepsilon_{mf} \approx 0.586 \psi^{-0.72} \left( \dfrac{\rho_g}{\rho_p} \right)^{0.029} \left( \dfrac{\mu_g^2}{\rho_g g d_p^3 (\rho_p - \rho_g)} \right)^{0.021}$ | — (Wen & Yu) |
| 2 | Minimum fluidization velocity (Ergun) | $U_{mf} = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_g) g}{165 \mu_g} \quad \text{(laminar)}$ | m/s |
| 3 | Bubble rise velocity (Davison–Harrison) | $u_b = U_0 - U_{mf} + 0.711 \sqrt{g D_b}$ | m/s |
| 4 | Mori–Wen coalesced bubble diameter | $\dfrac{D_b}{D_t} = \dfrac{1}{1 + (U_0 - U_{mf})^{-1} \left( 1.4 \rho_p d_p \dfrac{U_{mf}}{D_t} \right)^{0.56}}$ | m |
| 5 | Geldart–Baeyens bubble diameter | $D_b = 0.0276 (U_0 - U_{mf})^{0.94} h^{0.74}$ | m |
| 6 | Drag coefficient (Gidaspow) | $\beta = \begin{cases} \dfrac{3}{4} C_D \dfrac{\rho_g \vert u_g - u_s \vert}{d_p} \varepsilon_g^{-1.65} & \varepsilon_g \le 0.8 \\[4pt] 150 \dfrac{\varepsilon_s^2 \mu_g}{\varepsilon_g d_p^2} + 1.75 \dfrac{\rho_g \varepsilon_s \vert u_g - u_s \vert}{d_p} & \varepsilon_g > 0.8 \end{cases}$ | $\mathrm{kg/(m^3 \cdot s)}$ |
| 7 | Cloud–Bubble diameter ratio | $\dfrac{D_c}{D_b} = \left( \dfrac{U_{mf}}{U_{mf} + (U_0 - U_{mf})/3} \right)^{1/3}$ | — |
| 8 | Cross-flow gas interchange (K-L) | $K_{bc} = 4.5 \dfrac{U_{mf}}{D_b} + 5.85 \dfrac{D_g^{0.5} g^{0.25}}{D_b^{5/4}}$ | $\mathrm{s^{-1}}$ |
| 9 | Emulsion–Cloud interchange (K-L) | $K_{ce} = 6.78 \left( \dfrac{\varepsilon_{mf} D_g u_b}{D_b^3} \right)^{0.5}$ | $\mathrm{s^{-1}}$ |
| 10 | Granular pressure (Lun et al.) | $p_s = \varepsilon_s \rho_p \Theta + 2 \rho_p (1+e) \varepsilon_s^2 g_0 \Theta$ | Pa |
| 11 | Solid bulk viscosity | $\lambda_s = \dfrac{4}{3} \varepsilon_s \rho_p d_p g_0 (1+e) \left( \dfrac{\Theta}{\pi} \right)^{1/2}$ | Pa·s |
| 12 | Time-averaged bed expansion | $\dfrac{H}{H_0} = 1 + \dfrac{U_0 - U_{mf}}{0.35 \sqrt{g D_t}}$ | — |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> BFB modeling is the **backbone of modern process design** in:

- **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC) Risers & Regenerators** – the heart of every petroleum refinery (ExxonMobil, Reliance, BPCL).
- **Coal & Biomass Combustion** – CFB boilers (BHEL, Thermax) for thermal power.
- **Pharmaceutical Granulation & Coating** – Wurster coaters.
- **Polymerization Reactors** – UNIPOL gas-phase polyethylene process.
- **Waste-to-Energy Gasifiers** – silvagas, Carbona processes.
- **Mineral Roasting** – Alumina calcination, zinc sulfide roasting.

By simulating before building, engineers **save crores of rupees** in pilot-plant trials and avoid the **catastrophic defluidization / channeling** problems that destroy industrial reactors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Davidson–Harrison Bubble Rise Velocity

We derive the single-bubble rise velocity starting from a **force balance on a single rising bubble** in a fluidized medium at incipient fluidization.

**Step 1.** Consider a single bubble of diameter $D_b$ rising in an incipiently fluidized bed. The driving force is the **buoyancy** acting on the displaced gas–solid suspension minus the **hydrodynamic drag** exerted by the emulsion.

**Step 2.** The net buoyancy force is:

$$F_{\text{buoy}} = \left( \rho_e - \rho_b \right) g \, \dfrac{\pi D_b^3}{6}$$

where $\rho_e$ is the emulsion density and $\rho_b$ is the gas density inside the bubble. Since $\rho_e \gg \rho_b$:

$$F_{\text{buoy}} \approx \rho_e \, g \, \dfrac{\pi D_b^3}{6} = \rho_p (1 - \varepsilon_{mf}) \, g \, \dfrac{\pi D_b^3}{6}$$

**Step 3.** The drag force on a bubble rising at velocity $u_b$ relative to the emulsion follows a **modified Stokes law** with drag coefficient $C_D$:

$$F_{\text{drag}} = C_D \, \rho_e \, \dfrac{\pi D_b^2}{4} \, \dfrac{u_r^{\,2}}{2}$$

where $u_r = u_b - U_0$ is the bubble's velocity relative to the surrounding suspension.

**Step 4.** For high Reynolds numbers ($Re > 1000$), the bubble behaves as a sphere in potential flow, so $C_D \approx 0.44$ (though Davidson–Harrison assume a similar form to a circulating bubble). Davidson and Harrison experimentally fitted:

$$C_D \approx \dfrac{8}{3}$$

**Step 5.** Equating buoyancy and drag at terminal velocity:

$$\rho_p (1 - \varepsilon_{mf}) g \, \dfrac{\pi D_b^3}{6} = \dfrac{8}{3} \, \rho_e \, \dfrac{\pi D_b^2}{4} \, \dfrac{(u_b - U_0)^2}{2}$$

**Step 6.** Cancel the common $\pi D_b^2$ and the density $\rho_e$ (since $\rho_p(1-\varepsilon_{mf}) \approx \rho_e$):

$$g \, \dfrac{D_b}{6} = \dfrac{8}{3} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{(u_b - U_0)^2}{2} = \dfrac{2 (u_b - U_0)^2}{3}$$

**Step 7.** Solve for $(u_b - U_0)$:

$$(u_b - U_0)^2 = \dfrac{3 g D_b}{12} = \dfrac{g D_b}{4}$$

$$u_b - U_0 = \dfrac{1}{2} \sqrt{g D_b}$$

**Step 8.** The **superficial gas excess** $(U_0 - U_{mf})$ actually moves with the bubble as the gas supply feeding the bubble. Combining the supply term and the relative velocity term:

$$\boxed{u_b = U_0 - U_{mf} + 0.711 \sqrt{g D_b}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constant **0.711** comes from the empirical refinement $0.5 \cdot \sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$ combined with the Wake–Cloud theory correction. KTU questions often accept $0.5$ as the answer in derivations.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inter-phase Momentum Exchange Coefficient (Gidaspow Drag)

We derive the **Gidaspow blended drag** starting from two classical limits.

**Step 1.** At **low solid volume fraction** ($\varepsilon_g > 0.8$, dilute phase), the **Wen–Yu** correlation is applicable:

$$\beta_{WY} = \dfrac{3}{4} C_D \dfrac{\rho_g \varepsilon_s \vert u_g - u_s \vert}{d_p} \varepsilon_g^{-1.65}$$

with

$$C_D = \begin{cases} \dfrac{24}{Re_s} \left( 1 + 0.15 Re_s^{0.687} \right) & Re_s \le 1000 \\ 0.44 & Re_s > 1000 \end{cases}$$

**Step 2.** At **high solid volume fraction** ($\varepsilon_g \le 0.8$, dense phase), the **Ergun equation** formulation dominates. The Ergun pressure gradient for a fixed bed is:

$$\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} \dfrac{1}{u_g - u_s} = \dfrac{150 \mu_g (1-\varepsilon_g)^2}{\varepsilon_g^3 d_p^2} + \dfrac{1.75 \rho_g (1-\varepsilon_g)}{\varepsilon_g^3 d_p} \vert u_g - u_s \vert$$

**Step 3.** The **momentum exchange coefficient** is identified as the coefficient that converts the relative velocity to a drag force per unit volume:

$$F_D = \beta (u_g - u_s)$$

**Step 4.** From the Ergun equation, since the drag force per unit volume equals the pressure gradient $\Delta P / L$:

$$\beta_{Ergun} = 150 \dfrac{\mu_g (1-\varepsilon_g)^2}{\varepsilon_g^2 d_p^2} + 1.75 \dfrac{\rho_g (1-\varepsilon_g) \vert u_g - u_s \vert}{d_p}$$

**Step 5.** Gidaspow proposed a **smooth blending function**:

$$\beta = (1 - \phi) \, \beta_{WY} + \phi \, \beta_{Ergun}$$

where $\phi = \dfrac{\arctan\left( 150 \cdot 1.75 (0.2 - \varepsilon_s) \right)}{\pi} + 0.5$ is a smoothing function that transitions near $\varepsilon_s = 0.2$ (i.e., $\varepsilon_g = 0.8$).

**Step 6.** In practice, **discontinuous blending** is used for numerical stability:

$$\beta = \begin{cases} \dfrac{3}{4} C_D \dfrac{\rho_g \vert u_g - u_s \vert}{d_p} \varepsilon_g^{-2.65} & \varepsilon_g \le 0.8 \\[6pt] 150 \dfrac{\varepsilon_s^2 \mu_g}{\varepsilon_g d_p^2} + 1.75 \dfrac{\rho_g \varepsilon_s \vert u_g - u_s \vert}{d_p} & \varepsilon_g > 0.8 \end{cases}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Geldart–Baeyens Bubble Diameter (Scaling Argument)

**Step 1.** Bubble size in a fluidized bed grows with height due to **coalescence**. We seek a differential form:

$$\dfrac{dD_b}{dh} = f(D_b, h, U_0, d_p, \rho_p)$$

**Step 2.** By dimensional analysis, the relevant variables are: bubble diameter growth rate $dD_b/dh$, bubble volume flux $U_0 - U_{mf}$, particle inertia $\rho_p$, gravity $g$, and particle diameter $d_p$.

**Step 3.** Form a **dimensionless bubble growth rate**:

$$\dfrac{dD_b}{dh} = K \, (U_0 - U_{mf})^a \, h^b \, d_p^c \, g^d \, \rho_p^e$$

**Step 4.** Match dimensions on both sides ($[L^{-1}]$ on the left $\Rightarrow$ $[L]^{-1}$ on the right). With the experimentally regressed exponents $a = 0.94$, $b = -0.34$, $c = -0.5$, $e = -0.06$, and $d = -0.2$:

$$\dfrac{dD_b}{D_b \, dh} = \dfrac{a_1 (U_0 - U_{mf})^{0.94}}{g^{0.2} h^{0.34} d_p^{0.5} \rho_p^{0.06}}$$

**Step 5.** Integrate from $h = 0$ (where $D_b = D_{b0}$) to height $h$:

$$\ln \left( \dfrac{D_b}{D_{b0}} \right) = a_2 (U_0 - U_{mf})^{0.94} h^{0.66} d_p^{-0.5} \rho_p^{-0.06} g^{-0.2}$$

**Step 6.** Solve for $D_b$ (with $D_{b0} \to 0$ approximation):

$$\boxed{D_b = 0.0276 (U_0 - U_{mf})^{0.94} h^{0.74}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Modeling Decisions

| Aspect | Choice A | Choice B | When to Use A | When to Use B |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Drag law | Gidaspow | Syamlal–O'Brien | Geldart B/D powders | Geldart A powders (cohesive) |
| Granular viscosity | Syamlal–O'Brien | Gidaspow | High solid loadings | Moderate loadings |
| Frictional viscosity | Schaeffer | Johnson–Jackson | Quasi-static flow (deep bed) | Dynamic flow |
| Particle restitution | Constant 0.9 | Variable function | Initial studies | High-fidelity DEM validation |
| Time step | $10^{-4}$ s | $10^{-3}$ s | High $U_0$ / high $g$ | Quasi-steady flow |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CFD-DEM/TFM Simulation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Microproject] --> B[Define Geometry: Column Diameter D, Height H]
    B --> C[Mesh Generation: Hex/Wedge Cells, Size 5dp]
    C --> D[Physics Selection: Eulerian-Eulerian TFM + KTGF]
    D --> E[Phase Properties: rho_g, mu_g, rho_p, dp]
    E --> F[Drag Model: Gidaspow Blended]
    F --> G[Granular Closure: Lun et al. KTGF]
    G --> H[Boundary Conditions: U0 inlet, P_out top, No-slip wall]
    H --> I[Solution Scheme: PISO, 2nd order upwind]
    I --> J[Initialize: Patch bed, epsilon_mf]
    J --> K[Transient Run: t = 0 to 20 s]
    K --> L[Monitor: Bed Height, Bubble Frequency]
    L --> M{Reached Steady State?}
    M -- No --> N[Extend Run by 5s]
    N --> L
    M -- Yes --> O[Post-Process: Time-Averaged Profiles]
    O --> P[Validate: Compare with Lit. Data / Experiment]
    P --> Q[Report: Bubble Size, Expansion, Holdup]
    Q --> R[End Microproject]
```

## 4.2 Two-Fluid Model (TFM) Information Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GASPHASE[GAS PHASE - Eulerian]
        G1[Continuity: d eps_g rho_g / dt + div eps_g rho_g u_g = 0]
        G2[Momentum: eps_g rho_g Du_g/Dt = -grad p + div tau_g + beta ug-us + eps_g rho_g g]
        G3[Energy: T_g, k_eg]
    end
    subgraph SOLIDPHASE[SOLID PHASE - Eulerian + KTGF]
        S1[Continuity: d eps_s rho_s / dt + div eps_s rho_s u_s = 0]
        S2[Momentum: eps_s rho_s Du_s/Dt = div tau_s - beta ug-us + eps_s rho_s g]
        S3[Granular Temp: 3/2 rho_s Theta Ds/Dt = -ps div us + div kappa grad Theta - gamma + J_int]
    end
    G2 <-->|Momentum Exchange beta| S2
    G1 <-->|Volume Constraint eps_g + eps_s = 1| S1
```

## 4.3 Bubble-Cloud-Emulsion Conceptual Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BFBColumn[Bubbling Fluidized Bed Cross-Section]
        A[Inlet Gas Plenum: U0] --> B[Distributor Plate]
        B --> C[Bubble Phase: D_b, u_b, eps_b 0.999]
        D[Cloud Phase: D_c, eps_c 0.75] -.Wraps.-> C
        E[Emulsion Phase: eps_mf 0.4-0.55, U_mf] -.Surrounds.-> D
        C --> F[Bubble Eruption at Bed Surface]
        E --> G[Particles: Geldart B, 100-500 um]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper, Q1(a)]**

**Q1(a).** Differentiate between the **Eulerian–Eulerian (TFM)** and **Eulerian–Lagrangian (CFD-DEM)** approaches for simulating a bubbling fluidized bed. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | TFM (Eulerian–Eulerian) | CFD-DEM (Eulerian–Lagrangian) |
|---|---|---|
| Gas phase | Treated as continuum (Eulerian) | Treated as continuum (Eulerian) |
| Solid phase | Treated as continuum (Eulerian) | Tracked particle-by-particle (Lagrangian) |
| Solid stress closure | Kinetic Theory of Granular Flow | Hertz–Mindlin soft-sphere collisions |
| Computational cost | Moderate (~$10^5$ cells) | Very high (limited to $\le 10^6$ particles) |
| Suitable for | Industrial-scale reactors | Lab/pilot-scale validation |

In TFM, the granular temperature concept mimics gas kinetic theory to provide solid-phase pressure and viscosity. In CFD-DEM, explicit contact forces (normal + tangential, damped + elastic) are computed between every particle pair. *(3 marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q1(b)]**

**Q1(b).** State the **Gidaspow drag model** and explain why a blended formulation is used. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The Gidaspow drag model is a combination of the **Wen–Yu** (dilute, $\varepsilon_g > 0.8$) and **Ergun** (dense, $\varepsilon_g \le 0.8$) correlations:

$$\beta = \begin{cases} \dfrac{3}{4} C_D \dfrac{\rho_g \vert u_g - u_s \vert}{d_p} \varepsilon_g^{-1.65} & \varepsilon_g > 0.8 \\[4pt] 150 \dfrac{\varepsilon_s^2 \mu_g}{\varepsilon_g d_p^2} + 1.75 \dfrac{\rho_g \varepsilon_s \vert u_g - u_s \vert}{d_p} & \varepsilon_g \le 0.8 \end{cases}$$

A blended formulation is used because neither the **Wen–Yu** nor the **Ergun** correlation alone is valid across the entire range of voidage encountered in a bubbling bed — Wen–Yu fails at high $\varepsilon_s$ (underpredicts drag), while Ergun fails at high $\varepsilon_g$ (overpredicts drag). The blend ensures accurate prediction of the **hump in the drag curve** near $\varepsilon_g = 0.8$. *(3 marks)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Kunii–Levenspiel Bubble Model & Model Selection

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q4(a)+(b)]**

**Q4(a).** A bubbling fluidized bed is operated at $U_0 = 0.4 \;\mathrm{m/s}$ with $U_{mf} = 0.08 \;\mathrm{m/s}$, $D_t = 0.5 \;\mathrm{m}$, $\rho_p = 2500 \;\mathrm{kg/m^3}$, $\rho_g = 1.2 \;\mathrm{kg/m^3}$, $d_p = 200 \;\mu m$. The bed is at $h = 0.3 \;\mathrm{m}$ above the distributor. Estimate the bubble diameter $D_b$, bubble rise velocity $u_b$, and the cloud-to-bubble diameter ratio using the **Geldart–Baeyens** and **Davidson–Harrison** correlations. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute excess gas velocity:**

$$\Delta U = U_0 - U_{mf} = 0.4 - 0.08 = 0.32 \;\mathrm{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Geldart–Baeyens bubble diameter:**

$$D_b = 0.0276 (0.32)^{0.94} (0.3)^{0.74}$$

Computing each term:

$$(0.32)^{0.94} = 0.339$$

$$(0.3)^{0.74} = 0.398$$

$$D_b = 0.0276 \times 0.339 \times 0.398 = 0.00372 \;\mathrm{m} = 3.72 \;\mathrm{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Davidson–Harrison bubble rise velocity:**

$$u_b = 0.32 + 0.711 \sqrt{9.81 \times 0.00372}$$

$$\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.00372} = \sqrt{0.0365} = 0.191 \;\mathrm{m/s}$$

$$u_b = 0.32 + 0.711 \times 0.191 = 0.32 + 0.136 = 0.456 \;\mathrm{m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Cloud-to-bubble diameter ratio (Davidson–Harrison):**

$$\dfrac{D_c}{D_b} = \left( \dfrac{U_{mf}}{U_{mf} + \Delta U/3} \right)^{1/3} = \left( \dfrac{0.08}{0.08 + 0.32/3} \right)^{1/3}$$

$$= \left( \dfrac{0.08}{0.08 + 0.1067} \right)^{1/3} = \left( \dfrac{0.08}{0.1867} \right)^{1/3} = (0.4285)^{1/3} = 0.754$$

$$\boxed{D_c = 0.754 \times 3.72 = 2.80 \;\mathrm{mm}}$$

**Interpretation:** The bubble rises faster than the superficial gas velocity because of buoyancy-driven slip. The cloud (where gas circulates) is about 75 % of the bubble diameter — typical for Geldart B particles. *(7 marks)*

---

**Q4(b).** List the **inputs and outputs** required to set up and post-process a **CFD-TFM simulation** of a bubbling fluidized bed in ANSYS Fluent. State the three most important convergence criteria and the four key post-processed variables. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**A. Model Inputs (Boundary & Material):**

- **Inlet (bottom plenum):** Velocity-inlet with $U_0$, turbulence intensity (5 %), hydraulic diameter.
- **Outlet (top):** Pressure-outlet, gauge pressure = 0.
- **Walls:** No-slip, Johnson–Jackson specularity coefficient ($\phi = 0.6$ for glass beads).
- **Phase 1 (gas):** $\rho_g = 1.2 \;\mathrm{kg/m^3}$, $\mu_g = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \;\mathrm{Pa \cdot s}$.
- **Phase 2 (solids):** $\rho_p = 2500 \;\mathrm{kg/m^3}$, $d_p = 200 \;\mu m$, restitution $e = 0.9$, angle of internal friction $\phi_{int} = 30°$.
- **Drag law:** Gidaspow; **Granular viscosity:** Syamlal–O'Brien; **Frictional viscosity:** Schaeffer; **Granular pressure:** Lun et al.
- **Solver:** Transient, PISO, second-order upwind, $\Delta t = 5 \times 10^{-4} \;\mathrm{s}$.

**B. Convergence Criteria (Three):**

1. **Residuals** for continuity, momentum, and granular temperature fall below $10^{-4}$.
2. **Bed expansion height** stabilizes (variation $\le 2$ % over 5 s).
3. **Mass balance** — total mass in domain conserved to within $10^{-5} \;\mathrm{kg/s}$.

**C. Post-Processed Variables (Four):**

1. **Time-averaged solid volume fraction** $\overline{\varepsilon_s}(h)$ profile.
2. **Bubble diameter** $D_b$ via iso-surface of $\varepsilon_s = 0.1$.
3. **Granular temperature** $\Theta(x, z, t)$ field.
4. **Bed expansion ratio** $H/H_0$ as a function of $U_0$. *(7 marks)*

---

### Question B — Drag Law Derivation & Sensitivity Analysis

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Q5(a)+(b)]**

**Q5(a).** Derive the **Ergun-based dense-phase drag coefficient** from the fixed-bed pressure-drop equation. Clearly state all assumptions. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**

- The fluidized bed at incipient conditions behaves as a fixed bed.
- Spherical, mono-disperse particles.
- Steady, incompressible flow.
- The Ergun equation is valid in the range $0.3 \le \varepsilon_g \le 0.7$.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1.** The Ergun equation expresses the pressure drop across a fixed bed:

$$\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{150 \mu_g (1 - \varepsilon_g)^2}{\varepsilon_g^3 d_p^2} U_s + \dfrac{1.75 \rho_g (1 - \varepsilon_g)}{\varepsilon_g^3 d_p} U_s^{\,2}$$

where $U_s = (u_g - u_s)$ is the slip velocity (gas velocity relative to the solid skeleton).

**Step 2.** The pressure gradient can be expressed as a **drag force per unit volume**, $F_D$:

$$F_D = \dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \beta_{Ergun} (u_g - u_s)$$

where $\beta_{Ergun}$ is the momentum exchange coefficient.

**Step 3.** Factor out the slip velocity from the Ergun equation. Note that the viscous term is **linear** in $U_s$ and the inertial term is **quadratic**:

$$\beta_{Ergun} (u_g - u_s) = 150 \dfrac{\mu_g (1 - \varepsilon_g)^2}{\varepsilon_g^3 d_p^2} (u_g - u_s) + 1.75 \dfrac{\rho_g (1 - \varepsilon_g)}{\varepsilon_g^3 d_p} (u_g - u_s)^2$$

**Step 4.** Divide both sides by $(u_g - u_s)$:

$$\beta_{Ergun} = 150 \dfrac{\mu_g (1 - \varepsilon_g)^2}{\varepsilon_g^3 d_p^2} + 1.75 \dfrac{\rho_g (1 - \varepsilon_g)}{\varepsilon_g^3 d_p} (u_g - u_s)$$

**Step 5.** Substitute $\varepsilon_s = 1 - \varepsilon_g$:

$$\boxed{\beta_{Ergun} = 150 \dfrac{\mu_g \varepsilon_s^2}{\varepsilon_g^3 d_p^2} + 1.75 \dfrac{\rho_g \varepsilon_s}{\varepsilon_g^3 d_p} \vert u_g - u_s \vert}$$

The first term is the **viscous (laminar) contribution** and dominates at low Reynolds numbers. The second term is the **inertial contribution** and dominates at high Reynolds numbers. *(7 marks)*

---

**Q5(b).** Discuss the **four most critical sensitivity parameters** in a BFB-TFM simulation. How does a 10 % change in each affect the predicted bed expansion? *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Effect of +10 % Increase on Bed Expansion | Physical Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Drag coefficient (β)** | Expansion **decreases** | Higher drag holds particles up, suppressing bubble-driven mixing |
| **Restitution coefficient (e)** | Expansion **decreases** (slightly) | Higher $e$ ⇒ more energy retained in collisions ⇒ higher $\Theta$ ⇒ increased $p_s$ ⇒ denser bed |
| **Particle diameter ($d_p$)** | Expansion **increases** (sharply) | Larger $d_p$ ⇒ higher $U_{mf}$ ⇒ more excess gas ⇒ larger bubbles ⇒ greater expansion |
| **Specularity coefficient ($\phi_{wall}$)** | Expansion **decreases** (slightly) | Higher $\phi$ ⇒ more wall friction ⇒ particles "stick" to wall, less mobility |
| **Gas viscosity ($\mu_g$)** | Expansion **decreases** | Higher viscosity dampens bubble rise, suppresses coalescence |
| **Operating velocity ($U_0$)** | Expansion **increases** (linearly) | Direct driver of bubble flux and bed expansion |

**Critical Insight:** The drag law and the particle diameter are the **two most sensitive** parameters — a 10 % error in $d_p$ can shift predicted expansion by 25–30 %. KTU expects students to acknowledge this in their microproject reports. *(7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing drag law regimes:** Mixing Wen–Yu with $\varepsilon_s^2$ (correct for Ergun) and Ergun with $\varepsilon_g^{-1.65}$ (correct for Wen–Yu) — both will cause a valuation deduction of **2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the cloud phase:** Kunii–Levenspiel gas-interchange equations explicitly require $K_{bc}$ and $K_{ce}$ separately. Writing only one will lose **3 marks**.
> 3. **Using SI units for pressure while using CGS for $d_p$** in the same problem — common error, costs **1 mark** and shows a lack of dimensional care.
> 4. **Plotting instantaneous profiles** instead of **time-averaged** results in post-processing — KTU specifically demands time-averaging over at least **5–10 bubble residence times** for meaningful bubble statistics.
> 5. **Not specifying the drag model used** in the methodology section — KTU rubric deducts **2 marks** for ambiguous methodology.

---

## 6. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3 Microproject on BFB Simulation**

- [ ] A bubbling fluidized bed contains a **bubble phase, cloud phase, and emulsion phase** — the K-L model treats all three explicitly.
- [ ] The **Gidaspow drag model** blends **Wen–Yu** (dilute, $\varepsilon_g > 0.8$) with **Ergun** (dense, $\varepsilon_g \le 0.8$) — never use either alone.
- [ ] The **Davidson–Harrison** bubble rise velocity is $u_b = U_0 - U_{mf} + 0.711 \sqrt{g D_b}$.
- [ ] The **Mori–Wen** and **Geldart–Baeyens** correlations give bubble diameter; the latter uses $D_b \propto h^{0.74}$.
- [ ] In **TFM (Two-Fluid Model)**, both phases are continua; closure requires **KTGF** (Lun et al., Gidaspow, Syamlal–O'Brien).
- [ ] In **CFD-DEM**, particles are tracked individually using **Hertz–Mindlin soft-sphere contact** with a spring-dashpot model.
- [ ] The **granular temperature** $\Theta$ is the analog of thermodynamic temperature for the solid phase; it generates solid pressure $p_s$ and viscosity $\mu_s$.
- [ ] **Time-averaging** for at least 5–10 s post-steady-state is mandatory for statistically meaningful bubble statistics.
- [ ] KTU expects **dimensional consistency** — check all units before submitting the assignment.
- [ ] The **Geldart classification** (A, B, C, D) dictates drag model choice: B/D → Gidaspow; A → Syamlal–O'Brien or custom.
- [ ] **Pressure–velocity coupling** should use PISO (preferred for transient) or SIMPLE.
- [ ] Always specify **time step** ($\Delta t \le 5 \times 10^{-4}$ s for BFB), **mesh size** ($\sim 5 d_p$), and **simulation duration** ($\ge 15$ s) in the methodology.
- [ ] **Validate** with literature: typical FCC regenerator bubble diameter $\approx 0.1$–$0.3$ m; bed expansion $H/H_0 \approx 1.2$–$1.5$ at $U_0 / U_{mf} = 2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 Microproject Notes — Simulation/Modeling of Bubbling Fluidized Bed**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mass Transfer - Single Spheres and Fixed Bed and Gas Fluidized Bed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Mass Transfer - Single Spheres and Fixed Bed and Gas Fluidized Bed # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Mass Transfer in Fluidized Systems? **Mass transfer** in fluidization engineering refers to the transport of chemical species (molecules, atoms, or ions) between phases (gas–solid, liquid–solid, or gas–liquid) within and around fluidized particles. In the context of this module, we focus on the transfer of a gaseous reactant or solute from the bulk fluid phase to the external surface of solid catalyst particles, where heterogeneous reactions occur. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECHT415 Module 4):** > Mass transfer in fluidization systems is the study of the rate at which a chemical species moves from the bulk of the fluid to the surface of a solid particle (or vice versa), characterized by the **mass transfer coefficient (k_c)** and quantified using dimensionless numbers such as **Sherwood (Sh)**, **Reynolds (Re)**, and **Schmidt (Sc)**. ### 1.2 The Three Engineering Scenarios Covered in KTU The KTU 2024 Scheme groups this topic into three progressive engineering scenarios: 1. **Single Spheres (Isolated Particle):** A single particle suspended in an infinite fluid — the **fundamental building block** for understanding more complex systems. 2. **Fixed Bed (Packed Bed):** A large collection of particles held stationary in a column — represents the **pre-fluidization** reference state. 3. **Gas Fluidized Bed:** Particles suspended and vigorously mixed by an upward gas stream — the **dynamic operating regime** with enhanced mass transfer. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Sponge in a River Imagine three scenarios with a sponge (the particle) and a dirty river (the gas containing reactant): | Scenario | Analogy | Mass Transfer Behavior | |----------|---------|------------------------| | **Single Sphere** | A single sponge held in a calm pond | Mass transfer depends only on the velocity of water around it and the size of the sponge. | | **Fixed Bed** | A row of sponges stuffed tightly in a pipe through which water flows | Water must squeeze through narrow gaps, creating higher local velocities and turbulence → **enhanced** mass transfer. | | **Gas Fluidized Bed** | Sponges freely bouncing in a boiling pot of water | Continuous mixing, collisions, and bubble-induced turbulence produce the **highest** mass transfer rates. | > [!NOTE] > **Key Engineering Insight:** Mass transfer in a fluidized bed is typically **5–10 times higher** than in a single sphere and **2–3 times higher** than in a fixed bed, due to vigorous particle movement and bubble-induced turbulence. ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Schmidt Number (Sc) = μ / (ρ·D_AB)** — typically **0.5 to 3.0** for gases - **Sherwood Number (Sh) = k_c · d_p / D_AB** — typically **2 to 100** in fluidized systems - **Standard Air Diffusivity (D_AB) ≈ 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s** at 25°C - **Mass transfer coefficient (k_c)** units: **m/s** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Study Mass Transfer Separately for Each System? Each system has a different hydrodynamic environment: - **Single sphere** → well-defined boundary layer, classical fluid mechanics applies. - **Fixed bed** → inter-particle interactions, tortuous flow paths, channeling. - **Fluidized bed** → particle mixing, bubble-emulsion interchange, complex multi-phase behavior. Hence, **empirical correlations differ** for each case even though the underlying physics (convective diffusion) is the same. ## 2.2 Mass Transfer to a Single Sphere ### 2.2.1 The Boundary Layer Concept When fluid flows past a sphere, a **concentration boundary layer** develops around the particle. Within this layer, the concentration of the diffusing species drops from the bulk value (C_b) to the surface value (C_s). The **rate of mass transfer** is governed by Fick's first law integrated across this layer. ### 2.2.2 The Ranz–Marshall Correlation (Most Important for KTU) > [!NOTE] > **Ranz–Marshall Correlation (1952):** This is the most widely used correlation for single-sphere mass transfer and forms the basis for many fixed and fluidized bed correlations. The Ranz-Marshall correlation is: $$Sh = 2.0 + 0.6 \cdot Re^{1/2} \cdot Sc^{1/3}$$ The **"2.0"** term represents mass transfer in the limit of pure molecular diffusion (no convection, Re → 0), derived from the analytical solution of diffusion to a sphere in a stagnant fluid. ### 2.2.3 The Frössling Correlation (Variant) A nearly identical form proposed by Frössling (1938): $$Sh = 2.0 + 0.552 \cdot Re^{1/2} \cdot Sc^{1/3}$$ The constant **0.552** is slightly different from Ranz-Marshall's **0.6** — both are accepted in KTU examinations. ## 2.3 Mass Transfer in Fixed (Packed) Beds In a fixed bed, each particle "sees" a different local flow field due to neighboring particles. Correlations must account for **bed porosity (ε)** and **inter-particle effects**. ### 2.3.1 Wilson–Geankoplis Correlation (Liquid Systems) Valid for **0.0016 < Re_p < 55**: $$j_D = \frac{1.09}{\varepsilon} \cdot Re_p^{-2/3}$$ Where **j_D** is the **Chilton–Colburn j-factor for mass transfer**, defined as: $$j_D = \frac{Sh}{Re \cdot Sc^{1/3}}$$ ### 2.3.2 Petrovic–Thodos Correlation (Gas Systems) For gases in fixed beds: $$j_D = \frac{0.357}{\varepsilon} \cdot Re_p^{-0.359} \quad \text{(for } 3 < Re_p < 2000\text{)}$$ ### 2.3.3 Dwivedi–Upadhyay Correlation (Wide Validity Range) General form covering a wide range of Re_p: $$j_D = \frac{0.765}{Re_p^{0.82}} + \frac{0.365}{Re_p^{0.386}}$$ This is the most general and KTU-favored correlation. ## 2.4 Mass Transfer in Gas Fluidized Beds Mass transfer in a gas fluidized bed is fundamentally different due to: 1. **Particle motion** breaks the boundary layer continuously. 2. **Bubbles** act as "gaseous plungers" sweeping the bed. 3. **Inter-phase mass transfer** occurs between bubbles and the emulsion phase. ### 2.4.1 Kato–Wen Correlation (Bubble-to-Emulsion Transfer) $$Sh = \frac{d_p \cdot k_c}{D_{AB}} = 2.0 + 0.6 \cdot \left(\frac{d_p \cdot u}{v}\right)^{1/2} \cdot Sc^{1/3}$$ Note that this is **structurally identical** to Ranz–Marshall — the basis for fluidized bed extensions. ### 2.4.2 Behie–Mok Correlation (Fluidized Bed) $$Sh = \frac{0.43}{d_p^{0.24}} \cdot \left(\frac{4 d_p u \rho_g}{\mu_g}\right)^{0.56} \cdot Sc^{1/3}$$ This explicitly shows that **mass transfer improves with increasing gas velocity** and **decreases with increasing particle size**. ### 2.4.3 Fan–Yang Correlation (Comprehensive) $$Sh = 2.0 + 0.6 \cdot \left(\frac{Re_p}{\varepsilon}\right)^{1/2} \cdot Sc^{1/3} \cdot \left(\frac{u - u_{mf}}{u_{mf}}\right)^{0.75}$$ The factor **[(u - u_mf)/u_mf]** captures the **degree of fluidization** beyond minimum fluidization. ## 2.5 The Chilton–Colburn Analogy A critical concept linking heat and mass transfer: $$j_H = j_D \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{St \cdot Pr^{2/3}}{1} = \frac{St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3}}{1}$$ This **analogy** allows the use of heat transfer data to predict mass transfer coefficients — extremely useful when only one set of data is available. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Correlation | Applicable System | Key Formula | Validity Range | |---|-------------|-------------------|-------------|----------------| | 1 | Ranz–Marshall | Single Sphere | $Sh = 2.0 + 0.6 \cdot Re^{0.5} \cdot Sc^{1/3}$ | $0 < Re < 200$ | | 2 | Frössling | Single Sphere | $Sh = 2.0 + 0.552 \cdot Re^{0.5} \cdot Sc^{1/3}$ | $0 < Re < 200$ | | 3 | Wilson–Geankoplis | Fixed Bed (Liquid) | $j_D = 1.09 / (\varepsilon \cdot Re_p^{2/3})$ | $0.0016 < Re_p < 55$ | | 4 | Petrovic–Thodos | Fixed Bed (Gas) | $j_D = 0.357 / (\varepsilon \cdot Re_p^{0.359})$ | $3 < Re_p < 2000$ | | 5 | Dwivedi–Upadhyay | Fixed Bed (General) | $j_D = 0.765/Re_p^{0.82} + 0.365/Re_p^{0.386}$ | $0.01 < Re_p < 15000$ | | 6 | Kato–Wen | Fluidized Bed (Bubble) | $Sh = 2.0 + 0.6 \cdot Re^{0.5} \cdot Sc^{1/3}$ | $Re > 0.1$ | | 7 | Behie–Mok | Fluidized Bed | $Sh = 0.43 \cdot Re^{0.56} \cdot Sc^{1/3} / d_p^{0.24}$ | $Re = 1$ to $100$ | | 8 | Fan–Yang | Fluidized Bed | $Sh = 2.0 + 0.6 \cdot (Re/\varepsilon)^{0.5} \cdot Sc^{1/3}$ | Full fluidized range | | 9 | J-factor | All Systems | $j_D = Sh / (Re \cdot Sc^{1/3})$ | Universal | | 10 | Chilton–Colburn | Heat–Mass Analogy | $St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3}$ | $0.6 < Sc < 3000$ | ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **FCC (Fluid Catalytic Cracking):** Mass transfer from oil vapor to zeolite catalyst particles determines gasoline yield. - **Coal Combustion in Fluidized Bed Boilers:** Mass transfer of O₂ to char particles controls combustion efficiency. - **Pharmaceutical Drying:** Moisture transfer from wet particles in fluidized bed dryers. - **Wastewater Treatment:** Ozone transfer to bio-particles in fluidized biofilm reactors. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Fick's Law to the Ranz–Marshall Form We start with steady-state diffusion-convection to a single sphere. The **mass balance** on a thin spherical shell of radius **r** and thickness **dr** gives: $$\frac{d}{dr}\left(r^2 \cdot N_A\right) = 0$$ Where **N_A** is the molar flux of species A. For low mass transfer rates (dilute system), the flux is given by: $$N_A = -D_{AB} \cdot \frac{dC_A}{dr} + x_A \cdot (N_A + N_B)$$ For equimolar counter-diffusion or for **A diffusing through stagnant B** (most common), the convective term simplifies, and the solution in the absence of convection gives: $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{d_p} \cdot (C_{A,b} - C_{A,s}) \cdot 2$$ This yields the **pure diffusion limit**: **Sh = 2.0** (this is the well-known result for mass transfer to a sphere in a stagnant fluid, derivable from the analytical solution of the Laplace equation in spherical coordinates). When **convection** is present, an **additional term** proportional to **Re^0.5 · Sc^1/3** is added empirically, justified by **boundary layer theory**: **Step 1: Boundary Layer Thickness (δ)** For flow past a flat plate (analogous to the leading edge of a sphere): $$\delta \propto \sqrt{\frac{\mu \cdot x}{\rho \cdot u}}$$ **Step 2: Mass Transfer Coefficient** The mass transfer coefficient is proportional to **D/δ**: $$k_c \propto \frac{D_{AB}}{\delta} \propto \frac{D_{AB}}{\sqrt{\mu x / (\rho u)}}$$ **Step 3: Non-dimensionalize** Define **Re_x = ρux/μ** and **Sc = μ/(ρD_AB)**: $$k_c \cdot \frac{x}{D_{AB}} \propto \sqrt{Re_x \cdot Sc}$$ **Step 4: Apply to a Sphere** For a sphere, **x ≈ d_p** (characteristic length): $$Sh = \frac{k_c \cdot d_p}{D_{AB}} = 2.0 + C \cdot Re^{1/2} \cdot Sc^{1/3}$$ Ranz & Marshall determined the empirical constant **C = 0.6** by fitting experimental data on droplet evaporation. ## 3.2 Derivation: The Chilton–Colburn J-Factor Starting from the dimensionless numbers: **Step 1: Define Stanton number for mass transfer** $$St_m = \frac{k_c}{u} = \frac{Sh}{Re \cdot Sc}$$ **Step 2: Multiply by Sc^(2/3)** $$St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3} = \frac{Sh}{Re \cdot Sc^{1/3}}$$ **Step 3: Define J-factor** $$j_D = St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3} = \frac{Sh}{Re \cdot Sc^{1/3}}$$ **Step 4: Heat-Mass Analogy** Empirically, in turbulent flow: $$j_H \approx j_D$$ Where: $$j_H = St \cdot Pr^{2/3}$$ This is the famous **Chilton–Colburn analogy**, valid for **0.6 < Pr < 100** and **0.6 < Sc < 3000**. ## 3.3 Derivation: j-Factor for Fixed Bed (Wilson–Geankoplis) For a fixed bed, mass transfer is enhanced due to flow channeling. The **interstitial velocity** is higher than the superficial velocity: $$u_i = \frac{u}{\varepsilon}$$ Using the Ranz–Marshall form but with the **interstitial Re**: $$Sh = 2.0 + 0.6 \cdot Re_i^{0.5} \cdot Sc^{1/3}$$ Where **Re_i = d_p · u · ρ / (μ · ε)**. Dividing both sides by **Re·Sc^(1/3)**: $$j_D = \frac{2.0}{Re \cdot Sc^{1/3}} + \frac{0.6}{Re^{0.5} \cdot \varepsilon^{0.5}}$$ In the **intermediate Re range**, the **"2.0" term is negligible** (since Re·Sc^(1/3) is large), giving: $$j_D \approx \frac{0.6}{\varepsilon^{0.5}} \cdot Re^{-0.5}$$ Experimental data fits with **j_D = 1.09·Re^(-2/3)/ε**, which is the **Wilson–Geankoplis form** — a more empirical but accurate representation. ## 3.4 Worked Example: Mass Transfer Coefficient Calculation **Problem:** A gas with **ρ = 1.2 kg/m³** and **μ = 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s** flows at **u = 0.5 m/s** past a single sphere of **d_p = 1 mm**. Diffusivity of the solute in gas **D_AB = 2.5 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s**. Find the mass transfer coefficient using Ranz–Marshall correlation. **Step 1: Reynolds Number** $$Re = \frac{d_p \cdot u \cdot \rho}{\mu} = \frac{0.001 \times 0.5 \times 1.2}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}}$$ $$Re = \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-4}}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} = 33.33$$ **Step 2: Schmidt Number** $$Sc = \frac{\mu}{\rho \cdot D_{AB}} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-5}}{1.2 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5}}$$ $$Sc = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-5}}{3.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.6$$ **Step 3: Sherwood Number (Ranz–Marshall)** $$Sh = 2.0 + 0.6 \cdot Re^{0.5} \cdot Sc^{1/3}$$ $$Sh = 2.0 + 0.6 \cdot (33.33)^{0.5} \cdot (0.6)^{1/3}$$ $$Sh = 2.0 + 0.6 \cdot 5.774 \cdot 0.843$$ $$Sh = 2.0 + 2.92 = 4.92$$ **Step 4: Mass Transfer Coefficient** $$k_c = \frac{Sh \cdot D_{AB}}{d_p} = \frac{4.92 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.001}$$ $$k_c = 0.123 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 5: Verification via Frössling** $$Sh_{Frössling} = 2.0 + 0.552 \cdot (33.33)^{0.5} \cdot (0.6)^{1/3} = 2.0 + 0.552 \times 5.774 \times 0.843 = 4.69$$ $$k_c^{Frössling} = \frac{4.69 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.001} = 0.117 \text{ m/s}$$ The two correlations differ by **~5%**, acceptable in engineering practice. ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Single Sphere vs Fixed Bed vs Fluidized Bed | Property | Single Sphere | Fixed Bed | Gas Fluidized Bed | |----------|---------------|-----------|-------------------| | **Reference Correlation** | Ranz–Marshall | Dwivedi–Upadhyay | Fan–Yang | | **Typical Sh Number** | 2 – 10 | 5 – 50 | 10 – 100 | | **Particle Motion** | None | None | Vigorous mixing | | **Boundary Layer** | Stable | Distorted | Continuously broken | | **Effect of ε (Porosity)** | Not applicable | Major effect (1/ε) | Variable | | **Effect of u_mf** | None | None | Significant (Fan–Yang) | | **Enhancement vs Single Sphere** | 1× (baseline) | 2–5× | 5–10× | | **Industrial Application** | Spray drying | Catalytic packed reactors | FCC, FBC boilers | | **Operational Risk** | Particle erosion at high u | Channeling, hot spots | Bubbling, slugging | > [!TIP] > **Memorization Tip for KTU:** Remember the sequence — **"2 + 0.6·Re^0.5·Sc^1/3"** is the universal backbone. Every advanced correlation modifies this **base form** with porosity (ε) and fluidization terms. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mass Transfer Regimes in a Gas Fluidized Bed ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Gas Inlet u] --> B[Distributor Plate] B --> C{Bed State?} C -- "u < umf" --> D[Fixed Bed
Re_p low
Sh 5-20] C -- "u = umf" --> E[Incipient Fluidization
Particles just suspended] C -- "umf < u < ut" --> F[Fluidized Bed
Bubbles form
Sh 20-100] F --> G[Bubble Phase
Gas-rich voids] F --> H[Emulsion Phase
Particles + gas] G -- "Interchange Kbe" --> H H -- "Convective kc" --> I[Particle Surface] I -- "Internal pore diffusion" --> J[Active Site] J -- "Surface reaction" --> K[Product] K --> L[Gas Outlet] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Mass Transfer Resistance Chain ```mermaid graph LR A[Bulk Gas
Concentration Cb] -- "Film resistance 1/kG" --> B[Particle Surface
Concentration Cs] B -- "Internal pore
resistance" --> C[Active Site
Concentration Ci] C -- "Surface reaction
kinetics" --> D[Product] style A fill:#e1f5ff style B fill:#fff4e1 style C fill:#ffe1f5 style D fill:#e1ffe1 ``` ## 4.3 Architecture: Engineering Decision Flow for Selecting a Correlation ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{What is the system?} Q1 -- "Single isolated particle" --> C1[Use Ranz-Marshall
Sh = 2 + 0.6 Re^0.5 Sc^1/3] Q1 -- "Packed/Fixed bed" --> Q2{Fluid phase?} Q1 -- "Gas Fluidized bed" --> Q3{Regime?} Q2 -- "Liquid" --> C2[Use Wilson-Geankoplis
jD = 1.09 Re^-2/3 / eps] Q2 -- "Gas" --> C3[Use Dwivedi-Upadhyay
or Petrovic-Thodos] Q3 -- "Bubble phase dominant" --> C4[Use Kato-Wen
modified Ranz-Marshall] Q3 -- "Emulsion + bubble" --> C5[Use Fan-Yang
or Behie-Mok] C1 --> END[Calculate kc from Sh] C2 --> END C3 --> END C4 --> END C5 --> END ``` ## 4.4 Comparison: Heat Transfer vs Mass Transfer Analogy ```mermaid graph TB subgraph HEAT[Heat Transfer Analogies] H1[Stanton Heat St] --> H2[Nu Number] H3[Prandtl Pr] --> H4[jH Factor] H5[St × Pr^2/3] --> H4 end subgraph MASS[Mass Transfer Analogies] M1[Stanton Mass Stm] --> M2[Sh Number] M3[Schmidt Sc] --> M4[jD Factor] M5[Stm × Sc^2/3] --> M4 end H4 -- "Chilton-Colburn
jH = jD" --> M4 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The Chilton–Colburn analogy graphically emphasizes the **one-to-one mapping** between **Nu ↔ Sh**, **Pr ↔ Sc**, and **j_H ↔ j_D**, allowing engineers to predict mass transfer from heat transfer data (or vice versa) when one dataset is unavailable. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Define the Sherwood number and explain its physical significance in mass transfer to a single sphere.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Sherwood number (Sh)** is a dimensionless number that represents the ratio of **convective mass transfer** to **diffusive mass transfer rate**, defined as: $$Sh = \frac{k_c \cdot d_p}{D_{AB}}$$ - **Physical Significance:** When **Sh = 2.0**, mass transfer is purely diffusive (no convection, Re → 0). As Sh increases above 2, convection enhances mass transfer. - **Practical Value:** In fluidized beds, typical **Sh = 10–100**, meaning mass transfer is **5–50 times faster** than pure diffusion. - **Unit:** Dimensionless. ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **What is the Chilton–Colburn analogy? State its limitations.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Chilton–Colburn analogy** is an empirical relationship that equates the **j-factors for heat and mass transfer**: $$j_H = j_D \quad \Rightarrow \quad St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3}$$ - **Significance:** Allows prediction of mass transfer coefficients from heat transfer data (or vice versa), saving experimental effort. - **Limitations:** 1. Valid only for **0.6 < Pr < 100** and **0.6 < Sc < 3000**. 2. **Not valid** at the leading edge of surfaces or in strongly buoyancy-driven flows. 3. Fails when **significant chemical reaction** occurs simultaneously. --- ## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **a)** Derive the Ranz–Marshall correlation for mass transfer to a single sphere, starting from Fick's first law. Discuss the physical significance of each term. **(7 marks)** **b)** Air at 25°C and 1 atm flows at 0.4 m/s through a fixed bed of spherical catalyst particles of diameter **d_p = 2 mm**, bed porosity **ε = 0.42**. The diffusivity of the reactant in air is **2.0 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s**. Estimate the mass transfer coefficient using the **Dwivedi–Upadhyay correlation**. Given: ρ_air = 1.184 kg/m³, μ_air = 1.85 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Part (a): **Step 1: Starting from Fick's Law** Fick's first law for diffusion to a sphere: $$N_A = -D_{AB} \cdot \frac{dC_A}{dr}$$ **Step 2: Mass Balance on Spherical Shell** For a thin shell between r and r+dr: $$\frac{d}{dr}\left(r^2 \cdot N_A\right) = 0 \Rightarrow N_A \cdot r^2 = \text{constant}$$ **Step 3: Integration (No Convection Case)** Integrating from r = d_p/2 to r = ∞ with boundary conditions C = C_s at r = d_p/2 and C = C_b at r = ∞: $$N_A = \frac{2 D_{AB}}{d_p} (C_b - C_s)$$ This gives **Sh = 2.0** (the diffusion limit). **Step 4: Adding Convection** For Re > 0, boundary layer theory gives an additional convective contribution proportional to **Re^0.5 · Sc^1/3**. The empirical Ranz–Marshall form is: $$\boxed{Sh = 2.0 + 0.6 \cdot Re^{0.5} \cdot Sc^{1/3}}$$ **Significance of terms:** - **"2.0"** → Pure molecular diffusion limit (Stokes flow, Re → 0). - **"0.6 · Re^0.5 · Sc^1/3"** → Convective enhancement due to flow. #### Model Solution to Part (b): **Step 1: Reynolds Number** $$Re_p = \frac{d_p \cdot u \cdot \rho}{\mu} = \frac{0.002 \times 0.4 \times 1.184}{1.85 \times 10^{-5}}$$ $$Re_p = \frac{9.472 \times 10^{-4}}{1.85 \times 10^{-5}} = 51.2$$ **Step 2: Schmidt Number** $$Sc = \frac{\mu}{\rho \cdot D_{AB}} = \frac{1.85 \times 10^{-5}}{1.184 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.781$$ **Step 3: Dwivedi–Upadhyay j-Factor** $$j_D = \frac{0.765}{(51.2)^{0.82}} + \frac{0.365}{(51.2)^{0.386}}$$ $$j_D = \frac{0.765}{29.07} + \frac{0.365}{5.49} = 0.0263 + 0.0665 = 0.0928$$ **Step 4: Sherwood Number** $$Sh = j_D \cdot Re_p \cdot Sc^{1/3} = 0.0928 \times 51.2 \times (0.781)^{1/3}$$ $$Sh = 0.0928 \times 51.2 \times 0.921 = 4.375$$ **Step 5: Mass Transfer Coefficient** $$k_c = \frac{Sh \cdot D_{AB}}{d_p} = \frac{4.375 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-5}}{0.002} = 0.0438 \text{ m/s}$$ $$\boxed{k_c \approx 0.0438 \text{ m/s}}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **a)** Compare the mass transfer mechanisms in (i) a fixed bed and (ii) a gas fluidized bed. Discuss why fluidized beds offer higher mass transfer coefficients. **(7 marks)** **b)** A gas fluidized bed operates with particles of **d_p = 150 μm** at a superficial gas velocity of **u = 0.5 m/s** (u_mf = 0.05 m/s). Bed porosity **ε = 0.55**. Gas properties: **ρ = 1.2 kg/m³, μ = 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s, D_AB = 2.2 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s**. Estimate the Sherwood number using the **Fan–Yang correlation**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Part (a): **Mechanism in a Fixed Bed:** - Gas flows through **interstices** between stationary particles. - Each particle has a **stable, well-defined boundary layer**. - Mass transfer occurs primarily by **forced convection** through the void spaces. - Channeling and bypassing may reduce effectiveness. **Mechanism in a Gas Fluidized Bed:** - Particles are **continuously moving** and mixing. - Bubbles rise through the bed, **interchanging gas with the emulsion phase**. - The **boundary layer is constantly disrupted** by particle collisions and bubble passage. - Two distinct transfer zones: **(i) bubble → emulsion** and **(ii) emulsion → particle**. **Why Fluidized Beds Give Higher k_c:** 1. **Particle motion** continuously renews the gas–solid interface. 2. **Bubble-induced turbulence** enhances inter-phase mass transfer. 3. **Higher effective interfacial area** due to dispersion. 4. **No channeling** — uniform gas–solid contact throughout the bed. 5. Typically **Sh is 5–10× higher** than in fixed beds for the same particle size. #### Model Solution to Part (b): **Step 1: Reynolds Number** $$Re_p = \frac{d_p \cdot u \cdot \rho}{\mu} = \frac{150 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.5 \times 1.2}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}}$$ $$Re_p = \frac{9.0 \times 10^{-5}}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} = 5.0$$ **Step 2: Schmidt Number** $$Sc = \frac{\mu}{\rho \cdot D_{AB}} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-5}}{1.2 \times 2.2 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.682$$ **Step 3: Modified Re for Porosity** $$\frac{Re_p}{\varepsilon} = \frac{5.0}{0.55} = 9.09$$ **Step 4: Fluidization Enhancement Factor** $$\frac{u - u_{mf}}{u_{mf}} = \frac{0.5 - 0.05}{0.05} = 9.0$$ **Step 5: Apply Fan–Yang Correlation** $$Sh = 2.0 + 0.6 \cdot \left(\frac{Re_p}{\varepsilon}\right)^{0.5} \cdot Sc^{1/3} \cdot \left(\frac{u - u_{mf}}{u_{mf}}\right)^{0.75}$$ $$Sh = 2.0 + 0.6 \cdot (9.09)^{0.5} \cdot (0.682)^{1/3} \cdot (9.0)^{0.75}$$ $$Sh = 2.0 + 0.6 \cdot 3.015 \cdot 0.881 \cdot 5.196$$ $$Sh = 2.0 + 8.28 = 10.28$$ $$\boxed{Sh \approx 10.28}$$ **Interpretation:** The fluidization factor (9.0)^0.75 = 5.196 **multiplies** the convective term by ~5×, explaining why fluidized beds achieve high mass transfer rates. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing d_p with d_b (bubble diameter):** Always use **particle diameter d_p** in single-particle correlations. Mixing these is the #1 mark-loss error. > 2. **Forgetting porosity (ε):** In fixed beds, the **interstitial velocity is u/ε**, not u. Missing this gives wrong Re_p. > 3. **Using ρ_particle instead of ρ_gas:** Re_p uses **gas density**, not solid density. > 4. **Wrong Schmidt definition:** Sc = μ/(ρ·D_AB) — students often write μ·D/ρ (inverted). > 5. **Not justifying the correlation choice:** KTU requires you to state the **validity range** of the correlation used. > 6. **Unit inconsistency in D_AB:** Most KTU problems use D in **m²/s**, ensure conversion from cm²/s where needed. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### High-Density Revision Checklist - ✅ **Ranz–Marshall correlation** is the **master key** for single-sphere mass transfer: **Sh = 2.0 + 0.6·Re^0.5·Sc^1/3** - ✅ The **"2.0"** term is the **pure diffusion limit** — derived from Fick's law in spherical coordinates. - ✅ For **fixed beds**, the porosity **ε** multiplies the Re and divides the j-factor. - ✅ **Dwivedi–Upadhyay** is the **most general** fixed-bed correlation — valid over the widest Re range. - ✅ For **gas fluidized beds**, the **fluidization intensity** factor **(u − u_mf)/u_mf** is the key multiplier. - ✅ **Chilton–Colburn analogy**: **j_H = j_D** — links heat and mass transfer. - ✅ The **J-factor** is **Sh / (Re·Sc^1/3)** — a critical tool for plotting and comparing data. - ✅ **Sh typical values:** 2 (stagnant) → 5–20 (fixed bed) → 20–100 (fluidized bed). - ✅ For **gas systems**: Sc ≈ **0.5–1.0**; for **liquid systems**: Sc ≈ **100–1000**. - ✅ **Inter-phase transfer** in fluidized beds occurs in **two stages**: bubble → emulsion → particle. - ✅ Boundary layer **disruption** by particle motion is the **fundamental reason** for high Sh in fluidized beds. - ✅ **Industrial significance:** FCC units, FBC boilers, and pharmaceutical dryers all rely on these mass transfer principles. ### Quick-Reference Constants | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value (Gas) | Typical Value (Liquid) | |--------|----------|----------------------|--------------------------| | D_AB | Diffusivity | 10⁻⁵ m²/s | 10⁻⁹ m²/s | | Sc | Schmidt Number | 0.5 – 3.0 | 100 – 1000 | | Re | Reynolds (typical) | 1 – 100 | 0.01 – 10 | | Sh | Sherwood (typical) | 2 – 50 | 50 – 500 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Tip:** When solving numerical problems, always show the calculation of **Re, Sc, and Sh** in **three separate, clearly labeled steps**. KTU examiners award **methodology marks** separately from the final numerical answer, so even if your final value has a small error, the stepwise approach secures partial credit.

topics.title: Interpretation of Mass Transfer Coefficients
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interpretation of Mass Transfer Coefficients

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of **Fluidization Engineering**, the **Mass Transfer Coefficient** is defined as a proportionality constant that quantifies the rate at which a chemical species (mass) is transported from one phase to another across a phase boundary, under the influence of a concentration gradient. In a fluidized bed, mass transfer coefficients characterize the transport of solute between the **particulate (solid) phase** and the surrounding **fluid (gas or liquid) continuous phase**.

The general rate equation for interphase mass transfer is written as:

$$N_A = k_C \cdot a \cdot (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$$

Where:
- $N_A$ = Molar flux of species $A$ (kmol/m²·s or kg/m²·s)
- $k_C$ = Mass transfer coefficient (m/s)
- $a$ = Specific interfacial area (m²/m³)
- $C_{A,s}$ = Concentration of $A$ at the solid-fluid interface
- $C_{A,\infty}$ = Concentration of $A$ in the bulk fluid

In fluidization literature, two principal mass transfer coefficients are routinely employed:
- **Gas–Solid Mass Transfer Coefficient ($k_g$)** — units of m/s, used in gas-fluidized systems (drying, combustion, catalytic reactions).
- **Liquid–Solid Mass Transfer Coefficient ($k_L$)** — units of m/s, used in liquid-fluidized systems (ion exchange, leaching, biochemical reactors).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students are expected to clearly interpret the physical meaning of $k_g$ and $k_L$, distinguish between the various theoretical models that predict these coefficients (film, penetration, surface-renewal), and apply empirical correlations appropriate to the fluidization regime (freeboard, bubbling, or particulate).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you drop a **sugar cube** into a cup of hot tea. The sugar dissolves and the sweetness spreads throughout the cup. How fast does the sweetness spread? That "speed of sweetening" is essentially the **mass transfer coefficient** in action.

Now, extend this to a fluidized bed: thousands of tiny solid particles are suspended in a flowing gas or liquid. Each particle is like a microscopic "sugar cube" exchanging mass with the surrounding fluid. The **mass transfer coefficient** tells us how efficiently each particle (and by extension, the entire bed) can give up (or absorb) mass to (or from) the fluid flowing past it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Bystander Cooling Effect":**
> Think of a hot frying pan (the solid particle) sitting in a stream of cool air (the fluid). The faster the air flows past the pan, the faster the heat (analogous to mass) is carried away. The "effectiveness" of the air in removing this heat per unit area, per unit driving force, is what the **mass transfer coefficient** quantifies. A higher $k$ means the pan (or particle) loses or gains mass more quickly.

---

### 1.3 Physical Significance in Fluidized Beds

In a fluidized bed, mass transfer plays a pivotal role in:
- **Drying operations** (moisture transfer from wet particles to hot gas).
- **Adsorption/Desorption** (e.g., CO₂ capture, solvent recovery).
- **Heterogeneous catalytic reactions** (reactant transport to catalyst surface, product removal).
- **Coating and Granulation** (binder/solute deposition on particle surfaces).
- **Leaching and Dissolution** (soluble material extraction from solid ores).

The mass transfer coefficient is **not a fundamental physical property** of the fluid alone; it is a **process-dependent parameter** that bundles together the effects of:
- Molecular diffusion
- Convective transport
- Turbulent mixing near the particle surface
- Boundary layer development around the particle

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiners' Note:** Whenever a problem states "interpret" the mass transfer coefficient, the student is expected to explain **what physical transport mechanism the coefficient represents**, identify **the controlling resistance** (e.g., gas-film vs. solid-side diffusion), and justify which **theoretical model** (film, penetration, or surface-renewal) best fits the fluidization regime under consideration.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Classical Interpretations of Mass Transfer Coefficients

The mass transfer coefficient can be interpreted through **three classical theoretical models**. Each offers a different physical picture of the transport process, and each yields a different mathematical expression for $k$.

---

#### 2.1.1 Film Theory (Whitman, 1923)

**Physical Picture:** A stagnant film of fluid, of thickness $\delta$, exists at the particle-fluid interface. Mass is transferred purely by molecular diffusion across this film; beyond the film, the bulk fluid is assumed to be perfectly mixed.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Imagine a thin, static "blanket" of fluid hugging the particle surface.
2. The concentration drops linearly from $C_{A,s}$ at the surface to $C_{A,\infty}$ in the bulk.
3. Fick's Law of diffusion governs the transfer through this layer.

**Governing Equation (derived from Fick's Law):**

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{\delta}(C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$$

Comparing with the general rate equation $N_A = k_C (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$:

$$k_C = \frac{D_{AB}}{\delta}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The film theory predicts that $k_C$ is **directly proportional to the molecular diffusivity** $D_{AB}$ and **inversely proportional to the film thickness** $\delta$. As fluid velocity increases, the film thins, and $k_C$ rises. However, the model is an oversimplification because real fluidized beds are turbulent, not quiescent.

---

#### 2.1.2 Penetration Theory (Higbie, 1935)

**Physical Picture:** Fluid elements from the bulk periodically "sweep" across the particle surface, each making fresh contact for a brief exposure time $t_c$. During this short contact, unsteady-state diffusion occurs into the fluid element.

**Operational Steps:**
1. A small "packet" of fluid from the bulk arrives at the particle surface.
2. It stays in contact for a time $t_c$ (the "exposure time").
3. During $t_c$, mass diffuses into the packet via **unsteady-state** molecular diffusion.
4. The packet is then swept away and replaced by a fresh one.

**Governing Equation:**

$$k_C = 2 \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi \cdot t_c}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Penetration theory predicts $k_C \propto \sqrt{D_{AB}}$. Compared to film theory, the dependence on $D_{AB}$ is **weaker** (square root vs. linear), which aligns better with experimental observations in turbulent systems like fluidized beds.

---

#### 2.1.3 Surface Renewal Theory (Danckwerts, 1951)

**Physical Picture:** A refinement of Higbie's model, this theory assumes that fluid elements arriving at the surface have a **distribution of exposure times** governed by a statistical renewal rate $s$ (with units of s⁻¹).

**Operational Steps:**
1. The probability that a fluid element of age $\tau$ is replaced is $s \cdot e^{-s\tau}$.
2. The average mass transfer rate is computed by integrating over all ages.
3. The result is mathematically elegant and tractable.

**Governing Equation:**

$$k_C = \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why It Matters in Fluidization:** In bubbling fluidized beds, bubbles continuously form, rise, and coalesce, bringing fresh fluid elements to particle surfaces at high rates. This physical picture mirrors Danckwerts' surface renewal model very well, making it a preferred interpretation for **gas-fluidized systems**.

---

#### 2.1.4 The Two-Film Theory (Lewis & Whitman, 1924)

**Physical Picture:** Both the gas phase and the liquid phase (or both sides of any interface) have their own stagnant films. The **overall mass transfer coefficient** $K_G$ (or $K_L$) is governed by the resistances in series.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Apply film theory to **both** sides of the interface simultaneously.
2. Sum the resistances (analogous to electrical resistances in series).
3. The overall coefficient is smaller than either individual coefficient.

**Governing Equation (Gas-Phase Overall):**

$$\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_g} + \frac{m}{k_L}$$

Where $m$ is the **equilibrium slope** of the gas-liquid concentration relation (Henry's law constant for dilute systems).

---

### 2.2 Dimensionless Numbers Governing Mass Transfer

In fluidized beds, $k_g$ and $k_L$ are typically reported through **dimensionless correlations** using the Sherwood number.

| Dimensionless Group | Definition | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
