| **Sherwood Number (Sh)** | $Sh = \dfrac{k_C \cdot d_p}{D_{AB}}$ | Ratio of convective to diffusive mass transport |
| **Reynolds Number (Re)** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot u \cdot d_p}{\mu}$ | Ratio of inertial to viscous forces |
| **Schmidt Number (Sc)** | $Sc = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho \cdot D_{AB}}$ | Ratio of momentum diffusivity to mass diffusivity |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** These dimensionless numbers are the universal language of transport phenomena. By reporting mass transfer data as $Sh = f(Re, Sc)$, engineers can compare results across different fluids, particles, and scales.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Reference Table for KTU Board Examinations**

| **Quantity / Equation** | **Expression** | **Units** | **Applicable Regime** |
|---|---|---|---|
| General rate equation | $N_A = k_C \cdot a \cdot \Delta C$ | kmol/m³·s | All regimes |
| Film theory | $k_C = D_{AB} / \delta$ | m/s | Stagnant boundary layer |
| Penetration theory | $k_C = 2 \sqrt{D_{AB} / (\pi t_c)}$ | m/s | Bubbling beds |
| Surface renewal | $k_C = \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot s}$ | m/s | Turbulent contact |
| Sherwood number | $Sh = k_C d_p / D_{AB}$ | — | Dimensionless form |
| Reynolds number | $Re = \rho u d_p / \mu$ | — | Dimensionless form |
| Schmidt number | $Sc = \mu / (\rho D_{AB})$ | — | Fluid property group |
| Ranz-Marshall correlation | $Sh = 2 + 0.6 \, Re^{1/2} \, Sc^{1/3}$ | — | Single sphere, gas-fluidized |
| Frössling correlation | $Sh = 2 + 0.552 \, Re^{1/2} \, Sc^{1/3}$ | — | Single sphere, evaporation |
| Wilson-Geankoplis | $Sh = (1.09 / \varepsilon) \, Re^{0.33} \, Sc^{0.33}$ | — | Packed/fluidized (liquid) |

| Bubble-to-cloud mass transfer | $k_{bc} = 4.50 (u_{mf} / d_b) + 5.85 (D_{AB}^{0.5} g^{0.25} / d_b^{1.25})$ | m/s | Bubbling fluidized bed |

| Application | Dominant $k$ | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Catalytic cracking (FCC)** | $k_g$ (gas–solid) | Determines how fast vapor-phase hydrocarbons reach zeolite catalyst sites |
| **Fluidized bed dryer** | $k_g$ | Governs the rate of moisture removal from wet particles |
| **Wastewater treatment (biofilm)** | $k_L$ (liquid–solid) | Controls oxygen/substrate transport to microbial films |
| **Coal combustion in CFB** | $k_g$ (with reaction) | Couples with heterogeneous reaction kinetics |
| **Mineral leaching** | $k_L$ | Determines extraction rate of soluble metals |
| **Pharmaceutical granulation** | $k_L$ | Controls binder distribution on particle surfaces |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Mass Transfer Coefficient from Film Theory

**Starting Point — Fick's First Law of Diffusion:**

$$J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

**Step 1:** At steady state, the molar flux $J_A$ is constant throughout the film of thickness $\delta$.

**Step 2:** Integrate across the film from the interface ($z=0$, $C_A = C_{A,s}$) to the bulk ($z=\delta$, $C_A = C_{A,\infty}$):

$$J_A \int_{0}^{\delta} dz = -D_{AB} \int_{C_{A,s}}^{C_{A,\infty}} dC_A$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate both integrals:

$$J_A \cdot \delta = -D_{AB} \left[ C_{A,\infty} - C_{A,s} \right] = D_{AB} \left( C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty} \right)$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the flux:

$$J_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{\delta} \left( C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty} \right)$$

**Step 5:** Identify the mass transfer coefficient by comparison with $N_A = k_C \Delta C$:

$$k_C = \frac{D_{AB}}{\delta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The film coefficient is simply the **ratio of molecular diffusivity to an effective film thickness**. A thinner film (caused by higher fluid velocity or smaller particles) gives a larger $k_C$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Mass Transfer Coefficient from Penetration Theory

**Starting Point — Unsteady-State Diffusion into a Semi-Infinite Fluid:**

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$$

**Initial Condition:** The fluid element arrives at the surface with bulk concentration $C_{A,\infty}$.

$$C_A(z, 0) = C_{A,\infty}$$

**Boundary Conditions:**
- At the surface ($z=0$): $C_A(0, t) = C_{A,s}$
- Deep in the fluid ($z \to \infty$): $C_A(\infty, t) = C_{A,\infty}$

**Step 1:** The solution to this classic diffusion problem (Carslaw & Jaeger) is:

$$C_A(z, t) = C_{A,s} - (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) \, \text{erf}\left( \frac{z}{2\sqrt{D_{AB} t}} \right)$$

**Step 2:** The instantaneous mass flux at the surface is given by Fick's Law evaluated at $z=0$:

$$N_A(t) = -D_{AB} \left. \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z} \right|_{z=0}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the derivative:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z} = (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) \cdot \frac{d}{dz}\left[-\text{erf}\left(\frac{z}{2\sqrt{D_{AB} t}}\right)\right]$$

Recall: $\dfrac{d}{dz}\text{erf}(u) = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} e^{-u^2} \cdot \dfrac{du}{dz}$

At $z=0$, $u=0$, and $du/dz = 1/(2\sqrt{D_{AB} t})$:

$$\left. \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z} \right|_{z=0} = -(C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) \cdot \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \cdot \frac{1}{2\sqrt{D_{AB} t}}$$

**Step 4:** Therefore:

$$N_A(t) = -D_{AB} \cdot \left[ -(C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi D_{AB} t}} \right]$$

$$N_A(t) = (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) \cdot \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t}}$$

**Step 5:** Average the flux over the exposure time $t_c$:

$$\overline{N_A} = \frac{1}{t_c} \int_0^{t_c} (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) \cdot \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t}} \, dt$$

**Step 6:** Perform the integration:

$$\overline{N_A} = (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) \cdot \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi}} \cdot \frac{1}{t_c} \int_0^{t_c} t^{-1/2} \, dt$$

$$\overline{N_A} = (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) \cdot \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi}} \cdot \frac{1}{t_c} \cdot \left[ 2 t^{1/2} \right]_0^{t_c}$$

**Step 7:** Simplify:

$$\overline{N_A} = (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) \cdot \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi}} \cdot \frac{2}{\sqrt{t_c}}$$

**Step 8:** Identify the mass transfer coefficient:

$$k_C = 2 \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi \, t_c}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance:** This derivation shows clearly that $k_C$ in penetration theory depends on the **square root of diffusivity** and the **inverse square root of exposure time**. In bubbling fluidized beds, the bubble contact time $t_c$ is related to bubble size and rise velocity: $t_c \propto d_b / u_b$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Mass Transfer Coefficient from Surface Renewal Theory

**Starting Point:** Assume a probabilistic surface renewal with rate $s$ (fraction of surface renewed per unit time). The age distribution of surface elements is:

$$\phi(\tau) = s \, e^{-s \tau}$$

**Step 1:** The average mass transfer rate is:

$$\overline{N_A} = \int_0^{\infty} N_A(\tau) \, \phi(\tau) \, d\tau = \int_0^{\infty} (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi \tau}} \cdot s \, e^{-s\tau} \, d\tau$$

**Step 2:** Factor out the constants:

$$\overline{N_A} = (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) \cdot s \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi}} \int_0^{\infty} \tau^{-1/2} e^{-s\tau} \, d\tau$$

**Step 3:** Recognize the integral form of the Gamma function:

$$\int_0^{\infty} \tau^{n-1} e^{-s\tau} \, d\tau = \frac{\Gamma(n)}{s^n}$$

For $n = 1/2$:

$$\int_0^{\infty} \tau^{-1/2} e^{-s\tau} \, d\tau = \frac{\Gamma(1/2)}{s^{1/2}} = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{\sqrt{s}}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back:

$$\overline{N_A} = (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) \cdot s \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{\sqrt{s}} = (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot s}$$

**Step 5:** Identify the coefficient:

$$k_C = \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** The renewal rate $s$ in bubbling fluidized beds can be estimated from bubble dynamics. Larger, faster-rising bubbles produce higher $s$, increasing $k_C$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Two-Film Theory — Resistance-in-Series

**Step 1:** Consider gas and liquid films in series at a gas-liquid interface.

**Step 2:** At steady state, the flux through both films is equal:

$$N_A = k_g (p_A - p_{A,i}) = k_L (C_{A,i} - C_L)$$

**Step 3:** At the interface, equilibrium holds. For a linear equilibrium (Henry's Law region):

$$C_{A,i} = H \, p_{A,i} \quad \text{or} \quad p_{A,i} = m \, C_{A,i}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the interfacial concentration by eliminating $p_{A,i}$ and $C_{A,i}$.

Express the gas-side driving force:

$$p_A - p_{A,i} = \frac{N_A}{k_g}$$

Express the liquid-side driving force:

$$C_{A,i} - C_L = \frac{N_A}{k_L}$$

**Step 5:** Total driving force:

$$p_A - p_A^* = (p_A - p_{A,i}) + (p_{A,i} - p_A^*) = \frac{N_A}{k_g} + \frac{m N_A}{k_L}$$

Where $p_A^* = m C_L$ is the gas concentration in equilibrium with the bulk liquid.

**Step 6:** Define the overall coefficient $K_G$:

$$N_A = K_G (p_A - p_A^*)$$

**Step 7:** Equate and solve:

$$\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_g} + \frac{m}{k_L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Each term on the right-hand side is a **resistance** to mass transfer. The dominant phase (gas or liquid) controls the overall rate. In most gas-fluidized beds, the **gas-film resistance** is rate-limiting because $k_L \gg m \cdot k_g$ in the gas-side-controlled regime.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Bubble-to-Cloud Mass Transfer in Fluidized Beds

In a **bubbling fluidized bed**, gas inside a rising bubble exchanges mass with the surrounding "cloud" of gas that circulates with the bubble. Kunii and Levenspiel proposed:

$$k_{bc} = 4.50 \left(\frac{u_{mf}}{d_b}\right) + 5.85 \left(\frac{D_{AB}^{0.5} g^{0.25}}{d_b^{1.25}}\right)$$

**Step-by-step physical meaning:**

- **Term 1** $\left(4.50 \, u_{mf}/d_b\right)$: Bulk convection through the bed (gas through-flow contribution).
- **Term 2** $\left(5.85 \, D_{AB}^{0.5} g^{0.25} / d_b^{1.25}\right)$: Molecular diffusion across the bubble boundary.

**Derivation of Term 1 — Convective Throughflow:**

For a bubble of diameter $d_b$ rising through a bed at minimum fluidization velocity $u_{mf}$, the gas residence time in the cloud region is:

$$t_{res} \approx \frac{d_b}{u_b - u_{mf}}$$

The throughflow velocity of gas through the bubble is approximately $u_{mf}$ (gas flows downward relative to the rising bubble). The transfer rate per unit bubble surface is:

$$k_{conv} \sim \frac{u_{mf}}{d_b}$$

A more detailed hydrodynamic analysis (Kunii-Levenspiel) yields the coefficient **4.50** through geometric and similarity arguments.

**Derivation of Term 2 — Molecular Diffusion:**

During the residence time $t_{res}$, mass diffuses a characteristic distance $\sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot t_{res}}$:

$$\sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot t_{res}} = \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot \frac{d_b}{u_b}}$$

Using the bubble rise velocity in a fluidized bed (Davies-Taylor): $u_b \approx 0.711 \sqrt{g \, d_b}$, we get:

$$\sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot \frac{d_b}{u_b}} = \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot \frac{d_b}{0.711 \sqrt{g \, d_b}}} = \left(\frac{D_{AB}}{0.711}\right)^{0.5} \cdot \frac{d_b^{0.25}}{g^{0.25}}$$

Collecting the bubble-diameter dependence: $d_b^{0.25}/d_b = d_b^{-0.75}$. The numerical prefactor **5.85** arises from detailed matching of mass transfer coefficients at typical fluidized-bed operating conditions.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The Kunii-Levenspiel expression is widely tested in KTU Board examinations. Students are expected to know the **physical meaning of each term** and be able to **estimate the relative contribution** of convection vs. diffusion for a given bubble size.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Interpretation

| **Fluidization Regime** | **Dominant Mechanism** | **Best Theoretical Model** | **Typical $k_C$ Dependence** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Freeboard region** (above bed) | Gas–particle convection | Film theory with Ranz-Marshall | $k_g \propto Re^{1/2}$ |
| **Bubbling bed (cloud-phase)** | Gas throughflow + diffusion | Kunii-Levenspiel | $k_{bc} \propto u_{mf}/d_b + D_{AB}^{1/2}/d_b^{5/4}$ |
| **Particulate (homogeneous) fluidization** | Molecular diffusion at surface | Film theory | $k_g \propto D_{AB}/\delta$ |
| **Turbulent fluidized bed** | High renewal rate | Surface renewal / Penetration | $k_g \propto \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot s}$ |
| **Liquid-fluidized bed (ion exchange)** | Liquid film around particle | Frössling-type correlation | $k_L \propto Re^{1/2} Sc^{1/3}$ |
| **Spouted bed** | Spout–annulus interaction | Modified penetration | $k_g \propto \sqrt{D_{AB} u_{sp}/d_p}$ |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Define the mass transfer coefficient. Explain how the **film theory** interprets it.

**Model Answer:**

The mass transfer coefficient $k_C$ is the proportionality constant between the molar flux of a species and its concentration driving force across a phase boundary:

$$N_A = k_C \, (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$$

In **Whitman's film theory (1923)**, a stagnant film of effective thickness $\delta$ is assumed to exist at the solid-fluid interface. Mass transfer occurs solely by molecular diffusion across this film. By applying Fick's law at steady state:

$$k_C = \frac{D_{AB}}{\delta}$$

where $D_{AB}$ is the molecular diffusivity. A higher $k_C$ corresponds to a thinner film (i.e., stronger convection or higher fluid velocity), and vice versa. The film theory is most applicable when the flow is laminar or quiescent.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Compare the **penetration theory** and **surface renewal theory** of mass transfer. Which is more appropriate for a bubbling fluidized bed?

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Penetration Theory (Higbie) | Surface Renewal (Danckwerts) |
|---|---|---|
| **Surface exposure time** | Fixed, $t_c$ | Random, distribution $s \, e^{-s\tau}$ |
| **Governing equation** | $k_C = 2\sqrt{D_{AB}/(\pi t_c)}$ | $k_C = \sqrt{D_{AB} \cdot s}$ |
| **Dependence on $D_{AB}$** | $D_{AB}^{1/2}$ | $D_{AB}^{1/2}$ |
| **Best suited to** | Bubbling beds with regular contact | Turbulent or stochastic contact |

In a **bubbling fluidized bed**, both bubble rise and emulsion back-mixing create a stochastic renewal of gas at particle surfaces. The **surface renewal theory** is generally more appropriate because bubble behavior is inherently random, leading to a distribution of contact times rather than a single value $t_c$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4 Choice A]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **mass transfer coefficient** using Higbie's **penetration theory**, starting from Fick's second law of diffusion. State clearly all initial and boundary conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gas-fluidized bed operates with bubbles of average diameter $d_b = 0.05$ m. The minimum fluidization velocity is $u_{mf} = 0.02$ m/s. The molecular diffusivity of the reacting gas is $D_{AB} = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Compute the **bubble-to-cloud mass transfer coefficient** $k_{bc}$ using the Kunii-Levenspiel correlation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Governing equation:**

Fick's second law for unsteady-state diffusion into a semi-infinite fluid element:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$$

**Step 2 — Initial and boundary conditions:**

$$C_A(z, 0) = C_{A,\infty}, \quad C_A(0, t) = C_{A,s}, \quad C_A(\infty, t) = C_{A,\infty}$$

**Step 3 — Solution (Carslaw & Jaeger):**

$$C_A(z, t) = C_{A,s} - (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) \, \text{erf}\left( \frac{z}{2\sqrt{D_{AB} t}} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Instantaneous surface flux:**

$$N_A(t) = -D_{AB} \left.\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z}\right|_{z=0} = (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t}}$$

**Step 5 — Time-averaging over contact time $t_c$:**

$$\overline{N_A} = \frac{1}{t_c} \int_0^{t_c} (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t}} \, dt$$

$$= 2 (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi t_c}}$$

**Step 6 — Identification of $k_C$:**

$$\boxed{k_C = 2 \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi \, t_c}}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** The mass transfer coefficient depends on the **square root of diffusivity** and **inverse square root of contact time**. Bubbling fluidized beds have short contact times, giving high $k_C$.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $d_b = 0.05$ m
- $u_{mf} = 0.02$ m/s
- $D_{AB} = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Kunii-Levenspiel correlation:**

$$k_{bc} = 4.50 \left(\frac{u_{mf}}{d_b}\right) + 5.85 \left(\frac{D_{AB}^{0.5} \, g^{0.25}}{d_b^{1.25}}\right)$$

**Term 1 — Convective throughflow:**

$$T_1 = 4.50 \times \frac{0.02}{0.05} = 4.50 \times 0.40 = 1.80 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Term 2 — Molecular diffusion:**

$$D_{AB}^{0.5} = \sqrt{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.472 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m/s}^{1/2}$$

$$g^{0.25} = (9.81)^{0.25} = 1.772 \; \text{m}^{0.5}/\text{s}^{0.5}$$

$$d_b^{1.25} = (0.05)^{1.25} = 0.05^{1} \times 0.05^{0.25} = 0.05 \times 0.4729 = 0.02365 \; \text{m}^{1.25}$$

$$T_2 = 5.85 \times \frac{(4.472 \times 10^{-3}) \times 1.772}{0.02365}$$

$$T_2 = 5.85 \times \frac{7.924 \times 10^{-3}}{0.02365} = 5.85 \times 0.3351 = 1.960 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Total:**

$$k_{bc} = T_1 + T_2 = 1.80 + 1.96 = 3.76 \; \text{m/s}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** Both convection and diffusion contribute comparably in this case. For larger bubbles, $T_1$ dominates; for smaller bubbles (high $d_b^{-1.25}$ scaling), diffusion becomes significant.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain the **two-film theory** of mass transfer. Derive the expression for the **overall gas-phase mass transfer coefficient** $K_G$ in terms of individual film coefficients $k_g$ and $k_L$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **physical significance** of the mass transfer coefficient in fluidized bed operations. How does the **Chilton–Colburn analogy** relate mass and heat transfer coefficients? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Concept of two-film theory:**

Lewis and Whitman (1924) proposed that at a gas-liquid interface, **two stagnant films** exist — one in the gas phase and one in the liquid phase. Mass transfer is governed by steady-state molecular diffusion through each film in series.

**Step 2 — Equal flux condition:**

At steady state, the molar flux through both films must be equal:

$$N_A = k_g (p_A - p_{A,i}) = k_L (C_{A,i} - C_L)$$

**Step 3 — Interface equilibrium (Henry's law region):**

$$p_{A,i} = m \, C_{A,i}$$

**Step 4 — Expressing driving forces:**

$$p_A - p_{A,i} = \frac{N_A}{k_g}, \quad C_{A,i} - C_L = \frac{N_A}{k_L}$$

**Step 5 — Total driving force:**

$$p_A - p_A^* = (p_A - p_{A,i}) + (p_{A,i} - p_A^*)$$

Where $p_A^* = m \, C_L$ (gas in equilibrium with bulk liquid):

$$p_A - p_A^* = \frac{N_A}{k_g} + m \cdot \frac{N_A}{k_L} = N_A \left( \frac{1}{k_g} + \frac{m}{k_L} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Define overall coefficient:**

$$N_A = K_G (p_A - p_A^*)$$

**Step 7 — Final result (resistance in series):**

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_g} + \frac{m}{k_L}}$$

**Interpretation:** The overall resistance is the sum of gas-film and liquid-film resistances. The phase with the larger individual resistance **controls** the overall mass transfer rate.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Physical Significance of Mass Transfer Coefficient in Fluidized Beds:**

1. **Quantifies transport efficiency:** $k_C$ measures the rate at which mass crosses the particle-fluid interface per unit driving force. Higher $k_C$ → faster drying, faster reactions, faster adsorption.

2. **Identifies rate-limiting step:** Comparing individual film resistances reveals which phase (gas or liquid) controls the overall rate. This guides process optimization (e.g., increasing gas velocity if gas-film controls).

3. **Enables scale-up:** Dimensionless correlations ($Sh = f(Re, Sc)$) allow engineers to predict $k_C$ at industrial scale from laboratory data.

4. **Couples with reaction kinetics:** For surface reactions, the observed rate is governed by both intrinsic kinetics and mass transfer. The **Mears criterion** ($-\Delta H \cdot r_A \cdot \rho_b \cdot R \cdot E / (h \cdot T_b^2 \cdot R_g) < 0.3$) and **Weisz-Prater criterion** are used to verify negligible external mass transfer limitation.

5. **Critical for design of dryers, combustors, catalytic reactors, and bio-reactors.**

---

**The Chilton–Colburn Analogy (1934):**

Chilton and Colburn observed experimentally that the dimensionless groups governing heat and mass transfer in turbulent flow are related through the following empirical analogy:

$$j_D = j_H$$

Where:

$$j_D = \frac{k_C}{u} \, Sc^{2/3} = St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3}$$

$$j_H = \frac{h}{\rho \, c_p \, u} \, Pr^{2/3} = St_h \cdot Pr^{2/3}$$

$St_m$ and $St_h$ are the **Stanton numbers** for mass and heat transfer, respectively.

**Key insight:** The analogy states that the **$j$-factor for mass transfer equals the $j$-factor for heat transfer** at the same Reynolds number. This is hugely useful because:

- Heat transfer measurements (often easier) can predict mass transfer coefficients.
- For gases where $Sc \approx Pr \approx 1$, the analogy reduces to $St_m \approx St_h$, or equivalently, $Nu \approx Sh$.

$$\boxed{j_D = j_H \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{k_C \cdot Sc^{2/3}}{u} = \frac{h}{\rho c_p u} Pr^{2/3}}$$

**Limitation:** The analogy breaks down at low $Re$ (laminar flow) and when $Pr$ and $Sc$ differ greatly (e.g., liquid metals, highly viscous oils).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Leading to Mark Deductions in KTU 2024 Examinations:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $k_C$ with $K_C$:** $k_C$ is an **individual** film coefficient (one phase), while $K_C$ is the **overall** coefficient (resistance-in-series). Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this confusion.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting units of $k_C$:** Always write **m/s** or **m·s⁻¹**, NOT m²/s (which is diffusivity). This single-unit error costs 1–2 marks.
> 
> 3. **Wrong exponent in $D_{AB}$:** Film theory gives $k_C \propto D_{AB}^1$; penetration and surface renewal give $k_C \propto D_{AB}^{1/2}$. Many students write the film-theory form in penetration-theory problems.
> 
> 4. **Skipping derivation steps:** The KTU Board requires **explicit step-by-step derivations** for the 14-mark questions. Writing only the final answer without intermediate steps results in a 4–5 mark penalty.
> 
> 5. **Ignoring the $m$ factor in two-film theory:** In the gas-side overall coefficient, the $m$ (Henry's constant slope) multiplies the $1/k_L$ term. Forgetting $m$ is a common 1-mark error.
> 
> 6. **Mixing up Frössling and Ranz-Marshall coefficients:** Frössling uses 0.552; Ranz-Marshall uses 0.6. Examiners will accept either but expect the correct numerical value if a problem provides the equation.
> 
> 7. **Sign convention in flux equations:** Always present $N_A$ as **positive** when species moves from high to low concentration. Sign errors in $J_A = -D_{AB} \, dC/dz$ cost marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision Before KTU 2024 Board Exam**

- [ ] **Mass transfer coefficient** $k_C$ (units: m/s) is the **proportionality constant** between flux and driving concentration difference: $N_A = k_C \, \Delta C$.
- [ ] **Three classical models:** Film ($k_C = D_{AB}/\delta$), Penetration ($k_C = 2\sqrt{D_{AB}/(\pi t_c)}$), Surface Renewal ($k_C = \sqrt{D_{AB} s}$).
- [ ] **Film theory** → $k_C \propto D_{AB}^1$; **Penetration/Surface Renewal** → $k_C \propto D_{AB}^{1/2}$.
- [ ] **Two-film theory** → resistances in series: $1/K_G = 1/k_g + m/k_L$.
- [ ] **Sherwood number** $Sh = k_C d_p / D_{AB}$ is the **dimensionless** form of $k_C$.
- [ ] **Ranz-Marshall** for single sphere: $Sh = 2 + 0.6 \, Re^{1/2} Sc^{1/3}$.
- [ ] **Frössling** for evaporation: $Sh = 2 + 0.552 \, Re^{1/2} Sc^{1/3}$.
- [ ] **Wilson-Geankoplis** for liquid-fluidized/packed: $Sh = (1.09/\varepsilon) Re^{0.33} Sc^{0.33}$.
- [ ] **Kunii-Levenspiel** for bubble-cloud transfer: $k_{bc} = 4.50(u_{mf}/d_b) + 5.85(D_{AB}^{0.5} g^{0.25}/d_b^{1.25})$.
- [ ] **Chilton-Colburn analogy** → $j_D = j_H$ (mass and heat $j$-factors are equal at same $Re$).
- [ ] **Gas-fluidized beds** → use **penetration** or **surface renewal** theory; **Liquid-fluidized beds** → use **film theory** with appropriate correlations.
- [ ] **Rate-limiting phase:** Larger of $1/k_g$ vs. $m/k_L$ controls the overall transfer.
- [ ] **Bubble size scaling:** Smaller bubbles → higher $k_{bc}$ (larger interfacial area, shorter contact time).
- [ ] **Always state units** (m/s) and **sign convention** in the flux equation.
- [ ] For **KTU 14-mark questions**, present derivations with **all boundary conditions, integration steps, and identification of $k_C$** clearly visible.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Transfer - Single Spheres and Fixed Bed and Gas Fluidized Bed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Heat Transfer - Single Spheres and Fixed Bed and Gas Fluidized Bed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Heat Transfer in Particulate Systems

Heat transfer involving particles is one of the **most critical aspects** of fluidization engineering, governing the design of catalytic reactors, dryers, combustors, and heat exchangers. The topic is structured progressively from the simplest geometry — a **single isolated sphere** — to complex multi-particle systems like **fixed beds** and ultimately **gas fluidized beds** where particles are in dynamic suspension.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Heat transfer* in fluidized systems refers to the combined mechanisms of convection, conduction (through the gas film, particle phase, and bed structure), and radiation (at high temperatures) that govern thermal energy exchange between solid particles, the surrounding fluid, and the containing walls.

### 1.2 Heat Transfer to a Single Sphere

**Formal Definition:**
Heat transfer between a single spherical particle and a flowing fluid is governed by **forced convection** across the hydrodynamic boundary layer surrounding the particle. The thermal resistance is concentrated predominantly in the **gas film** surrounding the particle.

The process is characterized by three dimensionless groups:
- **Nusselt Number** $\left(Nu = \frac{hD_p}{k_f}\right)$ — ratio of convective to conductive heat transfer
- **Reynolds Number** $\left(Re_p = \frac{\rho_f u D_p}{\mu_f}\right)$ — ratio of inertial to viscous forces
- **Prandtl Number** $\left(Pr = \frac{\mu_f C_{pf}}{k_f}\right)$ — ratio of momentum to thermal diffusivity

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Boiling Tea Kettle:**
> Imagine a metal sphere suspended in a stream of hot gas, much like a tea ball in a cup of hot water. Initially, a thin layer of cooler fluid "sticks" to the sphere (the **thermal boundary layer**). As the gas flows faster (higher $Re_p$), this layer is stripped away, and the sphere heats up or cools down much more quickly. If the gas were thick like honey (high $Pr$), heat transfer would be sluggish; if it were thin like steam (low $Pr$), heat transfer would be rapid.

### 1.3 Heat Transfer in Fixed Beds

A **fixed bed** is a static packing of solid particles through which a fluid flows. Heat transfer here is more complex than for a single sphere because:
1. Particles contact each other, creating solid conduction paths.
2. The fluid flows through tortuous channels in the interstices.
3. Wall effects cause radial temperature gradients.
4. The geometry deviates from the ideal single-sphere case.

Three distinct thermal resistances are encountered:
- **External resistance** — between the bed and the containing wall
- **Internal (inter-particle) resistance** — between fluid and individual particles
- **Bed effective conductivity resistance** — through the solid-fluid matrix

### 1.4 Heat Transfer in Gas Fluidized Beds

When particles are fluidized, heat transfer becomes remarkably efficient — often **one to two orders of magnitude higher** than in a fixed bed at the same superficial velocity. The reason is the constant motion of particles, which continually sweep hot/cold zones into the bulk and disrupt thermal boundary layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Mechanisms of Heat Transfer in a Gas Fluidized Bed (Zabrodsky's Framework):**
> 1. **Particle Convective Component** ($h_{pc}$) — heat carried by moving particles
> 2. **Gas Convective Component** ($h_{gc}$) — heat carried by the flowing gas
> 3. **Radiative Component** ($h_r$) — significant only when $T > 700°C$
>
> The total coefficient: $h = h_{pc} + h_{gc} + h_r$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Boiling Popcorn Pot:**
> Picture a pot of popcorn kernels heating on a stove. As the kernels pop and bounce around (fluidization!), each one visits the hot bottom, picks up heat, travels to the cooler top, releases heat, and returns. This is exactly the **particle convective mechanism** — solid particles act as "thermal messengers." If the kernels sat still on the bottom (fixed bed), only the bottom layer would burn while the top stayed cold. This is why fluidization is revolutionary for uniform temperature distribution.

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants and Reference Values:**
> - Stefan-Boltzmann constant: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
> - Typical $Pr$ for air at 300 K: $Pr \approx 0.71$
> - Typical $Re_{mf}$ (minimum fluidization Reynolds): $10^{-3}$ to $10^2$
> - Bed-to-wall $h$ in gas fluidized bed: **100–600 W/m²K** (vs. 20–80 W/m²K in fixed bed)
> - Particle-to-gas $h$ in gas fluidized bed: **50–500 W/m²K**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Heat Transfer to a Single Sphere — Theory Breakdown

The thermal boundary layer thickness $\delta_t$ around a sphere shrinks as $Re_p$ increases, reducing convective resistance. The process is analyzed through these key steps:

**Step 1 — Film Theory (Low Re, $Re_p < 1$):**
At very low Reynolds numbers, flow is laminar and surrounds the sphere symmetrically. Heat transfer follows:

$$\frac{Nu}{Pr} = \text{constant}$$

The classical analytical result for a sphere in creeping flow is:

$$Nu = 2.0 \quad (\text{when } Re_p \to 0, \text{ conduction only})$$

**Step 2 — Boundary Layer Theory (Intermediate Re, $1 < Re_p < 10^4$):**
The flow separates, creating a wake region. Empirical correlations become necessary:

**Ranz-Marshall Correlation (1952):**

$$Nu = 2 + 0.6 \, Re_p^{1/2} \, Pr^{1/3}, \quad 0 < Re_p < 200, \quad 0 < Pr < \infty$$

**Frössling Correlation (1938):**

$$Nu = 2 + 0.6 \, Re_p^{1/2} \, Pr^{1/3}, \quad \text{similar form for } Re_p < 1000$$

**Step 3 — High Reynolds Number ($Re_p > 10^4$):**
A laminar-turbulent transition occurs. The exponent on $Re_p$ increases to 0.6.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight — Why does $Nu$ increase with $Re_p$?**
> Higher velocity means greater turbulence, thinner boundary layers, and a steeper temperature gradient at the particle surface — all of which enhance heat transfer. The **$Re_p^{1/2}$** dependence reflects the boundary layer's growth pattern along the sphere's surface.

### 2.2 Heat Transfer in Fixed Beds — Deep Analysis

The mechanism is dominated by three physical processes:

**A. Effective Thermal Conductivity ($k_{eb}$):**
The bed is treated as a quasi-homogeneous medium with an effective conductivity accounting for both solid and fluid contributions:

$$k_{eb} = k_{eb}^0 + k_{eb,t}$$

Where:
- $k_{eb}^0$ = stagnant conductivity (no flow)
- $k_{eb,t}$ = turbulent/dispersive conductivity due to flow

**Kunii & Levenspiel Model (Stagnant Bed):**

$$\frac{k_{eb}^0}{k_f} = \varepsilon \left[1 + \frac{(1-\varepsilon)}{\varepsilon} \cdot \frac{k_s}{k_f} \cdot \phi\right]$$

Where $\phi$ is a shape factor accounting for contact between particles (typically $\phi \approx 0.02$ to $0.05$ for spheres).

**B. Wall Heat Transfer Coefficient ($h_w$):**
Heat transfer between the bed surface and the wall is governed by a "packed-bed" Nusselt number:

**Leva Correlation:**

$$h_w D_p / k_f = 0.813 \left(\frac{D_p \, G}{\mu_f}\right)^{0.9} \exp\left(-\frac{6 D_p}{D_t}\right)$$

**C. Internal Heat Transfer (Fluid to Particle):**
Ranz-Marshall is still applied, but with a modified $Nu$:

$$Nu_p = 2 + 1.8 \, Re_p^{1/2} \, Pr^{1/3}, \quad 15 < Re_p < 8500$$

### 2.3 Heat Transfer in Gas Fluidized Beds — Deep Analysis

**Mechanism 1: Particle Convective Component**
Mickley & Fairbanks (1955) proposed:

$$h_{pc} = \rho_s C_{ps} u_s (1 - \varepsilon_{mf}) \left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{H}{H_0}\right)\right]$$

Where:
- $u_s$ = particle circulation velocity
- $H$ = vertical position in bed
- $H_0$ = characteristic length (~0.5 m)

**Mechanism 2: Gas Convective Component**
Gelperin & Einstein (1971):

$$h_{gc} = \frac{0.009 \, \rho_f C_{pf} u_0}{1 - \varepsilon_{mf}} \cdot Pr^{1/3} \left(\frac{\mu_f}{\mu_{s, wall}}\right)^{0.245}$$

**Mechanism 3: Radiative Component**
Important for high-T applications (e.g., combustion, coal gasification):

$$h_r = \sigma (\bar{T}^3) \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot \frac{1}{\frac{1}{\varepsilon_p} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_w} - 1}$$

**Xavier & Davidson Correlation (1977) — Most Used:**

$$Nu_{max} = 0.00082 \left(\frac{C_{pf} \rho_f D_p u_0}{k_f}\right)^{1.6} \left(\frac{\rho_s}{\rho_f}\right)^{0.65} \left(\frac{C_{ps}}{C_{pf}}\right)^{0.5}$$

**Wen & Chang (1980) Alternative:**

$$h = 0.16 \left(\frac{k_f}{D_p}\right) (C_{pf} \rho_f / k_f)^{0.4} \left(\frac{\rho_f u_0 D_p}{\mu_f}\right)^{0.76} \left(\frac{\rho_s C_{ps}}{\rho_f C_{pf}}\right)^{0.4}$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation / Correlation | Application Range | Key Variables |
|---|------------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $Nu = 2.0$ | Single sphere, $Re_p \to 0$ | $D_p, h, k_f$ |
| 2 | $Nu = 2 + 0.6 Re_p^{1/2} Pr^{1/3}$ (Ranz-Marshall) | Single sphere, $0 < Re_p < 200$ | Forced convection |
| 3 | $Nu = 2 + 1.8 Re_p^{1/2} Pr^{1/3}$ | Fixed bed, particle-fluid | $15 < Re_p < 8500$ |
| 4 | $h_w = 0.813(k_f/D_p) Re_p^{0.9} e^{-6D_p/D_t}$ | Fixed bed, wall | Leva, $Re_p < 100$ |
| 5 | $h_{pc} = \rho_s C_{ps} u_s (1-\varepsilon_{mf})[1-e^{-H/H_0}]$ | Fluidized bed, particle | Mickley-Fairbanks |
| 6 | $h = 0.009 \rho_f C_{pf} u_0 Pr^{1/3} / (1-\varepsilon_{mf})$ | Fluidized bed, gas | Gelperin-Einstein |
| 7 | $Nu_{max} = 0.00082(Re_p Pr_{bed})^{1.6}(\rho_s/\rho_f)^{0.65}(C_{ps}/C_{pf})^{0.5}$ | Fluidized bed, max $h$ | Xavier-Davidson |
| 8 | $h_r = \sigma \bar{T}^3 / [1/\varepsilon_p + 1/\varepsilon_w - 1]$ | $T > 700°C$ | Radiative |
| 9 | $k_{eb}^0/k_f = \varepsilon[1 + (1-\varepsilon)/\varepsilon \cdot (k_s/k_f)\phi]$ | Stagnant fixed bed | Effective conductivity |
| 10 | $h_{max}$ at $u_0/u_{mf} \approx 1.5 - 3$ | Empirical observation | Operating window |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **Single sphere correlations** → Catalyst particle design, droplet evaporation
> - **Fixed bed correlations** → Packed-bed reactor design, regenerator sizing
> - **Fluidized bed correlations** → FCC units, coal combustors, pharmaceutical dryers, heat exchangers
> - **Radiative correlations** → High-temperature processes (e.g., iron ore reduction, calcination)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Nusselt Number for a Single Sphere from Boundary Layer Theory

**Objective:** Derive the functional form $Nu = 2 + C \cdot Re_p^n Pr^m$ from physical scaling arguments.

**Step 1 — Setup the Heat Flux at the Sphere Surface**

The local convective heat flux from a sphere surface to the fluid is given by Newton's law of cooling:

$$q'' = h \, (T_s - T_\infty)$$

The total heat transfer rate is:

$$Q = h \, A_s \, (T_s - T_\infty) = h \, \pi D_p^2 \, (T_s - T_\infty)$$

**Step 2 — Apply Thermal Boundary Layer Concept**

The thermal boundary layer thickness $\delta_t(x)$ at a position $x$ on the sphere scales as:

$$\delta_t(x) \sim \left(\frac{\nu x}{u_\infty}\right)^{1/2} Pr^{-1/2}$$

This is the classical Pohlhausen result for laminar flow over a flat plate, adapted to the sphere's stagnation region.

**Step 3 — Estimate Local Heat Transfer Coefficient**

The local $h$ is:

$$h_{local} \sim \frac{k_f}{\delta_t} \sim k_f \left(\frac{u_\infty}{\nu x}\right)^{1/2} Pr^{1/2}$$

**Step 4 — Average Over the Sphere Surface**

The local $Nux$ averaged over the sphere's projected area gives:

$$\overline{Nu}_D = \frac{\bar{h} D_p}{k_f} = C \cdot Re_p^{1/2} \cdot Pr^{1/3}$$

Note: The exponent on $Pr$ is empirically adjusted to $1/3$ (not $1/2$) to account for varying thermal boundary conditions in experimental data.

**Step 5 — Add the Pure Conduction Limit**

At $Re_p \to 0$, only conduction exists, giving $Nu = 2$ (the classical result for a sphere in stagnant fluid). Combining:

$$\boxed{Nu = 2 + 0.6 \, Re_p^{1/2} \, Pr^{1/3}}$$

This is the **Ranz-Marshall correlation**.

**Step 6 — Limiting Cases Check**

Case A: $Re_p \to 0$:

$$Nu \to 2 + 0.6(0) Pr^{1/3} = 2 \quad \checkmark$$

Case B: $Re_p = 100, Pr = 0.7$ (typical air):

$$Nu = 2 + 0.6 \times 10 \times 0.7^{1/3} = 2 + 6 \times 0.888 = 7.33$$

Case C: $Re_p = 1000, Pr = 7$ (water):

$$Nu = 2 + 0.6 \times 31.6 \times 1.913 = 2 + 36.3 = 38.3$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Bed-to-Wall Heat Transfer Coefficient (Xavier-Davidson)

**Objective:** Show that $h_{max}$ occurs at a specific $u_0/u_{mf}$ ratio.

**Step 1 — Define Reduced Velocity**

$$U = \frac{u_0 - u_{mf}}{u_{mf}}$$

**Step 2 — Empirical Form of $h$ vs. $U$**

The Xavier-Davidson model proposes:

$$h = h_{max} \cdot [1 - \exp(-K \cdot U)]$$

Where $K$ is a constant. Differentiating to find the maximum:

$$\frac{dh}{dU} = h_{max} \cdot K \cdot \exp(-K \cdot U) > 0 \text{ always}$$

Wait — this gives monotonic increase. The empirical fact is that $h_{max}$ is observed at $U \approx 1.5 - 3$ experimentally due to bubble formation above this.

**Step 3 — Final Correlation**

$$Nu_{max} = 0.00082 \left(\frac{C_{pf} \rho_f D_p u_0}{k_f}\right)^{1.6} \left(\frac{\rho_s}{\rho_f}\right)^{0.65} \left(\frac{C_{ps}}{C_{pf}}\right)^{0.5}$$

Where the dimensionless group $(C_{pf} \rho_f D_p u_0 / k_f) = Re_p \cdot Pr$ (a Péclet-like number).

**Step 4 — Numerical Example**

For air fluidizing sand at atmospheric conditions:
- $D_p = 200 \, \mu m = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- $u_0 = 0.05$ m/s
- $\rho_f = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\rho_s = 2500$ kg/m³
- $C_{pf} = 1000$ J/kg·K, $C_{ps} = 800$ J/kg·K
- $k_f = 0.026$ W/m·K
- $\mu_f = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s

**Calculate Reynolds number:**

$$Re_p = \frac{\rho_f u_0 D_p}{\mu_f} = \frac{1.2 \times 0.05 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.667$$

**Calculate Prandtl number:**

$$Pr = \frac{\mu_f C_{pf}}{k_f} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-5} \times 1000}{0.026} = 0.692$$

**Calculate the Péclet-like group:**

$$Re_p \cdot Pr = 0.667 \times 0.692 = 0.461$$

**Compute $Nu_{max}$:**

$$Nu_{max} = 0.00082 \times (0.461)^{1.6} \times \left(\frac{2500}{1.2}\right)^{0.65} \times \left(\frac{800}{1000}\right)^{0.5}$$

$$= 0.00082 \times 0.305 \times 100 \times 0.894$$

$$= 0.0224$$

**Calculate $h_{max}$:**

$$h_{max} = \frac{Nu_{max} \cdot k_f}{D_p} = \frac{0.0224 \times 0.026}{2 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.91 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

This is low because the particle is fine and velocity is low. In practice, gas fluidized bed $h$ values are 100–600 W/m²K for larger velocities and bigger particles.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Comparing Single Sphere vs. Fluidized Bed

**Problem:** Compare the heat transfer coefficient for a single sphere of diameter 1 mm in air (Re=100, T=300K) with a gas fluidized bed of the same particles at the same conditions.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Single sphere (Ranz-Marshall):**

$$Nu = 2 + 0.6(100)^{1/2}(0.71)^{1/3} = 2 + 6 \times 0.892 = 7.35$$

$$h_{single} = \frac{Nu \cdot k_f}{D_p} = \frac{7.35 \times 0.026}{10^{-3}} = 191.1 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Gas fluidized bed (Xavier-Davidson, with typical correction):**

For fluidization at $u_0/u_{mf} = 2$, $h$ in fluidized bed is empirically found to be **5–10 times higher** than the single sphere value due to particle motion effects.

$$h_{fluidized} \approx 191.1 \times 7 = 1338 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> The **particle convective component** in the fluidized bed is responsible for the dramatic enhancement. Without fluidization, the boundary layer would have to be re-established every time the gas is heated; with fluidization, the particles physically transport heat at much higher rates than the gas alone can. This is why **fluidized bed combustors achieve >95% thermal efficiency**.

### 3.4 Derivation: Radiative Heat Transfer Coefficient

**Objective:** Derive $h_r$ for high-T fluidized beds.

**Step 1 — Stefan-Boltzmann Law for a Gray Surface:**

$$q''_{rad} = \varepsilon \sigma (T_s^4 - T_w^4)$$

**Step 2 — Linearize Around a Mean Temperature $\bar{T}$:**

$$T_s^4 - T_w^4 = (T_s - T_w)(T_s + T_w)(T_s^2 + T_w^2) \approx (T_s - T_w) \cdot 4\bar{T}^3$$

**Step 3 — Define Equivalent $h_r$:**

$$q''_{rad} = h_r (T_s - T_w) \implies h_r = 4 \varepsilon \sigma \bar{T}^3$$

**Step 4 — Accounting for Two Surfaces (Particle and Wall):**

For radiation between a particle (emissivity $\varepsilon_p$) and a wall (emissivity $\varepsilon_w$):

$$h_r = \frac{4 \sigma \bar{T}^3}{\frac{1}{\varepsilon_p} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_w} - 1}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Estimate at 1000 K:**

For $\varepsilon_p = 0.9, \varepsilon_w = 0.85$:

$$h_r = \frac{4 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1000)^3}{\frac{1}{0.9} + \frac{1}{0.85} - 1} = \frac{226.8}{0.288} = 787 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

This shows that **radiation dominates** above ~1000 K in fluidized combustors.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Heat Transfer Hierarchy in Particulate Systems

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Heat Transfer in Particulate Systems] --> B[Single Sphere]
    A --> C[Fixed Bed]
    A --> D[Gas Fluidized Bed]
    
    B --> B1[Forced Convection]
    B --> B2[Film Conduction]
    B --> B3[Nu = 2 + 0.6 Re^0.5 Pr^0.33]
    
    C --> C1[Effective Conductivity]
    C --> C2[Wall Heat Transfer]
    C --> C3[Internal Particle-Fluid]
    
    D --> D1[Particle Convective]
    D --> D2[Gas Convective]
    D --> D3[Radiative above 700C]
    
    D1 --> D1a[Mickley Fairbanks Model]
    D1 --> D1b[Particles as Thermal Messengers]
    
    D2 --> D2a[Gelperin Einstein Correlation]
    D2 --> D2b[Film Theory Application]
    
    D3 --> D3a[Stefan Boltzmann Law]
    D3 --> D3b[Dominant at High T]
```

### 4.2 Comparison of Heat Transfer Mechanisms

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph "Single Sphere"
        SS1[Particle Surface]
        SS2[Gas Film Boundary Layer]
        SS3[Bulk Fluid]
        SS1 --> SS2
        SS2 --> SS3
    end
    
    subgraph "Fixed Bed"
        FB1[Wall Surface]
        FB2[Particle-Fluid Matrix]
        FB3[Interparticle Contacts]
        FB1 --> FB2
        FB2 --> FB3
    end
    
    subgraph "Gas Fluidized Bed"
        GF1[Heated Wall]
        GF2[Bubble Phase]
        GF3[Emulsion Phase]
        GF4[Moving Particles]
        GF1 --> GF2
        GF1 --> GF3
        GF4 --> GF1
        GF4 --> GF2
    end
```

### 4.3 Heat Transfer Coefficient Comparison Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Choose System Type] --> Q1{Fluidization State?}
    Q1 -->|Static| Q2{Single Particle?}
    Q1 -->|Dynamic| Q3{Operating Temperature?}
    
    Q2 -->|Yes| P1[Use Ranz-Marshall]
    Q2 -->|No| P2[Use Leva or Kunii-Levenspiel]
    
    P1 --> P3[h = Nu kf / Dp]
    P2 --> P4[Effective Bed Conductivity Model]
    
    Q3 -->|T < 700C| P5[Gelperin-Einstein]
    Q3 -->|T > 700C| P6[Add Radiative Component]
    
    P5 --> P7[h = hpc + hgc]
    P6 --> P8[h = hpc + hgc + hr]
    
    P7 --> End[Design Heat Exchanger or Reactor]
    P8 --> End
    P3 --> End
    P4 --> End
```

### 4.4 Functional Block Diagram of Gas Fluidized Bed Heat Transfer

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph "External Heat Source or Sink"
        WH[Heated Wall Surface]
    end
    
    subgraph "Transport Mechanisms"
        PC[Particle Convective Heat Transfer]
        GC[Gas Convective Heat Transfer]
        RD[Radiative Heat Transfer]
    end

        EM[Emulsion Phase with Particles]
        BB[Bubble Phase with Gas Bypass]
    end

        PS[Particle Surface]
        GS[Gas Stream]
    end
    
    WH --> PC
    WH --> GC
    WH --> RD
    PC --> EM
    GC --> BB
    RD --> EM
    EM --> PS
    BB --> GS
    PS --> GB[Gas in Emulsion]
    GB --> BB
```

### 4.5 Decision Tree for Selecting Heat Transfer Correlation

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Identify Problem Type] --> B{System Configuration?}
    B -->|Single Particle| C[Apply Ranz-Marshall]
    B -->|Multiple Particles Static| D[Apply Kunii-Levenspiel]
    B -->|Multiple Particles Fluidized| E{Operating Conditions?}
    
    E -->|Low T less than 700C| F[Use Gelperin-Einstein or Xavier-Davidson]
    E -->|High T above 700C| G[Use Maskaev and Baskakov with Radiative]
    E -->|Large Scale Industrial| H[Use Denloye and Botterill]
    
    C --> I[Calculate h from Nu correlation]
    D --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    
    I --> J[Design Equipment Sizing]
    J --> K[Reactor or Heat Exchanger]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

State the **Ranz-Marshall correlation** for heat transfer to a single sphere. List the range of applicability.

**Model Answer:**

The Ranz-Marshall correlation for heat transfer between a single sphere and a flowing fluid is:

$$Nu = 2 + 0.6 \, Re_p^{0.5} \, Pr^{1/3}$$

Where:
- $Nu = hD_p/k_f$ is the Nusselt number
- $Re_p = \rho_f u D_p / \mu_f$ is the particle Reynolds number
- $Pr = \mu_f C_{pf} / k_f$ is the Prandtl number

**Range of Applicability:**
- $0 < Re_p < 200$
- $0 < Pr < \infty$ (covers gases, liquids)
- The first term (2) represents pure conduction in the limit of zero velocity
- The second term captures forced convection enhancement

> [!NOTE]
> **CO Mapping:** CO4 — Understand heat transfer mechanisms in fluidized systems.

---

**Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

Explain the **three mechanisms of heat transfer** in a gas fluidized bed as proposed by Zabrodsky.

**Model Answer:**

According to **Zabrodsky's framework**, the total bed-to-surface heat transfer coefficient in a gas fluidized bed is the sum of three components:

1. **Particle Convective Component ($h_{pc}$):** Heat transported by the motion of solid particles acting as thermal carriers between hot and cold zones. This is typically the **dominant mechanism** in bubbling fluidized beds.

2. **Gas Convective Component ($h_{gc}$):** Heat transferred by the gas flowing through the bed, both through the emulsion and bubble phases. This is similar to convection in fixed beds but enhanced by bubble-induced mixing.

3. **Radiative Component ($h_r$):** Heat transfer by thermal radiation. Becomes significant only at **high temperatures (T > 700°C)** and is calculated using the Stefan-Boltzmann law with view factors between particles and walls.

Total: $h_{total} = h_{pc} + h_{gc} + h_r$

> [!NOTE]
> **CO Mapping:** CO4 — Application of heat transfer theory to fluidization.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]**

(a) Derive the **Ranz-Marshall correlation** for heat transfer to a single sphere, explaining the physical significance of each term. (7 marks)

(b) For a gas fluidized bed operating at $u_0 = 0.15$ m/s with sand particles of $D_p = 500 \, \mu m$, calculate the **maximum bed-to-wall heat transfer coefficient** using the Xavier-Davidson correlation. Assume air at 300 K, $\rho_s = 2500$ kg/m³, $C_{ps} = 800$ J/kg·K, and use the maximum velocity condition. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

The derivation proceeds in the following steps:

**Step 1:** The local convective heat transfer coefficient at the sphere surface scales as the inverse of the thermal boundary layer thickness:

$$h_{local} \sim k_f / \delta_t$$

**Step 2:** The thermal boundary layer thickness over a sphere in the stagnation region scales as:

$$\delta_t \sim \sqrt{\frac{\nu x}{u_\infty}} \cdot Pr^{-1/2}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting and averaging over the sphere surface yields:

$$\overline{Nu}_D = C_1 Re_p^{1/2} Pr^{1/3}$$

where $C_1$ is a constant determined experimentally.

**Step 4:** Adding the conduction limit ($Nu = 2$ as $Re_p \to 0$):

$$Nu = 2 + 0.6 Re_p^{1/2} Pr^{1/3}$$

**Physical Significance:**
- The constant "2" represents pure conduction through a stagnant fluid — this is the **minimum possible heat transfer**.
- The term $0.6 Re_p^{1/2} Pr^{1/3}$ represents the **forced convection enhancement** due to flow.
- The exponent 1/2 on $Re_p$ comes from boundary layer growth theory.
- The exponent 1/3 on $Pr$ is empirical, accounting for finite thermal diffusivity.

**Part (b) — Xavier-Davidson Calculation:**

Given:
- $u_0 = 0.15$ m/s, $D_p = 500 \, \mu m = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- Air at 300 K: $\rho_f = 1.177$ kg/m³, $\mu_f = 1.846 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s
- $k_f = 0.0263$ W/m·K, $C_{pf} = 1007$ J/kg·K
- $\rho_s = 2500$ kg/m³, $C_{ps} = 800$ J/kg·K

**Step 1: Calculate $Re_p$:**

$$Re_p = \frac{\rho_f u_0 D_p}{\mu_f} = \frac{1.177 \times 0.15 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.846 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.78$$

**Step 2: Calculate $Pr$:**

$$Pr = \frac{\mu_f C_{pf}}{k_f} = \frac{1.846 \times 10^{-5} \times 1007}{0.0263} = 0.707$$

**Step 3: Calculate $Re_p \cdot Pr$ (Péclet-like group):**

$$Re_p \cdot Pr = 4.78 \times 0.707 = 3.38$$

**Step 4: Calculate density ratio term:**

$$\left(\frac{\rho_s}{\rho_f}\right)^{0.65} = \left(\frac{2500}{1.177}\right)^{0.65} = (2124)^{0.65} = 175.3$$

**Step 5: Calculate specific heat ratio term:**

$$\left(\frac{C_{ps}}{C_{pf}}\right)^{0.5} = \left(\frac{800}{1007}\right)^{0.5} = 0.891$$

**Step 6: Apply Xavier-Davidson correlation:**

$$Nu_{max} = 0.00082 \times (3.38)^{1.6} \times 175.3 \times 0.891$$

Computing $(3.38)^{1.6} = e^{1.6 \ln(3.38)} = e^{1.6 \times 1.217} = e^{1.947} = 7.01$:

$$Nu_{max} = 0.00082 \times 7.01 \times 175.3 \times 0.891 = 0.897$$

**Step 7: Calculate $h_{max}$:**

$$h_{max} = \frac{Nu_{max} \cdot k_f}{D_p} = \frac{0.897 \times 0.0263}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 47.2 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Result:** $h_{max} \approx 47.2$ W/m²K. The maximum occurs at $u_0/u_{mf} \approx 1.5 - 3$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]**

(a) With a neat sketch, describe the **heat transfer mechanisms** in a gas fluidized bed. Compare fixed bed and fluidized bed heat transfer coefficients. (7 marks)

(b) A **fixed bed** of spherical catalyst particles ($D_p = 2$ mm, $\rho_s = 1200$ kg/m³) is being used in a reactor. Air at 400 K flows through with $u_0 = 0.5$ m/s. Calculate the **fluid-to-particle heat transfer coefficient** using an appropriate correlation. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Heat Transfer Mechanisms and Comparison:**

**Sketch of Mechanisms in Gas Fluidized Bed:**

```
┌─────────────────────────┐
│     HEATED WALL         │
│  ═══════════════════    │ ← Wall (T_w)
│  ↓↓↓↓ Heat In ↓↓↓↓     │
├─────────────────────────┤
│ ○○○●○○●○○●○○●○○●○○  │ ← Bubbles + Particles
│ ●○●●○●●○●●○●●○●●○●  │   (Emulsion Phase)
│ ○●○●●○●●○●●○●●○●●○  │   T_p, T_g
│  ↑↑↑ Particles ↑↑↑     │ ← Particle Motion
│  ↑↑↑ Carry Heat ↑↑↑    │
└─────────────────────────┘
   FLUIDIZED BED SECTION
```

**Mechanisms:**
1. **Particle Convection** — Hot particles move from wall to bulk carrying heat
2. **Gas Convection** — Hot gas flows through bed, transferring heat to particles
3. **Radiation** — Significant only at T > 700°C

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Fixed Bed | Gas Fluidized Bed |
|----------|-----------|-------------------|
| Bed-to-wall $h$ (W/m²K) | 20–80 | 100–600 |
| Temperature uniformity | Poor (radial gradients) | Excellent (±2°C) |
| Mechanism dominant | Conduction through contacts | Particle convection |
| Hot spot risk | High | Very low |
| Particle mixing | None | Vigorous |

**Part (b) — Fixed Bed Calculation:**

Given:
- $D_p = 2$ mm $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $\rho_s = 1200$ kg/m³
- $u_0 = 0.5$ m/s
- Air at 400 K: $\rho_f = 0.524$ kg/m³, $\mu_f = 2.3 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $k_f = 0.0338$ W/m·K, $C_{pf} = 1014$ J/kg·K

**Step 1: Calculate $Re_p$:**

$$Re_p = \frac{\rho_f u_0 D_p}{\mu_f} = \frac{0.524 \times 0.5 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}{2.3 \times 10^{-5}} = 22.78$$

**Step 2: Verify the range for Ranz-Marshall with $C_1 = 1.8$ (fixed bed):**

Since $Re_p = 22.78$ is in the range $15 < Re_p < 8500$, the correlation is valid.

**Step 3: Calculate $Pr$:**

$$Pr = \frac{\mu_f C_{pf}}{k_f} = \frac{2.3 \times 10^{-5} \times 1014}{0.0338} = 0.690$$

**Step 4: Apply Ranz-Marshall (fixed-bed version):**

$$Nu_p = 2 + 1.8 Re_p^{1/2} Pr^{1/3}$$

$$Nu_p = 2 + 1.8 \times (22.78)^{0.5} \times (0.690)^{1/3}$$

$$Nu_p = 2 + 1.8 \times 4.773 \times 0.884 = 2 + 7.60 = 9.60$$

**Step 5: Calculate $h_p$:**

$$h_p = \frac{Nu_p \cdot k_f}{D_p} = \frac{9.60 \times 0.0338}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 162.2 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Result:** $h_p \approx 162$ W/m²K for the fluid-to-particle heat transfer in the fixed bed.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the "+2" term** in Ranz-Marshall — many students drop the conduction limit, losing 1 mark.
> 
> 2. **Mixing up particle and bed-to-wall coefficients** — the question must be read carefully. "Fluid-to-particle" uses $Nu_p$, while "bed-to-wall" uses $h_w$ from Leva's correlation.
> 
> 3. **Wrong units in $Re_p$** — $D_p$ must be in meters, $\mu_f$ in Pa·s, $\rho_f$ in kg/m³, $u_0$ in m/s. Mixing cm and m costs 2 marks.
> 
> 4. **Ignoring the radiative component** at high T — for fluidized combustors at 800°C+, the radiative term contributes 20–40% of total $h$ and must be included.
> 
> 5. **Using the wrong fluidization state** — students often apply fixed-bed correlations (Ranz-Marshall with 1.8) to a fluidized bed where Gelperin-Einstein or Xavier-Davidson is required. Always check the system state.
> 
> 6. **Not stating the validity range** of the chosen correlation — KTU examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for range specification.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4: Heat Transfer — Rapid Revision Checklist**
> 
> **Single Sphere:**
> - $Nu = 2 + 0.6 Re_p^{0.5} Pr^{1/3}$ (Ranz-Marshall)
> - Valid for $0 < Re_p < 200$
> - "2" = pure conduction limit; $0.6 Re_p^{0.5} Pr^{1/3}$ = convection
> - Higher $Re_p$ → thinner boundary layer → higher $h$
> 
> **Fixed Bed:**
> - Three resistance types: bed effective conductivity, wall-to-bed, fluid-to-particle
> - Kunii-Levenspiel: $k_{eb}^0/k_f = \varepsilon[1 + (1-\varepsilon)/\varepsilon \cdot (k_s/k_f)\phi]$
> - Leva for wall: $h_w = 0.813(k_f/D_p) Re_p^{0.9} e^{-6D_p/D_t}$
> - Modified Ranz-Marshall: $Nu_p = 2 + 1.8 Re_p^{0.5} Pr^{1/3}$
> 
> **Gas Fluidized Bed:**
> - Three mechanisms: particle convection, gas convection, radiation
> - Xavier-Davidson: $Nu_{max} = 0.00082(Re Pr)^{1.6}(\rho_s/\rho_f)^{0.65}(C_{ps}/C_{pf})^{0.5}$
> - Gelperin-Einstein: $h_{gc} = 0.009 \rho_f C_{pf} u_0 Pr^{1/3} / (1-\varepsilon_{mf})$
> - Mickley-Fairbanks: $h_{pc} = \rho_s C_{ps} u_s (1-\varepsilon_{mf})[1 - e^{-H/H_0}]$
> - $h_r = 4\sigma \bar{T}^3 / [1/\varepsilon_p + 1/\varepsilon_w - 1]$
> - $h_{max}$ occurs at $u_0/u_{mf} \approx 1.5 - 3$
> 
> **Comparison Numbers to Remember:**
> - Fixed bed $h$: 20–80 W/m²K
> - Fluidized bed $h$: 100–600 W/m²K
> - Radiation dominates above 1000 K
> - Fluidized bed temperature uniformity: ±2°C (excellent!)
> 
> **Dimensionless Numbers:**
> - $Nu = hD_p/k_f$
> - $Re_p = \rho_f u D_p/\mu_f$
> - $Pr = \mu_f C_{pf}/k_f$
> - Biot number rarely used (internal conduction)
> 
> **Industrial Applications:**
> - FCC regenerators: fluidized bed, high $h$
> - Coal combustors: fluidized bed with radiation
> - Pharmaceutical dryers: fluidized bed, controlled $T$
> - Heat exchangers: fluidized bed with immersed surfaces
> - Packed-bed reactors: fixed bed, lower $h$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interpretation of Heat Transfer Coefficients
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interpretation of Heat Transfer Coefficients

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

In a **fluidized bed**, heat is exchanged between three distinct phases: the **gaseous (continuous) phase**, the **particulate (dispersed) phase**, and the **vessel/interchange surfaces** (immersed coils, vertical/horizontal tubes, and outer walls). The "Interpretation of Heat Transfer Coefficients" refers to the physical meaning, magnitude, and dominant transport mechanism of the three principal convective coefficients that govern these exchanges.

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Transfer Coefficients in Fluidized Beds** are the proportionality constants that relate the local heat flux (W/m²) to the temperature driving force (K) between the gas–particle, gas–wall, and bed–surface interfaces. They are evaluated using dimensionless correlations of the form **Nu = f(Re, Pr, Ar, u/u_mf)**.

The three fundamental coefficients are:

1. **Gas-to-Particle Heat Transfer Coefficient (h_gp):** Rate at which thermal energy moves from the interstitial gas to the individual solid particles (and vice-versa). It is **intraparticle conduction + boundary-layer convection** around a single sphere.
2. **Bed-to-Immersed-Surface Heat Transfer Coefficient (h_s):** Rate at which heat is delivered from the fluidized mass (gas + particles) to an immersed object (cooling/heating coil, thermowell, or internal tube bundle).
3. **Gas-to-Wall Heat Transfer Coefficient (h_gw):** Rate of heat transfer from the gas phase alone (since particles rarely touch the wall in lean-phase transport) to the confining vessel wall.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Boiling Pot of Spheres"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine heating a pot of small marbles by blowing hot air from below. The marbles, suspended in the air, heat up almost instantly because every marble is bathed in hot gas (h_gp is huge). If you dip a cold spoon into the pot, the marbles bombard the spoon thousands of times per second, smashing against it and continuously replacing the slow boundary layer of cool air — that is why h_s is the **largest** of the three. Finally, the pot's outer wall sees mostly the rising hot air with only the rare bounce of a marble (h_gw is the **smallest**).

### 1.3 Typical Order of Magnitude (Standard Metrics)

| Coefficient | Order of Magnitude (W/m²·K) | Engineering Significance |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **h_gp** | **200 – 1 000** | Governs reactions limited by external particle heat-up |
| **h_s**  | **100 – 600** | **Design coefficient for immersed heat exchangers** |
| **h_gw** | **30 – 150**  | Governs vessel shell design and freeboard cooling |

These coefficients are conventionally **non-dimensionalised** as **Nusselt numbers** for correlation purposes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Mechanisms Behind Each Coefficient

#### (a) Gas-to-Particle (h_gp) — "Conduction + Forced Convection Around a Sphere"
- **Why it is high:** Particles are small (50 – 500 µm), so surface area per unit volume is enormous. The boundary-layer thickness around each particle is minuscule.
- **Mechanism:** Two parallel modes:
  1. **Molecular conduction** through the stagnant gas film (dominates at very low Re_p).
  2. **Forced convection** due to the slip velocity of gas past the particle (dominates at operating Re_p).
- **Interpretation of Nu = 2 (asymptote):** When Re_p → 0, the Nusselt number for a sphere in stagnant fluid converges to **Nu → 2**, representing pure conduction through an infinitely thin spherical shell — a direct consequence of the analytical solution of the Laplace equation in spherical coordinates.

#### (b) Bed-to-Surface (h_s) — "Particle Bombardment Model"
- **Why it is the highest:** Continuous bombardment of the heat-transfer surface by hot particles from the emulsion phase **sweeps away the thermal boundary layer**, preventing the build-up of insulating gas films.
- **Mechanism (in order of contribution):**
  1. **Particle convection** (dominant) — fast-moving solids carry their enthalpy to the surface.
  2. **Gas convection** — continuous phase contribution.
  3. **Radiation** — significant above ≈ 600 °C.

#### (c) Gas-to-Wall (h_gw) — "Lean-Phase Gas Convection"
- **Why it is the lowest:** The wall is mostly exposed to the lean, low-density gas phase. Only a small fraction of the cross-section contains particles; the rest is bubbles (which act as gas "short-circuits").
- **Mechanism:** Predominantly **forced gas convection**, with marginal contribution from the rare particle-wall collisions in the wall layer.

### 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Dominant Coefficient | Design Implication |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Catalytic cracking (FCC riser) | **h_gp** | Reactor sizing, catalyst deactivation by coking |
| Fluidized bed combustor (FBC) | **h_s** | Sizing of in-bed evaporator tubes |
| Coal gasifier membrane wall | **h_gw** | Refractory / membrane tube metallurgy |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All correlations are evaluated using SI units. Use absolute values with `\vert` notation in handwritten work.**

| # | Coefficient | Correlation (Ranz-Marshall / Wender-Cooper / Kunii-Levenspiel) | Validity Range |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **h_gp** (Ranz–Marshall) | $\dfrac{h_{gp} \, d_p}{k_g} = 2 + 0.6 \, Re_p^{0.5} \, Pr^{0.33}$ | $Re_p \lt 200$ |
| 2 | **h_s** (Wender & Cooper) | $\dfrac{h_s \, d_p}{k_g} (1-\varepsilon) = (1-\varepsilon) \left[ \dfrac{C_{pg} \, \rho_s \, \sqrt{g \, d_p^3}}{k_g} \right]^{0.43} \left[ \dfrac{\rho_s \, C_{ps}}{\rho_g \, C_{pg}} \right]^{0.25} \left( \dfrac{u_0}{u_{mf}} \right)^{0.25}$ | Geldart A & B |
| 3 | **h_gw** (Kunii & Levenspiel) | $\dfrac{h_{gw} \, d_p}{k_g} = 0.0028 \left( \dfrac{C_{pg} \, \rho_g}{k_g} \right)^{0.6} \left[ \dfrac{d_p^3 \, g \, (\rho_s - \rho_g)}{\rho_g \, \nu_g^2} \right]^{0.71} \left( \dfrac{u_0}{u_{mf}} \right)^{0.29}$ | $10^2 \lt Re_{mf} \lt 10^4$ |
| 4 | **Archimedes Number** | $Ar = \dfrac{d_p^3 \, \rho_g \, (\rho_s - \rho_g) \, g}{\mu_g^2}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | **Particle Reynolds** | $Re_p = \dfrac{d_p \, u_{slip} \, \rho_g}{\mu_g}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | **Prandtl Number** | $Pr = \dfrac{C_{pg} \, \mu_g}{k_g}$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | **Inter-phase Energy Balance** | $\rho_s \, C_{ps} \, \dfrac{dT_p}{dt} = h_{gp} \, a_p \, (T_g - T_p)$ | Unsteady particle heating |

**Where:**
- $d_p$ = particle diameter (m)   |   $k_g$ = gas thermal conductivity (W/m·K)
- $\varepsilon$ = bed voidage   |   $u_0$ = superficial gas velocity (m/s)
- $u_{mf}$ = minimum fluidization velocity (m/s)   |   $\rho_s, \rho_g$ = densities (kg/m³)
- $C_{pg}, C_{ps}$ = specific heats (J/kg·K)   |   $\mu_g$ = gas viscosity (Pa·s)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Asymptotic Limit Nu → 2 for a Sphere in Stagnant Fluid

We begin from **Fourier's law of conduction** applied to a thin spherical shell of gas surrounding a particle of diameter $d_p$.

**Step 1 — Energy flux through a spherical shell at radius r:**
By conservation of energy, the radial heat flux $q$ is constant through any shell:
$$q = -k_g \left( 4 \pi r^2 \right) \dfrac{dT}{dr}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate across the shell from $r = d_p/2$ to $r = \infty$:**
$$\int_{T_p}^{T_\infty} dT = -\dfrac{q}{4 \pi k_g} \int_{d_p/2}^{\infty} \dfrac{dr}{r^2}$$

Performing the integration gives:
$$T_\infty - T_p = \dfrac{q}{4 \pi k_g} \cdot \dfrac{2}{d_p}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the heat transfer coefficient:**
By definition, $q = h_{gp} \cdot \pi d_p^2 (T_\infty - T_p)$. Equating the two expressions for $q$ and cancelling the temperature difference:
$$h_{gp} = \dfrac{2 \, k_g}{d_p}$$

**Step 4 — Form the Nusselt number:**
$$\boxed{Nu_p = \dfrac{h_{gp} \, d_p}{k_g} = 2}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The constant "**2**" in the Ranz–Marshall correlation is *not* a fitted parameter — it is the **analytical conduction limit** of a sphere in a stagnant, infinite medium. It is the **minimum theoretical value** of Nu_p, valid when the gas approaches the particle with negligible velocity (creeping flow).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Energy Balance for a Single Particle (Basis of h_gp Measurement)

For a single spherical particle suspended in a gas of temperature $T_g$, the unsteady energy balance is:

**Step 1 — Accumulation = Convective inflow:**
$$\rho_s \, C_{ps} \, V_p \, \dfrac{dT_p}{dt} = h_{gp} \, A_p \, (T_g - T_p)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $V_p = \pi d_p^3 / 6$ and $A_p = \pi d_p^2$:**
$$\rho_s \, C_{ps} \left( \dfrac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \right) \dfrac{dT_p}{dt} = h_{gp} \, (\pi d_p^2) \, (T_g - T_p)$$

**Step 3 — Simplify and separate variables:**
$$\dfrac{dT_p}{T_g - T_p} = \dfrac{6 \, h_{gp}}{\rho_s \, C_{ps} \, d_p} \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from $T_{p,0}$ to $T_p(t)$:**
$$\ln \left( \dfrac{T_g - T_p}{T_g - T_{p,0}} \right) = -\dfrac{6 \, h_{gp}}{\rho_s \, C_{ps} \, d_p} \, t$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $h_{gp}$ in terms of measurable quantities:**
$$\boxed{h_{gp} = -\dfrac{\rho_s \, C_{ps} \, d_p}{6 \, t} \, \ln \left( \dfrac{T_g - T_p}{T_g - T_{p,0}} \right)}$$

**Interpretation:** The logarithmic "**6**" arises from the surface-to-volume ratio of a sphere ($A/V = 6/d_p$). Larger $h_{gp}$ values make the particle heat up faster (steeper log-decay). This expression is used in **differential bed heat-transfer experiments** to back-calculate $h_{gp}$.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of the Three Coefficients

| Property | **h_gp** | **h_s** | **h_gw** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Defining interface | Gas ↔ Particle | Bed (gas + particles) ↔ Immersed surface | Gas ↔ Vessel wall |
| Dominant mechanism | Conduction + Boundary-layer convection | Particle bombardment | Forced gas convection |
| Typical Nu (dimensionless) | **2 – 8** | **1 – 30** | **0.5 – 5** |
| Particle size effect | $Nu \propto d_p^{-0.5}$ (mild) | $Nu \propto d_p^{-0.5}$ | $Nu \propto d_p^{+0.71}$ (strong) |
| Velocity effect | $Nu \propto Re^{0.5}$ | $Nu \propto (u_0/u_{mf})^{0.25}$ | $Nu \propto (u_0/u_{mf})^{0.29}$ |
| Effect of gas thermal conductivity | $h \propto k_g$ (linear) | $h \propto k_g^{0.6}$ | $h \propto k_g^{0.4}$ |
| Where it is the design bottleneck | Catalyst regeneration | **FBC, dryer coil design** | Refractory / membrane wall |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Heat Transfer Pathways in a Fluidized Bed

The following **Block-Level Functional Architecture** maps the three heat-transfer pathways in a single fluidized-bed column. It captures the direction of energy flux, the dominant mode, and the symbols of the coefficients, replacing a physical cut-away drawing with a process-flow-style schematic suitable for board-examination reproduction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GASIN["GAS INLET HOT Tg"]:::gas
    PHEAT["PARTICLE HEAT UP ZONE"]:::particle
    SURR["EMULSION PHASE GAS + SOLIDS"]:::surr
    BED["IMMERSED SURFACE TUBE COIL"]:::bed
    WALL["VESSEL OUTER WALL"]:::wall
    HGP["hgp GAS TO PARTICLE W m2K"]:::coeff
    HS["hs BED TO SURFACE W m2K"]:::coeff
    HGW["hgw GAS TO WALL W m2K"]:::coeff
    GASOUT["EXIT GAS STACK"]:::gas

    GASIN --> HGP
    HGP --> PHEAT
    PHEAT --> SURR
    SURR --> HS
    HS --> BED
    SURR --> HGW
    HGW --> WALL
    GASIN --> HGW
    SURR --> GASOUT

    classDef gas fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef particle fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef surr fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef bed fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef wall fill:#D8BFD8,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef coeff fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Causal Hierarchy of the Three Coefficients

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["OPERATING PARAMETERS ug dp rho rho g Cp mu kg"]:::in
    B["DIMENSIONLESS GROUPS Re Pr Ar"]:::calc
    C["CORRELATIONS RANZ MARSHALL WENDER COOPER KUNII LEVENSPIEL"]:::corr
    D["HEAT TRANSFER COEFFICIENTS hgp hs hgw"]:::out
    E["DESIGN OUTPUT REACTOR TUBE AREA SHELL TEMPERATURE"]:::design

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E

    classDef in fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000000
    classDef calc fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,color:#000000
    classDef corr fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000000
    classDef out fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
    classDef design fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#A0522D,color:#000000
```

### 4.3 Temperature-Profile Schematic (Vertical Cross-Section)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TP["TEMPERATURE PROFILE ALONG BED HEIGHT"]
        direction TB
        L1["Tg INLET HIGH"]:::tgas
        L2["Tp PARTICLE APPROACHES Tg WITHIN mm"]:::tpart
        L3["Ts SURFACE TEMPERATURE COOLER THAN BED"]:::tsurf
        L4["Tw WALL TEMPERATURE LOWEST"]:::twall
        L5["Tg EXIT"]:::tgas
    end

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5

    classDef tgas fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef tpart fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000000
    classDef tsurf fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
    classDef twall fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000000
```

**Reading the diagram:** The temperature decreases from gas to particle (driven by h_gp), from emulsion to immersed surface (driven by h_s), and from gas to wall (driven by h_gw). The narrowest gap is between gas and particle — proof that **h_gp is the largest**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
**Define the gas-to-particle heat transfer coefficient (h_gp). What does the asymptotic value Nu_p = 2 physically represent?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Definition (2 marks):** The gas-to-particle heat transfer coefficient h_gp is the convective transport coefficient that quantifies the rate of heat exchange between the interstitial fluidising gas and the surface of an individual solid particle. It is defined by Newton's law of cooling applied at the particle surface: $q'' = h_{gp} (T_g - T_p)$, where $T_g$ is the bulk gas temperature and $T_p$ is the particle surface temperature.
> 2. **Interpretation of Nu = 2 (1 mark):** The asymptotic value of Nu_p = 2 represents **pure molecular conduction** through a stagnant, infinite gas medium surrounding a single sphere. It is obtained analytically by solving Fourier's equation in spherical coordinates and is the **minimum theoretical Nusselt number**, valid when the relative velocity of the gas past the particle approaches zero (creeping-flow limit).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**State the three principal heat transfer coefficients encountered in a fluidized bed and rank them in order of typical magnitude, with one reason each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **h_s (bed-to-surface)** — typically the **largest** (100–600 W/m²·K) because the immersed surface is continuously bombarded by hot emulsion-phase particles that wipe out the thermal boundary layer.
> 2. **h_gp (gas-to-particle)** — **intermediate** (200–1 000 W/m²·K at the microscale) because the gas boundary layer around a fine particle is extremely thin and particles are small in diameter.
> 3. **h_gw (gas-to-wall)** — typically the **smallest** (30–150 W/m²·K) because the wall is mostly swept by the lean gas phase, with only infrequent particle impacts.
> *(Note: even though h_gp and h_s are of comparable order, h_s is the controlling coefficient in almost all in-bed heat-exchanger designs.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a) Derive the Ranz-Marshall correlation for the gas-to-particle heat transfer coefficient. Interpret each term physically. (7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Step 1 (1 mark) — Statement of the correlation:**
> $$\dfrac{h_{gp} \, d_p}{k_g} = 2 + 0.6 \, Re_p^{0.5} \, Pr^{0.33}$$
>
> **Step 2 (2 marks) — Derivation of the conduction limit (Nu = 2):**
> Apply Fourier's law through a spherical shell, integrate from $r = d_p/2$ to $r = \infty$, and equate to the convective definition (as shown in §3.1 above). The result is the **asymptotic conduction limit** $Nu = 2$, which is the heat transfer under **zero relative velocity** between the gas and particle.
>
> **Step 3 (2 marks) — Dimensional-analysis origin of the convective term:**
> Buckingham-π analysis for forced convection past a sphere yields the functional form $Nu = f(Re, Pr)$. The exponent 0.5 on Re is the **laminar-boundary-layer exponent** for flow past a flat plate adapted to a sphere (analogous to Pohlhausen's exact solution). The exponent 0.33 on Pr reflects the standard **thermal boundary-layer scaling** for laminar flow.
>
> **Step 4 (2 marks) — Term-by-term physical interpretation:**
> - The **"2"** represents pure conduction in the absence of flow.
> - The **"0.6"** is a fitted coefficient for moderate Re_p (0–200).
> - **Re_p^0.5** captures the **convective enhancement** of heat transfer as the slip velocity between gas and particle increases.
> - **Pr^0.33** accounts for the **ratio of momentum to thermal diffusivity** in the boundary layer.

**(b) A fluidized bed of sand particles (d_p = 200 µm, ρ_s = 2 500 kg/m³, C_ps = 800 J/kg·K) is fluidised with air at 400 °C. The superficial velocity is u_0 = 0.3 m/s and the minimum fluidisation velocity is u_mf = 0.02 m/s. Calculate the gas-to-particle heat transfer coefficient using the Ranz–Marshall correlation. Given air properties at 400 °C: ρ_g = 0.524 kg/m³, μ_g = 3.1 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s, k_g = 0.0436 W/m·K, C_pg = 1 040 J/kg·K. (7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Step 1 (1 mark) — Calculate slip velocity (= u_0 − u_mf for dilute limit, or use u_0 in Ranz–Marshall for suspended particle):**
> $$u_{slip} \approx u_0 = 0.3 \text{ m/s}$$
>
> **Step 2 (1 mark) — Particle Reynolds number:**
> $$Re_p = \dfrac{d_p \, u_{slip} \, \rho_g}{\mu_g} = \dfrac{(2 \times 10^{-4})(0.3)(0.524)}{3.1 \times 10^{-5}}$$
> $$Re_p = \dfrac{3.144 \times 10^{-5}}{3.1 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 1.014$$
>
> **Step 3 (1 mark) — Prandtl number:**
> $$Pr = \dfrac{C_{pg} \, \mu_g}{k_g} = \dfrac{(1\,040)(3.1 \times 10^{-5})}{0.0436} = \dfrac{0.03224}{0.0436} \approx 0.74$$
>
> **Step 4 (2 marks) — Apply the Ranz–Marshall correlation:**
> $$Nu_p = 2 + 0.6 \, (Re_p)^{0.5} \, (Pr)^{0.33}$$
> $$Nu_p = 2 + 0.6 \times (1.014)^{0.5} \times (0.74)^{0.33}$$
> $$Nu_p = 2 + 0.6 \times 1.007 \times 0.906 = 2 + 0.5475 = 2.547$$
>
> **Step 5 (1 mark) — Calculate h_gp:**
> $$h_{gp} = \dfrac{Nu_p \, k_g}{d_p} = \dfrac{2.547 \times 0.0436}{2 \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{0.1110}{2 \times 10^{-4}}$$
> $$\boxed{h_{gp} \approx 555 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}}$$
>
> **Step 6 (1 mark) — Interpretation:** The convective term contributes only **~27 %** of the total Nu_p (2 → 2.55). At this Re_p, the particle is still in the **creeping-flow regime**; conduction dominates. To enhance h_gp, **larger particles at higher velocities** would be required.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a) Discuss, with a neat sketch, the physical mechanisms contributing to the bed-to-surface heat transfer coefficient (h_s) in a fluidized bed. (7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Step 1 (1 mark) — Definition:** h_s is the convective coefficient for heat transfer between the fluidized mass (gas + particles) and an immersed heat-transfer surface.
>
> **Step 2 (2 marks) — Three contributing mechanisms:**
> 1. **Particle convection (dominant, ~ 60 – 80 %):** Hot particles from the emulsion phase continuously strike the surface, deposit their enthalpy, and are replaced by cooler particles from the bulk. This **bombardment** repeatedly thins the thermal boundary layer.
> 2. **Gas convection (~ 15 – 30 %):** The interstitial gas, driven by bubbles, sweeps the surface. This contribution is similar to single-phase forced convection but enhanced by bubble-induced turbulence.
> 3. **Radiation (~ 5 – 10 % at T > 600 °C, otherwise negligible):** Significant in high-temperature combustors and dryers.
>
> **Step 3 (2 marks) — Sketch description (no need to draw — verbal description credited):** A vertical cylinder with gas inlet at the bottom, immersed horizontal tube in the centre, arrows showing particle streams hitting the tube, and a thin boundary layer marked on the tube.
>
> **Step 4 (2 marks) — Quantitative effect of operating variables (Wender–Cooper):**
> - $h_s \propto k_g^{0.6}$ — small effect of gas conductivity.
> - $h_s \propto d_p^{-0.5}$ — finer particles give **higher** h_s.
> - $h_s \propto (u_0/u_{mf})^{0.25}$ — modest effect of gas velocity.
> - **Maximum h_s** is observed at $u_0/u_{mf} \approx 1.5 - 3$; further increase in velocity causes **bubble by-passing** and reduces h_s.

**(b) Compare and interpret the three heat transfer coefficients (h_gp, h_s, h_gw) in a typical fluidized bed, with reference to the physical location and dominant mechanism. (7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Step 1 (2 marks) — Tabulated comparison:**
>
> | Property | h_gp | h_s | h_gw |
> |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
> | Location | Around each particle | At immersed surface | At outer wall |
> | Mechanism | Conduction + boundary-layer convection | Particle bombardment | Gas convection |
> | Magnitude (W/m²·K) | 200 – 1 000 | 100 – 600 | 30 – 150 |
> | Key dependence | $d_p, u_{slip}$ | $d_p, u_0/u_{mf}$ | $u_0, k_g$ |
>
> **Step 2 (2 marks) — Interpretation of h_gp:** h_gp is governed by the **single-particle Nusselt number**. It is the rate-limiting step for **catalyst regeneration** and **fast pyrolysis** of biomass where the particle must reach the reaction temperature in milliseconds.
>
> **Step 3 (2 marks) — Interpretation of h_s:** h_s is the **design coefficient** for in-bed heat exchangers (FBC evaporator tubes, dryer coils). Its unusually high value (relative to packed beds of the same particles) is the **key advantage** of fluidization for thermal processing.
>
> **Step 4 (1 mark) — Interpretation of h_gw:** h_gw governs **shell-side heat loss** and the design of **water-wall membrane tubes** in atmospheric FBCs. Its lower magnitude is offset by the larger surface area of the vessel.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Using "$\vert x \vert$" (vertical bar) inside LaTeX-rendered tables** → breaks the markdown table and costs a mark on neatness. Always write `\vert x \vert` or `\lvert x \rvert`.
> 2. **Forgetting to use $u_0/u_{mf}$ ratio** in Wender–Cooper and Kunii–Levenspiel correlations — KTU examiners will specifically look for the dimensionless group.
