> 3. **Interchanging "u_mf" with "u_0"** in the slip-velocity calculation. For Ranz–Marshall, use the **slip velocity** at the particle, not the superficial velocity. (For dilute phase it is acceptable to use u_0 as an approximation.)
> 4. **Writing "2 + 0.6 Re^0.5 Pr^0.33" without specifying "Ranz–Marshall"** by name. Always **name the correlation** explicitly — KTU mark scheme deducts 1 mark otherwise.
> 5. **Forgetting units** in the final answer of h_gp / h_s / h_gw. Always state **W/m²·K**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for the 14-Mark Module-4 Question**

- ☐ Three coefficients: **h_gp** (gas→particle), **h_s** (bed→immersed surface), **h_gw** (gas→wall).
- ☐ Order of magnitude (largest → smallest): **h_s ≈ h_gp > h_gw**.
- ☐ **Ranz–Marshall** is for **h_gp**: $Nu = 2 + 0.6 Re^{0.5} Pr^{0.33}$, valid for $Re_p \lt 200$.
- ☐ The constant **"2"** in Ranz–Marshall is **not empirical** — it is the **analytical conduction limit** of a sphere.
- ☐ **Wender–Cooper** is for **h_s**; **Kunii–Levenspiel** is for **h_gw** — memorise the dependence on $k_g$, $d_p$, and $u_0/u_{mf}$.
- ☐ The **6/d_p** factor comes from the sphere's **surface-to-volume ratio** — appears in all single-particle unsteady energy balances.
- ☐ **Particle bombardment** is the reason **h_s is so high**; **lean gas sweep** is the reason **h_gw is so low**.
- ☐ **h_gp** matters most in **catalytic reactors with rapid reactions**; **h_s** matters in **FBCs and dryers**; **h_gw** matters in **membrane-wall vessels**.
- ☐ Radiation is **negligible below 600 °C**; mention it only when the bed is high-temperature.
- ☐ For numerical problems, **always check units** of $d_p$ (µm → m), $\mu_g$ (cP → Pa·s) before plugging in.
- ☐ **Kunii–Levenspiel h_gw** correlation uses the **Archimedes number** ($Ar$) — do not confuse it with Re.
- ☐ **Typical exam traps:** wrong $u_0/u_{mf}$ ratio, missing "2" in Ranz–Marshall, using $u_0$ instead of $u_{slip}$.
- ☐ **Interpretation questions** (4–5 marks): Always conclude with the *engineering consequence* (e.g., "this explains why fluidized beds are preferred over packed beds for highly exothermic reactions").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Application of Fluidized Beds: Physical Operations: Heat Exchange
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Industrial Application of Fluidized Beds: Physical Operations: Heat Exchange

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fluidized Bed Heat Exchanger (FBHE)** is a contacting device in which a solid granular material is suspended in an upward-flowing gas (or liquid) stream such that the bed behaves as a fluid, and thermal energy is transferred between the fluidized solids and either an **immersed heat transfer surface** (internal/exchange surface located within the bed) or an **external circulating stream of solids** that transports heat into or out of the fluidized reactor.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 4 – Industrial Applications), heat exchange is classified as a **Physical Operation** because the principal goal is controlled transfer of sensible heat, without accompanying reaction (or with the reaction being secondary).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight – PECHT415 / Module 4**
> Heat exchange in fluidized beds is the **highest-coefficient solid-fluid contacting operation** in the chemical engineer’s toolbox. The fluidized state gives a heat transfer coefficient typically in the range of **200 – 1000 W/m²·K**, which is **4 to 20 times higher** than a comparable packed bed.

### Modes of Heat Exchange in Fluidized Beds

There are **two principal industrial configurations**:

1. **Internal Heat Exchange** – Heat transfer surfaces (tube bundles, coils, vertical/horizontal tubes, plate coils, bayonet tubes) are immersed directly inside the fluidized bed.
2. **External Heat Exchange** – A portion of the hot/cool solids is continuously withdrawn, passed through an external heat exchanger (e.g., a shell-and-tube unit), and returned to the bed. The solids act as the heat carrier.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The “Boiling Sand-Pot”

Imagine a pot of fine sand on a gas stove. As the gas velocity through the sand is increased:
- At low velocity, the sand is still and acts like a *packed bed* — heat moves only by slow conduction, and the bottom is much hotter than the top.
- At a critical velocity (**Umf**, the *minimum fluidization velocity*), the sand particles start to levitate, bubble, and behave like a *boiling liquid*. Suddenly, the temperature becomes uniform throughout the pot because hot particles are continuously carried up and cold particles tumble down.

This “boiling” action is exactly what happens in a **fluidized bed heat exchanger**. The gas bubbles act as a conveyor belt that sweeps hot particles into contact with every cooling/heating surface. This is why fluidized beds are often called **“isothermal reactors”** — the violent particle motion maintains temperature uniformity within a few degrees, even when the reaction is highly exothermic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Temperature Zones Inside a Fluidized Bed Heat Exchanger**
> 1. **Dense Phase (Emulsion) Zone** – below the surface; vigorous bubbling; **highest** heat transfer coefficient.
> 2. **Transition Zone** – just above the surface; splash and burst of bubbles; intermediate coefficient.
> 3. **Freeboard (Disengagement) Zone** – above the surface; dilute phase; lowest coefficient.

The dominant industrial design focuses on the **dense phase** region, because more than **80% of the total heat transfer** occurs here.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Fluidized Beds Give Such High Heat Transfer Coefficients

The coefficient is high because **four heat transfer mechanisms act in parallel**, and the fluidized state dramatically enhances two of them:

| Mechanism | Description | Contribution in Fluidized Bed |
|---|---|---|
| **Gas Conduction / Convection** | Heat carried by the interstitial gas film. | Low (gas films are poor conductors) |
| **Particle Convection** | Hot solid particles physically sweep across the surface, dumping heat by direct contact. | **Dominant (60–80%)** |
| **Gas Convection Due to Bubbles** | Bubbles stir the bed, refreshing the gas at the surface. | Significant (10–20%) |
| **Radiation** | Important only above 600–700 °C. | Small at industrial FBHE temperatures |

> [!NOTE]
> **Particle Convection (Surface Renewal Theory — Mickley & Fairbanks, 1955):**
> Tiny packets ("packets") of emulsion-phase gas + solids alternately contact the heat-transfer surface, absorb heat, get swept away, and are replaced by fresh packets. The time a packet spends in contact is *τ*. This *unsteady-state* contact gives very high transient heat flux — much higher than steady conduction would predict.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Design

1. **Step 1 — Estimate the Heat Duty (Q):** From the process energy balance, $Q = \dot{m} \, C_p \, \Delta T$.
2. **Step 2 — Select a Correlation for h:** Choose correlation based on tube orientation and operating regime $(U/U_{mf})$.
3. **Step 3 — Compute the Required Area (A):** $A = Q / (h \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD})$.
4. **Step 4 — Decide Tube Geometry:** Horizontal tube bundles are most common (large heat transfer area, easy maintenance); vertical tubes preferred for very exothermic reactions (better temperature uniformity).
5. **Step 5 — External Heat Exchanger (if applicable):** Compute the solids circulation rate $\dot{m}_s$ needed to carry the required heat to/from the bed: $Q = \dot{m}_s \cdot C_{ps} \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})$.

## 2.3 Key Factors Affecting the Heat Transfer Coefficient

| Factor | Effect on h | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| **Increasing (U − Umf)** | ↑ Increases up to ~1.5 Umf, then plateaus / decreases | More particles swept over surface, but excess gas creates gas film resistance |
| **Increasing particle size (dp)** | ↑ Increases | Larger particles carry more heat per visit |
| **Increasing ρs (dense particles)** | ↑ Increases | More thermal mass per particle |
| **Vertical vs Horizontal tubes** | Vertical ≈ 80–90% of horizontal at same conditions | Gravity assists particle drainage on horizontal tubes |
| **Tube pitch / bundle geometry** | Staggered > in-line | Staggered tubes disrupt bubbles, more contact |
| **Tube diameter (dt)** | Slight ↓ with ↑ dt | Bigger tube = less curvature effect on particles |
| **Pressure** | ↑ Increases | Higher gas density → better gas convective term |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All critical formulas you must memorize for KTU ESE in this topic**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Valid Range / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Minimum Fluidization Velocity (Wen & Yu)** | $Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^2 + 0.0408 \, Ar} - 33.7$ | $Ar = \dfrac{d_p^3 \, \rho_g \, (\rho_s - \rho_g) \, g}{\mu^2}$ |
| 2 | **Wender & Cooper (1958) — Horizontal Tubes** | $h = 11 \, d_p^{0.301} \, \rho_s^{0.654} \, k_g^{0.514} \, (U - U_{mf})^{0.718} \, \mu^{-0.486}$ | $\dfrac{U - U_{mf}}{U_{mf}} \ge 0.5$ ; English units in original |
| 3 | **Wender & Cooper (SI form)** | $\dfrac{h \, d_p}{k_g} = 0.85 \left( \dfrac{d_p (U - U_{mf}) \rho_g}{\mu} \right)^{0.5} \left( \dfrac{\rho_s}{\rho_g} \right)^{0.13} \left( \dfrac{C_{pg} \, \mu}{k_g} \right)^{0.43}$ | Horizontal tube bundles; SI form |
| 4 | **Mickley & Fairbanks (1955) — Vertical Tubes** | $h \cdot \tau^{0.5} = 1.13 \left[ k_{er} \, \rho_{er} \, C_{per} \right]^{0.5}$ | Surface-renewal / packet theory |
| 5 | **Vreedenberg (1958) — Vertical Tubes** | $Nu = 0.85 \, Ar^{0.18} \left( \dfrac{\rho_s}{\rho_g} \right)^{0.13} \left( \dfrac{\mu}{\mu_w} \right)^{0.3} \left( \dfrac{d_t}{D} \right)^{0.2}$ | Water-like / high-density systems |
| 6 | **Vreedenberg (1958) — Horizontal Tubes** | $Nu = 0.71 \, Ar^{0.18} \left( \dfrac{\rho_s}{\rho_g} \right)^{0.13} \left( \dfrac{\mu}{\mu_w} \right)^{0.3} \left( \dfrac{d_t}{D} \right)^{0.2}$ | Lower coefficient vs vertical |
| 7 | **Overall Heat Transfer (External Solids Loop)** | $Q = \dot{m}_s \, C_{ps} \, (T_{bed} - T_{in})$ | $C_{ps} \approx 0.8 - 1.2 \; kJ/kg\cdot K$ for typical FB solids |
| 8 | **LMTD** | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Counter-current preferred for FBHE |
| 9 | **Required Heat Transfer Area** | $A = \dfrac{Q}{h \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}}$ | Tube area, $m^2$ |
| 10 | **Typical h range** | $h \approx 200 - 1000 \; W/m^2 \cdot K$ | Compare: packed bed h ≈ 50 – 150 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**
> FBHEs are the *workhorse* heat-transfer device in **Fischer-Tropsch synthesis, catalytic cracking (FCC regenerator), coal combustion, waste heat recovery, aluminum smelter anode baking, and pharmaceutical drying**. Their high h-value allows compact equipment — a 1 m² fluidized surface can replace 5–10 m² of shell-and-tube area.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Packet-Theory Heat Transfer Coefficient (Mickley & Fairbanks, 1955)

This is the **most cited derivation in KTU fluidization** because it is rigorous yet elegant.

**Physical Setup:** Consider a single packet of emulsion phase (gas + solids) that contacts the heat-transfer surface for a short time $\tau$. During this time, heat penetrates the packet by **unsteady-state conduction** (semi-infinite solid assumption).

**Step 1 — Unsteady-state conduction into a semi-infinite medium:**

Fourier's second law in one dimension, with constant properties:

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \cdot \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$

**Step 2 — Boundary and initial conditions:**

At $t = 0$: $T(x, 0) = T_{bed}$ (uniform bed temperature).

At $x = 0$ (the wall): $T(0, t) = T_{wall}$ (constant wall temperature).

For $x \to \infty$: $T(\infty, t) = T_{bed}$.

**Step 3 — Apply the standard solution of Carslaw & Jaeger (semi-infinite, constant surface temperature):**

$$\frac{T(x,t) - T_{wall}}{T_{bed} - T_{wall}} = \mathrm{erf}\!\left( \frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Instantaneous local heat flux at the surface (x = 0):**

From Fourier's law, $q(t) = -k_{er} \left. \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x} \right|_{x=0}$. Differentiating the error-function solution:

$$q(t) = \frac{k_{er}\,(T_{wall} - T_{bed})}{\sqrt{\pi \, \alpha t}}$$

**Step 5 — Average heat flux over the contact time τ:**

$$\bar{q} = \frac{1}{\tau} \int_{0}^{\tau} q(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{\tau} \int_{0}^{\tau} \frac{k_{er}\,(T_{wall} - T_{bed})}{\sqrt{\pi \, \alpha t}} \, dt$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the integral (using $\int_0^{\tau} t^{-1/2} dt = 2\sqrt{\tau}$):**

$$\bar{q} = \frac{k_{er}\,(T_{wall} - T_{bed})}{\sqrt{\pi \, \alpha}} \cdot \frac{2\sqrt{\tau}}{\tau} = \frac{2 \, k_{er}\,(T_{wall} - T_{bed})}{\sqrt{\pi \, \alpha \, \tau}}$$

**Step 7 — Define the mean heat transfer coefficient, $h \equiv \bar{q}/(T_{wall} - T_{bed})$:**

$$h = \frac{2 \, k_{er}}{\sqrt{\pi \, \alpha \, \tau}}$$

**Step 8 — Replace thermal diffusivity by $\alpha_{er} = k_{er} / (\rho_{er} \, C_{per})$:**

$$h = \frac{2 \, k_{er}}{\sqrt{\pi \, \tau \cdot k_{er}/(\rho_{er} \, C_{per})}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \sqrt{\frac{k_{er} \, \rho_{er} \, C_{per}}{\tau}}$$

**Step 9 — Final compact form (Mickley–Fairbanks equation):**

$$\boxed{\,h \cdot \sqrt{\tau} \;=\; 1.13 \, \sqrt{k_{er} \, \rho_{er} \, C_{per}}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion logic — why the constant becomes 1.13:**
> $\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} = 1.128 \approx 1.13$. This is the same numerical coincidence that appears in the Gaussian error function. The product $h \sqrt{\tau}$ is **constant for a given emulsion phase**; what changes with operating conditions is $\tau$ (residence time of the packet on the wall).

**Step 10 — Engineering use of the result:**

To compute the mean $h$ in a real bed, you need an estimate of $\tau$. The packet residence time is empirically correlated with the gas velocity excess:

$$\tau \;\approx\; \frac{d_p}{U - U_{mf}}$$

Substituting this $\tau$ back into the Mickley–Fairbanks expression gives a working equation of the form:

$$h \;\propto\; \sqrt{k_{er} \, \rho_{er} \, C_{per} \, \frac{(U - U_{mf})}{d_p}}$$

This is why the **Wender–Cooper** and **Mickley–Fairbanks** equations look so similar: they are the *same physics* (packet renewal) expressed in slightly different empirical forms.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example – KTU Board Pattern

**Problem:** A fluidized bed of sand (ρs = 2500 kg/m³, dp = 250 μm, Cp = 0.84 kJ/kg·K) is fluidized with air at 1.1 Umf. Use the Wender & Cooper correlation to estimate h. The horizontal tube outer diameter is 50 mm.

**Given:** Air at 400 °C: ρg = 0.524 kg/m³, μg = 3.0 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s, kg = 0.0406 W/m·K, Cpg = 1020 J/kg·K, Umf = 0.10 m/s, U = 1.1 × Umf = 0.11 m/s.

**Step 1 — Compute (U − Umf):**
$$U - U_{mf} = 0.11 - 0.10 = 0.01 \; m/s$$

**Step 2 — Compute the dimensionless groups:**

$$Re_{p,\,excess} = \frac{d_p (U - U_{mf}) \rho_g}{\mu_g} = \frac{(250 \times 10^{-6})(0.01)(0.524)}{3.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.0437$$

**Step 3 — Apply Wender & Cooper (SI form):**

$$Nu = 0.85 \cdot (Re_p)^{0.5} \left( \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_g} \right)^{0.13} \left( \frac{C_{pg} \, \mu_g}{k_g} \right)^{0.43}$$

**Step 4 — Plug in numerical values:**

$$(Re_p)^{0.5} = \sqrt{0.0437} = 0.209$$

$$\left( \frac{2500}{0.524} \right)^{0.13} = (4771)^{0.13} \approx 2.84$$

$$\left( \frac{1020 \times 3.0 \times 10^{-5}}{0.0406} \right)^{0.43} = (0.754)^{0.43} \approx 0.891$$

**Step 5 — Combine:**

$$Nu = 0.85 \times 0.209 \times 2.84 \times 0.891 = 0.449$$

**Step 6 — Solve for h:**

$$h = \frac{Nu \cdot k_g}{d_p} = \frac{0.449 \times 0.0406}{250 \times 10^{-6}} = 72.9 \; W/m^2 \cdot K$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment on the result:** This h is low because $U - U_{mf}$ is only 0.01 m/s (operating too close to Umf). In practice, fluidized bed heat exchangers are run with $U / U_{mf}$ between 1.5 and 3, which would push h well above 200 W/m²·K. **This is the single most important operating tip for KTU exam numericals: never operate a heat-exchanger fluidized bed right at Umf — design for at least $U = 1.5\,U_{mf}$.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Industrial Fluidized Bed Heat Exchanger (Internal + External Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Air Blower / Fan] --> B[Air Preheater]
    B --> C[Windbox with Distributor Plate]
    C --> D[Fluidized Bed with Immersed Tube Bundle]
    D --> E[Cyclone Separator]
    E --> F[Bag Filter]
    F --> G[Stack]
    D --> H[Hot Solids Withdrawal]
    H --> I[External Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger]
    I --> J[Cooled Solids Return]
    J --> D
    D --> K[Cooling Water In to Tube Bundle]
    K --> L[Hot Water Out]
    L --> M[External Process]
    style A fill:#fff2cc
    style D fill:#c9e7ff
    style I fill:#ffd2cc
    style M fill:#d4f0c5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagram (for the KTU examiner):**
> - The **Air Blower** drives fluidization air upward through a distributor.
> - The **Fluidized Bed** contains a horizontal tube bundle (cooling/heating medium inside tubes, solids outside).
> - **Solid arrows** show gas flow, **dashed/process flow** arrows show thermal energy flow.
> - The **External Solids Loop** is used when the heat duty is too high for the immersed surfaces alone — hot sand is bled off, cooled in an external shell-and-tube exchanger, and returned.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Three Heat-Transfer Zones Inside a Fluidized Bed

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1[Freeboard Zone]
        F1[Dilute phase - lowest h]
    end
    subgraph S2[Transition Splash Zone]
        T1[Intermediate h - bubble burst zone]
    end
    subgraph S3[Dense Phase Emulsion Zone]
        E1[Highest h - 80 percent of total transfer]
    end
    subgraph S4[Distributor and Windbox]
        W1[Gas inlet - Umf attained]
    end
    W1 --> E1
    E1 --> T1
    T1 --> F1
    style S3 fill:#c9e7ff
    style S2 fill:#fff2cc
    style S1 fill:#ffd2cc
    style S4 fill:#d4f0c5
```

## 4.3 Typical Immersed Heat Exchanger Tube-Bundle Layouts

There are **three common layouts** in industry. The KTU examiner frequently asks: *"Compare vertical vs horizontal tube bundles."* Use this table.

| Layout | Typical Use | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Horizontal tube bundle** | Coal combustors, dryers | Largest heat-transfer area per bed volume; staggered pitch gives best h; easy to inspect | Tube erosion by particles; tubes act as baffles, may dead-zone the bed |
| **Vertical tube bundle** | Highly exothermic reactors (e.g., Fischer-Tropsch) | Less erosion; better gas distribution; easier particle drainage | Smaller area per bed volume; harder to clean |
| **Bayonet / U-tubes** | High-temperature applications (e.g., aluminum anode baking) | Free thermal expansion; replaceable | Complex internal flow, lower overall U |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Where Students Lose Marks on This Topic**
> 1. **Confusing "Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient" (U) with "Bed-to-Surface Heat Transfer Coefficient" (h).** In KTU fluidization, the symbol **h** refers to the *bed-to-surface* coefficient (the fluid mechanics side). The symbol **U** is the *overall* coefficient (includes tube-side and wall fouling resistances). Do not interchange.
> 2. **Forgetting the gas properties must be at film temperature**, not at the bulk gas temperature. Most correlations were developed with gas properties at *Tf* = (Tbed + Twall)/2.
> 3. **Using the wrong correlation constant for tube orientation.** Vreedenberg gives **0.85 for vertical** and **0.71 for horizontal** tubes — mixing them up costs a full mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the "excess" in (U − Umf).** Always use the *excess* gas velocity, not the total U, in the Wender–Cooper and Mickley–Fairbanks correlations.
> 5. **Design ratio trap:** If a question gives U = 1.1 Umf, you are at the **worst** operating point for heat transfer. KTU examiners love to test whether you know this.

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define "Bed-to-Surface Heat Transfer Coefficient" in a fluidized bed. State its typical industrial range and explain why it is much higher than in a packed bed.**

**Model Answer:**

The **bed-to-surface heat transfer coefficient (h)** is defined as the proportionality constant between the local heat flux from the fluidized bed to an immersed surface and the temperature difference between the bed and the surface:

$$q = h \, (T_{bed} - T_{wall})$$

- **Typical industrial range:** $\mathbf{h \approx 200 - 1000 \; W/m^2 \cdot K}$ in a fluidized bed.
- **Packed bed range:** $\mathbf{h \approx 50 - 150 \; W/m^2 \cdot K}$.

**Why fluidized bed gives higher h:**
1. The bubbles stir the bed, continuously bringing fresh hot particles into contact with the surface (particle convection / packet renewal).
2. The gas film is constantly disrupted by bubble eruption, removing the insulating gas boundary layer.
3. The violent mixing makes the bed behave nearly isothermally, so the *driving force* (ΔT) is fully available at every point.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Distinguish between "Internal" and "External" heat exchange in fluidized beds. Give one industrial example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Internal Heat Exchange | External Heat Exchange |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Heat-transfer surface (tube/coil) is **immersed inside** the bed. | Heat-transfer surface is **outside** the bed; hot/cool solids circulate. |
| **Heat carrier** | Tube-side fluid (water, steam, molten salt). | The **solids themselves** act as the heat carrier. |
| **Example** | Steam-generating tube bundle in a **fluidized bed coal combustor**. | Hot sand circulation between **FCC regenerator and riser reactor**; or **sand heater in a CFB boiler**. |
| **Best for** | Moderate heat duty, single-vessel operation. | Very high heat duty, or when a chemical reaction should be separated from the heat-transfer area. |

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A – 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the construction of an **immersed-tube fluidized bed heat exchanger** using a horizontal tube bundle. Explain the role of the windbox and distributor in ensuring uniform heat transfer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Mickley & Fairbanks packet-renewal equation** $h\sqrt{\tau} = 1.13 \sqrt{k_{er}\rho_{er}C_{per}}$ from the unsteady-state heat conduction equation. State the assumptions.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – Construction (7 marks):**

A horizontal-tube FBHE consists of:
1. **Cylindrical shell** with a refractory or water-cooled outer wall.
2. **Windbox (plenum chamber)** below the distributor — distributes air evenly, prevents dead zones.
3. **Distributor plate** (perforated, porous, or nozzle type) — supports the bed at rest, provides pressure drop ≈ 10–30% of bed ΔP for uniform fluidization.
4. **Horizontal tube bundle** — arranged in **staggered** pitch; staggered geometry is preferred because it disrupts bubble coalescence and forces particles through the bundle.
5. **Freeboard region** above the bed — disengages and returns entrained fines; cyclone recovers and recycles them.
6. **Dust collection system** (cyclone + bag filter) for clean stack emissions.

**Role of windbox + distributor in heat transfer:**
- The **windbox** equalizes the static pressure across the entire distributor area.
- The **distributor** atomizes the air into thousands of small jets — each jet is the **nucleation site for a bubble**. A poorly designed distributor → non-uniform bubbles → uneven heat transfer → hot spots that can sinter the bed material.
- For uniform heat transfer, the distributor pressure drop should be $\Delta P_{dist} \ge 0.1 \, \Delta P_{bed}$ (a KTU-standard rule of thumb).

**Part (b) – Derivation (7 marks):**

[See Section 3.1 of these notes for the **full 10-step derivation**. The student must write all 10 steps for full 7 marks: governing PDE, B/IC, semi-infinite solution, instantaneous flux q(t), average q(τ), substitution of α, identification of h, and the final boxed equation.]

**Assumptions to state explicitly (each worth partial credit):**
1. Bed is a collection of emulsion "packets," each behaving as a homogeneous continuum with effective properties $k_{er}, \rho_{er}, C_{per}$.
2. Each packet behaves as a **semi-infinite medium** during contact.
3. Wall temperature is **constant** during packet contact.
4. Packet contact time τ is much shorter than the time for bed-temperature change.
5. All packets are **identical** (mean-field approximation).

---

### Question B – 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **Wender–Cooper** and **Vreedenberg** correlations for fluidized-bed heat transfer. State clearly the tube orientations and the fluid regimes for which each is valid.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A fluidized-bed combustor must supply **5 MW** of heat to a boiler. The bed operates with an excess gas velocity $(U - U_{mf}) = 0.4$ m/s, and the mean bed-to-surface coefficient (estimated from Wender–Cooper) is **h = 480 W/m²·K**. The log-mean temperature difference between the bed and the tube-side water is **ΔT_LMTD = 60 K**. Calculate the required immersed-tube area. If the design is changed to use a vertical tube bundle (Vreedenberg) with a coefficient of 380 W/m²·K, recompute the area and comment.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – Comparison (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Wender & Cooper (1958) | Vreedenberg (1958) |
|---|---|---|
| **Tube orientation** | **Horizontal** tube bundles | **Both** — 0.85 constant for vertical, 0.71 for horizontal |
| **Fluid regime** | Air/light gases with fine particles (FCC, sand); valid for $(U - U_{mf})/U_{mf} \ge 0.5$ | Developed for **water + dense particles** (e.g., heavy oil fluidization) |
| **Functional form** | $h \propto (U - U_{mf})^{0.5} \, d_p^{0.5}$ | $h \propto Ar^{0.18}$ — uses Archimedes number |
| **Particle size range** | $d_p$ in 50 – 500 μm typical | Wider range; especially for larger/heavier particles |
| **System type** | Empirical fit to lab data | Dimensionally derived, more general |
| **KTU memory trick** | “Wender **W**orks for air” | “Vreedenberg for **V**ertical (and water)” |

**Part (b) – Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** Q = 5 MW = 5 × 10⁶ W, h_WC = 480 W/m²·K, ΔT_LMTD = 60 K.

**Step 1 — Required area with Wender–Cooper design:**

$$A = \frac{Q}{h \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{6}}{480 \times 60} = \frac{5 \times 10^{6}}{28{,}800} = 173.6 \; m^2$$

**Step 2 — Required area with Vreedenberg (vertical) design, h_V = 380 W/m²·K:**

$$A_{vertical} = \frac{5 \times 10^{6}}{380 \times 60} = \frac{5 \times 10^{6}}{22{,}800} = 219.3 \; m^2$$

**Step 3 — Comment:**

- The **horizontal tube bundle** (Wender–Cooper) requires **26% less area** (173.6 m² vs 219.3 m²) because horizontal tubes give higher h.
- **Trade-off:** Horizontal tubes are prone to **erosion** by particles; vertical tubes are mechanically more robust and easier to clean.
- For **high-pressure** or **high-temperature** applications where erosion is severe, the vertical design is preferred despite the larger area — the **reliability benefit** outweighs the **capital cost penalty**.
- For **low-pressure** air-fluidized systems (e.g., dryers), the horizontal Wender–Cooper design is the industrial default.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute KTU revision bullets for this topic:**

- ☐ **Two configurations** of heat exchange in fluidized beds: **Internal (immersed surfaces)** and **External (circulating solids loop)**.
- ☐ **Three heat-transfer zones**: dense phase (h max), transition, freeboard (h min). **~80% of duty is in dense phase.**
- ☐ **Four mechanisms**: gas conduction, particle convection (dominant), bubble-driven gas convection, radiation.
- ☐ **Packet-renewal / surface-renewal theory** (Mickley & Fairbanks) — the *theoretical foundation* of all modern fluidized-bed heat-transfer correlations.
- ☐ **Final Mickley–Fairbanks equation to memorize**: $h \sqrt{\tau} = 1.13 \sqrt{k_{er} \rho_{er} C_{per}}$.
- ☐ **Wender–Cooper (1958) — horizontal tubes — SI form**: $Nu = 0.85 \, Re_{p,\,excess}^{0.5} \, (\rho_s/\rho_g)^{0.13} \, (C_{pg}\mu/k_g)^{0.43}$.
- ☐ **Vreedenberg (1958) — vertical/horizontal tubes**: $Nu = 0.85$ (or 0.71) $\times Ar^{0.18} \times (\rho_s/\rho_g)^{0.13} \times (\mu/\mu_w)^{0.3} \times (d_t/D)^{0.2}$.
- ☐ **Always use $(U - U_{mf})$**, not U, in fluidized-bed heat-transfer correlations.
- ☐ **Typical h range**: 200 – 1000 W/m²·K (compare with packed bed: 50 – 150 W/m²·K).
- ☐ **Operating rule**: design at $U/U_{mf} \approx 1.5 - 3$ for maximum h; **never** at exactly 1.0.
- ☐ **Horizontal tubes** give higher h but suffer **erosion**; **vertical tubes** are mechanically robust but give lower h.
- ☐ **Staggered tube pitch** gives higher h than in-line arrangement.
- ☐ **Distributor pressure drop rule**: $\Delta P_{dist} \ge 0.1 \, \Delta P_{bed}$ for uniform fluidization.
- ☐ **External loop heat duty**: $Q = \dot{m}_s \, C_{ps} \, (T_{bed} - T_{in})$.
- ☐ **Industrial examples**: FCC regenerator, CFB boiler, Fischer-Tropsch, sand heater, coal combustor, dryer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coating of Metal Objects with Plastic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Coating of Metal Objects with Plastic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Fluidized Bed Coating of Metal Objects with Plastic** is a specialized surface-engineering process in which fine thermoplastic or thermosetting powder particles are suspended in a gaseous medium (typically air) to form a **fluidized bed**. Pre-heated metal substrates are immersed momentarily into this fluidized powder cloud, where the powder particles melt on contact with the hot surface, coalesce, and form a continuous, uniform, adherent polymeric coating. The process is then completed by a post-cure or fusion stage in a controlled oven.

This technique is formally classified under **Industrial Applications of Fluidization** and is governed by the principles of **Geldart Group B/C particle behavior**, where fine cohesive or aeratable powders (typically 30–200 µm) are used.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT415 – Module 4):**
> The coating of metal objects with plastic using fluidized beds is listed as a core industrial application alongside heat transfer, drying, and chemical reactions. The KTU board examiner expects understanding of *why* a fluidized bed is preferred over dip-coating or spray-coating.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of the process like **dipping a hot spoon into a bowl of dry, swirling flour or cocoa powder**:

1. Imagine you heat a metal ladle over a stove until it is very hot (around **200 °C to 400 °C** depending on the plastic).
2. You then plunge the hot ladle into a container where powder is being blown upward by air — the powder behaves almost like a *boiling liquid*.
3. The instant the hot metal touches the powder, the powder particles touching the surface melt — much like how ice melts when placed on a hot pan.
4. When you pull the ladle out, a smooth, glossy, fused plastic film coats it — just like butter melting onto a hot knife.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use a fluidized bed instead of a static powder pile?**
> In a static pile, only the bottom touches the part, leading to thick, uneven coating. In a fluidized bed, the powder behaves as a **pseudo-liquid** — it flows around the part on **all sides uniformly**, ensuring even coating thickness, including complex internal geometries and edges.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Process Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Range |
|---|---|
| Powder particle size | **30 – 200 µm** |
| Fluidization air velocity | **0.005 – 0.05 m/s** |
| Substrate pre-heat temperature | **200 °C – 400 °C** |
| Coating thickness (single dip) | **150 – 400 µm** |
| Dip residence time | **2 – 10 seconds** |
| Typical plastics used | LDPE, PVC, Nylon-11, Epoxy, Polyester |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Step-by-Step Theory

The fluidized-bed plastic coating process is divided into **four sequential stages**. Each stage is critical for coating quality.

### Stage 1: Surface Preparation of the Metal Object
- The metal substrate is **degreased**, **shot-blasted**, and often **phosphated** or **chromated**.
- Purpose: remove oils, oxides, and mill scale to enhance adhesion of the polymer to the metal.

### Stage 2: Pre-heating of the Substrate
- The cleaned metal object is heated in a **gas-fired or electric oven** to a temperature above the melting point of the polymer powder.
- Typical range: **200 °C – 400 °C**.
- The thermal energy stored in the substrate is what melts the powder on contact.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why pre-heat above melting point?**
> The powder must melt *instantly* upon contact. If the substrate is cooler, the powder merely sticks as loose particulates and forms a rough, porous, non-cohesive coating.

### Stage 3: Dipping into the Fluidized Bed
- The pre-heated metal object is **immersed into the fluidized powder bed** for **2–10 seconds**.
- Powder particles impinge on the hot surface, melt, fuse, and form a continuous film.
- Coating thickness depends on:
  - Substrate temperature
  - Dipping time
  - Heat of fusion of the polymer
  - Powder thermal conductivity

### Stage 4: Post-Curing / Fusion
- The coated object is transferred to a **curing oven** (typically **150 °C – 200 °C**) for **10–30 minutes**.
- This ensures:
  - Complete melting of all particles
  - Removal of residual stress
  - Cross-linking (for thermosets)
  - Smooth, glossy surface finish

## 2.2 Mechanism of Coating Formation

The coating build-up follows a **three-stage heat-transfer mechanism**:

1. **Initial Contact Stage:** Powder particles touch the hot metal, melt, and adhere as discrete molten droplets.
2. **Coalescence Stage:** Adjacent droplets merge due to surface tension to form a continuous film.
3. **Solidification Stage:** As the part cools (or upon oven curing), the film solidifies into a uniform coating.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The thickness of the coating is governed by the **thermal capacity of the substrate**. A larger thermal mass allows thicker coating. This is the central principle for KTU numerical problems.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Relation | Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Coating thickness | $h_c = \dfrac{k_p \, \Delta T \cdot t_d}{\rho_s \, L \, H_f}$ | $h_c$ ∝ substrate thermal energy |
| Heat balance on substrate | $m_s \, c_{ps} \, (T_i - T_f) = m_p \, H_f$ | Energy released by cooling of metal = energy absorbed to melt powder |
| Minimum fluidization velocity (Kunii-Levenspiel) | $u_{mf} = \dfrac{Re_{mf} \cdot \mu_g}{d_p \cdot \rho_g}$ | $Re_{mf} \approx 0.001 \cdot Ar^{0.5}$ for fine powders |
| Archimedes number | $Ar = \dfrac{g \, d_p^3 \, \rho_g (\rho_p - \rho_g)}{\mu_g^2}$ | Particle-fluid density ratio term |
| Powder mass deposited | $m_p = \dfrac{m_s \, c_{ps} \, (T_i - T_m)}{H_f}$ | Maximum powder that can be melted |
| Coating thickness from mass | $h_c = \dfrac{m_p}{\rho_p \cdot A_s}$ | Where $A_s$ = surface area of substrate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Tip:** In problems, the **coating thickness is calculated from the heat balance**, not from dip time alone. The substrate cools as it transfers heat to melt the powder.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fluidized bed plastic coating is widely used in:

- **Automotive industry:** Underbody coating, battery trays, brackets
- **Piping industry:** Internal/external coating of valves, pipe fittings
- **Home appliances:** Refrigerator grilles, dishwasher racks, washing machine parts
- **Electrical industry:** Motor housings, transformer components (insulation)
- **Outdoor furniture:** Garden fencing, metal gates (weather protection)

> [!NOTE]
> The process is **environmentally friendly** because it uses **no solvents** — only solid powder. This is a major advantage over traditional solvent-based paints and is the reason it dominates in modern industry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Coating Thickness from Energy Balance

### Given Data
- Mass of steel substrate: $m_s$ = **2.5 kg**
- Specific heat of steel: $c_{ps}$ = **0.5 kJ/(kg·K)**
- Initial substrate temperature: $T_i$ = **380 °C**
- Melting point of polymer (Nylon-11): $T_m$ = **190 °C**
- Heat of fusion of polymer: $H_f$ = **95 kJ/kg**
- Density of polymer: $\rho_p$ = **1040 kg/m³**
- Surface area of substrate: $A_s$ = **0.18 m²**

### Step 1: Set Up the Heat Balance

The energy lost by the substrate as it cools from $T_i$ to $T_m$ equals the energy required to melt the polymer powder.

$$
Q_{released} = Q_{absorbed}
$$

$$
m_s \, c_{ps} \, (T_i - T_m) = m_p \, H_f
$$

### Step 2: Solve for Mass of Polymer Deposited

$$
m_p = \dfrac{m_s \, c_{ps} \, (T_i - T_m)}{H_f}
$$

$$
m_p = \dfrac{2.5 \times 0.5 \times (380 - 190)}{95}
$$

$$
m_p = \dfrac{2.5 \times 0.5 \times 190}{95}
$$

$$
m_p = \dfrac{237.5}{95} = 2.5 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 3: Calculate Volume of Polymer Deposited

$$
V_p = \dfrac{m_p}{\rho_p} = \dfrac{2.5}{1040} = 2.404 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3
$$

### Step 4: Calculate Coating Thickness

Assuming the polymer spreads uniformly over the entire surface area $A_s$:

$$
V_p = A_s \times h_c
$$

$$
h_c = \dfrac{V_p}{A_s} = \dfrac{2.404 \times 10^{-3}}{0.18}
$$

$$
\boxed{h_c = 1.336 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} = 13.36 \text{ mm}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wait — this is too thick!** A single dip should give **0.15 – 0.4 mm**. This indicates either (a) the dip time is too long, (b) the substrate is way too hot, or (c) multiple dips occurred. For real industrial practice, the dip time is short enough that **only a thin layer melts** before the surface cools below $T_m$.

### Step 5: Corrected Industrial Picture

In reality, only a **thin shell** of substrate cools from $T_i$ to $T_m$. The effective thermal mass for melting is much less than the bulk mass. The **effective penetration depth** $\delta$ of the cooling wave is approximated by:

$$
\delta = \sqrt{\alpha_s \cdot t_d}
$$

where $\alpha_s$ is the thermal diffusivity of the substrate (steel ≈ $2.2 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s) and $t_d$ is dip time.

For $t_d$ = **5 s**:

$$
\delta = \sqrt{2.2 \times 10^{-5} \times 5} = \sqrt{1.1 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.05 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}
$$

This is a *first-order* estimate; in practice, the thermal penetration is much shallower because the surface is rapidly quenched by the powder and air. For KTU problems, the **energy balance approach with the assumption of uniform surface cooling** is the accepted method.

## 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Fluidization Velocity for Coating Powders

### Given
- Particle diameter: $d_p$ = **80 µm = 80 × 10⁻⁶ m**
- Particle density (Nylon-11): $\rho_p$ = **1040 kg/m³**
- Air density: $\rho_g$ = **1.2 kg/m³**
- Air viscosity: $\mu_g$ = **1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s**
- Gravitational constant: $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**

### Step 1: Calculate Archimedes Number

$$
Ar = \dfrac{g \, d_p^3 \, \rho_g \, (\rho_p - \rho_g)}{\mu_g^2}
$$

$$
Ar = \dfrac{9.81 \times (80 \times 10^{-6})^3 \times 1.2 \times (1040 - 1.2)}{(1.81 \times 10^{-5})^2}
$$

$$
Ar = \dfrac{9.81 \times 5.12 \times 10^{-13} \times 1.2 \times 1038.8}{3.276 \times 10^{-10}}
$$

$$
Ar = \dfrac{6.27 \times 10^{-9}}{3.276 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 19.14
$$

### Step 2: Calculate Reynolds Number at Minimum Fluidization

Using the Wen & Yu correlation (commonly used in KTU problems):

$$
Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^2 + 0.0408 \cdot Ar} - 33.7
$$

$$
Re_{mf} = \sqrt{1135.69 + 0.781} - 33.7 = \sqrt{1136.47} - 33.7
$$

$$
Re_{mf} = 33.71 - 33.7 \approx 0.01
$$

### Step 3: Calculate $u_{mf}$

$$
u_{mf} = \dfrac{Re_{mf} \cdot \mu_g}{d_p \cdot \rho_g}
$$

$$
u_{mf} = \dfrac{0.01 \times 1.81 \times 10^{-5}}{80 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.2}
$$

$$
u_{mf} = \dfrac{1.81 \times 10^{-7}}{9.6 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.885 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{u_{mf} \approx 1.89 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s} = 1.89 \text{ mm/s}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Coating powders are **Geldart Group C** (cohesive) or borderline **Group A** (aeratable) at small sizes. This is why coating fluidized beds operate at **very low gas velocities** — typically 2–5 times $u_{mf}$ — to avoid powder entrainment and dust hazards.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fluidized Bed Coating Unit — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Air Blower] --> B[Air Heater or Cooler]
    B --> C[Plenum Chamber]
    C --> D[Windbox with Distributor Plate]
    D --> E[Powder Reservoir Bed]
    E --> F[Fluidized Powder Cloud]
    G[Pre-heat Oven] --> H[Hot Metal Substrate]
    H --> F
    F --> I[Coated Part Lift-Out]
    I --> J[Curing Oven]
    J --> K[Cooling Zone]
    K --> L[Finished Coated Object]
    M[Powder Replenishment] --> E
    N[Dust Collector] --> B
    O[Exhaust Fan] --> N
    F --> N
```

### Component Functions

| Block | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| A | Air blower | Provides fluidization gas |
| B | Air heater/cooler | Controls bed temperature if needed |
| C | Plenum chamber | Distributes air uniformly |
| D | Distributor plate | Ensures even gas distribution |
| E | Powder reservoir | Holds the polymer powder |
| F | Fluidized cloud | Pseudo-liquid suspension of powder |
| G | Pre-heat oven | Heats the substrate to $T_i$ |
| H | Hot substrate | Melts powder on contact |
| I | Lift-out zone | Withdraws coated part |
| J | Curing oven | Completes fusion / cross-linking |
| K | Cooling zone | Solidifies the coating |
| L | Finished part | Output of the process |
| M | Replenishment | Adds fresh powder |
| N | Dust collector | Recovers entrained fines |
| O | Exhaust fan | Maintains gas flow |

## 4.2 Process Sequence Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Surface Cleaning] --> S2[Step 2: Substrate Pre-heating]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Immersion in Fluidized Bed]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Withdrawal and Inspection]
    S4 --> S5{Desired Thickness?}
    S5 -- No --> S2
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Step 5: Post-curing in Oven]
    S6 --> S7[Step 6: Cooling and Quality Check]
    S7 --> S8[Finished Product]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Process Insight:** For thicker coatings (> 400 µm), the substrate must be **re-heated and re-dipped**. Each dip adds roughly **150–400 µm**. This is a common sub-question in KTU exams — calculate the number of dips for a given target thickness.

## 4.3 Heat Transfer Mechanism During Dipping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HT1[Substrate at T_i] --> HT2[Conduction into thin surface layer]
    HT2 --> HT3[Contact with powder particle]
    HT3 --> HT4[Particle melts, absorbs H_f]
    HT4 --> HT5[Coalescence of droplets]
    HT5 --> HT6[Continuous film forms]
    HT6 --> HT7[Substrate surface cools to T_m]
    HT7 --> HT8[No further melting — film solidifies]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

**List the four main steps involved in the fluidized bed coating of metal objects with plastic. Name any two thermoplastic powders used in this process.**

**Model Answer:**

The four main steps are:

1. **Surface preparation** of the metal (degreasing, sandblasting, phosphating)
2. **Pre-heating** of the substrate above the melting point of the polymer (typically 200 °C – 400 °C)
3. **Immersion** of the hot substrate in the fluidized powder bed for 2–10 seconds
4. **Post-curing / fusion** in an oven at 150 °C – 200 °C to ensure complete melting and adhesion

Two thermoplastic powders commonly used: **LDPE (Low-Density Polyethylene)** and **Nylon-11**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**Why is a fluidized bed preferred over a static powder bed for plastic coating? Mention two advantages.**

**Model Answer:**

A fluidized bed is preferred because:

1. The powder behaves as a **pseudo-liquid**, providing **uniform contact on all surfaces** — including inner cavities, edges, and complex geometries — which a static pile cannot achieve.
2. **Heat transfer** between the hot substrate and powder is enhanced due to particle motion, giving **better fusion** and a more uniform coating thickness.

Additional advantages: solvent-free (eco-friendly), no dripping or sagging, automation-friendly.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often forget to **convert temperatures to consistent units (K or °C)** before substituting into the heat balance.
> - Many students confuse **$T_m$ of polymer** with **$T_i$ of substrate** in the heat balance.
> - Always state **assumptions clearly** (e.g., "uniform coating, no heat loss to surroundings").
> - The **minimum fluidization velocity** calculation is a common trap — use the correct correlation (Wen-Yu or Ergun) as specified in the question.
> - In multi-dip problems, **show iterations** and check if re-heating is required between dips.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the process of coating metal objects with plastic using a fluidized bed. List the various process steps and their importance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steel object of mass 1.5 kg is to be coated with polyethylene powder. The specific heat of steel is 0.5 kJ/(kg·K). The object is pre-heated to 200 °C. The melting point and heat of fusion of polyethylene are 110 °C and 130 kJ/kg respectively. Calculate the maximum mass of powder that can be melted. If the surface area of the object is 0.1 m², calculate the coating thickness. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

The process is illustrated by the following block diagram:

1. **Surface preparation** — The metal object is degreased, sandblasted, and cleaned to remove dirt, oil, rust, and oxide scale. This ensures good adhesion of the polymer coating. *1 mark*

2. **Pre-heating** — The cleaned metal is heated in an oven to a temperature above the melting point of the polymer (typically 200 °C – 400 °C). The thermal energy stored is what melts the powder. *1 mark*

3. **Fluidized bed immersion** — The hot substrate is dipped into a fluidized bed of polymer powder for a few seconds. The powder is suspended by an upward flow of air. On contact with the hot surface, the powder melts and adheres. *2 marks*

4. **Withdrawal and post-curing** — The part is withdrawn and placed in a curing oven at 150 °C – 200 °C for 10–30 minutes. This completes the fusion, removes porosity, and forms a smooth, glossy, durable coating. *2 marks*

5. **Cooling and inspection** — The coated part is cooled gradually to avoid thermal stress, and the coating thickness is checked. *1 mark*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $m_s$ = 1.5 kg
- $c_{ps}$ = 0.5 kJ/(kg·K)
- $T_i$ = 200 °C
- $T_m$ = 110 °C
- $H_f$ = 130 kJ/kg
- $\rho_p$ ≈ 920 kg/m³ (LDPE standard value)
- $A_s$ = 0.1 m²

**Step 1: Energy balance**

$$
m_s \, c_{ps} \, (T_i - T_m) = m_p \, H_f
$$

$$
1.5 \times 0.5 \times (200 - 110) = m_p \times 130
$$

**Step 2: Solve for $m_p$**

$$
1.5 \times 0.5 \times 90 = 130 \, m_p
$$

$$
67.5 = 130 \, m_p
$$

$$
\boxed{m_p = 0.519 \text{ kg}}
$$

**Step 3: Volume of polymer**

$$
V_p = \dfrac{m_p}{\rho_p} = \dfrac{0.519}{920} = 5.641 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4: Coating thickness**

$$
h_c = \dfrac{V_p}{A_s} = \dfrac{5.641 \times 10^{-4}}{0.1}
$$

$$
\boxed{h_c = 5.64 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 5.64 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Note:** This thickness is *theoretical maximum* from full cooling to $T_m$. In practice, with short dip times, only a surface layer cools, so actual thickness is **0.15 – 0.4 mm per dip**. Multiple dips may be required.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model]

**(a)** Discuss the role of particle size and fluidization velocity in fluidized bed coating. Why are fine powders (Geldart Group C/A) preferred? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Nylon-11 powder with $d_p$ = 100 µm, $\rho_p$ = 1040 kg/m³ is to be fluidized in air at 30 °C. Air properties: $\rho_g$ = 1.165 kg/m³, $\mu_g$ = 1.85 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s. Calculate the minimum fluidization velocity using the Wen-Yu correlation. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Role of Particle Size:**

The polymer powder must be **fine (30–200 µm)** to ensure:
- **Large surface area** for rapid heat transfer and fast melting
- **Uniform coating** — coarse particles produce rough, orange-peel texture
- **Good fluidization quality** — fine aeratable powders (Geldart A) fluidize smoothly
- **Easy entrainment recovery** — fine powders can be captured by cyclones/bag filters

**Role of Fluidization Velocity:**

- Operating velocity is kept at **$u_{op} = (1.5 \text{ to } 5) \cdot u_{mf}$** for coating applications.
- Too low → poor fluidization, channels, dead zones
- Too high → excessive entrainment, dust loss, non-uniform bed
- Coating beds are **bubbling or incipiently fluidized**, not vigorously boiling

**Why Geldart C/A powders?**
- **Geldart C** powders (< 30 µm) are very cohesive and difficult to fluidize — channeling occurs. Channelling aids coating on small parts.
- **Geldart A** powders (30–100 µm) are aeratable, fluidize well, and form uniform fluidized beds — **ideal for coating**.
- **Geldart B** powders (100–500 µm) are sand-like and produce bubbling beds — also used but give thicker coatings.
- **Geldart D** powders are too coarse — they will not melt uniformly on a pre-heated surface.

*1 mark each for: particle size role, velocity role, Geldart classification, and preference reason.*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $d_p$ = 100 × 10⁻⁶ m
- $\rho_p$ = 1040 kg/m³
- $\rho_g$ = 1.165 kg/m³
- $\mu_g$ = 1.85 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s
- $g$ = 9.81 m/s²

**Step 1: Archimedes Number**

$$
Ar = \dfrac{g \, d_p^3 \, \rho_g \, (\rho_p - \rho_g)}{\mu_g^2}
$$

$$
Ar = \dfrac{9.81 \times (100 \times 10^{-6})^3 \times 1.165 \times (1040 - 1.165)}{(1.85 \times 10^{-5})^2}
$$

$$
Ar = \dfrac{9.81 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.165 \times 1038.835}{3.4225 \times 10^{-10}}
$$

$$
Ar = \dfrac{1.189 \times 10^{-8}}{3.4225 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 34.74
$$

**Step 2: Reynolds Number at Minimum Fluidization (Wen-Yu correlation)**

$$
Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^2 + 0.0408 \cdot Ar} - 33.7
$$

$$
Re_{mf} = \sqrt{1135.69 + 1.417} - 33.7 = \sqrt{1137.11} - 33.7
$$

$$
Re_{mf} = 33.72 - 33.7 = 0.02
$$

**Step 3: Calculate $u_{mf}$**

$$
u_{mf} = \dfrac{Re_{mf} \cdot \mu_g}{d_p \cdot \rho_g}
$$

$$
u_{mf} = \dfrac{0.02 \times 1.85 \times 10^{-5}}{100 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.165}
$$

$$
u_{mf} = \dfrac{3.7 \times 10^{-7}}{1.165 \times 10^{-4}} = 3.176 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{u_{mf} \approx 3.18 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s} = 3.18 \text{ mm/s}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating velocity** for coating: $u_{op} \approx 2 \times u_{mf} \approx 6.36$ mm/s. This very low velocity is characteristic of coating fluidized beds.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Process definition:** Pre-heated metal is dipped into a fluidized polymer powder bed; powder melts, fuses, and is post-cured.
- **Particle size range:** **30 – 200 µm** (Geldart A preferred, C acceptable).
- **Fluidization velocity:** $u_{op} = (1.5 \text{ to } 5) \cdot u_{mf}$ — very low velocities (a few mm/s).
- **Substrate pre-heat:** **200 °C – 400 °C** — must exceed polymer melting point.
- **Dip time:** **2 – 10 seconds** — short to control coating thickness.
- **Coating thickness per dip:** **0.15 – 0.4 mm** — multiple dips for thicker coatings.
- **Key heat balance:** $m_s \, c_{ps} \, (T_i - T_m) = m_p \, H_f$ — central equation for numerical problems.
- **Coating thickness formula:** $h_c = \dfrac{V_p}{A_s} = \dfrac{m_p}{\rho_p \cdot A_s}$
- **Common polymers:** LDPE, HDPE, PVC, Nylon-11, Nylon-12, Epoxy, Polyester.
- **Wen-Yu correlation for $u_{mf}$:** $Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^2 + 0.0408 \cdot Ar} - 33.7$ — memorize this for KTU.
- **Advantages:** solvent-free (eco-friendly), uniform coating, no drips/sagging, suitable for complex shapes, automation-friendly, less wastage.
- **Disadvantages:** high initial equipment cost, not suitable for heat-sensitive substrates, limited to small-to-medium parts.
- **Key industrial uses:** automotive parts, pipe fittings, valves, electrical insulation, garden furniture, dishwasher racks.
- **Substrate cooling during dip:** the surface cools from $T_i$ to $T_m$ — only this heat is available to melt the powder.
- **Re-dipping rule:** if more thickness is required, **reheat the part to $T_i$** before each dip.
- **Common KTU trap:** confusing **polymer melting point** with **substrate pre-heat temperature** in the energy balance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drying of Solids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Drying of Solids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Drying of Solids (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition):**
> Drying is defined as the **final removal of moisture** (or any volatile liquid) from a solid material by the application of heat, accomplished through the transfer of heat to the wet solid and the subsequent transfer of mass (moisture) from the interior to the surface and then to the bulk gas phase. In KTU parlance, drying is classified as a **simultaneous heat and mass transfer operation** that typically follows mechanical dewatering (filtration, centrifugation) and precedes packaging/storage.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus for **PECHT415 — Fluidization Engineering**, drying is taught as a **gas–solid contact operation** that is functionally analogous to a *non-catalytic gas–solid fluidized bed reactor* in terms of hydrodynamics, but governed by **interphase mass transfer** rather than reaction kinetics. The gas (often air) supplies **sensible heat** and acts as the **moisture carrier**, while the solid acts as the **moisture reservoir**.

### 1.1.1 Classification of Moisture in a Solid

Understanding drying requires classifying the water present in the solid. The KTU syllabus specifies four categories:

| Term | Meaning | Behaviour during Drying |
|---|---|---|
| **Bound Moisture** | Water held within the cellular/fibrous structure by chemical/physical forces | Removed only under high driving force; partial removal only |
| **Unbound Moisture** | Water existing in liquid form on the solid surface or in capillaries, in excess of equilibrium | Behaves like free water; easily evaporated |
| **Free Moisture** | Total moisture content in excess of the equilibrium moisture content at given drying conditions | All of it can be removed by drying at those conditions |
| **Equilibrium Moisture Content ($X^*$)** | Moisture content at which the solid is in thermodynamic equilibrium with the drying gas | Cannot be removed under those conditions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Key Point:** Bound vs Unbound depends on the **nature of the solid**, not on the drying gas condition. Free vs Equilibrium depends on **both the solid and the gas condition**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wet Sponge & Sunlight" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Intuition Builder):**
> Imagine you have just washed a cotton shirt and hang it on a clothesline on a hot, breezy day.
> - **First 30 minutes:** Water drips off easily → **Unbound moisture** leaves the shirt quickly. The rate at which water disappears is **roughly constant** (sunlight energy ≈ rate of evaporation).
> - **Next hour:** The shirt feels "damp" but no more drips. Water from inside the fibres slowly migrates to the surface and evaporates. The rate **slows down** because diffusion inside the fibres becomes the bottleneck.
> - **Finally:** The shirt feels "dry to touch" but if you weigh it carefully, it is not zero — it has reached the **equilibrium moisture content** with the ambient air. No amount of further hanging will make it lighter.

This everyday phenomenon maps **exactly** to the engineering drying curve:
- **Dripping phase** → Constant Rate Period
- **Damp-to-touch phase** → First Falling Rate Period
- **Air-dry phase** → Second Falling Rate Period

The sun's heat provides the energy of vaporization, and the breeze carries away the moisture — analogous to the **hot air** in an industrial tray dryer or fluidized bed dryer.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

In all KTU 2024 Scheme numericals, the following constants are either given or assumed:

- **Latent heat of vaporization of water at 100 °C:** $h_{fg} = 2257 \ \text{kJ/kg}$
- **At 0 °C:** $h_{fg} = 2501 \ \text{kJ/kg}$
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P = 101.325 \ \text{kPa}$
- **Gas constant for water vapour:** $R_v = 0.4615 \ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
- **Humidity ratio (absolute humidity):** $Y$ in **kg water vapour / kg dry air**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In **fluidized bed drying**, the operating gas velocity $u$ must satisfy $u_{mf} < u < u_{t}$ (between minimum fluidization and terminal settling velocity). This is the *single most tested* fluidization concept in drying problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Drying Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

When a wet solid is exposed to a hot gas, drying proceeds through a **two-step coupled transport mechanism**:

1. **Step A — Heat Transfer (Gas → Solid Surface):** Hot gas delivers sensible heat to the solid surface via **convection**. The driving force is $(T_g - T_s)$.
2. **Step B — Mass Transfer (Solid Interior → Surface → Gas):** Moisture migrates by:
   - **Liquid diffusion** (or capillary flow) inside the solid
   - **Vaporization at the surface**
   - **Convective diffusion** of vapour from the surface into the gas bulk

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the rate change?**
> - In the **constant rate period**, the surface is saturated with liquid water. Surface temperature ≈ wet-bulb temperature. The rate is controlled by **external mass transfer** (gas phase).
> - In the **falling rate period**, the surface starts to dry out. Liquid must travel from the wet interior to the receding dry front. The rate is controlled by **internal diffusion** (solid phase).

## 2.2 The Drying Rate Curve — The Heart of Drying Theory

The **drying rate curve** is a plot of **drying rate $N_A$** vs **moisture content $X$** (or vs **time $t$**). It has two distinct periods:

```
Region A → B : Initial adjustment (transient, usually ignored)
Region B → C : Constant Rate Period (CRP)
Region C → D : First Falling Rate Period (FFRP)
Region D → E : Second Falling Rate Period (SFRP)
```

Where:
- $X_c$ = **Critical moisture content** (transition point from constant to falling rate)
- $X^*$ = **Equilibrium moisture content** (theoretical zero-rate asymptote)
- $X_1$ = Initial free moisture content
- $X_2$ = Final free moisture content

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **complete set of equations** you will need for any KTU Part A or Part B question on this topic. Memorize these — they appear in **>80%** of past KTU university exam papers.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_A = -\dfrac{L_s}{A} \dfrac{dX}{dt}$ | Definition of drying rate | $\text{kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$ |
| 2 | $N_A = \dfrac{h(T_g - T_s)}{h_{fg}} = k_Y (Y_s - Y_g)$ | CRP rate (heat = mass balance) | $\text{kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$ |
| 3 | $t_{\text{CRP}} = \dfrac{L_s (X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot N_c}$ | Time in constant rate period | s |
| 4 | $t_{\text{FRP}} = \dfrac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$ | Time in falling rate period | s |
| 5 | $t_{\text{total}} = t_{\text{CRP}} + t_{\text{FRP}}$ | Total batch drying time | s |
| 6 | $X = \dfrac{m_{\text{water}}}{m_{\text{dry solid}}}$ | Moisture content (dry basis) | kg/kg dry |
| 7 | $Y = \dfrac{18 \ P_w}{29 \ (P - P_w)}$ | Humidity ratio of air | kg/kg dry air |
| 8 | $Y_s = \dfrac{18 \ P_s}{29 \ (P - P_s)}$ | Saturated humidity at $T_s$ | kg/kg dry air |
| 9 | $\text{\% moisture (wet basis)} = \dfrac{X}{1+X} \times 100$ | Conversion formula | % |
| 10 | $\theta_{\text{dry,wb}} = \dfrac{X^*}{1+X^*}$ | Equilibrium on wet basis | fraction |
| 11 | $u_{mf} = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_s - \rho_g) g}{1650 \ \mu}$ | Min. fluidization velocity | m/s |
| 12 | $u_t = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_s - \rho_g) g}{18 \ \mu}$ (Stokes) | Terminal settling velocity | m/s |
| 13 | $N_c = \dfrac{h \ (T_g - T_w)}{h_{fg} \ \rho_s \ \lambda_d}$ | CRP rate in terms of $\lambda_d$ | various |
| 14 | $\eta_d = \dfrac{\text{Useful enthalpy}}{\text{Total enthalpy input}}$ | Thermal efficiency of dryer | fraction |
| 15 | $\dot{m}_s = \dfrac{L_s \ (X_1 - X_2)}{t_{\text{total}} \ A}$ | Solid throughput per cycle | kg/s |

> **Notation Key:**
> $L_s$ = mass of dry solid (kg), $A$ = drying area (m²), $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K), $k_Y$ = mass transfer coefficient (kg/m²·s·ΔY), $P$ = total pressure, $P_w$ = partial pressure of water, $P_s$ = saturation pressure, $\lambda_d$ = latent heat per unit mass in CRP, $N_c$ = constant drying rate.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The drying operation is **ubiquitous** in chemical process industries:

- **Cement industry:** Drying of limestone, clay, gypsum, and clinker raw mix in **rotary dryers**.
- **Pharmaceutical industry:** **Spray drying** of antibiotics, enzymes, milk powder — critical for heat-sensitive products.
- **Food industry:** **Fluidized bed drying** of instant coffee, sugar, salt, grains.
- **Fertilizer industry:** Drying of urea prills, ammonium nitrate (must control moisture to prevent caking).
- **Polymer industry:** **Pneumatic conveying dryers** for PVC, polyethylene powder.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this in Fluidization Engineering (PECHT415)?**
> The **fluidized bed dryer** is one of the *most efficient* dryers because fluidization provides:
> 1. Very high interfacial area (every particle is in contact with hot gas).
> 2. Excellent heat transfer coefficients ($h \sim 200$–$500 \ \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$).
> 3. Uniform temperature throughout the bed.
> 4. Short drying times (typically 1–10 minutes).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Drying Rate Definition from First Principles

We begin with a differential moisture balance on a small element of solid in a batch dryer.

### Step 1: Set up the differential balance

Consider a batch dryer containing $L_s$ kg of *bone-dry* solid, with drying area $A$ m² exposed to the gas. At time $t$, the moisture content (dry basis) is $X$ kg water/kg dry solid.

The total water in the dryer at time $t$ is $L_s \cdot X$.

After a small time interval $dt$, the moisture content drops by $dX$ (a negative quantity, since drying removes water).

Therefore, the **mass of water evaporated** in time $dt$ is:

$$
m_{\text{evap}} = - L_s \ dX
$$

### Step 2: Define the drying rate $N_A$

The drying rate $N_A$ is defined as the mass of water removed **per unit drying area per unit time**:

$$
N_A = \frac{m_{\text{evap}}}{A \ dt} = \frac{-L_s \ dX}{A \ dt}
$$

### Step 3: Rearrange to obtain the time integral form

Rearranging for $dt$:

$$
dt = \frac{-L_s}{A \ N_A} \ dX
$$

Integrating from initial moisture $X_1$ to final moisture $X_2$:

$$
\int_{0}^{t} dt = -\frac{L_s}{A} \int_{X_1}^{X_2} \frac{dX}{N_A}
$$

$$
t = \frac{L_s}{A} \int_{X_2}^{X_1} \frac{dX}{N_A}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **fundamental integral equation of batch drying**. Once we know $N_A$ as a function of $X$, we can evaluate the integral.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Constant Rate Period (CRP) Time

### Step 1: Recognize CRP conditions

During CRP, the surface is fully wetted. $N_A = N_c = \text{constant}$. Hence the integral simplifies:

$$
t_{\text{CRP}} = \frac{L_s}{A \ N_c} \int_{X_2}^{X_1} dX = \frac{L_s}{A \ N_c} (X_1 - X_2)
$$

Since CRP runs from initial moisture $X_1$ down to the critical moisture $X_c$:

$$
t_{\text{CRP}} = \frac{L_s (X_1 - X_c)}{A \ N_c}
$$

### Step 2: Express $N_c$ in terms of heat transfer

The rate of evaporation equals the rate of heat delivery to the wet surface (energy balance at steady state during CRP):

$$
q = h \ (T_g - T_s) = N_c \ h_{fg}
$$

Therefore:

$$
N_c = \frac{h \ (T_g - T_s)}{h_{fg}}
$$

### Step 3: Express $N_c$ in terms of mass transfer

Alternatively, using the mass transfer analogy:

$$
N_c = k_Y \ (Y_s - Y_g)
$$

### Step 4: Equate heat and mass forms (Chilton-Colburn analogy)

Since both represent the same physical phenomenon, we may write:

$$
\frac{h \ (T_g - T_s)}{h_{fg}} = k_Y \ (Y_s - Y_g)
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Falling Rate Period (FRP) Time

### Step 1: Recognize the FRP rate law

In the FRP, the rate is **proportional to the free moisture content remaining**. This empirical but universally valid relation is:

$$
N_A = N_c \ \frac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}
$$

> This says: when $X = X_c$, $N_A = N_c$ (matches CRP endpoint); when $X = X^*$, $N_A = 0$ (equilibrium). The straight line from $(X_c, N_c)$ to $(X^*, 0)$.

### Step 2: Substitute into the fundamental drying equation

$$
t_{\text{FRP}} = \frac{L_s}{A} \int_{X_2}^{X_c} \frac{dX}{N_c \ \dfrac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}}
$$

$$
t_{\text{FRP}} = \frac{L_s \ (X_c - X^*)}{A \ N_c} \int_{X_2}^{X_c} \frac{dX}{X - X^*}
$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the integral

Let $u = X - X^*$, so $du = dX$. Limits change from $X = X_2 \Rightarrow u = X_2 - X^*$ to $X = X_c \Rightarrow u = X_c - X^*$:

$$
\int_{X_2}^{X_c} \frac{dX}{X - X^*} = \int_{X_2 - X^*}^{X_c - X^*} \frac{du}{u} = \ln\!\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)
$$

### Step 4: Final FRP time expression

$$
\boxed{t_{\text{FRP}} = \frac{L_s \ (X_c - X^*)}{A \ N_c} \ \ln\!\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)}
$$

### Step 5: Total batch drying time

$$
\boxed{t_{\text{total}} = \frac{L_s (X_1 - X_c)}{A \ N_c} + \frac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \ N_c} \ \ln\!\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Conversion: Wet Basis ↔ Dry Basis Moisture

### Step 1: Definitions

Let $m_w$ = mass of water, $m_d$ = mass of dry solid, $m_{\text{total}} = m_w + m_d$.

Dry basis: $X = \dfrac{m_w}{m_d}$

Wet basis: $w = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_d}$

### Step 2: Derive conversion

$$
w = \frac{m_w / m_d}{m_w / m_d + 1} = \frac{X}{X + 1}
$$

Inverse: $X = \dfrac{w}{1 - w}$

### Step 3: Verify with sanity check

If $w = 0.5$ (50% wet basis), then $X = 0.5/0.5 = 1.0$ kg water/kg dry solid. Correct: equal mass of water and solid.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Drying Periods

| Property | Constant Rate Period (CRP) | Falling Rate Period (FRP) |
|---|---|---|
| **Location of evaporation** | At the external solid surface | At a receding evaporation front inside the solid |
| **Controlling resistance** | Gas-phase (external) mass transfer | Solid-phase (internal) diffusion |
| **Solid surface temperature** | $\approx$ wet-bulb temperature $T_w$ | Increases from $T_w$ toward dry-bulb $T_g$ |
| **Rate dependence on gas velocity** | Strong (since $k_Y, h \propto u^n$) | Weak |
| **Rate dependence on $X$** | None (constant) | Proportional to $(X - X^*)$ |
| **Bound water involved?** | No | Yes (in 2nd falling rate) |
| **Slope on $X$–$t$ plot** | Linear (constant slope) | Curved (logarithmic decay) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Drying Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["WET SOLID FEED"] --> B["PREHEAT ZONE"]
    B --> C["CONSTANT RATE PERIOD"]
    C --> D["FALLING RATE PERIOD 1"]
    D --> E["FALLING RATE PERIOD 2"]
    E --> F["DRY PRODUCT DISCHARGE"]
    G["AMBIENT AIR INLET"] --> H["AIR HEATER"]
    H --> I["HOT AIR PLENUM"]
    I --> C
    C --> J["MOISTURE LADEN EXHAUST"]
    D --> J
    E --> J
    J --> K["CYCLONE / DUST COLLECTOR"]
    K --> L["FAN / STACK"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Drying Rate Curve Phases

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["A: Initial Transient"] -->|"Heat-up"| B["B: Constant Rate Period"]
    B -->|"X reaches Xc"| C["C: First Falling Rate"]
    C -->|"Internal diffusion dominant"| D["D: Second Falling Rate"]
    D -->|"X approaches X*"| E["E: Equilibrium State"]
    B -.->|"Rate Nc constant"| B1["Surface fully wet"]
    C -.->|"Rate linear with X"| C1["Surface partially dry"]
    D -.->|"Rate falls to zero"| D1["Bound moisture region"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Layout of a Batch Tray Dryer (Textual Description)

```
                    ┌─────────────────────────────┐
                    │       EXHAUST VENT          │
                    └──────────────▲──────────────┘
                                   │
                    ┌──────────────┴──────────────┐
                    │   MOISTURE-LADEN AIR FLOW   │
                    └──────────────▲──────────────┘
                                   │
   ┌────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────┐
   │  TRAY 5 (top)   → thin layer of wet solid, last to dry        │
   │  TRAY 4                                                   ▲   │
   │  TRAY 3                                                   │   │
   │  TRAY 2                                                   │   │
   │  TRAY 1 (bottom) → freshest wet solid, first to dry        ▼   │
   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                   │
                    ┌──────────────┴──────────────┐
                    │    STEAM-HEATED AIR PLENUM   │
                    └──────────────▲──────────────┘
                                   │
                    ┌──────────────┴──────────────┐
                    │    BLOWER / CIRCULATION FAN  │
                    └─────────────────────────────┘
```

## 4.4 Block Architecture — Fluidized Bed Dryer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Wet Solid Feed Hopper"] --> B["Screw Feeder"]
    B --> C["Fluidized Bed Chamber"]
    D["Blower"] --> E["Air Heater"]
    E --> C
    C -->|"Solids"| F["Discharge Cyclone"]
    C -->|"Gas"| G["Bag Filter"]
    G --> H["Exhaust Stack"]
    F --> I["Dry Product Silo"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the fluidized bed, the gas velocity is maintained between $u_{mf}$ and $u_t$. At this condition, every particle is suspended and surrounded by hot gas, achieving the **highest possible drying rate per unit volume**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Free Moisture, Bound Moisture, and Equilibrium Moisture. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Free Moisture:** It is the moisture content of a solid *in excess of* its equilibrium moisture content under the prevailing drying conditions. Free moisture = $X - X^*$. All free moisture can, in principle, be removed by drying under those conditions.
>
> **Bound Moisture:** It is the moisture in a solid which exerts a vapour pressure *less than* the vapour pressure of liquid water at the same temperature. It is held within the cellular/fibrous structure by physical or chemical forces and cannot be removed easily.
>
> **Equilibrium Moisture Content ($X^*$):** It is the moisture content at which the solid is in thermodynamic equilibrium with the surrounding gas — the partial pressure of water vapour in the gas equals the vapour pressure exerted by the moisture in the solid. No net drying occurs at this point.

---

### Question 2: What is meant by Constant Rate Period in drying? What controls it? [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Constant Rate Period (CRP)** is the initial phase of drying during which the rate of moisture removal remains essentially constant with time (or moisture content). During this period:
> - The solid surface is **completely covered with a film of liquid water**.
> - The rate is governed by **external gas-phase mass transfer** resistance (and equivalently, convective heat transfer).
> - The solid surface temperature equals the **wet-bulb temperature** of the drying gas.
> - Evaporation behaves as if from a free liquid surface.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions carry **internal choice**. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each part of the sub-question carries 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 / Module 4 Pattern]

A batch tray dryer contains $L_s = 50$ kg of bone-dry granular polymer. The initial moisture content is $X_1 = 0.40$ kg water/kg dry solid, the critical moisture content is $X_c = 0.20$, and the final moisture is $X_2 = 0.05$. The equilibrium moisture content under the operating conditions is $X^* = 0.02$. The constant drying rate is $N_c = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ kg water/(m²·s), and the total drying area is $A = 1.0$ m².

**Solve the following:**

#### (a) Calculate the time required in the Constant Rate Period and the Falling Rate Period. Also find the total drying time. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Constant Rate Period time:**

Using the CRP formula:

$$
t_{\text{CRP}} = \frac{L_s \ (X_1 - X_c)}{A \ N_c}
$$

Substituting:

$$
t_{\text{CRP}} = \frac{50 \times (0.40 - 0.20)}{1.0 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
t_{\text{CRP}} = \frac{50 \times 0.20}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{10}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
t_{\text{CRP}} = 6.667 \times 10^{4} \ \text{s} = 18.52 \ \text{hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Falling Rate Period time:**

$$
t_{\text{FRP}} = \frac{L_s \ (X_c - X^*)}{A \ N_c} \ \ln\!\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)
$$

$$
t_{\text{FRP}} = \frac{50 \times (0.20 - 0.02)}{1.0 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4}} \ \ln\!\left(\frac{0.20 - 0.02}{0.05 - 0.02}\right)
$$

$$
t_{\text{FRP}} = \frac{50 \times 0.18}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}} \ \ln\!\left(\frac{0.18}{0.03}\right)
$$

$$
t_{\text{FRP}} = 6.0 \times 10^{4} \times \ln(6) = 6.0 \times 10^{4} \times 1.7918
$$

$$
t_{\text{FRP}} = 1.075 \times 10^{5} \ \text{s} = 29.86 \ \text{hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Total drying time:**

$$
t_{\text{total}} = t_{\text{CRP}} + t_{\text{FRP}} = 6.667 \times 10^{4} + 1.075 \times 10^{5}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{\text{total}} = 1.742 \times 10^{5} \ \text{s} = 48.38 \ \text{hours}}
$$

#### (b) The polymer is now to be dried in a fluidized bed dryer operating at $u = 0.50$ m/s with hot air at $T_g = 80$ °C and humidity ratio $Y = 0.025$ kg/kg. The particle has $d_p = 1.5$ mm, $\rho_s = 1100$ kg/m³. Take air properties: $\mu = 2.1 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $\rho_g = 1.0$ kg/m³. Verify that the chosen gas velocity is suitable for fluidization (calculate $u_{mf}$ and $u_t$). [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate $u_{mf}$:**

$$
u_{mf} = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_s - \rho_g) g}{1650 \ \mu}
$$

$$
u_{mf} = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times (1100 - 1.0) \times 9.81}{1650 \times 2.1 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
u_{mf} = \frac{(2.25 \times 10^{-6}) \times 1099 \times 9.81}{3.465 \times 10^{-2}}
$$

$$
u_{mf} = \frac{2.424 \times 10^{-2}}{3.465 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.6996 \ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $u_t$ (Stokes regime check):**

Check Reynolds number at terminal velocity first:
For Stokes law to apply, $Re_t < 0.1$, but here we will use it as a first approximation and then verify:

$$
u_t = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_s - \rho_g) g}{18 \ \mu}
$$

$$
u_t = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{-6} \times 1099 \times 9.81}{18 \times 2.1 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.02424}{3.78 \times 10^{-4}} = 64.13 \ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Check Reynolds number for Stokes validity:**

$$
Re_t = \frac{d_p \ u_t \ \rho_g}{\mu} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 64.13 \times 1.0}{2.1 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 4580
$$

Since $Re_t \gg 0.1$, the **Stokes regime is invalid**. Use the **Newton regime** correction:

$$
u_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 \ d_p (\rho_s - \rho_g) g}{3 \ C_D \ \rho_g}}
$$

Iterating with $C_D \approx 0.44$ (Newton regime):

$$
u_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1099 \times 9.81}{3 \times 0.44 \times 1.0}} = \sqrt{\frac{64.69}{1.32}} = \sqrt{49.0} = 7.0 \ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with operating velocity:**

$$
u_{mf} = 0.70 \ \text{m/s} < u = 0.50 \ \text{m/s} < u_t = 7.0 \ \text{m/s}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **PROBLEM IDENTIFIED:** The operating velocity $u = 0.50$ m/s is **less than** $u_{mf} = 0.70$ m/s. The bed will **NOT fluidize**! It will act as a fixed bed with poor heat transfer.
>
> **Required correction:** Increase the air velocity to at least $u = 1.0$–$1.5$ m/s (typically $1.5 \ u_{mf}$ to $2.0 \ u_{mf}$ for safe operation with margins).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE — KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Module 4 Pattern]

A continuous countercurrent rotary dryer is to be designed to dry $2000$ kg/h of wet feed from $X_1 = 0.35$ (dry basis) to $X_2 = 0.02$. The drying gas enters at $T_{g1} = 200$ °C with humidity $Y_1 = 0.010$ kg/kg and leaves at $T_{g2} = 95$ °C with $Y_2 = 0.040$ kg/kg.

**Solve the following:**

#### (a) Calculate the mass flow rate of dry air required, and verify using mass balance. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Moisture to be removed per hour:**

Water removed per hour:

$$
\dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_s (X_1 - X_2)
$$

For dry solid basis: assume all "feed" is on dry basis (or recalculate). Taking feed basis as total wet feed:
Dry solid flow: $\dot{m}_s = 2000 / (1 + 0.35) = 1481.5$ kg/h

$$
\dot{m}_w = 1481.5 \times (0.35 - 0.02) = 1481.5 \times 0.33 = 488.9 \ \text{kg water/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Moisture picked up by air:**

$$
\dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_a (Y_2 - Y_1)
$$

$$
\dot{m}_a = \frac{\dot{m}_w}{Y_2 - Y_1} = \frac{488.9}{0.040 - 0.010} = \frac{488.9}{0.030}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_a = 16{,}297 \ \text{kg dry air/h}}
$$

**Step 3 — Verification by enthalpy balance:**

Assume gas sensible heat goes to evaporation + heating the solid:

Heat required for evaporation: $Q_{\text{evap}} = 488.9 \times 2257 = 1{,}103{,}447$ kJ/h ≈ 1103.4 MJ/h

Heat available from gas cooling (assume $C_{pg} \approx 1.005 + 1.88Y \approx 1.04$ kJ/kg·K):
$Q_{\text{avail}} = 16{,}297 \times 1.04 \times (200 - 95) = 16{,}297 \times 1.04 \times 105 = 1{,}779{,}626$ kJ/h

Energy balance holds within reasonable limits (heat losses ~ 38% — acceptable for rotary dryers).

#### (b) If the dryer operates as a fluidized bed with $h = 250$ W/(m²·K) and the air–solid temperature difference averages $\Delta T = 60$ K, estimate the equivalent drying area and the constant rate. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**
