**Step 1 — Constant rate $N_c$:**

$$
N_c = \frac{h \ \Delta T}{h_{fg}} = \frac{250 \times 60}{2257 \times 10^{3}}
$$

$$
N_c = \frac{15000}{2.257 \times 10^{6}} = 6.647 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}
$$

**Step 2 — Total drying area required:**

Converting moisture to be removed per second: $\dot{m}_w = 488.9 / 3600 = 0.1358$ kg/s

$$
A = \frac{\dot{m}_w}{N_c} = \frac{0.1358}{6.647 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
\boxed{A = 20.43 \ \text{m}^2}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion:** Students often forget to convert kg/h → kg/s before using $N_c$ in SI units. This causes a 3600× error!
> 2. **Wet vs Dry basis:** Always confirm whether the given moisture is on dry or wet basis before computing. Use $X = w/(1-w)$ to convert.
> 3. **Forgetting $X^*$ in FRP:** Many students wrongly use $(X_c - X_2)$ instead of the logarithmic term. Marks are deducted for this.
> 4. **Stokes regime validity:** Always verify Reynolds number before applying $u_t = d_p^2 \Delta \rho g / (18\mu)$. If $Re > 0.1$, switch to Newton regime.
> 5. **Fluidization condition:** Most common error — using $u < u_{mf}$ thinking it is "safer." It is NOT. The bed will not fluidize!

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- [ ] **Drying** = simultaneous heat and mass transfer; final moisture removal step.
- [ ] **Bound moisture** depends on the *solid*; **Free moisture** depends on *both solid and gas*.
- [ ] **CRP** — surface wet, rate = constant, controlled by **gas-side** resistance.
- [ ] **FRP** — surface dry, rate falls linearly with free moisture, controlled by **solid-side** diffusion.
- [ ] **Critical moisture content $X_c$** is the *most important* parameter — it separates the two periods.
- [ ] The fundamental batch drying integral: $t = (L_s/A) \int dX/N_A$.
- [ ] CRP time formula: $t_{CRP} = L_s(X_1 - X_c) / (A N_c)$.
- [ ] FRP time formula: $t_{FRP} = [L_s (X_c - X^*) / (A N_c)] \cdot \ln[(X_c - X^*)/(X_2 - X^*)]$.
- [ ] $N_c$ can be expressed via **heat** ($h\Delta T/h_{fg}$) or **mass** ($k_Y \Delta Y$).
- [ ] Dry basis vs Wet basis: $X = w/(1-w)$; $w = X/(1+X)$.
- [ ] **Humidity ratio** $Y = 18 P_w / [29(P - P_w)]$.
- [ ] **Fluidized bed dryer:** velocity range $u_{mf} < u < u_t$; provides highest $h$ and shortest drying time.
- [ ] $u_{mf}$ formula (for $Re < 20$): $d_p^2 (\rho_s - \rho_g) g / (1650 \mu)$.
- [ ] $u_t$ formula: Stokes regime for $Re < 0.1$; switch to Newton regime otherwise.
- [ ] **Saturated humidity** $Y_s$ evaluated at surface temperature (wet-bulb temperature in CRP).
- [ ] For **spray dryers**: short residence time (1–10 s), suitable for heat-sensitive materials.
- [ ] For **tray dryers**: long batch time, used for pastes, slurries, granular solids.
- [ ] For **rotary dryers**: continuous, suitable for large throughput of free-flowing solids.
- [ ] For **fluidized bed dryers**: excellent heat transfer, narrow residence time distribution.
- [ ] Always **draw the drying curve** (rate vs $X$) before attempting any numerical.
- [ ] Always **label moisture content as dry basis** unless explicitly stated otherwise in KTU papers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adsorption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Adsorption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Adsorption** is a surface phenomenon in which molecules of a fluid (gas or liquid) — collectively called the **adsorbate** — accumulate on the surface of a solid (or sometimes liquid) material, known as the **adsorbent**. The actual solid surface where accumulation occurs is termed the **interface**. The reverse process — release of the adsorbed molecules — is called **desorption**.

In the context of **Fluidization Engineering (PECHT415)**, adsorption is critically important because fluidized beds provide excellent solid–fluid contact, dramatically enhancing adsorption kinetics compared to fixed beds. The continuous mixing of adsorbent particles in a fluidized state effectively eliminates external mass-transfer limitations and channeling.

### 1.2 Key Terminology Matrix

| Term | Symbol | Description |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Adsorbate | — | The substance being adsorbed (gas/liquid molecules) |
| Adsorbent | — | The solid surface material that adsorbs |
| Adsorption | — | Accumulation of molecules at the interface |
| Desorption | — | Reverse of adsorption (release from surface) |
| Loading | $q$ | Mass of adsorbate per unit mass of adsorbent |
| Capacity | $q_e$ | Equilibrium loading of adsorbate |
| Saturation | $q_m$ | Maximum monolayer loading capacity |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Imagine a crowded bus stop on a rainy day. People (the **adsorbate molecules**) are looking for shelter. The bus stop wall (the **adsorbent surface**) provides a place for them to stick around. Some people stay close to the wall (monolayer adsorption), while others might climb onto shoulders of others (multilayer adsorption). When the rain stops (desorption condition), people leave one by one.

### 1.4 Physical Adsorption vs Chemical Adsorption (Physisorption vs Chemisorption)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The distinction between **physisorption** and **chemisorption** is a high-weightage question in KTU exams. Memorize the comparative table below.

| Property | Physisorption (Physical) | Chemisorption (Chemical) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Binding force** | Weak **Van der Waals** forces | Strong **chemical (covalent/ionic) bonds** |
| **Enthalpy of adsorption** | Low ($\Delta H \approx 20$–$40$ kJ/mol) | High ($\Delta H \approx 40$–$400$ kJ/mol) |
| **Temperature range** | Low temperatures (below critical) | High temperatures |
| **Reversibility** | Highly reversible | Often irreversible |
| **Specificity** | Non-specific | Highly specific to surface sites |
| **Multilayer formation** | Yes (can form multiple layers) | No (strictly monolayer) |
| **Activation energy** | Negligible | Appreciable activation energy |
| **Surface coverage** | May occur on entire exposed surface | Limited to active sites only |
| **Example** | $N_2$ on activated carbon | $H_2$ on nickel catalyst |

### 1.5 Role of Adsorption in Fluidization Engineering

- **Fluidized Catalytic Cracking (FCC):** Hydrocarbon feed adsorbs onto zeolite catalyst particles.
- **Dry Scrubbing:** $SO_2$ removal using fluidized beds of lime/limestone.
- **Adsorption Cooling:** Using fluidized silica gel/RH (Type A zeolite) beds.
- **Wastewater Treatment:** Fluidized activated carbon beds for organic pollutant removal.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Adsorption Isotherms — Conceptual Foundation

An **adsorption isotherm** is the functional relationship at **constant temperature** between the equilibrium loading ($q_e$) on the adsorbent and the equilibrium concentration ($C_e$) or partial pressure ($P_e$) of the adsorbate in the bulk fluid. For a fixed temperature:

$$q_e = f(C_e) \quad \text{(liquid phase)} \quad \text{or} \quad q_e = f(P_e) \quad \text{(gaseous phase)}$$

### 2.2 Langmuir Isotherm (1918)

**Assumptions of the Langmuir Model:**

1. The adsorbent surface possesses a **finite number of identical adsorption sites**.
2. Each site can hold **only one adsorbate molecule** (monolayer coverage).
3. The heat of adsorption is **uniform** across all sites and independent of surface coverage ($\theta$).
4. No interaction between adjacent adsorbed molecules.
5. Equilibrium is dynamic: rate of adsorption $\leftrightarrow$ rate of desorption.

**Form of the Langmuir Isotherm:**

$$q_e = \frac{q_m K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e}$$

where:
- $q_e$ = equilibrium loading of adsorbate (mg/g or kg/kg)
- $q_m$ = maximum monolayer capacity (mg/g)
- $K_L$ = Langmuir equilibrium constant (L/mg or m³/kg)
- $C_e$ = equilibrium concentration of adsorbate in fluid (mg/L or kg/m³)

**Linearized Forms for Experimental Data Plotting:**

$$\frac{C_e}{q_e} = \frac{1}{q_m K_L} + \frac{C_e}{q_m} \quad \text{(Langmuir - Type I)}$$

$$\frac{1}{q_e} = \frac{1}{q_m} + \frac{1}{q_m K_L C_e} \quad \text{(Langmuir - Type II)}$$

A plot of $C_e / q_e$ versus $C_e$ gives a straight line with slope $1/q_m$ and intercept $1/(q_m K_L)$.

### 2.3 Separation Factor / Equilibrium Parameter ($R_L$)

The dimensionless **separation factor** (Hall's parameter) characterizes the Langmuir isotherm shape:

$$R_L = \frac{1}{1 + K_L C_0}$$

where $C_0$ is the **highest initial fluid-phase concentration**.

| $R_L$ Value | Isotherm Shape | Favourability |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| $R_L > 1$ | — | **Unfavourable** |
| $R_L = 1$ | Linear | — |
| $0 < R_L < 1$ | — | **Favourable** |
| $R_L = 0$ | Irreversible | **Highly Favourable** |

### 2.4 Freundlich Isotherm (Empirical)

Applies to **heterogeneous surfaces** (non-uniform adsorption energies) and is valid over a **limited concentration range**:

$$q_e = K_F \cdot C_e^{1/n}$$

where $K_F$ and $n$ are empirical constants; $1/n$ is the **heterogeneity factor** ($0 < 1/n < 1$ for favourable adsorption).

**Linearized Form:**

$$\ln q_e = \ln K_F + \frac{1}{n} \ln C_e$$

A plot of $\ln q_e$ versus $\ln C_e$ gives a straight line of slope $1/n$ and intercept $\ln K_F$.

### 2.5 BET Isotherm (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller, 1938)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** BET extends Langmuir's theory to **multilayer adsorption** and is the standard for measuring **specific surface area** of porous adsorbents (e.g., activated carbon, silica gel) used in fluidized beds.

$$\frac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \frac{1}{V_m C} + \frac{(C - 1)}{V_m C} \cdot \frac{P}{P_0}$$

where:
- $P$ = equilibrium partial pressure of adsorbate
- $P_0$ = saturation vapor pressure
- $V$ = volume of gas adsorbed at STP
- $V_m$ = volume of gas required for monolayer coverage
- $C$ = BET constant (related to heat of adsorption)

A plot of $\dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)}$ versus $P/P_0$ yields $V_m$ from slope and intercept. The **specific surface area** $S_{BET}$ is then computed.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Expression | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Langmuir** | $q_e = \dfrac{q_m K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e}$ | Monolayer on uniform surface |
| **Freundlich** | $q_e = K_F C_e^{1/n}$ | Heterogeneous surface (empirical) |
| **BET** | $\dfrac{P}{V(P_0-P)} = \dfrac{1}{V_m C} + \dfrac{(C-1)}{V_m C}\cdot \dfrac{P}{P_0}$ | Multilayer & surface area |
| **Separation factor** | $R_L = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_L C_0}$ | Favourability check |
| **Surface area (BET)** | $S_{BET} = \dfrac{V_m N_A A_m}{M_v W}$ | Specific surface area |
| **Single-stage mass balance** | $W(q_e - q_0) = V(C_0 - C_e)$ | Adsorber design |
| **Slater–Pellet rate (approx)** | $\dfrac{dq}{dt} = k_s (q^* - q)$ | LDF model |
| **Polanyi potential** | $\varepsilon = RT \ln(P_0/P)$ | Polanyi adsorption potential |
| **Temkin isotherm** | $q_e = B_T \ln(K_T C_e)$ | Account for adsorbate–adsorbate interaction |
| **Dubinin–Radushkevich** | $\ln q_e = \ln q_m - \beta \varepsilon^2$ | Microporous adsorbents |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** The Langmuir isotherm dominates reactor design of FCC units. The BET isotherm is the universal standard for catalyst surface area characterization. The Freundlich isotherm is commonly used in environmental engineering for activated sludge and wastewater adsorbents.

### 2.7 Practical Engineering Utility

- **Catalyst Selection:** Surface area determines reactivity in fluidized catalytic reactors.
- **Pollution Control:** Fluidized activated carbon for VOC and $SO_2$ capture.
- **Process Design:** Adsorption isotherms provide $q^*$ (equilibrium loading) needed for rate equations and column sizing.
- **Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA):** Cycles of adsorption and desorption at varying pressures.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Langmuir Isotherm from Kinetic Theory

The Langmuir equation can be rigorously derived from first principles by equating **adsorption rate** with **desorption rate** at equilibrium.

**Step 1 — Define fractional surface coverage:**
Let $\theta$ be the fraction of surface covered, and $(1 - \theta)$ the fraction bare.

$$\theta = \frac{q_e}{q_m}$$

**Step 2 — Rate of adsorption (forward process):**
Adsorption rate $\propto$ (bare surface fraction) $\times$ (fluid concentration).

$$r_{ads} = k_a \, (1 - \theta) \, C_e$$

where $k_a$ is the adsorption rate constant (L·mg⁻¹·min⁻¹).

**Step 3 — Rate of desorption (reverse process):**
Desorption rate $\propto$ (covered surface fraction).

$$r_{des} = k_d \, \theta$$

where $k_d$ is the desorption rate constant (min⁻¹).

**Step 4 — Apply dynamic equilibrium condition:**
At equilibrium, $r_{ads} = r_{des}$:

$$k_a \, (1 - \theta) \, C_e = k_d \, \theta$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\theta$:**

$$k_a C_e - k_a \theta C_e = k_d \theta$$

$$k_a C_e = \theta \, (k_d + k_a C_e)$$

$$\theta = \frac{k_a C_e}{k_d + k_a C_e} = \frac{(k_a / k_d) \, C_e}{1 + (k_a / k_d) \, C_e}$$

**Step 6 — Define Langmuir constant and substitute $q_e = q_m \theta$:**

$$K_L \equiv \frac{k_a}{k_d}$$

$$\boxed{q_e = \frac{q_m K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e}}$$

This is the canonical Langmuir isotherm. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation II: Material Balance for a Single-Stage Adsorber

A continuous-flow single-stage adsorber operates on a simple **input–output mass balance** principle.

**Given:**
- $C_0$ = inlet fluid concentration (kg adsorbate / m³ fluid)
- $C_e$ = outlet fluid concentration at equilibrium (kg/m³)
- $q_0$ = initial loading on adsorbent (kg adsorbate / kg solid) — assumed zero for fresh adsorbent
- $q_e$ = equilibrium loading (kg/kg)
- $V$ = volumetric flow rate of fluid (m³/s) or total fluid volume (m³)
- $\dot{m}_s$ = mass flow rate of solid (kg/s) or total solid mass $W$ (kg)

**Step 1 — Draw the control volume:**
A single-stage contactor where solid and fluid mix and reach equilibrium.

**Step 2 — Total adsorbate balance:**
Accumulation in solid phase = Removal from fluid phase.

$$W \, (q_e - q_0) = V \, (C_0 - C_e)$$

**Step 3 — For fresh adsorbent, $q_0 = 0$:**

$$\boxed{W \, q_e = V \, (C_0 - C_e)}$$

**Step 4 — Solids-to-fluid ratio:**
The required ratio of adsorbent mass to fluid volume (the **dosage**) is:

$$\frac{W}{V} = \frac{C_0 - C_e}{q_e}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting Langmuir isotherm for $q_e$:**

$$\frac{W}{V} = \frac{(C_0 - C_e)(1 + K_L C_e)}{q_m K_L C_e}$$

This equation can be solved directly for design — given a target $C_e$, one calculates the adsorbent dosage $W/V$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A wastewater stream contains $C_0 = 0.05$ kg/m³ of an organic pollutant. It is to be treated with activated carbon whose Langmuir parameters are $q_m = 0.20$ kg/kg and $K_L = 50$ m³/kg. The target outlet concentration is $C_e = 0.005$ kg/m³. Determine the **required adsorbent-to-fluid mass ratio** and the **separation factor**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the equilibrium loading $q_e$ from Langmuir:**

$$q_e = \frac{q_m K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e} = \frac{(0.20)(50)(0.005)}{1 + (50)(0.005)}$$

$$q_e = \frac{0.05}{1 + 0.25} = \frac{0.05}{1.25} = 0.04 \;\text{kg/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the dosage:**

$$\frac{W}{V} = \frac{C_0 - C_e}{q_e} = \frac{0.05 - 0.005}{0.04} = \frac{0.045}{0.04}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{W}{V} = 1.125 \;\text{kg adsorbent per m}^{3}\text{ of wastewater}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the separation factor $R_L$:**

$$R_L = \frac{1}{1 + K_L C_0} = \frac{1}{1 + (50)(0.05)} = \frac{1}{3.5}$$

$$\boxed{R_L \approx 0.286}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
Since $0 < R_L < 1$, the adsorption is **favourable** — physically, the pollutant strongly prefers the solid surface over the liquid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Insight:** Notice that the units of $K_L$ must always be the **inverse of the units of concentration** for dimensional consistency in the Langmuir expression.

### 4.4 Schematic Description (Textual Rendering)

For clarity, the **fluidized-bed adsorber** is composed of the following functional blocks in vertical sequence:

1. **Plenum Chamber:** Receives fluidizing gas from the blower; ensures uniform distribution.
2. **Distributor Plate:** Critical element that determines fluidization quality (pressure drop $\approx 0.05$–$0.10$ of bed weight per unit area).
3. **Fluidized Bed Zone:** Where adsorbent particles are suspended; intense solid–fluid contact enables rapid approach to equilibrium $q^*$.
4. **Freeboard / Elutriation Zone:** Above the dense bed; reduces entrainment of fines.
5. **Internal Cyclones:** Capture entrained adsorbent and return it via diplegs.
6. **Solid Withdrawal Line:** Removes loaded adsorbent continuously for regeneration.
7. **Regenerator:** Stripping column where desorption occurs (steam or hot gas).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Fluidization?** Because fluidization eliminates external film resistance and ensures **isothermal** conditions throughout the bed — the isotherm $q^*(C)$ directly governs local equilibrium, simplifying design calculations dramatically compared to fixed beds (where breakthrough curves must be solved).

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Differentiate between physisorption and chemisorption.** Mention any **four** distinguishing characteristics.

**Model Answer:**

Physisorption and chemisorption are two fundamentally different mechanisms by which molecules adhere to a solid surface:

| Aspect | Physisorption | Chemisorption |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. **Nature of force** | Weak Van der Waals forces | Strong chemical (covalent/ionic) bonds |
| 2. **Enthalpy change** | Low, $20$–$40$ kJ/mol | High, $40$–$400$ kJ/mol |
| 3. **Specificity** | Non-specific | Highly specific to surface sites |
| 4. **Layer formation** | Multimolecular layers possible | Strictly unimolecular (monolayer) |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Define the term "adsorption isotherm."** State the **Langmuir adsorption isotherm** with its assumptions.

**Model Answer:**

An **adsorption isotherm** is the mathematical relationship, at constant temperature, between the **equilibrium loading** ($q_e$) of an adsorbate on the adsorbent surface and the **equilibrium concentration** ($C_e$) or pressure ($P_e$) of the adsorbate in the surrounding fluid.

The **Langmuir isotherm** is given by:

$$q_e = \frac{q_m K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Surface has a fixed number of identical adsorption sites.
2. Each site holds only one molecule (monolayer).
3. Heat of adsorption is independent of surface coverage.
4. No interaction between adsorbed molecules.
5. Dynamic equilibrium between adsorption and desorption.

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the **Langmuir adsorption isotherm** starting from kinetic considerations. Clearly state all assumptions. Discuss the physical significance of each term in the resulting equation. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A gas mixture containing $C_0 = 0.20$ kg/m³ of an organic vapor is to be treated in a fluidized-bed adsorber using activated carbon whose Langmuir parameters are $q_m = 0.50$ kg/kg and $K_L = 30$ m³/kg. The target outlet concentration is $C_e = 0.01$ kg/m³. Compute:
- (i) The equilibrium loading $q_e$. **[2 Marks]**
- (ii) The required adsorbent dosage $W/V$. **[2 Marks]**
- (iii) The separation factor $R_L$ and comment on the favourability of adsorption. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

The Langmuir isotherm is derived by equating the rates of adsorption and desorption at equilibrium on a uniform surface.

Define $\theta$ = fractional surface coverage, so that the bare fraction is $(1 - \theta)$.

**Adsorption rate** is proportional to the bare surface fraction and the bulk concentration:

$$r_{ads} = k_a \, (1 - \theta) \, C_e$$

**Desorption rate** is proportional to the covered surface fraction:

$$r_{des} = k_d \, \theta$$

**Equilibrium condition** requires $r_{ads} = r_{des}$:

$$k_a (1 - \theta) C_e = k_d \theta$$

Expanding and solving for $\theta$:

$$k_a C_e - k_a C_e \theta = k_d \theta$$

$$k_a C_e = \theta \, (k_d + k_a C_e)$$

$$\theta = \frac{k_a C_e}{k_d + k_a C_e} = \frac{(k_a/k_d) C_e}{1 + (k_a/k_d) C_e}$$

Defining the Langmuir constant $K_L = k_a / k_d$ and substituting $q_e = q_m \theta$:

$$q_e = \frac{q_m K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e}$$

**Significance of terms:**
- $q_m$ = maximum monolayer capacity (surface saturation limit).
- $K_L$ = ratio of adsorption-to-desorption rate constants; reflects affinity.
- $C_e$ = bulk-phase concentration at equilibrium.
- As $C_e \to \infty$, $q_e \to q_m$ (saturation).
- As $C_e \to 0$, $q_e \approx q_m K_L C_e$ (Henry's law linear region).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Equilibrium loading:**

$$q_e = \frac{q_m K_L C_e}{1 + K_L C_e} = \frac{(0.50)(30)(0.01)}{1 + (30)(0.01)}$$

$$q_e = \frac{0.15}{1.30} = 0.1154 \;\text{kg/kg}$$

**(ii) Adsorbent dosage** (assuming fresh adsorbent, $q_0 = 0$):

$$\frac{W}{V} = \frac{C_0 - C_e}{q_e} = \frac{0.20 - 0.01}{0.1154} = \frac{0.19}{0.1154} \approx 1.647 \;\text{kg/m}^{3}$$

**(iii) Separation factor:**

$$R_L = \frac{1}{1 + K_L C_0} = \frac{1}{1 + (30)(0.20)} = \frac{1}{7.0} \approx 0.143$$

Since $0 < R_L < 1$, the adsorption is **favourable** — the organic vapor has strong affinity for the activated carbon surface.

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the working of a **fluidized-bed adsorber**. Explain why fluidization enhances adsorption kinetics compared to a fixed bed. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** The **BET isotherm** extends Langmuir's monolayer theory to multilayer adsorption. State the BET equation and explain the role of the constant $C$. A catalyst sample is found to adsorb $V = 90$ cm³ (at STP) of $N_2$ at relative pressure $P/P_0 = 0.30$ and $V = 130$ cm³ at $P/P_0 = 0.50$. Compute the monolayer volume $V_m$ and the BET constant $C$ given the BET equation. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Fluidized Bed Adsorber:**

A fluidized-bed adsorber consists of a vertical column with a **gas distributor** at the bottom, a **bed of solid adsorbent particles** above it, and **internal cyclones** in the freeboard region.

**Working Principle:** The fluidizing gas (or liquid) is introduced through the distributor at a velocity exceeding the **minimum fluidization velocity** ($U_{mf}$). This suspends the adsorbent particles, creating an intimate contact between the fluid and the solid surface. The adsorbate molecules diffuse rapidly to the solid particles, and equilibrium loading $q^*$ is approached. The loaded adsorbent is withdrawn from the bottom and sent to a regenerator; fresh adsorbent is added at the top (or top-side).

**Reasons Fluidization Enhances Adsorption Kinetics:**

1. **Elimination of External Film Resistance:** Continuous particle motion thins the boundary layer, increasing the external mass transfer coefficient $k_f$.
2. **Isothermal Operation:** Vigorous mixing ensures uniform temperature, avoiding hot spots.
3. **Uniform Concentration Profile:** Eliminates channeling and bypass flows common in fixed beds.
4. **High Interfacial Area:** All particles are fully exposed; no dead zones.
5. **Continuous Regeneration Capability:** Solids can be circulated between adsorber and regenerator without bed shut-down.

**(b) BET Isotherm:**

The BET equation is:

$$\frac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \frac{1}{V_m C} + \frac{(C - 1)}{V_m C} \cdot \frac{P}{P_0}$$

The constant $C$ is related to the heat of adsorption:

$$C \propto \exp\left(\frac{E_1 - E_L}{RT}\right)$$

where $E_1$ is the heat of adsorption of the first layer and $E_L$ is the heat of liquefaction. A high $C$ indicates strong adsorbate–adsorbent interaction.

**Numerical Calculation:**

Define $y = \dfrac{P}{V(P_0 - P)}$ and $x = \dfrac{P}{P_0}$.

**Data Point 1:** $x_1 = 0.30$, $V_1 = 90$ cm³

$$y_1 = \frac{0.30}{90(1 - 0.30)} = \frac{0.30}{90 \times 0.70} = \frac{0.30}{63} \approx 0.004762 \;\text{cm}^{-3}$$

**Data Point 2:** $x_2 = 0.50$, $V_2 = 130$ cm³

$$y_2 = \frac{0.50}{130(1 - 0.50)} = \frac{0.50}{130 \times 0.50} = \frac{0.50}{65} \approx 0.007692 \;\text{cm}^{-3}$$

**Slope and intercept** (linear equation $y = mx + b$):

$$m = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \frac{0.007692 - 0.004762}{0.50 - 0.30} = \frac{0.002930}{0.20} \approx 0.01465 \;\text{cm}^{-3}$$

$$b = y_1 - m x_1 = 0.004762 - (0.01465)(0.30) = 0.004762 - 0.004395 = 0.000367 \;\text{cm}^{-3}$$

**Now solve the BET system:**
- Slope $m = (C - 1)/(V_m C)$
- Intercept $b = 1/(V_m C)$

Adding: $m + b = (C - 1 + 1)/(V_m C) = C/(V_m C) = 1/V_m$

$$\boxed{V_m = \frac{1}{m + b} = \frac{1}{0.01465 + 0.000367} = \frac{1}{0.01502} \approx 66.6 \;\text{cm}^{3}}$$

Computing $C$ from the intercept:

$$\boxed{C = \frac{1}{b \, V_m} = \frac{1}{(0.000367)(66.6)} \approx 40.9}$$

A high $C$ value ($\approx 40.9$) indicates that the adsorbate–adsorbent interaction is significantly stronger than the adsorbate–adsorbate interaction in subsequent layers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Units are critical:** Many students lose 1–2 marks by omitting units in $q_e$, $K_L$, $R_L$ answers. Always write units explicitly.
> - **Linearization trap:** When asked to "verify Langmuir isotherm," you must specify which linearized form (Type I or Type II) you are using and plot $C_e/q_e$ vs. $C_e$.
> - **Misinterpretation of $R_L$:** A common mistake is reporting $R_L > 1$ as "favourable." Always remember $0 < R_L < 1$ indicates favourability.
> - **BET validity:** The BET equation is only valid in the range $0.05 < P/P_0 < 0.35$ — beyond this, capillary condensation invalidates the model.
> - **Solid–Fluid Mass Balance:** Students often forget to set $q_0 = 0$ for fresh adsorbent in single-stage calculations — a 2-mark deduction.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam Preparation:**

- [ ] **Adsorption** = surface accumulation; **desorption** = release. **Adsorbate** = substance adsorbed; **adsorbent** = solid surface.
- [ ] **Physisorption**: low $\Delta H$, multilayer, reversible, non-specific. **Chemisorption**: high $\Delta H$, monolayer, often irreversible, specific.
- [ ] **Langmuir** equation: $q_e = q_m K_L C_e / (1 + K_L C_e)$ — for uniform monolayer.
- [ ] **Freundlich** equation: $q_e = K_F C_e^{1/n}$ — empirical, for heterogeneous surfaces.
- [ ] **BET** equation extends Langmuir to multilayer; valid for $0.05 < P/P_0 < 0.35$; used to compute specific surface area.
- [ ] **Separation factor** $R_L = 1/(1 + K_L C_0)$. Favourability: $0 < R_L < 1$.
- [ ] **Single-stage mass balance:** $W q_e = V(C_0 - C_e)$ (fresh adsorbent).
- [ ] **Adsorbent dosage:** $W/V = (C_0 - C_e)/q_e$.
- [ ] **Fluidization advantage:** eliminates external film resistance, ensures isothermal operation, provides high interfacial area.
- [ ] **Surface coverage** $\theta = q_e / q_m$. As $C_e \to \infty$, $\theta \to 1$; as $C_e \to 0$, linear (Henry's law) regime.
- [ ] **Industrial applications:** FCC (fluidized catalytic cracking), $SO_2$ scrubbing, VOC abatement, PSA systems, fluidized-bed dryers.
- [ ] **Standard enthalpy range** to remember: Physisorption $20$–$40$ kJ/mol; Chemisorption $40$–$400$ kJ/mol.
- [ ] **Typical BET constant $C$** for strong adsorption: $C > 50$; weak: $C \approx 1$.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 4 Topic Notes — Adsorption**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Examination Pattern (NEP 2020 Aligned)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synthesis Reaction: Fischer - Tropsch Synthesis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Synthesis Reaction: Fischer - Tropsch Synthesis # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Fischer–Tropsch Synthesis (FTS)** is a **heterogeneous catalytic polymerization reaction** in which a mixture of **synthesis gas (syngas)** — predominantly **carbon monoxide (CO)** and **hydrogen (H₂)** — is converted over a solid transition-metal catalyst (typically **Fe**, **Co**, or **Ru**) into a wide spectrum of liquid and gaseous hydrocarbons, oxygenates, and water, following the general stoichiometric family: $$n\,\text{CO} + (2n+1)\,\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \text{(paraffin pathway)}$$ Within the framework of **Fluidization Engineering (PECHT415 – Module 4)**, FTS is studied as the **canonical industrial application of catalytic fluidized-bed reactors**, where excellent **heat transfer**, **isothermal operation**, and **catalyst regeneration capability** are exploited to manage the **strongly exothermic** reaction (ΔH₂₉₈ ≈ **–165 kJ/mol CO** for paraffins). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syngas Ratio Definition:** The molar ratio **H₂/CO** in the feed is termed the *syngas ratio* or *module*. For the paraffin family it asymptotically approaches **2.0** as chain length n → ∞. Typical industrial fluidized-bed FTS operates in the range **0.6 – 2.0** depending on the catalyst (Fe catalysts tolerate lower ratios because of in-situ WGS). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine you are building **"molecular Lego"** from scratch, but you don't have the right bricks. You are given only two simple blocks — **CO** (one carbon glued to one oxygen) and **H₂** (two hydrogens held together). You need to glue these tiny blocks one-by-one onto a growing chain, while continuously removing the "leftover" oxygen as water vapor. That is exactly what an FTS catalyst does: - A **CO molecule lands** on an active metal site → the C–O bond stretches and breaks. - The carbon atom **docks onto a growing hydrocarbon chain** (one carbon longer). - The oxygen is **swept away as H₂O** using hydrogen atoms. - The chain **keeps growing** until it either desorbs (becomes a product molecule) or gets terminated as paraffin/olefin/alcohol. **Real-world analogy:** Think of a **baker rolling dough into breadsticks of different lengths**. Every time he passes his hand over the dough, there's a chance (the *chain growth probability α*) the breadstick gets longer, and a chance (*1 – α*) it gets cut and sold. The same breadstick is the hydrocarbon — sometimes short (methane, LPG), sometimes medium (naphtha/diesel), sometimes very long (waxes). > [!NOTE] > **Key Industrial Analogy:** The **Sasol Synthol Circulating Fluidized Bed (CFB) Reactor** in South Africa, the **Sasol Slurry Phase Distillate™ Process**, and **Shell's Pearl GTL** in Qatar are the three flagship commercial FTS technologies — all of which are built on fluidization principles covered in PECHT415. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in FTS | Property | Typical Value (Industrial FTS) | |---|---| | Reaction Temperature (T) | **493 – 613 K (220 – 340 °C)** | | Reaction Pressure (P) | **10 – 40 bar** (Co-catalyst) / **10 – 30 bar** (Fe-catalyst) | | H₂/CO feed ratio | **0.6 – 2.1** | | Catalyst | **Fe, Co, Ru** (supported on Al₂O₃, SiO₂, TiO₂) | | Space velocity (GHSV) | **1 000 – 10 000 h⁻¹** | | ΔH (overall) | **–165 kJ/mol CO** converted | | Chain growth probability (α) | **0.70 – 0.95** | > [!NOTE] > FTS is **the most exothermic major industrial heterogeneous reaction** encountered in chemical engineering practice — comparable only to ammonia oxidation and partial oxidation of methane. This is precisely why **fluidized beds** (with their enormous heat-transfer coefficients, h ≈ 200–600 W/m²K) are the *only* practical reactor choice at commercial scale. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Reaction Chemistry — Primary and Side Reactions FTS is not a *single* reaction but a **family of parallel–consecutive reactions** governed by polymerization-type kinetics. ### 2.1.1 Primary (Desired) Reactions **(a) Paraffin formation (alkanes):** $$(2n+1)\,\text{H}_2 + n\,\text{CO} \longrightarrow \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **(b) Olefin formation (alkenes):** $$2n\,\text{H}_2 + n\,\text{CO} \longrightarrow \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n} + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **(c) Alcohol formation (oxygenates):** $$2n\,\text{H}_2 + n\,\text{CO} \longrightarrow \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+1}\text{OH} + (n-1)\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ ### 2.1.2 Secondary (Side) Reactions **(d) Water–Gas Shift (WGS) — important on Fe catalysts:** $$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H_{298} = -41\,\text{kJ/mol}$$ **(e) Boudouard Disproportionation — carbon deposition:** $$2\,\text{CO} \rightleftharpoons \text{C}_{(s)} + \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H_{298} = -172\,\text{kJ/mol}$$ **(f) Methanation (undesired at high T):** $$\text{CO} + 3\,\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ --- ## 2.2 Why and How — Structured Logical Breakdown 1. **Why FTS exists:** Petroleum (crude oil) is geographically and politically constrained. **Coal, natural gas, and biomass** can all be converted to syngas via gasification/reforming, and FTS unlocks a route to **liquid fuels from non-petroleum carbon sources** — the famous **Gas-to-Liquids (GTL)**, **Coal-to-Liquids (CTL)**, and **Biomass-to-Liquids (BTL)** pathways. 2. **How chain growth happens:** Catalytic surface polymerization — the **Anderson–Schulz–Flory (ASF) mechanism** — gives a statistical product distribution governed by a single parameter **α (chain growth probability)**. 3. **Why fluidized beds:** Three engineering reasons: - **Heat removal** (ΔH ≈ –165 kJ/mol, with thousands of kmol/h converted → MW-scale heat duty). - **Catalyst online regeneration** (coke-burning in a separate regenerator loop). - **Scalability** of CFB to >10 000 tonnes/day single-train capacity. 4. **How selectivity is controlled:** By tuning **T, P, H₂/CO, catalyst, α**, and reactor type. Lower T and higher P favour heavier products (waxes, diesel). --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination Ready) | # | Equation / Parameter | LaTeX Form | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | ASF mass-fraction distribution | $W_n/n \;=\; (1-\alpha)^2\,\alpha^{\,(n-1)}$ | Fraction of total carbon in product of carbon number n | | 2 | ASF mole-fraction distribution | $x_n \;=\; (1-\alpha)\,\alpha^{\,(n-1)}$ | Mole fraction of Cₙ product | | 3 | Chain growth probability | $\alpha \;=\; \dfrac{r_p}{r_p + r_t}$ | Ratio of propagation to (propagation + termination) rates | | 4 | CO conversion | $X_{CO} \;=\; \dfrac{F_{CO,in} - F_{CO,out}}{F_{CO,in}}$ | Fractional conversion of CO in reactor | | 5 | Syngas (module) ratio | $M \;=\; \dfrac{F_{H_2,in}}{F_{CO,in}}$ | Inlet H₂/CO molar ratio | | 6 | Stoichiometric H₂/CO for paraffin Cₙ | $(2n+1)/n$ | Tends to 2 as n → ∞ | | 7 | Hydrogen selectivity ratio | $S_{H_2} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{H}_2\,\text{used}}{\text{CO}\,\text{used}}$ | Actual H₂/CO consumption ratio | | 8 | Carbon selectivity to Cₙ | $S_{C_n} \;=\; \dfrac{n\,F_{C_n,out}}{\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k\,F_{C_k,out}}$ | Fraction of converted C going to Cₙ | | 9 | Overall carbon balance | $F_{C,in} \;=\; F_{C,out} + F_{C,deposit}$ | Includes coke and unconverted CO | | 10 | Reaction enthalpy (paraffin) | $\Delta H_{298} \approx -165\,\text{kJ/mol CO}$ | Heat to be removed per mole of CO reacted | | 11 | Catalyst deactivation rate | $-r_d \;=\; k_d\,C_c^{m}$ | Empirical; m = 1–2 typically | | 12 | Space velocity | $GHSV \;=\; \dfrac{F_{syngas}}{V_{cat}}\;\;[h^{-1}]$ | Gas hourly space velocity | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary condition for α:** $0 < \alpha < 1$. If **α → 0**, only methane forms (max methane selectivity). If **α → 1**, only heavy wax forms. The maximum C₂ selectivity is bounded at ≈ **0.33** (mole basis) — a hard physical limit of the ASF distribution, and a common viva/quiz question. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Energy security:** Countries with abundant coal/natural gas (South Africa — Sasol; Qatar — Shell Pearl GTL; China; India) use FTS for **strategic fuel independence**. - **Ultra-clean diesel:** FTS diesel has **near-zero sulfur** and **high cetane number (≈ 70)** — a premium blendstock. - **Wax production:** Hard waxes (α ≈ 0.95) feed specialty chemicals, lubricants, and Fischer–Tropsch-derived drilling fluids. - **Carbon capture integration:** "Green" FTS using **renewable H₂ + captured CO₂-derived CO** is an emerging route to e-fuels (Power-to-Liquids). - **Reactor design exam relevance:** FTS is the *only* reaction in the KTU syllabus where the student must simultaneously use **fluidization correlations + heterogeneous catalysis + ASF statistics + heat removal design** — it is a marquee problem for 14-mark questions. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Anderson–Schulz–Flory (ASF) Distribution ### 3.1.1 Mechanistic Premise (Start) Each growing surface species (denoted Cₙ*) has two competing events: 1. **Propagation:** adds one C-unit, rate $r_p$ (units: mol/(g_cat·s)). 2. **Termination:** desorbs as product, rate $r_t$ (units: mol/(g_cat·s)). Define the **chain growth probability** α as the probability that, at any given step, propagation occurs: $$\alpha \;=\; \frac{r_p}{r_p + r_t} \quad\quad\quad (1)$$ The probability of termination at that step is therefore: $$1-\alpha \;=\; \frac{r_t}{r_p + r_t} \quad\quad\quad (2)$$ ### 3.1.2 Probability of Forming a Cₙ Product (Single Chain History) For a chain of exactly *n* carbons to be released, the catalyst must: - Make **(n – 1) propagation steps** in a row. - Make **1 termination step** (the nᵗʰ event). Assuming steps are statistically independent (Markov chain), the probability is: $$P(n) \;=\; \alpha^{\,n-1}\,(1-\alpha) \quad\quad\quad (3)$$ ### 3.1.3 Normalization Check (Why mole fraction = P(n)) Because the sum of probabilities of *all* possible chain lengths (n = 1, 2, 3, ... ∞) must equal unity: $$\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} P(n) \;=\; (1-\alpha)\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \alpha^{\,n-1}$$ This is a geometric series with first term (1–α) and common ratio α. Since $\vert \alpha \vert < 1$: $$\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \alpha^{\,n-1} \;=\; \frac{1}{1-\alpha}$$ Therefore: $$\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} P(n) \;=\; (1-\alpha)\cdot \frac{1}{1-\alpha} \;=\; 1 \;\;\checkmark \quad\quad\quad (4)$$ Hence, the **mole fraction** of Cₙ in the total hydrocarbon product is: $$\boxed{\,x_n \;=\; (1-\alpha)\,\alpha^{\,n-1}\,} \quad\quad\quad (5)$$ ### 3.1.4 Conversion to Mass Fraction (W_n/n) The mole fraction $x_n$ counts molecules. To get **mass fraction**, we multiply by molecular weight $M_n$: $$W_n \;=\; \frac{x_n\,M_n}{\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} x_k\,M_k} \quad\quad\quad (6)$$ For paraffins, $M_n \approx 14n + 2 \approx 14n$ (per carbon), so $M_n/n \approx 14$ (a constant). This simplification yields the canonical **ASF plot form**: $$\boxed{\,\dfrac{W_n}{n} \;=\; (1-\alpha)^2\,\alpha^{\,n-1}\,} \quad\quad\quad (7)$$ A **log(W_n/n) vs n** plot is a **straight line** of slope $\ln\alpha$ and intercept $\ln\bigl[(1-\alpha)^2\bigr]$ — the experimental fingerprint used to extract α from FTS plant data. > [!IMPORTANT] > The factor (1–α)² appears *only* in the **mass fraction** form because the denominator (sum of $k\,x_k$) itself contains a (1–α) factor. The mole-fraction form (Eq. 5) has only (1–α)¹. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Stoichiometric H₂/CO Ratio for a Mixed Product Consider a feed that is split among paraffins of carbon number n with mole-fraction selectivity $S_{C_n}^{mol}$. The **moles of H₂ consumed** per mole of CO consumed is: $$\left(\frac{H_2}{CO}\right)_{stoich} \;=\; \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} x_n\,\frac{2n+1}{n} \quad\quad\quad (8)$$ Substituting the ASF distribution $x_n = (1-\alpha)\alpha^{n-1}$: $$\left(\frac{H_2}{CO}\right)_{stoich} \;=\; (1-\alpha)\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \alpha^{n-1}\left(2 + \frac{1}{n}\right)$$ Split the sum into two parts: $$= (1-\alpha)\left[2\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\alpha^{n-1} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{\alpha^{n-1}}{n}\right]$$ The first sum is the geometric series: $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\alpha^{n-1} = \dfrac{1}{1-\alpha}$. The second sum is the well-known logarithmic series: $$\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{\alpha^{n-1}}{n} \;=\; -\frac{\ln(1-\alpha)}{\alpha}$$ Combining and simplifying: $$\left(\frac{H_2}{CO}\right)_{stoich} \;=\; 2 - (1-\alpha)\,\frac{\ln(1-\alpha)}{\alpha} \quad\quad\quad (9)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Check boundary cases:** > - α → 0 (methane only, n = 1): $\bigl(H_2/CO\bigr)_{stoich} = 3$. ✓ (matches CH₄ stoichiometry: CO + 3H₂ → CH₄ + H₂O). > - α → 1 (waxes, n → ∞): $\bigl(H_2/CO\bigr)_{stoich} \to 2$. ✓ (paraffin asymptote). This single closed-form expression (Eq. 9) is **a KTU viva favourite** because it neatly couples product distribution (α) with feed requirements (syngas ratio). --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Mass Balance for an FTS Fluidized-Bed Reactor **Given:** A pilot FTS fluidized-bed reactor receives $F_{syngas,in} = 1000$ kmol/h with syngas ratio M = 2.0 (i.e. 666.67 kmol/h H₂ and 333.33 kmol/h CO). CO conversion $X_{CO} = 0.80$ and ASF chain-growth probability α = 0.70. Assume **paraffin-only** selectivity. Compute (a) the per-component outlet flow, (b) overall product distribution, (c) the heat duty to be removed. ### Step 1 — Outlet Flow of Unreacted Syngas $$F_{CO,out} \;=\; F_{CO,in}\,(1-X_{CO}) \;=\; 333.33 \times 0.20 \;=\; 66.67\;\text{kmol/h}$$ For H₂, first compute stoichiometric H₂ consumption. Using Eq. 9 with α = 0.70: $$\left(\frac{H_2}{CO}\right)_{stoich} \;=\; 2 - (1-0.70)\frac{\ln(1-0.70)}{0.70} \;=\; 2 - 0.30\cdot\frac{\ln(0.30)}{0.70}$$ $$\ln(0.30) \;=\; -1.2040 \;\Rightarrow\; 0.30 \times \frac{-1.2040}{0.70} \;=\; -0.5160$$ Therefore: $$\left(\frac{H_2}{CO}\right)_{stoich} \;=\; 2 - (-0.5160) \;=\; 2.516$$ > H₂ consumed = $333.33 \times 0.80 \times 2.516 = 670.93$ kmol/h. But inlet H₂ is only 666.67 kmol/h — slightly hydrogen-deficient. For examination clarity, we report the **theoretical** consumption; in real practice this would mean **H₂ is the limiting reactant** and conversion must be reduced or feed ratio increased. $$F_{H_2,out} \;=\; 666.67 - 666.67 = 0.00\;\text{kmol/h (hypothetical)}$$ ### Step 2 — Moles of Carbon Converted → Total Product Moles CO reacted = $333.33 \times 0.80 = 266.67$ kmol/h. Each reacted CO contributes **one carbon atom** to products. The total number of carbon atoms entering the hydrocarbon pool is therefore **266.67 kmol C/h**. ### Step 3 — ASF Mass-Fraction Distribution (Eq. 7) Compute $W_n/n$ for n = 1 to 6 using $\alpha = 0.70$: | n | $\alpha^{n-1}$ | $W_n/n = (1-\alpha)^2\alpha^{n-1}$ | $W_n$ (mass fraction) | Cₙ product | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 1.0000 | 0.0900 | 0.0900 | CH₄ | | 2 | 0.7000 | 0.0630 | 0.1260 | C₂H₆ | | 3 | 0.4900 | 0.0441 | 0.1323 | C₃H₈ | | 4 | 0.3430 | 0.0309 | 0.1235 | C₄H₁₀ | | 5 | 0.2401 | 0.0216 | 0.1081 | C₅H₁₂ | | 6 | 0.1681 | 0.0151 | 0.0907 | C₆H₁₄ | | ≥7 (C₅⁺) | remainder | — | ≈ 0.3294 | heavier | > **Sanity check:** $\sum W_n \approx 0.090+0.126+0.132+0.124+0.108+0.091+0.329 = 1.00$ ✓ ### Step 4 — Outlet Molar Flows of Each Product (Mass × Carbon Throughput) Total product mass rate ∝ 266.67 kmol C/h. The mass-flow rate of Cₙ product is: $$F_{C_n} \;=\; W_n \times \frac{F_{C,total}}{n} \;=\; \frac{W_n}{n} \times 266.67$$ | n | $F_{C_n}$ (kmol/h) | Role | |---|---|---| | 1 (CH₄) | 24.00 | Light gas | | 2 (C₂H₆) | 16.80 | LPG | | 3 (C₃H₈) | 11.76 | LPG | | 4 (C₄H₁₀) | 8.24 | LPG | | 5 (C₅H₁₂) | 5.76 | Naphtha | | 6 (C₆H₁₄) | 4.03 | Naphtha | | ≥7 (C₅⁺ wax) | 87.85 | Diesel/Wax | ### Step 5 — Heat Duty to be Removed by the Fluidized Bed Using ΔH = –165 kJ/mol CO reacted: $$Q \;=\; |\Delta H|\,X_{CO}\,F_{CO,in} \;=\; 165 \times 0.80 \times 333.33 \times 10^3 \;\text{mol/h}$$ $$Q \;=\; 44{,}000 \;\text{kJ/h (approx)} \;\approx\; 12.2\;\text{MW}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **minimum** heat-removal load. A real FTS CFB has multiple internal cooling coils embedded in the dense bed; the heat-transfer coefficient h ≈ **300 – 500 W/m²K** in the bubbling/fast fluidization regime — the reason fluidized beds dominate FTS reactor design. --- ## 3.4 KTU 14-Mark Analytical Matrix — Quick Recall | Parameter | Effect of ↑ Temperature | Effect of ↑ Pressure | Effect of ↑ α | Effect of Fe vs Co | |---|---|---|---|---| | CH₄ selectivity | ↑ sharply | ↓ slightly | ↓ strongly | Fe gives more CH₄ | | Wax / heavy HC | ↓ | ↑ | ↑↑ | Co gives more wax | | Olefin/paraffin ratio | ↓ (more paraffins) | ↓ | — | Fe → more olefins | | Catalyst life | ↓ (coking) | ↑ | ↑ (waxes protect) | Co lasts longer | | WGS activity | ↑ | — | — | Fe has high WGS, Co negligible | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block: FTS in a Circulating Fluidized Bed (CFB) Reactor — Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Syngas Feed: H2 + CO
Module M = 0.6 to 2.0] --> B1[Bottom Primary Cyclone
Solids Recovery] B1 --> C1[Fluidized Bed Reactor Riser
T = 493 to 613 K, P = 10 to 40 bar] C1 --> D1[Catalyst Regenerator Standpipe
Coke Combustion with O2] D1 --> E1[Regenerator Cyclone
Hot Catalyst Return] E1 --> C1 C1 --> F1[Top Secondary Cyclone
Fines Recovery] F1 --> G1[Product Condenser Train
Heavy Wax C19+] F1 --> G2[Product Condenser Train
Diesel C10 to C18] F1 --> G3[Product Condenser Train
Naphtha C5 to C9] F1 --> G4[Product Condenser Train
LPG C3 to C4] F1 --> G5[Tail Gas CH4, CO2, unreacted syngas] G1 --> H1[Hydrocracker / Dewaxing Unit
Optional] G2 --> H1 H1 --> I1[Final Distillate Product Pool] G3 --> I1 G4 --> I1 D1 --> J1[Flue Gas CO2 + H2O
Heat Recovery Boiler] C1 -. heat removal via internal coils .-> K1[Boiling Water / Steam
Q approx 12 MW per train] ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **riser reactor** (C1) is the heart of the CFB; catalyst circulates between reaction and regeneration sections. - **Fines** leaving with the product gas are recovered by cyclones and returned. - **Heat removal** is via membrane wall / internal bayonet tubes generating medium-pressure steam. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Block: ASF Product Distribution Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Syngas H2 + CO] --> S2[Catalyst Surface
Active Site] S2 --> P1[Step 1: CO Dissociation
C atom adsorbs] P1 --> P2{Propagation or Termination?} P2 -- with prob alpha --> P3[Add 1 Carbon Unit
Chain extends to Cn+1] P3 --> P2 P2 -- with prob 1 minus alpha --> P4[Terminate and Desorb] P4 --> S3{Carbon Number n} S3 -- n = 1 --> S4[Methane CH4] S3 -- n = 2,3,4 --> S5[LPG C2 to C4] S3 -- n = 5 to 11 --> S6[Gasoline Naphtha] S3 -- n = 10 to 20 --> S7[Diesel Cut] S3 -- n greater than 20 --> S8[Wax C5+ Fraction] ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The branching point **{Propagation or Termination?}** is the **single statistical decision** repeated for every chain — and the relative probabilities define α. - The product carbon-number *bins* map directly onto the ASF Eq. (5). --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Mass-Balance Functional Architecture for an FTS Reactor ```mermaid flowchart TD IN1[F_CO,in] --> MX1[Reactor Steady-State
Material Balance] IN2[F_H2,in] --> MX1 IN3[F_inerts,N2,CO2] --> MX1 MX1 --> OUT1[F_CO,out = F_CO,in times 1 minus X_CO] MX1 --> OUT2[F_H2,out from stoichiometry] MX1 --> OUT3[F_H2O,out = n times F_CO,reacted] MX1 --> OUT4[Hydrocarbon Products
per ASF distribution] MX1 --> OUT5[Heat Duty Q = 165 times F_CO,reacted
kJ per h] OUT4 --> BIN1[Bin: C1 to C4 gases] OUT4 --> BIN2[Bin: C5 to C9 naphtha] OUT4 --> BIN3[Bin: C10 to C20 diesel] OUT4 --> BIN4[Bin: C21 plus wax] ``` > [!NOTE] > All block-level functional flows above can be directly **translated into a process-flow diagram (PFD)** for design projects, and into **the system-level boundary** for an FTS reactor model in a control-systems or plant-wide simulation course. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]` **Define Fischer–Tropsch Synthesis. State the general stoichiometric equation for paraffin formation and write the range of operating temperature and pressure used in commercial FTS fluidized-bed reactors.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** FTS is a catalytic heterogeneous polymerization reaction in which syngas (CO + H₂) is converted over Fe, Co, or Ru catalysts into a mixture of liquid hydrocarbons and water. - **Stoichiometric equation (1 mark):** $n\,\text{CO} + (2n+1)\,\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$. - **Operating range (1 mark):** T = **493 – 613 K (220 – 340 °C)**, P = **10 – 40 bar**. --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]` **What is the Anderson–Schulz–Flory (ASF) distribution? Write the mole-fraction and mass-fraction forms of the ASF equation and identify the role of the chain growth probability α.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Concept (1 mark):** The ASF distribution is a statistical model describing the product carbon-number distribution in FTS, based on a single parameter α = probability of chain propagation at each step. - **Equations (1 mark):** Mole fraction: $x_n = (1-\alpha)\alpha^{n-1}$; Mass fraction: $W_n/n = (1-\alpha)^2\alpha^{n-1}$. - **Role of α (1 mark):** α dictates selectivity — low α (~0.5) gives light gases; high α (~0.9) gives heavy wax/diesel. Increasing T, decreasing P, and certain catalysts (Fe) reduce α. --- ## PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. All sub-parts must be answered. --- ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Sample, Module 4]` **(a) Discuss the main and side reactions occurring in Fischer–Tropsch Synthesis. Explain how operating conditions (T, P, H₂/CO ratio) and catalyst choice influence the product selectivity. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** *Main reactions:* 1. **Paraffins:** $n\text{CO} + (2n+1)\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2} + n\text{H}_2\text{O}$ 2. **Olefins:** $n\text{CO} + 2n\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n} + n\text{H}_2\text{O}$ 3. **Alcohols:** $n\text{CO} + 2n\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+1}\text{OH} + (n-1)\text{H}_2\text{O}$ *Side reactions:* 4. **Water–Gas Shift (WGS):** $\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$ 5. **Boudouard:** $2\text{CO} \rightarrow \text{C}_{(s)} + \text{CO}_2$ 6. **Methanation:** $\text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ *Influence of operating conditions:* - **Temperature ↑:** Increases reaction rate but shifts selectivity toward CH₄ (lowers α). Promotes WGS on Fe. Increases olefin readsorption, often *lowers* olefin yield. - **Pressure ↑:** Favors heavier products (↑ α), suppresses methane, increases wax. - **H₂/CO ratio ↑:** Promotes hydrogenation → paraffins, suppresses olefins and alcohols. - **Catalyst choice:** - **Fe:** Cheap, active for WGS, lower α, more olefins, sulfur-tolerant to some extent. - **Co:** Higher α, longer life, more paraffins, requires high H₂/CO ≈ 2.0, no WGS. - **Ru:** Highest activity, but very expensive (limited to research). **(b) Derive the Anderson–Schulz–Flory mole-fraction distribution equation starting from the definition of chain growth probability α. Show that the maximum C₂ mole fraction cannot exceed 1/3. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** *Derivation:* Define α = probability of propagation per step. For a chain of exactly n carbons to form, the catalyst must make (n – 1) propagations followed by 1 termination: $$P(n) \;=\; \alpha^{\,n-1}\,(1-\alpha)$$ Normalization using geometric series $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\alpha^{n-1} = \dfrac{1}{1-\alpha}$ gives $\sum P(n) = 1$, hence: $$\boxed{\,x_n \;=\; (1-\alpha)\,\alpha^{\,n-1}\,}$$ *Maximum C₂ calculation:* The C₂ mole fraction is: $$x_2 \;=\; (1-\alpha)\,\alpha$$ Differentiate w.r.t. α and set to zero: $$\frac{dx_2}{d\alpha} \;=\; 1 - 2\alpha \;=\; 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \alpha^* = 0.5$$ Substitute back: $$x_2^{max} \;=\; (1 - 0.5)(0.5) \;=\; 0.25$$ Therefore the **maximum C₂ mole fraction is 0.25** (and not 1/3). > **Note for the student:** The figure 1/3 ≈ 0.33 sometimes appears in textbooks as a *qualitative* bound; the **exact analytical maximum is 0.25 at α = 0.5**. Mentioning this nuance in the exam earns full credit. --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Sample, Module 4]` **(a) With the help of a block diagram, describe the operation of a Circulating Fluidized Bed (CFB) reactor for Fischer–Tropsch Synthesis. Compare its merits and demerits with a fixed-bed multitubular FTS reactor. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** *Block diagram (refer to Section 4.1 above):* The CFB has a **riser** (the actual FTS reactor), a **standpipe**, a **regenerator**, and **cyclones**. Catalyst continuously circulates: regenerated catalyst from the regenerator flows down the standpipe, is re-injected at the riser base, mixes with syngas, carries out FTS in the riser, exits to a primary cyclone where it is separated from product gas, and is then sent to the regenerator where coke is burnt off with O₂/air. The regenerated hot catalyst returns to close the loop. *Merits of CFB over fixed-bed multitubular:* 1. **Excellent temperature uniformity** (isothermal ±2 K) — vital because FTS is extremely exothermic. 2. **Online catalyst regeneration** — coke is continuously burnt, so catalyst activity remains stable for years. 3. **Scalability** — single-train capacity > 10 000 tpd, whereas fixed-bed tubes have length/pressure-drop constraints. 4. **Higher heat-transfer coefficients** (h = 300 – 600 W/m²K vs ≈ 100 W/m²K for fixed-bed tubes). 5. **No hot spots** — selectivity loss to methane is greatly reduced. *Demerits of CFB:* 1. **Higher capital cost** and mechanical complexity (cyclones, slide valves, catalyst hoppers). 2. **Catalyst attrition** — fines loss can be 1–5 %/day; needs robust catalyst formulation. 3. **Lower per-pass conversion** (typically 50 – 80 %) requires a syngas recycle loop. 4. **Difficult to operate at very high pressures** (> 40 bar) due to mechanical constraints on the loop. 5. **Product distribution slightly lighter** than Co-based fixed-bed because of higher operating temperature. **(b) An FTS fluidized-bed reactor is fed with 1500 kmol/h of syngas at H₂/CO = 2.0. CO conversion is 75 %, the ASF chain-growth probability is α = 0.75, and paraffin selectivity is assumed. Compute (i) the rate of water formation, (ii) total C₁ product rate, (iii) total C₅⁺ (wax) mass rate, and (iv) heat duty to be removed. Use ΔH = –165 kJ/mol CO. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** *Step 1 — Inlet flows:* $F_{CO,in} = 1500/3 = 500$ kmol/h; $F_{H_2,in} = 1000$ kmol/h. *Step 2 — CO reacted:* $F_{CO,reacted} = 500 \times 0.75 = 375$ kmol/h. *Step 3 — (i) Water formation:* For paraffins, 1 mole H₂O per mole CO reacted. Therefore: $$F_{H_2O} \;=\; 375\;\text{kmol/h} \;=\; 375 \times 18 \;=\; 6750\;\text{kg/h}$$ *Step 4 — (ii) C₁ (methane) production:* From ASF mass-fraction form (Eq. 7): $$\frac{W_1}{1} \;=\; (1-\alpha)^2\,\alpha^0 \;=\; (0.25)^2 \times 1 \;=\; 0.0625$$ Mass fraction of CH₄ in total hydrocarbon product = 0.0625. Total carbon throughput = 375 kmol C/h. The mass flow of C₁: $$F_{C_1} \;=\; 0.0625 \times \frac{375}{1} \;=\; 23.44\;\text{kmol/h} \;=\; 23.44 \times 16 \;=\; 375\;\text{kg/h}$$ *Step 5 — (iii) C₅⁺ wax mass rate:* Compute mass fractions up to n = 4 and subtract from 1: | n | $W_n/n$ | $W_n$ | |---|---|---| | 1 | 0.0625 | 0.0625 | | 2 | 0.0469 | 0.0938 | | 3 | 0.0352 | 0.1055 | | 4 | 0.0264 | 0.1055 | Sum $W_n$ for n = 1 to 4 = 0.3673. Therefore $W_{C_5^+} = 1 - 0.3673 = 0.6327$ (mass fraction of total hydrocarbons). The average carbon number in the C₅⁺ cut can be approximated. Total carbon = 375 kmol/h. Carbon in C₁ to C₄ = 23.44 + 2(11.72) + 3(7.84) + 4(5.86) ≈ 23.44 + 23.44 + 23.52 + 23.44 = 93.84 kmol C/h. Carbon in C₅⁺ = 375 – 93.84 = 281.16 kmol C/h. Moles of C₅⁺ molecules (assuming average n̄ = 15 for the wax): ≈ 281.16/15 ≈ 18.74 kmol/h. Mass of C₅⁺ ≈ 18.74 × (14 × 15 + 2) ≈ 18.74 × 212 ≈ **3 973 kg/h ≈ 4 t/h** of wax. *Step 6 — (iv) Heat duty:* $$Q \;=\; |\Delta H| \times F_{CO,reacted} \;=\; 165 \times 375 \times 10^3\;\text{kJ/h} \;=\; 6.19 \times 10^7\;\text{kJ/h}$$ Converting: $Q \approx 6.19\times 10^7 / 3600 \approx$ **17.2 MW** of heat must be removed by the fluidized-bed cooling coils. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Read Carefully!)** > 1. **Confusing mole-fraction vs mass-fraction ASF formula.** Students frequently write $(1-\alpha)\alpha^{n-1}$ when the question clearly asks for $W_n$ — **lose 1–2 marks** instantly. The mole-fraction has (1–α)¹; the mass-fraction has (1–α)². > 2. **Forgetting to normalize** the geometric series in derivations. Always show the $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\alpha^{n-1} = 1/(1-\alpha)$ step. > 3. **Wrong sign on WGS ΔH.** It is **exothermic** (–41 kJ/mol), not endothermic. Marks deducted if confused. > 4. **Mixing up syngas ratio direction.** "Higher H₂/CO → more paraffins" is correct; "higher H₂/CO → more methane" is **wrong** (it is *higher T* that gives more methane). > 5. **Heat duty units.** kJ/h vs MW. Conversion: divide kJ/h by 3600 to get kW. Examiners check unit consistency strictly. > 6. **α range.** Always state $0 < \alpha < 1$. Boundary values α → 0 (only CH₄) and α → 1 (only wax) must be quoted. > 7. **Forgetting WGS in Fe-catalyst FTS.** If α is not given, the WGS changes the in-reactor H₂/CO — a *classic* 14-mark trap. --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **FTS** converts **CO + H₂ → hydrocarbons + H₂O** over **Fe, Co, Ru** catalysts. - ✅ Three primary reactions: **paraffin, olefin, alcohol** — all exothermic. - ✅ Two key side reactions: **WGS** (on Fe) and **Boudouard** (coke). - ✅ ΔH ≈ **–165 kJ/mol CO** — the most exothermic industrial heterogeneous reaction → **fluidized beds are essential**. - ✅ **ASF mole fraction:** $x_n = (1-\alpha)\alpha^{n-1}$. - ✅ **ASF mass fraction:** $W_n/n = (1-\alpha)^2\alpha^{n-1}$. - ✅ **α → 0:** only methane; **α → 1:** only wax; **α = 0.5:** maximum C₂ at 25 mol%. - ✅ **Stoichiometric H₂/CO** for paraffins ranges from **3 (CH₄)** to **2 (long chains)**; closed form: $2 - (1-\alpha)\frac{\ln(1-\alpha)}{\alpha}$. - ✅ **T ↑** → α ↓, more CH₄, more olefins on Fe. **P ↑** → α ↑, more wax. **H₂/CO ↑** → more paraffins. - ✅ **Fe** catalyst → WGS active, more olefins, lower α. **Co** catalyst → WGS inactive, more paraffins, higher α, longer life. - ✅ **CFB** (Sasol Synthol) and **Slurry Bubble Column** (Sasol SSPD) and **Fixed-bed Multitubular** (Shell Pearl SMDS) are the three commercial reactor choices — all exploit fluidization principles taught in PECHT415. - ✅ **CO conversion** $X_{CO} = (F_{CO,in} - F_{CO,out})/F_{CO,in}$; **carbon selectivity** $S_{C_n} = n F_{C_n}/\sum k F_{C_k}$. - ✅ **Heat duty** in MW is the *key* design parameter — drives cooling-coil area calculation in the fluidized bed. - ✅ **H₂/CO feed ratio M** must be tuned: for Fe M ≈ 0.6–1.0; for Co M ≈ 2.0–2.1. - ✅ **Maximum CH₄ selectivity** (mole basis, ASF) is **100%** at α = 0; **maximum C₂** is **25%** at α = 0.5; **maximum C₃** is also 22.2% at α ≈ 0.42. - ✅ Real FTS product distributions **deviate from ideal ASF** at high and low n due to secondary reactions (readsorption, cracking, diffusion) — a 7-mark discussion point.

topics.title: Polymerization of Olefins
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Polymerization of Olefins

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Olefin polymerization** is a chain-growth (addition) chemical reaction in which light olefinic hydrocarbons — primarily ethylene ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_4$), propylene ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_6$), 1-butene, and 1-hexene — are linked together through successive opening of their carbon–carbon double bonds to form high-molecular-weight polymers such as **polyethylene (PE)**, **polypropylene (PP)**, and **linear low-density polyethylene (LLDPE)**.

In the context of fluidization engineering, olefin polymerization is overwhelmingly carried out in **gas-phase fluidized bed reactors (FBRs)** because:

- The polymerization reaction is **highly exothermic** ($\Delta H_{rxn} \approx -95$ to $-105$ kJ/mol for ethylene).
- Excellent **isothermal operation** is achieved via fluidization of growing polymer particles in a continuous recycle-gas loop.
- The catalyst particles act as both the reaction site and the in-situ seed for polymer particle growth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is a direct industrial application of fluidization. Students must correlate polymerization kinetics (chemistry) with fluidization hydrodynamics (reactor engineering). Memorize the UNIPOL process — it is the canonical gas-phase FBR olefin polymerization technology.

### 1.2 The Polymerization Reaction (Generic Form)

$$n \cdot \text{CH}_2\text{=CHR} \xrightarrow{\text{Catalyst, Heat, Pressure}} -(\text{CH}_2\text{-CHR})_n-$$

where $n$ = degree of polymerization (typically $10^3$ to $10^5$), and $\text{R}$ is $\text{H}$ (ethylene), $\text{CH}_3$ (propylene), or an alkyl group (higher $\alpha$-olefins).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The LEGO Brick Reactor"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> Imagine a giant **wind tunnel** (the fluidized bed) where millions of tiny **magnetic beads** (catalyst particles) are suspended in a swirling, bubbling gas stream (ethylene + nitrogen + hydrogen). Each bead acts like a **LEGO brick with sticky arms** — when a gaseous ethylene molecule bumps into an exposed arm, it snaps onto the chain. The bead grows larger and heavier as more monomers are added, but the turbulent gas keeps it suspended. When the bead is heavy enough, it is drained out the bottom as a polymer pellet, while fresh catalyst is injected at the top. This is exactly how a gas-phase polyethylene fluidized bed reactor operates.

### 1.4 Key Physical & Thermodynamic Constants

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| $\Delta H_{polymerization}$ (ethylene) | $\mathbf{-95 \text{ to } -105 \text{ kJ/mol}}$ | Exotherm magnitude — drives heat-removal design |
| Reaction pressure | $\mathbf{20 \text{ to } 40 \text{ bar}}$ | Maintains monomer solubility in polymer |
| Reaction temperature | $\mathbf{80 \text{ to } 110 \,^\circ\text{C}}$ | Below polymer melting point (gas-phase) |
| Superficial gas velocity ($U_0$) | $\mathbf{0.4 \text{ to } 0.8 \text{ m/s}}$ | 3–6× $U_{mf}$ to fluidize growing particles |
| Particle size range | $\mathbf{250 \text{ to } 1000 \, \mu\text{m}}$ | Catalyst seed → final product |
| Monomer conversion per pass | $\mathbf{2 \text{ to } 5\%}$ | Recycle gas restores unreacted monomer |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Olefin Polymerization Mechanisms

Olefin polymerization can proceed via **four distinct kinetic mechanisms**, depending on the catalyst used:

1. **Free-Radical Polymerization** (high-pressure PE, LDPE)
2. **Anionic Polymerization** (research, specialty elastomers)
3. **Cationic Polymerization** (isobutylene → butyl rubber)
4. **Coordination (Insertion) Polymerization** — *the dominant industrial mechanism for HDPE, LLDPE, and isotactic PP*. This is subdivided into:
   - **Ziegler–Natta (ZN) catalysis** (heterogeneous, multi-site)
   - **Phillips catalysis** (CrO$_3$/SiO$_2$)
   - **Metallocene catalysis** (homogeneous, single-site)

### 2.2 Free-Radical Polymerization — Step-by-Step Logic

A free-radical chain reaction proceeds in **three stages**:

| Stage | Purpose | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|
| **Initiation** | Generate active radical from initiator | $I \xrightarrow{k_d} 2R^\bullet$; $R^\bullet + M \xrightarrow{k_i} M_1^\bullet$ |
| **Propagation** | Chain growth by monomer addition | $M_n^\bullet + M \xrightarrow{k_p} M_{n+1}^\bullet$ |
| **Termination** | Deactivate growing chain | $M_n^\bullet + M_m^\bullet \xrightarrow{k_t} P_n + P_m$ (combination) |

### 2.3 Coordination Polymerization (Ziegler–Natta)

The Cossee–Arlman mechanism, which is the **only mechanism tested in KTU examinations**, proceeds as follows:

1. The Ti atom on the surface of a $\text{TiCl}_3$ (or $\text{MgCl}_2}$-supported) crystal possesses a **vacant coordination site** (a Lewis-acid vacancy).
2. An olefin monomer approaches and **$\pi$-coordinates** to the vacant site.
3. The coordinated monomer **inserts (migrates)** into the existing Ti–C bond (the polymer chain).
4. The vacant site is regenerated, and the cycle repeats.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ZN catalysts matter for fluidization:** They are *heterogeneous solids* — perfect for being injected as fine particles into a fluidized bed. The catalyst particle becomes the **nucleus** of the polymer particle, and polymer grows outward from its surface, replicating the original catalyst morphology 1:1 (the **"polymer-replica" phenomenon**).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following expressions. They are the **only equations** you need for derivation-type questions.

**Table 2.1 — Core Polymerization Kinetics Equations**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initiation rate | $R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$ | $f$ = initiator efficiency; $[I]$ = initiator conc. |
| 2 | Rate of polymerization | $R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{\dfrac{R_i}{2 k_t}}$ | Central KTU equation |
| 3 | Number-average chain length | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i} = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{2 f k_d [I]}$ | Determines molecular weight |
| 4 | Weight-average chain length | $\bar{X}_w = \dfrac{2 (1+p)}{1 + (1-p)(x/c)}$ | $p$ = monomer conversion |
| 5 | Mayo–Lewis copolymerization | $\dfrac{d[M_1]}{d[M_2]} = \dfrac{[M_1](r_1 [M_1] + [M_2])}{[M_2]([M_1] + r_2 [M_2])}$ | $r_1, r_2$ = reactivity ratios |
| 6 | Number of active sites (ZN) | $C^* = [C_0] \cdot X$ | $X$ = fraction active, typically 0.01–0.1 |
| 7 | Rate via ZN catalysis | $R_p = k_p [M] C^*$ | Single-site assumption |

**Table 2.2 — Fluidized Bed Polymerization Reactor Equations**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 8 | Steady-state heat balance | $R_p \cdot (-\Delta H_{rxn}) \cdot V_{bed} = \dot{m}_{gas} \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})$ | Recycle gas removes heat |
| 9 | Minimum fluidization velocity | $Re_{mf} = \sqrt{27.2^2 + 0.0408 \cdot Ar} - 27.2$ | Used to size $U_0$ |
| 10 | Particle growth rate | $\dfrac{d d_p}{dt} = \dfrac{2 R_p M_w \rho_{cat}}{d_{cat} \rho_{poly}}$ | Replication model |
| 11 | Polymer yield per unit catalyst | $Y = \dfrac{\text{kg polymer}}{\text{kg catalyst}}$ | Industrial: 5000–50000 |
| 12 | Space-time yield | $STY = \dfrac{\text{kg polymer}}{\text{m}^3_{\text{bed}} \cdot \text{h}}$ | Typical: 60–180 kg/(m³·h) |
| 13 | Geldart classification | LLDPE/HDPE particles → **Geldart Group B** | Bubbling fluidization |
| 14 | Recycle-gas superficial velocity | $U_0 = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{recycle}}{A_{bed}}$ | Set at $3 U_{mf}$ to $6 U_{mf}$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Polyolefins are the world's largest-volume synthetic polymers** (~190 million tonnes/year globally as of 2024).
- Over **75%** of all HDPE/LLDPE is produced in fluidized bed reactors (UNIPOL, INEOS, Borealis Borstar).
- Isotactic polypropylene (iPP), used in packaging, automotive, and medical devices, is also predominantly made in FBRs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Rate of Polymerization ($R_p$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Derive the classical expression $R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{R_i / 2 k_t}$ for a steady-state free-radical polymerization. *This is a guaranteed 7-mark derivation question in KTU.*

**Step 1 — Write the rate of each elementary step.**

The rate of monomer disappearance (i.e., rate of polymerization) occurs exclusively in the **propagation** step:

$$R_p = -\frac{d[M]}{dt} = k_p [M][M^\bullet]$$

Here, $[M^\bullet]$ is the total concentration of all propagating chain radicals ($M_1^\bullet + M_2^\bullet + \cdots$).

**Step 2 — Apply the steady-state approximation (SSA) to radicals.**

The radical concentration is extremely small and changes negligibly with time:

$$\frac{d[M^\bullet]}{dt} = R_i - R_t \approx 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R_i = R_t$$

The termination rate (assuming bimolecular termination by combination) is:

$$R_t = 2 k_t [M^\bullet]^2$$

(The factor 2 is sometimes omitted depending on convention; we retain it here for clarity.)

**Step 3 — Solve SSA for $[M^\bullet]$.**

$$R_i = 2 k_t [M^\bullet]^2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad [M^\bullet] = \sqrt{\frac{R_i}{2 k_t}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the propagation rate.**

$$R_p = k_p [M] \cdot \sqrt{\frac{R_i}{2 k_t}} = k_p [M] \sqrt{\frac{R_i}{2 k_t}}$$

$$\boxed{R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{\frac{R_i}{2 k_t}}}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

- $R_p \propto [M]^1$ → first order in monomer.
- $R_p \propto [I]^{1/2}$ → square-root order in initiator.
- Higher termination constant ($k_t$) suppresses the rate by destroying active radicals.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Number-Average Degree of Polymerization ($\bar{X}_n$)

**Step 1 — Definition.** The average number of monomer units per polymer chain equals the rate of chain growth divided by the rate of chain initiation (i.e., one chain is born per initiation event):

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{R_p}{R_i}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the expressions for $R_p$ and $R_i$.**

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{k_p [M] \sqrt{R_i / 2 k_t}}{R_i} = \frac{k_p [M]}{R_i \sqrt{2 k_t / R_i}} = \frac{k_p [M]}{\sqrt{2 k_t R_i}}$$

**Step 3 — Insert $R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$.**

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{k_p [M]}{\sqrt{2 k_t \cdot 2 f k_d [I]}} = \frac{k_p [M]}{2 \sqrt{f k_d k_t [I]}}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{X}_n = \frac{k_p [M]}{2 (f k_d k_t [I])^{1/2}}}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering significance.**

- To make **higher molecular weight** polymer (larger $\bar{X}_n$), **decrease** the initiator concentration or **increase** monomer concentration.
- This is why industrial ZN processes avoid chemical initiators entirely and rely on **transfer-agent hydrogen** to control molecular weight instead.

---

### 3.3 Heat-Balance Derivation for a Fluidized Bed Polymerization Reactor

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Show how the recycle-gas flow rate is sized to remove the heat of polymerization — the **single most important design calculation** in an olefin FBR.

**Step 1 — Energy released by polymerization.**

$$Q_{gen} = R_p \cdot (-\Delta H_{rxn}) \cdot V_{bed}$$

where $V_{bed}$ is the volume of the fluidized bed and $-\Delta H_{rxn} > 0$ since the reaction is exothermic.

**Step 2 — Energy absorbed by the recycle gas stream** (entering at $T_{in}$, leaving at $T_{out}$, assumed equal to bed temperature).

$$Q_{rem} = \dot{m}_{gas} \cdot C_{p,gas} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

**Step 3 — At steady state, generation equals removal.**

$$R_p \cdot (-\Delta H_{rxn}) \cdot V_{bed} = \dot{m}_{gas} \cdot C_{p,gas} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the recycle gas mass flow rate.**

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{gas} = \frac{R_p \cdot (-\Delta H_{rxn}) \cdot V_{bed}}{C_{p,gas} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical illustration.**

Take a typical UNIPOL-grade HDPE reactor:

- $V_{bed} = 75 \text{ m}^3$
- $R_p = 200 \text{ kg/(m}^3 \cdot \text{h)}$ polymer
- $-\Delta H_{rxn} = 3660 \text{ kJ/kg ethylene}$
- $C_{p,gas} = 1.7 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- $\Delta T = T_{out} - T_{in} = 5 \text{ K}$

$$\dot{m}_{gas} = \frac{200 \cdot 3660 \cdot 75}{1.7 \cdot 5} = \frac{5.49 \times 10^7}{8.5} \approx 6.46 \times 10^6 \text{ kg/h}$$

This enormous gas flow rate (≈ 1800 kg/s) is why industrial FBRs use a **closed-loop recycle compressor** with an external heat exchanger — the gas must be cooled and returned continuously.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Polymerization Reactor Types

| Feature | Bulk (Slurry) | Solution | Gas-Phase FBR |
|---|---|---|---|
| Polymer phase | Liquid monomer + suspended polymer | Dissolved in solvent | Solid particles in gas |
| Catalyst | ZN heterogeneous | ZN or metallocene | ZN or metallocene (heterogeneous) |
| Heat removal | Jacket + reflux condenser | Solvent vaporization | Recycle gas + external cooler |
| Operating $T$ | 50–80 °C | 130–250 °C | 80–110 °C |
| Operating $P$ | 5–30 bar | 30–100 bar | 20–40 bar |
| Space-time yield | Low–Medium | Low | **High** (60–180 kg/m³·h) |
| Polymer morphology | Irregular paste | Solution (no granules) | **Spherical, uniform** |
| Solvent required | No | Yes | **No** (advantage) |
| Environmental burden | Low–Medium | High (solvent recovery) | **Low** |
| Industrial examples | Spheripol (PP) | Dowlex solution PE | **UNIPOL, Borstar, INEOS** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 UNIPOL Gas-Phase Fluidized Bed Reactor — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional architecture of a UNIPOL-grade olefin polymerization fluidized bed reactor. (All node IDs are alphanumeric; labels are clean uppercase text without markdown tags.)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[FRESH ETHYLENE FEED] --> B[RECYCLE GAS MIXER]
    H[H2 TRANSFER AGENT] --> B
    N[N2 INERT DILUENT] --> B
    CAT[ZN CATALYST INJECTION] --> C[FLUIDIZED BED REACTOR]
    CO[COMONOMER C4 OR C6] --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D[EXPANSION SECTION]
    D --> E[RECYCLE GAS COMPRESSOR]
    E --> F[RECYCLE GAS COOLER]
    F --> B
    C --> G[PRODUCT DISCHARGE VESSEL]
    G --> P[POLYMER PELLET TO PURGING]
    P --> PR[FINAL PRODUCT TO CUSTOMER]
    C -.-> T[BED TEMPERATURE CONTROL]
    T --> CTL[PID CONTROLLER ACTING ON COOLER BYPASS VALVE]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- **Fresh ethylene** (A), hydrogen transfer agent (H), nitrogen diluent (N), and comonomer (CO) all mix with the cooled recycle stream in **B**.
- The mixture enters the bottom of the **fluidized bed reactor (C)** through a distributor plate, fluidizing the polymerizing particles.
- **Catalyst (CAT)** is injected directly into the bed as a fine powder.
- Gases leave the top, pass through the **expansion section (D)** to disengage entrained fines, and are recycled via **compressor (E)** and **cooler (F)**.
- Finished polymer is intermittently discharged from the bed side into **G** and sent for purging.

### 4.2 Cossee–Arlman Coordination–Insertion Mechanism

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant TS as Ti Surface Site
    participant V as Vacant Site
    participant M as Olefin Monomer
    participant C as Growing Polymer Chain
    Note over TS,C: Cycle 1 - Coordination
    TS->>V: 1. Vacant coordination site exposed
    M->>V: 2. Monomer pi-coordinates to Ti
    Note over TS,C: Cycle 2 - Insertion
    V->>C: 3. Monomer inserts into Ti-C bond
    C->>TS: 4. Chain migrates to vacant site
    TS->>V: 5. New vacancy formed
    Note over TS,C: Cycle repeats - chain grows by 1 unit each turn
```

### 4.3 Polymer Particle Growth (Replication Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Fresh Catalyst Particle d_cat approx 10 micron] --> G[Active polymerization on surface]
    G --> R[Polymer replicates catalyst morphology]
    R --> M[Monomer diffuses through polymer layer]
    M --> G
    G --> F[Final particle d_p approx 500 to 1000 micron]
    F --> DIS[Discharge from FBR]
```

**Interpretation:** The catalyst acts as a *template*. The polymer grows outward from each catalyst fragment, faithfully replicating its size and shape. This is why **catalyst particle size distribution directly controls the polymer particle size distribution** in the fluidized bed product.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**"With a neat sketch, explain the role of a fluidized bed reactor in olefin polymerization. Why is fluidization preferred over slurry or solution polymerization for HDPE production?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In olefin polymerization (e.g., HDPE, LLDPE), the fluidized bed reactor (FBR) suspends growing polymer–catalyst particles in an upward flow of monomer–gas mixture (ethylene + nitrogen + hydrogen + comonomer). The Ziegler–Natta or metallocene catalyst is injected as a fine powder and becomes the nucleus of each polymer particle.

Fluidization is preferred because:

1. **Excellent isothermal operation:** The vigorous gas–solid mixing removes the large exothermic heat ($\approx 95$–$105$ kJ/mol) uniformly, preventing localized hot spots that would melt the polymer and cause particle agglomeration.
2. **No solvent required:** Unlike slurry/solution processes, gas-phase FBR eliminates solvent recovery costs and environmental burden.
3. **Direct polymer morphology control:** Catalyst particles replicate 1:1 into polymer pellets, giving uniform spherical granules ready for extrusion without further pelletization.
4. **High space-time yield (60–180 kg/m³·h):** Continuous catalyst injection and product discharge enable large throughput.

(Diagrammatic representation: as in Section 4.1 above.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Differentiate between Ziegler–Natta and metallocene catalysts used in olefin polymerization."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Ziegler–Natta | Metallocene |
|---|---|---|
| Active site | Multi-site (heterogeneous) | Single-site (homogeneous, often supported) |
| Typical metal | Ti, Mg, Al chlorides | Zr, Hf, Ti with cyclopentadienyl ligands |
| Molecular-weight distribution (MWD) | Broad (PDI = 3–10) | Narrow (PDI ≈ 2) |
| Comonomer incorporation | Uneven | Uniform |
| Activity (kg PE / g cat) | Moderate (5–15) | Very high (10–100+) |
| Polymer property control | Limited | Excellent (tailor-made) |
| Cost | Low | High (cocatalyst MAO expensive) |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the mechanism of **Ziegler–Natta catalyzed polymerization of olefins** with the **Cossee–Arlman model**. Discuss why these catalysts are essential for isotactic polypropylene production. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** With the aid of a **neat block diagram**, describe the **UNIPOL gas-phase fluidized bed process** for HDPE production. Discuss the role of the recycle gas loop. **\[7 marks\]**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The Cossee–Arlman mechanism explains stereoregular polymerization on a heterogeneous transition-metal catalyst. The key features are:

1. **Active site geometry:** On the lateral faces of $\alpha$-TiCl$_3$ (or MgCl$_2$-supported TiCl$_4$) crystallites, Ti atoms have a **vacant octahedral coordination site** stabilized by chlorine ligands.

2. **Step 1 – Coordination:** An olefin monomer (e.g., propylene $\text{CH}_2\text{=CH–CH}_3$) approaches the vacant site and forms a $\pi$-complex via donation of its $\pi$-electrons to the empty d-orbital of Ti.

3. **Step 2 – Migratory insertion:** The coordinated monomer inserts into the existing Ti–C bond of the growing polymer chain, shifting the chain to the formerly vacant site and exposing a new vacancy.
