4. **Step 3 – Stereoregulation:** The chiral environment of the multi-chloride ligand sphere forces every incoming monomer to insert with the **same facial orientation**, producing **isotactic** polypropylene (all –CH$_3$ groups on the same side of the chain).

5. **Why essential for iPP:** Only ZN and metallocene catalysts provide the *stereospecific* active sites required for isotactic placement. Free-radical polymerization always gives atactic (amorphous, useless) polypropylene.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

Refer to the architecture in **Section 4.1**. The UNIPOL process operates as follows:

- **Feed system:** Ethylene, 1-butene or 1-hexene (comonomer for LLDPE), hydrogen (MW controller), and nitrogen (inert diluent) are combined with the cooled recycle stream.
- **Fluidized bed reactor:** A vertical cylindrical vessel (typically 3–10 m diameter, 10–30 m tall) with a **porous distributor plate** at the bottom. Operating at **80–110 °C** and **20–40 bar**, the gas velocity is set at **3 to 6 × U$_{mf}$** to maintain vigorous bubbling fluidization.
- **Catalyst injection:** ZN catalyst is injected as a dry powder or as a slurry in mineral oil through a dedicated port above the distributor.
- **Polymerization:** Monomer diffuses to active sites on catalyst fragments; polymer chains grow outward; particles enlarge from ~10 μm catalyst to ~500–1000 μm product.
- **Discharge:** Product is intermittently withdrawn via a valve system at the bed side.
- **Recycle loop:** Unreacted gas leaves through the top expansion section, is compressed by the recycle blower, cooled in an external heat exchanger (typically water-cooled), and reinjected at the bottom. This loop removes **~95%** of the reaction heat and maintains the bed within ±1 °C of the setpoint.
- **Why this is preferred:** No solvent, low pressure, continuous operation, uniform granules, easy scale-up — making UNIPOL the most economical route to HDPE/LLDPE.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** **Derive** the rate expression for free-radical polymerization of olefins, starting from the elementary steps. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A pilot-scale fluidized bed polyethylene reactor operates at the conditions given below. Determine the **required recycle gas mass flow rate** to maintain isothermal operation, and comment on the adequacy of fluidization at the resulting gas velocity. **\[7 marks\]**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Bed volume, $V_{bed}$ | $50 \text{ m}^3$ |
| Rate of polymerization, $R_p$ | $150 \text{ kg/(m}^3 \cdot \text{h)}$ |
| Heat of reaction, $-\Delta H_{rxn}$ | $3500 \text{ kJ/kg}$ |
| Recycle gas $C_p$ | $1.7 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$ |
| Allowed $\Delta T$ across cooler | $6 \text{ K}$ |
| Particle size, $d_p$ | $700 \text{ \mu m}$ |
| Particle density, $\rho_p$ | $940 \text{ kg/m}^3$ |
| Gas density, $\rho_g$ | $22 \text{ kg/m}^3$ |
| Gas viscosity, $\mu_g$ | $1.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ |
| Reactor cross-sectional area, $A_{bed}$ | $7.0 \text{ m}^2$ |

---

**Model Solution (a):**

See the full derivation in **Section 3.1** above. The key result is:

$$R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{\frac{R_i}{2 k_t}}$$

The derivation proceeds via:

- Initiation rate: $R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$
- Propagation: $R_p = k_p [M][M^\bullet]$
- Termination: $R_t = 2 k_t [M^\bullet]^2$
- Steady state on radicals: $R_i = R_t \Rightarrow [M^\bullet] = \sqrt{R_i / 2 k_t}$
- Back-substitution into $R_p$ yields the boxed result.

Also derive $\bar{X}_n = R_p / R_i$ (Section 3.2).

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Total heat generated by polymerization.**

$$Q_{gen} = R_p \cdot (-\Delta H_{rxn}) \cdot V_{bed} = 150 \cdot 3500 \cdot 50 = 2.625 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step 2 — Required recycle gas mass flow rate.**

$$\dot{m}_{gas} = \frac{Q_{gen}}{C_p \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{2.625 \times 10^7}{1.7 \cdot 6} = \frac{2.625 \times 10^7}{10.2} \approx 2.574 \times 10^6 \text{ kg/h}$$

Converting: $\dot{m}_{gas} \approx 715 \text{ kg/s}$.

**Step 3 — Compute $U_{mf}$ using Ergun-derived correlation.**

First, the Archimedes number:

$$Ar = \frac{\rho_g (\rho_p - \rho_g) g d_p^3}{\mu_g^2}$$

$$Ar = \frac{22 \cdot (940 - 22) \cdot 9.81 \cdot (700 \times 10^{-6})^3}{(1.5 \times 10^{-5})^2}$$

Compute numerator: $22 \cdot 918 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (3.43 \times 10^{-13}) = 22 \cdot 918 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 3.43 \times 10^{-13}$

$$= 22 \cdot 918 \cdot 3.364 \times 10^{-12} \approx 6.79 \times 10^{-8}$$

Denominator: $(1.5 \times 10^{-5})^2 = 2.25 \times 10^{-10}$

$$Ar \approx \frac{6.79 \times 10^{-8}}{2.25 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 302$$

Now apply the Wen & Yu correlation:

$$Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^2 + 0.0408 \cdot Ar} - 33.7$$

$$Re_{mf} = \sqrt{1135.7 + 12.32} - 33.7 = \sqrt{1148.0} - 33.7 \approx 33.88 - 33.7 = 0.18$$

(Using a slightly different constant — the **33.7 form** is widely accepted; with the 27.2 form used in the formula sheet, we get $Re_{mf} \approx 0.16$. Both are of order $10^{-1}$.)

**Step 4 — Convert $Re_{mf}$ to $U_{mf}$.**

$$U_{mf} = \frac{Re_{mf} \cdot \mu_g}{\rho_g \cdot d_p} = \frac{0.18 \cdot 1.5 \times 10^{-5}}{22 \cdot 700 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{2.7 \times 10^{-6}}{1.54 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 1.75 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}$$

So $U_{mf} \approx 0.000175 \text{ m/s}$.

**Step 5 — Compute actual superficial velocity from recycle gas flow.**

Convert volumetric flow rate using gas density:

$$\dot{V}_{recycle} = \frac{\dot{m}_{gas}}{\rho_g} = \frac{715}{22} \approx 32.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$U_0 = \frac{\dot{V}_{recycle}}{A_{bed}} = \frac{32.5}{7.0} \approx 4.64 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6 — Compare $U_0$ with $U_{mf}$.**

$$\frac{U_0}{U_{mf}} = \frac{4.64}{0.000175} \approx 2.65 \times 10^4$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> A common mistake is to compute $U_{mf}$ correctly but then compare with $U_0$ and conclude the fluidization is "inadequate because $U_0$ is too low". **The opposite is true** — $U_0 / U_{mf} \approx 2.6 \times 10^4$ means we are operating *very deep* into the bubbling/turbulent regime. This is intentional: industrial FBRs operate at $U_0 / U_{mf} = 3$–$6$ (sometimes up to $20$ for turbulent FBRs), not just barely above $U_{mf}$. The very large recycle gas flow is required purely for **heat removal**, not for fluidization. **Always report the ratio $U_0 / U_{mf}$ explicitly in your final answer** — it earns full marks.
>
> Another common pitfall: forgetting to convert $\dot{m}$ from kg/h to kg/s before computing $\dot{V}$ in m³/s. Loss of **1 mark** if units are inconsistent.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] Olefin polymerization = chain-growth addition across C=C; product is polyolefin (PE, PP).
- [x] Four mechanisms: free-radical, anionic, cationic, coordination (Ziegler–Natta / metallocene).
- [x] Free-radical kinetics: $R_p = k_p [M] \sqrt{R_i / 2 k_t}$ — first order in monomer, half order in initiator.
- [x] $\bar{X}_n = R_p / R_i$; control MW by [initiator] or [H$_2$] as chain-transfer agent.
- [x] Ziegler–Natta uses TiCl$_3$ or MgCl$_2$/TiCl$_4$ + Al-alkyl cocatalyst; **heterogeneous** → ideal for FBR.
- [x] Cossee–Arlman mechanism: coordination → migratory insertion → chain shift → new vacancy.
- [x] Metallocene catalysts are **single-site** → narrow MWD → superior polymer property control but expensive.
- [x] UNIPOL process = canonical gas-phase FBR for HDPE/LLDPE; 80–110 °C, 20–40 bar, $U_0/U_{mf} = 3$–$6$.
- [x] Polymer particles are **Geldart Group B** → easily fluidized, bubble-driven mixing.
- [x] **Replication phenomenon**: polymer particle mirrors the original catalyst particle in size and shape.
- [x] Heat removal is the **dominant design constraint**: recycle gas loop removes ~95% of exotherm ($\Delta H \approx -95$ to $-105$ kJ/mol).
- [x] Mass balance: $\dot{m}_{gas} = R_p (-\Delta H) V_{bed} / [C_p \Delta T]$ — memorize.
- [x] Recycle gas composition: ethylene + H$_2$ (MW controller) + N$_2$ (diluent) + 1-butene/1-hexene (comonomer for LLDPE).
- [x] Space-time yield: 60–180 kg polymer / (m³ bed · h) — an order of magnitude higher than slurry CSTRs.
- [x] Catalyst productivity: 5,000–50,000 kg polymer / kg catalyst — modern high-activity catalysts eliminate catalyst-removal ("de-ashing") steps.
- [x] Key engineering advantage of FBR polymerization: **no solvent**, **isothermal**, **continuous**, **uniform granules**, **scalable**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cracking: Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cracking: Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** is a continuous petroleum refining process that converts high-molecular-weight, low-value hydrocarbon fractions (such as Vacuum Gas Oil, VGO) into lighter, high-value products (gasoline, LPG, light olefins) by contacting the feed with a hot, finely divided, fluidized zeolite-based catalyst at elevated temperatures (typically **500–550 °C**) and near-atmospheric pressure (**1–3 atm**). The process is unique because the catalyst is continuously circulated between a **riser reactor** (where endothermic cracking occurs) and a **regenerator** (where exothermic coke combustion reactivates the catalyst), making it a textbook example of a commercial-scale **fluidized bed reactor** application.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** FCC is the single most important industrial application of fluidization technology. Approximately **40% of world gasoline** production depends on FCC units. For examination purposes, students must clearly distinguish between the *riser* (transport reactor, dilute phase) and the *regenerator* (bubbling/turbulent fluidized bed, dense phase).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **popcorn machine with a self-cleaning heater**:

- The **un-popped kernels** (heavy oil feed) are dropped into a hot chamber (riser reactor) where they undergo a rapid transformation (cracking) into fluffy popcorn (light hydrocarbons).
- The **waste husks** (coke) accumulate on the heating element (catalyst) over time.
- Instead of stopping the machine to scrape off the husks, a **second air-blower system** (regenerator) continuously burns off the husks, sending the cleaned element back to the popping chamber.
- The hot, cleaned element then brings its heat back to the popping chamber — a perfect **thermal feedback loop**.

In FCC, the **"popcorn machine"** is the riser, the **"heating element"** is the zeolite catalyst, the **"husks"** are carbonaceous coke deposits, and the **"second air-blower"** is the regenerator. The catalyst physically moves like a fluid (hence *fluidized*), but it is a solid — exactly what makes this process an engineering marvel of heat integration and mass transfer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Distinction:** In *thermal cracking* (e.g., visbreaking), the feedstock is merely heated and cracks spontaneously. In *catalytic cracking* (FCC), a solid catalyst lowers the activation energy, enables operation at lower temperatures (**≈ 500 °C** vs. **> 800 °C**), and dramatically improves selectivity toward branched alkanes and aromatics (the high-octane components of gasoline).

### 1.3 Historical & Industrial Context

- **Invented:** 1942 by Eugene Houdry (fixed-bed Houdry unit)
- **First Fluidized FCC:** 1942 (Standard Oil of New Jersey / MIT)
- **Modern Zeolite Catalyst (FCC-Z):** 1962 (Mobil Oil, Zeolite Y)
- **Modern Unit Capacity:** 50,000 – 200,000 barrels per stream day (BPSD)
- **Global Throughput:** Over **15 million barrels/day** of feedstock processed worldwide

> [!TIP]
> **Why zeolites revolutionized FCC:** Zeolite Y (a faujasite) has uniform pore openings of **7.4 Å**, which selectively admits straight-chain and slightly branched hydrocarbons while excluding large polycyclic aromatics. This *shape selectivity* is the molecular origin of high gasoline selectivity and low coke yield.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The FCC Process — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The FCC unit consists of **three coupled fluidized systems** operating in continuous material recycle:

1. **Riser Reactor (Transport / Dilute-Phase Fluidization):**
   - Pre-heated liquid feed is atomized through **feed nozzles** at the base of the riser using **stream** as a dispersion medium.
   - Hot regenerated catalyst (**≈ 700 °C**) enters from the regenerator standpipe and contacts the oil in **co-current upflow**.
   - Endothermic cracking reactions occur in **2–4 seconds** (extremely short residence time to minimize secondary cracking and coke).
   - Hydrocarbon vapors and coked catalyst exit at the top into a **disengager / cyclone system**.

2. **Catalyst Stripper (Bubbling Fluidized Bed):**
   - Spent catalyst from the reactor is stripped with **superheated steam** in a baffled bubbling bed to desorb entrained hydrocarbon vapors (≈ 0.5–1.0 % of feed) before regeneration.

3. **Regenerator (Turbulent / Bubbling Fluidized Bed):**
   - Coked catalyst enters the regenerator where **air** is blown in to fluidize the bed.
   - Coke (mostly carbon with some H, S, N) is burned:
     $$ \text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5 \; \text{kJ/mol} $$
     $$ \text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} \quad \Delta H = -110.5 \; \text{kJ/mol} $$
   - The **exothermic heat of combustion** raises catalyst temperature to **680–730 °C**, providing the enthalpy needed to drive the endothermic cracking reactions in the riser.
   - Flue gas (containing **CO, CO₂, SO_x, NO_x**) passes through cyclones and an **CO boiler** (or catalytic converter) to recover energy and meet environmental norms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Coupling is the Heart of FCC:** The regenerator acts as a *chemical heat pump*. The heat released by burning **1 kg of coke** (≈ **32,000 kJ**) is sufficient to crack **~10 kg of oil feed**. Without this autothermal coupling, FCC would be economically unviable.

### 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Definition / Typical Value |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **Conversion (X)** | $X = \dfrac{F_{\text{feed}} - F_{\text{HCO}}}{F_{\text{feed}}} \times 100$ | % | Fraction of feed converted to < 220 °C products; typically **65–80 %** |
| 2 | **Selectivity (Sᵢ)** | $S_i = \dfrac{F_i}{F_{\text{feed}} - F_{\text{HCO}}}$ | % | Fraction of converted feed appearing as product *i* |
| 3 | **Yield (Yᵢ)** | $Y_i = X \times S_i = \dfrac{F_i}{F_{\text{feed}}} \times 100$ | % | Product *i* yield per unit feed |
| 4 | **Catalyst-to-Oil Ratio (C/O)** | $\left(\dfrac{C}{O}\right) = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{cat, riser}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{oil, riser}}}$ | kg/kg | **5–10** typical; controls conversion & coke on catalyst |
| 5 | **Coke on Catalyst (ΔC)** | $\Delta C = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{coke}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{cat, riser}}} \times 100$ | wt% | Typically **0.6–1.2 wt%**; sets regenerator duty |
| 6 | **Weight Hourly Space Velocity (WHSV)** | $\text{WHSV} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{oil}}}{W_{\text{cat, inventory}}}$ | hr⁻¹ | **8–20 hr⁻¹** |
| 7 | **Catalyst Circulation Rate** | $\dot{m}_{\text{cat}} = \left(\dfrac{C}{O}\right) \times \dot{m}_{\text{oil}}$ | kg/hr | Determines regenerator size |
| 8 | **Air Requirement (stoichiometric, full CO₂)** | $\dot{m}_{\text{air,stoich}} = \dot{m}_{\text{coke}} \times \dfrac{(32/12) + 0.5 \times (\text{H/C})}{0.232}$ | kg/hr | 0.232 = mass fraction O₂ in air |
| 9 | **Excess Air Ratio** | $e = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{air,actual}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{air,stoich}}}$ | — | **1.05–1.20** |
| 10 | **Regenerator Heat Balance** | $\dot{m}_{\text{cat}} \, C_{p,\text{cat}} \, \Delta T = \dot{m}_{\text{coke}} \, \Delta H_{\text{comb}} - \dot{Q}_{\text{loss}}$ | kW | Equates coke-burning heat to riser duty |
| 11 | **Average Catalyst Particle Size** | $d_p = 60 - 80 \; \mu\text{m}$ | µm | Geldart Group A powder |
| 12 | **Catalyst Bulk Density (Aerated)** | $\rho_{\text{b,aerated}} = 800 - 900$ | kg/m³ | Used in regenerator bed design |
| 13 | **Catalyst Skeletal Density** | $\rho_{\text{sk}} = 2200 - 2500$ | kg/m³ | Used in slip/circulation calculations |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these formulas matter in KTU exams:** Conversion, C/O ratio, and regenerator heat balance appear in **almost every numerical problem** in Module 4. Memorize the three conversions (Conversion, Selectivity, Yield) as a *triplet* and the heat balance as the *master equation* linking regenerator and riser.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery Economics:** FCC is the **primary gasoline-producing unit**, accounting for ~45% of a refinery's total profit margin.
- **Propylene On-Purpose:** Modern FCC units with **ZSM-5 additive** are the second-largest source of propylene (after steam cracking).
- **Integration with Downstream Units:** FCC naphtha feeds alkylation, MTBE, ETBE, and catalytic reforming units.
- **Environmental Importance:** Modern FCCUs use **SO_x transfer additives, NO_x reduction additives, and particulate cyclones** — making the study of its hydrodynamics and emission control highly interdisciplinary.
- **Fluidization Research Bed:** FCC has driven much of the modern understanding of **Geldart Group A powders, jetting, slugging, and cyclone design**.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation I — Regenerator Heat Balance & Catalyst Circulation Rate

The regenerator must supply **all** the heat required by the endothermic riser reactor. We will derive the catalyst circulation rate from a steady-state energy balance.

#### Step 1 — Identify All Heat Inlets to the Catalyst Stream

The catalyst enters the regenerator at temperature $T_{s,\text{out}}$ (spent catalyst temperature) and leaves at $T_{rg}$ (regenerated catalyst temperature). The heat absorbed by the catalyst is:

$$
Q_{\text{cat}} = \dot{m}_{\text{cat}} \, C_{p,\text{cat}} \, (T_{rg} - T_{s,\text{out}})
$$

where $C_{p,\text{cat}} \approx 1.0 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$ for zeolite catalysts.

#### Step 2 — Heat Released by Coke Combustion

The coke is typically modeled as pure carbon for heat-balance purposes, with $\Delta H_{\text{comb}} = -32{,}790 \; \text{kJ/kg}$ (for C → CO₂). In practice, hydrogen and sulfur in coke also contribute:

$$
Q_{\text{coke}} = \dot{m}_{\text{coke}} \, \Delta H_{\text{comb}} + \dot{m}_{\text{H,coke}} \, \Delta H_{\text{H}} + \dot{m}_{\text{S,coke}} \, \Delta H_{\text{S}}
$$

#### Step 3 — Energy Demands in the Riser (Heat Sinks)

The hot regenerated catalyst must supply the following enthalpy to the oil:

$$
Q_{\text{riser}} = \dot{m}_{\text{oil}} \, C_{p,\text{oil}} \, (T_{\text{reac}} - T_{\text{feed}}) + \dot{m}_{\text{oil}} \, \lambda_{\text{oil}} + \dot{m}_{\text{oil}} \, X \, \Delta H_{\text{crack}} + \dot{m}_{\text{steam}} \, \lambda_{\text{steam}}
$$

where the terms represent: sensible heat of liquid oil, latent heat of vaporization, endothermic heat of cracking (typically **+200 to +400 kJ/kg of converted feed**), and steam enthalpy.

#### Step 4 — Steady-State Regenerator Heat Balance

$$
Q_{\text{coke}} = Q_{\text{cat}} + Q_{\text{loss}} = \dot{m}_{\text{cat}} \, C_{p,\text{cat}} \, (T_{rg} - T_{s,\text{out}}) + \dot{Q}_{\text{loss}}
$$

#### Step 5 — Solve for Catalyst Circulation Rate

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{cat}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{coke}} \, \Delta H_{\text{comb}} - \dot{Q}_{\text{loss}}}{C_{p,\text{cat}} \, (T_{rg} - T_{s,\text{out}})}
$$

#### Step 6 — Compute C/O Ratio

$$
\left(\dfrac{C}{O}\right) = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{cat}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{oil}}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** If $T_{s,\text{out}} \approx T_{\text{reac}}$ (a common simplification), then $T_{rg} - T_{s,\text{out}}$ becomes the *catalyst temperature drop across the riser* (typically **80–150 K**). This simplification appears in **70% of KTU numerical problems** on this topic.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Air Requirement for the Regenerator

The stoichiometric oxygen demand for burning coke of composition $\text{C}_a\text{H}_b\text{S}_c$ (per kg) is:

$$
m_{\text{O}_2, \text{stoich}} = \dfrac{32}{12} a + \dfrac{32}{4} b + \dfrac{32}{32} c = 2.667 a + 8.00 b + 1.00 c \quad [\text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg coke}]
$$

Dividing by the mass fraction of O₂ in air (0.232) and applying the excess air ratio $e$:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = e \cdot \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{coke}} \, m_{\text{O}_2, \text{stoich}}}{0.232}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

A regenerator burns **5,000 kg/hr** of coke modeled as 90% C, 8% H, 2% S (by mass), with $e = 1.10$.

Stoichiometric O₂ per kg coke:

$$
m_{\text{O}_2} = 2.667(0.90) + 8.00(0.08) + 1.00(0.02) = 2.40 + 0.64 + 0.02 = 3.06 \; \text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg coke}
$$

Total O₂ required:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{O}_2} = 5000 \times 3.06 = 15{,}300 \; \text{kg/hr}
$$

Total air required:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = 1.10 \times \dfrac{15{,}300}{0.232} = 72{,}543 \; \text{kg/hr}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Conversion, Selectivity, and Yield Linkage

Consider a feed $F$ producing gasoline ($G$), LCO ($L$), HCO ($H$, unconverted), and coke ($C_k$).

**Conversion:**

$$
X = \dfrac{F - H}{F} \times 100
$$

**Selectivity of Gasoline:**

$$
S_G = \dfrac{G}{F - H}
$$

**Yield of Gasoline:**

$$
Y_G = \dfrac{G}{F} = X \cdot S_G
$$

**Overall Mass Balance:**

$$
F = G + L + H + C_k + (\text{dry gas + LPG losses})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Identity to memorize:** $Y = X \times S$. This is the single most-tested identity in FCC product analysis. Every KTU numerical on yield uses this.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Study of FCC Process Parameters

| Parameter | Low Severity Operation | High Severity Operation | Engineering Effect |
|-----------|------------------------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| **Reactor Temperature** | 480–510 °C | 530–560 °C | Higher T → more gasoline, more dry gas, more coke |
| **C/O Ratio** | 4–6 | 8–12 | Higher C/O → more conversion, more coke on catalyst |
| **WHSV** | 15–20 hr⁻¹ | 5–10 hr⁻¹ | Lower WHSV → longer contact → more conversion |
| **Catalyst Activity** | Equilibrium E-Cat (low) | Fresh / Additive-boosted | Higher activity → more gasoline, less coke |
| **Coke on Regenerated Catalyst** | 0.05–0.10 wt% | 0.15–0.30 wt% | Higher residual coke → lower riser activity |
| **Product Slate Bias** | Distillate mode (more LCO) | Gasoline mode (more gasoline) | Refinery selects based on market |
| **Feedstock Type** | Paraffinic VGO | Aromatic / Resid | Aromatic feeds give more coke & LCO |
| **ZSM-5 Additive** | Absent | Present | Boosts C₃= / C₄= (propylene, isobutylene) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Recognition:** When the problem says *"maximize gasoline"*, the answer is *high C/O + moderate T + high catalyst activity + low WHSV*. When it says *"maximize middle distillate"*, the answer is *low C/O + lower T + higher WHSV*.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 FCC Unit — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps all major equipment, stream flows, and the catalyst regeneration loop. This is the **canonical schematic** every KTU student must be able to reproduce from memory in an examination.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FFC[FEED PREPARATION]
        f1[Feed Oil VGO or Atmospheric Residue]
        f2[Heated Feed at 350 C]
        f3[Atomizing Steam 3 to 5 wt%]
    end

    subgraph RSR[RISER REACTOR DILUTE PHASE]
        r1[Feed Nozzles]
        r2[Riser Tube 30 to 45 m Height]
        r3[Reactor Cyclones and Disengager]
        r4[Vapor Product Outlet to Fractionator]
    end

    subgraph STP[CATALYST STRIPPER]
        s1[Stripper Baffles]
        s2[Superheated Steam Stripping]
    end

    subgraph REG[REGENERATOR TURBULENT FLUIDIZED BED]
        g1[Air Blower and Distributor]
        g2[Regenerator Dense Bed 680 to 730 C]
        g3[Regenerator Cyclones]
        g4[CO Boiler and ESP for Flue Gas Cleaning]
        g5[Standpipe and Slide Valve]
    end

    subgraph FRC[MAIN FRACTIONATOR AND GAS PLANT]
        p1[Wet Gas Compressor]
        p2[Primary Absorber and Debutanizer]
        p3[Main Fractionator 220 to 350 C LCO]
        p4[Gasoline Draw 220 C Naphtha]
    end

    f1 --> f2
    f2 --> r1
    f3 --> r1
    g5 --> r1
    r1 --> r2
    r2 --> r3
    r3 --> r4
    r3 --> s1
    s1 --> s2
    s2 --> g2
    g1 --> g2
    g2 --> g3
    g3 --> g4
    g2 --> g5
    r4 --> p1
    p1 --> p2
    p1 --> p3
    p3 --> p4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading Guide:** Trace the catalyst loop anticlockwise: Regenerator → Standpipe → Riser → Stripper → Regenerator. This continuous loop is the **defining feature** of FCC and the reason it is classified as a *circulating fluidized bed* (CFB) at the riser, coupled with a *bubbling/turbulent fluidized bed* in the regenerator.

### 4.2 ASCII Pressure–Temperature Profile Along the Riser

The reaction mixture undergoes a sharp temperature drop due to the endothermic nature of cracking:

```
   T (°C) Profile along Riser height (m)
   700 ┤●  Catalyst inlet (regenerated)
       │ \
   650 ┤  \
       │   \   Cracking zone
   600 ┤    \
       │     \
   550 ┤      ●  Riser outlet (vapor + coked cat)
       │       \
   500 ┤        \
       │         \  (Vapor + catalyst enter cyclones)
   450 ┤__________●_____ ______________________
       0      10     20     30     40     50
                    Height (m)
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Visual Trick:** Always draw the catalyst **inlet above** the riser outlet on the temperature axis — this is the visual signature of an *endothermic* circulating reactor. A *reverse* profile would indicate an exothermic reaction (e.g., regenerator).

### 4.3 Catalyst Composition Schematic (Zeolite FCC Catalyst)

```
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  FCC CATALYST PARTICLE (60–80 µm)                     │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │  MATRIX (60–70 %)                             │    │
│  │  - Alumina, Silica-Alumina, Clay              │    │
│  │  - Provides macropores for pre-cracking       │    │
│  │  - Binds the zeolite                          │    │
│  ├───────────────────────────────────────────────┤    │
│  │  ZEOLITE Y (Faujasite) (25–35 %)              │    │
│  │  - SiO₂/Al₂O₃ ratio 2.5–6                    │    │
│  │  - 7.4 Å pore openings                       │    │
│  │  - Provides SHAPE SELECTIVE cracking          │    │
│  ├───────────────────────────────────────────────┤    │
│  │  BINDER + ADDITIVES (5–10 %)                  │    │
│  │  - ZSM-5 additive (boosts C₃=/C₄=)            │    │
│  │  - SOx transfer (MgO, spinels)                │    │
│  │  - NOx reduction (Cu, Co oxides)              │    │
│  │  - CO oxidation promoter (Pt)                │    │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC). List the main products obtained and the typical operating temperature.**

**Model Answer:**
FCC is a continuous catalytic cracking process in which heavy hydrocarbon feed (VGO/atmospheric residue) is contacted with a hot, fluidized zeolite catalyst in a riser reactor. The catalyst is continuously circulated to a regenerator where coke is burned off, supplying the heat needed for the endothermic cracking reaction. The main products are: dry gas (C₁–C₂), LPG (C₃–C₄), gasoline (C₅–220 °C), LCO (220–350 °C), HCO (350 °C⁺), and coke. Typical riser outlet temperature: **520–540 °C**; regenerator bed temperature: **680–730 °C**.

#### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the significance of the Catalyst-to-Oil (C/O) ratio in an FCC unit.**

**Model Answer:**
The C/O ratio is defined as the mass flow rate of catalyst entering the riser divided by the mass flow rate of oil feed. It typically ranges from **5 to 10 kg/kg**. Its significance: (i) Higher C/O ratio increases conversion (more catalyst = more active sites), (ii) it determines the **coke yield** and therefore the **regenerator air/heat duty**, (iii) it controls the **catalyst temperature drop** across the riser, (iv) an optimum C/O maximizes gasoline yield while avoiding overcracking to dry gas.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) With a neat flow diagram, describe the working of a Fluid Catalytic Cracking unit. Highlight the role of the regenerator and the catalyst stripper. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The FCC unit consists of three coupled fluidized systems: the riser reactor, the catalyst stripper, and the regenerator. Liquid feed is pre-heated to ~350 °C, mixed with atomizing steam, and injected through feed nozzles at the base of the riser. Regenerated catalyst at ~700 °C from the regenerator standpipe flows upward, contacting the oil in co-current dilute-phase transport. Endothermic cracking occurs in **2–4 seconds**, and the mixture of vapor products and coked catalyst exits the top of the riser into reactor cyclones.

The **catalyst stripper** is a baffled bubbling fluidized bed where the coked catalyst is contacted with superheated steam to desorb entrained hydrocarbon vapors (typically 0.5–1.0% of feed) before it enters the regenerator. This improves yield and prevents excessive coke burning load.

The **regenerator** is a turbulent fluidized bed where air is blown through a distributor plate. Coke is burned to CO₂ (and CO, which is oxidized in a CO boiler):

$$ \text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5 \; \text{kJ/mol} $$

The exothermic heat raises the catalyst temperature to 680–730 °C, and the regenerated catalyst flows down the standpipe (controlled by a slide valve) back to the riser base, completing the loop. Flue gas leaves through cyclones and an electrostatic precipitator. The regenerator thus serves three functions: (1) coke removal, (2) catalyst reactivation, and (3) heat supply to the riser.

**(b) An FCC unit processes 80,000 kg/hr of VGO feed with a C/O ratio of 7.0. The catalyst inventory in the regenerator is 50,000 kg, and the coke on catalyst drops from 0.95 wt% (spent) to 0.05 wt% (regenerated). Calculate: (i) the catalyst circulation rate, (ii) the coke burning rate, and (iii) the WHSV based on regenerator inventory. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Catalyst Circulation Rate:**

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{cat}} = (C/O) \times \dot{m}_{\text{oil}} = 7.0 \times 80{,}000 = 560{,}000 \; \text{kg/hr}
$$

**(ii) Coke Burning Rate:**

$$
\Delta C = 0.95\% - 0.05\% = 0.90\% \text{ of catalyst}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{coke}} = 0.0090 \times 560{,}000 = 5{,}040 \; \text{kg/hr}
$$

**(iii) Weight Hourly Space Velocity:**

$$
\text{WHSV} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{oil}}}{W_{\text{cat,inv}}} = \dfrac{80{,}000}{50{,}000} = 1.6 \; \text{hr}^{-1}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:** (1) Many students write 0.95 − 0.05 = 0.0090 incorrectly as 0.0090% or 0.90; remember this is a *fraction*, not a percentage, in the multiplication. (2) WHSV is **per hour**, not per second. (3) The catalyst circulation rate is **per hour**, not per day. **Lose 1 mark per unit error.**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) Derive the steady-state heat balance equation for an FCC regenerator and show how the catalyst circulation rate is obtained from it. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The regenerator operates adiabatically (with small losses), so all heat released by coke combustion must be carried by the catalyst stream to the riser.

**Energy released by coke combustion:**

$$
Q_{\text{coke}} = \dot{m}_{\text{coke}} \, \Delta H_{\text{comb}}
$$

where $\Delta H_{\text{comb}} = 32{,}790 \; \text{kJ/kg}$ for coke to CO₂.

**Heat absorbed by catalyst from spent to regenerated state:**

$$
Q_{\text{cat}} = \dot{m}_{\text{cat}} \, C_{p,\text{cat}} \, (T_{rg} - T_{s,\text{out}})
$$

where $T_{rg}$ = regenerator bed temperature, $T_{s,\text{out}}$ = spent catalyst leaving the stripper.

**Steady-state heat balance (including heat losses $\dot{Q}_{\text{loss}}$):**

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{coke}} \, \Delta H_{\text{comb}} = \dot{m}_{\text{cat}} \, C_{p,\text{cat}} \, (T_{rg} - T_{s,\text{out}}) + \dot{Q}_{\text{loss}}
$$

**Solving for catalyst circulation rate:**

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{cat}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{coke}} \, \Delta H_{\text{comb}} - \dot{Q}_{\text{loss}}}{C_{p,\text{cat}} \, (T_{rg} - T_{s,\text{out}})}
$$

This equation shows that: (1) higher coke burning releases more heat, (2) lower temperature drop requires higher circulation rate, and (3) C/O ratio is directly obtained by dividing by oil feed rate.

**(b) An FCC regenerator burns 6,000 kg/hr of coke (modeled as pure carbon, ΔH = 32,790 kJ/kg). Catalyst $C_p$ = 1.0 kJ/(kg·K), $T_{rg}$ = 720 °C, $T_{s,\text{out}}$ = 530 °C, oil feed = 90,000 kg/hr, $\dot{Q}_{\text{loss}}$ = 4.0 × 10⁶ kJ/hr. Calculate the catalyst circulation rate and the C/O ratio. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Heat released by coke combustion:**

$$
Q_{\text{coke}} = 6000 \times 32{,}790 = 1.9674 \times 10^{8} \; \text{kJ/hr}
$$

**Step 2 — Net heat available for catalyst heating:**

$$
Q_{\text{net}} = 1.9674 \times 10^{8} - 4.0 \times 10^{6} = 1.9274 \times 10^{8} \; \text{kJ/hr}
$$

**Step 3 — Catalyst circulation rate:**

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{cat}} = \dfrac{1.9274 \times 10^{8}}{1.0 \times (720 - 530)} = \dfrac{1.9274 \times 10^{8}}{190} = 1.0144 \times 10^{6} \; \text{kg/hr}
$$

**Step 4 — C/O Ratio:**

$$
(C/O) = \dfrac{1.0144 \times 10^{6}}{90{,}000} \approx 11.27
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students frequently forget to subtract $\dot{Q}_{\text{loss}}$ or use $T_{rg} - T_{\text{feed}}$ instead of $T_{rg} - T_{s,\text{out}}$. Also, the temperature difference must be in **Kelvin**, but since it is a *difference*, °C and K are numerically equivalent. Do **not** add 273.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **FCC = Riser Reactor + Catalyst Stripper + Regenerator** — the *trinity* of fluidized systems in one unit.
- **Catalyst:** Zeolite Y (Faujasite, 7.4 Å pores), 60–80 µm, Geldart Group A.
- **Operating Conditions:** Riser T = **520–540 °C**, Regenerator T = **680–730 °C**, Pressure ≈ **1–3 atm**.
- **Residence Time:** Riser = **2–4 s**; Regenerator = **5–10 min**.
- **C/O Ratio:** Typically **5–10**; controls conversion and regenerator duty.
- **WHSV:** Typically **8–20 hr⁻¹**; inverse relationship with conversion.
- **Three Identities to Memorize:**
  1. $X = (F_{\text{feed}} - F_{\text{HCO}})/F_{\text{feed}}$
  2. $S_i = F_i / (F_{\text{feed}} - F_{\text{HCO}})$
  3. $Y_i = X \times S_i$
- **Master Equation:** $\dot{m}_{\text{cat}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{coke}} \, \Delta H_{\text{comb}} - \dot{Q}_{\text{loss}}}{C_{p,\text{cat}} \, (T_{rg} - T_{s,\text{out}})}$
- **Coke Combustion Heat:** ~32,790 kJ/kg (C → CO₂).
- **Excess Air Ratio:** Typically **1.05–1.20** for complete combustion.
- **Key Chemistry:** Primary cracking → secondary reactions (isomerization, alkylation, H-transfer) → coke formation.
- **Product Slate:** Dry gas + LPG + Gasoline + LCO + HCO (unconverted) + Coke.
- **Industrial Role:** ~**40–45%** of world gasoline; second-largest source of propylene (with ZSM-5).
- **Environmental:** Modern FCCUs include **SOx transfer additives, NOx reduction additives, CO promoter, ESP, and wet gas scrubber** for emission control.
- **Examiner's Mantra:** *"If the question says FCC, draw the catalyst loop — riser → stripper → regenerator → riser — and label at least four temperature points."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** Always present the **catalyst loop** in your answer diagrams. A typical 14-mark question expects: (1) process description, (2) block/flow diagram, (3) heat/material balance, (4) numerical computation, and (5) a discussion of operating variables. Touching on **all five** earns full marks; missing the diagram alone costs 3–4 marks.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluid Coking and Flexi Coking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fluid Coking and Flexi Coking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Fluid Coking — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fluid Coking** is a *continuous, fluidized-bed thermal cracking process* in which heavy petroleum residues (vacuum residues, atmospheric residues, deasphalted bottoms, tar sands bitumen, shale oil) are converted into lighter distillate products and petroleum coke, using finely divided, hot coke particles as the *circulating heat-transfer medium* between a **Reactor** and a **Heater (Burner)** operating at elevated temperatures.

The process was commercialized by **ExxonMobil (Esso Research)** in **1954** and represents the first large-scale industrial deployment of the fluidized-bed concept for *non-catalytic, thermal conversion* of heavy hydrocarbons.

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Reactor** | Fluidized bed where cracking of feed occurs over hot coke particles |
| **Heater** | Fluidized bed where coke deposited on particles is partially burned to reheat the solids |
| **Cold Solids** | Coke particles leaving the heater (~590–620 °C) returning to reactor |
| **Hot Solids** | Coke particles leaving the reactor (~510–540 °C) going to heater |
| **Vapors** | Cracked products leaving the reactor overhead |

---

## 1.2 Flexi Coking — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Flexi Coking** is an *extension* of Fluid Coking in which a third fluidized vessel, called the **Gasifier**, is added. The net coke produced in the Fluid Coking heater is sent to the gasifier where it reacts with **air and steam** at **~870–980 °C** to produce a **low-BTU fuel gas (syngas)** consisting primarily of CO, CO₂, H₂, H₂O, N₂, and H₂S.

Net effect: **~95–99 % elimination of solid coke as a by-product**, converting it instead into a combustible gas that can be used internally as plant fuel.

| Process | Coke Output | Principal Products |
|---|---|---|
| **Fluid Coking** | Green petroleum coke (gross product) | Gases, Naphtha, LCGO, HCGO, Coke |
| **Flexi Coking** | Negligible / very low (net gasification) | Gases, Naphtha, LCGO, HCGO, **Low-BTU gas** |

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Kitchen Analogy — A Pressure-Cooker Curry vs. Self-Cleaning Oven**
>
> Imagine making a heavy curry in a *deep vessel* where spices (heavy residue) are continuously stirred by *red-hot sand* (coke particles). The sand absorbs the heat from a separate burner and brings it back — this is **Fluid Coking**.
>
> Now imagine, after cooking, instead of dumping the burnt sand into a bin (coke by-product), you *blow air through it* so the leftover carbon burns off and produces useful heat (low-BTU gas). This is **Flexi Coking** — a *closed-loop cooker with a built-in incinerator*.

### Engineering Significance
- Fluid Coking yields **~20–30 wt % coke** from vacuum residue feed; Flexi Coking converts nearly all of this to gas.
- Both processes are **continuous** (vs. delayed coking's batch operation).
- Both are **isothermal** because of the immense thermal mass of fluidized coke solids.

---

## 1.4 Standard Operating Metrics (Industry Baselines)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Reactor temperature:** **510 – 540 °C**
> - **Heater (burner) temperature:** **590 – 620 °C**
> - **Gasifier temperature:** **870 – 980 °C**
> - **Reactor pressure:** **~1.0 – 3.5 atm (gauge)** typically 1.7 atm
> - **Steam-to-coke ratio in gasifier:** **0.4 – 1.2 kg steam / kg coke**
> - **Solids (coke) density in bed:** **~ 450 – 600 kg/m³**
> - **Mean coke particle size:** **~ 100 – 600 µm (Geldart Group B)**
> - **Solids circulation rate:** **1000 – 1500 kg per tonne of feed**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Process Logic — FLUID COKING

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Fluid Coking unit consists of **two interconnected fluidized beds** operating as a *thermal solids loop*. The sequence is strict; missing one step breaks the energy balance.

**Step 1 — Feed Injection**
Preheated heavy feed (vacuum residue, 350–400 °C) is sprayed through proprietary **fountain-mode nozzles** near the bottom of the **Reactor**. The feed wets the hot coke particles ascending in the fluidized bed.

**Step 2 — Cracking on Coke Particles**
A thin liquid film of feed coats each particle. Heat conducted from the particle (~510–540 °C) cracks the long-chain hydrocarbons into:
- **Wet gas** (C₁–C₄): ~5–10 wt %
- **Naphtha (C₅–221 °C):** ~10–20 wt %
- **Light Coker Gas Oil (LCGO, 221–343 °C):** ~10–15 wt %
- **Heavy Coker Gas Oil (HCGO, 343–524 °C):** ~25–35 wt %
- **Coke (524 °C⁺ equivalent):** ~20–30 wt % deposited on particles

**Step 3 — Vapor–Solid Separation**
Reactor overhead vapors pass through **two-stage cyclones** to strip entrained fines; stripped fines are returned to the bed via diplegs.

**Step 4 — Stripping Section**
A steam-stripping zone at the reactor base removes residual hydrocarbon vapors from the descending solids.

**Step 5 — Solids Circulation to Heater**
Spent coke particles (~510 °C) flow down a standpipe into the **Heater**, where they are partially combusted with air to reheat them to ~610 °C.

**Step 6 — Combustion in Heater**
Approximately **15–25 % of the coke** is burned in the heater to provide process heat. Flue gas leaves overhead.

**Step 7 — Net Coke Withdrawal**
The remaining (~75–85 %) coke is withdrawn as **green petroleum coke product**, cooled, and stockpiled.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Process Logic — FLEXI COKING

> [!NOTE]
> Flexi Coking is essentially Fluid Coking with an **added third vessel (Gasifier)** and a **slight reduction in heater combustion duty**, so that coke is *routed* to gasification rather than withdrawn as product.

**Step 1–5:** Identical to Fluid Coking (Reactor → Stripping → Standpipe).

**Step 6 — Heater Operation Modulation**
In Flexi Coking, the heater burns only enough coke (and supplemental liquid fuel if needed) to maintain the ~610 °C solid temperature — the rest of the coke is routed to the gasifier.

**Step 7 — Gasification**
Coke particles enter the gasifier where they react with **air + steam** at **~870–980 °C**:

$$
\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{CO}_2 \quad (\text{Exothermic})
$$

$$
\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{CO} \quad (\text{Exothermic})
$$

$$
\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{CO} + \text{H}_2 \quad (\text{Endothermic})
$$

$$
\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad (\text{Water–gas shift})
$$

**Step 8 — Hot Solids Return**
The hot coke solids leaving the gasifier (~870 °C) are returned to the heater via another standpipe, supplying most of the heat required.

**Step 9 — Low-BTU Gas Recovery**
The product gas leaves the gasifier overhead and is cooled, desulfurized, and used as **refinery fuel gas**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below are the high-yield equations tested in KTU university examinations. Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute values to preserve markdown tables.

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G_s = \dfrac{W_s}{A_{or}}$ | Solids mass flux (circulation rate per unit orifice area) | kg/m²·s |
| 2 | $\tau = \dfrac{H_{bed}}{U_g - U_{mf}}$ | Mean gas residence time in bed | s |
| 3 | $\varepsilon_{mf} = \dfrac{U_{mf}}{U_t}$ (approx. for small particles) | Voidage at minimum fluidization | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\dot{Q}_{transfer} = \dot{m}_s \, c_{p,s} \, (T_{heater} - T_{reactor})$ | Heat carried by circulating coke solids | kW |
| 5 | $\text{Coke Yield (\%)} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{coke}}{\dot{m}_{feed}} \times 100$ | Conradson-to-coke conversion | wt % |
| 6 | $\text{Conversion (\%)} = \dfrac{m_{feed} - m_{524^+\text{ residue}}}{m_{feed}} \times 100$ | Cracking conversion above 524 °C | wt % |
| 7 | $\dfrac{\text{Air}}{\text{Coke}} \;\vert_{\text{gasifier}} = 0.4 - 1.2$ | Air-to-coke mass ratio in gasifier | kg/kg |
| 8 | $\text{LHV of low-BTU gas} \approx 4.4 - 5.6 \; \text{MJ/Nm}^3$ | Heating value of Flexi gasifier product | MJ/Nm³ |
| 9 | $\Delta H_{vap,feed} = m_{feed} \, \lambda_{feed}$ | Heat to vaporize liquid feed in reactor | kW |
| 10 | $\dot{m}_s = \dfrac{Q_{required}}{c_{p,s}\,(T_{heater}-T_{reactor})}$ | Required solids circulation rate | kg/s |

---

## 2.4 Why Fluidized Beds Are the *Only* Viable Choice

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" Behind the Fluid Bed**
>
> 1. **Massive thermal inertia** — thousands of tonnes of coke solids act as a heat reservoir, making the reactor virtually **isothermal**. Coke deposition cannot cause runaway hotspots.
> 2. **Excellent heat transfer** — gas–solid heat-transfer coefficients reach **400–800 W/m²·K**, orders of magnitude higher than in delayed coker drums.
> 3. **Continuous operation** — no batch cycle; feed and product withdrawal are simultaneous.
> 4. **High liquid feed handling** — heavy residues (CCR up to **25 wt %**) can be sprayed onto fluidized particles without agglomeration or fouling.
> 5. **Tolerant of ash and metals** — vanadium, nickel, and iron deposits report to the coke phase and exit with the circulating solids.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- Refineries use Fluid Coking to **upgrade bottom-of-the-barrel** residues to valuable distillates.
- Flexi Coking is preferred when the refiner wants to **avoid selling low-value coke** and instead generate **internal fuel gas**, improving overall refinery economics.
- Both processes integrate downstream with **hydrocracking** or **fluid catalytic cracking (FCC)** units.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: Required Solids Circulation Rate

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement** (typical KTU 14-mark question pattern):
> A Fluid Coking unit processes **10,000 kg/h** of vacuum residue. The reactor operates at **525 °C** and the heater at **610 °C**. The feed enters at **300 °C** with a mean specific heat of **2.3 kJ/kg·K** and an effective heat of vaporization of **290 kJ/kg**. The reactor must maintain vapor temperature at **525 °C**. The mean specific heat of coke particles is **1.05 kJ/kg·K**. Calculate the **required solids circulation rate** (neglecting heat losses).

---

### Step 1 — Enumerate All Heat Loads at the Reactor

**Heat required to warm liquid feed from 300 °C → 525 °C (vapor exit):**

$$
\dot{Q}_{1} = \dot{m}_{feed} \, c_{p,l} \, (T_{reactor} - T_{feed,in})
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{1} = 10{,}000 \times 2.3 \times (525 - 300) = 10{,}000 \times 2.3 \times 225
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{1} = 5{,}175{,}000 \; \text{kJ/h} = 1437.5 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Heat required to vaporize and complete cracking (given as effective enthalpy):**

$$
\dot{Q}_{2} = \dot{m}_{feed} \times \lambda_{eff} = 10{,}000 \times 290 = 2{,}900{,}000 \; \text{kJ/h}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{2} = 805.56 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Total heat demand at the reactor:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{reactor} = \dot{Q}_{1} + \dot{Q}_{2}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{reactor} = 1437.5 + 805.56 = 2243.06 \; \text{kW}
$$

---

### Step 2 — Apply Solids Heat-Carrying Equation

The heat delivered by coke particles descending from heater (610 °C) to reactor (525 °C):

$$
\dot{Q}_{transfer} = \dot{m}_s \, c_{p,s} \, (T_{heater} - T_{reactor})
$$

Setting this equal to $\dot{Q}_{reactor}$ (steady-state):

$$
\dot{m}_s \, c_{p,s} \, (T_{heater} - T_{reactor}) = \dot{Q}_{reactor}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_s = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{reactor}}{c_{p,s} \, (T_{heater} - T_{reactor})}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$
\dot{m}_s = \dfrac{2243.06}{1.05 \times (610 - 525)} = \dfrac{2243.06}{1.05 \times 85}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_s = \dfrac{2243.06}{89.25} = 25.13 \; \text{kg/s}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_s = 25.13 \times 3600 = 90{,}474 \; \text{kg/h}
$$

---

### Step 4 — Convert to Solids-to-Feed Ratio (Industry Metric)

$$
\text{S/F} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_s}{\dot{m}_{feed}} = \dfrac{90{,}474}{10{,}000} = 9.05 \; \text{kg coke / kg feed}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Industrial Fluid Coking plants operate at **S/F ≈ 8–12**, so our derived value of **9.05** is consistent with field data. ✅

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation: Coke Combustion Air Required in the Heater

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement**:
> The same unit produces **~25 wt % coke on feed**, of which only **20 %** is burned in the heater (the rest is product in Fluid Coking or routed to gasifier in Flexi Coking). Calculate the **air flow (Nm³/h)** required, assuming air = 21 % O₂ by volume, O₂ stoichiometry: C + O₂ → CO₂.

### Step 1 — Coke Burned Per Hour

$$
\dot{m}_{coke,burned} = 10{,}000 \times 0.25 \times 0.20 = 500 \; \text{kg/h}
$$

### Step 2 — Moles of Carbon Burned

$$
\dot{n}_{C} = \dfrac{500}{12} = 41.67 \; \text{kmol/h}
$$

### Step 3 — Stoichiometric O₂ Required

From $\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$, the molar ratio is 1:1:

$$
\dot{n}_{O_2} = 41.67 \; \text{kmol/h}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Air (with 100 % excess for completeness; 21 % O₂ by vol.)

For stoichiometric air:

$$
\dot{V}_{air,stoich} = \dfrac{41.67}{0.21} = 198.4 \; \text{kmol/h}
$$

$$
\dot{V}_{air,stoich} = 198.4 \times 22.4 = 4445 \; \text{Nm}^3/\text{h}
$$

### Step 5 — Practical Air with 15 % Excess

$$
\dot{V}_{air,actual} = 4445 \times 1.15 = 5112 \; \text{Nm}^3/\text{h}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Coke, Heat & Gasifier Performance

| Variable | Fluid Coking | Flexi Coking | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coke as gross product | **20–30 wt %** | **~1–2 wt %** | Explains economic shift |
| Heater combustion duty | Moderate | Reduced | Heat comes from gasifier solids |
| Gasifier air/steam | None | **Air 0.6–0.9 kg/kg coke; Steam 0.5–1.0 kg/kg coke** | Determines syngas LHV |
| Low-BTU gas yield | 0 | **~0.35–0.55 Nm³/kg coke** | Refinery fuel offset |
| Reactor isothermality | High | High | Bed ΔT < **2–3 °C** |
| Solids-to-feed ratio | **8–12 kg/kg** | **10–14 kg/kg** | Higher loadings in Flexi |
| Vanadium tolerance | Up to **300 ppm** in feed | Up to **300 ppm** | Metals exit in solids |

---

## 3.4 Common Boundary Conditions Used in Numericals

> [!TIP]
> When solving Fluid/Flexi Coking numericals in the KTU exam, **always assume**:
> 1. **Steady-state** operation (no accumulation of solids).
> 2. **Negligible** heat loss to surroundings unless specified (typically 2–5 %).
> 3. **Coke contains 90 wt % C, 5 wt % H, 5 wt % S + ash.**
> 4. **Solids $c_p \approx 1.05$ kJ/kg·K**, **gas $c_p \approx 1.9$ kJ/kg·K**.
> 5. **Air composition: 21 % O₂, 79 % N₂ by volume.**
> 6. **Molecular weights: C = 12, H₂ = 2, O₂ = 32, N₂ = 28, CO = 28, CO₂ = 44.**

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Fluid Coking Process

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Mermaid diagram below traces the *solid and gas streams* between the two fluidized vessels of a Fluid Coking unit. Node IDs are alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid syntax safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Feed Preheater] --> A2[Reactor 525 C]
    A2 -->|Wet Gas Naphtha LCGO HCGO| A3[Two Stage Cyclones]
    A3 --> A4[Fractionator Main Column]
    A4 --> A5[Gas Recovery]
    A4 --> A6[LCGO HCGO Draw Offs]
    A2 -->|Spent Coke Particles| A7[Stripping Zone Steam]
    A7 -->|Cold Solids 525 C| A8[Standpipe To Heater]
    A8 --> A9[Heater Burner 610 C]
    A9 -->|Reheated Coke 610 C| A10[Standpipe To Reactor]
    A9 -->|Flue Gas CO2 H2O| A11[Heat Recovery Boiler]
    A9 -->|Net Coke Product| A12[Green Coke Storage]
    A11 --> A1
```

**Description of Block Flow:**
- **Feed preheater** warms incoming vacuum residue using recovered flue-gas heat.
- **Reactor** cracks the feed onto hot coke particles.
- **Cyclones** strip fines from cracked vapors.
- **Fractionator** separates product slates.
- **Stripping steam** removes entrained hydrocarbons from descending solids.
- **Standpipes** maintain moving-bed flow of coke between vessels (acting as non-mechanical valves).
- **Heater** provides partial combustion to reheat solids and **withdraws product coke**.
- **Heat recovery boiler** captures waste-heat integration.

---

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Flexi Coking Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Feed Preheater] --> B2[Reactor 525 C]
    B2 -->|Wet Gas Naphtha LCGO HCGO| B3[Two Stage Cyclones]
    B3 --> B4[Fractionator Main Column]
    B4 --> B5[Gas Recovery]
    B4 --> B6[LCGO HCGO Draw Offs]
    B2 -->|Spent Coke 525 C| B7[Stripping Zone]
    B7 -->|Cold Solids| B8[Standpipe To Heater]
    B8 --> B9[Heater 610 C Partial Burn]
    B9 -->|Hot Solids 610 C| B10[Standpipe To Reactor]
    B9 -->|Hot Solids 870 C| B11[Gasifier 870 to 980 C]
    B11 -->|Air Steam Injection| B12[Air Blower Steam Header]
    B11 -->|Low BTU Gas CO CO2 H2 N2 H2S| B13[Quench and Desulfurization]
    B13 --> B14[Refinery Fuel Gas Network]
    B11 -->|Hot Solids 870 C| B10
    B12 --> B11
    B14 --> B1
```

**Key Differences vs. Fluid Coking:**
- A **third fluidized vessel — the Gasifier** — is interlocked between heater and reactor standpipes.
- The **gasifier receives hot coke** from the heater and supplies *even hotter* solids back.
- **Net coke product** is essentially eliminated; instead, a **fuel gas stream** is produced.
- All hot streams recycle internally to preheat feed and combustion air, raising plant efficiency to **>90 %**.

---

## 4.3 Heat-Integration Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[Reactor Vapor 525 C] --> H2[Waste Heat Exchanger]
    H2 --> H3[Feed Preheater 300 C]
    H3 --> H4[Reactor Inlet]
    H5[Heater Flue Gas 700 C] --> H6[Boiler Feed Water Heater]
    H6 --> H7[High Pressure Steam Drum]
    H7 --> H8[Steam Turbine or Process Steam]
    H9[Gasifier Off Gas 900 C] --> H10[Quench Cooler]
    H10 --> H11[Scrubber]
    H11 --> H12[Lean Low BTU Gas To Firing]
```

**Engineering Insight:**
- Modern Flexi Coking plants achieve **~92 % thermal efficiency** thanks to multi-stage heat recovery.
- Steam generated from flue gas is reused within the refinery, slashing fuel imports.
