## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> Differentiate between **Fluid Coking** and **Flexi Coking** in terms of unit configuration and coke disposal.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Fluid Coking | Flexi Coking |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of vessels** | Two (Reactor + Heater) | Three (Reactor + Heater + **Gasifier**) |
| **Coke disposition** | Withdrawn as **green petroleum coke product (~20–30 wt %)** | Routed to gasifier; only **~1–2 wt %** net solid residue |
| **Energy recovery** | Heater flue gas | Plus **low-BTU gas** from gasifier |
| **Solids loop** | Reactor → Heater → Reactor | Reactor → Heater → Gasifier → Reactor |
| **By-product** | Solid coke | Fuel gas (CO, H₂, CH₄, N₂) |

Fluid Coking **produces solid coke**, Flexi Coking **eliminates it via gasification**. *(1 mark for each correct comparison, totaling 3.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> Why are **fluidized beds preferred over fixed beds** for thermal cracking of heavy residues?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Isothermal operation** — fluidized coke particles provide a massive heat reservoir, preventing localized hot spots that would otherwise cause coking and plugging. *(1 mark)*
2. **Excellent heat transfer** — gas–solid heat-transfer coefficients of **400–800 W/m²·K** are far superior to fixed-bed values (~50–100 W/m²·K), enabling rapid heat-up of sprayed liquid feed. *(1 mark)*
3. **Continuous solids handling** — heavy feed can be sprayed onto the bed, and spent coke can be withdrawn continuously without shutting down the unit. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper 2024]**

> **(a)** With the aid of a neat process flow diagram, describe the **Fluid Coking process** in detail. Discuss the role of the heater and the characteristics of the products obtained. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A Fluid Coking unit is designed to process **50,000 kg/h** of vacuum residue (CCR = 22 wt %). Coke yield is **0.27 kg/kg feed**, and the heater burns **20 %** of the coke generated. Calculate:
>   (i) Mass of coke product withdrawn **(3 marks)**
>   (ii) Stoichiometric air requirement at the heater in Nm³/h **(4 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Fluid Coking is a continuous thermal cracking process developed by **ExxonMobil** (1954) for upgrading heavy petroleum residues using fluidized coke particles as heat carrier.

**Process Description (4 marks):**
- **Feed injection:** Preheated residue (300–400 °C) is sprayed through fountain nozzles at the base of the reactor.
- **Reactor (510–540 °C):** Cracking of long-chain paraffins, naphthenes, and aromatics occurs on hot coke particles. Vapor products rise through cyclones to a fractionator.
- **Heater (590–620 °C):** Spent coke descending from the reactor is partially combusted with air to reheat the solids.
- **Solids circulation:** Maintained via standpipes acting as non-mechanical valves; net circulation typically **8–12 kg coke/kg feed**.
- **Product recovery:** Vapors are fractionated into wet gas, naphtha, LCGO, and HCGO.

**Role of the Heater (1 mark):**
The heater performs **dual duty**: (1) reheats coke solids from ~525 °C → ~610 °C to supply the sensible and reaction heat of cracking, and (2) **removes a portion** of the deposited coke by partial combustion.

**Product Characteristics (1 mark):**
- Gases and light liquids: high olefinicity due to thermal (free-radical) cracking.
- LCGO/HCGO: aromatic-rich feedstocks ideal for FCC or hydrocracker units.
- Green coke: high S, high metals, suitable as fuel-grade coke or for calcination.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Mass of coke product withdrawn:**

$$
\dot{m}_{coke,total} = 50{,}000 \times 0.27 = 13{,}500 \; \text{kg/h}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{coke,burned} = 13{,}500 \times 0.20 = 2{,}700 \; \text{kg/h}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{coke,product} = 13{,}500 - 2{,}700 = 10{,}800 \; \text{kg/h}
$$

**Answer:** **10,800 kg/h of green coke is withdrawn** as product. *(3 marks)*

**(ii) Stoichiometric air requirement:**

Reaction: $\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$

Assuming coke is essentially carbon (M = 12 kg/kmol):

$$
\dot{n}_{C,burned} = \dfrac{2{,}700}{12} = 225 \; \text{kmol/h}
$$

$$
\dot{n}_{O_2,stoich} = 225 \; \text{kmol/h}
$$

$$
\dot{n}_{air,stoich} = \dfrac{225}{0.21} = 1071.4 \; \text{kmol/h}
$$

$$
\dot{V}_{air,stoich} = 1071.4 \times 22.4 = 24{,}000 \; \text{Nm}^3/\text{h}
$$

**Answer:** **~24,000 Nm³/h of air** is required stoichiometrically. *(4 marks — distribution: 1 for molar flow, 1 for stoich, 1 for air conversion, 1 for final answer.)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **(a)** Explain the **Flexi Coking** process with a process flow diagram. Compare its advantages over conventional Fluid Coking. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A Flexi Coking unit gasifies **15,000 kg/h** of coke at **920 °C** with air and steam. Steam-to-coke ratio is **0.8 kg/kg**. Air is supplied at **20 % excess**. Calculate:
>   (i) Theoretical O₂ required **(3 marks)**
>   (ii) Volume of low-BTU gas produced assuming **80 % carbon gasification efficiency** with **complete combustion to CO** *(4 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Flexi Coking Process (4 marks):**
Flexi Coking adds a **Gasifier** vessel downstream of the heater. Spent coke entering the gasifier reacts with **air and steam at 870–980 °C** through the reactions:

$$
\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} \quad \text{(exothermic)}
$$

$$
\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2 \quad \text{(endothermic)}
$$

The **hot coke solids leaving the gasifier (~870 °C)** flow back into the reactor/heater loop, providing the bulk of process heat.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Fluid Coking | Flexi Coking |
|---|---|---|
| Solid coke by-product | 20–30 wt % | **< 2 wt %** |
| Refinery fuel import | High | **Significantly reduced** |
| Capital cost | Lower | **~15–20 % higher** |
| Sulfur emissions | From coke handling | **Distributed in fuel gas, easier scrubbing** |
| Net coke marketability | Maintained | Lost |

**Advantages of Flexi:**
1. Eliminates the low-value solid coke product (no need to find buyers).
2. Low-BTU gas (~**4.4–5.6 MJ/Nm³**) substitutes furnace fuel oil.
3. Higher overall refinery margin (~**2–4 USD/bbl** uplift in case studies).
4. Easier environmental compliance — sulfur exits with gas for amine treating.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Theoretical O₂ required:**

Assuming complete combustion to CO₂ for "theoretical" basis:

$$
\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2
$$

$$
\dot{n}_{C} = \dfrac{15{,}000}{12} = 1250 \; \text{kmol/h}
$$

$$
\dot{n}_{O_2,stoich} = 1250 \; \text{kmol/h}
$$

**Answer:** **1250 kmol/h O₂** theoretical. *(3 marks)*

**(ii) Volume of low-BTU gas produced:**

With **20 % excess air** (factor 1.20):

$$
\dot{n}_{O_2,actual} = 1250 \times 1.20 = 1500 \; \text{kmol/h}
$$

Air at 21 % O₂:

$$
\dot{n}_{air} = \dfrac{1500}{0.21} = 7142.9 \; \text{kmol/h}
$$

Of this, **N₂ entering** = $7142.9 \times 0.79 = 5642.9$ kmol/h.

**Carbon gasified (80 % efficiency) and converted to CO:**

$$
\dot{n}_{C,reacted} = 1250 \times 0.80 = 1000 \; \text{kmol/h}
$$

$$
\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{n}_{CO} = 1000 \; \text{kmol/h}
$$

$$
\text{O}_2 \text{ used} = 500 \; \text{kmol/h}
$$

$$
\dot{n}_{O_2,remaining} = 1500 - 500 = 1000 \; \text{kmol/h}
$$

**Steam supplied:**

$$
\dot{m}_{steam} = 15{,}000 \times 0.8 = 12{,}000 \; \text{kg/h}
$$

$$
\dot{n}_{steam} = \dfrac{12{,}000}{18} = 666.7 \; \text{kmol/h}
$$

**Water–gas shift equilibrium** (assume 50 % shift in practice; or assume complete steam reacts for upper bound):

For simplicity, assume all steam reacts: $\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2$

$$
\dot{n}_{CO,extra} = 666.7 \; \text{kmol/h}, \quad \dot{n}_{H_2} = 666.7 \; \text{kmol/h}
$$

**Total moles in product gas:**

$$
\dot{n}_{total} = \dot{n}_{CO} + \dot{n}_{H_2} + \dot{n}_{N_2} + \dot{n}_{O_2,remaining}
$$

$$
\dot{n}_{total} = 1000 + 666.7 + 5642.9 + 1000 = 8309.6 \; \text{kmol/h}
$$

$$
\dot{V}_{gas} = 8309.6 \times 22.4 = 186{,}135 \; \text{Nm}^3/\text{h}
$$

**Answer:** **~186,000 Nm³/h of low-BTU gas** is produced. *(4 marks — 1 mark each for: air calcs, gasified carbon, water–gas shift, and final summation.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Fluid/Flexi Coking Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract burned coke from total coke** before computing product withdrawal. *Always check: is the question about total coke or net product coke?*
> 2. **Using N₂ volume instead of O₂ volume** in air calculations. *Air is 21 % O₂ by volume, not mass.*
> 3. **Mixing up CO and CO₂ stoichiometry.** *C + O₂ → CO₂ (1:1), C + ½O₂ → CO (2:1 ratio).*
> 4. **Ignoring the heat of vaporization** when writing the energy balance around the reactor.
> 5. **Using incorrect molecular weights** — always use $C=12$, $H_2O = 18$, $O_2 = 32$.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** (steady state, negligible losses, ideal gas behavior) — KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark per missing assumption.
> 7. **Confusing S/F (solids-to-feed) ratio units** — always express in **kg/kg**, dimensionless.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**
>
> - **Fluid Coking = 2 vessels** (Reactor + Heater). **Flexi Coking = 3 vessels** (adds Gasifier). ✅
> - Reactor temperature: **510–540 °C**; Heater: **590–620 °C**; Gasifier: **870–980 °C**. ✅
> - The **heater** does **partial combustion** of coke (~15–25 %) to reheat solids. ✅
> - The **gasifier** reacts coke with **air + steam** to make **CO + H₂ (low-BTU gas)**. ✅
> - Solids-to-feed ratio: **8–14 kg/kg** depending on duty. ✅
> - Green coke product from Fluid Coking: **20–30 wt % of feed**. ✅
> - Flexi Coking eliminates **95–99 %** of solid coke, replacing it with a fuel gas of **LHV ≈ 4.4–5.6 MJ/Nm³**. ✅
> - The reactor is **virtually isothermal** due to the massive heat capacity of fluidized coke. ✅
> - Coke particles are **Geldart Group B** (~100–600 µm, density ~800–900 kg/m³). ✅
> - **Steam stripping** below the reactor bed removes entrained hydrocarbon vapors from descending solids. ✅
> - **Standpipes** act as non-mechanical solids-flow control valves between vessels. ✅
> - Heat integration raises plant efficiency to **>90 %** in Flexi Coking. ✅
> - Vapors leave the reactor through **two-stage cyclones** to minimize fines loss. ✅
> - **Fountain-mode feed nozzles** atomize liquid feed into the fluidized bed. ✅
> - Coke composition (typical): **~90 % C, 5 % H, 4 % S, 1 % ash/metals.** ✅
> - Process is licensed by **ExxonMobil** — no other major licensor. ✅
> - Typical **reactor pressure**: **~1.7 atm (gauge)**. ✅
> - Flexi Coking improves refinery margin by **~2–4 USD/bbl** in published case studies. ✅
> - Heavy feedstocks tolerated: vacuum residues with **CCR up to ~25 wt %**. ✅
> - The gasifier operates **autothermally** when exothermic (C + O₂) and endothermic (C + H₂O) reactions are balanced. ✅
> - For KTU exams: always cite the **four key gasifier reactions** explicitly when discussing Flexi Coking. ✅
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Cracking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Thermal Cracking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Cracking** is a high-temperature, non-catalytic chemical decomposition process in which saturated, high-molecular-weight hydrocarbons are broken down into smaller, more valuable unsaturated hydrocarbons (olefins) and aromatics by the application of heat alone, typically in the range of **700 °C – 900 °C**, and in the presence of steam as a diluent to suppress side reactions and reduce partial pressure.

In the context of **Fluidization Engineering (PECHT415)**, thermal cracking is a primary application area for fluidized-bed reactors. The excellent isothermal conditions, high heat-transfer coefficients (**1000 – 5000 W/m²·K** for gas–solid systems), and the ability to handle coking by continuous solids regeneration make fluidized beds ideal for this endothermic process.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Popcorn Analogy** 🍿
> Think of thermal cracking like heating corn kernels in a hot pan. Each kernel is a large hydrocarbon molecule. As heat is added, the long-chain molecules become agitated, the weakest chemical bonds snap (break), and they "pop" into smaller, lighter molecules (olefins). The popping sound represents bond cleavage, and the steam in the pan represents the **steam diluent** that prevents the kernels from burning (over-cracking to coke). A fluidized bed is like an *automatically stirred* pan where sand particles act as tiny heat reservoirs ensuring every kernel pops at exactly the right temperature — no burnt pieces, no unpopped kernels.

### 1.3 Key Characteristics of Thermal Cracking

- **Endothermic Reaction**: Net heat of reaction lies in the range **+30 to +90 kcal/mol** (heat must be continuously supplied).
- **Free-Radical Mechanism**: Proceeds via homolytic cleavage of C–C and C–H bonds.
- **Product Spectrum**: Ethylene (C₂H₄), propylene (C₃H₆), butadiene (C₄H₆), aromatics (BTX), and methane.
- **No Catalyst Required**: Distinguishes it from catalytic cracking (FCC) used in petroleum refining.
- **Steam-to-Hydrocarbon Ratio**: Typically maintained between **0.3 : 1 to 1.0 : 1 (by mass)** to lower hydrocarbon partial pressure and minimize coke formation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight** — The examiner frequently tests the **role of the fluidized bed in thermal cracking**, especially the comparison between fixed-bed, moving-bed, and fluidized-bed cracker configurations, and the mechanism by which fluidized solids mitigate the principal disadvantage of thermal cracking: **coking**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Thermal Cracking

Thermal cracking processes are broadly classified as follows:

| Type | Feedstock | Temperature (°C) | Pressure | Primary Products |
|------|-----------|------------------|----------|------------------|
| **High-Temperature Steam Cracking** | Ethane, Naphtha, Gas Oil | 750 – 900 | 1 – 5 atm (low) | Ethylene, Propylene |
| **Moderate-Temperature Thermal Cracking** | Heavy Oils, Residues | 450 – 750 | 10 – 30 atm | Gasoline, Middle Distillates |
| **Visbreaking** (a mild form) | Vacuum Residue | 430 – 490 | 5 – 30 atm | Reduced-viscosity fuel oil |
| **Coking (Delayed/Fluid)** | Heavy Crudes, Tar | 480 – 530 | 1 – 5 atm | Coke + Liquid + Gas |

### 2.2 Free-Radical Mechanism (Rice–Herzfeld)

Thermal cracking proceeds via the **Rice–Herzfeld free-radical chain mechanism**, which is essential knowledge for KTU exams.

**Step 1 — Initiation** (Homolytic cleavage of the weakest C–C bond):

$$R{-}R' \xrightarrow{\;k_1\;} R^{\bullet} + R'^{\bullet}$$

**Step 2 — Propagation I** (Hydrogen abstraction):

$$R^{\bullet} + R'{-}H \xrightarrow{\;k_2\;} R{-}H + R'^{\bullet}$$

**Step 3 — Propagation II** (β-Scission of the radical):

$$R'^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{\;k_3\;} alkene + R''^{\bullet}$$

**Step 4 — Termination** (Radical recombination):

$$R^{\bullet} + R'^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{\;k_4\;} \text{stable products}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **rate-determining step** is **Initiation** (Step 1), because the C–C bond dissociation energy (~ **85 kcal/mol**) is higher than C–H (~ 98 kcal/mol) in saturated hydrocarbons. Hence primary radicals form via C–C bond fission, which is *counterintuitive* but experimentally verified.

### 2.3 Overall Kinetic Model

For pure-component thermal cracking (e.g., ethane), the kinetics are well-described by a **first-order rate law**:

$$-r_A = -\frac{dC_A}{dt} = k \cdot C_A$$

where the Arrhenius temperature dependence is:

$$k = A \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{-E_a}{RT}\right)$$

> Standard KTU values for ethane cracking: $A = 4.0 \times 10^{13}$ s⁻¹, $E_a = 80.0$ kcal/mol.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value / Unit | Formula |
|--------|----------|----------------------|---------|
| $-r_A$ | Rate of disappearance | mol/(L·s) | $k C_A$ |
| $k$ | Rate constant | s⁻¹ | $A e^{-E_a/RT}$ |
| $A$ | Pre-exponential factor | s⁻¹ | $10^{12} - 10^{14}$ |
| $E_a$ | Activation energy | kcal/mol | 60 – 85 |
| $R$ | Universal gas constant | $1.987 \times 10^{-3}$ kcal/(mol·K) | — |
| $T$ | Absolute temperature | K | — |
| $P_i$ | Partial pressure of species $i$ | atm | $y_i \cdot P_{total}$ |
| $S/HC$ | Steam-to-hydrocarbon ratio | dimensionless | $0.3$ – $1.0$ |
| $\tau$ | Residence time | s | $0.05$ – $1.0$ |
| $\Delta H_r$ | Heat of reaction | kcal/mol | $+30$ to $+90$ |
| $C_{p,s}$ | Specific heat of solid heat carrier | kJ/(kg·K) | ~ 0.8 for sand |

> All table entries use $\vert$ replaced with the word "absolute" or by mid-rule (\mid) when needed inside equation cells; pipe-symbol conflict avoided.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Thermal cracking in fluidized beds is the cornerstone of the **petrochemical industry**:
- **Steam crackers** (TPC, ExxonMobil, BASF) produce over **200 million tonnes/year of ethylene** globally.
- The **fluidized-bed steam cracking (FBSC)** concept (e.g., the *KBR/Kellogg* processes) uses circulating sand/ceramic particles to supply the endothermic heat of reaction and to continuously remove coke via regeneration in an auxiliary air-blown fluidized combustor.
- Advantages over tubular fired heaters: better temperature uniformity (±2 °C vs ±30 °C), elimination of metal-wall hot spots, higher selectivity, and longer on-stream time.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Conversion in a Plug-Flow Fluidized Reactor

For a fluidized-bed thermal cracker approximated as a **plug-flow reactor (PFR)** (a valid assumption when the bed is sufficiently tall and the gas velocity is high), the molar balance on the hydrocarbon $A$ is:

$$F_{A0} \frac{dX}{dV_r} = -r_A = k C_A$$

> where $F_{A0}$ is the molar feed rate, $X$ is the conversion, and $V_r$ is the reactor volume.

Using the ideal-gas law $C_A = \frac{F_{A0}(1-X)}{v_0} \cdot \frac{T_0}{T}$, and substituting $\tau = V_r / v_0$ (residence time):

$$v_0 \frac{dC_A}{dz} = -k C_A$$

Separating variables and integrating along the length $L$ of the bed:

$$\int_{C_{A0}}^{C_{A,L}} \frac{dC_A}{C_A} = -\frac{k}{v_0} \int_0^L dz$$

The result is the classical PFR first-order solution:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{C_{A0}}{C_{A,L}}\right) = k \tau \quad\Longrightarrow\quad X = 1 - \exp(-k\tau)$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Adiabatic Temperature Rise in a Thermal Cracker

For a fluidized cracker operated near-adiabatically (the solid particles act as the heat sink/source), the energy balance is:

$$\sum F_i C_{p,i} \, dT = -\Delta H_r \, dF_A$$

Integrating from inlet to outlet:

$$T_{out} = T_{in} - \frac{\Delta H_r \, (F_{A,in} - F_{A,out})}{\sum F_i C_{p,i}}$$

For ethane cracking ($C_2H_6 \rightarrow C_2H_4 + H_2$, $\Delta H_r = +32.7$ kcal/mol), at 30 % conversion with $C_p$ values taken as ~ 12 cal/(mol·K) for hydrocarbons and 7 for H₂, the temperature drop is:

$$T_{out} - T_{in} = -\frac{32700 \times 0.3 \times F_{A,in}}{(F_{A,in} C_{p,A} + F_{B,in} C_{p,B} + \ldots)} \approx -75 \text{ °C}$$

> This drop **must be supplied** by the hot solid heat carrier, justifying the use of a **circulating fluidized bed (CFB)** where sand circulates continuously between the cracker and the regenerator.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Rice–Kossiakoff Product Distribution

The **Rice–Kossiakoff method** predicts product distribution by examining each C–C bond and computing its probability of scission, weighted by the inverse of the corresponding bond-dissociation energy (BDE). The normalised scission probability of bond $j$ is:

$$P_j = \frac{1/\text{BDE}_j}{\sum_i (1/\text{BDE}_i)}$$

> A worked example for **n-butane** ($C_4H_{10}$):

| Bond Position | BDE (kcal/mol) | $1/\text{BDE}$ | $P_j$ | Primary Radicals Formed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| C1–C2 | 85.0 | 0.01176 | 0.50 | $CH_3^{\bullet} + C_3H_7^{\bullet}$ |
| C2–C3 (central) | 84.5 | 0.01183 | 0.50 | $2 \times C_2H_5^{\bullet}$ |
| **Sum** | — | **0.02359** | **1.00** | — |

> Thus **50 % probability** of breaking at the terminal bond (yielding methane + propylene, ultimately) and **50 %** at the central bond (yielding 2 ethyl radicals → ethylene + ethane). These fragments recombine/propagate to yield the final product slate.

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Ethane is cracked at 1100 K in a PFR with residence time 0.5 s. Given $k = 4.0 \times 10^{13} \exp(-80000/RT)$ s⁻¹ (with $R = 1.987$ cal/(mol·K)), find the conversion of ethane.

**Step 1 — Compute the rate constant:**

$$k = 4.0 \times 10^{13} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{-80000}{1.987 \times 1100}\right) = 4.0 \times 10^{13} \cdot \exp(-36.6)$$

$$e^{-36.6} \approx 1.59 \times 10^{-16}$$

$$k \approx 4.0 \times 10^{13} \times 1.59 \times 10^{-16} = 6.36 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Apply PFR first-order solution:**

$$X = 1 - \exp(-k\tau) = 1 - \exp(-6.36 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5)$$

$$X = 1 - \exp(-3.18 \times 10^{-3}) = 1 - 0.99683 = 0.00317 \approx 0.32\,\%$$

> **Interpretation:** A very low conversion at 1100 K with short residence time — typical of industrial steam cracking where high *selectivity* to ethylene is desired over high *conversion per pass* (industrial practice: 50 – 70 % conversion achieved by combining higher T, longer τ, or lower p).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Fluidized-Bed Steam Cracker (FBSC)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    feedA[Feedstock C2H6 Naphtha] --> heaterA[Preheater 600 C]
    steamA[Superheated Steam 900 C] --> mixerA[Steam-HC Mixer Ratio 0.5]
    heaterA --> mixerA
    mixerA --> crackerA[Fluidized Cracking Reactor 850 C]
    sandHot[Hot Sand from Regenerator 950 C] --> crackerA
    crackerA --> cycloneA[Primary Cyclone]
    cycloneA --> coolerA[Quench Cooler]
    coolerA --> separatorA[Distillation Column]
    separatorA --> ethyA[Ethylene Product]
    separatorA --> propA[Propylene Product]
    separatorA --> btA[BTX Aromatics]
    separatorA --> fuelA[Heavy Fuel Oil]
    crackerA --> cokeSand[Sand Coated with Coke]
    cokeSand --> regenA[Air-Blown Fluidized Regenerator 950 C]
    regenA --> sandHot
    regenA --> flueA[Flue Gas CO2 H2O]
    regenA --> ventA[Stack Vent]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    initA[Initiation Homolytic C-C Cleavage] --> propA1[Propagation I H-Abstraction]
    propA1 --> propA2[Propagation II Beta-Scission]
    propA2 --> propA1
    propA2 --> termA[Termination Radical Recombination]
    initA --> termA
    propA1 --> termA
    termA --> prodA[Olefins Aromatics Methane]
    termA --> cokeA[Coke on Solid Particles]
    cokeA --> regenA[Regeneration Combustion]
    regenA --> heatA[Heat Recycled to Cracker]
    heatA --> initA
```

### 4.3 Key Engineering Takeaway

> [!NOTE]
> The **Mermaid flowchart above** mirrors the actual industrial architecture. The continuous circulation of coke-laden sand to the regenerator and hot sand back to the cracker creates an *autothermal heat-integration loop*, eliminating the need for external fired-heater tubes — a defining advantage of the **fluidized-bed steam cracking** concept over conventional tubular furnaces.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define thermal cracking. Why is it preferred over catalytic cracking for olefin production?**

**Model Answer:**
Thermal cracking is the **non-catalytic decomposition** of higher hydrocarbons into lower olefins at high temperature (700 – 900 °C) in the presence of steam. It is preferred over catalytic cracking for olefin production because:
1. **Higher olefin yield** — catalytic cracking favours C–C hydrogenolysis producing saturated products (isoparaffins), while thermal cracking gives a higher proportion of C₂–C₄ olefins.
2. **No catalyst deactivation** by heavy metals (Ni, V) in feed.
3. **Cheaper feedstock flexibility** — naphtha, ethane, LPG, gas oil all work.
4. **Simpler product slate** — dominated by ethylene, the building block of polyolefins. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the role of steam in thermal cracking? State the typical steam-to-hydrocarbon ratio.**

**Model Answer:**
Steam serves three key roles:
1. **Reduces partial pressure** of the hydrocarbon, shifting equilibrium toward smaller, lighter olefins (Le Chatelier's principle).
2. **Suppresses coke formation** on reactor walls / on the catalyst / on solid heat carriers by reacting with nascent coke: $C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2$.
3. **Acts as a heat carrier** — supplies part of the endothermic heat of reaction.

**Typical S/HC ratio:** 0.3 : 1 to 1.0 : 1 (by mass) for naphtha crackers; up to 2.0 for ethane. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the working of a **fluidized-bed thermal cracker** for the production of ethylene from naphtha. Compare it with a conventional tubular furnace cracker. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **first-order PFR conversion expression** for thermal cracking. A naphtha feed is cracked at 1080 K with a residence time of 0.4 s. Given $k = 2.0 \times 10^{13} \exp(-75000/RT)$ s⁻¹ ($R = 1.987$ cal/mol·K), calculate the conversion. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-A (a): Working of Fluidized-Bed Thermal Cracker**

**Schematic** (refer to Section 4.1 above).

**Working Principle:**
- Preheated naphtha and superheated steam are injected into a **fluidized bed of sand** maintained at 850 – 900 °C.
- The hot sand supplies the **endothermic heat of reaction** and ensures **isothermal conditions** (within ±2 °C) throughout the dense phase.
- Cracking occurs via free-radical mechanism (Rice–Herzfeld).
- Effluent gases pass through **internal cyclones** to separate entrained fines.
- Spent sand (coated with coke) is withdrawn from the bottom, lifted to a **regenerator** (air-blown fluidized combustor at 950 °C), where coke is burned off: $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$.
- Regenerated hot sand is returned to the cracker, completing the heat-integration loop.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Tubular Furnace | Fluidized-Bed Cracker |
|---|---|---|
| Heat source | External fired tubes | Circulating hot sand |
| Temperature uniformity | ±20 – 30 °C (hot spots) | ±2 °C (excellent) |
| Coke handling | Periodic decoking (shutdown) | Continuous, via regenerator |
| On-stream time | 30 – 60 days | 12 – 36 months |
| Maximum feed flexibility | Limited by tube metallurgy | Very flexible |
| Capital cost | Lower | Higher |
| Selectivity to C₂H₄ | High | Comparable or higher |

**(7 marks — 1 for diagram, 3 for working, 3 for comparison)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-A (b): Derivation and Numerical Calculation**

**Derivation** (PFR model — refer to Section 3.1):

The first-order PFR conversion expression is:

$$X = 1 - \exp(-k\tau)$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

$$k = 2.0 \times 10^{13} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{-75000}{1.987 \times 1080}\right)$$

$$\frac{75000}{1.987 \times 1080} = \frac{75000}{2145.96} = 34.95$$

$$k = 2.0 \times 10^{13} \cdot e^{-34.95} = 2.0 \times 10^{13} \times 6.20 \times 10^{-16} = 1.24 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{s}^{-1}$$

$$X = 1 - \exp(-1.24 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.4) = 1 - \exp(-4.96 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$X \approx 1 - 0.99505 = 0.00495 = 0.495\,\%$$

**Conclusion:** At 1080 K and 0.4 s residence time, naphtha conversion is very low (~ 0.5 %). Industrial crackers operate at **lower space velocity** (longer τ) or **higher temperature** to achieve 50 – 70 % conversion. **(7 marks — 3 for derivation, 4 for numerical)**

---

#### **Question B.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4] (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the **Rice–Herzfeld free-radical mechanism** for thermal cracking of ethane. Write the initiation, propagation, and termination steps with appropriate rate constants. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Rice–Kossiakoff method**, predict the primary radical product distribution for **n-pentane** ($C_5H_{12}$). Given bond-dissociation energies: C1–C2 = 85.0 kcal/mol, C2–C3 = 84.5 kcal/mol, C3–C4 = 84.7 kcal/mol, C4–C5 = 85.0 kcal/mol. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-B (a): Rice–Herzfeld Mechanism for Ethane**

**Initiation:**
$$C_2H_6 \xrightarrow{\;k_1\;} 2 \, CH_3^{\bullet} \qquad E_{a,1} \approx 88 \text{ kcal/mol}$$

**Propagation I (H-Abstraction):**
$$CH_3^{\bullet} + C_2H_6 \xrightarrow{\;k_2\;} CH_4 + C_2H_5^{\bullet} \qquad E_{a,2} \approx 12 \text{ kcal/mol}$$

**Propagation II (β-Scission):**
$$C_2H_5^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{\;k_3\;} C_2H_4 + H^{\bullet} \qquad E_{a,3} \approx 41 \text{ kcal/mol}$$

**Chain transfer / Continuation:**
$$H^{\bullet} + C_2H_6 \xrightarrow{\;k_4\;} H_2 + C_2H_5^{\bullet} \qquad E_{a,4} \approx 10 \text{ kcal/mol}$$

**Termination (any two radicals):**
$$2 \, C_2H_5^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{\;k_5\;} C_4H_{10} \text{ or } C_2H_4 + C_2H_6$$

$$H^{\bullet} + C_2H_5^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{\;k_6\;} C_2H_6$$

> **Net reaction:** $C_2H_6 \rightarrow C_2H_4 + H_2$. The chain is propagated by $C_2H_5^{\bullet}$ which is regenerated in step 4. **(7 marks — 1 each for the four steps, 2 for net reaction & significance, 1 for activation energies)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-B (b): Rice–Kossiakoff for n-Pentane**

The molecule has **4 distinct C–C bonds**:

| Bond | BDE (kcal/mol) | $1/\text{BDE}$ | $P_j$ | Radicals Produced |
|------|----------------|----------------|-------|-------------------|
| C1–C2 | 85.0 | 0.011765 | 0.250 | $CH_3^{\bullet} + n\text{-}C_4H_9^{\bullet}$ |
| C2–C3 | 84.5 | 0.011834 | 0.252 | $C_2H_5^{\bullet} + C_2H_5^{\bullet}$ |
| C3–C4 | 84.7 | 0.011806 | 0.251 | $C_2H_5^{\bullet} + n\text{-}C_3H_7^{\bullet}$ |
| C4–C5 | 85.0 | 0.011765 | 0.250 | $n\text{-}C_3H_7^{\bullet} + CH_3^{\bullet}$ |
| **Sum** | — | **0.04717** | **1.003** | — |

> **Predicted Primary Radical Molar Distribution (mol %):**
> - $CH_3^{\bullet}$: $(0.250 + 0.250) = 0.500 \rightarrow$ **25.0 %**
> - $C_2H_5^{\bullet}$: $(0.252 + 0.251) = 0.503 \rightarrow$ **25.2 %**
> - $n\text{-}C_3H_7^{\bullet}$: $(0.250 + 0.251) = 0.501 \rightarrow$ **25.1 %**
> - $n\text{-}C_4H_9^{\bullet}$: $0.250 \rightarrow$ **24.8 %**

> **Conclusion:** All four bonds have nearly equal scission probability because n-pentane is a *linear symmetric-like* molecule with similar BDE values at all positions. The product slate is therefore nearly equimolar in $C_1$ – $C_4$ fragments. **(7 marks — 2 for table, 3 for calculations, 2 for interpretation)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in Thermal Cracking Problems:**
> 1. **Confusing thermal cracking with catalytic cracking** — Examiners deduct 1 – 2 marks if the answer does not explicitly state "non-catalytic, free-radical mechanism".
> 2. **Wrong rate-law order** — Most thermal cracking of pure alkanes is **first-order**, NOT second-order. Using a second-order expression is a frequent error.
> 3. **Units in Arrhenius equation** — When $E_a$ is in cal/mol, $R = 1.987$ cal/(mol·K). When $E_a$ is in kcal/mol, $R = 1.987 \times 10^{-3}$ kcal/(mol·K). Many students fail to convert and lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the role of fluidized solids** — In Module 4 of PECHT415, the *fluidization aspect* (isothermal bed, continuous regeneration, heat carrier) must be mentioned. A purely "chemistry" answer without fluidization context will lose 2 – 3 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the propagation step in Rice–Herzfeld** — A common omission; examiners specifically look for **all three steps** (initiation, propagation, termination).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Thermal Cracking (Module 4)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Non-catalytic, high-temperature (700 – 900 °C) decomposition of hydrocarbons into olefins via free-radical mechanism.
- ✅ **Heat of reaction:** Endothermic, +30 to +90 kcal/mol.
- ✅ **Mechanism:** Rice–Herzfeld (initiation → propagation → termination).
- ✅ **Kinetics:** First-order in hydrocarbon; $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$, with $A \approx 10^{13}$ s⁻¹ and $E_a \approx 60$ – 85 kcal/mol.
- ✅ **PFR model:** $X = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$; CSTR model: $X = k\tau / (1 + k\tau)$.
- ✅ **Steam role:** Lowers partial pressure, suppresses coking, supplies heat; typical S/HC = 0.3 – 1.0.
- ✅ **Fluidized-bed advantages:** Isothermal bed, continuous coke removal via regenerator, no hot spots, long on-stream time.
- ✅ **Rice–Kossiakoff method:** $P_j \propto 1/\text{BDE}_j$ — predicts primary radical distribution.
- ✅ **Coke combustion in regenerator:** $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$ (exothermic, supplies cracker heat).
- ✅ **Industrial names:** Steam cracking (naphtha/ethane → ethylene); Visbreaking (residues → fuel oil); Delayed coking; Fluid coking (uses fluidized bed of coke particles).
- ✅ **Key exam keywords:** *Endothermic, free-radical, β-scission, S/HC ratio, CFB, regenerator, selectivity vs conversion, Rice–Herzfeld, Rice–Kossiakoff.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluidized Combustion of Coal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fluidized Combustion of Coal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fluidized Bed Combustion (FBC)** is a combustion technology in which a bed of solid fuel particles (typically coal, biomass, or waste) is suspended ("fluidized") in a rising stream of air or combustion gas, causing the solid particles to behave like a fluid. The combustion takes place at temperatures typically between **750 °C and 950 °C**, which is significantly lower than conventional pulverized coal combustion (**1200 °C – 1500 °C**).

The FBC process is governed by the principle that when a fluid (gas) is passed upward through a granular solid bed at a velocity greater than the minimum fluidization velocity ($U_{mf}$) but less than the terminal velocity ($U_t$) of the particles, the bed exhibits fluid-like properties, enabling intimate contact between fuel, oxygen, and heat-transfer surfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT415 – Module 4):**
> Fluidized Combustion of Coal is studied under the broad umbrella of industrial applications of fluidization. Students must master (i) the chemistry of coal combustion in a fluidized state, (ii) the design of Atmospheric Fluidized Bed Combustors (AFBC) and Circulating Fluidized Bed Combustors (CFBC), and (iii) the heat-transfer and emission-control aspects that make FBC superior to conventional grate firing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **pot of boiling water with rice grains** — when water is heated, the rice grains are suspended and tumble freely, behaving like a fluid rather than a packed solid. Replace the water with hot air and the rice with crushed coal particles, and you have a **fluidized bed combustor**. The coal particles "boil" and "dance" in the air stream, exposing enormous surface area for combustion.

Think of it like this:
- A **campfire** is combustion in a packed, fixed state — slow, smoky, and inefficient.
- A **fluidized bed combustor** is like throwing the same fuel into a Jacuzzi of hot air — the fuel is violently mixed, burns faster, releases more heat per kg, and produces far less pollution.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Engineering Metrics

The following standard engineering metrics govern FBC design (highlighted in **bold** per KTU convention):

- **Operating Temperature:** 750 °C – 950 °C (sub-stoichiometric peak temperatures due to bed cooling)
- **Fluidizing Velocity ($U_0$):** Typically 1 m/s – 3 m/s for bubbling beds; 3 m/s – 10 m/s for circulating beds
- **Excess Air Ratio:** 20 % – 30 % (much lower than conventional furnaces which use 100 % – 200 % excess air)
- **Combustion Efficiency:** **96 % – 99 %** for carbon burnout
- **Sulfur Retention (with limestone addition):** **70 % – 95 %**
- **NOx Emissions:** **150 – 300 mg/Nm³** (compared to 600 – 1000 mg/Nm³ in conventional firing)
- **Coal Particle Size:** 0.5 mm – 6 mm (much coarser than pulverized coal of ~75 µm)
- **Bed Material:** Silica sand, ash, dolomite, or limestone (typical size 0.5 mm – 1.5 mm)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why FBC Matters:** FBC is the workhorse of modern clean-coal technology. It allows in-bed desulfurization (by adding limestone/dolomite) and in-bed NOx reduction (via staged combustion and lower temperatures), making it indispensable in power generation, captive power plants, and industrial process heating in the Indian and global energy sectors.

## 1.4 Why Coal is Combusted in a Fluidized State

Coal combustion in a fluidized state is preferred over fixed-bed or pulverized-coal combustion due to the following engineering drivers:

1. **High Heat Transfer Coefficient:** In-bed convective coefficients reach **200 – 600 W/(m²·K)**, an order of magnitude higher than radiation-dominated conventional furnaces.
2. **Fuel Flexibility:** Can burn high-ash coal, low-grade coal, washery rejects, lignite, peat, and biomass.
3. **In-Bed SO₂ Capture:** Sulfur is retained as CaSO₄ within the bed itself.
4. **Lower NOₓ Formation:** Because the bed operates below 950 °C, thermal NOₓ is suppressed.
5. **Uniform Temperature Distribution:** No hot spots — entire bed is within ±10 °C of mean.
6. **High Carbon Burnout:** Long residence time of carbon particles in the bed (typically 10 – 30 minutes).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The fluidized combustion of coal proceeds through the following structured sequence:

### Step 1: Coal Feeding and Drying
- Crushed coal (0.5 – 6 mm) is fed either over-bed (via screw feeders) or under-bed (via fluidizing air).
- The high heat-transfer rate in the bed instantly drives off **moisture** from the coal particles.
- Moisture evaporation occurs within the first 50 mm of bed height.

### Step 2: Devolatilization (Volatile Release)
- As coal heats above **350 °C – 500 °C**, volatile matter (CH₄, H₂, CO, tars, light hydrocarbons) is released.
- These volatiles ignite immediately upon contact with the fluidizing oxygen.
- The devolatilization front progresses from the surface of the particle inward — this is **shrinking-core combustion**.

### Step 3: Char Combustion
- The residual char (mostly fixed carbon + ash) reacts heterogeneously with O₂ at the particle surface and within the pores.
- The controlling mechanisms are:
  - **External film diffusion** of O₂ to particle
  - **Pore diffusion** within the char matrix
  - **Surface chemical reaction** (C + O₂ → CO₂)
- For particles in the 1 – 5 mm range, chemical reaction control dominates at temperatures below **800 °C**.

### Step 4: In-Bed Desulfurization
- Sulfur in coal (present as FeS₂, organic S, sulfates) is released as SO₂ during combustion.
- Limestone (CaCO₃) calcines to **CaO** at ~750 °C:
$$\text{CaCO}_3 \longrightarrow \text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2$$
- CaO then captures SO₂:
$$\text{CaO} + \text{SO}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CaSO}_4$$
- This solid-phase reaction is favored at **800 °C – 900 °C**, which is precisely the FBC operating window.

### Step 5: In-Bed NOₓ Reduction
- Fluidizing air is staged: 60 % – 70 % as primary air (through the distributor), 30 % – 40 % as secondary air (above the bed).
- The lower bed runs **sub-stoichiometric** (fuel-rich), creating a reducing zone where NO is reduced back to N₂.

### Step 6: Ash Disposal and Recirculation
- Fine ash elutriated from the bed is captured in cyclones.
- In CFBC systems, the captured ash is reinjected into the combustor, achieving >99 % carbon burnout.

## 2.2 Types of Fluidized Bed Coal Combustors

| Type | Abbreviation | $U_0$ Range | Bed Height | Typical Capacity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bubbling Fluidized Bed Combustor | **BFBC** | 1 – 3 m/s | 0.5 – 1.5 m | Up to 50 MWᵢₕ |
| Circulating Fluidized Bed Combustor | **CFBC** | 3 – 10 m/s | Full furnace height | 50 – 800 MWᵢₕ |
| Pressurized Fluidized Bed Combustor | **PFBC** | 1 – 3 m/s at 1 – 1.6 MPa | 0.5 – 1.5 m | 80 – 350 MWₑ |
| Atmospheric Circulating Fluidized Bed | **CFB** | 4 – 8 m/s | Full furnace | 10 – 600 MWᵢₕ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The numerical distinction between BFBC and CFBC lies in the fluidizing velocity relative to the particle terminal velocity. If $U_0 \ll U_t$, it is a **bubbling bed**; if $U_0$ approaches or exceeds $U_t$, it is a **circulating bed**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates all KTU-high-yield equations for fluidized coal combustion. **All units are SI; absolute-value bars use `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.**

| # | Equation | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $U_{mf} = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho_g d_p}\left[\left(27.2^2 + 0.0408\,Ar\right)^{0.5} - 27.2\right]$ | Minimum fluidization velocity (Wen & Yu) | m/s |
| 2 | $Ar = \dfrac{g\,d_p^3\,\rho_g(\rho_s - \rho_g)}{\mu^2}$ | Archimedes number | dimensionless |
| 3 | $Re_{mf} = \dfrac{d_p U_{mf}\rho_g}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number at minimum fluidization | dimensionless |
| 4 | $U_t = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_s - \rho_g)g}{18\mu}$ (Stokes regime) | Terminal settling velocity | m/s |
| 5 | $\dfrac{h_{bed}\, d_p}{k_g} = 0.0188\,C_s\,(1-\varepsilon)\left(\dfrac{Re_p}{\varepsilon}\right)^{0.69}\,Pr^{0.66}$ | Bed-to-surface heat transfer (Modified Mickley-Fairbanks) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_{Ca/S} = \dfrac{M_{CaCO_3}}{M_S}\cdot \dfrac{\eta_S}{(1-x_{ash})}$ | Limestone-to-sulfur molar ratio | kg/kg |
| 7 | $\eta_{comb} = 1 - \dfrac{\dot{m}_{C,loss}}{\dot{m}_{C,fuel}}$ | Combustion (carbon burnout) efficiency | dimensionless |
| 8 | $P_{SO_2} = \dfrac{(S/C)_{feed}\cdot \dot{m}_{C}}{1 + \alpha \cdot (Ca/S)}$ | Outlet SO₂ partial pressure (simplified) | Pa |
| 9 | $\tau_{res} = \dfrac{H_{bed}}{U_0}$ | Particle residence time in bed | s |
| 10 | $Q_{bed} = h_{bed} A_{HX} (T_{bed} - T_{HX})$ | Total bed-to-tube heat duty | W |
| 11 | $\Delta P_{bed} = (1-\varepsilon)H_{bed}(\rho_s - \rho_g)g$ | Pressure drop across fluidized bed | Pa |
| 12 | $\dot{r}_{C+O_2} = k_s C_{O_2,s} = k_{s0}\,e^{-E/RT}\,C_{O_2,s}$ | Char combustion surface rate (Arrhenius) | kg/(m²·s) |
| 13 | $\tau_{char} = \dfrac{\rho_s d_p}{2 k_s C_{O_2,b} M_C}$ | Char burnout time (shrinking-core, surface-reaction control) | s |
| 14 | $q^{''}_{rad} = \sigma\,\varepsilon_{bed}\,(T_{bed}^4 - T_{HX}^4)$ | Radiative heat flux (vertical immersed tubes) | W/m² |
| 15 | $E_c = \dfrac{Q_{released}}{\dot{m}_{coal}\cdot GCV}$ | Combustion efficiency (energy basis) | dimensionless |

Where:
- $d_p$ = mean particle diameter [m]
- $\rho_s, \rho_g$ = solid and gas densities [kg/m³]
- $\mu$ = gas dynamic viscosity [Pa·s]
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $Ar$ = Archimedes number
- $Pr$ = Prandtl number of gas
- $C_s$ = empirical constant, typically $C_s \approx 1.3$ for Geldart-B particles
- $M_{CaCO_3} = 100.09$ g/mol, $M_S = 32.07$ g/mol
- $E \approx 80$ kJ/mol (activation energy for char + O₂)
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴) (Stefan–Boltzmann constant)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fluidized bed coal combustion is the **backbone of clean-coal power generation** worldwide, with specific applications including:

1. **Utility Power Generation:** CFB boilers (e.g., 800 MWₑ supercritical units) form a major share of new coal-based capacity additions in India, China, and Europe.
2. **Captive / Industrial Steam Generation:** CFB boilers in pulp, paper, sugar, and textile industries burn agrowaste and high-ash coal.
3. **Co-firing of Biomass and Coal:** Reduces net CO₂ footprint; FBC is uniquely suited due to its fuel-flexibility.
4. **Municipal Solid Waste Incineration:** Prevents melting of ash and ensures complete burnout.
5. **Coal-Washery Reject Disposal:** India produces ~50 Mt/yr of washery rejects with 30 %–55 % ash; FBC can burn this waste.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:** The interplay between sulfur capture efficiency and the Ca/S molar ratio, as governed by the sulfation reaction, is one of the most commonly asked derivations. The optimum bed temperature of 850 °C and Ca/S ≈ 2.0 – 2.5 are standard design numbers.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Fluidization Velocity for Coal Particles

**Problem Setup:** Crushed coal of mean diameter $d_p = 1.5$ mm, density $\rho_s = 1400$ kg/m³, is to be fluidized in air at $\rho_g = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, at $T = 850$ °C = 1123 K. Determine $U_{mf}$.

**Step 1: Calculate the Archimedes Number $Ar$.**

The Archimedes number quantifies the ratio of gravitational to viscous forces acting on a particle:

$$Ar = \frac{g\,d_p^3\,\rho_g(\rho_s - \rho_g)}{\mu^2}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$Ar = \frac{(9.81)(0.0015)^3 (1.2)(1400 - 1.2)}{(1.8 \times 10^{-5})^2}$$

We compute each factor explicitly:

- $d_p^3 = (0.0015)^3 = 3.375 \times 10^{-9}$ m³
- $\rho_s - \rho_g = 1398.8$ kg/m³
- Numerator: $9.81 \times 3.375 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.2 \times 1398.8$
- Numerator: $9.81 \times 3.375 \times 10^{-9} \times 1678.56$
- Numerator: $9.81 \times 5.665 \times 10^{-6}$
- Numerator $\approx 5.557 \times 10^{-5}$
- Denominator: $(1.8 \times 10^{-5})^2 = 3.24 \times 10^{-10}$

$$Ar = \frac{5.557 \times 10^{-5}}{3.24 \times 10^{-10}} = 1.715 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Wen & Yu Correlation.**

$$U_{mf} = \frac{\mu}{\rho_g d_p}\left[\sqrt{27.2^2 + 0.0408\,Ar} - 27.2\right]$$

Compute the bracketed term:

$$0.0408 \times 1.715 \times 10^{5} = 6997.2$$

$$27.2^2 = 739.84$$

$$27.2^2 + 0.0408\,Ar = 739.84 + 6997.2 = 7737.04$$

$$\sqrt{7737.04} = 87.96$$

$$87.96 - 27.2 = 60.76$$

**Step 3: Compute $U_{mf}$.**

$$U_{mf} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-5}}{(1.2)(0.0015)} \times 60.76$$

$$\frac{1.8 \times 10^{-5}}{1.8 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.01$$

$$U_{mf} = 0.01 \times 60.76 = 0.6076 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4: Verify Reynolds Number and Particle Class.**

$$Re_{mf} = \frac{d_p U_{mf} \rho_g}{\mu} = \frac{(0.0015)(0.6076)(1.2)}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} = 60.76$$

Since $1 < Re_{mf} < 1000$, the particle operates in the **intermediate flow regime**, characteristic of Geldart-B (sand-like) particles — the ideal class for FBC operation. **The design fluidizing velocity is then chosen as $U_0 \approx 2 U_{mf} \approx 1.2$ m/s for bubbling FBC or $U_0 \approx 5\text{–}10\,U_{mf}$ for CFB.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — In-Bed Desulfurization (Ca/S Stoichiometry)

**Problem Setup:** A coal containing 2.5 % sulfur (by mass) is burned at 1000 kg/h with limestone (CaCO₃) of 95 % purity. The desired SO₂ capture efficiency is 85 %. Determine (a) the Ca/S molar ratio and (b) the limestone feed rate.

**Step 1: Express Sulfur Feed Rate.**

$$\dot{m}_S = \dot{m}_{coal} \times w_S = 1000 \times 0.025 = 25 \text{ kg S/h}$$

**Step 2: Convert to Moles.**

$$\dot{n}_S = \frac{25}{32.07} = 0.7795 \text{ kmol S/h}$$

**Step 3: Determine Moles of CaCO₃ Required.**

The sulfation stoichiometry is:

$$\text{CaCO}_3 + \text{SO}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CaSO}_4 + \text{CO}_2$$

Hence 1 mol SO₂ requires 1 mol CaCO₃. The fraction captured is $\eta_S = 0.85$:

$$\dot{n}_{CaCO_3,ideal} = 0.7795 \times 0.85 = 0.6626 \text{ kmol CaCO}_3/\text{h}$$

**Step 4: Apply Sulfation Efficiency Factor (Real Limestone).**

The molar ratio formula is:

$$\left(\frac{Ca}{S}\right)_{molar} = \frac{\dot{n}_{CaCO_3}}{\dot{n}_S} = \frac{\eta_S}{\chi_{CaCO_3}\cdot U_{CaO}}$$

For a typical liming efficiency $U_{CaO} \approx 0.45$ at 850 °C:

$$\left(\frac{Ca}{S}\right)_{molar} = \frac{0.85}{0.95 \times 0.45} = 1.988 \approx 2.0$$

**Step 5: Compute Mass Feed Rate of Limestone.**

$$\dot{n}_{CaCO_3,total} = 1.988 \times 0.7795 = 1.550 \text{ kmol/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{CaCO_3} = 1.550 \times 100.09 = 155.1 \text{ kg/h}$$

Allowing for 95 % purity:

$$\dot{m}_{limestone} = \frac{155.1}{0.95} = 163.3 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Conclusion:** Approximately **163.3 kg of limestone per hour** must be fed to the bed, with a **Ca/S molar ratio of ~2.0**, to achieve 85 % sulfur capture. This Ca/S = 2 is the textbook value that KTU examiners expect.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Char Burnout Time (Shrinking-Core Model)

**Problem Setup:** A char particle of diameter $d_p = 2$ mm and density $\rho_s = 700$ kg/m³ (after devolatilization) burns in air with bulk O₂ concentration $C_{O_2,b} = 21\,\%$ (mole fraction). The surface-reaction rate constant at 1173 K is $k_s = 0.05$ m/s. Find the burnout time.

**Step 1: Write the Shrinking-Core Equation.**

Under surface-reaction control, the burnout time for a spherical particle of diameter $d_p$ is:

$$\tau_{char} = \frac{\rho_s d_p}{2\,k_s\,C_{O_2,b}\,M_C}$$

**Step 2: Identify Molecular Weight of Carbon.**

$M_C = 12.01$ g/mol = $0.01201$ kg/mol

**Step 3: Convert O₂ Mole Fraction to Concentration.**

Assuming ideal gas at $T = 1123$ K and $P = 1$ atm:

$$C_{O_2,b} = \frac{y_{O_2} P}{R T} = \frac{0.21 \times 101325}{8.314 \times 1123} = \frac{21278.25}{9336.6} = 2.279 \text{ mol/m}^3$$

**Step 4: Substitute Values.**

$$\tau_{char} = \frac{(700)(0.002)}{2 \times 0.05 \times 2.279 \times 0.01201}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$2 \times 0.05 = 0.1$$

$$0.1 \times 2.279 = 0.2279$$

$$0.2279 \times 0.01201 = 2.737 \times 10^{-3}$$

Compute the numerator:

$$700 \times 0.002 = 1.4$$

Thus:

$$\tau_{char} = \frac{1.4}{2.737 \times 10^{-3}} = 511.5 \text{ s} \approx 8.5 \text{ min}$$

**Step 5: Validation.**

The result $\tau_{char} \approx 8.5$ min is consistent with experimental FBC data, which report 5 – 30 minutes for 1 – 5 mm coal char at 800 – 900 °C. The design residence time of coal in an FBC must therefore be at least 10 – 15 min for acceptable burnout.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Combustion Efficiency (Carbon-Loss Method)

**Problem Setup:** An FBC boiler is fired with 5000 kg/h coal containing 38 % carbon. Fly-ash collected is 320 kg/h, with 12 % unburned carbon. Bottom ash is 200 kg/h, with 4 % unburned carbon. Calculate the combustion efficiency.

**Step 1: Carbon Fed.**

$$\dot{m}_{C,fuel} = 5000 \times 0.38 = 1900 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 2: Carbon in Fly Ash.**

$$\dot{m}_{C,fly} = 320 \times 0.12 = 38.4 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 3: Carbon in Bottom Ash.**

$$\dot{m}_{C,bottom} = 200 \times 0.04 = 8.0 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 4: Total Carbon Loss.**

$$\dot{m}_{C,loss} = 38.4 + 8.0 = 46.4 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 5: Combustion Efficiency.**

$$\eta_{comb} = 1 - \frac{46.4}{1900} = 1 - 0.0244 = 0.9756 \approx 97.56\,\%$$

**Conclusion:** The FBC achieves a carbon burnout efficiency of **97.56 %**, which is excellent and typical of well-designed CFB units.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — BFBC vs. CFBC

| Parameter | BFBC | CFBC |
|---|---|---|
| Fluidizing velocity | 1 – 3 m/s | 3 – 10 m/s |
| Bed inventory | 1500 – 3000 kg/MWᵢₕ | 400 – 800 kg/MWᵢₕ |
| Combustion efficiency | 90 – 95 % | 95 – 99 % |
| Limestone utilization | 30 – 50 % | 60 – 90 % |
| NOₓ emission | 200 – 400 mg/Nm³ | 100 – 200 mg/Nm³ |
| LOI (loss on ignition) in ash | 5 – 20 % | 1 – 5 % |
| Footprint | Larger | Compact |
| Load turndown | 3:1 | 4:1 |
| Coal size | 0 – 6 mm | 0 – 8 mm |
| Typical application | Captive / small industrial | Utility / large industrial |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** CFBC is the modern preferred technology for utility-scale coal combustion due to (i) high combustion efficiency, (ii) excellent limestone utilization, and (iii) lower LOI in ash — all directly derivable from longer particle residence times and higher in-bed turbulence.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a CFBC Boiler

The Mermaid diagram below maps the functional architecture of a typical Circulating Fluidized Bed Combustor used for coal firing. All node IDs are alphanumeric, and labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text (no embedded markdown).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[COAL CRUSHER] --> B1[COAL BUNKER]
    A2[limestone bin] --> B2[limestone bunker]
    B1 --> C1[overbed feed screw]
    B2 --> C2[limestone screw feeder]
    C1 --> D1[furnace riser]
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[primary air fan]
    E1 --> F1[windbox and distributor]
    F1 --> D1
    D1 --> G1[cyclone separator]
    G1 --> G2[loopseal return]
    G2 --> D1
    D1 --> H1[external heat exchanger]
    H1 --> I1[ash cooling drum]
    I1 --> J1[ash silo]
    G1 --> K1[id fan]
    K1 --> L1[electrostatic precipitator]
    L1 --> M1[stack]
    D1 --> N1[secondary air injection]
    N1 --> D1
    D1 --> O1[bottom ash discharge]
    O1 --> I1
    D1 --> P1[waterwall evaporator panels]
    P1 --> Q1[steam drum and superheater]
    Q1 --> R1[steam turbine generator]
```

## 4.2 Functional Subsystem Description

The CFBC architecture above is composed of six integrated subsystems:

1. **Fuel & Sorbent Preparation Subsystem (A → C):**
   - The Coal Crusher (A1) reduces run-of-mine coal to 0 – 8 mm.
   - The Coal Bunker (B1) provides surge capacity.
   - The Overbed Feed Screw (C1) meters coal into the furnace riser.
   - The Limestone Bin (A2) → Bunker (B2) → Screw Feeder (C2) path supplies the sorbent for in-bed desulfurization.

2. **Air & Fluidization Subsystem (E → F):**
   - The Primary Air Fan (E1) supplies 60 % – 70 % of the stoichiometric air through the Windbox and Distributor (F1).
   - The Distributor (F1) ensures uniform fluidization velocity at the bottom of the riser.

3. **Combustion & Recirculation Subsystem (D → G):**
   - The Furnace Riser (D1) is the main reaction zone.
   - Hot gases carry partially burned particles upward into the Cyclone Separator (G1).
   - The Loopseal Return (G2) reinjects the captured solids back into the riser, completing the circulation loop.

4. **Heat Recovery Subsystem (H → P → Q → R):**
   - The External Heat Exchanger (H1) extracts heat from the recirculating solids.
   - The Waterwall Evaporator Panels (P1) and Steam Drum / Superheater (Q1) generate and superheat the steam.
   - The Steam Turbine Generator (R1) converts the steam's thermal energy into electricity.

5. **Emission Control Subsystem (L → M):**
   - The ID Fan (K1) draws flue gas from the cyclone.
   - The Electrostatic Precipitator (L1) removes > 99.9 % of particulates.
   - The Stack (M1) releases the cleaned flue gas to the atmosphere.

6. **Ash Management Subsystem (I → J, O → I):**
   - Bottom Ash Discharge (O1) and cooled fly ash from the cyclone are combined in the Ash Cooling Drum (I1).
   - The Ash Silo (J1) stores the dry ash for disposal or sale (e.g., to cement plants).

## 4.3 Schematic of the Coal Combustion Sequence Within the Bed

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[coal particle on bed surface] --> S2[moisture evaporation at 100 to 150 celsius]
    S2 --> S3[devolatilization at 350 to 500 celsius]
    S3 --> S4[volatiles ignition by o2]
    S3 --> S5[char residue with ash shell]
    S5 --> S6[o2 diffusion through ash layer]
    S6 --> S7[char surface reaction c plus o2 yields co2]
    S7 --> S8[ash skeleton collapses]
    S8 --> S9[ash elutriation or discharge]
    S4 --> S10[secondary reactions co plus o2 yields co2]
    S10 --> S11[heat release to bed and tubes]
    S7 --> S11
```

The schematic above captures the **shrinking-core model** of coal combustion: volatiles are released first, then oxygen diffuses through the ash layer to react with the residual char, and finally the ash skeleton is either elutriated or discharged.

The above functional diagrams serve as the conceptual template for those answers. Replace ASCII boxes with neat rectangles and arrows in the actual exam.
