# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q1.** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the principle of **Fluidized Bed Combustion of coal**. List any four advantages of FBC over conventional grate firing.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Fluidized bed combustion is a process in which crushed coal (0.5 – 6 mm) is suspended in a rising stream of preheated air, causing the bed of coal/ash/limestone particles to behave like a boiling fluid. Combustion occurs uniformly at **800 – 950 °C** throughout the bed.

**Principle (1 mark):** When air velocity $U_0$ exceeds the minimum fluidization velocity $U_{mf}$, the bed transitions from a fixed state to a fluidized state, providing excellent mixing of fuel, air, and heat-transfer surfaces.

**Sketch (1 mark):** A vertical combustor with windbox, distributor plate, fluidized bed (showing bubbles and particles), and coal/limestone feed points.

**Four advantages (1 mark — any 4 of the following):**
1. Fuel flexibility — can burn coal, biomass, and waste.
2. In-bed SO₂ capture by limestone addition (70 – 95 % efficiency).
3. Lower NOₓ emissions due to staged combustion and low temperature.
4. High heat-transfer coefficient (200 – 600 W/m²·K).
5. Uniform bed temperature (no hot spots).
6. Can burn low-grade, high-ash coal and washery rejects.

### Question 2 (3 marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q2.** Compare **Bubbling Fluidized Bed Combustor (BFBC)** and **Circulating Fluidized Bed Combustor (CFBC)** in terms of fluidizing velocity, combustion efficiency, and limestone utilization.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | BFBC | CFBC |
|---|---|---|
| Fluidizing velocity | 1 – 3 m/s (1 mark) | 3 – 10 m/s (1 mark) |
| Combustion efficiency | 90 – 95 % | 95 – 99 % |
| Limestone utilization | 30 – 50 % | 60 – 90 % |
| Particle residence time | 10 – 30 min | 30 – 60 min |
| LOI in ash | 5 – 20 % | 1 – 5 % (1 mark) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** CFBC outperforms BFBC in combustion efficiency and sorbent utilization due to its higher turbulence, longer residence time, and external solids recycle, making it the preferred technology for utility-scale applications.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q.A.** (a) Derive the Wen and Yu correlation for the **minimum fluidization velocity** of a coal particle, clearly stating the assumptions. **7 marks**

(b) A coal-fired FBC boiler burns **8000 kg/h** of coal containing **3.2 % sulfur** (by mass). Limestone (95 % pure CaCO₃) is to be fed to achieve **90 % sulfur capture**, with a CaO utilization of **40 %**. Calculate: (i) the required **Ca/S molar ratio**, and (ii) the **limestone feed rate in kg/h**. **7 marks**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. The bed is isothermal and at steady state.
2. Particles are spherical, uniform in size, and Geldart-B class.
3. The fluidizing gas behaves as an ideal gas.
4. Pressure drop across the bed equals the buoyant weight per unit area at incipient fluidization.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

At minimum fluidization, the pressure drop across the bed equals the weight of the particles per unit cross-sectional area:

$$\Delta P_{mf} = (1 - \varepsilon_{mf})H_{mf}(\rho_s - \rho_g)g$$

For the Wen & Yu correlation, the bed expansion and pressure drop are related by the Ergun equation:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{H_{mf}} \cdot \frac{d_p \varepsilon_{mf}^3}{(1-\varepsilon_{mf})^2} \cdot \frac{1}{\rho_g U_{mf}^2} = \frac{150(1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{Re_{mf}} + 1.75$$

For the typical voidage at minimum fluidization $\varepsilon_{mf} \approx 0.4$ for coal/ash, Wen and Yu simplified the Ergun equation into the explicit form:

$$U_{mf} = \frac{\mu}{\rho_g d_p}\left[\sqrt{27.2^2 + 0.0408\,Ar} - 27.2\right]$$

where the **Archimedes number** is:

$$Ar = \frac{g\,d_p^3\,\rho_g(\rho_s - \rho_g)}{\mu^2}$$

This correlation is valid for $Re_{mf} \in [0.001, 4000]$ and is the most widely used expression in FBC design.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Sulfur feed rate (1 mark):**

$$\dot{m}_S = 8000 \times 0.032 = 256 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$\dot{n}_S = \frac{256}{32.07} = 7.982 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 2 — Required Ca/S molar ratio (3 marks):**

From the in-bed sulfation efficiency model:

$$\left(\frac{Ca}{S}\right)_{molar} = \frac{\eta_S}{\chi_{CaCO_3} \cdot U_{CaO}} = \frac{0.90}{0.95 \times 0.40} = 2.368$$

**Step 3 — Moles of CaCO₃ required (1 mark):**

$$\dot{n}_{CaCO_3} = 2.368 \times 7.982 = 18.901 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 4 — Limestone feed rate (2 marks):**

$$\dot{m}_{CaCO_3, pure} = 18.901 \times 100.09 = 1891.2 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{limestone} = \frac{1891.2}{0.95} = 1990.7 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Final Answer:** Ca/S = **2.37** (molar); limestone feed rate ≈ **1991 kg/h**.

---

### Question B (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q.B.** (a) Explain the **shrinking-core model of char combustion** in a fluidized bed. Derive the expression for **burnout time** under surface-reaction-controlled regime. **7 marks**

(b) A CFB boiler operates at **$U_0 = 5$ m/s** with a bed height of **1.2 m**. The static bed height is **0.6 m** and the bed voidage at minimum fluidization is **$\varepsilon_{mf} = 0.42$**. The particle density is **1800 kg/m³** and gas density is **0.7 kg/m³**. Calculate: (i) the **bed expansion ratio**, and (ii) the **pressure drop across the bed**. **7 marks**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):**
A coal particle entering a fluidized bed first undergoes drying and devolatilization. The residual **char** is a porous sphere composed mostly of fixed carbon and ash. As the reaction proceeds, oxygen diffuses to the particle surface, reacts with carbon to form CO/CO₂, and the reaction front progressively retreats inward, leaving behind an **ash shell**. The unreacted core shrinks in size, hence the term "shrinking-core model."

The process is governed by three resistances in series:
1. External gas-film diffusion of O₂ to the particle surface.
2. Pore diffusion of O₂ through the ash layer to the unreacted core.
3. Surface chemical reaction at the C–O₂ interface.

**Derivation of Burnout Time (4 marks):**

Consider a spherical char particle of initial radius $R_0$ and density $\rho_s$. The surface reaction is:

$$\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2$$

with rate per unit surface area $r_s = k_s C_{O_2,s}$.

The carbon consumption rate for a particle of instantaneous radius $R$ is:

$$-\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 \rho_s\right) = 4\pi R^2 k_s C_{O_2,b}$$

Differentiating and simplifying:

$$-\rho_s \frac{dR}{dt} = k_s C_{O_2,b}$$

$$\int_{R_0}^{0} dR = -\frac{k_s C_{O_2,b}}{\rho_s} \int_{0}^{\tau_{char}} dt$$

$$\tau_{char} = \frac{\rho_s R_0}{k_s C_{O_2,b}}$$

Expressing in terms of particle diameter $d_p = 2R_0$:

$$\tau_{char} = \frac{\rho_s d_p}{2\,k_s\,C_{O_2,b}}$$

This is the **burnout time** under surface-reaction control, valid for $d_p < 1$ mm and $T < 800$ °C.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Bed expansion ratio (3 marks):**

For a bubbling fluidized bed, the bed expansion ratio $H/H_{mf}$ is given by:

$$\frac{H}{H_{mf}} = 1 + \frac{U_0 - U_{mf}}{U_b - U_{mf}}$$

where $U_b$ is the bubble rise velocity. For an approximate estimate, use the empirical correlation (Mori-Wen):

$$\frac{H - H_{mf}}{H_{mf}} = \frac{0.205(U_0 - U_{mf})}{0.55\sqrt{g d_b}} \cdot \frac{1 - \varepsilon_{mf}}{1 - \varepsilon}$$

For a quick estimation with $U_b \approx 0.6$ m/s (typical for $d_b \approx 0.2$ m):

$$\frac{H}{H_{mf}} = 1 + \frac{5 - 0.6}{0.6 - 0.6} \rightarrow \text{Use direct expansion formula}$$

Using the simplified voidage-based expansion:

$$\frac{H}{H_{mf}} = \frac{1 - \varepsilon_{mf}}{1 - \varepsilon}$$

With $\varepsilon \approx 0.7$ at $U_0 = 5$ m/s:

$$\frac{H}{H_{mf}} = \frac{1 - 0.42}{1 - 0.7} = \frac{0.58}{0.30} = 1.933$$

**Step 2 — Pressure drop across the bed (4 marks):**

The pressure drop is given by:

$$\Delta P_{bed} = (1 - \varepsilon_{mf}) H_{mf} (\rho_s - \rho_g) g$$

$$\Delta P_{bed} = (1 - 0.42) \times 0.6 \times (1800 - 0.7) \times 9.81$$

Compute each factor:

- $1 - \varepsilon_{mf} = 0.58$
- $H_{mf} = 0.6$ m
- $\rho_s - \rho_g = 1799.3$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

$$\Delta P_{bed} = 0.58 \times 0.6 \times 1799.3 \times 9.81$$

$$= 0.348 \times 1799.3 \times 9.81$$

$$= 626.16 \times 9.81$$

$$\Delta P_{bed} = 6142.4 \text{ Pa} \approx 6.14 \text{ kPa}$$

**Final Answer:** Bed expansion ratio ≈ **1.93**; Pressure drop ≈ **6.14 kPa**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Blunder in $U_{mf}$:** Many students forget to convert **mm to m** for particle diameter. The Archimedes number is extremely sensitive to $d_p$ (cubic power), so a unit slip introduces a **million-fold** error.
> 2. **Confusing $U_{mf}$ and $U_t$:** $U_{mf}$ is the velocity at which the bed just fluidizes; $U_t$ is the velocity at which a single particle is entrained. Marking one for the other is a 2-mark loss.
> 3. **Forgetting the purity factor in limestone feed rate:** Even if the Ca/S ratio is correct, forgetting to divide by purity $\chi_{CaCO_3} = 0.95$ loses **2 marks**.
> 4. **Mixing up combustion efficiency and carbon burnout:** Combustion efficiency (energy basis) ≈ carbon burnout only when no other losses exist. State the assumption clearly.
> 5. **Using $\Delta P$ formula for fluidized bed incorrectly:** The formula $\Delta P = (1-\varepsilon)H(\rho_s - \rho_g)g$ is **independent** of gas velocity once the bed is fluidized — using $U_0$ in the formula is wrong.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **FBC Definition:** Combustion of crushed coal (0.5 – 6 mm) in a bed suspended by air at $U_0 > U_{mf}$, with bed temperatures of **800 – 950 °C**.
- **Wen-Yu Correlation:** $U_{mf} = (\mu/\rho_g d_p)[\sqrt{27.2^2 + 0.0408\,Ar} - 27.2]$ is the **single most important equation** in FBC design.
- **Archimedes Number:** $Ar = g d_p^3 \rho_g (\rho_s - \rho_g)/\mu^2$ — always check **units** before substituting.
- **Two FBC Types:** BFBC ($U_0 \approx 1$ – 3 m/s) vs CFBC ($U_0 \approx 3$ – 10 m/s); CFBC gives higher efficiency and better sorbent utilization.
- **In-Bed SO₂ Capture:** Ca/S molar ratio ≈ 2.0 for 85 % capture; optimum temperature 850 °C; sulfation reaction: $\text{CaO} + \text{SO}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \to \text{CaSO}_4$.
- **NOₓ Control:** Staged air (60 – 70 % primary, 30 – 40 % secondary); sub-stoichiometric lower bed; thermal NOₓ suppressed below 950 °C.
- **Char Burnout Time (Shrinking-Core):** $\tau_{char} = \rho_s d_p / (2 k_s C_{O_2,b})$ for surface-reaction control; FBC residence times of 10 – 30 minutes comfortably exceed this.
- **Combustion Efficiency:** $\eta_{comb} = 1 - \dot{m}_{C,loss}/\dot{m}_{C,fuel}$; CFB units achieve **97 – 99 %**.
- **Bed Pressure Drop:** $\Delta P_{bed} = (1-\varepsilon_{mf}) H_{mf} (\rho_s - \rho_g) g$ — velocity-independent above $U_{mf}$.
- **Pressure Drop Equals Bed Weight per Unit Area:** This is the cardinal principle that defines $U_{mf}$ and is **frequently asked as a 2-mark short question**.
- **Heat Transfer:** In-bed convection coefficient (200 – 600 W/m²·K) >> radiation alone — use the Modified Mickley-Fairbanks correlation.
- **Fuel Flexibility:** FBC can burn high-ash Indian coal (35 – 50 % ash), washery rejects, lignite, and biomass — a major exam-relevant advantage.
- **PFBC:** Pressurized version (1 – 1.6 MPa) gives higher thermal efficiency (40 – 42 % vs 35 – 38 % for AFBC) but is mechanically complex.
- **Cyclone & Loopseal:** These are the defining components of CFB; their function (separating and reinjecting solids) is a high-yield descriptive question.
- **Key Operating Numbers to Memorize:**
  - $U_{mf} \approx 0.3$ – 1 m/s for 1 – 2 mm coal
  - $Ca/S \approx 2.0$ – 2.5 for 85 – 90 % SO₂ capture
  - $T_{bed} \approx 850 \pm 50$ °C
  - Combustion efficiency: BFBC 90 – 95 %; CFBC 95 – 99 %

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Strategy:** In the university exam, questions on fluidized combustion of coal almost always combine a **formula derivation** (Wen-Yu or sulfation) with a **numerical problem**. Practice 5 – 10 problems on minimum fluidization velocity, Ca/S stoichiometry, and char burnout time to secure full marks in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gasification of Coal and Coke
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gasification of Coal and Coke

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Gasification** is a thermochemical conversion process in which carbonaceous solid fuels (coal, coke, biomass, char) are partially oxidized and converted into a combustible gaseous product — **synthesis gas (syngas)** — primarily composed of **Carbon Monoxide (CO)**, **Hydrogen (H₂)**, **Methane (CH₄)**, and smaller fractions of CO₂, N₂, water vapor, and trace contaminants.

In the context of **Fluidization Engineering**, gasification is conducted in **fluidized bed reactors** (bubbling or circulating), where the solid fuel particles are suspended in an upward-flowing gas stream, providing excellent solid–gas contact, uniform temperature distribution, and high mass/heat transfer rates.

### Sub-Definitions of Key Product Gases

| Product Gas | Composition (typical) | Calorific Value | Gasifying Agent |
|-------------|----------------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Producer Gas** | CO (~25%), H₂ (~10%), N₂ (~55%) | **Low (4–6 MJ/Nm³)** | Air |
| **Water Gas (Blue Gas)** | CO (~40%), H₂ (~50%) | **Medium (10–12 MJ/Nm³)** | Steam |
| **Synthesis Gas (Syngas)** | CO + H₂ (in desired H₂/CO ratio) | **Medium (10–18 MJ/Nm³)** | O₂ + Steam |
| **Substitute Natural Gas (SNG)** | CH₄ (~90%) | **High (35–40 MJ/Nm³)** | H₂ (hydro-gasification) |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Syngas** is the generic term for the mixture of **CO + H₂** produced by gasification. The H₂/CO ratio can be adjusted (typically 1:1 to 3:1) for downstream synthesis of methanol, Fischer–Tropsch fuels, or ammonia.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Controlled Campfire"

Imagine a **regular campfire**. When you pile wood and light it, it burns with visible flames, releasing smoke, soot, and heat — most of the chemical energy leaves as **direct flame heat**, not as a usable gas.

> [!TIP]  
> **Gasification is a campfire running on "starvation diet."** Instead of plenty of oxygen (full combustion → only CO₂ + H₂O), we deliberately restrict the oxygen. The fuel is *partially* oxidized. Instead of getting full flame, we extract a **useful combustible gas (syngas)** that can be piped, cleaned, and burned efficiently in a furnace, turbine, or engine hundreds of meters away.

In a **fluidized bed gasifier**, the analogy is even more vivid:
- The solid fuel particles (char, coke) are like a **boiling liquid of sand** — suspended and dancing in the gas stream.
- A tiny bit of oxygen and lots of steam enter from the bottom.
- The carbon in coal/coke reacts with these gases to release **CO and H₂**, which exit the top as syngas.

---

## 1.3 Why Gasification Matters in Fluidization Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT415 Module 4):**  
> Fluidized bed gasifiers are **the single most commercially important application** of fluidization technology for solid fuel conversion. IGCC (Integrated Gasification Combined Cycle) power plants and CTL (Coal-to-Liquid) plants almost exclusively use fluidized bed or entrained-flow gasifiers.

**Key advantages of fluidized bed gasifiers:**
- **High specific throughput** (high surface area per unit volume)
- **Excellent temperature uniformity** (≈ ±10 °C across the bed)
- **Fuel flexibility** (coal, coke, biomass, MSW, petroleum coke)
- **In-situ desulfurization** possible by adding limestone/dolomite
- **Scalability** from 1 MW pilot to 600+ MW commercial units

---

## 1.4 Coal vs. Coke — A First Look

| Property | Coal | Coke |
|----------|------|------|
| Origin | Natural fossil fuel | Coal after destructive distillation (pyrolysis) |
| Volatile Matter (VM) | High (15–40%) | Very low (<2%) |
| Fixed Carbon | Moderate (40–70%) | Very high (>85%) |
| Reactivity | High (more porous, disordered carbon) | Low (graphitized, dense) |
| Moisture | Variable (5–30%) | Low (<5%) |
| Gasification Temperature | 800–950 °C (FB) | 900–1100 °C (FB) |
| Preferred Process | Air or O₂/steam FB gasification | Steam gasification (water gas) |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Coke is essentially de-volatilized coal.** When coal is heated to ~1000 °C in the absence of air, the volatiles escape, leaving behind a **dense, carbon-rich, low-reactivity** residue — coke. Because coke has no volatiles, it produces a **clean, tar-free syngas**, which is why it is preferred for the classic **water-gas reaction**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chemistry of Gasification — Primary Reactions

The gasification process involves **seven fundamental reactions**. A KTU student must memorize these with their enthalpies.

### (A) Heterogeneous Gas–Solid Reactions (involve solid carbon)

| # | Reaction | Enthalpy (kJ/mol) | Type |
|---|----------|------------------|------|
| **R1** | C + O₂ → CO₂ | **−393.5** | Combustion (exothermic) |
| **R2** | C + ½O₂ → CO | **−111.4** | Partial oxidation (exothermic) |
| **R3** | C + H₂O → CO + H₂ | **+131.3** | Steam gasification (endothermic) |
| **R4** | C + CO₂ → 2CO | **+172.5** | **Boudouard reaction** (endothermic) |
| **R5** | C + 2H₂ → CH₄ | **−74.8** | Hydro-gasification (exothermic) |

### (B) Homogeneous Gas–Gas Reactions (in gas phase)

| # | Reaction | Enthalpy (kJ/mol) | Type |
|---|----------|------------------|------|
| **R6** | CO + H₂O ⇌ CO₂ + H₂ | **−41.2** | Water-gas shift (slightly exo) |
| **R7** | CO + ½O₂ → CO₂ | **−283.0** | CO combustion (exothermic) |
| **R8** | H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O | **−241.8** | H₂ combustion (exothermic) |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **The two most critical reactions** in a fluidized bed gasifier are:
> 1. **R3 (Steam gasification / Water-gas reaction):** C + H₂O → CO + H₂ — the *workhorse* of producer gas generation.
> 2. **R4 (Boudouard reaction):** C + CO₂ → 2CO — the *primary* CO-generating step.
> These are both **endothermic**, so heat must be supplied — usually by partial combustion (R1/R2) in the lower bed.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Gasification Process

> [!TIP]  
> **Think of a fluidized bed gasifier as a "chemical assembly line"** that processes the carbon in 4 sequential zones:

1. **Zone 1 — Drying (100–200 °C):**  
   Moisture in coal evaporates. Negligible for coke. Endothermic.

2. **Zone 2 — Devolatilization / Pyrolysis (300–700 °C):**  
   Volatile matter in coal releases as **tar, light hydrocarbons, CO, H₂, CH₄, CO₂, H₂O vapor**.  
   Char (solid carbon) remains. **This step is unique to coal and absent in coke.**

3. **Zone 3 — Char Gasification (700–1100 °C):**  
   The char (or coke) reacts with steam, CO₂, and O₂ via R1–R6. **This is the rate-limiting step** that controls the overall gasifier throughput.

4. **Zone 4 — Homogeneous Gas-Phase Reactions:**  
   In the freeboard (above the bed), R6 (water-gas shift) and minor combustion (R7, R8) adjust the final gas composition.

---

## 2.3 Types of Gasification Processes (KTU High-Yield)

### 2.3.1 Air Gasification → **Producer Gas**

$$\text{Overall: } \quad \text{C} + \text{O}_2 + 3.76\text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} + 1.88\text{N}_2$$

- Diluted with **77% N₂** (from air), so calorific value is low.
- **Self-sustaining** — the combustion (R1) supplies heat for endothermic R3, R4.
- Air-to-fuel ratio is the **key operating knob**.

### 2.3.2 Steam Gasification → **Water Gas**

$$\text{Overall: } \quad \text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2$$

- **Endothermic** overall — requires external heat input (alternating with air-blow cycle in classic fixed-bed).
- In **fluidized bed**, heat is supplied continuously via internal combustion of a fraction of the feed char.
- Produces **high-quality medium-BTU gas** (~11 MJ/Nm³).

### 2.3.3 Oxygen/Steam Gasification → **Synthesis Gas**

$$\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2$$

- Combustion (R1) provides the heat; steam gasification (R3) consumes it.
- **No nitrogen dilution** → HHV ~10–18 MJ/Nm³.
- H₂/CO ratio controlled by steam/O₂ ratio.

### 2.3.4 Hydro-gasification → **SNG (Substitute Natural Gas)**

$$\text{C} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4$$

- Exothermic, but kinetically very slow.
- High pressure (30–70 bar) shifts equilibrium toward CH₄.
- Produces pipeline-quality gas (~37 MJ/Nm³).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **The following equations are the most frequently tested formulas** in KTU Module 4 examinations.

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} = \sum \Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{reactants})$ | Heat of reaction by Hess's law |
| 2 | $K_p(T) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}(T)}{RT}\right)$ | Equilibrium constant from standard Gibbs free energy |
| 3 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{K_{p,2}}{K_{p,1}}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | van 't Hoff equation — effect of T on Kₚ |
| 4 | $\dfrac{x_{CO}\cdot x_{H_2}}{x_{H_2O}} = K_{p,R6}$ | Water-gas shift equilibrium expression |
| 5 | $\dfrac{x_{CO}^2}{x_{CO_2}} = K_{p,R4}(T) \cdot P$ | Boudouard reaction (note: P in atm; here P refers to system pressure in bar) |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{cold gas}} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{\text{gas}} \cdot \text{HHV}_{\text{gas}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \cdot \text{HHV}_{\text{fuel}}} \times 100\%$ | **Cold Gas Efficiency (CGE)** — % of fuel energy in raw syngas |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{\text{gas}} \cdot \text{HHV}_{\text{gas}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \cdot \text{HHV}_{\text{fuel}} + Q_{\text{input}}} \times 100\%$ | Thermal efficiency with external heat |
| 8 | $\text{Carbon Conversion} = 1 - \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{unconverted C}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{C in feed}}}$ | Fraction of feed carbon gasified |
| 9 | $r_{c} = \dfrac{k_1\,P_{O_2}}{1 + k_2\,P_{O_2} + k_3\,P_{H_2O}} + k_4\,P_{H_2O}$ | Char gasification rate (Langmuir–Hinshelwood) |
| 10 | $\text{Steam Decomposition} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{H_2O,\text{in}} - \dot{n}_{H_2O,\text{out}}}{\dot{n}_{H_2O,\text{in}}} \times 100\%$ | % of inlet steam that reacts |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Important tip on notation:** In KTU problems, partial pressures may be denoted $p_i$ or $P_i$ and the absolute value bars (e.g. $P - P^{\circ}$) are written as $\vert P - P^{\circ} \vert$ to avoid markdown table breakage.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| **Power Generation** | IGCC power plants (e.g., 600 MW Buggenum, Netherlands; 250 MW Tampa, USA) |
| **Liquid Fuels** | Fischer–Tropsch diesel, methanol, DME (Sasol, South Africa) |
| **Chemicals** | Ammonia synthesis (H₂ source), oxo-alcohols (CO source) |
| **Steel Industry** | DRI (Direct Reduced Iron) — reduction of iron ore by syngas |
| **Hydrogen Economy** | Pre-combustion CO₂ capture from syngas |
| **Waste-to-Energy** | Gasification of MSW, RDF, sewage sludge |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat of the Water-Gas Reaction (R3)

> **Statement:** Show that $\Delta H^{\circ}_{298}$ for C(s) + H₂O(g) → CO(g) + H₂(g) is **+131.3 kJ/mol** using standard heats of formation.

**Standard Heats of Formation ($\Delta H_f^{\circ}$ at 298 K):**

| Species | $\Delta H_f^{\circ}$ (kJ/mol) |
|---------|-------------------------------|
| C (s, graphite) | 0 |
| H₂O (g) | −241.8 |
| CO (g) | −110.5 |
| H₂ (g) | 0 |

**Derivation:**

$$\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^{\circ} = \sum \Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{reactants})$$

$$\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^{\circ} = \left[\Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{CO}) + \Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{H}_2)\right] - \left[\Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{C}) + \Delta H_f^{\circ}(\text{H}_2\text{O})\right]$$

$$\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^{\circ} = [(-110.5) + (0)] - [(0) + (-241.8)]$$

$$\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^{\circ} = -110.5 - (-241.8)$$

$$\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^{\circ} = -110.5 + 241.8 = +131.3 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

> **Interpretation:** The positive sign confirms R3 is **endothermic** — heat must be supplied to drive the steam gasification of carbon. This heat is supplied by partial combustion (R1, R2) in the lower part of the fluidized bed.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Overall Energy Balance on a Fluidized Bed Gasifier

**Given:**
- Feed: $F$ kg/h of coal, with mass fractions: C = 0.65, H = 0.04, O = 0.08, S = 0.01, N = 0.01, Ash = 0.08, Moisture = 0.13.
- Air supply: $A$ kg/h at 25 °C, 50% relative humidity.
- Gasifier operates at 900 °C, 1 bar, steady state, adiabatic.
- HHV of coal = 25 MJ/kg.

**Step 1: Carbon Balance (per hour)**

$$\dot{n}_{C,\text{in}} = \frac{0.65 \cdot F}{12} \text{ kmol/h}$$

$$\dot{n}_{C,\text{out}} = \dot{n}_{CO} + \dot{n}_{CO_2} + \dot{n}_{CH_4} + \dot{n}_{\text{unconverted C}}$$

**Step 2: Oxygen Balance**

$$0.21 \cdot \frac{A}{29} + \frac{0.08 \cdot F}{32} = \dot{n}_{CO} + 2\dot{n}_{CO_2} + \dot{n}_{H_2O,\text{out}}$$

**Step 3: Hydrogen Balance**

$$2\dot{n}_{H_2O,\text{in}} + 2\dot{n}_{H_2O,\text{feed}} + \dot{n}_{CH_4} = 2\dot{n}_{H_2O,\text{out}} + 2\dot{n}_{H_2} + 4\dot{n}_{CH_4}$$

$$2\dot{n}_{H_2O,\text{in}} + 2 \cdot \frac{0.13F}{18} + \dot{n}_{CH_4} = 2\dot{n}_{H_2O,\text{out}} + 2\dot{n}_{H_2} + 4\dot{n}_{CH_4}$$

**Step 4: Energy Balance (Adiabatic, so $Q = 0$)**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{coal}} \cdot \text{HHV}_{\text{coal}} + \dot{m}_{\text{air}} \cdot h_{\text{air}} = \dot{m}_{\text{gas}} \cdot h_{\text{gas}} + \dot{m}_{\text{tar}} \cdot h_{\text{tar}} + \dot{m}_{\text{ash}} \cdot h_{\text{ash}}$$

For an approximate cold-gas efficiency, the simplified form is:

$$\eta_{\text{CGE}} = \frac{\dot{V}_{\text{syngas}} \cdot \text{HHV}_{\text{syngas}}}{F \cdot \text{HHV}_{\text{coal}}} \times 100\%$$

$$\dot{V}_{\text{syngas}} = \frac{\dot{n}_{\text{CO}} + \dot{n}_{H_2} + \dot{n}_{CH_4}}{P / (RT)} \quad \text{(using ideal gas law at STP)}$$

$$\dot{V}_{\text{syngas}} = 22.4 \cdot \left(\dot{n}_{CO} + \dot{n}_{H_2} + \dot{n}_{CH_4}\right) \quad \text{Nm}^3/\text{h}$$

> [!TIP]  
> **Substitution example:** If the solved mole flow rates are $\dot{n}_{CO} = 50$ kmol/h, $\dot{n}_{H_2} = 35$ kmol/h, $\dot{n}_{CH_4} = 5$ kmol/h, and the gas HHV is 12 MJ/Nm³, with $F = 1000$ kg/h and HHV of coal = 25 MJ/kg:
>
> $$\dot{V}_{\text{syngas}} = 22.4 \times (50 + 35 + 5) = 22.4 \times 90 = 2016 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}$$
>
> $$\eta_{\text{CGE}} = \frac{2016 \times 12}{1000 \times 25} \times 100\% = \frac{24192}{25000} \times 100\% = 96.8\%$$
>
> (Note: this is a high CGE, indicating efficient gasification.)

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Equilibrium Composition via Water-Gas Shift

For the water-gas shift reaction (R6) at temperature $T$:

$$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2$$

Let the initial mole numbers be: $n_{CO} = a$, $n_{H_2O} = b$, $n_{CO_2} = c$, $n_{H_2} = d$.  
Let $x$ = moles of CO reacted at equilibrium. Then:

| Species | Initial | Change | Equilibrium |
|---------|---------|--------|-------------|
| CO | $a$ | $-x$ | $a - x$ |
| H₂O | $b$ | $-x$ | $b - x$ |
| CO₂ | $c$ | $+x$ | $c + x$ |
| H₂ | $d$ | $+x$ | $d + x$ |
| **Total** | $a+b+c+d$ | 0 | $a+b+c+d$ |

The equilibrium constant (in terms of mole fractions, assuming ideal gas at total pressure $P$):

$$K_p = \frac{x_{CO_2}\cdot x_{H_2}}{x_{CO}\cdot x_{H_2O}}$$

$$K_p = \frac{(c+x)(d+x)}{(a-x)(b-x)}$$

Solving for $x$ (a quadratic) gives the equilibrium composition.

**Example:** For a feed of 1 kmol CO + 1 kmol H₂O at 1100 K, $K_p = 1.0$:

$$K_p = \frac{(0 + x)(0 + x)}{(1 - x)(1 - x)} = \frac{x^2}{(1-x)^2} = 1$$

$$\Rightarrow x^2 = (1-x)^2 \Rightarrow x = 1 - x \Rightarrow x = 0.5$$

At equilibrium: 0.5 kmol CO, 0.5 kmol H₂O, 0.5 kmol CO₂, 0.5 kmol H₂.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Steam Decomposition Ratio in Coke Gasification

**Given:** A fluidized bed steam gasifier processes 1000 kg/h of coke (95% C, 5% ash). Steam supplied at 1100 °C = 2500 kg/h. Exit gas contains 18% H₂O (mole basis, dry-gas-free).

**Step 1: Moles of carbon in feed**

$$\dot{n}_C = \frac{0.95 \times 1000}{12} = 79.17 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 2: Moles of steam input**

$$\dot{n}_{H_2O,\text{in}} = \frac{2500}{18} = 138.89 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 3: Steam decomposition** (assuming exit is dry-basis 18% H₂O → so 82% dry gas). On a wet basis:

$$y_{H_2O,\text{wet}} = \frac{0.18}{1 + 0.18} \times 100\% \approx 15.25\%$$

Let $D$ = kmol/h of steam decomposed. Then by stoichiometry (R3): $D$ kmol CO + $D$ kmol H₂ produced.

**Dry gas composition (before adding unreacted steam):**
- CO: 79.17 kmol/h (assuming all C → CO)
- H₂: $D$ kmol/h
- N₂: 0
- Total dry = $79.17 + D$ kmol/h

**Unreacted steam = $138.89 - D$ kmol/h**

**Wet-gas condition:**

$$y_{H_2O} = \frac{138.89 - D}{138.89 - D + 79.17 + D} = \frac{138.89 - D}{218.06}$$

Setting this equal to 0.1525:

$$138.89 - D = 0.1525 \times 218.06 = 33.25$$

$$D = 138.89 - 33.25 = 105.64 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Steam decomposition ratio:**

$$\text{SD} = \frac{D}{\dot{n}_{H_2O,\text{in}}} \times 100\% = \frac{105.64}{138.89} \times 100\% = 76.07\%$$

> **Result:** **Steam decomposition ≈ 76%** — typical for a well-operated fluidized bed coke gasifier at 900–950 °C.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Coal vs. Coke Fluidized Bed Gasifier

| Parameter | Coal FB Gasifier | Coke FB Gasifier |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|
| Operating temperature | 800–950 °C | 900–1050 °C |
| Devolatilization step | **Yes — critical** | Negligible (no volatiles) |
| Tar formation | Yes — must be cracked | **Virtually zero** |
| Char reactivity | High (porous, disordered) | Low (graphitic) |
| Residence time needed | 5–15 s | 15–30 s |
| Bed material | Sand, olivine, limestone | Sand, alumina |
| Steam-to-carbon ratio (S/C) | 0.5–1.0 | 1.0–2.0 (excess to compensate low reactivity) |
| Typical cold gas efficiency | 70–85% | 60–75% |
| Main product gas | Producer gas or syngas | **Water gas** (CO + H₂) |
| Major issue | Tar, alkali vapors, fouling | High ash fusion, slagging |
| Scale-up status | Commercial (Winkler, U-Gas, KRW) | Commercial (limited) |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Examination Insight:** Questions often ask, "Why is coke preferred for water-gas manufacture over coal?"  
> **Answer:** Coke has *no volatile matter* → no tar, no soot, no light hydrocarbons in product → gives a *clean* CO + H₂ mixture suitable for downstream catalysis (Fischer–Tropsch, ammonia, methanol). Coal produces tar that must be removed in a complex secondary step.

> **Reading the diagram:** Coal from the hopper, preheated air, superheated steam, and limestone all enter the fluidized bed reactor. The bed operates at 800–950 °C, where drying, devolatilization, and char gasification occur simultaneously. Ash is withdrawn at the bottom. Raw syngas + entrained fines go to a primary cyclone (fines returned to bed), then to a syngas cooler (waste-heat boiler), venturi scrubber (tar/particulates removed), compressor, water-gas shift reactor (H₂/CO ratio tuned), acid-gas removal (H₂S, COS, CO₂), and finally clean syngas exits for synthesis.
