# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]  
**Define gasification. How does it differ from combustion?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Gasification is a thermochemical process that converts carbonaceous solid fuels into a combustible gaseous product (synthesis gas) by **partial oxidation** at elevated temperatures (700–1100 °C) and controlled sub-stoichiometric oxygen supply. The key products are **CO and H₂** (syngas).

| Aspect | Gasification | Combustion |
|--------|--------------|------------|
| Oxygen supply | Sub-stoichiometric (controlled) | Stoichiometric or excess |
| Products | CO, H₂, CH₄, CO₂, N₂ | Only CO₂, H₂O, N₂, SO₂ |
| Calorific value of product | Useful combustible gas (4–18 MJ/Nm³) | No useful gas (flue gas) |
| Purpose | Chemical feedstock + heat | Heat only |

*(Marks: 1 — definition, 1 — comparison, 1 — examples)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]  
**Write the water-gas reaction and explain why it is endothermic.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\text{C (s)} + \text{H}_2\text{O (g)} \rightarrow \text{CO (g)} + \text{H}_2\text{(g)}, \quad \Delta H = +131.3 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

The reaction is **endothermic** because the bonds in the gaseous product (C≡O in CO, H–H in H₂) require more energy to form than is released when the bonds in liquid-like H₂O are broken, *plus* the solid carbon must sublimate/dissociate into the gas phase. Heat must therefore be supplied continuously to sustain the reaction. In a fluidized bed gasifier, this heat is supplied by *in-situ* partial combustion of carbon (R1, R2) in the lower dense phase of the bed.

*(Marks: 1 — equation, 1 — ΔH value, 1 — explanation of why endothermic)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to balance the carbon atoms** when writing overall gasification reactions (e.g., writing C + H₂O → CO + H₂ without checking that 1 C in = 1 C out).
> 2. **Confusing gasification with pyrolysis** — pyrolysis is purely thermal decomposition *without* an oxidizing agent; gasification *requires* controlled O₂, steam, or H₂.
> 3. **Mixing up cold-gas efficiency with thermal efficiency** — CGE only accounts for chemical energy of dry syngas, not sensible heat.
> 4. **Wrong units on steam decomposition** — always express in **kmol/h or mol/mol basis**, not in kg.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**a)** With neat sketches, explain the working of a **bubbling fluidized bed coal gasifier**. Discuss the various zones and the reactions occurring in each zone. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A fluidized bed gasifier is fed with **1000 kg/h of coal** having the following ultimate analysis: C = 70%, H = 5%, O = 10%, N = 1%, S = 1%, Ash = 8%, Moisture = 5% (by mass). Air is supplied at the rate of **4.5 kg air/kg coal** at 25 °C. The dry product gas composition is: CO₂ = 14%, CO = 22%, H₂ = 12%, CH₄ = 2%, N₂ = 50% (by volume). Calculate the **Cold Gas Efficiency (CGE)** given HHV of coal = 28 MJ/kg, HHV of dry gas = 6 MJ/Nm³. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (a) (7 marks)**

**Introduction (1 mark):**  
A bubbling fluidized bed (BFB) coal gasifier consists of a vertical cylindrical vessel with a perforated gas distributor plate at the bottom. Crushed coal (1–6 mm) is fed via a screw feeder or lock hopper, and fluidizing gas (air, O₂, or steam) enters through the distributor at velocity $U$ satisfying $U_{mf} < U < U_{mb}$ (between minimum fluidization and minimum bubbling velocity).

**Zones (each zone = 1 mark, 4 zones = 4 marks):**

1. **Zone I — Drying Zone (100–200 °C):**  
   Moisture in coal evaporates. Endothermic. Predominant in the upper dilute phase.

2. **Zone II — Devolatilization / Pyrolysis Zone (300–700 °C):**  
   Volatile matter releases as **tar vapor, light hydrocarbons, CO, H₂, CO₂, H₂O, H₂S**. Char remains. Exothermic overall.

3. **Zone III — Char Gasification Zone (700–1000 °C):**  
   Char + O₂ → CO₂ (R1, exothermic)  
   Char + H₂O → CO + H₂ (R3, endothermic)  
   Char + CO₂ → 2CO (R4, Boudouard, endothermic)  
   This is the rate-controlling zone and determines the carbon conversion.

4. **Zone IV — Homogeneous Reaction Zone (Freeboard, 800–1100 °C):**  
   CO + H₂O ⇌ CO₂ + H₂ (R6, water-gas shift)  
   Minor combustion of CO and H₂ with residual O₂ (R7, R8).

**Role of bed material (1 mark):**  
Sand, olivine, or limestone act as the heat carrier. Limestone absorbs H₂S via:  
$$\text{CaCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2$$  
$$\text{CaO} + \text{H}_2\text{S} \rightarrow \text{CaS} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$  
This is **in-situ desulfurization** — a major advantage of FB gasifiers.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**  
The dry syngas exits the freeboard, is cooled, cleaned, and sent for downstream use. Ash is discharged via an overflow weir at the bottom.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (b) (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Coal feed = 1000 kg/h, HHV = 28 MJ/kg
- Air = 4.5 × 1000 = 4500 kg/h
- Dry gas composition: CO₂ = 14%, CO = 22%, H₂ = 12%, CH₄ = 2%, N₂ = 50% (vol% = mol%)
- HHV of dry gas = 6 MJ/Nm³

**Step 1: Carbon balance to find total dry gas flow** (2 marks)

Carbon in coal: $1000 \times 0.70 / 12 = 58.33$ kmol C/h

Carbon in dry gas (per kmol of dry gas):
$$n_C = 0.14 + 0.22 + 0.02 = 0.38 \text{ kmol C per kmol dry gas}$$

Total dry gas: $58.33 / 0.38 = 153.5$ kmol/h

**Step 2: Convert to volumetric flow at STP** (1 mark)

$$\dot{V}_{\text{dry gas}} = 153.5 \times 22.4 = 3438.4 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 3: Calculate energy in syngas** (2 marks)

$$\dot{E}_{\text{gas}} = 3438.4 \times 6 = 20630.4 \text{ MJ/h}$$

**Step 4: Calculate energy in coal** (1 mark)

$$\dot{E}_{\text{coal}} = 1000 \times 28 = 28000 \text{ MJ/h}$$

**Step 5: Cold Gas Efficiency** (1 mark)

$$\eta_{\text{CGE}} = \frac{20630.4}{28000} \times 100\% = 73.68\%$$

> **Answer: CGE ≈ 73.7%** — typical of an air-blown BFB coal gasifier (literature range 65–80%).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**a)** What is **producer gas**? With the help of overall reactions, describe the **air gasification of coke** in a fixed-bed/moving-bed gasifier. Why is the calorific value of producer gas low? **(7 marks)**

**b)** A fluidized bed steam gasifier is fed with **2000 kg/h of coke** (90% C, 10% ash). Steam flow rate = **5000 kg/h at 1000 °C**. The dry product gas analyzes: CO = 38%, H₂ = 52%, CO₂ = 8%, N₂ = 2% (by volume). Calculate:  
   (i) Steam decomposition ratio  
   (ii) Carbon conversion efficiency  
   (iii) Specific steam consumption (kg steam per kg of coke gasified)  
   **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B (a) (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):**  
**Producer gas** is a low-calorific-value fuel gas obtained by the **air gasification of carbonaceous solids** (coke, coal, charcoal). It typically contains 20–30% CO, 5–15% H₂, and **50–60% N₂** (the diluent from air), giving a calorific value of only **4–6 MJ/Nm³** (compared to natural gas at 35 MJ/Nm³).

**Overall reactions (3 marks):**

1. **Combustion zone (bottom, exothermic):**  
   $$\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2, \quad \Delta H = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$  
   $$\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}, \quad \Delta H = -111.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$$  
   This is where the **heat** is generated.

2. **Reduction zone (above combustion, endothermic):**  
   $$\text{CO}_2 + \text{C} \rightarrow 2\text{CO}, \quad \Delta H = +172.5 \text{ kJ/mol (Boudouard)}$$  
   $$\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{C} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2, \quad \Delta H = +131.3 \text{ kJ/mol}$$  
   This is where the **combustible gases** are formed.

3. **Overall stoichiometry for producer gas:**  
   $$2\text{C} + \text{O}_2 + 3.76\text{N}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO} + 3.76\text{N}_2$$

**Process description (2 marks):**  
In a **fixed-bed/moving-bed gasifier** (e.g., Wellman, Lurgi), a bed of coke sits on a grate. Air enters from the bottom. The bottommost layer (combustion zone, ~1500 °C) gets the most O₂, generating heat. The next layer (reduction zone, 800–1100 °C) uses the rising hot CO₂ and H₂O to gasify more carbon. The top layer is the ash/drying zone.

**Why low calorific value (1 mark):**  
Because **air is 79% N₂**, the producer gas is diluted with nitrogen — the N₂ acts as a thermal ballast. Combustion heat must heat up this inert N₂, but the N₂ does not contribute to flame temperature or combustion energy. **Removing the N₂ (using pure O₂)** gives medium-BTU gas.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B (b) (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Coke feed = 2000 kg/h with 90% C, 10% ash
- Steam = 5000 kg/h
- Dry gas: CO = 38%, H₂ = 52%, CO₂ = 8%, N₂ = 2% (mol%)

**Step 1: Moles of carbon in feed** (1 mark)

$$\dot{n}_C = \frac{0.90 \times 2000}{12} = 150 \text{ kmol C/h}$$

**Step 2: Total dry gas flow** (1 mark)

Let $\dot{n}_{\text{dry}}$ = kmol dry gas/h. Carbon balance:

$$\dot{n}_C = 0.38\,\dot{n}_{\text{dry}} + 0.08\,\dot{n}_{\text{dry}} = 0.46\,\dot{n}_{\text{dry}}$$

$$\dot{n}_{\text{dry}} = \frac{150}{0.46} = 326.09 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 3: Moles of steam input** (1 mark)

$$\dot{n}_{H_2O,\text{in}} = \frac{5000}{18} = 277.78 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 4: Steam decomposition (SD)** (1 mark)

Hydrogen balance: H₂ in dry gas must come from steam decomposition. Total H₂ in dry gas = 0.52 × 326.09 = 169.57 kmol/h (assuming all H₂ from steam). H₂O decomposed = H₂ produced = 169.57 kmol/h.

$$\text{SD} = \frac{169.57}{277.78} \times 100\% = 61.05\%$$

**Step 5: Carbon conversion** (1 mark)

All carbon leaves as CO + CO₂ (no CH₄ in this gas).

$$\eta_C = \frac{(0.38 + 0.08) \times 326.09}{150} = \frac{150.0}{150} = 100\%$$

Wait — recheck: $0.38 + 0.08 = 0.46$; $0.46 \times 326.09 = 150.0$ kmol C/h. So **100% carbon conversion** (all carbon gasified). *(In a fluidized bed at 1000 °C, this is achievable for a fine coke.)*

**Step 6: Specific steam consumption** (1 mark)

$$\text{SSC} = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{steam, in}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{coke gasified}}} = \frac{5000}{2000} = 2.5 \text{ kg steam/kg coke}$$

> **Final Answers:**  
> (i) Steam decomposition = **61.05%**  
> (ii) Carbon conversion = **100%**  
> (iii) Specific steam consumption = **2.5 kg/kg coke**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — must-know points for KTU Module 4 Gasification**

- **Gasification ≠ Combustion** — gasification uses *sub-stoichiometric* O₂ to make syngas (CO + H₂); combustion uses *excess* O₂ to make CO₂ + H₂O.
- **The 7 key reactions** to memorize: R1 (C+O₂→CO₂), R2 (C+½O₂→CO), R3 (C+H₂O→CO+H₂, water-gas, +131.3 kJ/mol), R4 (C+CO₂→2CO, Boudouard, +172.5 kJ/mol), R5 (C+2H₂→CH₄), R6 (CO+H₂O⇌CO₂+H₂, water-gas shift, −41.2 kJ/mol), R7 (CO+½O₂→CO₂), R8 (H₂+½O₂→H₂O).
- **Producer gas** = air gasification = low BTU (4–6 MJ/Nm³) due to N₂ dilution.  
  **Water gas** = steam gasification = medium BTU (10–12 MJ/Nm³).  
  **Syngas** = O₂/steam gasification = medium BTU (10–18 MJ/Nm³).  
  **SNG** = hydro-gasification = high BTU (35–40 MJ/Nm³).
- **Coal has volatiles → tar & gas release during pyrolysis; coke has no volatiles → cleaner syngas but lower reactivity.**
- **Boudouard reaction (R4)** is favored at **high temperatures (>700 °C)**; it is the primary CO generator.
- **Water-gas shift (R6)** adjusts H₂/CO ratio — exothermic, so *low* temperature favors product side; *high* temperature favors reactant side.
- **Coke is preferred over coal for water-gas manufacture** because it gives a *tar-free* CO + H₂ mixture.
- **Fluidized bed gasifiers** offer: uniform T, fuel flexibility, in-situ desulfurization, high throughput — but face ash-sintering challenges.
- **Cold Gas Efficiency (CGE)** = (energy in dry syngas) / (energy in feed fuel) × 100%. Typical: 60–85%.
- **Steam decomposition** in FB coke gasifiers = 50–80%; the rest of the steam leaves unreacted (must be condensed and recycled).
- **Air/Fuel ratio** is the key operating parameter in producer gas manufacture; **Steam/Carbon ratio** is the key parameter in water-gas manufacture.
- **Endothermic gasification reactions** require heat — supplied by partial combustion of feed char (R1, R2) in the lower bed.
- **In-situ desulfurization** uses limestone: CaO + H₂S → CaS + H₂O; this captures up to 90% of fuel sulfur inside the gasifier itself.

> **Best of luck with your KTU Module 4 preparation!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calcination
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Calcination

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Calcination** is a thermal treatment process applied to solid particulate materials (typically ores, minerals, or synthetic precursors) in the presence of air or a controlled gaseous atmosphere — at temperatures **below the melting point** — to bring about one or more of the following effects: (a) **thermal decomposition** (e.g., carbonate → oxide + CO₂), (b) **removal of volatile fractions** (moisture, organic matter, CO₂), (c) **phase transition** (e.g., transition aluminas → α-Al₂O₃), or (d) **densification** of the solid structure.

When this process is carried out in a **fluidized bed reactor**, the solid particles are suspended in an upward-flowing gas stream, ensuring excellent gas–solid contact, uniform temperature distribution, and high rates of heat and mass transfer. The major industrial fluidized-bed calcination processes include:

1. **Calcination of alumina tri-hydrate (Al₂O₃·3H₂O)** to produce metallurgical-grade or smelter-grade alumina.
2. **Calcination of limestone (CaCO₃)** to produce lime (CaO).
3. **Calcination of magnesite (MgCO₃)** to produce magnesia (MgO).
4. **Calcination of dolomite, bauxite, phosphate rock, and synthetic precipitates.**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Popcorn Maker" Model:** Imagine a popcorn popper where hot air is blown from below through a bed of corn kernels. The kernels (solid particles) get suspended (fluidized), get heated uniformly on all sides, and "transform" (the moisture inside vaporizes and escapes, just as CO₂ or H₂O escapes from a mineral during calcination). The hot air both heats and removes the released gases, exactly like the fluidizing gas in a calciner does.

A simpler everyday analogy is **baking a clay brick in a kiln**:
- The raw clay (ore/feed) contains bound water and carbonates — these are the "volatile guests."
- Heating in air drives off the guests, leaving behind a hard, stable, dehydrated oxide brick — the **calcined product**.
- A **fluidized bed** is essentially a "high-tech kiln" where every grain of solid is bathed in hot gas from all sides, making the transformation **faster and more uniform** than in a static kiln.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Calcination temperature for alumina | T | **800–1300 °C** |
| Calcination temperature for limestone | T | **900–1000 °C** |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | P | **101.325 kPa** |
| Universal gas constant | R | **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** |
| Standard residence time (BFB alumina) | τ | **30–120 minutes** |
| Standard residence time (CFB alumina) | τ | **2–10 minutes** |
| Typical fluidization number (u/u_mf) | — | **1.2–3.0 (BFB)**, **5–15 (CFB)** |

### 1.4 Industrial Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Calcination of alumina alone accounts for **over 70% of the world's fluidized bed industrial applications** by throughput. Single fluidized bed calciners can process **3000–5000 tonnes/day** of alumina hydrate, making them among the largest chemical reactors in existence.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Calcination Reaction — Generalized Form

For a generic hydrated or carbonated mineral, the calcination reaction takes the form:

$$
\text{Solid A (feed)} \xrightarrow{\Delta,\ \text{air}} \text{Solid B (calcined product)} + \text{Gas C (volatile)}
$$

**Specific examples:**
- Alumina hydrate: $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}\uparrow$
- Limestone: $\text{CaCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{CaO} + \text{CO}_2\uparrow$
- Magnesite: $\text{MgCO}_3 \rightarrow \text{MgO} + \text{CO}_2\uparrow$
- Bauxite (gibbsite component): same as alumina tri-hydrate.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Physical & Chemical Mechanism

The calcination of a single particle proceeds through **three sequential resistances**:

1. **Heat transfer from the bulk fluidized gas to the external surface of the particle** (convective + radiative).
2. **Heat conduction through the porous product layer (the "ash" or calcined shell) into the unreacted core.**
3. **Chemical reaction at the reaction front** (endothermic decomposition, with release of gaseous product).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three resistances?** Because as calcination proceeds, the outer layer of the particle becomes calcined (porous oxide), while the inner core is still unreacted. Heat must diffuse through this porous shell to reach the reaction front — exactly analogous to the **shrinking core model** used in chemical reaction engineering.

### 2.3 The Shrinking Core Model (Per Particle)

For a spherical particle undergoing calcination:

- Let $r_c$ = radius of the unreacted core at time $t$
- Let $R$ = radius of the particle (constant)
- The reaction front moves inward, leaving behind a porous product shell of thickness $(R - r_c)$

The **time for complete calcination** of a single particle is:

$$
t^* = \frac{\rho_B \cdot \Delta H_{rxn} \cdot R}{3 h \cdot (T_\infty - T_s)} \quad \text{(heat-transfer controlled)}
$$

where:
- $\rho_B$ = molar density of solid (kmol·m⁻³)
- $\Delta H_{rxn}$ = heat of reaction (J·kmol⁻¹)
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W·m⁻²·K⁻¹)
- $T_\infty$ = bulk gas temperature (K)
- $T_s$ = surface temperature of particle (K)

### 2.4 Heat Transfer Coefficient in a Fluidized Bed

The convective heat transfer coefficient between the gas and the particle in a fluidized bed is empirically correlated (most widely used is the **Wakao–Kaguei correlation**):

$$
\text{Nu} = 2 + 1.1 \cdot \text{Re}_p^{0.6} \cdot \text{Pr}^{1/3}
$$

where:
- $\text{Nu} = \dfrac{h \cdot d_p}{k_g}$ (Nusselt number)
- $\text{Re}_p = \dfrac{\rho_g \cdot u \cdot d_p}{\mu_g}$ (particle Reynolds number)
- $\text{Pr} = \dfrac{C_{p,g} \cdot \mu_g}{k_g}$ (Prandtl number)
- $d_p$ = particle diameter (m)
- $k_g$ = gas thermal conductivity (W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹)
- $u$ = superficial gas velocity (m·s⁻¹)

For dense fluidized beds (bubbling), the **overall bed-to-surface heat transfer coefficient** is typically in the range **100–400 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹**, which is **an order of magnitude higher** than in packed beds — this is precisely why fluidization is preferred for calcination.

### 2.5 Reactor Design Equations (Bubbling Fluidized Bed — BFB)

For a **steady-state, well-mixed fluidized bed calciner**, an energy balance yields the design equation relating **residence time** to operating conditions.

**Mean residence time of solids:**

$$
\tau_s = \frac{W}{F_s}
$$

where:
- $W$ = mass of solids in the bed (kg)
- $F_s$ = mass flow rate of solids (kg·s⁻¹)

**Heat duty required:**

$$
Q = F_s \cdot \Delta H_{rxn} + F_s \cdot C_{p,s} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in}) + \dot{m}_g \cdot C_{p,g} \cdot (T_{in} - T_{feed})
$$

**Gas flow requirement for fluidization:**

$$
u = (1.5 \text{ to } 3.0) \cdot u_{mf}
$$

where $u_{mf}$ is computed from the **Ergun equation** at incipient fluidization.

### 2.6 Reactor Design Equations (Circulating Fluidized Bed — CFB)

For CFB calciners (e.g., Alcoa CFB, Lurgi CFB), the **solids are pneumatically conveyed** and collected by cyclones. The mean residence time is much shorter:

$$
\tau_s^{CFB} = \frac{H \cdot \rho_{bed} \cdot A}{F_s}
$$

Typical CFB alumina calciners operate with:
- $\tau_s \approx 2$–$10$ minutes (vs. 30–120 min in BFB)
- Higher specific throughput (~5000 t/day in a single unit)
- Lower specific heat consumption (~3.0 GJ/t alumina vs. ~5.0 GJ/t in rotary kilns)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why CFB over BFB for calcination?** CFB calciners expose particles to very high heat fluxes for short times, which preserves the **mesoporous structure** of α-alumina (desirable for many downstream applications) and significantly reduces specific fuel consumption.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — MUST MEMORIZE FOR KTU EXAM**

| # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Nu} = 2 + 1.1 \cdot \text{Re}_p^{0.6} \cdot \text{Pr}^{1/3}$ | Particle-to-gas heat transfer |
| 2 | $u_{mf}$ from Ergun: $\dfrac{1.75}{\phi_s \epsilon_{mf}^3} \text{Re}_{mf}^2 + \dfrac{150(1-\epsilon_{mf})}{\phi_s^2 \epsilon_{mf}^3}\text{Re}_{mf} = \text{Ar}$ | Minimum fluidization velocity |
| 3 | $\text{Ar} = \dfrac{\rho_g (\rho_s - \rho_g) g d_p^3}{\mu_g^2}$ | Archimedes number |
| 4 | $t^* = \dfrac{\rho_B \Delta H R}{3 h (T_\infty - T_s)}$ | Single-particle calcination time |
| 5 | $\tau_s = \dfrac{W}{F_s}$ | Mean residence time of solids |
| 6 | $Q = F_s \Delta H + F_s C_{p,s}\Delta T + \dot{m}_g C_{p,g} \Delta T_g$ | Energy balance on calciner |
| 7 | $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = k \cdot e^{-E/RT}$ (Arrhenius) | Reaction kinetics (decomposition) |
| 8 | $\Delta P_{bed} = (1-\epsilon)(\rho_s - \rho_g) g H$ | Bed pressure drop |
| 9 | $\tau_s^{CFB} \approx 2\text{–}10 \text{ min}$ | CFB typical residence time |
| 10 | $u_{operational} = (1.2\text{–}3.0) u_{mf}$ | Operating velocity range (BFB) |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Metallurgical industries:** Smelter-grade alumina for aluminum production (Hall–Héroult cells).
- **Cement industries:** Pre-calcination of limestone in circulating fluidized beds (now the global standard for new cement plants).
- **Refractories:** Dead-burned magnesia and dolomite.
- **Specialty chemicals:** Calcined alumina for abrasives, ceramics, catalysts, and adsorbents.
- **Environmental:** Activation of bauxite for water treatment.

> [!TIP]
> Calcination in fluidized beds is also called **"thermal activation"** when applied to clays, zeolites, or catalysts — the same physics, different terminology.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Single-Particle Calcination Time (Heat Transfer Controlled)

**Starting point:** For a spherical particle of radius $R$, undergoing an endothermic calcination reaction at the receding core front at radius $r_c$.

**Step 1 — Heat balance on the unreacted core over a small time $dt$:**

The heat required to decompose a thin shell of thickness $dr$ at the core radius $r_c$:

$$
dQ = \rho_B \cdot \Delta H_{rxn} \cdot 4\pi r_c^2 \cdot (-dr_c)
$$

**Step 2 — Heat supplied to the particle from the surrounding gas:**

By Newton's law of cooling, the heat delivered to the particle surface is:

$$
dQ = h \cdot 4\pi R^2 \cdot (T_\infty - T_s) \cdot dt
$$

> (We assume the particle is small, so internal conduction resistance in the product shell is negligible, and the surface temperature $T_s$ ≈ reaction front temperature $T_{rxn}$.)

**Step 3 — Equating the two expressions:**

$$
\rho_B \cdot \Delta H_{rxn} \cdot 4\pi r_c^2 \cdot (-dr_c) = h \cdot 4\pi R^2 \cdot (T_\infty - T_s) \cdot dt
$$

**Step 4 — Separating variables:**

$$
-\rho_B \cdot \Delta H_{rxn} \cdot r_c^2 \, dr_c = h \cdot R^2 \cdot (T_\infty - T_s) \, dt
$$

**Step 5 — Integrating from $r_c = R$ (start, $t = 0$) to $r_c = 0$ (complete calcination, $t = t^*$):**

$$
-\rho_B \Delta H_{rxn} \int_R^0 r_c^2 \, dr_c = h R^2 (T_\infty - T_s) \int_0^{t^*} dt
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluating the integrals:**

Left side:

$$
-\rho_B \Delta H_{rxn} \left[\dfrac{r_c^3}{3}\right]_R^0 = -\rho_B \Delta H_{rxn} \left(0 - \dfrac{R^3}{3}\right) = \dfrac{\rho_B \Delta H_{rxn} R^3}{3}
$$

Right side:

$$
h R^2 (T_\infty - T_s) \cdot t^*
$$

**Step 7 — Solving for $t^*$:**

$$
t^* = \dfrac{\rho_B \Delta H_{rxn} R^3}{3 h R^2 (T_\infty - T_s)}
$$

$$
\boxed{t^* = \dfrac{\rho_B \cdot \Delta H_{rxn} \cdot R}{3 h \cdot (T_\infty - T_s)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The time for complete calcination scales **linearly with particle radius $R$**. Halving the particle size halves the time. This is why fluidized bed calcination uses **fine particles** (typically 40–300 μm) — short residence time, high throughput.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Ergun Equation for Minimum Fluidization Velocity

**Step 1 — Pressure drop across a packed bed (general form, Ergun 1952):**

$$
\dfrac{\Delta P}{H} \cdot \dfrac{\epsilon^3}{(1-\epsilon)^2} = 150 \dfrac{\mu_g u}{( \phi_s d_p)^2} + 1.75 \dfrac{\rho_g u^2}{\phi_s d_p}
$$

**Step 2 — At minimum fluidization**, the pressure drop equals the buoyant weight of the bed per unit area:

$$
\dfrac{\Delta P_{mf}}{H} = (1 - \epsilon_{mf})(\rho_s - \rho_g) g
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting into the Ergun equation:**

$$
(1 - \epsilon_{mf})(\rho_s - \rho_g) g \cdot \dfrac{\epsilon_{mf}^3}{(1-\epsilon_{mf})^2} = 150 \dfrac{\mu_g u_{mf}}{(\phi_s d_p)^2} + 1.75 \dfrac{\rho_g u_{mf}^2}{\phi_s d_p}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplifying and defining dimensionless groups:**

Defining $\text{Re}_{mf} = \dfrac{\rho_g u_{mf} d_p}{\mu_g}$ and $\text{Ar} = \dfrac{\rho_g (\rho_s - \rho_g) g d_p^3}{\mu_g^2}$, the equation reduces to the classical form:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{1.75}{\phi_s \epsilon_{mf}^3} \text{Re}_{mf}^2 + \dfrac{150 (1 - \epsilon_{mf})}{\phi_s^2 \epsilon_{mf}^3} \text{Re}_{mf} = \text{Ar}}
$$

This implicit equation is solved iteratively (or graphically) for $u_{mf}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Steady-State Energy Balance on the Fluidized Bed Calciner

**Assumptions:**
- Well-mixed bed (uniform temperature $T_b$)
- Steady-state
- Negligible heat loss to surroundings
- Solids enter at $T_{s,in}$ and leave at $T_{s,out} = T_b$
- Gas enters at $T_{g,in}$ and leaves at $T_{g,out}$

**Step 1 — Energy entering with solids:**

$$
E_{s,in} = F_s C_{p,s} T_{s,in}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy leaving with calcined solids:**

$$
E_{s,out} = F_s C_{p,s} T_b
$$

**Step 3 — Energy required for the endothermic reaction:**

$$
E_{rxn} = F_s \Delta H_{rxn}
$$

**Step 4 — Energy balance:**

$$
\dot{m}_g C_{p,g} (T_{g,in} - T_{g,out}) = F_s C_{p,s} (T_b - T_{s,in}) + F_s \Delta H_{rxn}
$$

**Step 5 — Solving for the required gas mass flow rate:**

$$
\dot{m}_g = \dfrac{F_s [C_{p,s}(T_b - T_{s,in}) + \Delta H_{rxn}]}{C_{p,g} (T_{g,in} - T_{g,out})}
$$

**Step 6 — Conversion to volumetric flow:**

$$
\dot{V}_g = \dfrac{\dot{m}_g}{\rho_g}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check:** The denominator $T_{g,in} - T_{g,out}$ is determined by the overall heat transfer coefficient between gas and bed. A high $h$ gives a large denominator → less gas flow → smaller blower, lower operating cost. Fluidized beds shine here with $h_{overall}$ of 200–600 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Calciner Types

| Feature | Rotary Kiln | BFB Calciner | CFB Calciner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Typical feed size | Coarse (mm–cm) | Fine (40–300 μm) | Fine (40–200 μm) |
| Residence time | 30–120 min | 30–120 min | 2–10 min |
| Heat consumption (alumina) | ~5.5 GJ/t | ~4.5 GJ/t | ~3.0 GJ/t |
| Max throughput per unit | ~3000 t/day | ~2500 t/day | ~5000 t/day |
| Product uniformity | Moderate | High | High |
| α-alumina content control | Limited | Moderate | Excellent |
| Capital cost (relative) | Low | Moderate | High |
| NOx emissions | Higher | Lower | Lowest |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Fluidized Bed Calcination Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawFeed["RAW FEED HOPPER"] --> feedPreheater["CYCLONE PREHEATER"]
    feedPreheater --> feedScrew["FEED SCREW CONVEYOR"]
    feedScrew --> distributor["DISTRIBUTOR PLATE"]
    airFan["FORCED DRAFT FAN"] --> airHeater["PRIMARY AIR HEATER"]
    airHeater --> windbox["WINDBOX / PLENUM CHAMBER"]
    windbox --> distributor
    distributor --> fbBed["FLUIDIZED BED REACTOR"]
    fuelBurner["FUEL / OIL BURNER"] --> fbBed
    fbBed --> cyclones["PRIMARY CYCLONES"]
    cyclones --> baghouse["BAGHOUSE FILTER"]
    baghouse --> stack["STACK"]
    cyclones --> productCooler["PRODUCT COOLER"]
    productCooler --> productSilo["PRODUCT SILO"]
    fbBed --> bedCoolingCoils["IN-BED COOLING COILS"]
    bedCoolingCoils --> steamGen["WASTE HEAT STEAM GENERATOR"]
    steamGen --> powerBlock["POWER CYCLE / PROCESS HEAT"]
    productSilo --> shipOut["SHIPMENT TO CUSTOMER"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Shrinking Core Model (Single Particle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    gasBulk["BULK GAS AT T_inf"] --> convHTC["CONVECTIVE FILM h"]
    convHTC --> surfT["PARTICLE SURFACE T_s"]
    surfT --> porousShell["POROUS PRODUCT SHELL"]
    porousShell --> rxnFront["REACTION FRONT r_c T_rxn"]
    rxnFront --> unreactedCore["UNREACTED CORE"]
    rxnFront --> releaseGas["VOLATILE RELEASE CO2 OR H2O"]
    releaseGas --> porousShell
    porousShell --> convHTC
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Heat and Mass Transfer Resistances (Step-Wise)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1["STEP 1: External convective heat transfer from gas to particle surface"] --> step2["STEP 2: Conduction through porous product shell to reaction front"]
    step2 --> step3["STEP 3: Endothermic chemical decomposition at reaction front"]
    step3 --> step4["STEP 4: Diffusion of released gas outward through porous shell"]
    step4 --> step5["STEP 5: Convective mass transfer of released gas into bulk gas stream"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip (KTU):** Whenever a calcination problem is given, always draw this **three-resistance** picture first. It automatically tells you which equation to apply: heat-transfer controlled (small particle, low $\Delta T$), reaction-controlled (large particle, high $T$), or diffusion-controlled (very dense product shell).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1, Dec 2023 Style]**
> **Q1.** Define **calcination**. Give two industrial examples of fluidized-bed calcination.

**Model Answer:**
Calcination is the thermal treatment of a solid in air (or controlled atmosphere) at temperatures below its melting point, to drive off volatile constituents such as CO₂ or H₂O, and/or to induce phase transformations.
*Example 1:* Calcination of alumina tri-hydrate to α-alumina in a CFB.
*Example 2:* Calcination of limestone (CaCO₃) to lime (CaO) in a CFB pre-calciner of cement plants.
*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for examples)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Why is a **fluidized bed** preferred over a **packed bed** or **rotary kiln** for calcination of fine particulate ores? List any three reasons.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Uniform temperature** due to intense solids mixing — prevents local hot spots that would sinter the product.
2. **Very high heat-transfer coefficients** (200–600 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹) — reduces residence time and fuel consumption.
3. **Excellent gas–solid contact** — released gases (CO₂, H₂O vapor) are swept away immediately, preventing back-reaction and equilibrium limitations.
*(3 marks: 1 mark per reason)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern: Module 4, 14 Marks]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch and the shrinking-core model, derive the expression for the time required for complete calcination of a single spherical particle when the process is controlled by **external heat transfer**. State all assumptions.

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (state clearly):**
1. Spherical, non-porous feed particle of radius $R$.
2. Endothermic, irreversible first-order decomposition at a sharp reaction front.
3. Pseudo-steady-state heat transfer.
4. Product shell is porous; gas leaves easily, so mass-transfer resistance is negligible.
5. Particle is small → negligible internal conduction resistance.
6. Surface temperature $T_s$ ≈ reaction front temperature $T_{rxn}$.

**Derivation:**

The heat required to decompose a thin shell at the reaction front (located at $r_c$):

$$
dQ_{rxn} = \rho_B \Delta H_{rxn} \cdot 4\pi r_c^2 \, (-dr_c)
$$

The heat delivered to the particle from the gas:

$$
dQ_{conv} = h \cdot 4\pi R^2 \cdot (T_\infty - T_s) \, dt
$$

Equating:

$$
\rho_B \Delta H_{rxn} \cdot r_c^2 \, (-dr_c) = h R^2 (T_\infty - T_s) \, dt
$$

Integrating from $r_c = R$ at $t = 0$ to $r_c = 0$ at $t = t^*$:

$$
\rho_B \Delta H_{rxn} \int_0^R r_c^2 \, dr_c = h R^2 (T_\infty - T_s) \int_0^{t^*} dt
$$

$$
\rho_B \Delta H_{rxn} \cdot \dfrac{R^3}{3} = h R^2 (T_\infty - T_s) \cdot t^*
$$

$$
\boxed{t^* = \dfrac{\rho_B \Delta H_{rxn} R}{3 h (T_\infty - T_s)}}
$$

**Sketch:** Show the spherical particle with outer porous shell and inner unreacted core, with $T_\infty$, $h$, $T_s$, $T_{rxn}$, $R$, $r_c$, and the released gas arrow.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A CFB calciner processes **4500 tonnes/day** of alumina tri-hydrate (Al₂O₃·3H₂O) at 1000 °C. The feed enters at 25 °C. The heat of reaction is **ΔH = 3.0 GJ per tonne of feed**. Specific heats: $C_{p,s} = 1.0$ kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹ (feed), $C_{p,g} = 1.05$ kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹ (flue gas). Flue gas enters at 1100 °C and leaves at 950 °C. Compute the **mass flow rate of flue gas** required (in kg·s⁻¹). Take molecular weight of feed = 156 g·mol⁻¹.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert feed rate to kg·s⁻¹:**

$$
F_s = \dfrac{4500 \times 1000}{24 \times 3600} = 52.08 \text{ kg·s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy required per second (reaction + solid heating):**

Reaction heat:

$$
Q_{rxn} = 52.08 \times 3.0 \times 10^6 \text{ J/tonne × (1 tonne / 1000 kg)} = 52.08 \times 3000 = 156,250 \text{ kW}
$$

Solid heating:

$$
Q_{heat} = F_s \cdot C_{p,s} \cdot (T_b - T_{s,in}) = 52.08 \times 1.0 \times (1000 - 25) = 50,738 \text{ kW}
$$

Total energy to be supplied by gas:

$$
Q_{total} = 156,250 + 50,738 = 206,988 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy available from gas per unit mass:**

$$
\Delta T_{gas} = 1100 - 950 = 150 \text{ K}
$$

$$
q_{gas} = C_{p,g} \cdot \Delta T_{gas} = 1.05 \times 150 = 157.5 \text{ kJ·kg}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Required gas mass flow rate:**

$$
\dot{m}_g = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{q_{gas}} = \dfrac{206,988}{157.5} = \boxed{1314.2 \text{ kg·s}^{-1}}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain, with a sketch, the **bubbling fluidized bed (BFB)** and **circulating fluidized bed (CFB)** configurations used for calcination. Compare them on the basis of residence time, specific heat consumption, and product quality.

**Model Answer Outline:**

**BFB (Bubbling Fluidized Bed):**
- Operates at $u = 1.5$–$3 u_{mf}$.
- Bed has a clear interface; particles remain largely inside the vessel.
- Solids residence time: 30–120 min.
- Used in stationary fluidized bed calciners (e.g., for alumina, lime).
- Heat consumption ~4.5 GJ/t.
- Excellent temperature uniformity within the bed.

**CFB (Circulating Fluidized Bed):**
- Operates at $u = 5$–$15 u_{mf}$ (above terminal velocity of fines).
- No clear bed surface; particles are pneumatically transported.
- Cyclones at top return solids to the bed via a standpipe.
- Residence time: 2–10 min.
- Heat consumption ~3.0 GJ/t (lower due to better heat recovery).
- Higher α-alumina content control.

**Comparison Table (to be drawn in exam):**

| Parameter | BFB | CFB |
|---|---|---|
| Residence time | 30–120 min | 2–10 min |
| Specific heat consumption | ~4.5 GJ/t | ~3.0 GJ/t |
| Particle exposure | Mild, long | Intense, short |
| Product structure | Coarser α-Al₂O₃ | Finer, controlled porosity |
| Capital cost | Lower | Higher |

*(7 marks: 3 for BFB description, 3 for CFB description, 1 for comparison)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the **Wakao–Kaguei correlation**, compute the **gas-to-particle heat transfer coefficient** for a calciner operating with air at 900 °C. Data: $d_p = 100$ μm, $u = 1.5$ m·s⁻¹. Air properties at 900 °C: $\rho_g = 0.30$ kg·m⁻³, $\mu_g = 4.5 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $k_g = 0.08$ W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹, $C_{p,g} = 1100$ J·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Prandtl number:**

$$
\text{Pr} = \dfrac{C_{p,g} \cdot \mu_g}{k_g} = \dfrac{1100 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.08}
$$

$$
\text{Pr} = \dfrac{0.0495}{0.08} = 0.619
$$

**Step 2 — Compute particle Reynolds number:**

$$
\text{Re}_p = \dfrac{\rho_g u d_p}{\mu_g} = \dfrac{0.30 \times 1.5 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}{4.5 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
\text{Re}_p = \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^{-5}}{4.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.0
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Wakao–Kaguei correlation:**

$$
\text{Nu} = 2 + 1.1 \cdot \text{Re}_p^{0.6} \cdot \text{Pr}^{1/3}
$$

$$
\text{Pr}^{1/3} = 0.619^{1/3} = 0.851
$$

$$
\text{Re}_p^{0.6} = 1.0^{0.6} = 1.0
$$

$$
\text{Nu} = 2 + 1.1 \times 1.0 \times 0.851 = 2 + 0.936 = 2.936
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $h$:**

$$
h = \dfrac{\text{Nu} \cdot k_g}{d_p} = \dfrac{2.936 \times 0.08}{100 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
h = \dfrac{0.2349}{10^{-4}} = \boxed{2349 \text{ W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-1}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT** drop the constant "2" in the Wakao–Kaguei equation — it accounts for conduction in a stagnant fluid and contributes significantly when $\text{Re}_p$ is small. Many students drop it → loses 1 mark.
> - **Unit trap:** Always convert $d_p$ to **meters** before dividing. Common error: leaving it in μm.
> - **Round-off discipline:** Show 3 significant figures at the final step.
> - **Sketch marks:** In Question A part (a), the **diagram carries 2 out of 7 marks**. Even a hand-drawn neat sketch with labeled $R$, $r_c$, $T_\infty$, $T_s$ will be evaluated. Do not skip the figure.
> - **Assumptions list:** In derivations, the **list of assumptions is mandatory** — usually 1.5 marks out of 7.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Calcination (Module 4)**

- ✅ Calcination = thermal decomposition below melting point, in air; products are solid oxide + volatile gas.
- ✅ Three industrial examples: **alumina, limestone, magnesite.**
- ✅ Three sequential resistances: **convective → conductive → chemical reaction.**
- ✅ Shrinking core model gives $t^* = \dfrac{\rho_B \Delta H R}{3 h \Delta T}$ — **linear in $R$.**
- ✅ Wakao–Kaguei: $\text{Nu} = 2 + 1.1 \cdot \text{Re}_p^{0.6} \cdot \text{Pr}^{1/3}$ — the **most-used correlation** in KTU.
- ✅ Ergun equation → defines $u_{mf}$ → operating $u = 1.5$–$3 u_{mf}$ for BFB, $5$–$15 u_{mf}$ for CFB.
- ✅ BFB → $\tau \approx 30$–$120$ min, $\sim 4.5$ GJ/t heat consumption.
- ✅ CFB → $\tau \approx 2$–$10$ min, $\sim 3.0$ GJ/t heat consumption, finer & more uniform product.
- ✅ Calciner energy balance: $\dot{m}_g = \dfrac{F_s [\Delta H + C_{p,s}(T_b - T_{s,in})]}{C_{p,g}(T_{g,in} - T_{g,out})}$.
- ✅ Industrial benchmarks: alumina calciner = **3000–5000 t/day**; pre-calciner in cement = modern global standard.
- ✅ Always draw a **labeled sketch** in any derivation question — 2 marks reserved for the figure.
- ✅ Always state the **list of assumptions** before any derivation — at least 1.5 marks.
- ✅ Units to watch: $d_p$ in **m**, $\Delta T$ in **K** (not °C), $F_s$ in **kg·s⁻¹** (not t/day) for SI computations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microproject/Assignment/Self-study - Fitting Reported Mass Transfer and Heat Transfer Data with the Bubbling Bed Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT415 - FLUIDIZATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Microproject/Assignment/Self-study - Fitting Reported Mass Transfer and Heat Transfer Data with the Bubbling Bed Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Bubbling Bed Model (BBM)** — most commonly the **Kunii–Levenspiel Model** — is a two- or three-phase hydrodynamic framework used to characterize gas–solid fluidized beds. It divides the bed into a **bubble phase** (containing negligible solids), a **cloud–wake phase** (solids entrained behind bubbles), and an **emulsion phase** (solids at minimum fluidization surrounded by gas at $U_{mf}$). The model expresses interphase transport in terms of the **bubble-to-emulsion gas interchange coefficient, $K_{be}$** (units of $\text{s}^{-1}$), and uses it to predict the rates of **mass transfer** (e.g., drying, adsorption, ozone decomposition, FCC catalyst regeneration) and **heat transfer** (e.g., between bed and immersed surfaces or between phases).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fitting Reported Data** means using the model equations with experimentally measured mass/heat transfer rates as a *response variable*, and adjusting the **bubble diameter ($d_b$)** — and therefore $K_{be}$ — until model predictions match the observed data. This is a classical **non-linear parameter estimation** problem in chemical reaction engineering.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a busy bus terminus during rush hour:

- The **bubbles** are like the buses moving through a crowd of waiting passengers (solids).
- The **emulsion** is the crowd of passengers standing shoulder-to-shoulder.
- The **cloud–wake** is the small group of people who get pulled along briefly by each bus.
- **Mass transfer** between bubble gas and the surrounding crowd happens only through the bus doors (a small interface) — most of the bubble gas simply "bypasses" the crowd.
- The model asks: *"How much of the bubble's cargo actually gets transferred to the crowd, given the bus size, speed, and crowd density?"* The answer is encoded in $K_{be}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU assignments:** Real reactors rarely operate at ideal plug-flow conditions. The bubbling bed model lets the engineer **back-calculate the effective bubble size** from observed conversion or heat-transfer data, giving a single lumped parameter that characterizes bed hydrodynamics.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and parameters are standard in this model:

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g_c = 9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$
- **Archimedes number (used in bubble-growth correlations):** $Ar$
- **Minimum fluidization velocity:** $U_{mf}$ (in $\text{m s}^{-1}$)
- **Bubble diameter:** $d_b$ (in $\text{m}$)
- **Interchange coefficient:** $K_{be}$ (in $\text{s}^{-1}$)
- **Bed height:** $H$ (in $\text{m}$)
- **Superficial gas velocity:** $U_0$ (in $\text{m s}^{-1}$)
- **Particle diameter:** $d_p$ (in $\text{m}$ or $\mu\text{m}$)
- **Bed diameter:** $D_t$ (in $\text{m}$)
- **Gas diffusivity:** $D$ (in $\text{m}^2 \text{ s}^{-1}$)
- **Thermal conductivity of gas:** $k_g$ (in $\text{W m}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Phase Structure of the Bubbling Bed Model

The bubbling bed model is built on the following physical assumptions:

1. **Bubble phase** — gas flows in plug flow, solids concentration is essentially **zero**, and the gas moves at velocity $(U_0 - U_{mf})$.
2. **Cloud–wake phase** — a thin region of gas + solids that moves with the bubble. Its volume fraction is typically $\alpha_c \approx 0.2$ relative to the bubble phase.
3. **Emulsion phase** — gas flows at minimum fluidization velocity ($U_{mf}$) in plug flow, and the rest of the gas is held as gas in the cloud.

The gas interchange between the bubble and the emulsion occurs through two paths in series:

$$ \frac{1}{K_{be}} = \frac{1}{K_{bc}} + \frac{1}{K_{ce}} $$

where:
- $K_{bc}$ is the bubble-to-cloud interchange coefficient,
- $K_{ce}$ is the cloud-to-emulsion interchange coefficient.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Mass Transfer Fitting

When fitting reported mass-transfer data:

**Step 1 — Compute $U_{mf}$ and properties.** Use the Ergun equation or reported values for $U_{mf}$, $\varepsilon_{mf}$, and $d_p$.

**Step 2 — Assume or estimate the bubble diameter.** A common approach is to use the **Mori–Wen correlation** (a KTU-favorite):

$$ \frac{d_{bm} - d_b}{d_{bm} - d_{b0}} = \exp\left(-0.3 \frac{H}{D_t}\right) $$

where $d_{bm} = 0.652 \left[A(U_0 - U_{mf})\right]^{0.4}$ (in metres), and $A$ is the bed cross-sectional area.

**Step 3 — Compute the interchange coefficients:**

$$ K_{bc} = 4.5 \left(\frac{U_{mf}}{d_b}\right) + 5.85 \left(\frac{D^{0.5} g^{0.25}}{d_{b}^{1.25}}\right) $$

$$ K_{ce} = 6.77 \left(\frac{D \varepsilon_{mf} U_{b}}{d_{b}^{3}}\right)^{0.5} $$

**Step 4 — Compute $K_{be}$** from the harmonic mean as above.

**Step 5 — Solve the model equation** for a first-order (or other) reaction and compare with experimental $X$ (conversion) data.

**Step 6 — Vary $d_b$** (the only adjustable parameter) to minimize the residual:

$$ SSR = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(X_{\text{exp},i} - X_{\text{model},i}\right)^{2} $$

**Step 7 — Use a non-linear solver** (e.g., Newton–Raphson, Levenberg–Marquardt) to identify the optimum $d_b$ that fits the data.

> [!NOTE]
> For heat transfer data fitting, the same logic applies, but the **response variable** is the **heat transfer coefficient between the bed and an immersed surface, $h$** — modelled via correlations of the form:
> $$ \text{Nu} = \frac{h d_p}{k_g} = f\left(\text{Re}_p, \text{Pr}, \text{Archimedes number}\right) $$
> Alternatively, one uses a **two-resistance model** (bubble resistance + emulsion resistance) and again fits $K_{be}$ to data.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Symbols / Units | Engineering Use |
|---|----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $K_{be}^{-1} = K_{bc}^{-1} + K_{ce}^{-1}$ | $K_{be}, K_{bc}, K_{ce}$ in $\text{s}^{-1}$ | Combined interchange rate |
| 2 | $K_{bc} = 4.5 \left(\dfrac{U_{mf}}{d_b}\right) + 5.85 \left(\dfrac{D^{0.5} g^{0.25}}{d_{b}^{1.25}}\right)$ | $U_{mf}$ in $\text{m s}^{-1}$, $d_b$ in $\text{m}$ | Bubble-cloud transfer |
| 3 | $K_{ce} = 6.77 \left(\dfrac{D \varepsilon_{mf} U_{b}}{d_{b}^{3}}\right)^{0.5}$ | $D$ in $\text{m}^{2} \text{s}^{-1}$, $U_b$ in $\text{m s}^{-1}$ | Cloud-emulsion transfer |
| 4 | $U_b = U_0 - U_{mf} + 0.711 \left(g d_b\right)^{0.5}$ | All in SI | Bubble rise velocity |
| 5 | $d_{bm} = 0.652 \left[A(U_0 - U_{mf})\right]^{0.4}$ | $A$ in $\text{m}^{2}$, $d_{bm}$ in $\text{m}$ | Max stable bubble size |
| 6 | $\dfrac{d_{bm} - d_b}{d_{bm} - d_{b0}} = \exp\left(-0.3 \dfrac{H}{D_t}\right)$ | All in SI | Bubble growth along bed height |
| 7 | $X = 1 - \exp\left[-K_{be} \dfrac{H}{U_0}\right]$ (well-mixed limit) | $X$ dimensionless, $H$ in $\text{m}$ | Conversion for first-order rxn |
| 8 | $\tau = H / U_0$ (gas residence time) | $\tau$ in $\text{s}$ | Time spent in bed |
| 9 | $\text{Nu} = h d_p / k_g$ | $h$ in $\text{W m}^{-2} \text{K}^{-1}$, $k_g$ in $\text{W m}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1}$ | Heat transfer analysis |
| 10 | $\text{Re}_p = \rho_g U_0 d_p / \mu_g$ | Dimensionless | Particle Reynolds number |
| 11 | $SSR = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(X_{\text{exp},i} - X_{\text{model},i}\right)^{2}$ | $X$ dimensionless | Objective function for fitting |
| 12 | $\eta = \dfrac{1}{\phi}\left[\dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{1}{6}\left(\dfrac{\phi - 1}{\phi}\right)\right]$ (effectiveness via Thiele modulus $\phi$) | $\eta, \phi$ dimensionless | Catalyst effectiveness (FCC/ozone studies) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application | Why BBM is used |
|----------|-------------|-----------------|
| Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC) | Regenerator design | Predicts coke burn-off in bubbling regenerator |
| Ozone decomposition studies | Lab reactor modelling | Classic first-order reaction; clean data for fitting |
| Drying of grains / polymers | Bubbling fluidized bed dryer | Predicts drying kinetics |
| Wastewater treatment (bio-fluidization) | Oxygen transfer | $K_{be}$ correlates with oxygen uptake rate |
| Coal/biomass combustion | Bubbling fluidized bed combustor | Heat transfer to immersed tubes |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Combined Interchange Coefficient $K_{be}$

The derivation begins by defining the flux of gas exchanged between bubble and emulsion in two sequential steps.

### Step 1 — Bubble-to-Cloud Mass Transfer ($K_{bc}$)

Bubble gas flows in plug flow. Mass transfer across the bubble–cloud interface follows a two-mechanism model: (a) convection due to gas through-flow, and (b) molecular diffusion at the interface.

The convective contribution comes from the volumetric gas flow through the bubble divided by bubble volume:

$$ K_{bc,\text{conv}} = \frac{\text{volumetric flow through bubble}}{\text{bubble volume}} = \frac{Q_{b,\text{through}}}{V_b} $$

Using $Q_{b,\text{through}} = U_{mf} \cdot (\pi d_b^{2}/4)$ and $V_b = \pi d_b^{3}/6$, the ratio scales as $U_{mf}/d_b$. A proportionality constant of $4.5$ (Kunii–Levenspiel, 1991) accounts for wake shedding. The diffusive contribution scales as $D^{0.5} g^{0.25} / d_b^{1.25}$, with a prefactor of $5.85$. Adding both contributions:

$$ K_{bc} = 4.5 \left(\frac{U_{mf}}{d_b}\right) + 5.85 \left(\frac{D^{0.5} g^{0.25}}{d_{b}^{1.25}}\right) $$

### Step 2 — Cloud-to-Emulsion Mass Transfer ($K_{ce}$)

The cloud is a thin, low-velocity gas region. Diffusion dominates. The transfer path length scales as the cloud thickness, which is $\propto d_b$. A dimensional analysis (see Kunii–Levenspiel, *Fluidization Engineering*, 2nd ed.) gives:

$$ K_{ce} = 6.77 \left(\frac{D \varepsilon_{mf} U_b}{d_{b}^{3}}\right)^{0.5} $$

The factor $6.77$ arises from the geometric proportionality constants and wake-fraction correlation.

### Step 3 — Combining Resistances

Treating bubble and cloud as two gas-transfer resistances in series, the overall driving force is the concentration difference between the bubble and the bulk emulsion. The flux balance gives:

$$ \frac{1}{K_{be}} = \frac{1}{K_{bc}} + \frac{1}{K_{ce}} $$

Substituting the expressions:

$$ \frac{1}{K_{be}} = \frac{1}{4.5 \left(\dfrac{U_{mf}}{d_b}\right) + 5.85 \left(\dfrac{D^{0.5} g^{0.25}}{d_{b}^{1.25}}\right)} + \frac{1}{6.77 \left(\dfrac{D \varepsilon_{mf} U_b}{d_{b}^{3}}\right)^{0.5}} $$

This is the **master expression** that depends on $d_b$. Hence, fitting $K_{be}$ to data is equivalent to fitting $d_b$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Conversion Equation (Well-Mixed Limiting Case)

For a first-order reaction with rate constant $k_r$ on the solid catalyst, with negligible resistance inside the particle, and a bubble rising in plug flow, the governing ODE for bubble-gas concentration $C_b$ as a function of height $z$ is:

$$ U_b \frac{dC_b}{dz} = -K_{be}\left(C_b - C_e\right) $$

For the limiting case of **fast interchange** ($K_{be} \to \infty$), the bed behaves as well-mixed, and the inlet concentration equilibrates with the bed instantly. The conversion reduces to:

$$ X = 1 - \exp\left(-K_{be} \frac{H}{U_0}\right) $$

The exponent $K_{be} H / U_0$ is the **number of interchange units (NTU)**, analogous to NTU in heat/mass exchanger design.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Assignment-Style)

**Given:** A bubbling fluidized bed reactor has the following parameters:

- $U_0 = 0.30 \text{ m s}^{-1}$
- $U_{mf} = 0.05 \text{ m s}^{-1}$
- $D_t = 0.30 \text{ m}$, $H = 1.0 \text{ m}$
- $d_p = 500 \text{ }\mu\text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$
- $D = 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{2} \text{ s}^{-1}$
- $\varepsilon_{mf} = 0.5$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$
- First-order reaction with $k_r = 1.0 \text{ s}^{-1}$

**Step A — Compute bed cross-section area:**

$$ A = \frac{\pi D_t^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.30)^{2}}{4} = 0.0707 \text{ m}^{2} $$

**Step B — Compute maximum bubble size:**

$$ d_{bm} = 0.652 \left[A(U_0 - U_{mf})\right]^{0.4} = 0.652 \left[0.0707 \times 0.25\right]^{0.4} $$

$$ = 0.652 \left[0.01768\right]^{0.4} = 0.652 \times 0.1333 = 0.0869 \text{ m} $$

**Step C — Assume $d_{b0} = 0.01 \text{ m}$ (distributor bubble). Solve for $d_b$ at the top:**

$$ \frac{0.0869 - d_b}{0.0869 - 0.01} = \exp\left(-0.3 \frac{1.0}{0.30}\right) = \exp(-1.0) = 0.368 $$

$$ 0.0869 - d_b = 0.0769 \times 0.368 = 0.0283 \Rightarrow d_b = 0.0586 \text{ m} $$

**Step D — Compute bubble rise velocity:**

$$ U_b = U_0 - U_{mf} + 0.711 \sqrt{g d_b} = 0.30 - 0.05 + 0.711 \sqrt{9.81 \times 0.0586} $$

$$ = 0.25 + 0.711 \times 0.758 = 0.25 + 0.539 = 0.789 \text{ m s}^{-1} $$

**Step E — Compute $K_{bc}$:**

$$ K_{bc} = 4.5 \times \frac{0.05}{0.0586} + 5.85 \times \frac{(2.0 \times 10^{-5})^{0.5} \times (9.81)^{0.25}}{(0.0586)^{1.25}} $$

$$ = 4.5 \times 0.853 + 5.85 \times \frac{4.472 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.772}{0.0330} $$

$$ = 3.84 + 5.85 \times \frac{7.93 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0330} = 3.84 + 5.85 \times 0.2403 = 3.84 + 1.406 = 5.25 \text{ s}^{-1} $$

**Step F — Compute $K_{ce}$:**

$$ K_{ce} = 6.77 \left(\frac{2.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.5 \times 0.789}{(0.0586)^{3}}\right)^{0.5} $$

$$ = 6.77 \left(\frac{7.89 \times 10^{-6}}{2.012 \times 10^{-4}}\right)^{0.5} = 6.77 \times (0.03921)^{0.5} = 6.77 \times 0.1980 = 1.34 \text{ s}^{-1} $$

**Step G — Compute $K_{be}$:**

$$ \frac{1}{K_{be}} = \frac{1}{5.25} + \frac{1}{1.34} = 0.1905 + 0.7463 = 0.9368 $$

$$ K_{be} = 1.067 \text{ s}^{-1} $$

**Step H — Compute conversion in well-mixed limit:**

$$ X = 1 - \exp\left(-1.067 \times \frac{1.0}{0.30}\right) = 1 - \exp(-3.557) = 1 - 0.0285 = 0.9715 $$

Thus, **97.15% conversion** is predicted. If experimental data shows a lower conversion (say 80%), then the model is over-predicting and $d_b$ must be re-estimated (typically larger) until the predicted $X$ matches.

**Step I — Iterative adjustment using a non-linear solver:**

If $X_{\text{exp}} = 0.80$, solve:

$$ 0.80 = 1 - \exp\left(-K_{be}(d_b) \frac{H}{U_0}\right) $$

$$ \exp\left(-K_{be}(d_b) \cdot 3.333\right) = 0.20 \Rightarrow K_{be}(d_b) = -\frac{\ln 0.20}{3.333} = 0.4825 \text{ s}^{-1} $$

Now invert: solve $K_{be}(d_b) = 0.4825$ numerically. Through trial:

- Try $d_b = 0.20$ m → recompute $U_b$, $K_{bc}$, $K_{ce}$ → $K_{be} \approx 0.48 \text{ s}^{-1}$ ✓

Hence, the **fitted bubble diameter is 0.20 m**, indicating a much larger bubble than predicted by the Mori–Wen correlation. This is typical when distributors are poorly designed or when particle size distribution is broad.

> [!TIP]
> A **higher fitted $d_b$** relative to Mori–Wen prediction suggests poor gas distribution, large jet penetration, or slugging behaviour.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choice of Objective Function

| Method | Formula | Best Use |
|--------|---------|----------|
| Sum of Squared Residuals (SSR) | $\sum (X_{\text{exp}} - X_{\text{model}})^{2}$ | Normally distributed errors |
| Sum of Absolute Residuals (SAR) | $\sum \vert X_{\text{exp}} - X_{\text{model}} \vert$ | Robust to outliers |
| Relative SSR | $\sum \left[\dfrac{X_{\text{exp}} - X_{\text{model}}}{X_{\text{exp}}}\right]^{2}$ | When $X$ spans orders of magnitude |
| Log-Likelihood | $\sum \left[\ln(X_{\text{exp}}) - \ln(X_{\text{model}})\right]^{2}$ | Multiplicative errors |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU microproject reports, **SSR with non-linear Levenberg–Marquardt minimization** is the standard approach. Many simulation platforms (e.g., MATLAB `lsqnonlin`, Python `scipy.optimize.least_squares`, or Excel Solver) can be used.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three-Phase Structure of the Bubbling Bed

```mermaid
graph TD
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`
**Define the bubble-to-emulsion interchange coefficient $K_{be}$ used in the Kunii–Levenspiel bubbling bed model. What is its physical significance?**

**Model Answer:**
The bubble-to-emulsion interchange coefficient $K_{be}$ (units of $\text{s}^{-1}$) represents the **volumetric rate of gas exchange between the bubble phase and the emulsion phase** per unit volume of the bed. Physically, it quantifies how rapidly gas molecules in a rising bubble transfer to the surrounding catalyst-rich emulsion, where most of the chemical reaction occurs. It is calculated from two resistances in series:

$$ \frac{1}{K_{be}} = \frac{1}{K_{bc}} + \frac{1}{K_{ce}} $$

A large $K_{be}$ (small bubbles, slow gas flow) means bubble gas quickly equilibrates with the emulsion, while a small $K_{be}$ (large bubbles, fast gas flow) means significant gas bypass and lower conversion per unit bed height.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`
**What is the purpose of "fitting" reported mass transfer data with the bubbling bed model?**

**Model Answer:**
Fitting reported mass transfer (or heat transfer) data with the bubbling bed model serves to:

1. **Back-calculate the effective bubble diameter ($d_b$)** prevailing in the experimental system, since $d_b$ is difficult to measure directly.
2. **Validate the hydrodynamic assumptions** of the Kunii–Levenspiel framework against real operating data.
3. **Scale-up** the laboratory reactor to industrial size, using the fitted $d_b$ to predict performance in larger beds.
4. **Diagnose operating problems** such as slugging, poor gas distribution, or jetting, which manifest as anomalously large fitted bubble diameters.

The fit is typically performed by **minimizing the sum of squared residuals** between experimental and predicted conversion (or heat transfer coefficient) values.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Derive the expression for the bubble-to-emulsion gas interchange coefficient $K_{be}$ in the Kunii–Levenspiel bubbling bed model, starting from the bubble-to-cloud and cloud-to-emulsion interchange coefficients. State clearly the units and physical meaning of each term.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
A bubbling fluidized bed has $U_0 = 0.40 \text{ m s}^{-1}$, $U_{mf} = 0.04 \text{ m s}^{-1}$, $d_p = 600 \text{ }\mu\text{m}$, $D = 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{2} \text{ s}^{-1}$, $\varepsilon_{mf} = 0.48$, $D_t = 0.50 \text{ m}$, $H = 1.2 \text{ m}$, and $d_{b0} = 0.015 \text{ m}$. Estimate the bubble diameter at the top of the bed, the interchange coefficient $K_{be}$, and the conversion for a first-order reaction (assume $K_{be} H / U_0 \approx 3.6$).

#### Model Solution (a)

**Step 1 — Define the two resistances in series.**
The bubble gas exchanges mass with the surrounding emulsion in two stages: (1) bubble-to-cloud ($K_{bc}$) and (2) cloud-to-emulsion ($K_{ce}$). The overall driving force is the concentration difference between bubble and bulk emulsion.

**Step 2 — Write the resistance-in-series equation.**

$$ \frac{1}{K_{be}} = \frac{1}{K_{bc}} + \frac{1}{K_{ce}} $$

This is the harmonic mean of the two individual rates.

**Step 3 — Express $K_{bc}$.** Convection through bubble + diffusion at interface:

$$ K_{bc} = 4.5 \left(\frac{U_{mf}}{d_b}\right) + 5.85 \left(\frac{D^{0.5} g^{0.25}}{d_{b}^{1.25}}\right) $$

**Step 4 — Express $K_{ce}$.** Diffusion-dominated, geometric scaling:

$$ K_{ce} = 6.77 \left(\frac{D \varepsilon_{mf} U_b}{d_{b}^{3}}\right)^{0.5} $$

**Step 5 — Combine to give $K_{be}$:**

$$ K_{be} = \left[\frac{1}{4.5 \left(\dfrac{U_{mf}}{d_b}\right) + 5.85 \left(\dfrac{D^{0.5} g^{0.25}}{d_{b}^{1.25}}\right)} + \frac{1}{6.77 \left(\dfrac{D \varepsilon_{mf} U_b}{d_{b}^{3}}\right)^{0.5}}\right]^{-1} $$

**Units:** $K_{bc}, K_{ce}, K_{be}$ all in $\text{s}^{-1}$. Physically, $K_{be}$ represents the **fraction of bubble gas volume that exchanges with the emulsion per unit time**.

#### Model Solution (b)

**Step 1 — Compute $A$ and $d_{bm}$:**

$$ A = \frac{\pi (0.50)^{2}}{4} = 0.1963 \text{ m}^{2} $$

$$ d_{bm} = 0.652 \left[0.1963 \times (0.40 - 0.04)\right]^{0.4} = 0.652 \left[0.0707\right]^{0.4} = 0.652 \times 0.343 = 0.2237 \text{ m} $$

**Step 2 — Solve for $d_b$ at top:**

$$ \frac{0.2237 - d_b}{0.2237 - 0.015} = \exp\left(-0.3 \times \frac{1.2}{0.50}\right) = \exp(-0.72) = 0.4868 $$

$$ 0.2237 - d_b = 0.2087 \times 0.4868 = 0.1016 \Rightarrow d_b = 0.1221 \text{ m} $$

**Step 3 — Compute $U_b$:**

$$ U_b = 0.40 - 0.04 + 0.711 \sqrt{9.81 \times 0.1221} = 0.36 + 0.711 \times 1.095 = 0.36 + 0.779 = 1.139 \text{ m s}^{-1} $$

**Step 4 — Compute $K_{bc}$:**

$$ K_{bc} = 4.5 \times \frac{0.04}{0.1221} + 5.85 \times \frac{(2.5 \times 10^{-5})^{0.5} \times (9.81)^{0.25}}{(0.1221)^{1.25}} $$

$$ = 4.5 \times 0.3276 + 5.85 \times \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.772}{0.0823} = 1.474 + 5.85 \times 0.1077 = 1.474 + 0.630 = 2.104 \text{ s}^{-1} $$

**Step 5 — Compute $K_{ce}$:**

$$ K_{ce} = 6.77 \left(\frac{2.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.48 \times 1.139}{(0.1221)^{3}}\right)^{0.5} = 6.77 \left(\frac{1.367 \times 10^{-5}}{1.821 \times 10^{-3}}\right)^{0.5} $$

$$ = 6.77 \times (7.51 \times 10^{-3})^{0.5} = 6.77 \times 0.0866 = 0.5866 \text{ s}^{-1} $$

**Step 6 — Compute $K_{be}$:**

$$ \frac{1}{K_{be}} = \frac{1}{2.104} + \frac{1}{0.5866} = 0.4753 + 1.705 = 2.180 $$

$$ K_{be} = 0.4587 \text{ s}^{-1} $$

**Step 7 — Conversion:**

$$ X = 1 - \exp(-3.6) = 1 - 0.0273 = 0.9727 \text{ or } 97.27\% $$

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Explain the procedure for fitting reported mass transfer data with the bubbling bed model. List the parameters that are typically treated as inputs, adjustable variables, and response variables.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
The following experimental conversion data were obtained for a first-order ozone decomposition reaction in a lab-scale bubbling fluidized bed:

| $U_0$ (m/s) | $X_{\text{exp}}$ |
|--------------|------------------|
| 0.10 | 0.65 |
| 0.20 | 0.55 |
| 0.30 | 0.45 |
| 0.40 | 0.38 |

Using $U_{mf} = 0.025 \text{ m s}^{-1}$, $D = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{2} \text{ s}^{-1}$, $\varepsilon_{mf} = 0.50$, $H = 0.6 \text{ m}$, $d_{b0} = 0.005 \text{ m}$, $D_t = 0.10 \text{ m}$, fit the data to the bubbling bed model and comment on the trend in $K_{be}$ as $U_0$ increases.

#### Model Solution (a)

**Step 1 — Input parameters (fixed from experiment):**
- $U_0, U_{mf}, D, \varepsilon_{mf}, D_t, H, d_{b0}, d_p, g$

**Step 2 — Adjustable parameter:**
- Bubble diameter $d_b$ (or equivalently, $K_{be}$)

**Step 3 — Response variable:**
- Conversion $X$ (for mass transfer studies) or heat transfer coefficient $h$ (for thermal studies)

**Step 4 — Procedure:**

1. Compute Mori–Wen maximum bubble size $d_{bm}$ from $A$ and $(U_0 - U_{mf})$.
2. Compute initial $d_b$ profile using the exponential growth equation.
3. Calculate $U_b$, $K_{bc}$, $K_{ce}$, and $K_{be}$.
4. Compute $X_{\text{model}}$ using the chosen reactor model (well-mixed, plug flow, or two-phase).
5. Compute the residual $r_i = X_{\text{exp},i} - X_{\text{model},i}$ and the objective function $SSR = \sum r_i^{2}$.
6. Use a non-linear optimization algorithm (Newton–Raphson, Levenberg–Marquardt, or Excel Solver) to vary $d_b$ until SSR is minimized.
7. Report the fitted $d_b$ and $K_{be}$ along with a residual plot.
8. Validate by checking that the fitted $d_b$ lies in the physically reasonable range (1 cm to $D_t$).

#### Model Solution (b)

**Step 1 — Compute $A$ and $d_{bm}$ for each $U_0$:**

$$ A = \frac{\pi (0.10)^{2}}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2} $$

For $U_0 = 0.10$ m/s: $d_{bm} = 0.652 [7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.075]^{0.4} = 0.652 \times (5.89 \times 10^{-4})^{0.4}$

$$ = 0.652 \times 0.0577 = 0.0376 \text{ m} $$

**Step 2 — Compute $d_b$ at top of bed:**

$$ \frac{0.0376 - d_b}{0.0376 - 0.005} = \exp\left(-0.3 \times \frac{0.6}{0.10}\right) = \exp(-1.8) = 0.1653 $$

$$ 0.0376 - d_b = 0.0326 \times 0.1653 = 0.00539 \Rightarrow d_b = 0.0322 \text{ m} $$

**Step 3 — Compute $U_b$ and $K_{be}$:**

$$ U_b = 0.10 - 0.025 + 0.711 \sqrt{9.81 \times 0.0322} = 0.075 + 0.711 \times 0.5624 = 0.075 + 0.400 = 0.475 \text{ m s}^{-1} $$

$$ K_{bc} = 4.5 \times \frac{0.025}{0.0322} + 5.85 \times \frac{(1.8 \times 10^{-5})^{0.5} \times (9.81)^{0.25}}{(0.0322)^{1.25}} $$

$$ = 3.494 + 5.85 \times \frac{4.243 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.772}{0.01584} = 3.494 + 5.85 \times 0.4744 = 3.494 + 2.776 = 6.270 \text{ s}^{-1} $$

$$ K_{ce} = 6.77 \left(\frac{1.8 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.50 \times 0.475}{(0.0322)^{3}}\right)^{0.5} = 6.77 \left(\frac{4.275 \times 10^{-6}}{3.339 \times 10^{-5}}\right)^{0.5} $$

$$ = 6.77 \times (0.1281)^{0.5} = 6.77 \times 0.3579 = 2.423 \text{ s}^{-1} $$

$$ \frac{1}{K_{be}} = \frac{1}{6.270} + \frac{1}{2.423} = 0.1595 + 0.4127 = 0.5722 \Rightarrow K_{be} = 1.748 \text{ s}^{-1} $$

**Step 4 — Compute $X_{\text{model}}$:**

$$ \tau = H / U_0 = 0.6 / 0.10 = 6.0 \text{ s} $$

$$ X_{\text{model}} = 1 - \exp(-K_{be} \tau) = 1 - \exp(-1.748 \times 6.0) = 1 - \exp(-10.49) \approx 1 - 2.76 \times 10^{-5} \approx 1.00 $$

**Comparison:** $X_{\text{model}} = 1.00$ but $X_{\text{exp}} = 0.65$. The model over-predicts, indicating the **fitted $d_b$ must be larger** than the Mori–Wen prediction. Iterating with $d_b \approx 0.10$ m would give a $K_{be} \approx 0.6 \text{ s}^{-1}$, yielding $X_{\text{model}} \approx 1 - \exp(-3.6) = 0.973$ — still too high, so a still larger bubble (e.g., $d_b = 0.15$ m) is needed.

**Step 5 — Repeat for other $U_0$ values** to find the trend.

**Final trend:** As $U_0$ increases, the fitted $d_b$ increases sharply (often faster than Mori–Wen predicts) and $K_{be}$ decreases. This is because larger gas throughputs promote bubble coalescence and reduce contact time, lowering the apparent interchange rate per unit gas volume. The fitted $d_b$ at high $U_0$ may approach $D_t$, indicating incipient slugging.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the harmonic mean:** Students often add $K_{bc}$ and $K_{ce}$ directly. The correct combination is **resistances in series**, i.e., reciprocals add. This is a 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** $d_b$ must be in metres, not mm, in the Kunii–Levenspiel correlations. A factor of $10^{3}$ error will cost 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Bubble size out of range:** If fitted $d_b > 0.6 D_t$, the bed is in slugging regime, and the model breaks down. Always comment on the regime.
> 4. **No mention of distributor effect:** $d_{b0}$ depends on distributor design (porous plate vs. perforated plate vs. nozzle). A 1-mark deduction if not mentioned.
> 5. **Ignoring cloud-wake fraction:** The Kunii–Levenspiel model assumes $\alpha_c \approx 0.2$. If the model is being fit carefully, this should be mentioned.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam / Microproject Defence:**

- **Bubbling Bed Model** = Kunii–Levenspiel 3-phase model: bubble + cloud–wake + emulsion.
- **Interchange coefficient $K_{be}$** = harmonic mean of $K_{bc}$ and $K_{ce}$.
- **Bubble size $d_b$** = the only adjustable parameter when fitting data.
- **Mori–Wen correlation** gives $d_{bm} = 0.652 [A(U_0 - U_{mf})]^{0.4}$ in metres.
- **Bubble growth equation** = exponential approach to $d_{bm}$ with characteristic length $D_t/0.3$.
- **Bubble rise velocity** = $U_b = U_0 - U_{mf} + 0.711 \sqrt{g d_b}$.
- **Conversion in well-mixed limit** = $X = 1 - \exp(-K_{be} H / U_0)$.
- **Fitting method** = minimize SSR using a non-linear solver; vary $d_b$.
- **Trend with $U_0$** = as $U_0$ rises, fitted $d_b$ rises and $K_{be}$ falls (slugging onset).
- **Heat-transfer-fitting variant** = use $h$ instead of $X$; fit via Nusselt–Reynolds–Prandtl correlation with $K_{be}$-modified Re.
- **Validation** = check fitted $d_b$ lies in $0.01 \text{ m} < d_b < 0.6 D_t$.
- **Slugging regime** = $d_b > 0.6 D_t$ — bubbling bed model is no longer valid; switch to slug-flow model.
- **Distributor effect** = $d_{b0}$ depends on distributor; typical values 0.005–0.05 m.
- **Cloud-wake fraction** = $\alpha_c \approx 0.2$ for Geldart B particles.
- **First-order assumption** = common for ozone decomposition, a classic KTU microproject reaction.
